title: How to listen
subtitle: Music is like stairs
goal: Train the ear to notice up, down, big steps, small steps, loud, soft.

pages.figure: Cleffy front and center, waving.
pages.cleffy: Hi. I'm Cleffy. I'm going to teach you piano. Before we touch a single key, I want to ask you something. When a song gets stuck in your head, what's actually stuck? It's not the words. It's not even the instruments. It's the shape.

pages.figure: An illustration of a staircase. A small dot bounces up one stair at a time, then down. Soft piano notes play in sync with each step.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole secret. Music is like stairs. Seriously. Every song you've ever heard — the notes are just feet moving up and down a staircase. Sometimes they take one stair at a time. We call that a STEP. Sometimes they hop over a stair. We call that a SKIP. Sometimes they jump over a bunch of stairs. We call that a LEAP. Step, skip, leap. Those three words are going to show up a lot. Remember them.

pages.figure: Cleffy smiling, standing at the bottom of a staircase.
pages.cleffy: You've been listening to music your whole life. So you already know how the stairs feel. You just haven't put words to it yet. Let's change that.

pages.figure: A tall staircase. A dot sits at the very top. Then another dot at the very bottom.

audio.A: C6 single note — very high
audio.B: C2 single note — very low

pages.cleffy: Listen. Here's one note way up at the top of the stairs. And here's one all the way down at the bottom. You can already tell which is which. Nobody had to teach you that. High is high. Low is low. Good. Keep that feeling.

pages.figure: Two playback cards side by side. Card A: dot walking UP three stairs. Card B: dot walking DOWN three stairs.

audio.A: C-D-E ascending
audio.B: E-D-C descending

pages.cleffy: Now, not one note. A tiny melody. One of these walks up the stairs. The other walks down. Listen to both. Then tell me which one went up.

corrections.cleffy: Other way. Listen again — the first note starts low and each one goes a little higher. That's walking up the stairs.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. One play button. Two answers: Up the stairs, Down the stairs.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Up the stairs or down?
pages.completion_cleffy: Nice. You just did the first job of a musician — noticing which way the music goes. Hold onto that.

pages.figure: Two stair figures. Card A: a small dot steps onto every single stair, one at a time. Card B: a dot skips — hopping over one stair each time.

audio.A: C-D-E-F stepwise ascending
audio.B: C-E-G-B arpeggiated ascending

pages.cleffy: Watch this. Both of these go up the stairs. But one takes steps. The other takes skips. Listen. Hear it? The first one touches every stair. Step, step, step. The second one hops over stairs. Skip, skip, skip. And if it jumps over a BUNCH of stairs at once, that's a leap. We'll meet those soon. Step, skip, leap. Those are the three moves in music. That's the whole thing.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Play button. Two answers: Step, Skip.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Was that a step or a skip?

corrections.cleffy: That one actually touched every stair. No hopping. When there are no gaps, it's a step.
That one had a hop in it. When you hear a gap between the notes, the stairs got skipped. That's a skip.

pages.figure: Stairs with a giant speaker symbol. One dot stomps loudly up the stairs. Then a feather drifts quietly down.

audio.A: Short phrase, loud
audio.B: Same phrase, soft

pages.cleffy: Same stairs, but not the same feeling. You can stomp up. Or you can tiptoe up. Music can be loud, or soft, or anything in between. Loud isn't better. Soft isn't weaker. They just feel different. A lullaby tiptoes on purpose. A parade stomps on purpose.

pages.figure: Stairs with a clock. One dot sprints up. One dot strolls up slowly.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle at 60 bpm
audio.B: Twinkle Twinkle at 140 bpm

pages.cleffy: And speed. You can run up the stairs. Or take your time. Same tune. Two different moods. Faster isn't harder. It's just a different feeling.

pages.figure: One staircase with icons around it — up/down arrows, a speaker, a feather, a runner, a turtle, and three feet labeled step, skip, leap.
pages.cleffy: Up and down. Steps, skips, leaps. Loud and soft. Fast and slow. That's almost everything music has. I'm not joking. Mozart, Taylor Swift, whoever — they're all just rearranging those few things. Once your ears can hear them, the page can teach them to you.

pages.figure: Clean play button. A faint staircase pattern in the background. No keyboard yet.

audio.A: First 8 bars of Moonlight Sonata 1st movement

pages.cleffy: Let me play you something real. It's from a piece called Moonlight Sonata, by Beethoven. Don't try to figure out the notes. Just listen for the stairs. Notice when it climbs. Notice when it drops. Notice if it's loud or quiet.
pages.followup_cleffy: Did you hear it? The stairs go up, slowly. Then they come down. And it's quiet the whole time — Beethoven wanted gentle stomps. Here's the wild part. You just read music with your ears. For the rest of this course, we'll just teach you to read the same thing with your eyes.

pages.figure: A staircase fades into a musical staff. Same five lines. Same shape.
pages.cleffy: Soon, we'll put music on a page. Like this. Here's what no one tells beginners: the page is just a picture of the stairs. Up on the page means up the stairs. Down on the page means down. If your ears know the stairs, the page is easy. It's just a map. If your ears don't know the stairs, the page is gibberish. That's when people quit piano. We're not going to do that. We started here for a reason.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Play button and two answers per round. The question rotates.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. I'll mix up the questions. Listen each time, then pick.

pages.figure: A small banner — One quick check before we wrap up.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. This isn't the test. Just seeing how it feels.

pages.figure: Postcard card — Lesson 1, ears first. Cleffy sitting at the bottom of a staircase, content.
pages.cleffy: You did the single hardest thing anyone does in music. You paid attention. Tomorrow we actually meet the piano. Same ears. New toy. See you then.

questions.audio: C-E-G ascending
questions.prompt: Did that walk up or down the stairs?

questions.audio: loud_phrase_then_soft_phrase
questions.prompt: Which came second, loud or soft?

questions.audio: A=C-D-E-F stepwise, B=C-E-G-C arpeggio
questions.prompt: Which one was steps — A or B?

questions.audio: Same tune slow then fast
questions.prompt: Which one was faster, A or B?

remediation.title: Let's try that again, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey. That's totally normal. Ears are like muscles — they just need a few reps. Let's do these a little slower, and I'll play the stairs so you can hear them twice.
rules: Both demo cards auto-play before the student answers

completion.cleffy: One lesson in. Two hundred and forty-nine to go. You picked a good day to start.

title: Meet the piano
subtitle: A staircase on its side
goal: Learn that the piano is a sideways staircase: low on the left, high on the right. Touch a key for the first time.

pages.figure: Cleffy waving beside a grand piano. Side-on view so you can see the whole keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Welcome back. Yesterday we did all of that with your ears. Today we actually meet the piano. Don't worry about hitting a wrong note. There aren't any. We're just exploring.

pages.figure: Animation — a vertical staircase slowly tilts 90 degrees until it lies flat on its side. Then the stairs morph into piano keys. Same shape, new angle.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole secret of the piano in one sentence. The piano is a staircase. It's just lying on its side. Low notes live on the left. High notes live on the right. Every key is one stair. Take a step to the right, the note gets higher. Take a step to the left, the note gets lower. That's it. That's the piano.

pages.figure: Full keyboard, zoomed out. An arrow labeled 'low' points to the far left, an arrow labeled 'high' points to the far right. Gentle glow in the middle.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole thing. Left end, right end, and a big middle. That's where your hands will usually live — right in the middle. See all those keys? Don't let the number scare you. You already know the shape. It's just stairs.

pages.figure: Three playback buttons. Left button lit blue. Right button lit red. Middle button lit yellow.

audio.A: Lowest C on the piano (C1)
audio.B: Highest C on the piano (C8)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Here's the key all the way on the left — the very bottom of the stairs. Huge, rumbly sound. Now the key all the way on the right — the top. Tiny, tingly sound. And here's the middle. That one probably sounds the most like music to you. Because your ears are used to living in the middle.

pages.figure: Virtual keyboard, full width. No labels. Every key gently pulsing like it wants to be tapped.
pages.cleffy: Okay. Your turn. Tap any key. Any one. You cannot get this wrong. I promise. Just feel what a piano does.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Right there. That's you playing piano. Try a few more if you want. Poke around.

pages.figure: Keyboard with the LEFT third glowing blue.
pages.cleffy: Now pick any key on the LEFT side. Down at the bottom of the stairs. Listen to how deep it sounds.
pages.followup_cleffy: Good. That's what low sounds like. Heavy. Like a big drum.

pages.figure: Keyboard with the RIGHT third glowing red.
pages.cleffy: Now pick any key on the RIGHT side. Up at the top.
pages.followup_cleffy: Tiny and bright, right? Like a bell. Same piano. Totally different feeling.

pages.figure: Keyboard with the MIDDLE third glowing yellow.
pages.cleffy: Now pick any key right in the middle. That's the part of the piano where most music lives.
pages.followup_cleffy: Warm. Singable. That's home base.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Play button. Two answer buttons: Left side, Right side.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. I'll play a key. You tell me — did it come from the left side of the piano, or the right?

interaction.question: Where on the piano was that note?

corrections.cleffy: That one was low and rumbly. Low lives on the left. Remember — bottom of the stairs.
That one was bright and tiny. High lives on the right. Top of the stairs.

pages.figure: Keyboard with four white keys in the middle glowing, numbered 1-2-3-4 going right.
pages.cleffy: Now we're going up the stairs. Tap those four keys in order, left to right. One, two, three, four. Each one is one step higher than the last.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hear it climb? You just played four steps up the stairs. That's a melody. A tiny one. But a real one.

pages.figure: Same four keys, now numbered 1-2-3-4 going LEFT.
pages.cleffy: Now let's come back down. Tap them in the other direction.
pages.followup_cleffy: Down the stairs. Same keys, different direction. Notice how different it feels? Going down sounds a little sleepier.

pages.figure: Keyboard on screen. A single key flashes green and plays its note. An arrow shows where it is.
pages.cleffy: Watch what I play. Listen to it. Then tap the same key.
pages.followup_cleffy: See, hear, play. That's the whole loop. We'll do that a lot.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two adjacent white keys in the middle glow — a 'step' label floats above them.
pages.cleffy: Remember steps from yesterday? On a piano, a step is just the next stair over. Tap two keys right next to each other, going right. Any two.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's a step up. Took one stair. No hopping. Try a step going DOWN now. Two keys next to each other, going left.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Play button. Two answer buttons: Step up, Step down.
pages.cleffy: Four more. I'll play two notes. You tell me if they went a step up or a step down.

pages.figure: Banner — One quick check before we wrap up.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Same as before — this isn't the real test, just checking how it lands.

pages.figure: Postcard card — Lesson 2, you met the piano. Cleffy standing on top of a tipped-over staircase, arms raised.
pages.cleffy: That's it. You touched the piano for the first time. Low left, high right, middle is home. Tomorrow, we start noticing the black keys. They're not scary. They're a map. See you then.

questions.figure: Keyboard image with a key circled on the LEFT third.
questions.prompt: Is this key on the low side or the high side?

questions.figure: Keyboard image with a key circled on the right end.
questions.prompt: This key will sound...

questions.figure: Keyboard image with a key circled in the middle.
questions.prompt: Where on the piano does most music live?

questions.audio: A low note (C2)
questions.prompt: Did that come from the left or right side of the piano?

questions.audio: A high note (C7)
questions.prompt: What about this one?
Tap any key on the LEFT side of the piano.
questions.accept: any key in left third of keyboard

remediation.title: The piano, one more time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey. No big deal. The piano is a new thing — takes a minute to get your bearings. Let's go through it again, slower. The stairs aren't going anywhere.
rules: Each section re-presented with extra visual highlights
Audio plays at 80% speed for the hear section

completion.cleffy: Two lessons down. You've used your ears AND your hands now. Tomorrow, we crack open the black keys. They hold a secret.

title: The black-key map
subtitle: 2-groups, 3-groups, forever
goal: Recognize groups of 2 and 3 black keys across the keyboard.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano, pointing at the black keys.
pages.cleffy: Back so soon. Good. Today I'm going to show you something I wish someone had told me the first time I looked at a piano. The black keys are a map.

pages.figure: Zoom on the middle of the keyboard. Black keys gently pulse — groups of 2 highlighted coral, groups of 3 highlighted sky.
pages.cleffy: Look at the black keys. They're not random. They come in little groups. Count them. Two. Then three. Then two. Then three. Forever. All the way up, all the way down. 2-group, 3-group, 2-group, 3-group. That's the map.

pages.figure: Keyboard scrolls slowly left to right. 2-groups flash coral, 3-groups flash sky as they pass.
pages.cleffy: Watch it scroll. Low end of the piano, all the way up to the high end. The pattern doesn't care where you are. 2, 3, 2, 3, 2, 3. Your piano has eighty-eight keys. Same pattern the whole way. Once you see it, you can't un-see it.

pages.figure: Keyboard with 6 black-key groups visible. Tap prompt.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Tap any group of 2 black keys. Any one.

corrections.cleffy: That's three. Count them — one, two, three. We want the shorter kind.
Black keys only for this one. The short ones that sit above.

pages.figure: Same keyboard. Tap any group of 3.
pages.cleffy: Now a group of 3.

corrections.cleffy: That's the short one. Look for three black keys in a row.

pages.figure: Two playback cards. A: two keys lit. B: three keys lit.

audio.A: Group of 2 played as chord (C#-D#)
audio.B: Group of 3 played as chord (F#-G#-A#)

pages.cleffy: They sound different, too. The 2-group has a sweeter sound. The 3-group is a little bit darker. You don't need to memorize why. Just notice you can hear a difference.

pages.figure: Two mystery play buttons. Prompt: which one had three keys?
pages.cleffy: Here. Listen to both. Tell me which one had three keys in it.

corrections.cleffy: Hear that extra one in the middle? When it's fuller, there are more keys stacked. That's the 3-group.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Play button. Answers: 2-group, 3-group.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. 2-group or 3-group?

pages.figure: Virtual keyboard with one 2-group glowing gold.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Tap both of those black keys. Top one first, then the bottom.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just played a 2-group. Try it the other way now — bottom, then top.

pages.figure: Keyboard with one 3-group glowing gold.
pages.cleffy: Now the 3-group. Bottom to top — one, two, three.

pages.figure: Full keyboard, no highlights.
pages.cleffy: Real test. Tap any 2-group, anywhere on the keyboard. High, low, middle. Your pick.

pages.figure: Same keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Now any 3-group.
pages.followup_cleffy: You can do this anywhere on the piano now. Low, high, middle — the pattern is the same.

pages.figure: A card shows either a 2 or a 3. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: I'll show you a number. You play that kind of group — anywhere you like.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mix of SEE and HEAR questions.
pages.cleffy: Last warm-up. Mix of looking and listening.

pages.figure: Banner — quick check before the real one.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Not the test yet — just seeing how it lands.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 3, the black-key map. Cleffy pointing at a 2-group with a little flag that says HERE.
pages.cleffy: You found the map. Tomorrow you use it. There's a white key that lives right next to every 2-group. It's called C. It's the most important note on the piano. See you then.

questions.figure: Keyboard image with a black-key group circled.
questions.prompt: Is this a 2-group or a 3-group?

questions.figure: Keyboard image with a 3-group circled.
questions.prompt: Is this a 2-group or a 3-group?

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any 3-group.

questions.audio: A 2-group played as chord
questions.prompt: Was that a 2-group or a 3-group?

questions.audio: A 3-group played as chord
questions.prompt: What about this one?
Play any 2-group.
questions.accept: any 2-group on keyboard

remediation.title: The map, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey, the pattern takes a minute to see. That's normal. Let me show you again, slower — and we'll count them together.

completion.cleffy: Three lessons in. You have ears, you have a keyboard, and now you have a map. Tomorrow we use it to find our first note.

title: Finding C
subtitle: Your first landmark
goal: Find C anywhere on the keyboard — right before every 2-group.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of the keyboard. One white key glows gold.
pages.cleffy: You know the black-key map. Good. Now we put our first flag in the ground. Every building needs a front door. Every map needs a landmark. Ours is a white key called C.

pages.figure: Zoom on one 2-group. The white key directly to the LEFT of it glows gold. Label: C.
pages.cleffy: C is the white key that sits right before every 2-group. Right before. On the left. Touching it. Every 2-group has a C hiding next to it, waiting to be found. That's the whole rule.

pages.figure: Keyboard scrolls. Every C lights up gold as it passes.
pages.cleffy: Watch the whole piano light up. Every single C, low to high. There's a lot of them. Seven, actually. They're all the same letter, just in different parts of the piano.

pages.figure: Zoomed keyboard. Multiple 2-groups visible. Prompt: tap any C.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Tap any C. Remember — the white key right before a 2-group.

corrections.cleffy: Close — but look for the 2-group right next to it. C is the one touching the left side of two black keys.

pages.figure: Different zoom, new 2-groups. Tap any C on the RIGHT side.
pages.cleffy: Now find a C on the high side — the right end of the piano.

pages.figure: Three play buttons — low C, Middle C, high C.

audio.A: C2 solo
audio.B: C4 (Middle C) solo

pages.cleffy: All of these are C. This one's low. This one's in the middle. This one's high. Different octaves, same letter, same feeling — just taller or shorter.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Middle C pulsing gold. The words Middle C appear beside it.

audio.A: Middle C (C4) solo, held

pages.cleffy: This one is special. It's the C right in the middle of the piano. We call it Middle C. It's going to show up every lesson for the rest of the course. Get comfortable with it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing.
pages.cleffy: Tap Middle C. Right there — the one in the middle.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's home base for the rest of your piano life.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Lowest C glowing, way on the left.
pages.cleffy: Now the lowest C on the piano. Way left.
pages.followup_cleffy: Deep, right? That's about as low as C gets.

pages.figure: Highest C glowing, right end.
pages.cleffy: Highest C, right end.
pages.followup_cleffy: Same letter. Wildly different sound.

pages.figure: Full keyboard, no highlights.
pages.cleffy: Now tap any C — you pick.

pages.figure: Keyboard with all 8 Cs gently pulsing.
pages.cleffy: Fun one. Tap three different Cs in a row — any three. Low, middle, high — or whatever order you want.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just played C three times in three different parts of the piano. Same note. Different heights. That's an octave relationship, and we'll get to it.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Play button. Answers: Low C, High C.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Low C or high C?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Cleffy plays a C. An arrow points to it briefly. Then student plays the same one.
pages.cleffy: Watch where I play. Listen. Then tap the same one.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Just a warm-up.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 4, you found C. Cleffy planting a gold flag on a C key.
pages.cleffy: You planted the flag. Tomorrow we make sure you can find C without thinking — fast, anywhere. Then we learn a second landmark. Its name is F.

questions.figure: Keyboard with a white key circled (is C).
questions.prompt: Is this a C?

questions.figure: Keyboard with a white key circled (not C).
questions.prompt: Is this a C?

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any C.

questions.audio: Middle C
questions.prompt: Was that near the middle of the piano, or far from it?

questions.audio: Low C (C2)
questions.prompt: Low or high C?
Play Middle C.
questions.accept: C4

remediation.title: C, once more, together
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey. C is everywhere, but it can take a minute to spot the first time. Let's go again — slower, with lots of 2-groups on the screen.

completion.cleffy: Four lessons down. You can find C on any piano, anywhere. Most people can't say that. Nice.

title: Any C, anywhere
subtitle: Act 1 · End of the first five
goal: Prove you own C — find it anywhere on the piano, by sight and by sound.
piece_context: Every piece you'll ever play starts somewhere on this keyboard. Today you own the landmark we'll count from once we start reading.

pages.figure: Cleffy standing between a small gold trophy and the piano, looking pleased.
pages.cleffy: You made it. Five lessons in. Today we put it all together and you prove something to yourself — that you know where C lives, anywhere on the piano. This is the end of Act 1. Let's go.

pages.figure: Four small panels: a staircase, a horizontal piano, the black-key map, a keyboard with every C glowing.
pages.cleffy: Quick recap of the whole act. Lesson 1 — music is stairs. Lesson 2 — the piano is stairs, lying sideways. Lesson 3 — the black keys are the map. Lesson 4 — C is the landmark, right before every 2-group. Hold all of that in your head. We're using all of it today.

pages.figure: Full keyboard, no highlights.
pages.cleffy: First challenge. Find a C on the LEFT side of the piano. Tap it.

pages.figure: Full keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Now a C on the RIGHT side. Up high.

pages.figure: Full keyboard. Middle area gently pulses.
pages.cleffy: And Middle C. The one right in the middle.

pages.figure: Full keyboard. Counter shows 0 of 5 Cs found. Each tapped C turns gold and stays.
pages.cleffy: Now for fun. Find five different Cs. Any five. Each one lights up when you tap it.
pages.followup_cleffy: There you go. Five Cs, five different parts of the piano. All the same letter. Something just clicked.

pages.figure: Four play buttons labeled A, B, C, D. Each plays a C in a different octave.

audio.A: C2
audio.B: C4 Middle C

pages.cleffy: Listen to four different Cs. They're all C. Different heights. Same letter.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 5. Play button. Three answers: Low C, Middle C, High C.
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. I'll play a C somewhere on the piano. Tell me — low, middle, or high.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Cleffy plays a C. Then student plays the same one back.
pages.cleffy: I'll play a C. You listen, then find it and play it back. Three rounds.

pages.figure: Keyboard with three C positions labeled 1, 2, 3 from low to high.
pages.cleffy: Your first little piano moment. Play three Cs — low, then middle, then high. One, two, three. Slow. Steady. No rush.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hear that? You just climbed up the stairs using the same note in three places. That's not a trick. That's music.

pages.figure: Same keyboard, numbers now run 3 at low, 1 at high.
pages.cleffy: Now come back down. High, middle, low.
pages.followup_cleffy: Down the stairs. Same Cs. Sleepier feel, right? Direction matters, even when the note stays the same.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mixed questions.
pages.cleffy: One last warm-up. Mix of everything from the last five lessons.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before the real test.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. You got this.

pages.figure: Celebration card — Act 1 complete. A small trophy with 1/10 acts. Cleffy giving a thumbs up.
pages.cleffy: That's Act 1. You have ears. You have a keyboard. You have a map. You have C. That's already more than most people who sit down at a piano. Tomorrow we find F. One more landmark, and the keyboard starts to feel like yours.

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any C on the left side.
questions.accept: any C in left half

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any C on the right side.
questions.accept: any C in right half

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap Middle C.
questions.accept: C4

questions.audio: Random C somewhere on keyboard
questions.prompt: Low, middle, or high C?

questions.audio: Another random C
questions.prompt: Low, middle, or high C?
Play three different Cs in a row — going up.
questions.accept: 3 Cs ascending

remediation.title: Act 1, one more pass
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey. This is the first real checkpoint — it's supposed to feel bigger. Let's walk through Act 1 again, slower, with everything highlighted.

completion.cleffy: You closed out your first act. That is a real thing. Act 2 opens tomorrow — we find F, then the rest of the alphabet, then your hands do something besides poke. Good work today.

title: Finding F
subtitle: Your second landmark
goal: Find F anywhere on the keyboard — right before every 3-group.

pages.figure: Keyboard with a gold flag on C. Cleffy holding a second flag, blue.
pages.cleffy: Act 2 starts now. You found C yesterday. Today we find its partner. A second landmark — one that lives on the other kind of group.

pages.figure: Zoom on a 3-group. The white key directly to the LEFT of it glows blue. Label: F.
pages.cleffy: F lives right before every 3-group. Same idea as C, different group. C — look for two black keys. F — look for three. Same move, different signpost.

pages.figure: Side-by-side: 2-group with C in gold, 3-group with F in blue.
pages.cleffy: C is before a 2-group. F is before a 3-group. That's the only rule. If you know which group you're looking at, you know which letter you just found.

pages.figure: Keyboard scrolls. Every F lights up blue.
pages.cleffy: Every single F on the keyboard, lighting up. There are seven of them. Same pattern as the Cs, just shifted over.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Multiple 3-groups visible. Tap any F.
pages.cleffy: Tap any F.

corrections.cleffy: Look for a 3-group. F is touching the left side of it.
That's a C — the 2-group landmark. F is the 3-group one.

pages.figure: Three play buttons — low F, middle F, high F.

audio.A: F2
audio.B: F4

pages.cleffy: Low F, middle F, high F. Same letter, different heights.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A plays Middle C, B plays Middle F.

audio.A: C4
audio.B: F4

pages.cleffy: Here's C and F in the same area. Listen to both. F is a few stairs higher than C. Not hugely different. But different. Your ear will learn this one over time.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. C or F?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Was that C or F? Both in the middle of the piano, so it's a real listen.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle F glowing blue.
pages.cleffy: Tap middle F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A low F glowing.
pages.cleffy: A low F — left side of the piano.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Middle C (gold) and Middle F (blue) glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play C and then F, right next to each other in the middle. C first, then F.
pages.followup_cleffy: That distance — C to F — is bigger than a step. We'll meet the proper name for this size of move later. For now, just feel how far apart they are.

pages.figure: Full keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Now find any F anywhere. Your pick.

pages.figure: Card says C or F each round. Student plays the named note.
pages.cleffy: I'll name a note — C or F. You play it, anywhere on the piano. Four rounds.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mix of questions.
pages.cleffy: Last warm-up.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones before the test.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 6. Keyboard with C (gold) and F (blue) flags both planted. Cleffy holding a small compass.
pages.cleffy: Two landmarks. C and F. Tomorrow we fill in the rest of the letters — five more white-key names. Then the whole keyboard has a name for every note.

questions.figure: Keyboard with F circled.
questions.prompt: Is this F?

questions.figure: Keyboard with C circled (trick question).
questions.prompt: Is this F?

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any F.

questions.audio: Middle F
questions.prompt: Was that C or F?

questions.audio: Middle C
questions.prompt: C or F?
Play middle F.
questions.accept: F4

remediation.title: F, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: F hides in the 3-group. Let's make sure your eyes find the 3-group fast, and F pops out naturally right before it.

completion.cleffy: Six lessons. Two landmarks. You know the spine of the keyboard now. Tomorrow the rest of the letters fall into place.

title: The musical alphabet
subtitle: Seven letters, and then it repeats
goal: Name every white key on the piano — A through G, looping forever.

pages.figure: Full keyboard. C and F labeled. Question marks on the other white keys.
pages.cleffy: You know C. You know F. That's two. There are more. Five more. Today we name them. Good news — it's the regular alphabet, but short. A through G. That's it.

pages.figure: A row: A B C D E F G. Then another A B C D E F G. Then a third.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole rule. The musical alphabet is seven letters. A, B, C, D, E, F, G. Then it starts over. A, B, C, D, E, F, G. And again. No H. No I. Ever. Just those seven, over and over, forever.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C labeled. Each white key to its right gets labeled as it lights up: D, E, F, G, A, B, C.
pages.cleffy: Start from C. Go right one white key — that's D. Next one — E. Next — F, which you know. Then G. Then A. Then B. Then C again — one octave up. C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C.

pages.figure: Same keyboard, now showing letters going LEFT from Middle C: B, A, G, F, E, D, C.
pages.cleffy: And the other way. Go left from C. The white key to its left is B. Then A, G, F, E, D, C. The letters just walk backwards down the alphabet.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: tap the D right next to Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Test. Tap the D right next to Middle C.

corrections.cleffy: One too far. D is the white key right after C.
That's C. Go one white key to the right.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: tap the A nearest to Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Now A — the closest one to middle.

corrections.cleffy: Start at C. Count right — D, E, F, G, A. Five keys right of C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Each note lights up as Cleffy names it — C through C.

audio.A: C major scale ascending, Cleffy naming each letter

pages.cleffy: I'll play and name them going up. Listen — this is the whole white-key alphabet. C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C. All of them. Eight notes, seven names, landing back on C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Notes descend with names.

audio.A: C major scale descending with names

pages.cleffy: Now back down. C, B, A, G, F, E, D, C. Same letters, reverse order.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Seven answer buttons: C, D, E, F, G, A, B.
pages.cleffy: I'll play a white key near the middle. Name it. All seven letters are on the table now.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C through G glowing in order.
pages.cleffy: Walk up the stairs. C, D, E, F, G. One white key at a time.
pages.followup_cleffy: First five letters, in order, out of your hands.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G through C glowing.
pages.cleffy: Back down. G, F, E, D, C.

pages.figure: Card shows: D. Keyboard with no highlights.
pages.cleffy: I name a letter. You play it, anywhere. First — D.

pages.figure: Series of letter cards: A, E, G, B.
pages.cleffy: Four more. I name each, you find it.

pages.figure: Mixed prompts — see, hear, play.
pages.cleffy: Mix of listening and playing. Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 7. A keyboard with every white key labeled with its letter.
pages.cleffy: Every white key on the piano now has a name. Tomorrow, something cool. Every C sounds like every other C — just higher or lower. Same with every D. Same with every letter. That's called an octave. It's the piano's oldest trick.

questions.figure: Keyboard with one white key circled (a D).
questions.prompt: What letter is this?

questions.figure: Keyboard with a different key circled (an A).
questions.prompt: What letter?

questions.figure: Full keyboard.
questions.prompt: Tap any E.

questions.audio: D4 solo
questions.prompt: Which letter?

questions.audio: A4 solo
questions.prompt: Which letter?
Play G — anywhere on the piano.
questions.accept: any G

remediation.title: The alphabet, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Seven letters is a lot to grab at once. Let's walk through them going up and down a few times, with the names hovering above every key. Muscle memory first.

completion.cleffy: You know every white key on the piano by name now. Seriously. That took you seven days. Some people never get there.

title: Octaves
subtitle: Same letter, higher or lower
goal: Understand that every letter repeats up and down the piano — that's an octave.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing gold. Two arrows point to the Cs on either side — one low, one high.
pages.cleffy: Remember yesterday, when we said every C is still a C? Low C, Middle C, high C — same letter, different parts of the piano. There's a word for that relationship. It's the piano's oldest trick. It's called an octave.

pages.figure: Keyboard zoomed in. Middle C to the next C above it — all 8 white keys between them counted: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8.
pages.cleffy: Octave just means eight. If you start on any note and count eight white keys up — you land on the same letter again. C to the next C. D to the next D. G to the next G. Always eight keys. Always the same letter.

pages.figure: Full keyboard. All 7 Cs glow gold. Curved arrows connect each neighboring pair with the label octave.
pages.cleffy: Every C on the piano is an octave away from the next one. The piano has seven Cs. That's six octaves worth of Cs. And the same is true for every other letter.

pages.figure: Keyboard zoomed. Student counts the 7 white keys between two Cs (D, E, F, G, A, B) — plus the second C makes 8.
pages.cleffy: Let me show you why it's called eight. Start on C. Then D, E, F, G, A, B — that's seven steps. Then the next C — that's the eighth. Hence octave. Pretty literal name.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two Gs highlighted — Middle G and the G an octave above.
pages.cleffy: It's not just for C. Pick any letter. Here are two Gs, one octave apart. They look different on the keyboard — one is higher, one is lower. But the same letter. Same function.

pages.figure: Keyboard. One D glowing. Prompt: tap another D, any other one.
pages.cleffy: Here's a D. Tap any OTHER D on the keyboard — doesn't matter which.

interaction.accept: any D except the highlighted one

corrections.cleffy: Look for the same letter. The D right next to you was just a reminder — find another D somewhere else on the piano.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two Cs flash — one low, one high — and they play at the same time.

audio.A: Two Cs played simultaneously (C3 + C5)

pages.cleffy: Listen. This is what two Cs sound like when you play them at the same time (two notes together). Notice how it almost sounds like ONE note — just a bigger one. That's the octave's magic. The two notes blend so well, your ear can barely separate them.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Low C plays, then high C plays right after.

audio.A: C3, pause, C5

pages.cleffy: Now one at a time. Even played separately — they sound like the same note, just in a different voice.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: two Cs (octave apart). B: C and D (a step apart).

audio.A: C4 + C5 together
audio.B: C4 + D4 together

pages.cleffy: Two mystery chords. One is two of the same letter, an octave apart. The other is two different letters right next to each other. Which one sounds smoother — A or B?

corrections.cleffy: Listen again. Same-letter pairs blend. Different-letter pairs right next to each other feel a little tense — like they're arguing.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing gold. High C (one octave up) also glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play Middle C, then the C right above it. Just tap both, one then the other.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's an octave — played one at a time. Sounds like the same note stepping up a floor.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two Gs highlighted — Middle G (G4) and G5.
pages.cleffy: Now try it with G. Middle G, then the G right above.
pages.followup_cleffy: Same move, different letter. Same octave relationship.

pages.figure: Full keyboard. No highlights.
pages.cleffy: Your choice. Pick any letter. Play it, then play the same letter an octave up.

interaction.accept: two of the same letter, one octave apart

pages.figure: Keyboard. Cleffy plays two notes — either an octave or a step. You name it.
pages.cleffy: I play two notes. You tell me — octave (same letter) or step (different letter, right next to each other)?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mixed questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix of seeing and hearing.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 8. Two Cs on a keyboard, connected by a loop labeled octave. Cleffy smiling.
pages.cleffy: Same letter. Different floor. That's an octave. Tomorrow we give your fingers numbers, so I can actually tell you which finger to use. The day after, you play your first real song.

questions.figure: Keyboard with two keys circled (same letter, one octave apart).
questions.prompt: Are these the same letter?

questions.figure: Keyboard with two keys circled (different letters, a step apart).
questions.prompt: Are these the same letter?

questions.figure: Keyboard. One E glowing.
questions.prompt: Tap an E an octave above it.

questions.audio: Two Cs played together
questions.prompt: Did that sound like one note or two different notes?

questions.audio: C and D played together
questions.prompt: Was that an octave (same letter) or a step (different letter)?
Play two Ds, one octave apart.
questions.accept: D in two different octaves

remediation.title: Octaves, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Octaves are subtle — the notes almost hide inside each other. Let's listen a few more times, with both notes on screen together.

completion.cleffy: Every letter has twins, triplets, quintuplets — all across the piano. That's a big idea. You've got it.

title: Finger numbers
subtitle: Your hands, by the numbers
goal: Know which finger is which number on both hands. Thumbs are 1, pinkies are 5.
piece_context: From tomorrow on, I'll tell you which finger to use for which key. So today your fingers get their names.

pages.figure: Cleffy looking at two hands floating beside him. The fingers wiggle.
pages.cleffy: Look at your hands. You've been tapping the piano with whatever finger felt handy. Today we get organized. Every finger on your hands — left and right — gets a number.

pages.figure: Both hands shown palm-out, side by side. Each finger has a big number label: thumbs = 1, pointer = 2, middle = 3, ring = 4, pinky = 5.
pages.cleffy: Thumbs are 1. Pointers are 2. Middles are 3. Ring fingers are 4. Pinkies are 5. Both hands use the same numbers. Your left thumb is 1. Your right thumb is also 1.

pages.figure: Both thumbs glow. A curly brace connects them. Label: both = 1.
pages.cleffy: Why are both thumbs 1? Because music notation only uses numbers 1 through 5, ever. If left and right used different numbers, things would get really confusing really fast. So whoever invented this decided — thumb is 1 on both hands, done.

pages.figure: Just the right hand, fingers labeled 1-5.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. Thumb 1, pointer 2, middle 3, ring 4, pinky 5. Count them: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

pages.figure: Just the left hand, fingers labeled 1-5. Thumb is on the RIGHT side of the image this time (because the palm faces you).
pages.cleffy: Left hand. Same numbers. Thumb still 1. If you look at your left hand with the palm facing you — thumb is on the right side. Doesn't matter. Still 1.

pages.figure: Right hand diagram. Finger 3 (middle finger) is circled in red.
pages.cleffy: Which finger is that?

corrections.cleffy: Close. Count from the thumb — thumb 1, pointer 2, middle 3. The circled one is the middle. So it's 3.

pages.figure: Left hand diagram. Finger 5 (pinky) is circled.
pages.cleffy: This one?

corrections.cleffy: Pinky. Smallest finger, on the outside. That's 5.

pages.figure: Virtual keyboard. A blue circle with 3 inside appears above Middle C.
pages.cleffy: On the keyboard, I'll sometimes put a number above a key. That's the finger I want you to use. Right hand, finger 3. Tap Middle C with your middle finger.
pages.followup_cleffy: Real piano or physical keyboard, this matters a lot. On a touch screen I can't see which finger you actually used — so I'll trust you. But the more you practice using the right one, the easier real playing gets.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A blue 1 above any key. Student picks with thumb.
pages.cleffy: Right hand, finger 1. Thumb. Any key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A 5 above a key on the right.
pages.cleffy: Right hand, finger 5. Pinky. Any high key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five keys glow in order — C, D, E, F, G. Each has a number 1-5 above it.
pages.cleffy: Put your right hand on C, D, E, F, G — one finger per key. Thumb on C, pointer on D, middle on E, ring on F, pinky on G. That's called a five-finger position. It's the first thing every pianist learns.

pages.figure: Five fingers on C-D-E-F-G. Cleffy calls out a number, you tap that key.
pages.cleffy: Keep your hand right where it is. I'll call out a number. Whatever I say, tap the key your hand is on with that finger.
pages.followup_cleffy: Every finger has a note now. That's how pianists think. Not "which key do I hit?" But "which finger plays next?"

pages.figure: Left hand at C3-D3-E3-F3-G3. Finger 5 on C3 (low), finger 1 on G3 (high).
pages.cleffy: Quick preview of your left hand. When you put your left hand on the same five notes but down low, the numbers flip. Your pinky is on the lowest key. Your thumb is on the highest. Left-hand finger 5 is on low C. Left-hand finger 1 is on G. We'll use this later. For now, just know it flips.

pages.figure: Rotating questions — sometimes a hand diagram, sometimes a keyboard prompt.
pages.cleffy: Mix of seeing and playing. Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 9. Both hands labeled with numbers, waving.
pages.cleffy: Your fingers have names now. Tomorrow — and this is a big deal — you play a real song. By ear. No reading. Just follow along. It's called Hot Cross Buns. Three notes. You're ready.

questions.figure: Right hand, finger 2 circled.
questions.prompt: Which finger number?

questions.figure: Left hand, finger 1 circled.
questions.prompt: Which finger number?

questions.figure: Right hand, finger 4 circled.
questions.prompt: Which finger number?
Both hands use the same finger numbers. What number are both thumbs?
Put your right hand on C-D-E-F-G (five-finger position). Tap finger 3.
questions.accept: any tap on E4 with declared finger 3

remediation.title: Fingers, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: The number-your-fingers thing can feel weird at first. Let's go through it again with one hand at a time, slower.

completion.cleffy: Nine lessons in. Your hands have a roll call now. Tomorrow — song time.

title: Hot Cross Buns by ear
subtitle: Act 2 · Your first real song
goal: Play a full song — Hot Cross Buns — by listening and finding the notes. No reading yet.
piece_context: This is you playing piano. A real song people recognize. Not a drill. A song.

piece.title: Hot Cross Buns
piece.notes: E D C | E D C | C C C C | D D D D | E D C
piece.difficulty: beginner — 3 notes total

pages.figure: Cleffy holding sheet music that says Hot Cross Buns across the top. He tosses it over his shoulder.
pages.cleffy: Today you play a song. I know — that sounds big. It's not. It's three notes. But at the end of this lesson, you will have played a real song on a piano. Not a drill. A song. By ear. Ready?

pages.figure: Play button. Cleffy plays Hot Cross Buns once, straight through.

audio.A: Hot Cross Buns performed cleanly

pages.cleffy: First — just listen. This is the whole song. [Cleffy plays it] You know this one. Everyone knows this one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three keys glow: E (green), D (blue), C (gold). Labels above each.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole secret. The song uses three notes. E, D, and C. That's it. Three next-door neighbors. Steps, all of them. E is a step above D. D is a step above C.

pages.figure: Zoomed keyboard. Middle C labeled gold. D labeled blue. Arrow points to E, prompt: tap E.
pages.cleffy: Find E. It's right next to D, which is right next to C. C, D, E — three white keys in a row, starting from C. Tap E.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E, D, C glowing in order with numbers 3, 2, 1 above (right-hand fingering).
pages.cleffy: Put your right hand on C, D, E. Thumb on C, pointer on D, middle on E. Now play E, then D, then C — using fingers 3, 2, 1. That's Hot, Cross, Buns.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just played the first line. Hot. Cross. Buns.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Instruction: play E-D-C, then again.
pages.cleffy: The first line happens twice. Hot cross buns. Hot cross buns. Play E-D-C, then pause, then play it again.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C and D glowing. Instruction: four Cs, then four Ds.
pages.cleffy: Middle part. This is the one-a-penny, two-a-penny line. Tap C four times. Then tap D four times. One, one, one, one — two, two, two, two.
pages.followup_cleffy: One a penny. Two a penny.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E D C glowing one more time.
pages.cleffy: Last line. Back to Hot Cross Buns. E, D, C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Each key lights up just before you need to tap it, like a guide. Slow tempo.
pages.cleffy: Now the whole song, with training wheels on. The key you need to tap next will glow before you play it. Go slow. No rush.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the whole song. First time through. Breathe.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Keys still glow, but at normal tempo.
pages.cleffy: Now at normal speed with guides still on. Same keys, just faster. You've got this.

pages.figure: Keyboard. NO glowing keys. Just the keyboard. A title card says: Hot Cross Buns — you play it.
pages.cleffy: One more time. No highlights. No help. Just you. Play Hot Cross Buns.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was you. Playing piano. By ear. A thing you could not do two weeks ago.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The C, D, E one octave higher all glow.
pages.cleffy: Fun version. Play it one octave higher. Same song, same three fingers, same pattern — just up a floor.
pages.followup_cleffy: Same song, different floor. That's octaves at work.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before the mastery test.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration card — Act 2 complete. A small trophy with 2/10 acts. Cleffy clapping.
pages.cleffy: That's Act 2. You know every white key. You know octaves. Your fingers have numbers. And you played a real song. Starting tomorrow, we look at music on a page. Get ready — it's going to be a lot less scary than it looks. See you in Act 3.

questions.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: tap E.
questions.prompt: Tap E.

questions.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: tap D.
questions.prompt: Tap D.

questions.audio: First line of Hot Cross Buns (E-D-C)
questions.prompt: Was that the first line of Hot Cross Buns?
Play the first line of Hot Cross Buns: E, D, C.
questions.accept: E4 D4 C4 in order

remediation.title: Hot Cross Buns, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hey. Your first song. It takes a few tries. That's totally fine. Let's split it into really small pieces and rebuild it, bar by bar.

completion.cleffy: Ten lessons. Two acts. A song under your hands. Halfway through the first twenty-five. You're closer to Happy Birthday than you realize.

title: The staff
subtitle: Stairs on paper
goal: Meet the five-line staff. Understand it's just a picture of the stairs you already know.
piece_context: Every piece of written music in the world sits on a staff. Today we meet it.

pages.figure: A piano keyboard on the bottom of the screen. Above it, empty space. Cleffy holds up a blank sheet of paper.
pages.cleffy: You've been playing by ear. That's how every great pianist starts. But today we meet the thing that lets you play ANY song, not just ones you've heard. Written music. I know — sheet music looks scary. It's not. Watch.

pages.figure: Empty paper. Then — bump! — five horizontal lines draw themselves on the page, evenly spaced.
pages.cleffy: This is a staff. Five lines. Four spaces in between. That's it. That's the whole thing.

pages.figure: Animation — a vertical staircase slowly rotates 90 degrees and morphs INTO the five-line staff. The stair steps become the lines of the staff.
pages.cleffy: Now watch this — because this is the whole secret of written music. Remember the stairs from Lesson 1? The staff IS the stairs. Someone just drew them on paper. Lines and spaces are your stairs. Higher on the page means higher in pitch — up the stairs. Lower on the page means lower pitch — down. That's the whole idea.

pages.figure: The staff. A single round note sits on the middle line. Another note sits one line below it. Another sits in a space.
pages.cleffy: A note is just a little oval. Where it sits on the staff tells you how high or low it is. On a line — it's on that stair. In a space — it's on the stair between those two lines. Higher on the page is higher sound. That's it.

pages.figure: Two notes on the staff, one higher than the other. An arrow points up.
pages.cleffy: Look at these two notes. Which one is higher?

corrections.cleffy: Higher on the page means higher in pitch. The one that sits further up is the higher note. Up and down on the page matches up and down on the piano.

pages.figure: Staff. An arrow points to each line one at a time: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — counting from the bottom up.
pages.cleffy: Five lines. We count them from the bottom up. Line 1 is the lowest. Line 5 is the highest. You don't need to memorize this — it's just counting.

pages.figure: Staff. An arrow points to each space from bottom to top: space 1, 2, 3, 4.
pages.cleffy: Four spaces. Also counted bottom to top. Space 1 is the lowest, space 4 is the highest.

pages.figure: Staff. A single note sits on line 3 (the middle line).
pages.cleffy: Is this note on a line, or in a space?

corrections.cleffy: Look at the note. The line goes through the middle of it. If a line cuts through it, it's on a line.

pages.figure: Staff. A single note sits in space 2.
pages.cleffy: What about this one?

pages.figure: Staff. Two notes appear one at a time. First low on the staff, then high on the staff. Each plays a tone that matches.

audio.A: Two notes — low pitch, then high pitch

pages.cleffy: Here's a note low on the staff. Listen. Now a note high on the staff. Low on the page = low sound. High on the page = high sound. Just like the stairs.

pages.figure: Staff shows two notes per round — one higher, one lower.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Looking at the staff, is the second note higher or lower than the first?

pages.figure: Staff at top. Keyboard at bottom. A note appears on a random line or space. An arrow connects it to a key on the keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Here's the magic link. For every spot on the staff, there's a matching key on the piano. Higher on the staff = further right on the piano. You'll learn the exact spots over the next few lessons. For now, just trust the link.

pages.figure: Staff with three notes drawn: going up, up, up.
pages.cleffy: Three notes. The shape goes up, up, up. Tap three keys going up on the piano — any three. The shape is what matters, not the exact notes.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just read a shape off the page. Without names, without anything fancy — your eyes saw "going up" and your hands went up. That's reading music.

pages.figure: Mixed: some staff questions, some hearing questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix of looking at the staff and listening.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 11. A staff with a staircase behind it, fading. Cleffy giving a small confident nod.
pages.cleffy: The staff is just stairs on paper. Remember that. Tomorrow you meet someone you've known this whole time. That curly thing at the start of the staff? That's me.

questions.figure: Staff with a note on line 2.
questions.prompt: Line or space?

questions.figure: Staff with a note in space 3.
questions.prompt: Line or space?

questions.figure: Staff with two notes. One high, one low.
questions.prompt: Which note is higher?

questions.audio: Two notes — ascending
questions.prompt: Did the music go up or down?

questions.figure: Staff with three ascending notes.
questions.prompt: Play three keys that go UP the keyboard.
questions.accept: any 3 ascending keys

remediation.title: The staff, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Paper music is a big shift. Takes a minute. Let's go again with the stairs right next to the staff, side by side.

completion.cleffy: You met the staff. That was the hard part. Really. From here it gets way more specific — we start naming exactly where notes live.

title: The treble clef
subtitle: Meet your mascot — again
goal: Understand the treble clef symbol, what it does, and recognize it on a staff.

pages.figure: Cleffy, standing next to an empty staff. He clears his throat, looking a little shy.
pages.cleffy: Okay. I have a secret I've been keeping for twelve lessons. I'm not just a made-up character with a hat. I AM the weird curly thing at the start of a piece of music. Look.

pages.figure: Cleffy slowly morphs into the treble clef symbol on the staff. Back and forth a few times — Cleffy, treble clef, Cleffy, treble clef.
pages.cleffy: I'm the treble clef. My shape is the same shape you've seen as my body this whole time. That swirl on my chest — that's the treble clef's loop. Surprise.

pages.figure: Staff with the treble clef drawn at the beginning. The clef's loop hugs line 2 (the G line).
pages.cleffy: Here's what the treble clef actually does. It's a road sign at the start of the staff. It tells you: this staff is for higher notes. Specifically — the curly loop wraps around one line, and that line is called G. We'll use that later. For now, just know — treble clef means higher notes. Usually your right hand.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Both blank. Top staff has treble clef at start. Bottom staff has a different, simpler clef (bass) at start.
pages.cleffy: Music has two main staves. Top one usually uses the treble clef. That's me — higher notes, right hand. Bottom one uses a different clef called bass. We meet it in a few weeks. Two clefs, two staves, one piano. Most written music uses both.

pages.figure: Four mini-staves. Three have treble clefs, one has a different clef.
pages.cleffy: Which one of these does NOT have a treble clef?

corrections.cleffy: The treble clef is the fancy spiral one — the one that looks like me. The other one has dots and a thicker shape.

pages.figure: Treble clef on staff. The clef's loop is highlighted. An arrow points to the line the loop wraps around. Label: G.
pages.cleffy: Look at the curl at the bottom of the clef. See how it wraps around one specific line? That line is G. Whenever you see a note sitting on that line, it's called G. That's a handy landmark. But the REAL anchor — the note we'll count from — comes in two lessons. Middle C. Hang tight.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 glowing green. A staff with a note on the G line.

audio.A: G4 note

pages.cleffy: That's the sound of G. Middle-ish area of the piano. The note on the G line of the staff — the one I wrap around — sounds like that.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Find G on the piano. Above F — which you already know — go one more white key to the right. Tap G.

pages.figure: Staff with treble clef and three notes — one on the G line, one above, one below. Each labeled with how far from G.
pages.cleffy: G is useful as a quick landmark. See a note near the middle of the staff? Count steps from G and you can name it. But the note we'll ACTUALLY anchor the whole method to is Middle C, coming in Lesson 14. For now, just remember — that line I wrap around is G.

pages.figure: Staff with treble clef. A note sits on the G line.
pages.cleffy: Is that note on the G line?

pages.figure: Staff with treble clef. A note sits in the space above G.
pages.cleffy: What about this one?

corrections.cleffy: Look closely. The G line is where the curl wraps. This note is sitting in the space above it — not ON it.

pages.figure: Staff with treble clef. A note appears. Student hears a sound. Is it G or not?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. You'll see a note on the staff and hear it at the same time. Is the note on the G line — the one I wrap around?

pages.figure: Staff with treble clef. A note sits on the G line.
pages.cleffy: See the note. Find G on the piano. Play it.

pages.figure: Mix of identify-the-clef and find-G questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 12. Cleffy blending back into the treble clef with a little wave. A caption — That's me.
pages.cleffy: Now you know the secret. I'm the treble clef. I show up at the start of every piece written for the right hand. Tomorrow — lines and spaces. How to tell them apart. No names yet — just the shape. Patience pays off. I promise.

questions.figure: Four staves. Two have treble clefs, two have something else.
questions.prompt: Which staff has a treble clef?

questions.prompt: Is this note on the G line?

questions.figure: Staff with treble clef, note in a space.
questions.prompt: Is this note on the G line?

questions.audio: G4 played
questions.prompt: That sound is the note on the G line.
Tap G on the piano.
questions.accept: G4

remediation.title: The clef, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: The G-line thing can feel abstract. Let me show you — slowly — how the clef wraps, and how that one line becomes the anchor for everything.

completion.cleffy: You know me, officially. The treble clef. Now you'll see me on every piece of music for the rest of your life.

title: Lines and spaces
subtitle: Shape first, names later
goal: Tell at a glance whether a note is on a line or in a space — without naming it yet.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding two signs — one says LINE, the other says SPACE.
pages.cleffy: You've been waiting for me to tell you the names of all the notes. I'm going to make you wait one more lesson. Trust me on this. Today we lock in something more important than names — knowing at a glance if a note is on a line or in a space.

pages.figure: Two notes side by side on a staff. One on a line — the line cuts through the middle of the note. One in a space — the note sits between two lines, untouched.
pages.cleffy: Here's the thing. If you jump straight to names — FACE, EGBDF, all that — you end up counting every note from scratch. That's slow. That's how people get stuck. But if you can GLANCE at a note and instantly know if it's on a line or a space, you read way faster. Shape first. Names second. The Sonata method is built on this.

pages.figure: Zoomed in. A note on a line — clearly a line going through the middle of the oval.
pages.cleffy: Line notes have a line cutting through them. Right through the middle. Like a skewer.

pages.figure: Zoomed in. A note in a space — sitting like a tooth between two lines, not touching either.
pages.cleffy: Space notes sit between two lines. Nothing cuts through them. They're in the gap — like a tooth in a gum. Weird image but now you won't forget.

pages.figure: Staff with a single note in space 3.
pages.cleffy: Line or space?

pages.figure: Staff with a single note on line 4.
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Single note on staff each time.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Quick — line or space?

pages.figure: Staff with a note on line 2.
pages.cleffy: Now a harder one. If it IS a line, which line? Counting from the bottom.

corrections.cleffy: Count from the bottom line up. Bottom is 1.

pages.figure: Staff with a note in space 3.
pages.cleffy: Which space is that note in?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Each round: a note flashes on the staff for just 2 seconds. You pick.
pages.cleffy: Speed round. A note will appear for a moment. Line or space — quick. We're training your eyes to see it instantly.

pages.figure: Two notes on the staff — one higher, one lower. Sometimes same type (line+line), sometimes different (line+space).
pages.cleffy: Now two notes. Are they the same type, or different? Both lines, both spaces — same. One of each — different.

pages.figure: Two notes — both on lines, one line apart. Label: line → line.
pages.cleffy: Here's a preview of something you'll see a lot. Line to the next line. Both on lines. That shape is a skip — remember skips? Line to next line, or space to next space. That's the odd rule.

pages.figure: Two notes — line, then next space up. Label: line → space.
pages.cleffy: And this one — line to space. Space to line. That's a step. The even rule. We'll lock that in next week when we start Act 4. For now, just notice the shape exists.

pages.figure: Mix of line/space, count, and same/different questions.
pages.cleffy: Last warm-up. Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 13. A staff with two notes, one on a line (labeled line) and one in a space (labeled space). Cleffy giving a thumbs up.
pages.cleffy: Line or space. At a glance. Tomorrow we give notes names. Just one first — Middle C. If you know one note and you know shapes, you can find every other note. That's the whole method. You're almost reading music.

questions.figure: Staff, note on line.
questions.prompt: Line or space?

questions.figure: Staff, note in space.
questions.prompt: Line or space?

questions.figure: Staff, two line-notes one line apart.
questions.prompt: Are both notes the same type?

questions.figure: Staff, one line-note and one space-note.
questions.prompt: Are both notes the same type?
Looking at a staff (shown), which note is higher?

questions.figure: Staff with a note high up.
questions.prompt: Tap a high key on the piano to match this note.
questions.accept: any key in right half of keyboard

remediation.title: Lines and spaces, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Shape recognition is muscle memory. Normal to take a few passes. Let's go through it with bigger zoomed-in notes so the difference is obvious.

completion.cleffy: Shape before names. That's the Sonata method in one sentence. You just locked it in.

title: Middle C
subtitle: The one anchor that unlocks everything
goal: Recognize Middle C on the staff — and know it's the same Middle C you've been playing.
piece_context: This is the most important note in the course. Every other note gets named by how far it is from Middle C.

pages.figure: A staff with a small note hanging just below the bottom line, attached by a tiny extra line. Cleffy points at it with a proud smile.
pages.cleffy: Everything in this course — every note name, every piece you'll ever play — starts with this one. This is Middle C. Our anchor. Learn this one, and the rest fall into place.

pages.figure: Treble-clef staff. Middle C drawn just below the bottom line, sitting on its own little short line (the ledger line).
pages.cleffy: Middle C lives just below the treble staff. See that tiny little line cutting through it? That's a ledger line — a stretch of the staff for notes that don't fit on the main five lines. Middle C hangs below the staff, on its own.

pages.figure: Two staves — treble on top, bass on bottom. Middle C sits BETWEEN them, shared by both.
pages.cleffy: It's called Middle C for a reason. It sits in the middle of the two staves. You see it under the treble clef staff — just below the bottom line. Or above the bass clef staff — just above its top line. Same note. Written two ways, depending on which clef you're reading. For now — we only care about treble. So — just below the treble staff.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C on top. Keyboard below with Middle C (C4) glowing gold. An arrow connects them.
pages.cleffy: Here's the link that matters. This note on the staff — is THAT key on the piano. The same Middle C you've been tapping since Lesson 4. Nothing new on the piano. Just a new way to WRITE the note we already know.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C. Keyboard with Middle C glowing. Big play button.

audio.A: Middle C (C4) held

pages.cleffy: That's Middle C. You've heard it before. Now, when you see this exact shape on the page — a note below the staff with a small line through it — you'll hear that sound in your head. Eye sees it. Ear hears it. Finger plays it.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C drawn.
pages.cleffy: Point to Middle C.

pages.figure: Staff with three notes: one on the bottom line (E), one in space 1 (F), one below staff on ledger line (Middle C).
pages.cleffy: Which one is Middle C?

corrections.cleffy: Close — but that one's sitting on the very bottom line of the staff. Middle C is below the staff, on its own little line.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C. Keyboard shown. Middle C on the piano glows gold.
pages.cleffy: See the note. Find Middle C on the piano. Play it.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the whole loop. Written C — played C. You just read your first real note.

pages.figure: Three staves shown one at a time. On each, a note is drawn. Sometimes it's Middle C, sometimes it's a different note nearby.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Each time, a single note on a staff. Is it Middle C, yes or no?

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Middle C held
audio.B: D4 held (a step above)

pages.cleffy: Two notes. Which one was Middle C?

corrections.cleffy: Middle C has a specific sound. Listen to it a few more times — you'll memorize it.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C. Student sees, hears it, plays it.
pages.cleffy: Full loop. See Middle C. Listen to it. Play it.

pages.figure: Same staff again.
pages.cleffy: One more time. Getting the muscle memory.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C and, next to it, D (the note in the space right above the ledger line).
pages.cleffy: Quick preview. Right above Middle C — in the space just below the bottom line of the staff — is the next white key. That's D. D is a step up from Middle C. Middle C on a line, D in the space right above. Line to space. That's a step. We'll officially meet D and the rest in a couple lessons. Just plant the seed.

pages.figure: Mix of staff reading and listening.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix it all up.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 14. Middle C on a staff with a little gold flag planted on it. Cleffy saluting.
pages.cleffy: One anchor note. One link between the page and the piano. Tomorrow you prove it — see Middle C, hear it, play it, over and over, fast. That's the end of Act 3. After that, we finally use the Sonata method. For real.

questions.figure: Staff with Middle C.
questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?

questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?

questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?

questions.figure: Staff with Middle C drawn.
questions.prompt: Play the note shown.

remediation.title: Middle C, locked in
remediation.opening_cleffy: Middle C is THE anchor for everything. Worth taking an extra minute on. Let's go through it one more time — see, hear, play, see, hear, play.

completion.cleffy: The anchor is in. Every future note gets measured from this one. That's how the Sonata method works.

title: Reading Middle C
subtitle: Act 3 · You just read music
goal: See Middle C, hear Middle C, play Middle C — without thinking. Fast and confident.
piece_context: Most people never learn to read sheet music. Today you do — one note at a time, starting here.

pages.figure: Cleffy standing between a trophy and a staff. The staff has Middle C on it.
pages.cleffy: End of Act 3. You met the staff. You met me, the treble clef. You learned line vs space. You met Middle C. Today you prove you can read that one note, fast and cold. This is a smaller checkpoint than Act 1's — but it's the most important one so far. You're about to read music. For real. Let's go.

pages.figure: Four small panels: the five-line staff, the treble clef, line-vs-space, Middle C on a ledger line.
pages.cleffy: Quick recap. L11 — the staff is stairs on paper. L12 — the treble clef is me, and the loop hugs the G line as a landmark. L13 — notes are either on a line or in a space, and shape matters more than names. L14 — Middle C is our anchor. Below the staff, on its own little line. Use all of it today.

pages.figure: Staff. Middle C flashes briefly, then disappears.
pages.cleffy: Recognize Middle C in under two seconds.

pages.figure: Staff. Each round shows a note — sometimes Middle C, sometimes a neighbor.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Is this Middle C? Yes or no. Quick.

pages.figure: Play button. No staff shown.

audio.A: Either Middle C or another note

pages.cleffy: Now with your ears only. I'll play a single note. Is it Middle C?

pages.figure: Full keyboard, no highlights.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Find Middle C on the piano.

pages.figure: Staff with Middle C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: See. Hear. Play. Three rounds. Middle C on the staff — middle C on the piano — same note.

pages.figure: Staff with four Middle Cs in a row. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: The staff shows Middle C, four times in a row. Play it four times.
pages.followup_cleffy: Same note. Same key. Same sound. You just READ a mini-piece. Boring as it is, it's a real read.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A = Middle C. B = a note one step above (D4).
pages.cleffy: Which one is Middle C — the first or the second?

pages.figure: Staff. Middle C appears. Keyboard below. You have 3 seconds to play it.
pages.cleffy: Speed round. When Middle C appears on the staff, play it fast. Three rounds.

pages.figure: A short ledger-line-only staff with several Middle Cs in rhythm: one long note, then two shorter, then a longer one.
pages.cleffy: Your first tiny mini-piece. Three notes, all Middle C, slightly different lengths. Follow the dots. Don't stress timing — just hit Middle C each time a dot lights up.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mixed questions.
pages.cleffy: One last warm-up. Four rounds, mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Almost done with Act 3.

pages.figure: Celebration card — Act 3 complete. Trophy with 3/10 acts engraved. Cleffy holding up a pristine sheet of paper with a Middle C on it.
pages.cleffy: That's Act 3. You just read music. I know it's one note. I know it's going slow. But this was the whole point — building one rock-solid anchor. Starting tomorrow, we use it. We add steps, skips, and leaps — and every one of them is measured from Middle C. The Sonata method officially begins in Act 4. See you there.

remediation.title: Act 3, one more look
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act 3 is the one where sheet music clicks. It's normal if it takes two passes. Let's walk through everything again — staff, clef, line-space, Middle C — slowly, all connected.

completion.cleffy: Act 3, closed. You read music. A small amount of music — but real music, on a real staff, with a real anchor. Next up is the Sonata method, for real. Step, skip, leap — all measured from where you are right now.

title: Steps
subtitle: The smallest move in music
goal: Recognize a step on the staff at a glance — a line to the next space, or a space to the next line.
piece_context: Steps are the most common move in music. Happy Birthday is mostly steps. Für Elise is mostly steps. Moonlight is mostly steps. Own them.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up a small sign: THE METHOD BEGINS.
pages.cleffy: You're fifteen lessons in. You have the keyboard. You have the staff. You have Middle C. Now I show you the thing that makes the whole Sonata method work. It starts with the smallest possible move. A step.

pages.figure: Staff with two notes side by side. The first is on line 1. The second is in space 1. A little arrow connects them labeled STEP.
pages.cleffy: A step is a note moving to its neighbor. Line to the very next space. Or space to the very next line. That's it. No gaps. Neighbors only. On the staircase you already know — a step is one stair.

pages.figure: Staff with two examples side by side. Left: line → space above (step up). Right: space → line below (step down).
pages.cleffy: Up or down doesn't matter. Line up to the next space. That's a step up. Space down to the next line. That's a step down. Both are steps. Smallest move possible.

pages.figure: Three pairs of notes. A: line to next space (step). B: line to next line (NOT a step, something bigger). C: line to two lines up (NOT a step, a much bigger move).
pages.cleffy: Let me show you what's NOT a step. A — line to next space. That's a step. B — line to next line. That jumped over a space in the middle. Bigger than a step. C — line to two lines up. Too far — that's a much bigger move. A step is always a single jump between one line and the space touching it. Anything bigger is not a step. We'll name the bigger ones over the next few lessons.

pages.figure: Staff. A pair of notes: line 2 → space 2 above.
pages.cleffy: Is this a step?

pages.figure: Staff. A pair of notes: line 1 → line 2 (skip).
pages.cleffy: What about these two?

corrections.cleffy: Look closely. Both notes are sitting ON lines. There's a space between them that neither note touches. That's a skip — line to line. A step always goes line-to-space or space-to-line.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. A pair of notes each round. Step, yes or no?
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Quick. Is it a step?

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: Middle C and D (a step). B: Middle C alone.

audio.A: C4 then D4 (step)
audio.B: C4 alone

pages.cleffy: Now with your ears. When a step happens on the piano, the two notes are right next to each other. Tiny move. Smooth. Barely a gap. Listen to the first one. Now the second one — just one note. A step is that tiny gap between two neighbor notes.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C-D (step). B: C-E (skip).

audio.A: C4 then D4
audio.B: C4 then E4

pages.cleffy: Which one is a step?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Step or skip?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Step or skip by ear.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing gold. Instruction: play C, then a step up from C.
pages.cleffy: Put your right hand thumb on Middle C. Now play C, then the next white key to the right. That's a step up.
pages.followup_cleffy: C to D. A step. On the stairs, you went up one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4 glowing. Instruction: play D, then step down.
pages.cleffy: Now a step DOWN. Play D, then the white key left of it — which is C.
pages.followup_cleffy: D to C. Step down. Same move, opposite direction.

pages.figure: Staff with two notes — line 1 and space 1 above it. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: First real reading moment. Those two notes on the staff are a step apart. See the step on the staff. Play a step on the piano. Anywhere you want.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just READ a step. Page to piano. No names, no counting. That's the method.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Each round: a pair of notes on staff. Read the shape, play a matching step.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Each one, see the shape — then play a step on the piano matching the direction.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones before the test.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 16, STEPS. A staircase with one small step highlighted. Cleffy giving a serious nod.
pages.cleffy: That's a step. The smallest move in music. The method has officially started. Tomorrow — the odd/even rule. This is the big idea. Steps are always line-to-space or space-to-line. That means they're EVEN. You'll see why that matters.

real_piano: Put your right hand thumb on Middle C. Play C-D-C-D-C-D, slowly, ten times. Just two notes, stepping.
Move your thumb to F. Play F-G-F-G, ten times. Same shape, different spot on the keyboard.
Do it with your left hand too, low on the keyboard. Just get comfortable with step-up, step-down.
virtual_only: Open lesson 16 and replay pages 11–14 (the step drills) a couple times

questions.prompt: Is this a step?

questions.prompt: Is this a step?

questions.figure: Staff. Space 2 → line 2 below (step down).
questions.prompt: Is this a step?

questions.prompt: Step or skip?

Play a step up, anywhere on the keyboard.
