remediation.title: Steps, again
remediation.opening_cleffy: Steps are the foundation of everything that comes next, so it's worth an extra lap. Let's see lots more examples, side by side.

completion.cleffy: Step nailed. Smallest move locked in. Tomorrow — why steps are always line-to-space or space-to-line. The odd/even rule that makes reading fast.

title: The even rule
subtitle: Steps always switch kinds
goal: Recognize a step by shape alone — if the kind switched (line↔space), it's a step.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a sign: ONE TRICK TO READ FAST.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday you met steps. Today I give you the trick that lets you spot a step at a glance — no counting, no thinking. Just look. It's called the even rule. This is half of the Sonata method's main pattern.

pages.figure: Staff with three examples of steps — line to space, space to line, line to space. Each one, an arrow points out that the KIND changes.
pages.cleffy: Here's the rule. If it's a step, the kind always switches. Line becomes space. Or space becomes line. Every single time. No exceptions.

pages.figure: A staff with every line labeled 'line' and every space labeled 'space', alternating. An arrow shows: line-space-line-space-line, etc.
pages.cleffy: Think about how a staff is built. Line, space, line, space, line, space, line, space, line. They alternate. So the closest neighbor to a line is always a space. And the closest neighbor to a space is always a line. Moving one stair — a step — always lands you on the opposite kind. That's all the rule is.

pages.figure: Three pairs of notes shown. Student applies the even rule: did the kind switch?
pages.cleffy: Let's use it. For each pair — did the kind switch? If yes — it's a step. If no — it's NOT a step. Something bigger.

pages.figure: Staff with a pair: line 2 to space 2 above.
pages.cleffy: Kinds switched — line to space. Step?

pages.figure: Staff with a pair: line 1 to line 2. Both lines.
pages.cleffy: Both lines. Did the kind switch?

corrections.cleffy: Look again. Both notes are on lines. Line → line means the kind did NOT switch. So — not a step. Something bigger.

pages.figure: Staff with pair: space 3 → line 3 below.
pages.cleffy: And this one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 8. Each round: a pair of notes. Student applies the even rule.
pages.cleffy: Eight rounds. Apply the rule — did the kind switch?

pages.figure: Same kind of drill, faster. Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six more, even quicker. Trust your eye.

pages.figure: Two playback cards. A: a step (C-D). B: a bigger move (C-E).

audio.A: C4 to D4
audio.B: C4 to E4

pages.cleffy: Listen. Step — tiny, smooth, barely a gap. Bigger move — you hear air between them. The rule holds by ear too: steps feel close. Bigger moves feel wider.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Step, or bigger?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Right hand in five-finger position C-D-E-F-G. Each adjacent pair highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Put your right hand on C D E F G. Play them in order — all steps, all switching kinds if you were reading them on a staff.
pages.followup_cleffy: Every pair you just played was a step. On a staff, they'd alternate line-space-line-space-line. The even rule in action.

pages.figure: Staff with four notes: line 1, space 1, line 2, space 2. All step-neighbors.
pages.cleffy: Four notes, all steps up — kind switches every time. Read the shape. Play four steps up on the piano, any starting note.

pages.figure: Mix of see / hear / play step recognition.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 17. A staff with a step-up pair highlighted and a label: kinds switched — step.
pages.cleffy: Step = kinds switch. That's the whole rule for today. There's another rule — for when kinds stay the same — and we meet it when we meet skips. For now, your eye already knows what a step looks like. That's a big deal.

real_piano: Right hand, five-finger position starting on Middle C. Play C-D-E-F-G, then reverse. Ten times.
Start on G instead — G-A-B-C-D. All steps, new starting point.
Try just two notes at a time — step up, step down. Focus on the feel of the tiny move.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8–9 (the speed drills) two or three times. Aim for instant recognition.

questions.figure: Line → next space
questions.prompt: Step, or not a step?

questions.figure: Line → next line
questions.prompt: Step, or not a step?

questions.prompt: Step, or not a step?

questions.prompt: Step or bigger?

Play four steps in a row, going up.

remediation.title: The even rule, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The switch-kinds thing takes a minute. Let's walk through a bunch of examples side by side — same kind and different kind — until your eye sees it fast.

completion.cleffy: Even rule: locked. Your eye reads steps by shape now. That's half the Sonata method.

title: Steps up, steps down
subtitle: Direction matters as much as size
goal: Read a step and know INSTANTLY if it goes up or down, without thinking about it.

pages.figure: Staff with two pairs. Left: step up (line → space above). Right: step down (line → space below).
pages.cleffy: You know a step is a move between a line and a neighbor space. Today we lock in direction. Same size. Opposite feelings. Up-step and down-step.

pages.figure: Large staff. An animation — dot steps up one spot, bounces back down. Arrow labels: UP / DOWN.
pages.cleffy: Step up — the note lifts. Higher on the page, higher in sound. Step down — the note drops. Lower on the page, lower in sound. Page direction matches piano direction. Always.

pages.figure: Staff with one pair: line 2 → space 2 above.
pages.cleffy: Here's a step. Is it going up or down?

pages.figure: Staff with one pair: space 3 → line 3 below.
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Each round: a step. Up or down?
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Quick. Up or down?

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: C4 to D4 (step up)
audio.B: D4 to C4 (step down)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Going up tends to lift — the feeling rises. Going down tends to settle — it relaxes. You'll feel it differently in different pieces. But the direction always has a flavor.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds by ear. Step up or step down?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a step up from Middle C. Thumb on C, pointer on D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Now a step down from E. Middle finger on E, pointer on D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, D, C, D glowing as a pattern.
pages.cleffy: Step up, step down, step up, step down. C to D, D to C, C to D, D to C. Back and forth. Walking up and down one stair.
pages.followup_cleffy: Feel the little wobble? That's the smallest thing music can do. You just did it.

pages.figure: Staff with a pair of step-apart notes. Student plays on the keyboard in the matching direction.
pages.cleffy: See the step. Read the direction. Play a step on the piano going the same way.

pages.figure: Staff with four notes in a little melody: up, up, down, down. All steps.
pages.cleffy: Now a tiny four-note melody. All steps. Up. Up. Down. Down. Read the shape. Play it, anywhere on the piano.
pages.followup_cleffy: You just read a melody. Four notes of shape. No names needed.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Each round: a new four-note step melody.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Different shapes each time. Read and play.

pages.figure: Mix of direction and size drills.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix of listening and looking.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 18. A little staff with four-note melody drawn, up/down arrows under each move.
pages.cleffy: Size plus direction. You can read steps in both directions now. Tomorrow — drill day. No new ideas, just faster and faster recognition. The day after, you play Hot Cross Buns. But not by ear this time. From the page.

real_piano: Right hand on C-D-E-F-G. Play C-D, then back D-C. Do that ten times. Up-down, up-down.
Next, up-up-down-down: C-D-E-D-C. Ten times. Tiny step-melody.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-13 (the direction drills and the four-note melodies)

questions.figure: Step going up.
questions.prompt: Up or down?

questions.figure: Step going down.
questions.prompt: Up or down?

questions.prompt: Up or down?

questions.prompt: Step up or step down?

questions.audio: F4 to E4
questions.prompt: Step up or step down?

questions.figure: Staff with step going down.
questions.prompt: Play a step in the same direction (down).
questions.accept: two adjacent keys descending

remediation.title: Direction drill
remediation.opening_cleffy: Direction trips some people up the first time — it feels obvious once it clicks. Just more reps at slightly slower speed.

completion.cleffy: Up-step and down-step are both second nature now. Almost at the end of Act 4.

title: Steps drill
subtitle: Faster, tighter, yours
goal: Drill step recognition until it's reflex — SEE, HEAR, and PLAY, all at speed.
piece_context: Tomorrow you read a full song. Today we make sure your step-reading is fast enough to keep up.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a blank staff.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday we locked in steps. Today — no new rules. Just reps. By the end of this lesson, reading a step should take you no longer than recognizing a face. Let's go.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 8. Each round: a pair of notes. Step or not? Very short time limit.
pages.cleffy: Eight rounds. Fast. Step, or not a step?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six more. All of these ARE steps. Up or down?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six by ear. Step or something bigger?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six more by ear. All steps. Up or down?

pages.figure: Keyboard. A prompt appears: 'Step up' or 'Step down'. You play it, any two keys.
pages.cleffy: I'll call a direction. You play a step that way. Anywhere on the keyboard.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Start from any note. Play five consecutive steps up (5 notes total).
pages.cleffy: Five notes going up. Start wherever. Just walk up the stairs, one step at a time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Now five notes going down.
pages.cleffy: And five going down.

pages.figure: Staff with a 5-note step melody: up, up, down, up, down.
pages.cleffy: Read this shape. Play it on the piano, any starting note.

pages.figure: Staff with a different 5-note step melody: down, up, up, down, down.
pages.cleffy: New shape. Read, play.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones before mastery.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 19. A tiny trophy with the number 19 and a glowing step-up pair on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Fast and clean. That's step-reading, basically locked. Tomorrow — the end of Act 4. You read Hot Cross Buns from the page. Not by ear. From the page. That closes a beautiful loop with Lesson 10, and Tier 1 has two more lessons after that.

real_piano: Right hand, five-finger position. Play it ascending, C-D-E-F-G, then descending G-F-E-D-C. Five times.
Try each finger on each step. Get your muscle memory tight.
virtual_only: Replay this lesson's drills, focusing on the speed rounds

questions.prompt: Step or not?

questions.figure: Line to line (skip)
questions.prompt: Step or not?

questions.figure: Step down
questions.prompt: Up or down?

questions.audio: Step
questions.prompt: Step or bigger?

questions.audio: Step down
questions.prompt: Up or down?
Play five steps going up, starting from any note.
questions.accept: 5 adjacent white keys ascending

remediation.title: More reps
remediation.opening_cleffy: Drill lessons are the place to grind. If one mode (seeing, hearing, or playing) felt slow, that's the one we focus on.

completion.cleffy: Nineteen lessons. Your step recognition is basically reflex now. That's going to show up in every piece you play for the rest of your life.

title: Hot Cross Buns, from the page
subtitle: Act 4 · The same song — but you read it
goal: Read Hot Cross Buns off the staff and play it. The Sonata method, completed for the first time.
piece_context: Ten lessons ago, you played Hot Cross Buns by ear. Today you read it off a page. Same song, completely different skill.

piece.title: Hot Cross Buns
piece.notes: E D C | E D C | C C C C | D D D D | E D C
piece.difficulty: beginner — read it using steps

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up two pages side by side. Left says LESSON 10 — BY EAR. Right says LESSON 20 — BY READING.
pages.cleffy: Remember this song? Ten lessons ago, you played Hot Cross Buns by listening to me and copying. Today, you read it off a staff. Same song. Different skill. This one matters a lot more.

pages.figure: Clean, simple staff with treble clef. The first line of Hot Cross Buns written: three notes — E on line 1, D in the space below it, Middle C below the staff. Each note's name NOT shown.
pages.cleffy: Here's Hot Cross Buns, written on a staff. Don't panic. Look at the shape. Three notes. Each one is one step lower than the last. Down, down, down.

pages.figure: Same staff, with Middle C highlighted. Arrow pointing to it.
pages.cleffy: The last note is on its own ledger line, below the staff. You know that one. That's Middle C. From there, work UP — one step up is D. Another step up is E. Those are the three notes. E, D, C.

pages.figure: Staff with the first line: E D C. Each step labeled 'step down' with a small arrow.
pages.cleffy: See how each note is a step below the last? E to D is a step down. D to C is a step down. Three notes, stepping down the stairs.

pages.figure: Staff with E D C. Keyboard below. E, D, and C glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play the first line. Right hand — middle finger on E, pointer on D, thumb on C. Then play them. E, D, C.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hot. Cross. Buns. You didn't hear me play it first. You just READ it.

pages.figure: Staff with the second line: E D C again. Cleffy pointing at how it's identical to line 1.
pages.cleffy: Second line is identical. Same three notes, same shape. Hot Cross Buns, twice.

pages.figure: Staff showing first two lines. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play both lines. E D C, pause, E D C.

pages.figure: Staff. Line 3 shows: C C C C | D D D D. Four Cs followed by four Ds.
pages.cleffy: Middle line is new. Four Cs in a row, then four Ds. That's One-a-penny, two-a-penny. The first four notes — all on Middle C. Same note, four times. The next four — all one step up. D, D, D, D.

pages.figure: Same staff. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play the middle line. Four Cs, then four Ds.

pages.figure: Staff with final line: E D C again.
pages.cleffy: Last line — same as the first. E D C. Hot Cross Buns.

pages.figure: Full song on staff. Keyboard below. Each key lights up just before you need to play it (training wheels).
pages.cleffy: Full song, slow. The key will glow before you need to play it. Read along with the page as you go.

pages.figure: Full song on staff. Keyboard. NO key glowing. You read the page and play.
pages.cleffy: Now the real test. No highlights. Just the staff and the piano. Read Hot Cross Buns off the page. Play it.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was you, reading sheet music. Playing a song from the page. Ten lessons ago you couldn't do that. You did it from memory. This is different. This is reading.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones. Before your Act 4 celebration.

pages.figure: Celebration card — Act 4 complete. A small trophy engraved with 4/10 acts. A staff with Hot Cross Buns drawn on it. Cleffy grinning.
pages.cleffy: That's Act 4. The Sonata method, in use. For real. You read notes off a staff, turned them into steps, and played a song. Tomorrow — you already did skips. Next up: rhythm. Then Happy Birthday, five lessons away. The finish line of Tier 1 is visible from here. See you.

real_piano: Play Hot Cross Buns from memory. Twice slow, twice normal speed.
Try playing it starting on G instead of E — so G F E, G F E, E E E E, F F F F, G F E.
If you have paper: draw the first line of Hot Cross Buns as three notes on a staff.
virtual_only: Replay page 12 (the full song, no guides) three times this week

questions.figure: Staff showing E D C (first line of Hot Cross Buns).
questions.prompt: What direction does this line go?

questions.figure: Staff showing C C C C (four repeated notes).
questions.prompt: Are these notes moving, or repeating?

questions.audio: First line of Hot Cross Buns
questions.prompt: Did that go down in steps?

questions.figure: Staff with first line of Hot Cross Buns.
questions.prompt: Read the three notes and play them on the piano.
questions.accept: E4 D4 C4 in order

remediation.title: Reading Hot Cross Buns, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: The first time you read a whole song off the page, it's a lot. Let's do it bar by bar, very slow, each note with its step labeled.

completion.cleffy: Twenty lessons in. Act 4 sealed. You read a song off a page. You are officially doing the thing. Five more lessons and Tier 1 is done.

title: Skips
subtitle: Hopping over a stair
goal: Recognize a skip on the staff — line to line, or space to space.
piece_context: Happy Birthday is full of skips. So is Für Elise. Most melodies are half steps, half skips. Today you meet the other half.

pages.figure: Cleffy on a staircase, hopping OVER one step.
pages.cleffy: You know steps. Smallest move — one stair. Today we meet the next one. A skip hops over a single stair. Slightly bigger, still close.

pages.figure: Staff with two notes. First on line 1. Second on line 2. A little arrow labeled SKIP. In the space between them (space 1), a ghost note drawn that got 'skipped over'.
pages.cleffy: A skip is line to the next line. Or space to the next space. The kind STAYS the same — line stays line, space stays space. In the middle, there's one spot that got hopped over. That's a skip.

pages.figure: A chart. Step = switch kinds (even). Skip = same kind (odd).
pages.cleffy: Remember Lesson 17? Steps switch kinds. Skips are the opposite. They stay on the same kind. Line to line — both lines, same kind. Skip. Space to space — both spaces, same kind. Also a skip. That's the odd half of the odd/even rule.

pages.figure: Two examples. Left: line 1 → line 2 (skip up). Right: space 3 → space 2 below (skip down).
pages.cleffy: Skips go up or down, same as steps. Line up to next line. Space down to next space. Just a slightly bigger hop than a step.

pages.figure: Three pairs. A: line → next space (step, NOT skip). B: line → next line (skip). C: line → line 3 spots up (leap, NOT skip).
pages.cleffy: What's NOT a skip? A — line to next space. Kinds switched — that's a step. C — line to line with a whole line in between. Too far — that's a leap. B — line to next line. That's our skip. One line gap.

pages.figure: Staff with pair: space 2 → space 3 above.
pages.cleffy: Skip or not?

pages.figure: Staff with pair: line 2 → space 2 above (step).
pages.cleffy: And this?

corrections.cleffy: Look — one's on a line and the other's on a space. Kinds switched. That's a step. Skips stay line-line or space-space.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Step, skip, or something else?
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Step, skip, or bigger?

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C to D (step). B: C to E (skip).

audio.A: C4 to D4
audio.B: C4 to E4

pages.cleffy: Here's a step and a skip, side by side. Hear it? The skip has a little more air in it. A small hop feeling. The step is so close they almost blur.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Step or skip by ear?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds by ear.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing. The next white key after D (which is E) also glowing. D in between is dimmed.
pages.cleffy: Play a skip up from Middle C. Skip means hop over one white key. Thumb on C, then middle finger on E — you're skipping over D.
pages.followup_cleffy: C to E. A skip. Hopped over D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 glowing. C4 glowing. D between dimmed.
pages.cleffy: Skip down. From E, hop back to C.

pages.figure: Staff with a pair — line 1 to line 2. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Read the shape. That's a skip up. Play a skip up on the piano.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Each round: a skip on the staff, student plays matching skip.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. See the skip on the staff, play one on the piano.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 21. A staircase with a dot hopping over one stair. Step and skip both labeled on a staff below.
pages.cleffy: Step switches kinds. Skip keeps kinds. That's the Sonata method's first main rule, locked in. Tomorrow — mix them. Real melodies use both. Three lessons after that — Happy Birthday.

real_piano: Right hand, thumb on C. Play C-E, then E-C. That's skip up, skip down. Ten times.
Move to F. Play F-A, A-F. Same skip move, new location.
Try step-skip-step: C-D-F. Step up, then skip up. Slow.
virtual_only: Replay pages 13-14 (the skip drills) a couple times

questions.figure: Line 1 to line 2
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.figure: Line 2 to space 2
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.figure: Space 1 to space 2
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.audio: C4 to E4
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.audio: F4 to G4
questions.prompt: Step or skip?
Play a skip up from any note.
questions.accept: two white keys with one skipped in between

remediation.title: Skips, one more pass
remediation.opening_cleffy: Skip vs step is the biggest mix-up point in early reading. Let's walk through lots more side-by-side examples.

completion.cleffy: Step and skip, both in the bank. That's two of the three moves in music. Leap is coming in a couple weeks.

title: Steps and skips, together
subtitle: The first real reading
goal: Read a short melody made of steps AND skips without pausing to think.
piece_context: Every song is some blend of steps and skips. Today we mix them — and start reading the way a real musician reads.

pages.figure: Staff. A small melody — up step, up skip, down step, down step. Arrows above each pair labeling the move.
pages.cleffy: You know steps. You know skips. Music uses both, mixed together, all the time. Today we read melodies that have both — at speed.

pages.figure: Staff with 5 notes: C-D-F-E-D. Each move labeled underneath: step, skip, step, step.
pages.cleffy: Here's a 5-note melody. First note C. Then D — that's a step up. Then F — a skip up. Hopped over E. Then E — step down. Then D — step down. Every pair has a name. Your job is to SEE the shape instantly.

pages.figure: Staff with a pair. Student clicks step or skip. 4 rounds cycling through different pairs.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds, just pairs. Step or skip?

pages.figure: Staff with three notes: line 1, space 1, line 2. Move 1 = step, move 2 = step.
pages.cleffy: Three-note melody. Both moves are steps. See the shape? Line → space → line. Two steps up in a row.

pages.figure: Staff with three notes: line 1, line 2, space 2. Move 1 = skip, move 2 = step.
pages.cleffy: Different shape. Two moves — what are they?

pages.figure: Staff with a 4-note melody. Student clicks each pair's label in sequence.
pages.cleffy: Four notes, three moves. Tell me each one in order.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: A 4-note melody: step-skip-step

pages.cleffy: Close your eyes. Listen. That's step, then skip, then step. See if you can hear each one. We'll do this a lot — hearing intervals is half the skill.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Each round: hear a short melody, identify one move.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. I'll play two notes. Step or skip?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: play step, skip, step, starting from Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Step up, skip up, step up. Start on Middle C. Step to D. Skip to F. Step to G. C → D → F → G.
pages.followup_cleffy: Four notes. Three moves. Two steps, one skip, all going up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Reverse the sequence: G → F → D → C.
pages.cleffy: Now come back down. G → F → D → C. Step down, skip down, step down.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: C, D, F, E. Moves are: step up, skip up, step down.
pages.cleffy: Read the shape on the staff. Moves: step up, skip up, step down. Play it, starting on Middle C.

pages.figure: Same staff melody. Starting note NOT specified — pick your own.
pages.cleffy: Now play the same SHAPE, but start on any note. Step up, skip up, step down — just follow the shape, not the specific keys.
pages.followup_cleffy: Shape-based reading. That's the Sonata method in motion. You just transposed a melody without knowing the word transpose.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Each round: a new 4-note melody. Read, play.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Different melodies each time. Read and play.

pages.figure: Mix of see / hear / play prompts.
pages.cleffy: Last warm-up. Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 22. Staff with a little 5-note melody drawn, each move labeled S (step) or K (skip).
pages.cleffy: Real reading. Shape at a time, not note by note. Tomorrow — rhythm. How long each note lasts. After that — bars. Then Happy Birthday, and Tier 1 is done.

real_piano: Right hand in five-finger position C-D-E-F-G. Play C-E-D-F-E. Step? Skip? Try naming each move out loud.
Make up your own 4-note melody using only steps and skips. Play it three times.

questions.figure: Staff pair — step up
questions.prompt: Which move?

questions.figure: Staff pair — skip up
questions.prompt: Which move?

questions.figure: Staff 3-note melody: line, space, line
questions.prompt: What are the two moves?

questions.audio: Step up
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.audio: Skip down
questions.prompt: Step or skip?
Play step up, skip up, step up — starting from Middle C.
questions.accept: C4 D4 F4 G4

remediation.title: Steps and skips, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Mixing the two shapes is where it gets harder. Extra reps, each pair labeled slowly.

completion.cleffy: You just read a melody the way real pianists read — shape by shape. Three lessons left in Tier 1.

title: How long does a note last?
subtitle: Quarter, half, and whole notes
goal: Read three basic note durations from a staff — and clap the right rhythm.
piece_context: You've been reading pitches. Now we add time. Together they become a real song.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up three different notes drawn big: whole, half, quarter.
pages.cleffy: Up until now, every note you've read has been the same length. Real music isn't like that. Some notes are quick. Some hang around. Today we learn the three most common note lengths.

pages.figure: Three notes drawn in a row. Whole note (open oval, no stem). Half note (open oval with stem). Quarter note (filled oval with stem).
pages.cleffy: Three shapes to recognize. Whole note — empty oval, no stem. Lasts a long time. Half note — empty oval with a stem. Medium. Quarter note — FILLED oval with a stem. Quick.

pages.figure: Chart. Whole = 4 beats. Half = 2 beats. Quarter = 1 beat.
pages.cleffy: How long exactly? Quarter note = 1 beat. The basic pulse. Half note = 2 beats. Twice as long. Whole note = 4 beats in 4/4 time (which we meet tomorrow). Four times as long as a quarter. Think of a beat like a heartbeat — a steady pulse. The notes just say how many beats each sound gets.

pages.figure: The three notes. Arrows show that FILLED means SHORT, EMPTY means LONG, STEM means STEM (shorter than whole).
pages.cleffy: Quick memory trick. Looking at the shape: If it's FILLED IN — it's quick. One beat. If it's OPEN — it's longer. If it has NO stem at all — it's the longest. Four beats. Filled means fast. Empty means slow.

pages.figure: A single quarter note on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Which note is that?

pages.figure: A single whole note on a staff.
pages.cleffy: And this one?

pages.figure: A half note on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Last identify: this one.

pages.figure: A metronome ticks. Each tick — a quarter note fills in on the staff, one after another, in time with the clicks.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 bpm, 4 quarter notes

pages.cleffy: This is the quarter-note pulse. One note per beat. Tap along with your finger. Each click of the metronome is one beat. Each note lasts one click.

pages.figure: Metronome ticks. Half notes appear and LAST for 2 clicks each.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 bpm, 2 half notes (each lasting 2 clicks)

pages.cleffy: Now half notes. Each note holds through TWO clicks. Tap once. Keep that sound going in your head for the second click too.

pages.figure: Metronome ticks. A single whole note lasts for ALL 4 clicks.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 bpm, 1 whole note lasting 4 clicks

pages.cleffy: Whole note. One note, four beats. Tap and hold. This is the longest note we'll meet for a while.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Each round: a short rhythm pattern shown + clicked.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. I'll play a short rhythm — either all quarters, all halves, or one whole. Which length were the notes?

pages.figure: Staff. Middle C written four times as quarter notes. Metronome below.
pages.cleffy: Play Middle C, four times, once per beat. Follow the metronome. Quarters.

pages.figure: Staff. Middle C as: half, half, quarter, quarter. Metronome below.
pages.cleffy: Same note, different lengths. Two halves, then two quarters. Hold the halves for two beats each.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 23. The three note shapes lined up with their beat counts.
pages.cleffy: Filled = fast. Empty = slow. No stem = slowest. Tomorrow — bars. Groups of beats. Once you see bars, you can count. Then — Happy Birthday.

pages.figure: Preview of a staff with bar lines and a 4/4 time signature.
pages.cleffy: Sneak peek. Tomorrow this funny '4 over 4' at the start of the staff makes sense. It tells you how beats are grouped. See you then.

real_piano: Clap a steady pulse. One clap per second. Count 1-2-3-4, over and over.
Alternate: two quarter claps (one per second), then hold for two seconds (like a half). Repeat.
Hold for 4 full seconds without clapping. That's a whole note of silence + counting.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8-10 (the metronome exercises) two times

questions.figure: A filled quarter note

questions.figure: A half note
questions.prompt: How long is this note?

questions.figure: A whole note
questions.prompt: How long is this note?

questions.audio: Metronome with 4 quarter notes
questions.prompt: How many notes did you hear?

questions.figure: Staff with two halves and two quarters
questions.prompt: Play Middle C with the rhythm shown. Follow the metronome.

remediation.title: Rhythm, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rhythm is a whole new axis. It's normal to take a couple passes. Let's redo it with the metronome very slow, and lots of clapping.

completion.cleffy: Three durations in your pocket. Quarter, half, whole. Two more lessons in Tier 1.

title: Bars and 4/4 time
subtitle: Four beats per bar
goal: Read bar-lines and time signatures. Understand how beats group in 4/4.
piece_context: Every piece of music has a pulse — and that pulse is usually grouped into fours. Happy Birthday is in 3s — which we'll meet later. Most of your repertoire will be in 4s.

pages.figure: Staff with a few notes on it. A vertical line cuts through the staff between notes 4 and 5. Labeled BAR-LINE.
pages.cleffy: Look at that vertical line cutting through the staff. That's a bar-line. It divides music into chunks. Each chunk is called a bar. And bars are how you keep track of where you are in a piece.

pages.figure: Staff with three bar-lines, creating four bars. Each bar has 4 quarter notes.
pages.cleffy: Here's what it looks like. Bar-lines split the staff into sections. Each section — each bar — has the same number of beats inside it. Usually four. That's called 4/4 time, which we'll meet in a second.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: notes with NO bar-lines, looking chaotic. Right: the same notes with bar-lines, looking organized.
pages.cleffy: Without bar-lines, music is just a long line of dots. With them, you can say 'I'm in bar 3' or 'start from bar 8'. It's the grid that makes sheet music navigable.

pages.figure: Staff with a big 4 stacked over another big 4 right at the start — the time signature. Label: 4/4.
pages.cleffy: See this '4 over 4' at the start of the staff? That's a time signature. It tells you the rhythm rules for the whole piece. Top number: how many beats are in each bar. Bottom number: what kind of note gets one beat. 4/4 — four beats per bar, and the quarter note (that's the 4 on bottom) gets one beat.

pages.figure: One bar with four quarter notes in it. Arrow counting 1-2-3-4 beneath them.
pages.cleffy: Easiest 4/4 bar. Four quarter notes. Each note = one beat. Four notes fill up the bar exactly. Count: 1, 2, 3, 4. Then a bar-line. Then start over.

pages.figure: One bar with two half notes in it. Counting below.
pages.cleffy: Same bar, different notes. Two half notes. Each half note is 2 beats. Two halves = 4 beats = one full bar.

pages.figure: One bar with a single whole note in it.
pages.cleffy: One whole note. That IS a full bar. 4 beats, all on one note.

pages.figure: Staff showing one bar with: quarter, quarter, half.
pages.cleffy: How many beats is this bar? Add them up.

corrections.cleffy: Quarter = 1. Quarter = 1. Half = 2. 1 + 1 + 2 = 4. Full bar of 4/4.

pages.figure: Staff with one bar containing: half, quarter. Then a question mark for the last note.
pages.cleffy: One bar of 4/4. So far, 2 + 1 = 3 beats. What's missing?

pages.figure: Metronome. A bar of 4 quarter notes plays, then a pause equal to one bar, then another bar plays.

audio.A: Metronome 4 quarters, silent bar, 4 quarters

pages.cleffy: Listen to two bars of four quarter notes, separated by silence. Count 1-2-3-4 along with it. Let the pulse settle in.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Each round: a bar plays, student counts beats.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Each bar, count the notes. How many beats did it total?

pages.figure: Staff with one bar of 4/4. Four quarter notes on Middle C. Metronome.
pages.cleffy: Play Middle C, four times, in time with the metronome. One bar of 4/4.

pages.figure: Two bars of Middle C. Bar 1: four quarters. Bar 2: half, half.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Four quarters, then two halves. Both stay on Middle C. Keep the beat steady through the bar-line.

pages.figure: Staff with 2 bars. Melody and rhythm: C (half), D (half) | E (quarter), D (quarter), C (half).
pages.cleffy: First real melody WITH rhythm. Two bars. Bar 1: C, hold for 2 beats. D, hold for 2 beats. Bar 2: E quick, D quick, C hold for 2 beats. Read shape AND time. Both together.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 24. A staff with clean bar-lines and a 4/4 time signature. Cleffy pointing at bar 1.
pages.cleffy: Bars and beats. Now you can count your way through any piece. Tomorrow — you close Tier 1 with Happy Birthday. It's in 3/4 time — three beats per bar instead of four — but you already have the tools. See you at the finish line.

real_piano: With a metronome at 60 bpm, play Middle C four times. That's one bar of 4/4.
Next: play Middle C as a whole note. One note, hold through four clicks.
Mix: two halves, then four quarters, then one whole. Each pattern is one bar.

questions.figure: A bar with 2 half notes in 4/4
questions.prompt: Is this bar complete?

questions.figure: A bar with 3 quarter notes in 4/4
questions.prompt: Is this bar complete?

questions.audio: One bar of 4 quarter notes on Middle C
questions.prompt: How many beats did you count?

questions.figure: One bar of 4/4 with two halves on Middle C
questions.prompt: Play it in time with the metronome.
questions.accept: C4 + C4 at half-note durations

remediation.title: Bars, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Bars are the structural bones of music — worth an extra minute. Let's look at 4/4 with a giant, slow metronome and count each beat carefully.

completion.cleffy: Bars, counted. Time signatures, introduced. Tomorrow — Tier 1 ends with Happy Birthday, the right way.

title: Happy Birthday
subtitle: Tier 1 · The finish line
goal: Read and play Happy Birthday from a page. End of Tier 1. You are officially a piano-reading beginner.
piece_context: The first piece you can say you really PLAY. Not just tap the right keys — read it off a page, count the rhythm, use the right fingers. A whole performance, end to end.

piece.title: Happy Birthday to You
piece.difficulty: beginner — steps + skips, simple rhythms
piece.full_sequence: G G A G C B | G G A G D C | G G G(high) E C B A | F F E C D C

pages.figure: Cleffy at the top of a staircase. 24 stairs behind him. A small cake at the top.
pages.cleffy: Twenty-five lessons. You touched a piano for the first time at lesson 2. You read your first note at lesson 15. Today you read a whole song. A real one. One people recognize. Happy Birthday. Let's go.

pages.figure: A clean staff showing the full Happy Birthday melody — 4 lines of music.
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole song. Don't panic at the page. Four lines of music. Mostly steps and skips. A few repeated notes. That's it. We'll build it line by line.

pages.figure: Close-up of the time signature at the start: 3/4.
pages.cleffy: Quick note. This song is in 3/4, not 4/4. Three beats per bar, not four. Count 1-2-3, 1-2-3. Like a waltz. Everything else you know still works — notes, shapes, durations. Just three beats in each box.

pages.figure: Only the first line of Happy Birthday: G G A G C B.
pages.cleffy: Line 1. Happy birthday to you. G, G, A, G, C, B. First two notes are the same — repeated note. No move. Then up a step to A. Down a step back to G. Then a leap up to C. Down a step to B. Two repeats, step, step, leap, step. That's the whole first line.

pages.figure: Keyboard with G, A, B, C all glowing. Fingering numbers shown: 5 on G (pinky), 4 on A, 3 on B, 2 on C. (Right hand in G position.)
pages.cleffy: Right hand in G position. Pinky on G, ring finger on A, middle on B, pointer on C. Play the first line: G, G, A, G, C, B. Slow. Happy. Birthday. To. You.

pages.figure: Line 2: G G A G D C.
pages.cleffy: Line 2. Happy birthday to you. Almost the same. G, G, A, G — identical start to line 1. Then D (higher than C) and C (just below D). Leap up, step down.

pages.figure: Keyboard with G, A, C, D glowing. Right-hand position shifting slightly for D.
pages.cleffy: Play line 2. G, G, A, G, D, C. That last pair is higher than line 1.

pages.figure: Staff with both lines visible. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Both lines, back to back.

pages.figure: Line 3: G G G(high) E C B A. The third G is an octave higher than the first two.
pages.cleffy: Line 3. Happy birthday dear Sonata. G, G — same as before. Then the third G jumps up a whole octave. That's the famous leap in this song. From that high G, down a skip to E. Down a skip to C. Down a step to B. Down a step to A. Two low G's, a big jump up, then stepping down, down, down.

pages.figure: Keyboard with low G and high G both glowing, plus E, C, B, A. The octave leap is highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Play line 3. Watch out — the third G is a whole octave higher than the first two. Big jump up. Your hand will have to shift up for that high G, then stretch down from E to A on the way home. G, G, high G, E, C, B, A.

pages.figure: Line 4: F F E C D C.
pages.cleffy: Last line. Happy birthday to you. F, F — repeated. Down to E. Leap down to C. Up a step to D. Down a step to C. This is the resolution — ends on C, which is home.

pages.figure: Keyboard with C through F glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play line 4. F, F, E, C, D, C.

pages.figure: Full Happy Birthday on staff. Keyboard. Training wheels ON — keys glow before you play.
pages.cleffy: All four lines. Slow. Training wheels on — keys will glow. Breathe. Take your time.

pages.figure: Full song on staff. Keyboard below. NO glowing keys. Just you and the page.
pages.cleffy: Now the real thing. No highlights. Read it. Play it. Happy Birthday, by you.
pages.followup_cleffy: There. That was Happy Birthday, read off a page, played by you. Twenty-five lessons ago you didn't know what a staff was. Now you just read a song off one.

pages.figure: Full song. Metronome at 80 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now at a real tempo. No rush still — 80 beats per minute, waltz-paced. Play it through without stopping. Even if you stumble. Keep going to the end.

pages.figure: The staff with words under each note: Hap-py birth-day to you...
pages.cleffy: Bonus. Play it again, and sing along in your head. Hap-py birth-day to you. Hap-py birth-day to you. Hap-py birth-day dear so-and-so. Hap-py birth-day to you.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before the boss.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. This is the Tier 1 boss so it's a little bigger than the act tests.

pages.figure: A staircase with 25 stairs, each one labeled with the lesson number. Cleffy at the top with a small golden trophy.
pages.cleffy: Look back down the stairs. Twenty-five lessons. You learned to listen, to look at a piano, to find any note, to read a staff, to use the Sonata method, to count rhythm, and to play a real song. Most people who try to learn piano as adults never get here. You did.

pages.figure: A pyramid with Tier 1 at the base, colored gold. Tiers 2–6 visible but faded above. Tier 4 highlighted briefly — Moonlight 1st movement.
pages.cleffy: Here's what's ahead. Tier 2 brings your left hand in. You'll read both staves at once. Tier 4 — Moonlight 1st movement. That's life-changing. But you don't have to finish the whole course. Every tier is a real achievement. Where you stop is up to you.

pages.figure: Celebration card — TIER 1 COMPLETE 👑. A big golden trophy. Cleffy holding a cake with candles on it.
pages.cleffy: Tier 1. Complete. You play piano now. Not just 'I can tap some keys.' You actually read music and play it. Take a day off if you want. Then come back — Tier 2 starts with your left hand, and it's going to be fun. See you in Act 6.

real_piano: Play Happy Birthday through, once a day. Slow. From the page if possible.
Try playing it for a family member or friend. A real little performance.
If you want a challenge — try it starting on a different note. Pick F. Shape stays the same.
virtual_only: Replay the full song pages (13–15) a couple times

questions.figure: The first line of Happy Birthday on a staff
questions.prompt: Which move is between the 4th and 5th notes (G to C)?

questions.figure: A half note on a staff
questions.prompt: How long does it last?

questions.figure: A bar in 3/4 time
questions.prompt: How many beats per bar?

questions.figure: A pair of notes: line 1 to line 2
questions.prompt: Step or skip?

questions.audio: The first line of Happy Birthday
questions.prompt: Was the last move (C to B) a step up, step down, or something bigger?

questions.audio: A step and a skip played in order
questions.prompt: Step first, skip first, or two skips?
Play the first line of Happy Birthday (G G A G C B) from memory or the page.
questions.accept: G4 G4 A4 G4 C5 B4 in order

remediation.title: Happy Birthday, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: No shame. This was a big one. Let's take it apart and rebuild — two bars at a time, with every note labeled and every rhythm clapped. You'll get there.

completion.cleffy: Happy Birthday. Tier 1. Done. You're a piano reader now. You'll always be one. That's not something anyone can take from you. Big rest tonight. Tomorrow, Tier 2 opens — and your left hand starts earning its keep.

title: The bass clef
subtitle: A second staff for your left hand
goal: Meet the bass clef and understand it's the staff for lower notes — usually the left hand.

pages.figure: Cleffy next to two stacked staves. Top one has a treble clef (familiar). Bottom one has a new, heavier clef (unfamiliar).
pages.cleffy: Welcome to Tier 2. You finished Tier 1 with one staff, one clef, one hand. Today we add the other half of the piano. There's a second staff. It has a different clef. It's for your left hand.

pages.figure: Close-up of the bass clef symbol. Two small dots sit next to a curl that hugs one line.
pages.cleffy: This is the bass clef. Heavier shape than me. Different job. Where I (the treble clef) sit on top of music for higher notes — usually the right hand — the bass clef sits on the bottom, for lower notes — usually the left hand.

pages.figure: A piano keyboard. Middle highlighted. Arrows point LEFT half to a bass-clef staff and RIGHT half to a treble-clef staff.
pages.cleffy: The piano has so many notes that one staff can't hold them all. So we split it. Lower half of the piano goes on the bass staff. Upper half goes on the treble staff. Two staves. One piano. No overlap — except for one special note in the middle. We'll meet that soon.

pages.figure: Side-by-side: a treble staff and a bass staff. Both have five lines and four spaces. Same staff, different clef symbol.
pages.cleffy: Here's something friendly. The bass staff is the same shape as the treble staff. Five lines. Four spaces. Exactly what you've already learned. It's just the clef symbol that changes. Everything else about reading it is familiar.

pages.figure: Two mini-staves. One has a treble clef, one has a bass clef.
pages.cleffy: Which one is the bass clef — A or B?

pages.figure: Zoomed bass clef. The two dots highlighted in blue. Arrow labels: DOTS.
pages.cleffy: Notice the two dots. They're not decoration. They're a road sign. They point at a line. That line is important — it's the anchor line for the bass clef. We'll name it in the next lesson.

pages.figure: Bass clef on staff. The two dots flanking a line. Arrow pointing at that line.
pages.cleffy: The line between the dots is the one that matters. Remember that shape — dots hugging a line.

pages.figure: Bass clef staff with 3 lines highlighted. Student picks the line between the two dots.
pages.cleffy: Which line is between the two dots?

pages.figure: Keyboard. A low key around bass F highlighted. Big play button.

audio.A: F3 — bass F, one octave below Middle F

pages.cleffy: Here's the kind of note that lives on a bass staff. Deeper. More chest-rumble than ear-tingle. Your right hand rarely plays notes this low. Your left hand lives here.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: Middle C (treble range). B: an octave below Middle C (bass range).

audio.A: C4
audio.B: C3

pages.cleffy: Listen. Treble-range C. Bass-range C. Same letter. Different staff. Different feeling.

pages.figure: Play button. Two answer buttons.

audio.A: A random bass-range or treble-range note

pages.cleffy: Which staff would this note live on? Bass (low) or treble (high)?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Left half glowing blue, labeled LEFT HAND / BASS. Right half glowing gold, labeled RIGHT HAND / TREBLE.
pages.cleffy: Here's the practical split. Left half of the keyboard — left hand — bass staff. Right half — right hand — treble staff. Some pieces break this rule. But most follow it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A low F3 glowing. Student taps it.
pages.cleffy: Find the low F — left side of the piano, before a 3-group. Tap it with any left-hand finger.

pages.figure: Mix of bass/treble questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 26. Two staves side by side. Treble labeled 'right hand'. Bass labeled 'left hand.' Cleffy pointing at bass clef with a smile.
pages.cleffy: Second staff. Second clef. Left hand's turn. Tomorrow we find a landmark on the bass staff — the bass clef's version of C. Its name is bass F.

real_piano: With your LEFT hand, play a few keys on the LEFT side of the piano. Just get your bearings — notice how much deeper they sound.
Play a low F (before any 3-group on the left half) five times with your left thumb.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9–11 (the bass-range audio drills) a couple times

questions.figure: Two mini-staves, one treble one bass.
questions.prompt: Which clef is bass?

questions.figure: Bass clef staff, line between the two dots highlighted.
questions.prompt: Is this line the one the dots are hugging?

questions.audio: A low F3
questions.prompt: Would this note live on the bass staff or the treble staff?
Tap any low note on the left side of the keyboard with your left hand.
questions.accept: any key in left half

remediation.title: The bass clef, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: New clef is a big deal — worth a second look. Let's see the two clefs side by side with plenty of zoom.

completion.cleffy: Second clef, met. Tier 2 is off the ground. Tomorrow we find bass F — the landmark the two dots are pointing at.

title: Finding bass F
subtitle: The two dots are pointing at F
goal: Identify bass F on the staff and play it on the piano.

pages.figure: Bass clef close-up. The two dots pulsing around one line.
pages.cleffy: You saw the dots yesterday. Today we name the line they're pointing at. It's one you already know — from Lesson 6. That line is F.

pages.figure: Bass clef on staff. Line 4 (counting from bottom) highlighted blue. The two dots flank it. Label: F.
pages.cleffy: The line between the two dots of the bass clef is F. Specifically, the F just below Middle C — bass F. Not the same F as Middle F (which lives on the treble staff). A different F, one octave lower.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle F4 glowing blue. F3 (one octave below) glowing blue too. Arrow between them labeled ONE OCTAVE.
pages.cleffy: Remember octaves? Same letter, different floor. Middle F lives in the middle of the piano. Bass F lives one octave lower, on the left side. They're both F. The bass staff just points at the lower one.

pages.figure: Bass clef with two dots drawn in red, clearly hugging line 4. Below: text 'the dots POINT at F.'
pages.cleffy: Memory trick: the dots are pointing at F. One dot just above the F line. One dot just below. Both pointing IN. If you can remember that, you'll never lose bass F.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note on line 4 (bass F).
pages.cleffy: Is this note on the F line?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note in space 3 (one space above F).
pages.cleffy: And this one?

corrections.cleffy: Look — this note is sitting in a space above the F line, not on it. The F line is the one between the two dots.

pages.figure: Keyboard with F3 glowing. Bass staff with note on F line.

audio.A: F3 held

pages.cleffy: That's the sound of bass F. Deep. Full. Memorize this tone. It'll be your anchor on the bass staff.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Random — either F3 or a neighbor

pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Is that bass F?

pages.figure: Keyboard. F3 glowing. Reminder: the white key just before a 3-group on the left half.
pages.cleffy: Play bass F. Same trick as before — right before a 3-group. Just lower on the piano than Middle F.

pages.figure: Full keyboard, no highlights.
pages.cleffy: Now play ANY F, but on the LEFT side of the piano. Both F3 and F2 count. You pick.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Treble with Middle C highlighted gold. Bass with bass F highlighted blue.
pages.cleffy: You now have two anchors. Middle C — the flag in the middle, for the treble staff. Bass F — the flag on the bass staff, between the two dots. Every other note, on either staff, gets measured from these two.

pages.figure: Bass staff with note on F line. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: See bass F on the staff. Listen. Play it on the piano.

pages.figure: Same again.
pages.cleffy: One more time. Lock in the link.

pages.figure: Mix of F vs other.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mix.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 27. Bass clef with a little blue flag planted on the F line.
pages.cleffy: Two anchors: Middle C on the treble staff, bass F on the bass staff. Tomorrow your left hand gets fingers. After that, we add a second landmark and get to work.

real_piano: Left hand, thumb on bass F. Play it five times, slow.
Move your thumb to other Fs on the left side of the piano. Play each one a few times. Notice how they all sound like F, just different depths.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9-10 (play bass F) a couple times

questions.figure: Bass clef with note on line 4 (F).
questions.prompt: Is this bass F?

questions.figure: Bass clef with note on line 2 (not F).
questions.prompt: Is this bass F?

questions.audio: Bass F (F3)
questions.prompt: Is that bass F?
Play bass F on the piano.
questions.accept: F3

remediation.title: Bass F, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: Bass F is the anchor for half the music you'll ever read. Let's nail it down — slower, with the two dots clearly pointing.

completion.cleffy: Two landmarks, two anchors. Now both staves make sense. Left hand's turn tomorrow.

title: Your left hand, by the numbers
subtitle: Same fingers, flipped position
goal: Play comfortably with a left-hand five-finger position. Know which number is which finger.
piece_context: From here on, your left hand does real work. Time to get organized over there too.

pages.figure: Left hand floating beside Cleffy, fingers wiggling. A big label: LH.
pages.cleffy: You've been using your right hand for twenty-seven lessons. Today the left hand stops watching and starts playing. Good news — the numbering is exactly the same.

pages.figure: Both hands shown palm-out, side by side. Thumbs = 1. Pinkies = 5. Mirrored.
pages.cleffy: Left hand uses 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Thumb is 1. Pointer 2. Middle 3. Ring 4. Pinky 5. Same numbers as the right hand. Both thumbs are 1.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Right hand in C-D-E-F-G position (thumb on C, pinky on G). Left hand in bass — on C3-D3-E3-F3-G3 — pinky on low C, thumb on G3.
pages.cleffy: Here's the catch — where your hands sit is mirrored. Right hand going up — thumb is on the LEFT of the hand, pinky on the right. Left hand going up — pinky is on the LEFT, thumb on the RIGHT. The numbers stay the same. The hand flips.

pages.figure: Close-up of both hands resting on the keyboard. Arrows show thumb positions — right thumb left, left thumb right. They ALMOST meet in the middle.
pages.cleffy: Think about it this way. Thumbs are closer to your body. So when both hands sit on the piano, both thumbs point toward the middle. That's why left-hand 1 is the high-ish note, not the low one.

pages.figure: Left hand diagram. Finger 3 (middle) circled.
pages.cleffy: Which finger?

pages.figure: Left hand diagram. Finger 5 (pinky) circled.
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Left hand diagram. Finger 1 (thumb) circled.
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Low G3 glowing. A blue 1 above it.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, finger 1. Thumb. Tap low G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Low C3 glowing. A blue 5 above it.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, finger 5. Pinky. Tap low C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3, D3, E3, F3, G3 all glowing. Labels above: 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 (LH fingers).
pages.cleffy: Now the whole left-hand five-finger position. Pinky on low C (finger 5). Ring on D. Middle on E. Pointer on F. Thumb on G. Your hand should feel relaxed — no stretch, no squeeze.

pages.figure: Left hand sitting on C3-G3. Cleffy calls out a number.
pages.cleffy: Keep your hand where it is. I call a number, you tap that key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing gold (RH thumb area), G3 glowing blue (LH thumb area). Arrow between them.
pages.cleffy: Try both thumbs at once. Right-hand thumb on Middle C. Left-hand thumb on G3. Tap them together.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's both hands making sound at the same time. Tiny moment. Big deal.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH five-finger position. Arrows show LH walking UP: finger 5, 4, 3, 2, 1.
pages.cleffy: Left hand walking up. Pinky, ring, middle, pointer, thumb. C3, D3, E3, F3, G3.

pages.figure: Mix of finger-ID and play questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 28. Both hands on the keyboard, thumbs pointing inward, fingers numbered.
pages.cleffy: Left hand is organized now. Fingers numbered, position set. Tomorrow — one more bass-clef landmark. Then we read.

real_piano: Left hand, five-finger position on C3-D3-E3-F3-G3. Play ascending (pinky to thumb), then descending. Ten times.
Practice this: RH thumb on Middle C, LH thumb on G3. Tap both hands together. Simple, but it's the first real two-hand move.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10-13 (the LH five-finger position drills)

questions.figure: Left hand, finger 2 circled.
questions.prompt: Which number?

questions.figure: Left hand, finger 4 circled.
questions.prompt: Which number?
When the left hand is in the C-G position, which finger is on low C?
Put your left hand on C3-G3. Tap finger 3.
questions.accept: E3

remediation.title: Left hand, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The flipped-hand thing can feel weird the first time. Let's do it again with both hands mirrored on screen.

completion.cleffy: Both hands have numbers now. Both have fingers. Both know where they live. Ready for the grand staff.

title: Bass F and bass C
subtitle: Two landmarks on the bass staff
goal: Find bass F and bass C on the staff and on the piano. Two anchors for LH reading.

pages.figure: Bass staff with bass F labeled (line 4, between the dots). Question mark where bass C will appear.
pages.cleffy: You know bass F. That's one landmark. Today a second. Same strategy as the treble clef — two flags, then the rest of the notes fall into place. Meet bass C.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Bass F highlighted on line 4. Bass C highlighted in space 2 (four spots below F).
pages.cleffy: Bass C lives in the second space of the bass staff. From bass F, count down — line, space, line, space. You land on C. Different C than Middle C. Two octaves lower, actually.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing gold. One octave below Middle C (C3) glowing blue with label BASS C.
pages.cleffy: On the piano, bass C sits one octave below Middle C. Left-hand pinky, when you're in the C3-G3 position — that's bass C. You already played it in Lesson 28.

pages.figure: Bass staff with both landmarks highlighted: bass F (line 4, blue) and bass C (space 2, gold).
pages.cleffy: Two flags on the bass staff now. Bass F up high, bass C down low. Four spots apart on the staff — a familiar shape from the treble side.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note on line 4 (F).
pages.cleffy: Bass F or bass C?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note in space 2 (C).
pages.cleffy: And this one?

pages.figure: Two play buttons. Keyboard shows bass C and bass F glowing.

audio.A: C3 (bass C)
audio.B: F3 (bass F)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Bass C. Then bass F. Deeper than Middle C. Deeper than Middle F. Your left hand's home range.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Audio, student picks.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Bass C or bass F?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bass C (C3) glowing. LH pinky labeled 5.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, pinky on bass C. Tap it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bass F (F3) glowing. LH pointer (2) labeled.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, pointer on bass F. Tap it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Both bass C and bass F glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play bass C, then bass F. Pinky then pointer. Feel the distance.
pages.followup_cleffy: C to F — the same size of move as on the treble side. Bigger than a step, bigger than a skip. We'll formally name it in Tier 2, Act 8.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a single note. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. See a note on the bass staff, hear it, play it. All of them will be bass C or bass F.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Bass C (space 2, bass staff) highlighted. Middle C (ledger line below treble) highlighted. Arrow connecting them labeled ONE OCTAVE.
pages.cleffy: Quick preview. Middle C, which lives below the treble staff, is one octave above bass C. Same letter — C. Different floor. There's a third C between them too. But those live at different spots on different staves. We'll formalize the relationship in Lesson 34.

pages.figure: Mix of bass C / bass F by sight and sound.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 29. Bass staff with two flags planted — bass F on line 4 and bass C in space 2. Cleffy giving a thumbs up.
pages.cleffy: Two bass-staff landmarks. Two anchors. Tomorrow — steps in the left hand. Same rules as the right.

real_piano: Left hand, pinky on bass C. Play bass C five times.
Then pointer on bass F. Play bass F five times.
Alternate them — pinky C, pointer F — five times each.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-12 (the play bass C to bass F exercise)

questions.figure: Bass staff with note in space 2 (bass C).

Play bass C on the piano.

remediation.title: Two bass landmarks, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two landmarks to keep straight. Slow look at each, side by side.

completion.cleffy: Two bass anchors. Ready to read. Tomorrow: steps in the left hand — act boss.

title: Steps in the left hand
subtitle: Act 6 · The method, now in the bass
goal: Read and play steps on the bass staff with your left hand. Prove the Sonata method works everywhere.
piece_context: Everything you learned about steps in Tier 1 — on the treble staff, with your right hand — works exactly the same on the bass staff with your left.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up two flags — one gold (treble side), one blue (bass side). Between them, a glowing sign: STEP.
pages.cleffy: You made it to the end of Act 6. Today we prove something cool. Everything you learned about steps in Tier 1 — line to space, space to line, kinds switch — it all works in the bass clef too. Same rule. New staff. Left hand this time.

pages.figure: Bass staff showing three step examples: line to space above, space to line below, line to space. Each labeled STEP.
pages.cleffy: Remember the even rule? Steps switch kinds. Same on the bass staff. Line to next space, or space to next line. If you see two notes and one's on a line and the next is touching a space — that's a step.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: treble staff with a step pair (line 2 → space 2). Right: bass staff with a step pair (line 3 → space 3). Both look identical.
pages.cleffy: Look at these two pairs side by side. One's treble. One's bass. The shape is the same. Step up on a treble staff looks exactly like a step up on a bass staff.

pages.figure: Bass staff with line 2 → space 2 above.
pages.cleffy: Step or not, on the bass staff?

pages.figure: Bass staff with line 3 → line 4.
pages.cleffy: And this?

corrections.cleffy: Both on lines — kind didn't switch. Not a step. Same reasoning as on the treble staff.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Bass-staff pairs. Step or not?
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. All bass-staff pairs. Step or not a step?

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH five-finger position. A step in the bass range played.

audio.A: C3 to D3 (step up in bass)

pages.cleffy: Here's a bass-range step. Sounds just like a treble step — close, smooth — but deeper. The relationship is the same. The pitch zone is just lower.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds by ear. All in the bass range. Step or bigger?

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH in C3-G3 position. Pinky (5) on C3, ring (4) on D3. Both glowing.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, step up from bass C. Pinky on C3. Then ring on D3.
pages.followup_cleffy: Step up. Pinky → ring. C3 → D3. Same move as right-hand thumb → pointer — just flipped direction.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E3 glowing, then D3. LH middle (3) to ring (4).
pages.cleffy: Step down in the left hand. Middle finger on E3, then ring on D3.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a step pair (space 2 to line 3). Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: See the step on the bass staff. Play a step up in the LH five-finger position.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 5 ascending step-notes starting from bass C.
pages.cleffy: Five notes all stepping up on the bass staff. Play them with the left hand — pinky, ring, middle, pointer, thumb.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 5 descending step-notes from G3 to C3.
pages.cleffy: And back down. Thumb, pointer, middle, ring, pinky.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 6 mastery incoming.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Tier 1 pyramid next to Tier 2 pyramid. Tier 1 with a gold trophy. Tier 2 with Act 6 highlighted in progress.
pages.cleffy: Six acts in. You know both staves, both clefs, both hands. Steps work on both. The method is the same everywhere. That's a big idea. That's why we call it a method.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 6 complete. Small trophy engraved 6/10. Cleffy saluting.
pages.cleffy: Act 6 closed. Tomorrow — LH skips, then LH rhythm, then the grand staff brings the two hands together. Merrily We Roll Along is five lessons away. Your first piece with both hands.

real_piano: Left hand, five-finger position C3-G3. Play C3-D3-E3-F3-G3, then back down. Five times slow.
Isolate just steps: play C3-D3, then E3-D3, then D3-E3. Each pair is a step. Ten total.
Right hand warm-up: do the same five-finger walk on C4-G4. Both hands should feel equally confident.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-13 (the LH step reading drills)

remediation.title: Left-hand steps, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Left hand takes a minute to get going. Let's re-do steps with LH slowly.

completion.cleffy: Act 6: sealed. The method is now running in both hands. Six acts behind you. Four tiers ahead. Keep going.

title: Skips in the left hand
subtitle: Same rule, new staff
goal: Read and play skips on the bass staff with your left hand.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a skip pair — line 2 to line 3.
pages.cleffy: Steps work in the bass staff. So do skips. Same rule. Same shape. Just the left hand this time.

pages.figure: Chart. Step = kinds switch. Skip = kinds stay the same.
pages.cleffy: Remember — skips keep kinds. Line to line. Or space to space. One spot got hopped over in the middle.

pages.figure: Bass staff with three skip pairs: line→line up, space→space up, line→line down.
pages.cleffy: Three bass-staff skips. Same shape whether they go up or down. Both on lines, or both in spaces.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a pair: line 2 → line 3.
pages.cleffy: Skip or not?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a pair: line 2 → space 2.
pages.cleffy: And this?

corrections.cleffy: Line to space. Kind switched. That's a step, not a skip.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Step, skip, or bigger?
pages.cleffy: Six rounds on the bass staff. Step, skip, or bigger?

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3 to E3 played.

audio.A: C3 to E3 (bass skip)

pages.cleffy: Bass-range skip. Two left-hand fingers apart — pinky to middle. A small hop.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Step or skip in the bass range?

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3 glowing (LH pinky 5), E3 glowing (LH middle 3). D3 dimmed (hopped over).
pages.cleffy: Left hand, skip up from bass C. Pinky, then middle finger. C3 to E3. You're hopping over D3.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G3 glowing (LH thumb 1), E3 glowing (LH middle 3).
pages.cleffy: Now skip down. Thumb, then middle. G3 to E3.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH position. A melody: C3 → D3 (step) → F3 (skip) → E3 (step).
pages.cleffy: Step and skip, both hands of experience coming together. C3 to D3 (step up). D3 to F3 (skip up). F3 to E3 (step down). Play it with LH.

pages.figure: Bass staff with skip pair. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: See a skip on the bass staff. Play a skip up in the LH.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 4 notes. Shape: step up, skip up, step down.
pages.cleffy: Four-note bass melody. Step up, skip up, step down. Read and play.

pages.figure: Mix of step/skip bass questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 31. Bass staff with step pair and skip pair labeled side by side.
pages.cleffy: Skips, locked in for both hands. Tomorrow we add rhythm to the left hand. Then the grand staff. Then we play.

real_piano: Left hand, pinky on C3, middle on E3. Play C3-E3, then E3-C3. Skip up, skip down. Ten times.
Mix it: C3 (step to) D3 (step to) E3 (skip to) G3 (skip down to) E3. Slow.
