remediation.title: LH skips, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Step/skip mix-up is normal. Same drill as before, slower pacing, both hands available.

completion.cleffy: Both moves, both hands, both staves. The method is running on full power now.

title: Rhythm in the left hand
subtitle: Quarters and halves, but lower
goal: Read and play quarter and half notes in the left hand, in time with a metronome.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Metronome in the corner. Quarter notes across both staves in time.
pages.cleffy: Rhythm works the same everywhere. Quarter note is still one beat. Half note is still two. Whole note is still four. What changes in the left hand? Nothing. The rules don't care which hand plays.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Quarter, half, whole notes drawn — same shapes as on the treble staff.
pages.cleffy: Same three shapes you already know. Filled quarter. Empty half. No-stem whole. On the bass staff — identical.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a quarter note.
pages.cleffy: Quick check. Which duration?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a half note.
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Bass staff with four quarter notes on bass F. Metronome ticking at 60 bpm.

audio.A: 4 bass F quarter notes in time with metronome

pages.cleffy: Listen to four quarter notes played on bass F. Each one lasts one click.

pages.figure: Bass staff with two half notes on bass C. Metronome at 60 bpm.

audio.A: 2 bass C half notes, each lasting 2 clicks

pages.cleffy: Now two half notes. Each one holds through two clicks.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 4 quarter notes on bass C. LH pinky (5) labeled. Metronome at 60 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Left-hand pinky on bass C. Play four quarter notes, one per click.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 2 half notes on bass F. LH pointer (2) labeled.
pages.cleffy: Pointer finger on bass F. Two half notes. Each held through two clicks.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Two bars. Bar 1: quarter, quarter, half. Bar 2: half, half.
pages.cleffy: Mix them. Two bars of 4/4. Bar 1: quarter, quarter, half (one click, one click, two clicks). Bar 2: half, half.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 4 notes and rhythm: C3 half, D3 half, E3 quarter, D3 quarter, C3 half. Bar-lines drawn.
pages.cleffy: Real bass melody now, with rhythm AND pitch. Read the shape. Read the time. Play it with LH.

pages.figure: Bass staff with 8 quarter notes alternating bass C and bass F. Metronome.
pages.cleffy: Eight quarter notes, alternating bass C and bass F. Steady. Don't rush. Don't drag. Match the metronome.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Each round: a bass-staff bar plays. How many beats?
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Bass bar plays. How many beats in each bar?

pages.figure: Bass staff. One bar with bass C as a whole note (4 beats). This is actually Bar 1 of the LH of Merrily We Roll Along, simplified.
pages.cleffy: Preview of tomorrow's lesson. The left hand in Merrily We Roll Along is almost stupidly simple. Mostly it just holds bass C. Play bass C as a whole note — four beats, pinky on C3.

pages.figure: Mix of duration ID and bass rhythm questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 32. Bass staff with a metronome beside it, steady tick marks.
pages.cleffy: Left hand can read rhythm too. Tomorrow — the grand staff. Both hands, both staves, same page. The big one.

real_piano: Left hand, pinky on bass C. Play four quarter notes with a metronome at 60 bpm.
Then two half notes on bass F. Hold each through two clicks.
Mix: quarter, quarter, half (one bar of 4/4). Repeat ten times.

questions.figure: Bass staff, a quarter note.
questions.prompt: How long is this note?

questions.figure: Bass staff, a half note.
questions.prompt: How long is this note?

questions.audio: Four quarter notes on bass F
questions.prompt: How many notes did you hear?
Play bass C four times as quarter notes in time with the metronome.
questions.accept: C3 × 4 with quarter durations at set tempo

remediation.title: LH rhythm, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rhythm in a new hand feels weird the first time. Very slow metronome, basic patterns.

completion.cleffy: Left hand plays, left hand counts. Ready for the big one.

title: The grand staff
subtitle: Both staves, one piece
goal: Understand how the treble and bass staves connect into the grand staff, and how to read both at once.
piece_context: Nearly every piece of piano music uses a grand staff. Today we learn to see both staves as one system.

pages.figure: Treble staff floating above, bass staff below, a big gap between them. Cleffy holds a glowing brace that will connect them.
pages.cleffy: You've been reading two staves separately. Today we connect them. Welcome to the grand staff. This is how almost every piece of piano music is written.

pages.figure: Grand staff drawn — treble clef on top, bass clef on bottom, a curly brace connecting them on the left.
pages.cleffy: The grand staff is two staves stacked. Treble on top — right hand. Bass on the bottom — left hand. A curly brace on the left ties them together. That means: play both at the same time.

pages.figure: Grand staff with no notes yet. Just clefs and the brace. Labels: TREBLE, BASS.
pages.cleffy: Both clefs at once. Both hands at once. That's new. We're going to handle it carefully.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Treble has a single Middle C. Bass has a single bass C. Both in bar 1.
pages.cleffy: Simplest grand staff ever. Top: Middle C (treble). Bottom: bass C (an octave below). Right hand plays top. Left hand plays bottom. At the same time.

pages.figure: Same grand staff. Highlight the TOP note only, then fade it out and highlight the BOTTOM note.
pages.cleffy: Here's the trick when it feels overwhelming. Read one staff first. Top first. What's the RH doing? Middle C. Now the bottom. What's the LH doing? Bass C. Two separate reads. Then combine.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar. Treble: two quarter notes on Middle C. Bass: one whole note on bass C.
pages.cleffy: One bar. Read it in two passes. Pass 1 — right hand: two Middle Cs, quarter notes. Pass 2 — left hand: one bass C, whole note held through both.

pages.figure: Same bar. RH highlighted. Metronome 60 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Play the right hand alone first. Two quarter notes on Middle C.

pages.figure: Same bar. LH highlighted. Metronome 60 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now the left hand alone. One bass C, held through a whole bar.

pages.figure: Same bar. Both hands highlighted. Metronome at slow 50 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now together. Press both notes at the same time for beat 1. Then the LH holds (still pressed) while the RH plays the second quarter. Both hands — but the left just stays down.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the grand staff in action. Right hand moves. Left hand holds. Both hands at once.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Treble: C4-D4-E4-D4 (quarter notes). Bass: bass C whole note (held through all four).
pages.cleffy: Second example. RH has four notes. LH holds one note the whole time. Breathe. This looks like a lot. It isn't.

pages.figure: Same. RH highlighted.
pages.cleffy: RH first: C, D, E, D — four quarters.

pages.figure: Same. Both hands highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Both together. LH holds bass C the whole time. RH plays the melody. Slow tempo. Forgiving rhythm. Focus on hands doing different things without crashing.
pages.followup_cleffy: That was you playing two hands at once. Don't worry if it felt sloppy. First attempts are supposed to.

pages.figure: Grand staff with arrows showing eye movement: glance top, glance bottom, glance top, glance bottom.
pages.cleffy: Here's the eye trick: when reading a grand staff, your eyes bounce between the two staves fast. Top, bottom, top, bottom. You'll never read both perfectly at the exact same moment. You scan.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones. Just recognition — no two-hand playing in the test this time.

pages.figure: Grand staff with both examples laid out.
pages.cleffy: Today was a big day. Two hands is a real skill. Don't be hard on yourself if it felt weird. It IS weird, the first time.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 33. Grand staff with brace connecting treble and bass, both clefs visible. Cleffy giving a reassuring thumbs up.
pages.cleffy: You survived Wall Number Two. Both hands reading together. Tomorrow — Middle C becomes the bridge between the two staves. Two more lessons and you're playing Merrily We Roll Along.

real_piano: Hardest practice so far. Start with just your RH on Middle C. Four quarter notes, metronome at 50.
Then just LH holding bass C as a whole note.
Now TRY both — RH plays four quarters, LH holds bass C. Expect it to feel awkward. It always does the first few days.
If it's really hard, go back to hands separately for a day. No shame.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-12 (the hands-together drills) slowly. Multiple times.
practice_this_week.estimated_time: 10-15 min/day — this one deserves more time

questions.figure: Grand staff. Arrow pointing at the top staff.
questions.prompt: Which hand reads the top staff?

questions.figure: Grand staff. Brace circled.
questions.prompt: What does the brace on the left of the two staves mean?
options: Play both staves at the same time

questions.audio: A Middle C and a bass C played together
questions.prompt: Did you hear one note or two?
options: Two notes together (one high, one low)

questions.prompt: Play Middle C with your right hand and bass C with your left hand, at the same time.
questions.accept: C4 and C3 played simultaneously

remediation.title: The grand staff, very slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: This is THE wall. Almost everyone takes two or three passes. That's totally fine. Let's separate the hands completely, then bring them together very slowly.

completion.cleffy: You just did the hardest thing in Tier 2. Hands together is real. Two more lessons and you perform.

title: Middle C, on both clefs
subtitle: The bridge between the two staves
goal: See Middle C written two different ways — below treble, above bass — and recognize it's the same note.

pages.figure: Grand staff. In the gap between the two staves, Middle C glows gold, sitting on its own little ledger line.
pages.cleffy: Here's a cool thing about the grand staff. One note lives in the middle — between both staves. It's the same note whether you read it from above or below. It's the bridge. You already know it — Middle C.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: treble staff alone with Middle C (ledger line BELOW staff). Right: bass staff alone with Middle C (ledger line ABOVE staff). Both arrows point to the same piano key — C4.
pages.cleffy: Middle C can be written two ways. On the treble staff — below the five lines, on its own little ledger line. On the bass staff — above the five lines, on its own little ledger line. Same note. Same piano key. Just written from two different directions.

pages.figure: Grand staff with Middle C shared between the two staves — one ledger line halfway between both.
pages.cleffy: Why two ways? Because sometimes the right hand plays Middle C (reads it from above). Sometimes the left hand plays it (reads it from below). Whoever plays it just reads it from their own staff. Same key either way.

pages.figure: Treble staff with Middle C highlighted below the staff on a ledger line.
pages.cleffy: From the treble staff, Middle C hangs just below. You already know this one — we met it in Lesson 14.

pages.figure: Bass staff with Middle C highlighted just above the staff on a ledger line.
pages.cleffy: From the bass staff, Middle C sits just above. Mirror image. Same distance from the staff. Just on the other side.

pages.figure: Treble staff with a note on its ledger line below the staff.
pages.cleffy: Is this Middle C?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note on its ledger line above the staff.
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Bass staff with a note on line 5 (the top line) — close to Middle C but not quite.
pages.cleffy: Trick question. This one?

corrections.cleffy: Close. This note is ON the top line of the bass staff — not ABOVE it. Middle C hangs above the staff on its own ledger line.

pages.figure: Two play buttons side by side.

audio.A: Middle C (from treble staff reading)
audio.B: Middle C (from bass staff reading)

pages.cleffy: Both of these are Middle C. Same sound. It has to be — it's the same key on the piano. Written two ways, played one way.

pages.figure: Treble staff with Middle C. Keyboard with Middle C glowing gold. RH thumb labeled.
pages.cleffy: Right-hand thumb on Middle C. Play it.

pages.figure: Bass staff with Middle C. Keyboard with Middle C glowing gold. LH thumb labeled.
pages.cleffy: Now play the SAME note with your LEFT hand thumb. It's one key over from your LH five-finger position (if you're on C3-G3, it's one past your thumb).
pages.followup_cleffy: Same key. Different hand. Same Middle C.

pages.figure: Treble staff with Middle C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Round 1. Middle C from the treble staff. Play with RH.

pages.figure: Bass staff with Middle C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Round 2. Middle C from the bass staff. Play with... whichever hand feels right. Either works.

pages.figure: Mix of treble/bass Middle C questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 34. Grand staff with Middle C glowing gold between the two staves, an arrow showing it's the same note read from either direction.
pages.cleffy: One note. Two ways to write it. That's the bridge between the two staves. Tomorrow — your first two-hand piece. We play.

real_piano: Play Middle C with your right-hand thumb. Five times.
Play Middle C with your left-hand thumb. Five times.
Alternate: RH Middle C, LH Middle C, RH Middle C. Same note, swap hands.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10-13 (play Middle C from both clefs)

questions.figure: Treble staff, Middle C below the staff on a ledger line.
questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?

questions.figure: Bass staff, Middle C above the staff on a ledger line.
questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?

questions.audio: Middle C
questions.prompt: Is this Middle C?
Play Middle C with either hand.
questions.accept: C4

remediation.title: Middle C, both sides
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two ways to write the same note takes a minute. Let's see it zoomed in, both sides, and connect the shape to the sound.

completion.cleffy: One bridge note. Both staves now talk to each other. Tomorrow you play Merrily We Roll Along. Two hands. A song.

title: Merrily We Roll Along
subtitle: Act 7 · Your first two-hand piece
goal: Read and play a real piece using both hands together, with a simple LH and a familiar RH melody.
piece_context: You've played with both hands separately. Today they work together for the first time on a real song.

piece.title: Merrily We Roll Along
piece.notes_rh: E D C D E E E | D D D | E G G | E D C D E E E E D D E D C
piece.difficulty: elementary — RH melody + LH anchor

pages.figure: Cleffy holding sheet music that says Merrily We Roll Along. He hands a copy to the student.
pages.cleffy: You made it to Act 7's finish line. Today you play a piece with both hands. Real song. Two staves. Both of you. Merrily We Roll Along. Ready?

pages.figure: Big play button. Sheet music preview of Merrily We Roll Along.

audio.A: Merrily We Roll Along performed cleanly, both hands

pages.cleffy: Listen first. This is the whole song. Most of it is the melody you know. There's also a quiet LH note holding underneath.

pages.figure: Treble staff with the RH melody of Merrily We Roll Along written out. All steps and a few skips.
pages.cleffy: Right hand first. It's all steps and one skip. Mostly stepping around C, D, E. One spot has a skip up to G. You already know every move in this melody.

pages.figure: Bass staff with a whole note on bass C. Held through every bar. Very simple.
pages.cleffy: Left hand is stupidly simple. One note. Bass C. Held the whole song. Your LH pinky sits on bass C and stays there. That's it. No moving.

pages.figure: Treble staff with first phrase: E-D-C-D-E-E-E. Keyboard below, RH position.
pages.cleffy: Play the first phrase with just your right hand. E, D, C, D, E, E, E. Follow the dots. Fingers 3, 2, 1, 2, 3, 3, 3.

pages.figure: Treble staff: D-D-D, then E-G-G.
pages.cleffy: Second phrase. D three times, then E, then a skip up to G, then G again. Fingers 2-2-2, 3-5-5.

pages.figure: Treble staff: E-D-C-D-E-E-E-E-D-D-E-D-C.
pages.cleffy: Last phrase. Longer. All steps. Similar to phrase 1 but longer tail. You can do this without thinking too hard.

pages.figure: Bass staff with one whole note on bass C. Metronome at 60 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now the left hand. Pinky on bass C. Press it. Hold it. That's... the whole left-hand part of the song. One note.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Just two notes — RH Middle C, LH bass C. Both quarter notes. Metronome at 50 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Before the whole song — two-hand warm-up. Play Middle C with your RH and bass C with your LH at the same time. Once. Then again. Same time both hands.

pages.figure: Grand staff with full Merrily We Roll Along. Training wheels ON — each RH note glows when it's time. LH bass C stays lit the whole time.
pages.cleffy: Full song, slow, with guides. Your LH pinky just holds bass C for the whole piece. Your RH plays the melody. Don't rush.

pages.figure: Grand staff with the whole piece. No key glowing. Just the grand staff and you.
pages.cleffy: Again. No highlights. You read, you play, both hands. Stumble? Keep going.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was two hands, reading a grand staff, playing a song. Big moment.

pages.figure: Same grand staff. Metronome at 80 bpm.
pages.cleffy: One more time, up to proper tempo. 80 bpm. Don't stop if you mess up. Just keep going.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before Act 7 ends.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 7 COMPLETE. A trophy engraved with 7/10 acts. Cleffy holding up the sheet music with both hands.
pages.cleffy: Act 7 is done. You played a real piece with both hands. Even if it felt shaky — that's normal. First two-hand song is always the scrappy one. Tier 1's Happy Birthday is still the bigger milestone — but this was the one where both hands finally worked together. Next up — leaps, the third and last member of the method. Then more reading. Then Mary Had a Little Lamb.

real_piano: Play Merrily We Roll Along hands separately every day this week. RH one day. LH another. Then both.
When you try hands together, start REALLY slow. Half speed. Then build up.
LH is just bass C held. Once you trust your LH to just stay there, the piece gets way easier.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10-11 (guided + no-guides runs) three times this week

questions.figure: Grand staff for Merrily. RH first phrase highlighted.
questions.prompt: Is the RH mostly stepping, skipping, or jumping big?

questions.figure: Grand staff for Merrily. LH lower staff highlighted — a single whole note.
questions.prompt: How many different notes does the LH play in the whole piece?

questions.audio: First phrase of Merrily We Roll Along
questions.prompt: Does the melody mostly move in steps?
Play Middle C with your right hand and bass C with your left hand at the same time.
questions.accept: C4 and C3 simultaneously

remediation.title: Merrily, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two-hand debut. Totally fine if it took a few tries. We're going to break it into four bars, each one practiced hands-separately, then together, really slow.

completion.cleffy: Act 7, closed. First two-hand piece, done. Tomorrow: leaps. The last of step/skip/leap.

title: Leaps
subtitle: The third move in the method
goal: Recognize a leap — three or more spots apart — by sight, sound, and play.

pages.figure: Cleffy on a staircase jumping THREE steps at once.
pages.cleffy: Step. Skip. Leap. You've known those three words since Lesson 1. Today we finish the set. A leap is three or more spots on the staff.

pages.figure: Three staff pairs shown. A: line to next space (step). B: line to next line (skip). C: line to TWO lines up (leap).
pages.cleffy: Step — one spot. Smallest move. Skip — two spots. Hops over one. Leap — three or more. Jumps over a bunch.

pages.figure: Four leap pairs on staff — 4th, 5th, 6th, octave — each labeled.
pages.cleffy: Leaps come in different sizes. The smallest leap jumps over two spots. The biggest we'll see for now is an octave — same letter, one floor up. All of these are leaps.

pages.figure: Staff: line 1 to line 3 (leap).
pages.cleffy: Step, skip, or leap?

pages.figure: Staff: line 1 to space 1.
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Staff: line 2 to line 3.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Step, skip, or leap?

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C-E (skip). B: C-G (leap).

audio.A: C4 to E4
audio.B: C4 to G4

pages.cleffy: A skip and a leap side by side. Leap has more air. The gap is bigger. You can hear the distance.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Step, skip, or leap?
pages.cleffy: Four by ear.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and G4 glowing. Thumb and pinky of RH labeled.
pages.cleffy: Play a leap up. RH thumb on Middle C, pinky on G. Tap C, then G. That's a leap.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 and C4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Now come back. G to C. Leap down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Instruction: step, skip, leap, all starting from C.
pages.cleffy: Show me all three moves from Middle C. Step up (C-D). Then skip up (C-E). Then leap up (C-G).
pages.followup_cleffy: Step, skip, leap. All three moves, one hand, all from Middle C. That's the whole Sonata method right there.

pages.figure: Staff with leap pair. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Read the leap. Play a leap up on the piano.

pages.figure: Four rounds mixed.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Step, skip, or leap — see, hear, play.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 36. Staircase with three feet: one stepping, one skipping, one leaping.
pages.cleffy: All three moves, named and known. Tomorrow we lock in leap shapes — how to spot one at a glance.

real_piano: RH thumb on Middle C, pinky on G. Play C-G (leap up), G-C (leap down). Ten times.
LH pinky on bass C, thumb on G3. Same thing: bass-C to G3 leap. Ten times.
Try step-skip-leap from any note. Muscle memory for all three distances.

questions.figure: Staff, line 1 to line 3.
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.figure: Staff, space 2 to space 3.
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.figure: Staff, line 2 to space 2.
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.audio: C4 to G4
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.audio: C4 to D4
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?
Play a leap up from Middle C.
questions.accept: C4 and any note 3+ steps above

remediation.title: Leaps, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Leap is the biggest of the three moves. Extra time is fine. Let's see and hear lots more.

completion.cleffy: The method is complete. Step, skip, leap — all three, locked in.

title: Leap shapes
subtitle: Spot a leap at a glance
goal: Recognize common leaps (4th, 5th, 6th, octave) by their visual shape on the staff.

pages.figure: Staff with four leap pairs lined up, sizes increasing from left to right.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday you learned that a leap is three or more spots apart. Today we lock in the common sizes — so when a leap shows up in a real piece, your eye knows which one it is without counting.

pages.figure: Four labeled leaps on staff: 4th (C-F), 5th (C-G), 6th (C-A), octave (C-C).
pages.cleffy: Four leaps you'll see all the time. A 4th — three spots apart. C to F. A 5th — four spots. C to G. A 6th — five spots. C to A. An octave — seven spots. C to the next C up.

pages.figure: Four leap pairs with ghost notes drawn in the gaps — clearly showing the gap size.
pages.cleffy: Your eye can size a leap by how much blank space sits between the notes. Small gap — 4th. Medium gap — 5th or 6th. Full octave — they look FAR apart.

pages.figure: Staff: C to G (5th).
pages.cleffy: This leap — small (4th), medium (5th/6th), or octave?

pages.figure: Staff: C4 to C5 (octave).
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Identify the leap size.

pages.figure: Four play buttons, each a different leap size.

audio.A: C-F (4th)
audio.B: C-G (5th)

pages.cleffy: Leaps sound different depending on size. 4th has a small, open feeling. 5th sounds strong. 6th is reach-y. Octave sounds like ONE note. You don't need to name them yet by ear — just notice the difference.

pages.figure: Play button. Either an octave or a 4th.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Octave, or smaller leap?

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and F4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 4th. C to F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and G4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 5th. C to G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and C5 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play an octave. Middle C to the next C up. Thumb and pinky stretch.
pages.followup_cleffy: Octaves are big stretches. If it hurts, don't force it — just tap both keys.

pages.figure: Staff with a random leap pair. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Read the leap shape, play any matching leap.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: C-D-G-E. Moves: step up, leap up, skip down.
pages.cleffy: Four-note melody with a leap in the middle. Read and play.

pages.figure: Mix of leap-size questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 37. Four leaps on a staff with labels: 4th, 5th, 6th, octave.
pages.cleffy: Your eye knows the common leap sizes now. Tomorrow — hearing them. Small leap or big leap, at a glance.

real_piano: RH thumb on Middle C. Stretch pinky to G (5th). Play C-G ten times.
Stretch pinky further to A (6th). Play C-A ten times.
Octave stretch: thumb Middle C, pinky on the next C. If it's hard, don't push — but try.

questions.figure: Staff, C to F (4th).
questions.prompt: What size leap?

questions.figure: Staff, C to A (6th).
questions.prompt: What size leap?

questions.figure: Staff, C to next C up (octave).
questions.prompt: What size leap?

questions.audio: C4 to C5 octave
questions.prompt: Octave or smaller?

questions.audio: C4 to F4 (4th)
questions.prompt: Octave or smaller?
Play a 5th starting from Middle C.
questions.accept: C4 and G4

remediation.title: Leap shapes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Leap sizes take time. Let's zoom in and count the gap together on each one.

completion.cleffy: Four leap shapes, recognized by eye. Sharp.

title: Small vs big leap
subtitle: Hearing the size
goal: Hear the difference between a small leap (4th/5th) and a big leap (6th/octave).

pages.figure: Ear icon next to a staircase. A small leap and a big leap both drawn.
pages.cleffy: You can see leap sizes. Today your ear learns to hear them too. Two categories to start: small leap and big leap.

pages.figure: Two leap pairs on staff. A: C-G (5th, small-side). B: C-C5 (octave, big).
pages.cleffy: Small leap — 4th or 5th. Kind of close. Big leap — 6th, 7th, or octave. Wide open. Two buckets. That's enough for now.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: C4 to G4 (5th, small)
audio.B: C4 to C5 (octave, big)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Small leap first, big leap second. Big leap has more space — your ear can feel it stretching.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Small or big leap?

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: C4 to E4 (skip)
audio.B: C4 to G4 (leap)

pages.cleffy: Now harder. One is a skip, one is a leap. Skip is smaller than any leap. Don't let them fool you.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Skip or leap?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Step, skip, or leap by ear.
pages.cleffy: Now mix it all. Step, skip, or leap?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C and G4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a small leap — C to G. That's a 5th.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and A4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Now a big leap — C to A. That's a 6th.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and C5 glowing.
pages.cleffy: And an octave. Same letter, one floor up.

pages.figure: Staff with a leap up, then a leap down, labeled.
pages.cleffy: Read each leap. Play a matching leap in the right direction.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: C-G-E-D (leap up, skip down, step down).
pages.cleffy: Four notes. Leap up, then skip down, then step down. Read and play.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Fast rounds. Step, skip, or leap.

pages.figure: Mixed four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 38. Ear icon with three arrows: small, medium, big.
pages.cleffy: Ear is trained. Small leaps vs big leaps. Tomorrow — which finger lands where when a leap shows up in a piece.

real_piano: RH thumb on Middle C. Play C-G (small leap). Then C-A (medium). Then C-C up (octave big).
LH pinky on bass C. Same three leaps moving up.

questions.figure: Staff: C to G.
questions.prompt: Small leap or big leap?

questions.figure: Staff: C4 to C5.
questions.prompt: Small leap or big leap?

questions.audio: C4 to G4 (small leap)
questions.prompt: Small or big?

questions.audio: C4 to C5 (octave)
questions.prompt: Small or big?
Play a big leap from Middle C.
questions.accept: C4 and any note 5+ steps above

remediation.title: Ear training, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Leap sizes by ear take reps. Let's do more, slower, with replays.

completion.cleffy: Ears can size leaps now. One last leap lesson tomorrow — fingering.

title: Leap fingering
subtitle: Plan which finger lands where
goal: Choose fingers intelligently when a leap appears. Avoid the crash.
piece_context: When your hand has to leap, you need to plan — otherwise you collide with yourself.

pages.figure: Hand about to play a leap, with a question mark between two keys.
pages.cleffy: Leaps come with a planning problem. If you don't decide which finger lands on the second note BEFORE you jump, you'll crash — or end up in a weird hand position. Today we practice planning.

pages.figure: Two hand attempts side by side. Left: wrong fingering, tangled fingers. Right: correct fingering, clean.
pages.cleffy: Good fingering means your hand is already shaped for what comes next. Bad fingering means you play one note, realize your fingers are tangled, panic.

pages.figure: RH palm. Thumb and pinky highlighted. Arrow shows they're at the far ends.
pages.cleffy: The thumb and pinky are farthest apart. When a leap goes up, thumb usually stays low, pinky reaches high. When a leap goes down, flip it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH thumb on Middle C (labeled 1). RH pinky on G (labeled 5). C-G is a 5th.
pages.cleffy: Standard setup: thumb on the lower note, pinky on the higher. That's a 5-finger position for a 5th. Your hand is ready — you just tap thumb, then pinky.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 labeled 1, G4 labeled 5.
pages.cleffy: Play the leap with the planned fingering. Thumb on C, pinky on G. Tap both.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 labeled 5, C4 labeled 1.
pages.cleffy: Leap down. Pinky on G, thumb on C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 labeled 1, A4 labeled 5. Thumb and pinky stretched to a 6th.
pages.cleffy: Bigger leaps need a bigger stretch between thumb and pinky. A 6th — C to A — pinky has to reach. Your hand opens up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C and A glowing, numbered 1 and 5.
pages.cleffy: Play C to A with fingers 1 and 5.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and C5 glowing. Hand stretched to its max.
pages.cleffy: Octave — biggest reach. Most adult hands can do it. Kids and smaller hands can't always — and that's fine. Just tap both keys. Don't force the stretch.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and C5 glowing, numbered 1 and 5.
pages.cleffy: Try an octave. Thumb on C, pinky reaching for the C above. If it hurts, let go and just tap both.

pages.figure: Staff with 3-note melody: C (thumb) - G (pinky) - A (stretch). Fingering shown above each note.
pages.cleffy: Here's the real trick — planning for the NEXT leap while you're playing the current one. Three notes: C, G, A. Thumb, pinky, then stretch pinky further. Look ahead. Your fingers set up before the leap, not during.

pages.figure: Same staff. Keyboard. Numbers above each key.
pages.cleffy: Play C-G-A. Thumb, pinky, stretch pinky further.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: C-G-E-C4 (ending on the same C). Mixed moves.
pages.cleffy: Four notes. Plan your fingering as you go. C (1) → G (5) → E (3) → C (1). The middle note uses a finger in between.

pages.figure: Mix of step/skip/leap and fingering questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 39. Hand with fingers numbered, ready for a leap.
pages.cleffy: Fingering is choreography. Plan before you play. Tomorrow — the full method review. Step, skip, leap — all three, at speed.

real_piano: RH thumb on C. Play C-G with thumb-pinky five times. Feel the hand shape.
Stretch to A (6th). Play C-A ten times. Pinky reaches.
Octave attempt: C-C. If your hand is too small, just tap both keys — no stretch required yet.

questions.figure: Staff with a leap labeled C to G. Question: which finger pair?
questions.prompt: For a 5th in the right hand, which finger pair makes sense?

questions.figure: Keyboard. Thumb on Middle C, pinky on A.
questions.prompt: Can your hand play C to A comfortably with thumb-pinky?

questions.figure: Staff with a leap labeled C to G.
questions.prompt: For this leap, thumb and pinky is a good pair. Agree?

questions.audio: C4 to G4 (5th)
questions.prompt: Did you hear a leap or a step?
Play Middle C with your thumb, then G with your pinky.
questions.accept: C4 and G4 with declared 1 and 5

remediation.title: Leap fingering, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hand choreography is real practice. Let's slow it down and feel each leap.

completion.cleffy: Leaps, planned. Tomorrow we review the whole method at speed.

title: Step, skip, leap — all three
subtitle: Act 8 · The full method, at speed
goal: Recognize step, skip, and leap at a glance and by ear. The full Sonata method, locked in.
piece_context: The three moves of the Sonata method are now all yours. Every piece you ever read uses them.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a trophy. Behind him, three staircases labeled STEP, SKIP, LEAP.
pages.cleffy: Act 8, final lesson. Today you prove you own all three. Step. Skip. Leap. At speed. By eye, by ear, by hand.

pages.figure: Three-panel chart. Step = one spot, kinds switch. Skip = two spots, kinds keep. Leap = three+ spots, bigger jump.
pages.cleffy: Recap. Step — smallest move. Line-to-space or space-to-line. Skip — one bigger. Line-to-line or space-to-space. Leap — three or more spots.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 8. 2-second time limit. Step, skip, leap.
pages.cleffy: Eight rounds at speed. Trust your eye.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Still faster.
pages.cleffy: Six more. Quicker this time.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six by ear. Step, skip, or leap?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Mixed size leaps.
pages.cleffy: Four more. Some of the leaps are small, some big. Still just step/skip/leap.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Prompt rotates: step, skip, leap.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. I name a move. You play it, any direction.

pages.figure: Staff with 3 notes: step up, skip up. Various starting points.
pages.cleffy: Read a 3-note melody. All three moves mixed. See the shape, play the matching shape.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: step, leap, step.
pages.cleffy: Four-note melody. Step up, leap up, step down.

pages.figure: Staff with 5 notes: step, skip, leap, step, skip.
pages.cleffy: Five notes. All three moves at least once.

pages.figure: Grand staff with a treble melody and a bass melody, both using step/skip/leap.
pages.cleffy: The method works on both staves. Always has. Both hands can read any of the three moves.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Mixed see/hear/play.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Every mode.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 8 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. This is the last act 8 step before the real test.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 8 COMPLETE. Trophy engraved 8/10 acts. Three small feet labeled STEP, SKIP, LEAP dancing.
pages.cleffy: Act 8 closed. The method is completely yours now. Every piece of music you'll ever read is built out of those three moves. Next — the rest of the notes on the treble staff get names, derived from Middle C. Then Mary Had a Little Lamb.

real_piano: RH thumb on Middle C. Play step (C-D), skip (C-E), leap (C-G). Ten times each.
Mix it: step up, skip down, leap up. Make up your own 4-note melodies.
LH too: same three moves in the bass range.
virtual_only: Replay pages 3-6 (the speed drills). Aim for faster, accurate.

questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.figure: Staff, skip up.
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.figure: Staff, leap (4th).
questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

questions.prompt: Step, skip, or leap?

Play a leap up from any note.

remediation.title: All three, once more
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act 8 boss. Let's slow it down and do another pass on step, skip, and leap side by side.

completion.cleffy: The Sonata method is yours. All three moves, both hands, both staves. Eight of ten acts done.

title: Treble lines, derived from C
subtitle: E, G, B, D, F — counted from Middle C
goal: Name every line of the treble staff by counting from Middle C — no mnemonics.

pages.figure: Treble staff with Middle C labeled below, and question marks on each line.
pages.cleffy: Time for names. Real ones, derived from what you know. The Sonata method doesn't use FACE or Every Good Boy. We count from the anchor. Middle C.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Middle C glowing on its ledger line below the staff.
pages.cleffy: Start here. Middle C. From Middle C, count up.

pages.figure: Middle C → D (space just below staff) → E (bottom line of staff). Each labeled.
pages.cleffy: Step up from C lands on D. Step up from D lands on E. E is the bottom line of the treble staff. First line.

pages.figure: Treble staff with all five lines labeled: E, G, B, D, F from bottom to top.
pages.cleffy: From E, keep skipping. E → G → B → D → F. Five lines, top to bottom: F, D, B, G, E. Every line is a skip from the last.

pages.figure: Staff with line 1 and line 2 highlighted. Ghost note between them (in space 1).
pages.cleffy: Why skips? Because line to line is a skip — the odd rule. So lines go E, G, B, D, F by skipping up from E each time.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Note on bottom line (E).
pages.cleffy: Which letter?

pages.figure: Treble staff. Note on line 3 (middle line, B).
pages.cleffy: And this one?

pages.figure: Treble staff. Note on line 5 (top line, F).
pages.cleffy: Top line — what letter?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Each round: a note on a treble line.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Name the line. Use the five letters: E, G, B, D, F.

pages.figure: Treble staff with notes. Each plays its audio.

audio.A: E4
audio.B: G4

pages.cleffy: Listen to each line note. E. G. B. D. F. Your ear is starting to link pitch to staff position.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 glowing. Label on staff line 1.
pages.cleffy: Play E on the piano. It's a step up from D, which is a step up from Middle C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Now G. The line right above E, with one line between.

pages.figure: Staff with a line note. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. See a line note. Play it. Name it.

pages.figure: Mix of name and play questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 41. Treble staff with all 5 lines labeled E, G, B, D, F. Cleffy pointing at them in order.
pages.cleffy: Five line names. Derived from Middle C, not memorized with tricks. Tomorrow — the spaces.

real_piano: On a real staff (or drawing one), label each line E, G, B, D, F.
Quiz yourself: I say a line number (1-5), you say the letter. Time yourself.
Play each line note on the piano, going up. E, G, B, D, F.

questions.figure: Treble staff, note on line 1 (E).
questions.prompt: Which letter?

questions.figure: Treble staff, note on line 3 (B).
questions.prompt: Which letter?

questions.figure: Treble staff, note on line 5 (F).
questions.prompt: Which letter?

questions.audio: G4 (line 2)
questions.prompt: Which line note?
Play B (line 3 on the treble staff).
questions.accept: B4

remediation.title: Treble lines, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Five names is a lot. Let's walk them from Middle C upward, one line at a time, slower.

completion.cleffy: Treble lines, named by the method. No mnemonics needed.

title: Treble spaces, derived from C
subtitle: F, A, C, E — the four spaces
goal: Name every space of the treble staff by counting from Middle C. All five lines and four spaces now named.

pages.figure: Treble staff with lines labeled (E G B D F) and question marks on the spaces.
pages.cleffy: Five lines, named. Four spaces to go. Same trick — count from Middle C. No mnemonics.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Middle C → D → E (line 1) → F (space 1). Each labeled.
pages.cleffy: Start at Middle C. Step up: D. Step up: E (line 1). Step up: F. F is the first space on the treble staff.

pages.figure: Treble staff with all four spaces labeled: F, A, C, E from bottom to top.
pages.cleffy: From F, skip up — lands on A. Skip up — C. Skip up — E. Four spaces: F, A, C, E. Bottom to top.

pages.figure: Staff with space 1 and space 2 highlighted. Ghost note on the line between.
pages.cleffy: Same reason as lines — space to space is a skip. Both lines and spaces are every other letter.

pages.figure: Treble staff, note in space 1 (F).
pages.cleffy: Which letter?

pages.figure: Treble staff, note in space 3 (C).
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Name the space.

pages.figure: Treble staff with random note (line or space).
pages.cleffy: Now mix. Line or space? Name it.

remediation.title: Treble spaces, slower

title: Ledger lines
subtitle: Notes outside the staff
goal: Read notes above and below the treble staff — notes that sit on ledger lines.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Middle C below on a ledger line (familiar). Above the staff, another note on a ledger line (new).
pages.cleffy: Middle C sits on a little line below the staff. You've known that since Lesson 14. Those little lines are called ledger lines. Notes above the staff use them too.

pages.figure: Treble staff with ledger lines drawn above and below. Several notes placed on them.
pages.cleffy: Ledger lines extend the staff. Above it. Below it. Each ledger line is just another stair — the staff keeps going, it just doesn't draw every line.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Middle C on ledger line 1 below. Below that — B. Further down — A (ledger line 2).
pages.cleffy: Below the treble staff. Middle C is on the first ledger line below. The note just below that — B (space). Then A, on the second ledger line below.

pages.figure: Treble staff with F (top line, labeled). Above it — G (space above staff) — A (first ledger line above) — B (space) — C (second ledger line above, high C).
pages.cleffy: Above the treble staff. F is the top line. The space above is G. Then A on the first ledger line above. Keep going — B, then C (high C, two octaves above Middle C).

pages.figure: Treble staff, note on ledger line 1 below (Middle C).
pages.cleffy: Which letter?

pages.figure: Treble staff, note on ledger line 1 above (A5).
pages.cleffy: This one?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 5.
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. Name the ledger-line note.

pages.figure: Two playback cards.

audio.A: Middle C (below staff)
audio.B: A5 (above staff)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Below the staff sounds low. Above sounds high. Extends in both directions naturally.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C glowing. Staff with note on ledger line below.
pages.cleffy: Play Middle C. You know this one — same old C4.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A5 glowing. Staff with note on ledger line above.
pages.cleffy: Now A above the staff. Further right on the piano.

pages.figure: Staff with a ledger-line note (below or above).
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. See the note, play it.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 notes: Middle C → E → G → A (above staff).
pages.cleffy: Four notes going up. Starts on Middle C (ledger below), ends on A (ledger above). Play the climb.

pages.figure: Staff with notes way above and below — ledger lines stretching far out.
pages.cleffy: Ledger lines let you write notes that fall outside the five-line staff. Pieces can have notes way up or way down. Ledger lines just keep the grid going.

pages.figure: Mix of ledger-line reading.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

title: RH five-finger positions
subtitle: C position and G position
goal: Comfortably play in both C position and G position with your right hand.

pages.figure: Hand hovering over keyboard. Two outlined spots labeled C POSITION and G POSITION.
pages.cleffy: Your right hand has a few places it likes to sit. The two most common are called C position and G position. Each one gets five fingers on five consecutive white keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4-D4-E4-F4-G4 glowing. Numbers 1-5 above.
pages.cleffy: C position — thumb on Middle C. Pointer on D. Middle on E. Ring on F. Pinky on G. You've been in this one since Lesson 9.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4-A4-B4-C5-D5 glowing. Numbers 1-5 above.
pages.cleffy: G position — thumb on G (same G that's under your pinky in C position). Pointer on A. Middle on B. Ring on C (high C, one octave above Middle). Pinky on D. You just shifted right three white keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C position and G position side-by-side, with G highlighted in both.
pages.cleffy: Notice — G is the thumb in G position, and it was the pinky in C position. The positions overlap at G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C position highlighted. Fingers numbered.
pages.cleffy: Play C position ascending. Thumb through pinky.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G position highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Now G position. Thumb through pinky. G, A, B, C, D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C position, then G position, then C position again.
pages.cleffy: Shift between them. Play C position (thumb on C). Then lift your hand and move thumb to G. Play G position.

pages.figure: Treble staff showing all 5 notes of C position: Middle C (ledger), D (space below staff), E (line 1), F (space 1), G (line 2).
pages.cleffy: C position on the staff. Middle C at the bottom (ledger), up to G (line 2).

pages.figure: Treble staff showing G position: G (line 2), A (space 2), B (line 3), C (space 3), D (line 4).
pages.cleffy: G position on the staff. G (line 2) at the bottom, up to D (line 4). Stays inside the staff — no ledger lines needed.

pages.figure: Staff with 5 notes: C-D-E-F-G (C position). Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Read this line in C position. Play it.

pages.figure: Staff with 5 notes: G-A-B-C-D (G position).
pages.cleffy: Same thing, G position.

pages.figure: Staff with a melody in C position. Prompt to pick the position.
pages.cleffy: I show you a melody. Which position would your hand use?

pages.figure: Staff with 4-note melody in G position: G-B-A-G. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Melody in G position. Hand on G-A-B-C-D. Play G, B, A, G. Thumb, middle, pointer, thumb.

pages.figure: Position identification + playing questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 44. Keyboard with C position and G position outlined as two hand shapes.
pages.cleffy: Two hand homes for the right hand. C position and G position. Tomorrow — you play Mary Had a Little Lamb. All in one position.

real_piano: C position. Play C-D-E-F-G ascending, then descending. Ten times.
G position. Play G-A-B-C-D ascending and back. Ten times.
Shift between them: C position then G position, three times.

title: Mary Had a Little Lamb
subtitle: Act 9 · One hand, one position, one song
goal: Read and play Mary Had a Little Lamb in C position — a full piece from one hand.
piece_context: You played a two-hand piece in Lesson 35. Today you play a one-handed piece — but with a real melody you'll recognize.

piece.title: Mary Had a Little Lamb
piece.notes: E D C D E E E | D D D E G G | E D C D E E E E D D E D C
piece.difficulty: elementary — one hand, C position

pages.figure: Cleffy holding sheet music with Mary Had a Little Lamb at the top.
pages.cleffy: End of Act 9. Your second act boss piece. Mary Had a Little Lamb. Right hand only, all in C position. You already know the tune.

pages.figure: Play button. Sheet music of Mary Had a Little Lamb.

audio.A: Mary Had a Little Lamb performed cleanly

pages.cleffy: Listen. The whole thing. Mostly steps. A couple of skips. No leaps.

pages.figure: Treble staff with Mary Had a Little Lamb written out. Three phrases.
pages.cleffy: Three phrases. All in C position. Thumb on Middle C, pinky on G. Hand stays put the whole time.

pages.figure: First phrase: E D C D E E E. Moves labeled: step down, step down, step up, step up, step up, repeat, repeat.
pages.cleffy: First phrase. Mary had a little lamb. E, D, C, D, E, E, E. Starts on E, steps down to C, steps back up to E. Then holds E.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C position. E-D-C-D-E-E-E glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 1. Middle, pointer, thumb, pointer, middle, middle, middle.

pages.figure: Second phrase: D D D E G G. Moves labeled.
pages.cleffy: Second phrase. Little lamb, little lamb. Three Ds. Then E. Then a skip up to G. Then G again.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D-D-D-E-G-G glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 2. Pointer three times, middle, pinky, pinky.

pages.figure: Third phrase: E D C D E E E E D D E D C. Longer.
pages.cleffy: Third phrase. His fleece was white as snow. E D C D E E E E D D E D C. Same ideas as phrase 1, but longer, and ends on C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The full last phrase glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 3. Follow the dots.

pages.figure: Full piece on staff. Keyboard below. Guides ON.
pages.cleffy: Whole song, slow. Guides on.

pages.figure: Full piece. No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read and play.
pages.followup_cleffy: Good. Mary Had a Little Lamb, read off the page. You know every move in there — just steps, one skip, and a few repeats.

pages.figure: Full piece at a real tempo.
pages.cleffy: Up to proper tempo now. 80 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner — last check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration — ACT 9 COMPLETE. Trophy 9/10. Sheet music of Mary floating beside Cleffy.
pages.cleffy: Act 9, done. Your second boss piece. Next — Act 10. Bass-staff note names, a both-hand drill, and Tier 2 keeps moving.

real_piano: RH in C position. Play Mary Had a Little Lamb from the page, slow. Three times a day.
Try it at 80 bpm once it feels steady.
If you want a challenge — play it starting on G (in G position). Same shape, new notes.

pages.figure: Grand staff. No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides now.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: E-D-C. LH: C3-E3 (LH moves too).
pages.cleffy: LH does something now. C3 on beat 1, E3 on beat 3. RH keeps going. This is closer to real music.

pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 10 boss check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration — ACT 10 COMPLETE. Trophy showing acts complete so far in Tier 2.
pages.cleffy: Act 10, done. Both hands, both staves, all named notes, real reading. Halfway through Tier 2. Rhythm gets deeper next — eighth notes, rests, dotted notes.

real_piano: The two-bar exercise: RH C-D-E-F then G-F-E-D. LH holds bass C under both bars.
Build up speed slowly. Match a metronome.

questions.figure: Grand staff with a bar. RH has 4 notes, LH has a whole.

Play RH Middle C and LH bass C at the same time.

remediation.title: Both-hand drill, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two-hand work is the hardest thing so far. Break it into bars, hands separate, then together.

completion.cleffy: Act 10, sealed. Real two-hand reading. Halfway through Tier 2.

title: Eighth notes
subtitle: Two notes per beat
goal: Recognize and count eighth notes — two per beat, twice as fast as quarters.

pages.figure: Staff with a row of quarter notes. Below: same row but each quarter split into two eighth notes.
pages.cleffy: Quarter note is one beat. What if you want half a beat? That's an eighth note. Two eighth notes fit in one quarter.

pages.figure: Eighth note drawn — filled oval with stem AND a flag. Then two eighth notes beamed together.
pages.cleffy: Shape: filled oval, stem, and a little flag on top. When two or more eighths are in a row, their flags turn into a beam connecting them.

pages.figure: Bar of 4/4 with eighth notes. Beat count: 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and.
pages.cleffy: Eighth notes count: 1-and-2-and-3-and-4-and. The number is the quarter-note beat. The 'and' is the eighth in between.

pages.figure: Metronome. Eighth notes alternating with spaces.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 + 8 eighth notes on Middle C

pages.cleffy: Listen. Two taps per click. That's eighth notes.

pages.figure: Staff with either a quarter or an eighth pair.
pages.cleffy: Which is this?

options: Two eighths (1 beat total, two taps)

pages.figure: Staff with eighth pair (beamed).
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Quarter, half, whole, or eighth pair?

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: 4 quarter notes
audio.B: 8 eighth notes (same bar time, twice as many notes)

pages.cleffy: Same length of time. Twice as many notes. Listen to each.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. Metronome at 60. Eighth notes on C.
pages.cleffy: Tap Middle C on every click AND every "and". Two taps per beat.

pages.figure: Bar: quarter, quarter, two eighths, two eighths.
pages.cleffy: Mix. Two quarters, then four eighths.

pages.figure: Staff. C (quarter), D (quarter), E-F-E-D (eighths), C (quarter). One bar in 4/4.
pages.cleffy: Read and play.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. A bar plays. How many notes?

pages.figure: Bar of 8 eighth notes ascending C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C.
pages.cleffy: 8 eighth notes, one bar. Scale up from Middle C.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 51. Beamed eighth notes drawn on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Eighth notes, locked. Rhythm gets richer from here. Tomorrow — rests. Silence in music.

real_piano: Metronome at 60. Tap Middle C on every click. Now add a tap on every 'and' (so two per click).
Play C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C as eighth notes in one bar.

questions.figure: Two eighth notes beamed
questions.prompt: How many beats do these take together?

questions.figure: Single eighth note (with flag)
questions.prompt: How many beats?

questions.audio: 8 eighth notes in 4/4
questions.prompt: How many notes total?
Play four eighth notes on Middle C.
questions.accept: C4 × 4 at eighth duration

remediation.title: Eighth notes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Splitting a beat in half takes a minute. Slower metronome, clear count.

completion.cleffy: Eighth notes, understood. Faster rhythm is now on the table.

title: Rests
subtitle: Silence is music too
goal: Read quarter, half, and whole rests and play music with gaps of silence.

pages.figure: Staff with a row of notes. In the middle, a blank space marked REST.
pages.cleffy: Music has silence in it too. Planned silence. A rest is a symbol that says "don't play. Wait." Each rest has a length, same as a note.

pages.figure: Quarter rest, half rest, whole rest drawn on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Three rests for now. Quarter rest — squiggly symbol. 1 beat of silence. Half rest — small block sitting on line 3. 2 beats. Whole rest — small block hanging from line 4. 4 beats (a whole bar of silence in 4/4).

pages.figure: Half rest (sits on top of line) vs whole rest (hangs from line). Side by side.
pages.cleffy: Trick: half rest sits ON line 3. Whole rest HANGS from line 4. Or: half = hat on line. Whole = hole under line.

pages.figure: Staff with a quarter rest.
pages.cleffy: Which rest?

pages.figure: Staff with a half rest.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Staff with a whole rest.
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Name the rest.

pages.figure: Bar: quarter, quarter, quarter rest, quarter note. Metronome.

audio.A: Metronome 60, 4 beats — note, note, silence, note

pages.cleffy: Listen. 1, 2, [silence], 4. That silence on beat 3 is a quarter rest. The beat still happens. Nothing is played.

pages.figure: Bar: C (quarter), C (quarter), quarter rest, C (quarter). Metronome.
pages.cleffy: Tap Middle C on beat 1 and 2. Silence on beat 3. Tap again on 4.

pages.figure: Bar: C (half), half rest, in 4/4.
pages.cleffy: Two beats of C, then two beats of silence.

pages.figure: Whole rest.
pages.cleffy: A whole bar of silence. Count 1-2-3-4 in your head.

pages.figure: Two bars. Bar 1: C (quarter), rest, C (quarter), C (quarter). Bar 2: D (half), rest (half).
pages.cleffy: Two bars with rests. Read and play.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which rest did you hear?

pages.figure: Mixed rhythm questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 52. Three rests lined up with their beat values.
pages.cleffy: Rests. Silence with a length. Tomorrow — dotted notes. Notes that last a little longer.

real_piano: Metronome at 60. Tap, tap, silence, tap. Four beats. Repeat.
Now: tap for two clicks, rest for two clicks. Keep counting.

questions.figure: Half rest on staff.
questions.prompt: How many beats of silence?

questions.figure: Quarter rest
questions.prompt: How many beats?

questions.figure: Whole rest
questions.prompt: How many beats?

questions.audio: Quarter note, quarter rest, quarter note, quarter rest
questions.prompt: How many notes in 4 beats?
Play: C, rest, C, rest in 4/4 as quarters.
questions.accept: C4, rest, C4, rest

remediation.title: Rests, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Silence is weird to read. Let's clap patterns with pauses.

completion.cleffy: Rests, in. Music now has pauses in it.

title: Dotted notes
subtitle: A dot adds half the note's value again
goal: Read and count dotted quarter and dotted half notes.

pages.figure: Staff. A half note next to it, plus a little dot. Label: half note PLUS half.
pages.cleffy: Notice the little dot next to a note? That changes how long it lasts. A dot adds half the note's value to itself.

pages.figure: Dotted half note drawn. 2 + 1 = 3 beats.
pages.cleffy: Dotted half note. Half = 2 beats. Dot adds half of 2, which is 1. Total: 3 beats.

pages.figure: Dotted quarter note drawn. 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 beats.
pages.cleffy: Dotted quarter. Quarter = 1 beat. Dot adds half of 1 (which is half). Total: 1.5 beats. A quarter-and-an-eighth combined.

pages.figure: Dotted half note on staff.
pages.cleffy: How many beats?

pages.figure: Dotted quarter.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. How many beats?

pages.figure: Metronome. Bar: dotted half, quarter, in 4/4.

audio.A: Dotted half held for 3 clicks, then quarter on beat 4

pages.cleffy: Listen. First note holds through 3 clicks. Then one more beat.

pages.figure: Metronome. Bar: dotted quarter, eighth, half.

audio.A: Dotted quarter (1.5 beats) + eighth + half

pages.cleffy: Dotted quarter then eighth — very common pattern. 1.5 + 0.5 = 2 beats total.

pages.figure: Bar: C dotted half, D quarter.
pages.cleffy: Play: Middle C held for 3 beats, then D on beat 4.

pages.figure: Bar: dotted quarter + eighth pair patterns.
pages.cleffy: Dotted quarter + eighth. Holds 1.5 beats, then a quick note.

pages.figure: Staff: D dotted half, E quarter, then C half, rest half.
pages.cleffy: Two-bar melody with a dotted half. Read and play.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which duration?

pages.figure: First bar of Happy Birthday — with dotted eighth + sixteenth pattern. Just dotted quarter for now.
pages.cleffy: Happy Birthday starts with a dotted note. You've played it before. G (dotted quarter) — G (eighth) — A (quarter) — G (quarter) — C (quarter) — B (half).

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 53. Dotted half drawn, with labels 2 + 1 = 3 beats.
pages.cleffy: Dot = half again. Easy math, great shortcut. Tomorrow — ties and slurs. They look alike but aren't.

real_piano: Play Middle C as a dotted half note. Hold for three clicks.
Try the Happy Birthday opening with dotted quarter + eighth.

questions.figure: Dotted half note
questions.prompt: How many beats?

questions.figure: Dotted quarter
questions.prompt: How many beats?

questions.audio: Dotted half held 3 clicks
questions.prompt: How long?
Play Middle C as a dotted half. Hold through 3 clicks.
questions.accept: C4 dotted half

remediation.title: Dotted notes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Dot math is a rep thing. Slower examples.

completion.cleffy: Dotted notes, understood. Rhythm is almost a full vocabulary now.

title: Ties vs slurs
subtitle: They look alike — they aren't
goal: Tell a tie from a slur and play each correctly.

pages.figure: Staff with a tie and a slur side by side. Both are curved lines — but they do different things.
pages.cleffy: Two curved-line symbols that look identical. One is a tie. One is a slur. They do totally different things.

pages.figure: Two notes of the SAME pitch joined by a curved line. Label: TIE.
pages.cleffy: A tie connects two notes of the SAME pitch. You play the first note, then hold it through the second note's duration. Don't tap again. Just hold.

pages.figure: Two notes of DIFFERENT pitches joined by a curved line. Label: SLUR.
pages.cleffy: A slur tells you to connect notes smoothly. A tie specifically extends a same-pitch note across beats. You play each note under a slur, but make them smooth — connect them in sound. Later lessons will cover "smooth" more (that's called legato). For now: slur = play connected.

pages.figure: Side by side. Tie: C-C. Slur: C-D.
pages.cleffy: Rule of thumb: same pitch = tie. Different pitch = slur.

pages.figure: Staff with two Cs joined by curve.
pages.cleffy: Tie or slur?

pages.figure: Staff with C-D joined by curve.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Tie or slur?

pages.figure: Metronome. Two quarter notes of C tied.

audio.A: Middle C played and held for 2 beats (tied quarters)

pages.cleffy: Listen. Two quarters tied. Sounds like one half note — because it is.

pages.figure: Two quarter notes C-D slurred (played smoothly).

audio.A: C-D played connected (slurred)

pages.cleffy: Two notes slurred. Both play, but connected — no gap between them.

pages.figure: Staff: C quarter tied to another C quarter.
pages.cleffy: Tap C once. Hold through 2 beats. Don't tap again.

pages.figure: Staff: C quarter slurred to D quarter.
pages.cleffy: Play both notes. Connect them — the second should start the moment the first ends.

pages.figure: Staff: C tied to C (2 beats), then slurred D-E-F quarters.
pages.cleffy: Read and play. Tie on the C. Slur on D-E-F.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Was that a tie (held one note) or a slur (two different notes)?

pages.figure: Mixed questions.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 54. A tie and a slur on a staff, clearly labeled.
pages.cleffy: Curved lines, clarified. Tomorrow — Ode to Joy, your first classical melody.

real_piano: Play Middle C tied across two beats. Feel the hold.
Play C to D slurred. Make them connect — no gap.

questions.figure: Two notes of same pitch joined by curve
questions.prompt: Tie or slur?

questions.figure: Two different notes joined by curve
questions.prompt: Tie or slur?

questions.audio: Middle C held for 2 beats (tied)
questions.prompt: Was that one note held, or two notes connected?
Play Middle C as a tied-across-two-beats note.
questions.accept: C4 held for 2 beats

remediation.title: Ties vs slurs, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Same vs different pitch — slow look at each pattern.

completion.cleffy: Ties and slurs, sorted.

title: Ode to Joy
subtitle: Act 11 · Your first classical melody
goal: Read and play Ode to Joy — a real classical melody from the 9th Symphony.
piece_context: Beethoven's Ode to Joy. You've heard it. Now you play it.

piece.title: Ode to Joy (theme)
piece.notes: E E F G | G F E D | C C D E | E D D | E E F G | G F E D | C C D E | D C C
piece.difficulty: elementary — RH in C position, steps and one skip

pages.figure: Cleffy holding sheet music titled Ode to Joy (theme from Beethoven's Symphony No. 9).
pages.cleffy: Ode to Joy. Beethoven wrote it for a symphony and a chorus. You know the tune. Today, you play it.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Ode to Joy performed at moderate tempo, RH only

pages.cleffy: Listen first. Whole tune.

pages.figure: First phrase on staff: E E F G G F E D. All steps. Labels under moves.
pages.cleffy: First phrase. E E F G G F E D. Starts on E. Repeats E. Steps up to F. Step up to G. Back down step by step.

pages.figure: Keyboard, RH in C position.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 1. Middle, middle, ring, pinky, pinky, ring, middle, pointer.

pages.figure: Second phrase: C C D E E D D. Step by step mostly.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 2. C C D E E D D. Down to C, up to E, back to D.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 2.

pages.figure: Third phrase: same as phrase 1.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 3 is identical to phrase 1. Repeats work.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Play it.

pages.figure: Phrase 4: C C D E D C C. Ends on C.
pages.cleffy: Final phrase. C C D E D C C. Lands on home — C.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play the finale.

pages.figure: Full Ode to Joy on staff. Guides on, tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Whole tune, slow, with guides.

pages.figure: No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read and play.

pages.figure: At 90 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now at tempo. 90 bpm.

pages.figure: Celebration — ACT 11 COMPLETE. Trophy 11/15 acts. Beethoven profile with sheet music.
pages.cleffy: Act 11, sealed. First real classical melody — Beethoven, Ode to Joy. Tomorrow dynamics start. How loud, how soft.

real_piano: Play Ode to Joy once a day, slow.
Work up to 90 bpm.
If you know anyone musical, play it for them. Tell them it's your first Beethoven.

questions.figure: First phrase of Ode to Joy

Play the first four notes of Ode to Joy.
questions.accept: E4 E4 F4 G4

remediation.opening_cleffy: Beethoven's tune, broken into four phrases, slow.

title: Loud and soft
subtitle: p and f — your first dynamics
goal: Read and play the two basic dynamic markings: p (piano = soft) and f (forte = loud).

pages.figure: Staff with a big 'p' under one note and a big 'f' under another.
pages.cleffy: Notes have a volume. Composers tell you how loud to play. Two markings to start: p and f.

pages.figure: Staff with 'p' drawn big. Label: PIANO = SOFT.
pages.cleffy: 'p' stands for piano. In Italian, piano means SOFT. Yes — the instrument is named after the same word. The piano used to be called pianoforte. When you see 'p' under a note, play it gently.

pages.figure: Staff with 'f'. Label: FORTE = LOUD.
pages.cleffy: 'f' stands for forte. Italian for LOUD. When you see 'f' under a note, play it strong.

pages.figure: Staff with p.
pages.cleffy: Loud or soft?

pages.figure: Staff with f.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. p or f?

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: C played softly

pages.cleffy: Listen to soft. That's p.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: C played loudly

pages.cleffy: Now loud. That's f.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Soft or loud?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. 'p' marking.
pages.cleffy: Tap Middle C softly. Light touch.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. 'f' marking.
pages.cleffy: Now tap Middle C loudly. Stronger touch.

pages.figure: Staff: C (p) then C (f). Alternating. Metronome.
pages.cleffy: Four Cs. Alternate soft, loud, soft, loud.

pages.figure: Staff: C-D-E-F with 'p' marking at start.
pages.cleffy: Four-note phrase marked p. Play all four softly.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 56. p and f symbols drawn big with labels SOFT and LOUD.
pages.cleffy: Two dynamic markings. You can read loud and soft now. Tomorrow — the shape of a phrase.

real_piano: Play Middle C softly, then loudly. Feel the difference in your touch.
Play Ode to Joy once soft, once loud.

questions.figure: p marking
questions.prompt: Play loud or soft?

questions.figure: f marking
questions.prompt: Play loud or soft?

questions.audio: A soft note
questions.prompt: Soft or loud?
Play Middle C softly, then loudly.
questions.accept: C4 then C4 with dynamic change

remediation.title: p vs f, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two letters, two volumes. Slow walkthrough.

completion.cleffy: Loud and soft, on the page and in your hand.

title: Shape of a phrase
subtitle: Music breathes
goal: Feel how a musical phrase rises and falls — and play it that way.

pages.figure: Wave shape drawn over a staff — a phrase arches up and comes back down.
pages.cleffy: A phrase is a musical sentence. It has a beginning, a middle, and an end. And just like a sentence, it has a shape. It rises, peaks, relaxes.

pages.figure: Arch drawn over 5 notes on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Most phrases arch. They start quiet, get louder in the middle, soften at the end. That's called phrase shape — and you can play it even without markings.

pages.figure: Side by side: spoken sentence 'I really love this' with stress on middle. And a musical phrase with stress in the middle.
pages.cleffy: Think of a sentence. You don't say every word at the same volume. You stress the important one in the middle. Music is the same.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Phrase played flat — same volume throughout
audio.B: Same phrase played with shape — arches up and down

pages.cleffy: Listen. Flat first. Then shaped. Same notes. Completely different feeling.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.
pages.cleffy: Two recordings. Which one is shaped?

pages.figure: Staff with a 5-note phrase. Dynamics drawn: p → louder → peak → softer → p.
pages.cleffy: Here's a phrase with dynamics written in. Starts p. Grows. Peaks in the middle. Comes back down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-D-E-D-C. Arrow shows volume rising then falling.
pages.cleffy: Play this: C D E D C. Start soft. Get a bit louder at E (the peak). Come back to soft.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Scale up and back. Arrow shows arch.
pages.cleffy: Seven-note arch: C-D-E-F-E-D-C. Shape it.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Shaped or flat?

pages.figure: First phrase of Ode to Joy with arch drawn above it.
pages.cleffy: Try it with Ode to Joy's first phrase. E E F G — that's the climb. Get slightly louder as you rise. Then G F E D — softer coming down.

pages.figure: Two phrases side by side. One arches up. One stays flat. Label: some phrases are flat on purpose.
pages.cleffy: Not every phrase arches. Some stay flat, some fall, some rise. Shape depends on the music. Your job is to notice what the melody is doing.

pages.figure: Staff: G-F-E-D-C, all descending. Label: falling phrase.
pages.cleffy: Descending phrase. Start slightly louder, fade as it falls.

pages.figure: Three recordings.

audio.A: Rising phrase
audio.B: Falling phrase

pages.cleffy: Three shapes. Listen and notice how each feels.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 57. A phrase with dynamic arch drawn above.
pages.cleffy: Phrases have shape. Your hand can give it to them. Tomorrow — the exact symbols for volume changes. Crescendo and diminuendo.

real_piano: Play Ode to Joy with phrase shape — each phrase arches slightly.
Don't overdo it at first. Just a little louder in the middle, a little softer at the ends.

questions.figure: Staff with a rising phrase.
questions.prompt: As this phrase rises, should the volume typically...

questions.audio: Shaped phrase
questions.prompt: Is this flat or shaped?
Play a C-D-E-D-C phrase with an arch shape.
questions.accept: C-D-E-D-C with volume increase at E

remediation.title: Phrase shape, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Phrase shape is a feel thing. Slow examples, clear arches.

completion.cleffy: Phrases can breathe now. Your playing will feel more alive.

title: Crescendo and diminuendo
subtitle: Getting louder, getting softer
goal: Read the two volume-change symbols and play them correctly.

pages.figure: Two triangular symbols on a staff: one opens outward (<), one closes inward (>).
pages.cleffy: Two more dynamic symbols. They're not letters — they're lines that grow or shrink.

pages.figure: A hairpin opening to the right: <. Label: CRESCENDO = GET LOUDER.
pages.cleffy: Crescendo. In Italian, it means "growing". The symbol opens from a point into a wide mouth. Play gets louder as the line grows.

pages.figure: Hairpin closing: >. Label: DIMINUENDO = GET SOFTER.
pages.cleffy: Diminuendo. Italian for "diminishing". Opens wide and closes to a point. Play gets softer as the line shrinks.

pages.figure: Two arrows. < = opens right = louder. > = closes right = softer.
pages.cleffy: Memory trick — follow the shape. Opening mouth = getting bigger = louder. Closing mouth = getting smaller = softer.

pages.figure: Staff with a <.
pages.cleffy: Louder or softer?

pages.figure: Staff with >.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Crescendo or diminuendo?

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: A scale played with crescendo

pages.cleffy: Hear it. Starts soft, grows.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Same scale played with diminuendo

pages.cleffy: Now getting softer. Starts loud, shrinks.

pages.figure: Staff: C-D-E-F-G with crescendo marking.
pages.cleffy: Play C D E F G. Start soft. Get louder as you go.

pages.figure: Staff: G-F-E-D-C with diminuendo.
pages.cleffy: Now come back down. Start loud. Get softer.

pages.figure: Staff: C-E-G-E-C with cresc then dim.
pages.cleffy: Arch with marks. C to G gets louder. G back to C gets softer.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds by ear. Growing or shrinking?

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 58. Both hairpins drawn big with arrows showing direction.
pages.cleffy: Growing, shrinking. You can shape a phrase with just these two signs. Tomorrow we drill them. Then Ode to Joy with real dynamics.

real_piano: Play a C-major five-finger scale with a crescendo (soft to loud) ascending, diminuendo back down.
Try it with Ode to Joy — arch each phrase.

questions.prompt: Louder or softer?
Louder or softer?

questions.audio: Crescendo on a scale
questions.prompt: Crescendo or diminuendo?
Play C-D-E-F-G with a crescendo.
questions.accept: 5 notes ascending with volume increasing

remediation.title: Crescendo/diminuendo, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two shapes, two volumes. Slow with visuals.

completion.cleffy: Growing and shrinking. Real expression on the page now.

title: Dynamic drill
subtitle: See, hear, play — all four markings
goal: Quickly identify p, f, crescendo, and diminuendo by sight, sound, and play.

pages.figure: Four dynamic markings drawn in a row: p, f, <, >.
pages.cleffy: Four dynamic markings. Drill day. p = soft. f = loud. < = louder. > = softer.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.

title: Ode to Joy, with shape
subtitle: Act 12 · Same piece, now expressive
goal: Play Ode to Joy with dynamics — phrase shape, crescendos, and a soft-to-loud arc.
piece_context: You played Ode to Joy flat in Lesson 55. Today you play it alive.

pages.figure: Ode to Joy sheet music, this time with p, f, and crescendo/diminuendo marks added.
pages.cleffy: Same tune. Different version. Today you add the dynamics. Suddenly it's music, not just notes.

pages.figure: Two playback cards.

audio.A: Ode to Joy flat
audio.B: Ode to Joy with dynamics

pages.cleffy: Listen to both. Flat first. Shaped second. The shaped one is the one people remember.

pages.figure: Full Ode to Joy with dynamic markings drawn: p at start, crescendo at peak, diminuendo at end, new p repeat, final rising phrase.
pages.cleffy: Here's the plan. Phrase 1 starts p. Grows slightly. Softens to end. Phrase 2 stays medium. Phrase 3 repeats 1 but this time growing stronger. Phrase 4 softens to p and rests on C.

pages.figure: First phrase with p + slight crescendo.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 1. Start soft. Slight growth at the peak (G). Soften the ending.

pages.figure: Phrase 2 at medium dynamic.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 2. C C D E E D D. Medium-loud. Let the climb feel slightly bigger.

pages.figure: Phrase 3, same notes as 1 but marked f.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 3. Same notes as phrase 1. Play it fuller — f at the start, drive the climb.

pages.figure: Phrase 4 with diminuendo toward the end.
pages.cleffy: Final phrase. C C D E D C C. Get softer as you come home.

pages.figure: Full tune, dynamics written. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Whole tune with shape. Slow.

pages.figure: Same. No guides. Tempo 90.
pages.cleffy: No guides. At tempo. Shape it.

pages.figure: Cleffy plays your version back, then a flat version.
pages.cleffy: Can you hear the difference now? Dynamics aren't decoration. They're what makes it music.

pages.figure: Full tune, tempo 90, shape attention.
pages.cleffy: One more time, your best attempt.

pages.figure: Image: same tune played flat vs shaped, audience yawning vs engaged.
pages.cleffy: Dynamics are the difference between a robot playing piano and a person playing piano. You played Beethoven today. Keep doing that.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 12 boss check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration — ACT 12 COMPLETE. Trophy 12/15.
pages.cleffy: Act 12, sealed. You played Beethoven expressively. Tomorrow starts Act 13 — sharps, flats, and naturals.

real_piano: Ode to Joy with dynamics, every day this week.
Record yourself. Listen back. Hear the shape.
