title: Sharps
subtitle: One semitone higher
goal: Read the sharp symbol and play the black key just above a white key.

pages.figure: Staff with a note followed by a sharp symbol (#). Black key glowing on keyboard.
pages.cleffy: New symbol. The sharp. #. It turns a white-key note into the black key just above it. That's it. One rule.

pages.figure: Staff: F then F-sharp. Keyboard: white F and the black key just above (F#) glowing.
pages.cleffy: When you see F#, you play the black key right above F. Same row. Just one little shift to the right (higher).

pages.figure: Full keyboard. Each white key with a black key above it highlighted, showing: C# is above C, D# is above D, F# is above F, G# is above G, A# is above A.
pages.cleffy: Every white key that has a black key above it can be sharpened. C-D-F-G-A all have sharps. E and B don't have a black key right above — their sharps land on the next white key. We'll get to that later.

pages.figure: Close-up of the sharp symbol — like a tic-tac-toe grid.
pages.cleffy: The symbol — two horizontal lines, two vertical lines. Kinda looks like #. Sits just before the note it modifies.

pages.figure: Staff with F#.
pages.cleffy: Which note is marked?

pages.figure: Play buttons.

audio.A: F4

pages.cleffy: F. Then F-sharp. Higher by a semitone. Barely different — but your ear will pick it up with practice.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F# glowing (black key above F4).
pages.cleffy: Play F-sharp. The black key right above F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C# glowing (black key above C4).
pages.cleffy: Now C-sharp.

pages.figure: Staff: F, F#, G (three notes). Step up via a sharp.
pages.cleffy: A sharp is part of a step. F to F# — tiny step. F# to G — another tiny step. F to G is two of those tiny steps. That's why F-F-sharp-G works.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F, F#, G labeled.
pages.cleffy: Play F, F-sharp, G. Three close notes.

pages.figure: Staff: C, C#, D. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Read and play. C, C-sharp, D.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Was that a sharp or a plain white key?

pages.figure: Staff with a sharp note. Keyboard with multiple black keys — which one is it?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Match the sharp on the staff to the right black key.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 61. # symbol drawn big. Keyboard with black keys above C, D, F, G, A labeled as their sharp versions.
pages.cleffy: Sharps: raise it by a tiny step. Tomorrow — flats. Lower it instead.

real_piano: Play F, F#, G slowly. Then C, C#, D. Then G, G#, A.
Notice how the black key sits right between two white keys.

questions.figure: Staff with F# marked
questions.prompt: Which note?

questions.figure: Keyboard. Black key between C and D.
questions.prompt: What's this note called?

questions.audio: C# played
questions.prompt: Sharp (black key) or natural (white)?
Play F-sharp.

remediation.title: Sharps, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: One little shift right. Slow pass.

completion.cleffy: Sharps, in. Black keys now have names.

title: Flats
subtitle: One semitone lower
goal: Read the flat symbol and play the black key just below a white key.

pages.figure: Staff with B♭ (B-flat).
pages.cleffy: Flats. Same idea as sharps, opposite direction. Flat symbol lowers a note by a tiny step — to the black key just below.

pages.figure: Staff: B then B♭. Keyboard: B white key and black key just below it.
pages.cleffy: B-flat means play the black key right below B. Remember sharp raised? Flat lowers.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Black keys below white keys labeled: B♭, A♭, G♭, E♭, D♭.
pages.cleffy: Every white key that has a black key below it has a flat version. B, A, G, E, D all have flats.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Black key between A and B labeled TWO ways: A# above, B♭ below.
pages.cleffy: Something tricky: the same black key can be called two names. A-sharp and B-flat are the same key. Just called different things depending on context. Don't stress. We'll learn when to use which later.

pages.figure: Close-up. Flat symbol looks like a lowercase b.
pages.cleffy: The symbol looks like a b. That's the flat. Sits just before the note.

pages.figure: Staff with B♭.
pages.cleffy: Which note?

pages.figure: Play buttons.

audio.A: B4

pages.cleffy: B. Then B-flat. B-flat is lower by a tiny step.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Black key below B glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play B-flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E♭ glowing (black key below E).
pages.cleffy: E-flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B, B♭, A descending.
pages.cleffy: Step down using a flat. B, B-flat, A.

pages.figure: Staff: B, B♭, A.
pages.cleffy: Read and play.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Sharp (raised) or flat (lowered)? Both are black keys — but the CONTEXT matters. Here, just tell me if it went up or down from the last note.

pages.figure: Staff with sharps and flats mixed.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Is this a sharp or a flat?

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 62. Flat symbol drawn, keyboard with flats labeled.
pages.cleffy: Flats: lower by a tiny step. Tomorrow — the natural sign. For when you want to UNdo a sharp or flat.

real_piano: Play B, B-flat, A. Then E, E-flat, D.
Notice how the flat takes you to a black key just below the white key.

questions.figure: Staff with B♭
questions.prompt: Which note?

questions.figure: Keyboard with the black key just below E highlighted.
questions.prompt: E-flat is which black key?

questions.audio: B♭
questions.prompt: Is this B or B-flat?
Play B-flat.

remediation.title: Flats, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: One step down to the black key. Slow walk.

completion.cleffy: Flats, in. Black keys have two names each — both work.

title: Naturals
subtitle: Cancel the sharp or flat
goal: Read the natural symbol and understand when it resets a note back to the white key.

pages.figure: Staff with F, then F#, then F♮ (natural).
pages.cleffy: One more symbol. The natural sign. If a note was sharped or flatted, the natural cancels it back to the regular white key.

pages.figure: Staff: F, F#, then F with a natural sign. Arrow showing it goes back to plain F.
pages.cleffy: When you see a natural (♮), play the plain white key. Not the sharp. Not the flat. The natural.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F#. Bar 2: first note F# (still), second note F-natural.
pages.cleffy: Naturals show up when a note was just sharped (or flatted) and the composer wants to undo it mid-piece.

pages.figure: Close-up of natural sign — small rectangle.
pages.cleffy: Natural looks like a small rectangle. Sits just before the note.

pages.figure: Staff: F♮
pages.cleffy: Which note do you play?

pages.figure: Staff: B♮ (after being flatted earlier)
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: F#
audio.B: F natural

pages.cleffy: F-sharp, then F natural. Natural is lower by a tiny step.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F glowing (white key, not F#).
pages.cleffy: Play F natural — the white key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F, F#, F♮, G.
pages.cleffy: Walk: F, F#, back to F natural, then G.

pages.figure: Three accidentals lined up: #, ♭, ♮.
pages.cleffy: Three signs to know now. # = raise. ♭ = lower. ♮ = cancel.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Sharp, flat, or natural?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Sharp, flat, or natural (played against context)?

pages.figure: Staff: F, F#, F♮, G. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Read and play.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 63. Three accidentals side by side: sharp, flat, natural.
pages.cleffy: All three accidentals, known. Tomorrow — how they work inside a bar of music.

real_piano: Play F, F#, F♮, G. Feel the sharp go up and the natural cancel it.
Try the same with B: B, B♭, B♮, C.

questions.figure: F♮
questions.prompt: Which key?

questions.figure: Three accidentals in a row.
questions.prompt: Which one cancels a sharp or flat?

questions.audio: F natural after an F-sharp
questions.prompt: Did the second note go up or down?
Play F natural.

remediation.title: Naturals, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Natural cancels. Slow look with before/after examples.

completion.cleffy: Three accidentals, all done.

title: Accidentals in a bar
subtitle: They last until the bar-line
goal: Understand how sharps, flats, and naturals persist within a bar — and reset at each bar-line.

pages.figure: Staff. Bar 1: F# on beat 1, another F (no sharp written) on beat 3. Bar 2: F (no sharp). Arrows highlight what each F is.
pages.cleffy: Here's a rule that trips up a lot of people. Once you see a sharp or flat, it STAYS ACTIVE for the rest of that bar. Then at the bar-line, everything resets.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F# (explicit), then F (no symbol). Label: both are F#.
pages.cleffy: Bar 1. First F is marked sharp. Second F has no symbol — but it's ALSO sharp. Why? Because the sharp still applies to every F in the rest of the bar.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F#. Bar 2: first F written plain. Arrow: bar-line reset.
pages.cleffy: Bar 2. First F has no symbol. So it's plain F — natural. The bar-line erased the sharp.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F# (beat 1), F♮ (beat 3). Arrow: natural sign mid-bar.
pages.cleffy: What if you want a natural F in the same bar as a sharp one? Write a natural sign. It cancels for the rest of the bar.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F# on beat 1. Beat 3: F (no symbol).
pages.cleffy: The F on beat 3 — what do you play?

pages.figure: Bar 1: F#. Bar 2: F (no symbol).
pages.cleffy: The F at the start of bar 2?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Sharp or natural?

pages.figure: Play button. A short melody with the sharp carrying across a bar.

audio.A: Two-bar melody with F# persisting through bar 1

pages.cleffy: Listen. The F-sharp shows up on beat 1 of bar 1. When another F plays later in that bar, it's still sharp.

pages.figure: Bar 1: F# quarter, G quarter, F (no symbol) quarter, F# (implied) quarter.
pages.cleffy: Play this bar. F-sharp twice, even though only the first one has a sharp written.

pages.figure: Two bars. Bar 1: F# F (still sharp). Bar 2: F F (both natural).
pages.cleffy: Two bars. First bar has the sharp. Second bar is all natural.

pages.figure: One bar. Beat 1: F#. Beat 2: F♮ (explicit natural).
pages.cleffy: Explicit natural mid-bar cancels the sharp from beat 2 onward.

pages.figure: Staff as above.
pages.cleffy: Play it.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four.

pages.figure: Two bars. Bar 1: C, C#, C, B (three C#'s then B). Bar 2: C (no symbol).
pages.cleffy: Read carefully. The C mid-bar 1 is STILL sharp. The C at start of bar 2 is NOT sharp.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 64. Two bars with clear accidentals showing persist-then-reset rule.
pages.cleffy: Accidentals live for one bar. Reset at the bar-line. Tomorrow — Jingle Bells. Real piece with sharps.

real_piano: Play F#, F (still sharp), F (still sharp) — one bar.
Then a new bar starting on F — now back to natural.
The bar-line does the resetting. Watch for it.

questions.figure: Bar 1: F# beat 1, F no-symbol beat 3.
questions.prompt: What do you play on beat 3?

questions.figure: Bar 2: F with no symbol.
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.audio: Two-bar melody where F carries sharp through bar 1, resets bar 2
questions.prompt: Did the F change between bar 1 and bar 2?
Play F-sharp, G, then F still sharp, then bar-line, then plain F.
questions.accept: F#4 G4 F#4 then bar-line then F4

remediation.opening_cleffy: Persist-until-bar-line is the confusing part. Slow examples, clear resets.

completion.cleffy: Accidentals now behave like they do in real music.

title: Jingle Bells
subtitle: Act 13 · First piece with sharps
goal: Play a simplified Jingle Bells with an F# in it — your first piece using accidentals.
piece_context: Your first piece with a sharp. It'll still feel familiar — the sharp sneaks in on one note.

piece.title: Jingle Bells (simplified)
piece.notes: F# F# F# | F# F# F# | F# A D E F# (D major — two sharps: F# and C#)
piece.key_center: D major (2 sharps: F# and C#)
piece.difficulty: elementary — mostly steps in D major, featuring F#

pages.figure: Cleffy with sheet music labeled Jingle Bells. Sleigh bells in the background.
pages.cleffy: Act 13 boss. Jingle Bells. Same tune you've heard a thousand times. But now you play it in D major — two sharps: F# and C#.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Jingle Bells simplified, RH

pages.cleffy: Listen. Three phrases. Lots of repeated notes. In the key of D — F# all over the place.

pages.figure: Staff showing where F# appears in Jingle Bells (D major).
pages.cleffy: Here's the sharp. In D major, every F is F-sharp — it shows up all through this tune. Remember — F-sharp is the black key just above F.

pages.figure: First phrase of Jingle Bells. All F# notes, repeated. F# F# F# (quarter notes).
pages.cleffy: First phrase. Jingle bells jingle bells. F-sharp, F-sharp, F-sharp. Repeated three times.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F#4 highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Play F-sharp three times. All quarters.

pages.figure: Phrase 2: F# A D E F# (going up and resolving, in D major).
pages.cleffy: Second part — jingle all the way. F-sharp, A, D, E, F-sharp. Goes up to A, then drops and comes back up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase 2 notes.
pages.cleffy: Play F-sharp, A, D, E, F-sharp.

pages.figure: Phrase 3 in D major (featuring F#).
pages.cleffy: Third phrase. Oh what fun it is to ride. G G G G G F-sharp F-sharp F-sharp F-sharp E E F-sharp E A. Every F in this tune is F-sharp — that's the key of D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F# highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Before the phrase — just tap F-sharp twice to get it in your hand.

pages.cleffy: Simplified phrase. Notice every F is F-sharp — that's the key of D doing its job.

title: Why key signatures exist
subtitle: Sharps and flats told to you up front
goal: Understand that a key signature lists which notes are always sharp or flat in a piece — so they don't have to be marked every time.

pages.figure: Staff with a single sharp at the start (F#) — that's a key signature of G major.
pages.cleffy: See that sharp sign right after the clef? That's not for the first note. It's for the whole piece. It says: every F in this piece is F-sharp. Unless I tell you otherwise.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: a piece with F# written on EVERY F. Right: same piece with a key signature — no extra symbols needed.
pages.cleffy: Without key signatures, composers would have to write a sharp on every single F. Exhausting. So they put the rule at the beginning and we just read it once.

pages.figure: Key signature shown: one sharp on the F line.
pages.cleffy: A key signature sits right after the clef. Before the time signature. Whatever sharps or flats are listed — they apply to EVERY note on that line or space, in every bar.

pages.figure: Staff with F# in the key signature. Label: G MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: Most common key signature with ONE sharp: G major. It says: every F is sharp. Every F. Always.

pages.figure: Staff with B♭ in the key signature. Label: F MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: Most common key signature with ONE flat: F major. It says: every B is flat.

pages.figure: Staff with F# in key signature. Note on F line.
pages.cleffy: Key says all Fs are sharp. What do you play?

pages.figure: Staff with B♭ key. Note on B line.
pages.cleffy: Key says all Bs are flat. What do you play?

pages.figure: Staff with F# in key signature. A note with explicit ♮ in the middle of a bar.
pages.cleffy: A natural sign can override the key — within its bar. If you see F♮ inside a G-major piece, that F is natural. But only for that bar.

pages.figure: Staff with F# key. Various notes.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Given a G-major key signature, what do you play for each note?

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Scale in C major (no sharps/flats)
audio.B: Scale in G major (F#)

pages.cleffy: Listen. C major scale. G major scale (uses F#). Both sound "major" — bright, settled. Just different starting points.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G A B C D (5-finger scale in G). Key signature shows F#.
pages.cleffy: Play a 5-finger scale in G — just G A B C D. No F in these five notes, so no sharps actually come up — but the key is G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F G A B♭ C (5-finger scale in F). Key has B♭. The B in this position is B-flat.
pages.cleffy: F major 5-finger — F G A B-flat C. Notice the B is flat.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Short phrase, pick the key.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Does this phrase have a sharp or a flat in its key?

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 66. Staff with F# key at the start.
pages.cleffy: Key signatures — one rule at the door, applies to the whole piece. Tomorrow — G major in detail. A whole piece with F# as a given.

real_piano: Play a G-major 5-finger position. G A B C D, ascending and back.
Play an F-major 5-finger position. F G A B-flat C. Notice the B-flat black key.
Try playing Ode to Joy starting on G (G A B C etc) — without the sharps.

Play G-A-B-C-D.

remediation.title: Key signatures, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The "rule at the door" idea takes a minute. Slow walkthrough.

completion.cleffy: Key signatures make sense now.

title: G major
subtitle: One sharp — F-sharp, always
goal: Read and play a simple piece in G major.
piece_context: First real key-signature experience. Every F becomes F-sharp.

pages.figure: Treble staff with F# key signature prominent. Label: G major.
pages.cleffy: One sharp. F-sharp. That's the G-major key signature. It shows up everywhere — pop, classical, folk. Most-used key on piano.

pages.figure: Close-up of the F# symbol sitting on the top line of the treble staff.
pages.cleffy: The F-sharp sits on the top line of the treble staff. That's line 5 — the F line. Wherever an F is written (any octave), it's sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G A B C D (in G position). Key signature F#.
pages.cleffy: G position — G A B C D. No F in the five-finger range, so no sharps pop up here. But the piece is still in G major. You just haven't met an F yet.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G A B C D E F# G (full G major scale). Key signature with F#.
pages.cleffy: Full G scale. G A B C D E F-sharp G. Watch the F — it's sharp. Black key.

pages.figure: Staff with F# key. Simple melody: G B G D G. Mostly skips.
pages.cleffy: Here's a 5-note melody in G major. No F, so no sharp comes up. Still in G major — the sharp is there, just not used.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G B G D G.
pages.cleffy: Play it. G position.

pages.figure: Staff with F# key. Melody: G A B F# (sharp!) G. The F gets sharped automatically.

title: F major
subtitle: One flat — B-flat, always
goal: Read and play a simple piece in F major.

pages.figure: Treble staff with B♭ key signature.
pages.cleffy: One flat. B-flat. F-major key signature. Very common — Amazing Grace, tons of hymns, lots of classical.

pages.figure: Close-up of ♭ symbol on the middle line (B line) of the treble staff.
pages.cleffy: The flat sits on line 3 — the B line. Every B, any octave, gets flatted.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F G A B♭ C (F position). Key signature: B♭.
pages.cleffy: F position is F G A B-flat C. Notice the B is flat — that's the only black key in this five-finger range.

pages.figure: Full F-major scale: F G A B♭ C D E F. Key signature B♭.
pages.cleffy: Full F major scale. F G A B-flat C D E F. Only the B is flat. Everything else is natural.

pages.figure: Simple F major melody on staff: F G A B♭ A G F.
pages.cleffy: A melody in F. F G A B-flat A G F. Ends on F. The B is automatically flat because of the key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same melody.
pages.cleffy: Play it.

pages.figure: Staff in F major. Various notes.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. In F major — what do you play?

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: F major scale

pages.cleffy: F major scale. A bit warmer than G major. Softer landing.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: G major scale

pages.cleffy: G first, then F. Both "major" — both bright. Different starting notes, different feel.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full F scale.

pages.figure: Staff in F major: F G A B♭ C B♭ A G F.
pages.cleffy: Read and play.

pages.figure: Play button, four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. G major (F# in key), F major (B♭ in key), or C major (neither)?

pages.figure: Table: C major (no sharps/flats), G major (1 sharp: F#), F major (1 flat: B♭).
pages.cleffy: Three keys you know now. C major — zero sharps, zero flats. Pure white keys. G major — one sharp (F#). F major — one flat (B♭).

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

title: Reading in G and F
subtitle: Same shapes, new colors
goal: Read and play melodies fluently in C, G, and F major — recognizing the key signature at a glance.

pages.figure: Three staves stacked: one in C, one in G, one in F. Same melody shape on all three.
pages.cleffy: Same melody. Three keys. The shape doesn't change. Just the colors of a few notes.

pages.figure: Three staves with clear key signatures: blank (C), one sharp (G), one flat (F).
pages.cleffy: First thing to read — the key signature. No sharps/flats: C major. One sharp (on the F line): G major. One flat (on the B line): F major.

pages.figure: Staff with one sharp.
pages.cleffy: Which key?

pages.figure: Staff with one flat.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Staff with no sharps or flats.
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Three staves with the melody C-D-E-D-C in C, G-A-B-A-G in G, F-G-A-G-F in F.
pages.cleffy: Same shape. Step up step up, step down step down. Starts on different notes — C, G, or F. Sonata method in action: you read shape, not specific pitches.

pages.figure: Melody in C. C D E D C.
pages.cleffy: Play it in C.

pages.figure: Same melody in G. G A B A G.
pages.cleffy: Now in G.

pages.figure: Same melody in F. F G A G F.
pages.cleffy: And in F.

pages.figure: Melody in G major that uses F# somewhere. G F# E D C.
pages.cleffy: Sometimes G-major melodies have F in them. Then the sharp matters. Play G F-sharp E D C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G F# E D C.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Watch the F.

pages.figure: Melody in F major that uses B♭. F A Bb A F.
pages.cleffy: F-major melody with B-flat. F A B-flat A F.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 69. Three staves stacked with C, G, F major key signatures side by side.

Same shape in G major: G-A-B-A-G.
Same in F: F-G-A-G-F.

title: Amazing Grace
subtitle: Act 14 · A piece in F major
goal: Read and play Amazing Grace in F major, understanding how the key signature governs the piece.
piece_context: First piece in an F-major key signature. The melody happens to be pentatonic — no B's at all — but the key signature still rules the whole piece.

piece.title: Amazing Grace
piece.key_center: F major (melody is pentatonic — no B's appear)
piece.difficulty: elementary — F major, steps + some leaps

pages.figure: Sheet music with Amazing Grace title. F major key signature (B♭) visible.
pages.cleffy: Amazing Grace. Act 14 boss piece. First piece in an F-major key signature — the flat on B is baked in.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Amazing Grace played fully

pages.cleffy: Listen. In 3/4. Gentle tune. Fun fact — this melody never plays a B at all. But the key signature still tells us: if one showed up, it'd be flat.

pages.figure: Phrase 1: C F A F A G F D (a-maz-ing grace how sweet the sound).
pages.cleffy: Phrase 1. C F A F A G F D. Pickup C. Leap to F. Up to A. Step-step home toward D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F major position.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 1. Slow.

pages.figure: Phrase 2: C F A G F (that saved a wretch).
pages.cleffy: Phrase 2. C F A G F. Same shape, lands on F this time. Still no B — this tune is pentatonic.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase 2.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 2.

pages.figure: Phrase 3: A C5 A F (I once was lost). Notice: no B anywhere — yet the key signature still governs.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 3. A C A F. Leap up to high C, float down. The key signature says B's are flat — but this melody doesn't use B. The signature tells you what would happen if it did.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase 3.
pages.cleffy: Play. All white keys here — but we're still in F major.

pages.figure: Phrase 4 continued: G F D F A G F (but now I see). Resolves to F.
pages.cleffy: Final phrase. G F D F A G F. Comes home to F. Gentle landing.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 4.

pages.figure: Full Amazing Grace. Key signature B♭. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Full song, slow, with guides.

pages.figure: Same, no guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read and play.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 14 boss.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration — ACT 14 COMPLETE. Trophy 14/15.
pages.cleffy: Act 14 done. Four lessons left in Tier 2. Next — patterns. Scales, shapes, sequences. Then Für Elise.

real_piano: Play Amazing Grace from the page daily. Watch the key signature even though no B's appear — it still sets the tonal home.
Add phrase shape. Each line arches slightly.

title: Five-finger scales
subtitle: The building block of every scale
goal: Play five-finger scales in C, G, and F major smoothly, ascending and descending.

pages.figure: Keyboard with C-D-E-F-G highlighted — a 5-finger ladder.
pages.cleffy: Before full scales come five-finger scales. Just the first five notes. They're the skeleton of every scale, and your hand is already shaped for them.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C D E F G. Numbers 1-5.
pages.cleffy: C major 5-finger — C D E F G. All white keys. Thumb to pinky in C position.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C position.
pages.cleffy: Play C D E F G, then back G F E D C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G A B C D. Numbers 1-5.
pages.cleffy: G major 5-finger — G A B C D. Still all white keys in this 5-note range (F doesn't appear).

pages.figure: Keyboard. G position.
pages.cleffy: Play G A B C D and back.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F G A B♭ C. Fourth note is black.
pages.cleffy: F major 5-finger — F G A B-flat C. Fourth note is the black key. Ring finger plays B-flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F position with B♭ highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Play F G A B-flat C and back.

pages.figure: Three playback cards.

audio.A: C 5-finger
audio.B: G 5-finger

pages.cleffy: Listen to all three. Same shape. Different starting note. One has a black key.

pages.figure: Three keyboards side by side with C, G, F 5-finger scales highlighted. Same shape, different spots.
pages.cleffy: Notice — on the piano, F major's 5-finger has a black key in the middle. C and G major have all white keys (in their first 5 notes). Your hand shape is the same regardless.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two hands on C positions — RH on Middle C area, LH on bass C area.
pages.cleffy: Both hands on C major 5-finger. Together.

pages.figure: Both hands on G 5-finger.
pages.cleffy: Both hands on G 5-finger.

pages.figure: Grand staff with C 5-finger scale in both clefs.
pages.cleffy: Read both staves. Play both hands.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which scale?

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

title: Contour
subtitle: The shape of a melody
goal: Recognize the overall shape of a melody — up, down, arch, wave — at a glance.

pages.figure: Three short melodies drawn on staves. Above each: a wavy line showing the overall shape.
pages.cleffy: Zoom out. Forget the individual notes. Just look at the general shape the melody makes on the page. That's called contour.

pages.figure: Melody going up steadily, with an arrow up over it.
pages.cleffy: Rising contour. Every note higher than the last (or close).

pages.figure: Melody going down. Arrow pointing down.
pages.cleffy: Falling contour. Downhill.

pages.figure: Melody that goes up and comes back down. Arrow shaped like an arch.
pages.cleffy: Arch. Up, then down. Very common in classical melodies.

pages.figure: Melody that goes up-down-up-down. Wavy line above.
pages.cleffy: Wave. Wobbles around a central pitch.

pages.figure: A rising melody on staff.
pages.cleffy: Which contour?

pages.figure: An arched melody.
pages.cleffy: This?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Identify the contour.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Rising melody
audio.B: Falling melody

pages.cleffy: Ear can hear contour too. Listen to each type.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Which contour?

pages.figure: Ode to Joy first phrase with the contour drawn above — it's a small arch.
pages.cleffy: Ode to Joy's first phrase is an arch. Starts on E, goes up to G, comes back to D.

pages.figure: Amazing Grace phrase 1 with arch over it.
pages.cleffy: Amazing Grace phrase 1: F A C C A C A F. Also an arch — up to C, back to F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Play an ascending then descending 5-finger scale — that's an arch.
pages.cleffy: Play an arch. Up the five-finger scale, then back.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 72. Four contour shapes drawn.
pages.cleffy: Four shapes. Now you read the BIG picture of a melody first. Tomorrow — sequences. Same shape, moved.

real_piano: Look at any piece you know. Trace the contour of each phrase. Rising, falling, arched, or wavy?
Pick one phrase and play it while thinking about its contour.

questions.figure: Rising melody
questions.prompt: Which contour?

questions.figure: Arch melody
questions.prompt: Which contour?

questions.audio: Falling melody
questions.prompt: Which contour?
Play a rising contour — any five notes going up.
questions.accept: 5 ascending notes

remediation.title: Contour, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four shapes. Slow with visuals.

completion.cleffy: Contour recognized. You read melodies at the shape level.

title: Sequences
subtitle: Same shape, moved
goal: Recognize a sequence — a musical pattern repeated at a different pitch — and play it.

pages.figure: Staff with two tiny melodies. Second one is identical to the first but starts a step higher. Both highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Look at this. Same shape. Played twice. Second time, it starts a step higher. That's a sequence.

pages.figure: Staff: 3-note pattern (C-D-E), then same pattern starting on D (D-E-F).
pages.cleffy: A sequence is when you take a short pattern — usually 3 to 5 notes — and repeat it at a different pitch.

pages.figure: C-D-E, then D-E-F, then E-F-G. Three repeats, each starting a step higher.
pages.cleffy: Three times. Each starts a step higher. That's a step sequence — very common in classical music.

pages.figure: C-E-G, then D-F-A, then E-G-B. Skip sequence.
pages.cleffy: Or the pattern itself could be skips. Same idea.

pages.figure: A long melody that's really just a 3-note sequence repeated several times.
pages.cleffy: Composers love sequences. They sound satisfying AND they're cheap to write. And for you — once you recognize one, you read faster. Same shape, just new starting note.

pages.figure: Staff with a sequence (C-D-E, D-E-F, E-F-G) written in full.
pages.cleffy: Is this a sequence?

pages.figure: Staff with a random melody that ISN'T a sequence.
pages.cleffy: And this?

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Step sequence (C-D-E / D-E-F / E-F-G)

pages.cleffy: Listen to a step sequence. The pattern repeats, walking upward.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three repeats.
pages.cleffy: Play it. C-D-E, D-E-F, E-F-G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G, D-F-A, E-G-B.
pages.cleffy: Skip sequence. C-E-G, then D-F-A, then E-G-B.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Sequence going DOWN. E-D-C, D-C-B, C-B-A.
pages.cleffy: Descending sequence. Same shape, each repeat a step lower.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four by ear. Is it a sequence?

pages.figure: Canon in D opening pattern — a sequence.
pages.cleffy: Canon in D, Pachelbel — the famous wedding piece — is mostly sequences. You'll play a simplified version in Tier 3.

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 73. Short pattern repeated at three pitches.
pages.cleffy: Sequences. When you see them, reading gets faster. Tomorrow — cadences. How phrases end.

real_piano: Play a step sequence: C-D-E, D-E-F, E-F-G.
Play a skip sequence: C-E-G, D-F-A, E-G-B.
Make up your own 3-note pattern. Play it 3 times, each starting a step higher.

questions.figure: Sequence of 3-note patterns
questions.prompt: Is this a sequence?

questions.audio: Step sequence
questions.prompt: Is this a sequence?
Play a step sequence starting on C. (Pattern: 3 ascending notes, repeated a step up.)
questions.accept: CDE DEF EFG

remediation.title: Sequences, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Repeat patterns. Slow.

completion.cleffy: Sequences recognized. Pattern-reading opens up.

title: Simple cadences
subtitle: How phrases end
goal: Hear and recognize when a musical phrase feels like it's 'landing' — ending.

pages.figure: Staff with a phrase. Last note glowing. Label: THIS FEELS LIKE AN ENDING.
pages.cleffy: Phrases have endings. Some feel final, some feel like a comma. That's called a cadence. You already feel them. Today we name them.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Phrase ending on home note (C in C major)
audio.B: Phrase ending on a note that feels unresolved (G in C major, hanging)

pages.cleffy: First: phrase ends on C — feels finished. Second: phrase ends on G — feels like it wants to keep going. Same notes, different ending note. Totally different feeling.

pages.figure: Staff in C. A melody ending on C, labeled HOME.
pages.cleffy: The "home" note of a key is the key's letter. In C major — C feels like home. In G major, G. In F major, F. When a melody lands on home, it feels final.

pages.figure: Melody ending on G in C major.
pages.cleffy: When a melody lands on a different note — it feels unfinished. Like a question.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Does the phrase feel finished or unfinished?

pages.figure: Staff with two phrases. First ends on a non-home note (unfinished). Second ends on home (finished).
pages.cleffy: Composers often write phrases in pairs. First phrase unfinished (question). Second phrase finished (answer).

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-D-E-F (ends on F, feels unresolved).
pages.cleffy: Play C-D-E-F. Ends on F — not home. Feels hanging.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F-E-D-C (ends on home C).
pages.cleffy: Now F-E-D-C. Ends on C. Home. Finished.

pages.figure: Staff with both phrases in sequence.
pages.cleffy: Play both. Question (unfinished), then answer (finished).

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Question or answer?

pages.figure: Ode to Joy last phrase ending on C.
pages.cleffy: Ode to Joy ends on C. Because it's in C major. Home note. Feels final.

pages.figure: Amazing Grace ending on F.
pages.cleffy: Amazing Grace ends on F. Because it's in F major. Same idea. Different key. Same feeling of "finished."

pages.figure: Play button. A phrase ends on home. Which key?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. A phrase plays and ends on the home note. What key is it in?

pages.figure: Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Mixed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 74. Two phrases drawn — one ending hanging, one ending on home.
pages.cleffy: Phrases end. Some feel final, some feel open. Tomorrow — the big one. Tier 2 boss. Für Elise.

real_piano: Play any melody you know. Notice where the phrases feel finished vs where they feel open.
Play C-D-E-F (hanging). Then F-E-D-C (resolving). Feel the difference.

questions.figure: Phrase in C major ending on C
questions.prompt: Does this feel finished or unfinished?

questions.audio: Phrase ending on home note
questions.prompt: Finished or unfinished?
Play a 4-note phrase in C major that ENDS on C.
questions.accept: 4 notes ending on C4

remediation.title: Cadences, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Finished vs unfinished. Slow listen.

completion.cleffy: Cadences felt. One lesson left in Tier 2.

title: Für Elise (theme)
subtitle: Tier 2 · The finish line
goal: Read and play the opening theme of Für Elise — the end-of-Tier-2 piece.
piece_context: Beethoven's Für Elise. One of the most recognized piano pieces in history. You play its opening theme. Tier 2 complete.

piece.title: Für Elise (opening theme)
piece.difficulty: elementary — mostly steps, one sharp, famous tune

pages.figure: Cleffy at the top of a pyramid (Tier 2 complete). The famous Für Elise opening shown on a staff.
pages.cleffy: End of Tier 2. Fifty lessons in. Today you play Für Elise. Most people never learn it. You will.

pages.figure: Full opening theme of Für Elise written on staff. Just the first 8-16 bars.
pages.cleffy: Here's the opening. Probably the most recognized piano melody ever written. Mostly steps. One specific pattern — E, D-sharp, E, D-sharp, E... — repeats.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: Für Elise theme, RH only, played cleanly

pages.cleffy: Listen. You've heard this before. Now you play it.

pages.figure: Staff showing the signature E, D#, E, D#, E pattern.
pages.cleffy: The core move. E. D-sharp. E. D-sharp. E. That's the pattern that makes Für Elise Für Elise. D-sharp is the black key just below E. Step down, step up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 and D#4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play it. E, D-sharp, E, D-sharp, E. Middle finger, pointer, middle, pointer, middle.

pages.figure: Full first phrase: E D# E D# E B D C A.
pages.cleffy: First phrase. E D# E D# E B D C A. After the E-D# pattern, the notes descend: B, D, C, A. Lands on A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full phrase.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 1. Slow.

pages.figure: Phrase 2: C E A B (with a small LH accompaniment pattern, simplified to single notes for now).
pages.cleffy: Phrase 2. C E A B. Going back up from A to B.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase 2.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 2.

pages.figure: Phrase 3: E D# E D# E B D C A (same as phrase 1).
pages.cleffy: Phrase 3 is identical to phrase 1. Für Elise loves returning to its core move.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Same as phrase 1. Play it.

pages.figure: Phrase 4: C E A (short resolution).
pages.cleffy: Phrase 4 closes the theme. C, E, A. Lands on A — home note of A minor.

pages.figure: Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play phrase 4.

pages.figure: Full Für Elise theme. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Full theme, slow, with guides.

pages.figure: No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read, you play.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. You just played Für Elise. Fifty lessons ago, you didn't know what a staff was.

pages.figure: Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: At tempo. 80 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner — Tier 2 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. Tier boss is a bigger test — take your time.

pages.figure: A pyramid showing Tier 1 (gold) and Tier 2 (gold). Cleffy at the top of Tier 2.
pages.cleffy: Look back. Tier 1: you learned to read one staff, play one hand. Tier 2: both clefs. Both hands. Full method — step, skip, leap. Dynamics. Accidentals. Three keys. Real pieces. Seven pieces in your hands now: Hot Cross Buns, Happy Birthday, Merrily We Roll Along, Mary Had a Little Lamb, Ode to Joy, Jingle Bells, Amazing Grace — and now the opening of Für Elise.

pages.figure: Pyramid preview with Tier 3 highlighted. Minuet in G, Clair de Lune mentioned.
pages.cleffy: Tier 3 is articulation (how you touch keys), fingering, chords, and sight-reading. Pieces: Minuet in G, Canon in D, Gymnopédie, Clair de Lune opening. You don't have to do Tier 3 today. Take a break. You earned it.

pages.figure: Celebration — TIER 2 COMPLETE 👑. Big golden trophy. Cleffy holding Für Elise sheet music.
pages.cleffy: Tier 2. Complete. You play piano. You read sheet music. You use a method most adult self-teachers never learn. That's real. Next tier opens when you're ready. I'll be here.

real_piano: Für Elise once a day. Slow at first. Build up to tempo.
Play it for someone in your life. Tell them it's Beethoven.
If you have time — revisit Hot Cross Buns and Happy Birthday. Notice how easy they feel now.
virtual_only: Replay pages 14-16 until the theme feels automatic

questions.figure: Core pattern E-D#-E
questions.prompt: What's the move between E and D#?

questions.figure: Für Elise phrase 1 ending on A
questions.prompt: Does the phrase end on home note?
options: Yes (A is home in A minor)

questions.figure: D# on keyboard
questions.prompt: D-sharp is the black key...

questions.audio: Für Elise opening
questions.prompt: Do you recognize this tune?

questions.audio: Core E-D#-E pattern
questions.prompt: Does this move include a black key?
Play the first 5 notes of Für Elise: E, D#, E, D#, E.
questions.accept: E4 D#4 E4 D#4 E4

remediation.title: Für Elise, phrase by phrase
remediation.opening_cleffy: Tier boss — big one. Let's break the theme into four phrases and rebuild very slowly.

completion.cleffy: Tier 2, sealed. You play piano. Real piano. Real pieces. Real reading. Fifty lessons of work. Take a breath. Then Tier 3 whenever you're ready.

title: Legato vs staccato
subtitle: Act 16 · Articulation
goal: Hear and play the difference between smooth-connected notes and short-detached notes.
piece_context: Tier 3 begins. You know how to read. Now we add craft. First up — how you touch the keys.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up two hands — one smooth and flowing, one with fingers popping up. Title: THE TOUCH FAMILY.
pages.cleffy: New tier. New chapter. You can read. Now — how you touch the keys. Same notes can sound smooth or short. That's articulation.

pages.figure: Staff with four quarter notes connected by a curved line above them. Label: LEGATO.
pages.cleffy: First member of the touch family — legato. Smooth. Connected. One note flows into the next. No gap between the sound of one note and the next.

pages.figure: Staff with four quarter notes, each with a small dot above it. Label: STACCATO.
pages.cleffy: Second member — staccato. Short. Detached. Each note finishes before the next starts. The dot above the note is the sign.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: legato. B: staccato.

audio.A: C-D-E-F played smooth and connected
audio.B: C-D-E-F played short and bouncy

pages.cleffy: Same four notes. Two different touches. First — legato. Smooth. Second — staccato. Short. Hear the space between the notes in the second one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 D4 E4 F4 glowing. A curved slur above them.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E, F — legato. Smooth. Stay down on C until D presses. Stay on D until E. Don't rush. No gaps.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 D4 E4 F4 with dots above each.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Staccato this time. Touch. Release. Touch. Release. Short. Bouncy.

pages.figure: Cartoon hand — finger 2 pressing D while finger 1 still on C. Overlap highlighted.
pages.cleffy: The trick for legato — the old finger stays down until the new finger presses. Tiny overlap. No silence between notes.

pages.figure: Cartoon hand — finger bounces off the key. Small arrow up.
pages.cleffy: The trick for staccato — finger bounces. Press and release fast. The note is halved — half sound, half silence.

pages.figure: Staff with a slur over five notes. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Five notes, legato. G, A, B, C, D. Smooth.

pages.figure: Staff with five notes, each dotted. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Same five notes. All staccato. Pop each one off.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Legato or staccato?

pages.figure: Staff: three notes with a slur (legato), then three notes with dots (staccato).
pages.cleffy: Now both in one phrase. First three legato. Last three staccato. Smooth, smooth, smooth — pop, pop, pop.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 76. Two pictures: flowing ribbon (legato) and bouncing ball (staccato).
pages.cleffy: Two touches. Legato — smooth. Staccato — short. Tomorrow — the line that tells you to play legato. Slurs.

real_piano: Play C D E F G A. Once legato (smooth). Once staccato (short). Feel the difference in your fingers.
Play any melody you know. First all legato. Then all staccato. The tune changes character.

questions.figure: Staff with four notes under a curved line
questions.prompt: How should these notes be played?

questions.figure: Staff with four notes, each with a dot above it
questions.prompt: How should these notes be played?

questions.audio: Four notes played short and bouncy
questions.prompt: Legato or staccato?
Play C, D, E, F legato — smooth, no gaps.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 played connected

remediation.title: Legato and staccato, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: New touch. Takes a minute. We'll slow it down — one note at a time, legato first, then staccato.

completion.cleffy: Tier 3 is open. First touch unlocked. Tomorrow — slurs. The curved line that makes legato official.

title: Slurs
subtitle: Act 16 · Articulation
goal: Read a slur on the staff and play the notes underneath it smoothly connected.
piece_context: Yesterday you played legato. Today you meet the symbol that asks for it.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a curved line arching over three notes on a staff.
pages.cleffy: That curved line is a slur. It means — connect these notes. Play them legato.

pages.figure: Staff with five notes. A curved line arches from the first to the last. Label: SLUR.
pages.cleffy: A slur starts on one note and ends on another. Every note under the arc is played smoothly, as one group. One breath. No gaps.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: four notes, no slur. Right: same four notes, with a slur.
pages.cleffy: Same notes, two ways. No slur — play how you like. With a slur — connect them. Smooth.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: slurred. B: not slurred.

audio.A: C D E F G played connected (slurred)
audio.B: C D E F G played with tiny gaps between each note

pages.cleffy: First — slurred. Smooth line. One gesture. Second — detached. Five little gestures.

pages.figure: Staff with three notes and a slur above them. Keyboard below: C4 D4 E4.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E — under one slur. Smooth. Finger 1 stays down until 2 presses. Finger 2 stays until 3. One breath.

pages.figure: Staff with a slur that arches high. Arrow showing it can curve up or down.
pages.cleffy: Slurs live above or below the notes. Same meaning either way. Above for lower notes. Below for higher ones. Just what fits.

pages.figure: Staff with a six-note slur over F G A B C D.
pages.cleffy: Six-note slur. Every note connected. Don't breathe between them.

pages.figure: Staff with two separate slurs — one over notes 1-3, another over notes 4-6. Small gap between.
pages.cleffy: Two slurs next to each other. Two groups. Smooth inside each group. Tiny lift between them. That lift is where the phrase breathes.

pages.figure: Staff with two three-note slurs. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play the first group smooth. Lift. Play the second group smooth.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: slur over C-D-E (three different notes). Right: tie between two Cs (same note).
pages.cleffy: Remember ties from Act 11? Ties and slurs look alike. Tie — same note, held longer. Slur — different notes, connected smooth. The notes tell you which.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Slurred or separated?

pages.figure: Staff with a short melody. First four notes slurred, last note separate.
pages.cleffy: Melody with a slur. Four notes smooth, one note on its own.

pages.figure: Same melody, tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: One more time, at tempo. Keep the slur smooth.
pages.followup_cleffy: The slur is where the music breathes.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 77. A curved line arching over three notes, like a little bridge.
pages.cleffy: Slur means connect. Tomorrow — the mark that says play THIS note louder.

real_piano: Play a C-D-E-F-G scale, all slurred. Then play it with two slurs (C-D-E, F-G). Hear the lift.
Find a piece of music around the house. Count the slurs. Notice where the music breathes.

questions.figure: Curved line arching over four different notes
questions.prompt: What does this mark mean?

questions.figure: Two separate slurs over six notes
questions.prompt: How many smooth groups?

questions.audio: Five notes played connected
questions.prompt: Are these notes slurred?
Play C, D, E, F under a slur. Keep them smooth.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 connected

remediation.title: Slurs, phrase by phrase
remediation.opening_cleffy: Slurs take a minute to feel natural. We'll do three-note slurs, then longer, until the connection is reliable.

completion.cleffy: Slurs read. Slurs played. Tomorrow — one note louder than the rest. Accent marks.

title: Accent marks
subtitle: Act 16 · Articulation
goal: Read and play an accent — one note leaned into, louder than the notes around it.
piece_context: Legato, slurs, now emphasis. The touch family keeps growing.

pages.figure: Staff with five even notes. The third one has a sideways wedge (>) above it. The wedge is highlighted.
pages.cleffy: See that sideways wedge? That's an accent. It means — play THIS one louder.

pages.figure: Zoom-in on a quarter note with a > above it. Label: ACCENT. The > points right.
pages.cleffy: Shaped like a greater-than sign. Points right. Over or under a note — doesn't matter. Same meaning. Lean into the note. A little extra weight.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Four even notes, same volume
audio.B: Four notes, the third one louder (accented)

pages.cleffy: First — all even. Second — same notes. But number three is louder. That's an accent.

pages.figure: Cartoon finger pressing a key harder. Tiny burst around the key.
pages.cleffy: Accent is in your finger. Press a bit harder on that one note. Not banging. Just — more weight. Lean in.

pages.figure: Staff: C D E F. Accent on E. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E, F. Lean on E. Regular, regular, LOUDER, regular.

pages.figure: Staff: G A B C. Accent on G.
pages.cleffy: Same idea, accent on the first note. LOUDER, soft, soft, soft.

pages.figure: Four notes in a row. Accents on beats 1 and 3. Arrows below marking the pulse.
pages.cleffy: Accents create pulse. A strong beat followed by weaker ones. It's how music walks. And dances. And marches.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which note is accented — 1, 2, 3, or 4?

pages.figure: Staff: C D E F G A. Accents on C and G. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Six notes. Accents on 1 and 4. Lean-soft-soft-lean-soft-soft.

pages.figure: Staff with a slur over four notes. An accent on the first note only.
pages.cleffy: Accents work with other marks. Slur plus accent — lean into the first note, then smooth the rest.

pages.figure: Keyboard with four notes. Accent on first, slur over all.
pages.cleffy: LEAN on the C. Then smooth through D, E, F.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. A short phrase plays.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Does the phrase have accents or not?

pages.figure: Short phrase with two accents. Tempo 90.
pages.cleffy: Play it at tempo. Accents stay clear.
pages.followup_cleffy: An accent is a finger that cares.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 78. A wedge > glowing over a single note.
pages.cleffy: Accent means lean. One note louder than the rest. Tomorrow — two more touches. Tenuto and marcato.

real_piano: Play a scale. Put an accent on every third note. Feel the pulse.
Play Ode to Joy. Try accenting the first note of each phrase. The piece shapes itself.

questions.figure: Note with a > above it
questions.prompt: What does this mark mean?

questions.figure: Four notes, accent on the second
questions.prompt: Which note is loudest?

questions.audio: Four notes, accent on the last
questions.prompt: Which note is accented?
Play C, D, E, F with an accent on E.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 with E louder

remediation.title: Accents, one note at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Accents can feel like banging. We'll slow down and find "lean" instead of "hit."

completion.cleffy: Accent read. Accent played. Tomorrow — two more. Tenuto for held. Marcato for firm.

title: Tenuto and marcato
subtitle: Act 16 · Articulation
goal: Read and play tenuto (held full value) and marcato (firmly marked, stronger than accent).
piece_context: Two more touches. Then the boss piece — Minuet in G. Articulation in real music.

pages.figure: Staff with two notes. First has a short horizontal line above it. Second has a wedge pointing up (^).
pages.cleffy: Two new marks today. The dash — tenuto. The upward wedge — marcato.

pages.figure: Zoom on a quarter note with a horizontal dash above it. Label: TENUTO.
pages.cleffy: Tenuto. A flat dash above the note. Means — hold this note its full value. Don't shorten it. A little weight, but mostly — stay on it.

pages.figure: Side by side. Left: dash above note (tenuto). Right: dot above note (staccato).
pages.cleffy: Opposite of staccato. Staccato — shorten. Tenuto — hold its full length. Same-looking marks, opposite meanings. The dash is flat. The dot is round.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Four quarter notes at normal length
audio.B: Four quarter notes, each held slightly longer and rounder — tenuto

pages.cleffy: First — normal. Second — tenuto. Each note sits on the keys a little more. Feels heavier. Feels more important.

pages.figure: Staff: C D E F, each with a tenuto dash. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E, F — tenuto. Sit on each note. Don't rush off it. Press with a little weight.

pages.figure: Zoom on a quarter note with an upward wedge (^) above it. Label: MARCATO.
pages.cleffy: Marcato. An upward wedge above the note. Means — firmly marked. Stronger than an accent. Louder AND a little short.

pages.figure: Side by side. Left: > accent. Right: ^ marcato.
pages.cleffy: Accent is a lean. Marcato is a punch. Both louder than the notes around them. Marcato more. Think — big announcement.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Four notes, accent on the 3rd (>)
audio.B: Four notes, marcato on the 3rd (^)

pages.cleffy: Accent. Now marcato. Feel the extra weight on marcato? Bigger. More committed.

pages.figure: Staff: G A B C. Marcato on the first G.
pages.cleffy: Play G, A, B, C. Marcato on G. Strong, firm, committed. Then the rest normal.

pages.figure: A chart of the touch family. Legato (slur curve). Staccato (dot). Tenuto (dash). Accent (>). Marcato (^).
pages.cleffy: Touch family so far — Slur for smooth. Dot for short. Dash for held. Wedge-right for lean. Wedge-up for punch. Five touches. Every one changes how the note feels.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which articulation is it — staccato, tenuto, or marcato?

pages.figure: Staff: six notes. Mixed marks — tenuto, tenuto, marcato, staccato, staccato, tenuto.
pages.cleffy: Six notes, every touch different. Held. Held. PUNCH. Short. Short. Held.

pages.figure: Mixed articulation phrase. Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: At tempo. Marks stay clear.
pages.followup_cleffy: Five touches. You have them all.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 79. The touch family chart with all five members lit up.
pages.cleffy: The touch family is complete. Tomorrow — you use them. Real piece. Real Baroque. Minuet in G.

real_piano: Play a C scale up and back. First all tenuto. Then all marcato. Then mixed.
Find a piece of music. Scan for dashes, wedges, dots, slurs, accents. Name each one.

questions.figure: Note with a flat dash above it
questions.prompt: What does this mark mean?

questions.figure: Note with an upward wedge ^
questions.prompt: What does this mark mean?

questions.figure: Side by side: > and ^
questions.prompt: Which one is louder and heavier?

questions.audio: A note played tenuto — full value, with weight
questions.prompt: Tenuto or staccato?
Play G, A, B, C with marcato on the G.
questions.accept: G4 A4 B4 C5 with G punched

remediation.title: Two more touches, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Tenuto and marcato are subtle differences from what you already know. We'll isolate each one, just a few notes at a time.

completion.cleffy: Five touches read. Five touches played. Tomorrow — Act 16 boss. Minuet in G. You put the whole family to work.

title: Minuet in G (articulation)
subtitle: Act 16 · Your first Baroque piece
goal: Play the opening of Minuet in G using legato and staccato — feel the touch of a real Baroque piece.
piece_context: Petzold's Minuet in G — long attributed to Bach. One of the most recognized Baroque melodies. Today you play its opening and use articulation to shape it.

piece.title: Minuet in G
piece.composer: Petzold (long attributed to Bach, BWV Anh. 114)
piece.notes_rh: D5 G4 A4 B4 C5 D5 | G4 G4
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — articulation focus, mostly stepwise, one sharp (F#)

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a Baroque-era scroll that says Minuet in G. A small wig on top of the scroll.
pages.cleffy: Act 16 finish line. Today — a real Baroque piece. Minuet in G. Around 250 years old. Still played everywhere.

pages.figure: Portrait of Christian Petzold beside a portrait of Bach. Note underneath: 'Written by Petzold, long believed to be Bach.'
pages.cleffy: For 250 years, everyone thought Bach wrote this. Turns out it was Christian Petzold, a friend of Bach's. Bach's wife liked it enough to copy it into her family notebook. Either way — it's gorgeous. And today, yours.

pages.figure: Big play button. Sheet music preview of the opening.

audio.A: Minuet in G, opening phrase, played cleanly with articulation

pages.cleffy: Listen. Focus on how the notes feel. Some connect. Some bounce. That shape — that's articulation doing its job.

pages.figure: Treble staff with the opening melody: D5 | G4 A4 B4 C5 | D5 G4 G4. Key signature with one sharp (F#). 3/4 time.
pages.cleffy: Here it is. G major — one sharp, F-sharp. Three beats per bar. That's 3/4 — the minuet dance. It starts high on D5. Then steps its way back up from G.

pages.figure: Same staff, now with articulation marks. D5 marcato-ish (strong downbeat). G4 A4 B4 C5 slurred (legato). D5 staccato. G4 G4 tenuto.
pages.cleffy: The touch plan. Opening D — strong. The climb G-A-B-C — legato, one breath. High D — short, sparkling. Final two Gs — held, settled. Five different feelings in eight notes. That's craft.

pages.figure: Keyboard with D5 glowing. RH finger 5.
pages.cleffy: Opening note alone. D5. Your right pinky. Press it with weight. This is how the piece announces itself.

pages.figure: Keyboard with G4 A4 B4 C5 glowing. Slur above. RH fingers 1-2-3-4.
pages.cleffy: The climb. G, A, B, C. Slur it. Smooth. Each finger stays until the next presses. Fingers 1, 2, 3, 4.

pages.figure: Keyboard with D5 glowing. Small dot above. RH finger 5.
pages.cleffy: Back to high D. Staccato this time. Short. Bouncy. Pinky touches and lifts.

pages.figure: Keyboard with G4 glowing twice. Tenuto dashes above each. RH finger 1.
pages.cleffy: Closing. Two Gs, tenuto. Thumb on G, press, hold. Release. Press, hold. Settled. Calm. The phrase lands.

pages.figure: Full treble staff with all articulation marks. Tempo 60. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Whole opening, slow, with guides. D. Climb smooth. High D short. Two Gs held.

pages.figure: Same staff. No guides. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Again. No highlights. You read the marks. You shape the touch.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Eight notes. Five touches. Real Baroque music. What you just did is what a pianist does.

pages.figure: Same staff. Tempo 100 — minuet feel.
pages.cleffy: At tempo. 100 bpm. Minuet feel. The articulation is where the music lives now, not in the notes.

pages.figure: Same notes, two versions. Top: all legato. Bottom: with full articulation plan.
pages.cleffy: One more — same notes, all legato this time. Then you'll hear why the articulation version sounds so much more alive.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 16 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time — act boss.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 16 COMPLETE 🎯. A pyramid with Acts 1-15 gold and Act 16 glowing. Cleffy holding the Petzold scroll and tipping a tiny wig.
pages.cleffy: Act 16, done. Two weeks ago, you finished Für Elise and thought that was the peak. Today you played a Baroque piece with five different touches in eight notes. That's craft. That's Tier 3. Tomorrow — fingering. The hidden choreography under every piece you'll play from here on out.

real_piano: Play the Minuet in G opening once a day. Slow first. Then at minuet tempo.
Try playing it all legato one time. Then with the full articulation plan. Hear what changes.
Find someone and play it for them. Tell them it's 250 years old.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 11, and 12 three times this week.

questions.figure: Opening of Minuet in G with articulation marks visible
questions.prompt: The climb G-A-B-C is under a slur. How do you play it?

questions.figure: Key signature with one sharp
questions.prompt: What key is Minuet in G in?

questions.figure: Time signature 3/4
questions.prompt: How many beats per bar?

questions.audio: Opening of Minuet in G played with articulation
questions.prompt: Are all the notes played the same length?
Play the Minuet opening: D5, G4, A4, B4, C5, D5, G4, G4 — with the climb legato.
questions.accept: All 8 notes in order, climb connected

remediation.title: Minuet in G, one touch at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. First Baroque piece. Lot going on. We'll rebuild it one touch at a time — legato climb, then staccato D, then the tenuto ending. When each piece is reliable, we put them back together.

completion.cleffy: Act 16, sealed. The touch family — yours. Baroque music — started. Tomorrow, fingering. The choreography under everything.

title: Why fingering matters
subtitle: Act 17 · Fingering
goal: Understand that which finger plays which key is a plan, not an accident.
piece_context: Act 17 is about your hand. Not the notes — your hand. What fingers land where, and why it matters.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing to a hand hovering over a keyboard. Tiny numbers 1-5 floating above the fingers.
pages.cleffy: New act. New idea. Not which notes. Which fingers. Every note you play is played by a specific finger. That choice matters.

pages.figure: Staff with C-D-E-F-G. Finger numbers 1-2-3-4-5 written above each note.
pages.cleffy: Look. Five notes. Five fingers. One finger per note. That's a fingering. A plan for the hand.

pages.figure: Dancer silhouette next to a hand silhouette. Both have numbered positions.
pages.cleffy: Think of it as choreography. Dancers don't decide mid-move which foot lands where. Neither do pianists. The hand has a plan before the first note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-D-E-F-G glowing. Prompt: play all five notes using ONLY finger 2.
pages.cleffy: Try this. Play C-D-E-F-G using only your pointer — finger 2. Move your whole hand for each note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same notes. This time fingers 1-2-3-4-5 labeled.
pages.cleffy: Now again. One finger per note. Thumb on C, pointer on D, middle on E, ring on F, pinky on G. Hand stays still. Fingers do the work.
pages.followup_cleffy: Feel the difference? Same notes. Way smoother with a plan.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: hand scrambling. Right: hand calm, fingers falling in order.
pages.cleffy: Good fingering means the hand doesn't scramble. It means you can play faster. Smoother. With less effort. Bad fingering means you stumble over your own hand.

pages.figure: Staff with notes and small numbers 1, 3, 5 printed above. Composer fingerings.
pages.cleffy: Composers and editors write fingerings above the notes. Small numbers. 1 is thumb. 5 is pinky. Same for both hands — always counted from the thumb.

pages.figure: Staff with C-E-G. Finger numbers 1-3-5 above each note. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play these three notes using the fingerings written. Thumb on C. Middle on E. Pinky on G.

pages.figure: Cleffy sketching on paper. A line of notes. Arrows pointing to each. Finger number above each arrow.
pages.cleffy: Before you play something new — look at it. Plan the fingers. Start. End. And the tricky spots in the middle.

pages.figure: Staff: G-A-B-C-D (5 steps up from G). No finger numbers yet. Prompt: plan fingers, then play.
pages.cleffy: Five notes — G, A, B, C, D. Before you play: plan. What finger on G? Thumb. Then 2, 3, 4, 5. Now play.

pages.figure: Staff: D-C-B-A-G. Prompt: plan from the END backward.
pages.cleffy: Now backward. D-C-B-A-G. Plan from the last note. If G is the end, pinky should not be on D. Start pinky on D? No. Start with finger 5 on D, land on thumb for G. 5-4-3-2-1.

pages.figure: Banner — quick check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 81. A hand with finger numbers glowing, keys underneath.
pages.cleffy: Fingering is choreography. A plan for your hand. Tomorrow — the first big trick. Thumb-under. For when five fingers aren't enough.

real_piano: Play any five-note pattern you know. Before you play, name the finger on each note out loud.
Play C-D-E-F-G with the wrong fingering, then the right one. Feel the difference.

questions.figure: Note C with finger number 1 written above
questions.prompt: Which finger plays this note?

questions.figure: Staff C-D-E-F-G with fingerings 1-2-3-4-5
questions.prompt: How many different fingers play these notes?

questions.audio: Smooth five-note run vs choppy five-note run
questions.prompt: Which one sounds more planned?
Play C-D-E-F-G using one finger per note, thumb on C.
questions.accept: Five notes ascending, hand still

remediation.title: Fingering, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Choreography for the hand. Let's slow down. One finger at a time.

completion.cleffy: Fingering makes sense now. A plan, not an accident. Tomorrow: thumb-under. The first hand trick.

title: Thumb-under
subtitle: Act 17 · Fingering
goal: Pass the thumb under the hand to continue a scale past five fingers.
piece_context: Five fingers. Eight notes in a scale. Something has to give. Thumb-under is the fix.

pages.figure: Staff: C major scale C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C ascending. Hand hovering, finger numbers visible.
pages.cleffy: A scale has eight notes. Your hand has five fingers. You see the problem. Today, the fix. Thumb-under.

pages.figure: Hand at C position with fingers 1-2-3-4-5 on C-D-E-F-G. Notes F and A glowing above G with a question mark.
pages.cleffy: Thumb on C. Pinky on G. That's five notes. The scale wants to keep going — A, B, C. But your hand is out of fingers.

pages.figure: Animated hand diagram. Thumb passing UNDER fingers 2 and 3. Arrow showing it reaching to F.
pages.cleffy: The trick: the thumb sneaks under the hand. While middle finger holds E, the thumb slides under and lands on F. Hand keeps moving up. No gap.

pages.figure: Hand drawn as an arch — fingers 2-3-4 forming a bridge. Thumb ducking under like a boat.
pages.cleffy: Under the bridge. Your fingers 2, 3, 4 are the bridge. The thumb is a small boat going under. Quiet. Smooth. No bump.

pages.figure: Play button. C major scale RH ascending.

audio.A: C major scale ascending, RH, smooth

pages.cleffy: Listen. C major scale up to high C. Smooth — no gap where the thumb moved. That's thumb-under, done right.

pages.figure: Staff: C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C with numbers 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5 above each note. The 3→1 boundary highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Here's the fingering for RH C major ascending. 1, 2, 3 — then thumb under to 1 — then 2, 3, 4, 5. The pass happens between E and F. That's the spot.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, D4, E4 glowing. Fingers 1-2-3.
pages.cleffy: Start small. Play C-D-E. Fingers 1, 2, 3. Hold E when you land.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 still held by middle finger. Thumb animating under to F4.
pages.cleffy: Now the pass. Hold E with finger 3. Slide your thumb under the hand. Land on F. Just those two — E, then F with the thumb.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full C major scale. Fingering 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5 labeled above each key.
pages.cleffy: Full scale up. C to high C. Slow. Fingers 1, 2, 3 — thumb under — 1, 2, 3, 4, 5.

pages.figure: Staff: C-B-A-G-F-E-D-C descending with fingers 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1. The 1→3 boundary highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Coming down is the mirror. Pinky on high C. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 — then middle finger reaches OVER the thumb to land on E — then 3, 2, 1. That's finger-over. Tomorrow's lesson.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full C major scale descending. Fingering 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1 labeled.
pages.cleffy: For now — just play down. Trust the fingering.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full scale up and back down. Guides on. Tempo 70.
pages.cleffy: Both directions. Up — thumb under between E and F. Down — finger crosses over between thumb and E.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Eight notes with five fingers. The scale is whole now.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full scale up and down. No guides. Tempo 90.
pages.cleffy: Faster. No guides.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 82. A hand arched like a bridge, thumb ducking under.
pages.cleffy: Thumb-under. Under the bridge. Tomorrow — the opposite move. Crossing over.

real_piano: C major scale, RH, slow. Up only. Focus on the thumb passing under between E and F.
Hold E with finger 3, then pass the thumb under and play F — just those two notes — ten times.
Whole scale up and down, slow. Don't rush the pass.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8-9 until the pass feels smooth

questions.figure: Staff C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C with fingerings
questions.prompt: Between which two notes does the thumb pass under?

questions.figure: Hand diagram — thumb underneath the other fingers
questions.prompt: What's this move called?

questions.audio: Smooth C major scale
questions.prompt: Can you hear where the thumb passed?
Play C-D-E-F ascending. Use thumb-under between E and F.
questions.accept: Four notes ascending with thumb landing on F

remediation.title: Thumb-under, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The pass takes practice. No shame. Slow motion helps.

completion.cleffy: Thumb passes under. Scales go beyond five fingers. Tomorrow: crossing over.

title: Crossing over
subtitle: Act 17 · Fingering
goal: Pass a longer finger over the thumb to continue a scale in the other direction.
piece_context: Thumb-under was for going up with the right hand. Crossing-over is the reverse — going down, or going up with the left hand.

pages.figure: Staff: C major scale descending, C5 down to C4. Hand hovering with fingers labeled.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday, thumb-under. Going up. Today, the reverse. Crossing over. Going down.

pages.figure: Hand at top of scale — pinky on high C. Fingers 5-4-3-2-1 descending to F. Arrow showing need to keep going.
pages.cleffy: RH descending. Pinky on high C. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 lands you on F. Still need to play E, D, C. Thumb's already used. Now what?

pages.figure: Hand diagram. Thumb holding F. Middle finger arching up and OVER the thumb to reach E.
pages.cleffy: The fix: middle finger reaches OVER the thumb. The thumb stays low — the longer finger crosses above it to the next key. Opposite direction from yesterday. Same idea.

pages.figure: Side view of a hand. Thumb low like a beam. Finger 3 arching over it like a leg stepping over a log.
pages.cleffy: Thumb-under was a boat under a bridge. Crossing-over is a leg stepping over a low log. Thumb stays. Finger goes over.

pages.figure: Play button. C major scale descending, RH.

audio.A: C major scale descending, RH, smooth

pages.cleffy: Listen. Scale coming down. No gap where the finger crossed.

pages.figure: Staff: C5-B4-A4-G4-F4-E4-D4-C4 with fingering 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1. The 1→3 boundary highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Fingering for RH C major descending. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 — cross 3 over — 3, 2, 1. The cross happens between F and E.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5-B4-A4-G4-F4 glowing. Fingers 5-4-3-2-1.
pages.cleffy: First five notes down. Pinky on high C. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1. Land on F with the thumb.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Thumb on F4. Middle finger crossing over to E4.
pages.cleffy: Now the cross. Thumb on F. Middle finger arches over, lands on E. Just those two. F with thumb, then E with finger 3.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full C major scale descending. Fingering 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1 labeled.
pages.cleffy: Full scale, top to bottom. Slow. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 — cross — 3, 2, 1.

pages.figure: Bass staff: C3-D3-E3-F3-G3-A3-B3-C4 ascending. Fingering 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1.
pages.cleffy: Left hand does the same thing — but going up. LH C major ascending: pinky on low C. 5, 4, 3, 2, 1 — then middle finger crosses over — 3, 2, 1. Cross happens between F and G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH C major scale ascending. Fingering 5-4-3-2-1-3-2-1.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, ascending. Pinky on low C. Cross 3 over the thumb between F and G.
pages.followup_cleffy: Same trick. Different hand. Different direction.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH C major scale up and down. Guides on. Tempo 75.
pages.cleffy: RH — up and back down. Thumb-under going up. Cross-over coming down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH scale up and down. No guides. Tempo 90.
pages.cleffy: Faster. No guides.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 83. A hand with finger 3 arching over the thumb.
pages.cleffy: Crossing over. Thumb stays low, finger steps over. Tomorrow — one more fingering trick. Same key, swap fingers.

real_piano: RH C major scale up and down. Focus on both moves — thumb-under going up, cross-over coming down.
LH C major scale up only. Focus on the cross between F and G.
Play the scale slow enough that you don't hear a gap at either crossing point.

questions.figure: Staff RH C major descending with fingering
questions.prompt: What finger crosses over the thumb in RH descending?

questions.figure: Hand with thumb low and finger 3 arching over
questions.prompt: What's this move?

questions.audio: Smooth descending C major scale
questions.prompt: Smooth or choppy?
Play C-B-A-G-F-E. Cross finger 3 over the thumb between F and E.
questions.accept: Six notes descending with cross-over executed

remediation.title: Crossing over, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Same idea as yesterday, flipped. Let's isolate the cross and slow it right down.

completion.cleffy: Cross-over, done. Scales flow both directions. Tomorrow: finger substitution. The quietest trick of all.

title: Finger substitution
subtitle: Act 17 · Fingering
goal: Swap fingers on a held key silently, so the hand can reposition mid-phrase.
piece_context: The third and quietest fingering trick. Same key stays down. Different finger takes over.

pages.figure: Close-up of a hand holding one key. Arrow showing finger 3 leaving the key as finger 1 takes over. The key never lifts.
pages.cleffy: One key. Two fingers. No re-striking. Today's trick is the sneakiest. Finger substitution.

pages.figure: Animated diagram. Finger 3 is pressing E4. Finger 1 slides in, takes over. Finger 3 releases. E4 never stops sounding.
pages.cleffy: Press a note with one finger. While it's held — slide another finger underneath. The new finger takes the weight. The old finger lifts off. The key never rises. The note keeps sounding. Nobody hears the swap.

pages.figure: Staff with a long held note, then a fast run beginning. Finger 3 on the held note, finger 1 waiting to take over before the run.
pages.cleffy: Why? Because sometimes you need a finger free. You're holding a note. A fast passage is coming up. The finger that's holding is the finger you need. Swap, then run.

pages.figure: Play button. A held note with no audible break.

audio.A: A single sustained E4, held for four seconds, with a substitution happening in the middle

pages.cleffy: Listen. One note held for four seconds. Somewhere in the middle, fingers swapped. You can't hear it. That's the point.

pages.figure: Side-view of a hand on a white key. Finger 3 pressing. Finger 1 arriving with the TIP touching the key just behind the other fingertip.
pages.cleffy: The mechanic. Finger 3 is pressing the key. Finger 1 arrives — tip touches the key next to the other finger. Weight shifts to finger 1. Finger 3 floats up. Key stays down the whole time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 glowing. Label: press with finger 3, then substitute to finger 1.
pages.cleffy: Try it. Press E with finger 3. Hold it. Now slide your thumb tip onto the same key, next to finger 3. Shift your weight to the thumb. Lift finger 3. Key should still be down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 held. Then sequence runs E4 → F4 → G4 with thumb-2-3 after substitution.
pages.cleffy: Now in context. Press E with finger 3. Substitute to thumb — silently. Now your thumb is on E, fingers 2-3-4-5 are free. Play E, F, G with 1-2-3.

pages.figure: Staff: long held C, then a phrase that needs the hand to shift position. Substitution marked with tiny 5→1.
pages.cleffy: Another use. You're holding a long note with your pinky. Substitute to the thumb. Now the hand can reach up the keyboard without letting the note go.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 glowing. Press with pinky, substitute to thumb, then reach up to C5.
pages.cleffy: Pinky on G. Hold it. Substitute — thumb slides onto G. Pinky lifts. Now your hand is shifted up. Play C5.

pages.figure: Staff snippet with a note and tiny numbers '3-1' above it, connected by a small hyphen or slash.
pages.cleffy: In sheet music, substitution is written as two numbers on one note. "3-1" means start with finger 3, end with finger 1. Same key. Two fingers. In order.

pages.figure: Staff: F4 marked 2-1, then a short phrase F-G-A-B with fingers 1-2-3-4.
pages.cleffy: Read it. F marked 2-1. Press F with pointer, substitute to thumb. Then play G-A-B with fingers 2-3-4. Your hand's already in position because you substituted.

pages.figure: Staff: E4 held for two beats with 3-1 substitution, then a scale run E-F-G-A-B-C up using thumb-under after.
pages.cleffy: Real use. Hold E with finger 3 — substitute to thumb. Then scale up E-F-G-A-B-C. With the thumb on E, fingers 2-3-4-5 and back to thumb are ready to roll.
pages.followup_cleffy: Silent swap. Smooth run. That's the whole trick.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 84. A held note with two fingers side by side on it, one lifting.
pages.cleffy: Substitution. Same key, two fingers, no break. Tomorrow — the boss. Minuet in G, with every fingering planned.

real_piano: Pick any note. Press with finger 3. Substitute to thumb. Substitute back. Do this ten times on the same key.
Try a held note with 5-1 substitution. Feel the hand shift up the keyboard.
Listen: if the note wobbles or double-strikes, the weight shifted too fast. Slow down.

questions.figure: Note marked 3-1
questions.prompt: What does 3-1 mean?
options: Two fingers, same key, in order

questions.figure: Hand substituting thumb for finger 3 on E
questions.prompt: Does the key come back up during the swap?

questions.audio: A sustained note with a silent finger swap
questions.prompt: How many times was the note struck?
Play E4. Press with finger 3. Substitute to thumb. Keep the note sounding.
questions.accept: E4 sustained with one strike

remediation.title: Substitution, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Silent swap. Tricky at first. Let's slow it down to single-key drills.

completion.cleffy: Substitution, understood. Three tricks now in your hand — thumb-under, cross-over, substitution. Tomorrow: act boss. Minuet in G, with a planned hand.

title: Minuet in G (fingering)
