subtitle: Act 17 · The planned hand
goal: Play the Minuet in G opening with every finger planned — using thumb-under and cross-over.
piece_context: Act 17's finish line. Same Minuet melody from Act 16 — but this time the hand is choreographed end to end.

piece.title: Minuet in G (opening, fingering focus)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — planned fingering across a six-note range

pages.figure: Cleffy holding the Minuet in G sheet music. Small pencil marks — finger numbers — floating above every note.
pages.cleffy: End of Act 17. Same piece you learned last week. New job. This time, every finger is planned.

pages.figure: Play button. Minuet in G opening, clean.

audio.A: Minuet in G opening phrase — D5, G4, A4, B4, C5, D5, G4, G4 — played smoothly

pages.cleffy: Listen. The familiar opening. Same notes. You're going to play them without stumbling over your own hand.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Minuet in G opening: D5 G4 A4 B4 C5 D5 G4 G4. No fingerings yet.
pages.cleffy: Here's the phrase. Eight notes. D, G, A, B, C, D, G, G. D up high — drops down to G — walks up to D — drops back to G twice.

pages.figure: Staff showing the full range from G4 to D5. Hand diagram showing five fingers can't span both ends naturally.
pages.cleffy: Six-note range. G up to D. That's six white keys. Five fingers. One move needed somewhere.

pages.figure: Staff with fingering written above each note: 5-1-2-3-4-5-1-5 (D5=5, G4=1, A4=2, B4=3, C5=4, D5=5, G4=1, G4=5).
pages.cleffy: The plan. Five, one, two, three, four, five — one — five. Pinky on the high D pickup. Thumb drops to G. Fingers 2, 3, 4, 5 walk up to D. Thumb snaps back to G. Pinky hovers for the last G.

pages.figure: Hand diagram. Pinky on D5, then the whole hand shifting so thumb lands on G4. Arrow showing the hop.
pages.cleffy: First move — the drop. Pinky plays high D. Hand hops down so thumb lands on G. It's a leap. Plan the landing before you hop.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D5 and G4 glowing. Fingers 5 and 1.
pages.cleffy: Play just those two. Pinky on D, then thumb on G.

pages.figure: Keyboard close-up. G4-A4-B4-C5-D5 glowing. Fingers 1-2-3-4-5 labeled. No thumb-under needed — five keys, five fingers.
pages.cleffy: Now the walk up. G to A to B to C to D. Five keys. Five fingers. No thumb-under needed. One finger per note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 through D5. Fingering 1-2-3-4-5.
pages.cleffy: Play the walk. Thumb on G. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 up to D.

pages.figure: Hand diagram. Pinky on D5, then the whole hand hopping back so thumb lands on G4. Arrow highlighting the shift.
pages.cleffy: Now the tricky part. You're on D with your pinky. Next note is G — way back. Position shift. The hand hops to a new spot so the thumb can land on G. Not a thumb-under — a leap that big needs the whole hand to move.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D5 held by pinky. Hand animating back so thumb lands on G4.
pages.cleffy: Just that move. D with pinky, then hand hops to new position — thumb on G.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Minuet in G opening with every finger labeled. Tempo 60. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Full phrase, slow, guides on. 5-1-2-3-4-5-1-5. Trust the plan.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Same phrase. No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read the fingerings. You play. Stumble? Keep going.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Same notes as Act 16. Different hand. This hand knows where it's going.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Tempo 100. Minuet tempo.
pages.cleffy: At tempo. 100 bpm. The real Minuet feel. One, two, three. One, two, three.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 17 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 17 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding up a hand diagram with every finger numbered. Minuet in G sheet music behind him.
pages.cleffy: Act 17, done. You have three hand tricks now. Thumb-under. Cross-over. Substitution. And one planned piece to prove they work. Next act: chords. Three-note stacks. Major and minor. Broken and blocked. Canon in D is waiting at the end of Act 18.

real_piano: Minuet in G opening with the planned fingering — every day this week.
Isolate the thumb-under at the end: D with pinky, G with thumb. Ten times a day.
Write the finger numbers above the notes on paper. Play from your own markings.
Play the same phrase with the WRONG fingering once. Feel how it stumbles. Reset.
virtual_only: Replay pages 12-14 (full phrase, guided → no guides → at tempo) three times

questions.figure: Minuet opening with fingering 5-1-2-3-4-5-1-5
questions.prompt: What finger plays the first note (D5)?

questions.figure: Last two notes of the phrase — D then G
questions.prompt: What move happens between the D and the G?

questions.figure: G-A-B-C-D walk-up
questions.prompt: Does this segment need a thumb-under?
options: No — five fingers fit five keys

questions.audio: Smooth Minuet opening
questions.prompt: Can you hear the thumb-under?
Play D5, then thumb-under to G4. Just those two notes.
questions.accept: D5 then G4 with smooth pass

remediation.title: Minuet, finger by finger
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — bigger piece. Let's rebuild the phrase in three chunks: the drop, the walk, the position shift. Then put them back together.

completion.cleffy: Act 17, closed. You have a planned hand. Minuet in G — articulation in Act 16, fingering in Act 17. Same piece, two layers deeper. Next up: chords. Three notes at once.

title: Triads
subtitle: Act 18 · Chords
goal: See and play a triad — three notes stacked as root, third, fifth.
piece_context: Single notes made melodies. Three notes at once makes harmony. Act 18 opens with the basic building block of chords — the triad.

pages.figure: Staff with three notes stacked on top of each other — C, E, G. Label: ONE SOUND, THREE NOTES.
pages.cleffy: New thing today. Three notes. Played at the same time. That's a chord. The simplest kind is a triad.

pages.figure: Staff showing C on line, E on line, G on line. All three notes drawn as one tower. Labels: ROOT, THIRD, FIFTH.
pages.cleffy: A triad is three notes stacked. The bottom note is the root. The middle is a third above it. The top is a fifth above. Root, third, fifth. Always.

pages.figure: Keyboard showing C-E-G highlighted. Three white keys, each skipping one. Label: LINE, SKIP, LINE, SKIP, LINE.
pages.cleffy: On the staff, a triad sits on three lines in a row. Or three spaces in a row. Line-line-line. Space-space-space. On the keyboard — skip a note, skip a note.

pages.figure: Play button. C-E-G chord pictured on the staff.

audio.A: C major triad played as one block

pages.cleffy: Listen. Three notes, one sound. C, E, G together. That's a C triad.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 glowing. RH fingers 1, 3, 5.
pages.cleffy: Play C, E, G at the same time. Thumb, middle, pinky. Press all three down together.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's your first chord.

pages.figure: Staff with C-E-G. Label: C TRIAD. Arrow points to the root C.
pages.cleffy: A triad is named after its root. C on the bottom — C triad. G on the bottom — G triad. The root is the name.

pages.figure: Staff showing G-B-D stacked. Keyboard below shows G4, B4, D5 highlighted.
pages.cleffy: G triad. Root G. Skip up to B. Skip up to D. G, B, D. Line-line-line.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4, B4, D5 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play G, B, D together. Same hand shape as C — just moved up.

pages.figure: Staff showing D-F#-A. F# labeled as black key. Label: D TRIAD.
pages.cleffy: D triad. Root D. Third up is F-sharp. Fifth up is A. The F has a sharp. Black key between F and G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4, F#4, A4 glowing. F# shown as a black key.
pages.cleffy: Play D, F-sharp, A. Thumb on D, middle on the black key, pinky on A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C triad, then G triad, then D triad — played in sequence.
pages.cleffy: All three, one after the other. C triad. G triad. D triad. Same shape, three homes.

pages.figure: Three triads drawn side by side on the treble staff: C-E-G, G-B-D, D-F#-A.
pages.cleffy: On the staff, triads look like snowmen. Three notes stacked. Read the bottom note to name the chord.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 86. Three snowman-chords drawn on a staff.
pages.cleffy: Triads. Root, third, fifth. Tomorrow — same shape, but two flavors. Bright and sad.

real_piano: Play C-E-G, G-B-D, D-F#-A every morning. Same hand shape, different starting notes.
Find the triad on any white-key root. Skip a note, skip a note. That's a triad.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5, 8, 10 until the shape feels automatic.

questions.figure: Staff with C-E-G stacked.
questions.prompt: What's the root of this triad?

questions.figure: Staff with G-B-D stacked.
questions.prompt: What do we call three notes stacked like this?

questions.figure: Keyboard with three white keys highlighted: D, F, A.
questions.prompt: Are these three notes all on lines (or all on spaces) when written on a staff?
options: Yes — skip a note, skip a note
No — they're next to each other

questions.audio: C major triad played as a block
questions.prompt: How many notes sounded at once?
Play a C triad with your right hand — C, E, G together.
questions.accept: C4, E4, G4 sounded simultaneously

remediation.title: Triads, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three notes at once is a jump. Let's build a triad note by note — root, add a third, add a fifth.

completion.cleffy: First chord is yours. Tomorrow — major vs minor.

title: Major vs minor
subtitle: Act 18 · Chords
goal: Hear and play the difference between a major triad and a minor triad.
piece_context: Triads come in two main flavors. Same shape, one note moves by a half step — and the whole mood changes.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: a sun labeled MAJOR. Right: a rain cloud labeled MINOR. Both with the same triad-snowman shape underneath.
pages.cleffy: Same three notes. One note moves a hair. The whole mood flips. That's major and minor.

pages.figure: Two play buttons — MAJOR and MINOR.

audio.A: C major triad (C-E-G)
audio.B: C minor triad (C-Eb-G)

pages.cleffy: First one — bright. That's major. Second one — sad. That's minor. Root is the same. Top is the same. Middle note moved.

pages.figure: C major triad and C minor triad side by side. Arrow showing E dropped to E-flat. Label: THE MIDDLE NOTE.
pages.cleffy: The third moves. The middle note. Major — the third is a little higher. Minor — the third is a half step lower. Root and fifth don't change.

pages.figure: Keyboard showing C major triad (C-E-G), then C minor triad (C-Eb-G). E-flat is the black key just below E.
pages.cleffy: From C major to C minor — E drops to E-flat. E-flat is the black key just below E. Same root C. Same fifth G. Just the middle pulled down one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 glowing. Label: C MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: C major first. Thumb, middle, pinky. C, E, G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, Eb4, G4 glowing. Eb shown as a black key.
pages.cleffy: Now C minor. Middle finger slides down to E-flat — the black key. C, E-flat, G.
pages.followup_cleffy: Feel the mood change?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Major or minor?

pages.figure: Staff showing A-C-E. Label: A MINOR. All white keys.
pages.cleffy: Here's A minor. Root A. Third C. Fifth E. All white keys. But it's minor — the third between A and C is the small kind.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A3, C4, E4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play A minor. A, C, E together.

pages.figure: Two staves. Left: D-F-A labeled D MINOR. Right: E-G-B labeled E MINOR.
pages.cleffy: Two more. D minor is D, F, A. E minor is E, G, B. All white keys. All minor. The ear tells you before the theory does.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4, F4, A4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play D minor. D, F, A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major then C minor shown side by side.
pages.cleffy: Now both. C major, then C minor. Same root. Hear the drop.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Mixed. Major or minor?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 87. A sun and a cloud sharing the same triad shape.
pages.cleffy: Major, bright. Minor, sad. One note does all the work. Tomorrow — same chords, but rolled. One note at a time.

real_piano: Play C major, then C minor. Back and forth. Feel the mood flip.
Try A minor, D minor, E minor. All white keys. All sad.
Find a song you like. Is it major or minor? Most will be obvious once you listen for it.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5-6 and 11-12 until the shift feels automatic.

questions.figure: C major triad and C minor triad side by side.
questions.prompt: Which note moves between major and minor?

questions.figure: Keyboard with A-C-E highlighted.
questions.prompt: Is this A triad major or minor?

questions.figure: Label: C major.
questions.prompt: How does the third of a major triad compare to the third of a minor triad?

questions.audio: A major triad (bright)
questions.prompt: Major or minor?

questions.audio: A minor triad (sad)
questions.prompt: Major or minor?
Play A minor — A, C, E together.
questions.accept: A3, C4, E4 sounded simultaneously

remediation.title: Major and minor, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Major vs minor is an ear thing before it's a theory thing. Let's just listen — a lot — before we play again.

completion.cleffy: Two flavors. Known. Tomorrow — broken chords.

title: Broken chords
subtitle: Act 18 · Chords
goal: Play the notes of a triad one at a time instead of all at once — a broken chord.
piece_context: Chords don't have to stack. You can also roll the notes out one at a time. Same notes, different feel.

pages.figure: Staff on the left — C-E-G stacked as a block chord. Arrow to the right — C, E, G written as three separate quarter notes.
pages.cleffy: Same three notes. Played one at a time instead of all together. That's a broken chord. Some people call it an arpeggio.

pages.figure: Two play buttons — BLOCK and BROKEN.

audio.A: C major triad played as a block
audio.B: C major triad rolled — C, E, G one at a time

pages.cleffy: First: block. All together. Second: broken. Rolled. Same chord. Different feel — broken sounds open, like water.

pages.figure: Staff with C, E, G written as three quarter notes in sequence. Bracket over them reads C TRIAD.
pages.cleffy: On the page, a broken chord is just three notes in a row. But the shape is still C-E-G — skip, skip. Your eye will start to see the chord inside the melody.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 light up one at a time.
pages.cleffy: Roll a C chord. Thumb on C, middle on E, pinky on G. One finger at a time. Don't hold the keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G-E-C — up and back down.
pages.cleffy: Up and back. C, E, G, E, C. Same fingers — 1, 3, 5, 3, 1.

pages.figure: Two staves. Left: G-B-D broken. Right: F-A-C broken.
pages.cleffy: Two more chords rolled. G broken — G, B, D. F broken — F, A, C. Same 1-3-5 hand shape. New starting note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4-B4-D5 rolled.
pages.cleffy: G broken. G, B, D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F4-A4-C5 rolled.
pages.cleffy: F broken. F, A, C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, G, F chords rolled one after another.
pages.cleffy: All three, rolled back to back. C, then G, then F. Nine notes total. Slow.

pages.figure: Staff showing C-E-G as three quarter notes. Metronome at 80 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Now with a metronome. Each note on a beat. 1, 2, 3. C, E, G. Don't rush.

pages.figure: Excerpt of a simple piece (Canon-style accompaniment) showing broken chords in the LH as three separate notes per bar.
pages.cleffy: This is how a lot of music sounds. One hand holds the melody. The other rolls chords underneath. You'll hear it everywhere once you know what to listen for.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C broken, G broken, F broken — at tempo.
pages.cleffy: One more pass. Smooth.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's nine notes, three chords, one hand.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Block or broken?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 88. A triad snowman next to a line of three dots — same notes, two ways.
pages.cleffy: Broken chords. Same notes, rolled. Tomorrow — same chord, different stacking order. Inversions.

real_piano: Roll C, G, F chords up and back every day. 1-3-5-3-1, each root.
Listen to any pop song. Hear the rolled chords in the background? Now you know what that is.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5, 9, 10 until the roll feels smooth.

questions.figure: Staff with C, E, G as three separate quarter notes.
questions.prompt: What's this called when a chord's notes are written one at a time?

questions.figure: Keyboard with C-E-G highlighted in order.
questions.prompt: Which fingers play a rolled C chord in the right hand?

questions.audio: C triad rolled — C, E, G one at a time
questions.prompt: Is this a block chord or a broken chord?
Roll a C chord — play C, E, G one at a time, in order.
questions.accept: C4 then E4 then G4 in sequence

remediation.title: Broken chords, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rolling three notes cleanly takes a few tries. Let's slow it way down — one note per second, metronome on.

completion.cleffy: Block and broken. Two ways to play one chord. Tomorrow — same chord, different order of notes.

title: Chord inversions (see)
subtitle: Act 18 · Chords
goal: Recognize that a chord can be stacked in three different orders — and still be the same chord.
piece_context: A C chord is C, E, G. But the order doesn't have to stay that way. Today you learn to spot a C chord even when it's stacked differently. Playing the shapes comes in L101 — today is seeing.

pages.figure: Three triad snowmen in a row. First: C-E-G. Second: E-G-C. Third: G-C-E. Labels: ROOT POSITION, 1st INVERSION, 2nd INVERSION. All three labeled C CHORD.
pages.cleffy: A chord can be stacked three different ways. C, E, G. Or E, G, C. Or G, C, E. Same three letters. Still a C chord. Just reshuffled.

pages.figure: Staff with C-E-G stacked. Root C on the bottom. Label: ROOT POSITION.
pages.cleffy: Root position. The root sits on the bottom. This is the shape you already know from Lesson 86. C, E, G — that's root position of C.

pages.figure: Staff with E-G-C stacked. E at the bottom. Label: 1st INVERSION. Arrow showing C moved up an octave.
pages.cleffy: First inversion. The root jumps to the top. Bottom note is now E. Then G. Then C on top. Still a C chord — the letters are still C, E, G.

pages.figure: Staff with G-C-E stacked. G at the bottom. Label: 2nd INVERSION.
pages.cleffy: Second inversion. The third moves up too. G on the bottom. Then C. Then E on top. Still a C chord. Still those three letters.

pages.figure: Keyboard shown three times. C-E-G (root pos). E-G-C above it (1st inv). G-C-E above that (2nd inv). Each labeled.
pages.cleffy: On the keyboard, you can see the gaps. Root position — small gap, bigger gap. First inversion — bigger gap on the bottom. Second inversion — big gap in the middle.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. Root / 1st / 2nd inversion of C.

audio.A: C major root position
audio.B: C major first inversion

pages.cleffy: Listen to all three. Same three letters every time. They sound related. Like the same word, said with a different emphasis.

pages.figure: Three triads. Student sees the bottom note of each and has to tell root/1st/2nd. Legend on the side.
pages.cleffy: Here's the trick. Look at the bottom note. If it's the root — root position. Third on bottom — first inversion. Fifth on bottom — second inversion. Bottom note is always the clue.

pages.figure: G chord in three orders. G-B-D (root), B-D-G (1st), D-G-B (2nd). All labeled G CHORD.
pages.cleffy: Same trick for any chord. G chord inverts too. G-B-D. B-D-G. D-G-B. Still G every time.

pages.figure: Two short phrases on a staff. Top: C chord → F chord in root position, with a big jump. Bottom: C chord → F chord where F is in 2nd inversion, staying close. Arrow: SMOOTHER.
pages.cleffy: Why do inversions exist? So your hand doesn't have to jump all over the piano. When two chords share a note, inversions let them stay close — smoother.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Two chords play. Are they the same chord (different inversion) or different chords?

pages.figure: Staff showing three inversions of a C chord. Visually — root pos looks like a tight stack, 1st inv has C up top, 2nd inv has a gap.
pages.cleffy: On the staff, you'll spot inversions by shape. Root position is tight. Inversions have one note pushed up — the stack opens up.

pages.figure: Staff showing E-G-C stacked (first inversion of C). Three labels offered.
pages.cleffy: Quick look — what's on the bottom of this stack? E. E is the third of C. So — first inversion. Still a C chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G root position glowing. Note — PLAY IS NEXT LESSON ARC; TODAY JUST ONE.
pages.cleffy: Today is mostly seeing. But once — play C in root position, just to hold the shape in your hand. C, E, G.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 89. Three C-chord stacks in a line, each labeled. Note at the bottom: Playing inversions — Lesson 101.
pages.cleffy: Inversions. Same chord, different order. Spot them by the bottom note. Tomorrow — act boss. Canon in D. Playing the inversions yourself — that comes later, in Lesson 101.

real_piano: Write out C-E-G, E-G-C, G-C-E on paper. Label each one. Do the same for G-B-D.
When you listen to music this week, try to notice when a chord sounds 'stable' (root position) vs 'floating' (an inversion). Don't overthink — just notice.
virtual_only: Replay pages 3-5 and 7 until spotting the bottom note feels automatic.

questions.figure: Staff with E-G-C stacked.
questions.prompt: This is a C chord. Which inversion?

questions.figure: Staff with G-C-E stacked.
questions.prompt: This is a C chord. Which inversion?

questions.figure: Staff with C-E-G stacked.
questions.prompt: How do you tell this is root position?
options: The root is on the bottom
The fifth is on the bottom

questions.audio: C major root position, then C major first inversion
questions.prompt: Are these the same chord?
Play a C chord in root position — C, E, G together.
questions.accept: C4, E4, G4 simultaneously

remediation.title: Inversions, one shape at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three shapes for one chord is a lot at first. Let's just stare at the bottom note — it's the whole trick.

completion.cleffy: Inversions spotted. Tomorrow — act 18 boss. Canon in D.

title: Canon in D
subtitle: Act 18 · Your first chord progression
goal: Play the famous 8-chord Canon in D progression in the left hand, with a simple right-hand melody on top.
piece_context: Pachelbel's Canon in D is built on the most famous chord progression in music. Eight chords, one per bar, repeated forever. Today you play those chords — and a simple descending melody on top.

piece.title: Canon in D (simplified)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — LH block triads + simple RH descent

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a scroll that reads PACHELBEL'S CANON IN D. Eight chord stacks drawn underneath in a row.
pages.cleffy: End of Act 18. Chord week wraps here. Canon in D. Three hundred years old. Still the most-used progression in music. Eight chords. Your left hand plays all of them.

pages.figure: Big play button. A simplified Canon in D plays — LH chords with a descending RH melody.

audio.A: Simplified Canon in D — LH block chords, RH scale descent

pages.cleffy: Listen. The LH is the engine. Eight chords, looping. The RH just steps down. Simple melody, deep chords under it.

pages.figure: Staff showing eight stacked triads in a row, one per bar. Labels underneath: D, A, Bm, F#m, G, D, G, A.
pages.cleffy: Here they are. All eight. D. A. B minor. F-sharp minor. G. D. G. A. Three majors, two minors, repeated. One chord per bar.

pages.figure: Same eight chords, now with notes labeled: D-F#-A | A-C#-E | B-D-F# | F#-A-C# | G-B-D | D-F#-A | G-B-D | A-C#-E.
pages.cleffy: Each chord is a triad you already know how to build. Root, third, fifth. D chord — D, F-sharp, A. A chord — A, C-sharp, E. And so on. Sharps because we're in D major — F-sharp and C-sharp, every time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First four chords — D, A, Bm, F#m — highlighted one by one, in LH register.
pages.cleffy: First four chords in the left hand. One chord, one bar. D, A, B minor, F-sharp minor. Slow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Last four chords — G, D, G, A.
pages.cleffy: Second half. G, D, G, A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full eight-chord LH loop.
pages.cleffy: All eight, back to back. One per bar. Don't rush — let each chord ring.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the whole engine of Canon in D. One loop.

pages.figure: Treble staff showing a simple descending melody — F#, E, D, C#, B, A, B, C# — one note per bar.
pages.cleffy: Right hand now. One note per bar. F-sharp, E, D, C-sharp, B, A, B, C-sharp. It just steps down, then hops back up at the end.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH melody — F#4, E4, D4, C#4, B3, A3, B3, C#4.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Slow. Each note gets a whole bar.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Just bar 1 — RH F#4, LH D major triad. Both land together.
pages.cleffy: Warm-up. Just the first bar. Both hands land together. RH F-sharp. LH D chord. Once.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full eight bars. Guides ON. LH chord stacks, RH melody on top.
pages.cleffy: Full thing. Both hands. Guides on. Very slow. Each bar — one melody note on top, one chord underneath.

pages.figure: Grand staff. No highlights.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read, you play. If a chord feels wobbly, slow down. Don't stop.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Eight chords of Pachelbel. You played the progression a million songs are built on.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Tempo 70.
pages.cleffy: One more time, a little faster. 70 bpm. Don't stop if you stumble. The loop keeps going.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before Act 18 ends.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — so a bigger check.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 18 COMPLETE 🎯. Trophy engraved 18/50. Eight chord stacks in a row across the bottom.
pages.cleffy: Act 18, done. Triads. Major, minor. Broken. Inverted. And now — a real chord progression under a melody. Canon in D, in your hands. Pachelbel would be fine with it. Next act — sight-reading. Eyes ahead, always.

real_piano: Play the LH eight-chord loop every day. Don't worry about RH at first — just the chords.
Once LH is automatic, add the RH melody. One note per bar.
Listen to pop music this week. Half the songs use a version of this same progression. You'll start hearing it.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7, 11, 12 until both hands feel locked together.

questions.figure: Staff showing eight triads labeled D, A, Bm, F#m, G, D, G, A.
questions.prompt: How many chords are in the Canon in D loop?

questions.figure: Staff with the D chord stacked: D-F#-A.
questions.prompt: Which note in the D triad is on a black key?

questions.figure: Eight Canon chords labeled with major and minor markings.
questions.prompt: Are there any minor chords in the Canon loop?

questions.audio: First four chords of the Canon — D, A, Bm, F#m
questions.prompt: Do all four chords sound the same, or do some feel sadder?
options: Some feel sad — those are the minor ones

questions.audio: Canon in D full loop
questions.prompt: Do the chords repeat in a loop?
Play the first two chords of Canon in D in the left hand — D major then A major.
questions.accept: D-F#-A followed by A-C#-E in the left-hand register

remediation.title: Canon in D, one chord at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss is a big one. Let's break it down — one LH chord per page, then two, then four, then all eight. Then add RH when both hands feel ready.

completion.cleffy: Act 18 — closed. You play triads. Major and minor. Block and broken. Root position and inversions. And you play a real chord progression under a real melody. Next up — sight-reading. Your eyes go first.

title: Reading 4 bars ahead
subtitle: Act 19 · Sight-reading
goal: Your eyes should always be ahead of your hands.
piece_context: You've been reading note-by-note. Sight-reading is different. Your eyes scout ahead while your hands finish what they already know.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing down at a staff. Two circles on the music: one around the bar the hand is playing, one four bars further right, glowing.
pages.cleffy: New skill today. Sight-reading. Eyes live ahead of hands. Always.

pages.figure: Staff with 8 bars. Bar 1 marked HANDS. Bar 4 marked EYES. Arrow labeled SCOUTS AHEAD.
pages.cleffy: Your hands play bar 1. Your eyes are already on bar 4 or 5. That gap is the whole trick.

pages.figure: Split panel. Left: eyes glued to current note, hands stumbling. Right: eyes ahead, hands flowing.
pages.cleffy: If your eyes wait on the note you're playing, you freeze at every new one. If your eyes scout ahead, your hands already know what's coming. One feels panicky. One feels calm.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: EYES FROZEN.

audio.A: A halting performance — pauses at every note, uneven

pages.cleffy: This is what eyes-stuck-on-current-note sounds like. Stop-start. Panicked.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: EYES AHEAD.

audio.A: A steady, smooth performance at moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: And this is eyes-ahead. Steady. Even when it's slow, it moves.

pages.figure: Four bars of simple stepwise RH melody in C major. Quarter notes: C D E F | G F E D | C D E F | G A G F.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. All steps. C major. First — just look. Scan left to right. Find the highest note. Find the lowest.

pages.figure: Same four bars. A moving highlight one bar AHEAD of the note being played.
pages.cleffy: Play it slow. Here's the rule — keep your eyes on the bar to the RIGHT of your hand. Slow tempo. 60 bpm.

pages.figure: Diagram: HANDS on bar 1, EYES on bar 2, gap labeled 'scout distance'.
pages.cleffy: The distance between eyes and hands is your scout distance. At first — one bar. Later — two, three, four. Bigger gap, smoother playing.

pages.figure: Eight bars of stepwise LH + RH. Highlight leads by two bars now.
pages.cleffy: Longer passage. Keep eyes TWO bars ahead. Don't stop if you blur a note. Just keep moving.

pages.figure: Close-up of an eye with arrows: 1. contour (up/down), 2. landmarks (middle C, bass F), 3. accidentals, 4. rhythm shape.
pages.cleffy: What do the eyes actually look for? Shape. Landmarks. Accidentals. Rhythm. Not every note. The PATTERN.

pages.figure: Four bars. Student sees them for 5 seconds, then plays.
pages.cleffy: Scan first. Look at the whole thing. Then play. You'll read better when you already know what's coming.

pages.figure: Cleffy shrugging, hand open.
pages.cleffy: It feels weird at first. Hands doing one thing. Eyes doing another. That's the whole skill. Gets easier every time.

pages.figure: 12 bars of simple melody. Highlight leads 3 bars ahead.
pages.cleffy: One longer run. Eyes ahead. Tempo stays slow. Stumble? Hands keep moving. Eyes keep scouting.
pages.followup_cleffy: Eyes were ahead. You felt it.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 91. Cleffy with eyes drawn big, pointing forward.
pages.cleffy: Day one of sight-reading. Eyes scout. Hands follow. Tomorrow — making the gap bigger.

real_piano: Pick any easy piece you've learned. Play it with eyes ALWAYS a bar ahead of your hands.
Open any method book. Play each exercise once, slow, eyes-ahead. Don't go back.

questions.figure: Staff with 8 bars, one bar highlighted for hands, one bar highlighted ahead for eyes.
questions.prompt: Where should your eyes be while your hands play bar 1?

questions.figure: Split image: stopped-at-note vs flowing.
questions.prompt: Which picture shows good sight-reading?

questions.audio: Halting, start-stop performance
questions.prompt: Eyes ahead or eyes stuck?
Play 4 bars of stepwise C major (provided), keeping eyes a bar ahead.
questions.accept: 4 bars played at steady tempo, no long freezes

remediation.title: Eyes ahead, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: New habit — breaking a year of eye-on-hand reading is hard. We'll slow it down. One bar scout distance. Steady tempo. Just that.

completion.cleffy: Scouts out. Hands calm. Tomorrow — we make the habit permanent.

title: Eyes ahead, always
subtitle: Act 19 · Sight-reading
goal: Never stare at the note you're playing.
piece_context: Yesterday we introduced eyes-ahead. Today we make it automatic. The rule has no exceptions.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a finger over his lips, pointing ahead. The word NEVER painted above the note currently being played.
pages.cleffy: One rule today. Never stare at the note you're playing.

pages.figure: Staff. Current note circled with a big red X over it. A note 3 bars ahead with a green eye icon.
pages.cleffy: Where your hand is — don't look there. Where your hand is GOING — that's where eyes live. Flip that habit. Forever.

pages.figure: Two racetracks. One car with headlights on its own bumper. One car with headlights far down the track.
pages.cleffy: Headlights pointed at your bumper? You can't see turns coming. Headlights pointed down the road? You ride smooth. Same with reading.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: eyes on note. B: eyes ahead.

audio.A: Choppy, start-stop playback of a simple melody
audio.B: Smooth, even version of the same melody

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Two readers. One stares. One scouts.

pages.figure: Four bars of RH melody. A ghosted ahead-highlight at all times.
pages.cleffy: Warm-up. Play these 4 bars. The highlight is always two notes ahead of your finger. Let your eyes follow the highlight, not your hand.

pages.figure: Cleffy shrugging. Hand on keyboard without eyes looking down.
pages.cleffy: "But I need to look to find the key." No. Your fingers know the keyboard by feel now. Trust them. Your eyes have another job.

pages.figure: Keyboard partially covered by a paper strip — you can see the music, but not your hand. RH plays a simple line.
pages.cleffy: Test. Don't look at your hand. Just at the staff. Simple C-major line. Five notes.

pages.figure: Same paper-strip setup. Eight-note line.
pages.cleffy: Longer one. Still no peeking at your hand. If your finger lands wrong — let it. Keep going.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hands knew more than you thought.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Both clefs. Two separate eye icons — one scouting RH, one scouting LH.
pages.cleffy: Both hands now. Eyes still scout ahead on both staves at once. Sounds impossible. Isn't.

pages.figure: Four bars of grand staff. RH stepwise melody over LH quarter notes.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Slow. Eyes one bar ahead. Don't look down. Don't look back.

pages.figure: Staff with a small X on one note. Below: KEEP MOVING.
pages.cleffy: You'll miss notes. Everyone does. The rule — eyes keep moving. Don't go back to fix it. Fix it next time you see it.

pages.figure: Six bars. Metronome on. Red text: DO NOT GO BACK.
pages.cleffy: Six bars. If you hit wrong — keep going. If your eyes drop to your hand — lift them back up.

pages.figure: A path worn into grass. Label: NEW HABIT.
pages.cleffy: Habit takes weeks. Not minutes. Every time you sit down — remind yourself. Eyes ahead. Keep reminding until you stop needing to.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 92. Eyes drawn big, mid-keyboard without a hand in sight.
pages.cleffy: Rule: never stare at the note you're playing. Tomorrow — what to do when you trip.

real_piano: Cover your hands with a scarf or cloth while playing something easy. Read only.
Every practice session, first 2 minutes — just notice where your eyes go. Correct gently.

questions.figure: A current note with an X over it and a note 3 bars ahead with an eye.
questions.prompt: Where should your eyes be?

questions.figure: A player with head down, eyes on hand.
questions.prompt: Is this good sight-reading form?

questions.audio: Smooth, even melody
questions.prompt: Eyes ahead or eyes stuck?
Play a simple 8-note line without looking at your hand.
questions.accept: Line played with eyes on staff, not hand

remediation.title: Eyes up, all the time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Habit is stubborn. Eyes drop back to hands without asking. Slower drills. Shorter passages. Same rule. Practiced more.

completion.cleffy: Eyes stay up. Even when it's hard. Tomorrow — what to do when you stumble.

title: Recovering gracefully
subtitle: Act 19 · Sight-reading
goal: Stumbles happen — keep the beat.
piece_context: Real sight-reading includes mistakes. The skill isn't avoiding them — it's not falling apart when they happen.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a small bandage on his finger. Behind him, a metronome ticking steadily.
pages.cleffy: You will trip over notes. Everyone does. Today — what matters is the next beat, not the one you missed.

pages.figure: Two staffs side by side. A: wrong note, player keeps going. B: wrong note, player stops and tries again.
pages.cleffy: Wrong note — not a big deal. Stopping to fix it — bigger deal. The beat is the thing that has to survive.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Player hits wrong note, stops, restarts a bar
audio.B: Player hits wrong note, keeps flowing. Listener barely notices.

pages.cleffy: First — heard the stumble loud and clear. Because of the STOP. Second — maybe you heard a wrong note. Maybe not. Because the beat kept going.

pages.figure: Four bars. Note 3 has a red X on it. A dotted arrow skips from note 2 straight to note 4.
pages.cleffy: When you miss a note, skip it. Don't re-play. Don't go back. Land on the NEXT right note, on the next right beat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. 5-note line. Note 3 grayed out — you deliberately skip it.
pages.cleffy: Practice the skill. Play notes 1, 2, then skip note 3, land on note 4, then 5. On beat, no pause.

pages.figure: Staff with circles around downbeats of each bar — the strong beat.
pages.cleffy: If you lose your place badly — aim for a landmark. The next downbeat. The start of the next bar. A chord you recognize. Reset there.

pages.figure: Eight bars. A red X on a bar you're told to 'miss.' Next downbeat glows green.
pages.cleffy: Play 8 bars. When you hit bar 4 — pretend you lost your place. Eyes to the next downbeat (bar 5). Land there. On beat.

pages.figure: Metronome with a heart inside. Label: THIS IS THE PULSE.
pages.cleffy: The beat is a heartbeat. Don't stop it. Wrong notes heal. A broken beat sounds like broken music.

pages.figure: 12 bars, easy melody. Metronome ticking loud. Instructions: DO NOT STOP.
pages.cleffy: Metronome on. Play through. If you mess up — let it happen. Your job is to arrive at the next beat, on time, with SOMETHING.

pages.figure: Three panels. 1: 'skip it' (wrong note, keep going). 2: 'approximate' (wrong but close). 3: 'land at next bar'.
pages.cleffy: Three moves when you trip. One — skip. Two — play close to right, keep going. Three — land at the next downbeat. All three beat stopping.

pages.figure: Grand staff. 8 bars. Simple both-hands passage.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Something will go wrong. That's the point. Feel what it's like to keep playing through it.
pages.followup_cleffy: Beat survived. That's the win.

pages.figure: Cleffy sitting down, elbows on knees.
pages.cleffy: Real talk. Stopping to fix a wrong note is a comfort habit. It feels safer. It isn't. It trains you to break.

pages.figure: 12 bars. No retry button visible. Banner: ONE TAKE.
pages.cleffy: One take. Whatever happens, happens. Keep the pulse. Eyes ahead. Recover on the fly.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 93. A wobbly line that keeps going.
pages.cleffy: Trips are part of reading. Keep the beat. That's the whole art of recovery. Tomorrow — rhythm by itself.

real_piano: Pick any piece you haven't fully learned. Play it through ONCE with a metronome. No stopping.
Every time you catch yourself wanting to restart a bar — don't. Keep going.

questions.figure: Staff with a missed note — arrow to next downbeat.
questions.prompt: What's the best recovery move?

questions.figure: Two recordings shown as waveforms. One with a long silence mid-piece. One steady.
questions.prompt: Which is better recovery?

questions.audio: A performance with one wrong note but steady beat
questions.prompt: Did the beat survive?
Play 8 bars with a metronome. If you miss a note, don't stop. Keep the beat.
questions.accept: 8 bars played without stopping, beat held

remediation.title: Just the beat
remediation.opening_cleffy: The stopping habit is deep. Let's isolate it. Metronome on. Slower tempo. One rule — don't stop, even once.

completion.cleffy: Graceful recovery. Muscle learned. Tomorrow — just rhythm, no notes.

title: Rhythm-only sight-read
subtitle: Act 19 · Sight-reading
goal: Tap the rhythm before you touch a key.
piece_context: Rhythm and pitch are two skills. Separate them. Tap the rhythm first, on any one key or a table, and the notes come easier.

pages.figure: Cleffy tapping a beat on a tabletop. A staff beside him showing rhythms with stems but no pitches — just note-heads on one line.
pages.cleffy: Sight-reading trick — half the work is rhythm. Tap the rhythm first. Then add notes.

pages.figure: Venn diagram. Circle 1: PITCH (what notes). Circle 2: RHYTHM (when and how long). Overlap: PLAYING.
pages.cleffy: Reading is two brains at once. What notes. When. How long. Separate them. Easier.

pages.figure: Staff with notes all on one line (no pitch variation) — just stems and rhythms. Quarters, halves, eighths.
pages.cleffy: Rhythm-only notation. Every note on the same line. You don't care what pitch. You care HOW LONG.

pages.figure: Play button. A steady clap-clap-clap-hold.

audio.A: A rhythm clapped: quarter quarter quarter half, repeated

pages.cleffy: Listen. Quarter, quarter, quarter, half. No pitch. Just time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 only — all notes shown as C4. Rhythm: q q q h.
pages.cleffy: Play this rhythm on just Middle C. Quarter, quarter, quarter, half. Tap it. Feel the length.

pages.figure: Staff — same-line notes. Now with eighth-note pairs added in.
pages.cleffy: Add eighth notes. Two per beat. Feels faster because it is.

pages.figure: Keyboard. All C4. Rhythm: q q ee q h, where ee = two eighths.
pages.cleffy: Still one note. Eighths on beat 3.

pages.figure: Staff with a dotted quarter + eighth, and a quarter rest.
pages.cleffy: Dotted quarter + eighth. Then a rest. Rests count too. Silence is in the rhythm.

pages.figure: Keyboard. One note. Rhythm: dotted-q e q rest q h.
pages.cleffy: Play it. One pitch. Just the time.

pages.figure: Diagram. Step 1: SEE music. Step 2: CLAP rhythm. Step 3: PLAY.
pages.cleffy: Real habit now. Before you play any new piece — tap the rhythm first. On your leg. On the table. On one key. THEN play the notes.

pages.figure: Staff with 4 bars of rhythm. Same-line notes. Pattern: q q e e | h q | e e e e q | h h (2 bars of half notes over 2 held).
pages.cleffy: New rhythm. You haven't seen it yet. Tap it first — on Middle C. Slow. Count in your head. One and two and three and four.

pages.figure: Staff. Top: same rhythm with pitches filled in (a real melody). Bottom: same rhythm rhythm-only.
pages.cleffy: Once the rhythm is in your body — add pitches. Now the melody is easy. You already know the WHEN. Just need the WHAT.

pages.figure: Staff. Same rhythm as page 11, now with pitches C D E F G A G F E D C.
pages.cleffy: Same rhythm. Now with pitches. Rhythm already lives in your hand.
pages.followup_cleffy: Notice how much easier it felt.

pages.figure: Hand-lettered sign: TAP BEFORE YOU PLAY.
pages.cleffy: Rule. Tap before you play. Every new piece. Every time.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 94. A hand tapping on a tabletop with music behind it.
pages.cleffy: Rhythm first. Pitch second. Tomorrow — Satie. Act 19 boss.

real_piano: Pick a new piece. Before playing it — tap the rhythm of the RH on your leg. All the way through. Then play.
Find a piece with tricky rhythm. Tap it on one key. Then add pitches. Feel the difference.

questions.figure: Rhythm-only staff: q q h
questions.prompt: How many beats total?

questions.figure: Diagram of the tap-first workflow.
questions.prompt: What comes before adding pitch?

questions.audio: A rhythm clapped: q e e q h
questions.prompt: How many notes did you hear?
On Middle C only, play the rhythm: quarter, quarter, half.
questions.accept: C4 × 3 with correct durations

remediation.title: Rhythm only, really slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two brains at once is a lot. Let's strip it down. One note. Slow tempo. Count out loud. Rhythm only.

completion.cleffy: Rhythm first. The trick every sight-reader uses. Tomorrow — Satie. Slow, spacious, beautiful. Act 19 boss.

title: Gymnopédie No. 1
subtitle: Act 19 · First Satie
goal: Slow and spacious — first Satie piece.
piece_context: Erik Satie's Gymnopédie No. 1. 1888. Slow, spare, dreamlike. The piece that invented ambient music. You play the opening phrase.

piece.title: Gymnopédie No. 1 (opening)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — very slow, legato, LH waltz pattern, RH simple melody

pages.figure: Cleffy sitting at a piano in soft light. Sheet music labeled GYMNOPÉDIE NO. 1 in front of him.
pages.cleffy: Erik Satie. Paris. 1888. He wrote three pieces called Gymnopédies. This is the first. Slow. Spacious. Sad, but gentle about it.

pages.figure: Big play button. Sheet music preview.

audio.A: Gymnopédie No. 1 opening, played with full pedal, very slow

pages.cleffy: Listen. Nothing is in a hurry. Every note gets its space. This isn't a piece you conquer. It's one you settle into.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH shown as three notes per bar (low D2, high A3, middle D3). RH shown as a slow melody above.
pages.cleffy: Two parts. Left hand — a slow waltz. Deep low D, then jump up to A, then settle back down to a middle D. Three beats. Right hand — a long, singing melody on top. (A simplified version — the real Satie LH is a bass note and a chord, but we're using single notes so you can hear each voice clearly.)

pages.figure: Bass staff. Bar showing D2 (beat 1), A3 (beat 2), D3 (beat 3). Labeled LOW-HIGH-MIDDLE.
pages.cleffy: Left hand, one bar at a time. Beat 1 — low D. Way down the keyboard. Beat 2 — A, a big jump up. The highest of the three. Beat 3 — D, just a step below that A. Middle-range. Low. High. Middle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D2, A3, D3 highlighted in order. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: Play it slow. D2, A3, D3. Low-high-middle. Let each note ring.

pages.figure: Two notes with a slur above them. Label: HOLD THE FIRST UNTIL THE SECOND PRESSES.
pages.cleffy: Legato. Smooth. No gaps. Hold each note until the next one starts. Every note connects to the one before. Satie needs this.

pages.figure: Bass staff, 4 bars of waltz bass. Metronome at 60.
pages.cleffy: LH only. Four bars. Legato. Each note held until the next presses.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Simple melody: F#4 (held), A4 (held), G4, F#4, E4 — long notes.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. Simple. Starts on F-sharp above Middle C. F-sharp is the black key between F and G. Then up to A. Then down slowly: G, F-sharp, E. Long notes. Space between.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F#4, A4, G4, F#4, E4 highlighted in sequence. Each half or whole note.
pages.cleffy: RH only. Five notes. Very slow. Let them breathe.

pages.figure: Staff with a note and a huge gap before the next. Label: LET IT RING.
pages.cleffy: Satie's secret — the silence between notes. Don't rush to the next one. Let the last one fade a beat. Space is part of the music.

pages.figure: Same RH melody. Tempo marked 54. 'As slow as feels right.'
pages.cleffy: Slower still. 54 bpm. Breathe between notes.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH held notes floating above LH's slow waltz. Alignment dots showing RH F# starts on beat 1 of bar 1, LH plays D-A-D underneath.
pages.cleffy: Put them together. RH holds F-sharp for a full bar (three beats). Underneath — LH plays D, A, D. RH moves to A in bar 2. LH keeps waltzing.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Guides ON. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Guides on. Slow. RH holds. LH waltzes underneath. Feel how the LH fills the space while the RH sustains.

pages.figure: Grand staff. No guides. Same tempo.
pages.cleffy: Again. No guides. Just you, the music, the quiet.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That's Satie. Slow, spacious, quiet. There's a mood in this piece that didn't exist before 1888.

pages.figure: Banner — last check before Act 19 closes.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 19 COMPLETE. Satie's face sketched small. A trophy. Cleffy at the piano, eyes half-closed.
pages.cleffy: Act 19, done. Sight-reading has a toolkit now. Eyes ahead. Recovery without stopping. Rhythm before notes. And Satie — a different kind of playing. Slow. Honest. Patient. Next up — chords. I-IV-V. The three chords behind most of popular music.

real_piano: Gymnopédie opening, once a day. Slower than you think is reasonable. Let it breathe.
Try it with the sustain pedal if you have one. Press when LH hits beat 1. Release and repress each bar.
Listen to the full Satie recording. Several pianists. Same notes, wildly different feel.
virtual_only: Replay pages 13-14 slowly. Then try at 54 bpm.

questions.figure: LH pattern D2-A3-D3 highlighted across bars.
questions.prompt: What's the LH shape called?

questions.figure: RH melody line F#-A-G-F#-E.
questions.prompt: Does this melody mostly move in...

questions.audio: Opening of Gymnopédie No. 1
questions.prompt: What's the feel?
Play the LH waltz: D2, A3, D3. Slow and legato.
questions.accept: Three notes played smoothly in order

remediation.title: Gymnopédie, hands separately
remediation.opening_cleffy: Satie is slow, but the hands moving together is tricky the first time. Let's split it. LH waltz on its own. RH melody on its own. Then combine.

completion.cleffy: First Satie. Quiet moment. Act 19 closed. Sight-reading is in your hands now — forever. Next act — chords. The I-IV-V engine.

title: Primary chords I–IV–V
subtitle: Act 20 · Chords & accompaniment
goal: Meet the three chords that build most pop and classical music — I, IV, and V in C major.
piece_context: You've seen triads and inversions. Now the three that do most of the work in Western music — the primary chords. Every pop song you know uses these.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up three colored chord blocks labeled I, IV, V.
pages.cleffy: Three chords. That's it. Most pop songs. Most hymns. A huge chunk of classical. All built from three chords. Today you meet them.

pages.figure: Staff in C major. C chord, F chord, G chord written as triads. Roman numerals I, IV, V labeled below.
pages.cleffy: Musicians number chords with Roman numerals. I (one), IV (four), V (five). In C major — I is C, IV is F, V is G. Why those numbers? Count up the scale. C is 1. F is 4. G is 5.

pages.figure: Keyboard and staff. C-E-G stacked. Label: I (C major). Home chord.
pages.cleffy: Chord I. C, E, G. The home chord of C major. Root-third-fifth, built on the 1st note of the scale. Feels like rest. Like a full stop.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4-E4-G4 all glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play the I chord. C, E, G. All three at once.

pages.figure: Keyboard and staff. F-A-C stacked. Label: IV (F major).
pages.cleffy: Chord IV. F, A, C. Built on the 4th note of the scale. Feels like motion. Like the song is going somewhere.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F4-A4-C5 all glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play the IV chord. F, A, C.

pages.figure: Keyboard and staff. G-B-D stacked. Label: V (G major).
pages.cleffy: Chord V. G, B, D. Built on the 5th note of the scale. Feels like tension. Like it wants to go home to I.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4-B4-D5 all glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play the V chord. G, B, D.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: I. B: IV. C: V.

audio.A: C major triad (C-E-G) held
audio.B: F major triad (F-A-C) held

pages.cleffy: Listen. I — rest. IV — lift. V — tension. Same three colors over and over in the music you already love.

pages.figure: Staff: I - IV - V - I. Four chord boxes.
pages.cleffy: Play them in order. I, IV, V, back to I. Hear the story — home, away, pulling, home.
pages.followup_cleffy: That little loop is the backbone of thousands of songs.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. A chord plays. I, IV, or V?

pages.figure: Scale 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-1 with 1, 4, 5 highlighted gold.
pages.cleffy: Why 1, 4, 5? Between them they cover every note of the scale. C-E-G, F-A-C, G-B-D. Seven notes, all accounted for. That's why you can harmonize almost any tune with just these three.

pages.figure: Keyboard. I - IV - V - I cycle, repeated twice.
pages.cleffy: One more time through the loop. Twice.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 96. Three colored blocks labeled I, IV, V with arrows cycling between them.
pages.cleffy: I, IV, V. Home. Away. Pulling. Home. Tomorrow — where they show up in an actual tune.

real_piano: Play I, IV, V, I in C major every day. Slow. Listen to each chord settle.
Try it starting on F (I = F, IV = Bb, V = C). Same three-chord shape, new key.

questions.figure: C-E-G triad on staff
questions.prompt: Which chord is this in C major?

questions.figure: G-B-D triad on staff
questions.prompt: Which chord is this in C major?

questions.audio: F major triad (F-A-C)
questions.prompt: In C major, this is...
Play the I chord in C major (C, E, G together).
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 simultaneously

remediation.title: I, IV, V slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three chords is a lot at once. We'll take them one at a time — play, hear, name — then stack them up.

completion.cleffy: Three chords. Met. Tomorrow — when and why they change inside a song.

title: Chord changes in a tune
subtitle: Act 20 · Chords & accompaniment
goal: Hear when the harmony under a melody shifts, and learn why it moves at all.
piece_context: Chords don't stay forever. They change under the melody — usually once per bar, sometimes more. Today you spot where.

pages.figure: A melody line on top staff. Chord boxes underneath — I, I, IV, V. Arrows showing where each chord sits.
pages.cleffy: You know the three chords. Real songs don't sit on one forever. The chord changes. Today — where it changes, and why.

pages.figure: Scale 1-2-3-4-5-6-7. The notes of C chord glow, then F chord, then G chord.
pages.cleffy: Each chord fits certain melody notes. I (C-E-G) fits C, E, G in the melody. IV (F-A-C) fits F, A, C. V (G-B-D) fits G, B, D. When the melody moves to notes a chord doesn't hold — the chord has to change.

pages.figure: Play button. A melody with only the C chord under it the whole time. Some notes clash.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle played with I chord held under the whole thing

pages.cleffy: Listen. Same chord under the whole tune. Some parts sound fine. Some sound wrong. That's why harmony moves.

pages.figure: Play button. Same melody with chords changing.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle with I-I-IV-I-V-I-V-I chord changes

pages.cleffy: Same melody. Chords change where they need to. Feel the difference. Everything fits now.

pages.figure: Twinkle Twinkle first 4 bars. Bar 1: C C G G (I). Bar 2: A A G (I). Bar 3: F F E E (IV). Bar 4: D D C (I).
pages.cleffy: Twinkle, first four bars. Chord under each bar. Bar 1: C, C, G, G — all in I. Bar 2: A, A, G — IV would fit, but I still works. Stay on I. Bar 3: F, F, E, E — F doesn't fit I. Change to IV. Bar 4: D, D, C — back to I.

pages.figure: Twinkle first 8 bars with chord symbols: C C F C | G C G C. (Simplified I I IV I V I V I.)
pages.cleffy: Full pattern for the opening: I, I, IV, I, V, I, V, I. C, C, F, C, G, C, G, C. Eight bars. Four chord changes. That's a real song.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Chord boxes in order: C, C, F, C, G, C, G, C.
pages.cleffy: Play the chord sequence on its own. One chord per bar.

pages.figure: Play button. Twinkle RH melody with the chords you just played underneath.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle with clear chord changes on each bar

pages.cleffy: Now listen. Melody on top. The chords you just played, underneath. Hear where the chord shifts. That's what we listened for.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Two bars play. Does the chord change between them?

pages.figure: Staff with bar lines. A chord symbol sits at the start of each bar.
pages.cleffy: Common rule of thumb — one chord per bar. Simple songs often hold one chord for a whole bar, then move at the bar line. Not always. But a good starting guess.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. A bar plays with its chord. I, IV, or V?

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH Twinkle melody first 8 bars. LH chord symbols shown.
pages.cleffy: Play the chord sequence again, slowly. The melody will play above on its own. Your job — keep the chords in time.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 97. A melody line with chord boxes underneath, shifting at bar lines.
pages.cleffy: Chords move because the melody moves past them. Tomorrow — how the left hand actually plays the chord. Blocked.

real_piano: Play the Twinkle chord sequence (C C F C G C G C) once a day. Whole-note chords, one per bar.
Pick any simple song you know. Try to hear where the chord changes. Sing-along on YouTube helps.

questions.figure: Bar with melody notes F, F, E, E in C major
questions.prompt: Which chord fits this bar best?

questions.audio: Two bars — first on I, second on IV
questions.prompt: Did the chord change between the bars?
Play I, IV, V, I as four whole-note chords.
questions.accept: C-E-G, F-A-C, G-B-D, C-E-G in order

remediation.title: Chord changes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hearing the shift takes a few listens. We'll go two bars at a time, chord by chord, until the moves become obvious.

completion.cleffy: Chords change where the melody asks them to. Tomorrow — the left hand takes the chord on.

title: Blocked accompaniment
subtitle: Act 20 · Chords & accompaniment
goal: Hold a full triad in the left hand while the right hand plays a melody.
piece_context: The simplest accompaniment in music — LH grabs the whole chord at once, holds it, lets it ring. Your RH melody floats on top.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH single notes on top. LH three stacked notes as a whole-note block.
pages.cleffy: RH plays the tune. LH grabs the whole chord. Holds it. That's blocked accompaniment. Simplest kind there is.

pages.figure: Close-up of LH hand position. Pinky on C3, middle on E3, thumb on G3.
pages.cleffy: LH plays all three chord notes at once. For I in C: pinky on C, middle on E, thumb on G. Press them together. Hold.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3-E3-G3 glowing in LH.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. Play the I chord blocked. C, E, G together. Hold it for four beats.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F3-A3-C4 glowing in LH.
pages.cleffy: Now IV. F, A, C. Pinky on F. Held. Whole bar.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G3-B3-D4 glowing in LH.
pages.cleffy: V. G, B, D. Pinky on G.

pages.figure: Four-bar staff, LH only. I - IV - V - I. Each a whole-note block.
pages.cleffy: All four in a row. I, IV, V, I. Left hand only. One chord per bar.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH melody: C D E C. LH: I chord held underneath as a whole note.
pages.cleffy: Now the other hand joins. RH plays C, D, E, C — four quarter notes. LH holds the I chord underneath the whole bar.

pages.figure: Treble staff. C D E C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Just the right hand first. C, D, E, C.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH melody + LH blocked I. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. LH presses the chord on beat 1 and holds. RH plays C, D, E, C on top.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1: RH C D E C, LH I block. Bar 2: RH F E D C, LH IV block.
pages.cleffy: Two bars now. Two different chords. Bar 1: RH C-D-E-C over I. Bar 2: RH F-E-D-C over IV. LH has to let go of I and grab IV on beat 1 of bar 2.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Two bars. LH changes chord when the bar line arrives.

pages.figure: Grand staff. I - IV - V - I. RH simple stepwise melody. LH blocked chords held.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. I, IV, V, I. LH blocks each chord. RH walks a simple melody on top. Slow.
pages.followup_cleffy: That sound — the chord ringing under a tune — that's most of Western music.

pages.figure: Same four bars. No glowing keys.
pages.cleffy: Again. No highlights.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 98. A whole-note chord block in the bass, a flowing melody above.
pages.cleffy: Blocked. Chord grabs, chord holds. Tomorrow — same notes, rolled out one at a time. Broken.

real_piano: Play the I-IV-V-I blocked progression, LH only, every day. Slow. Clean chord grabs.
Once LH is easy, add any RH melody you know that stays in C, E, G territory — Twinkle works.

questions.figure: LH staff with three stacked notes held as a whole note
questions.prompt: What kind of accompaniment is this?

questions.audio: A chord played as a solid block, held for a full bar
questions.prompt: Blocked or broken?
Play the I chord blocked in the left hand (C3, E3, G3 together).
questions.accept: C3 E3 G3 simultaneously

remediation.title: Blocked chords, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two hands doing different things — takes some time. We'll start with LH alone, then add one RH note, then a whole bar.

completion.cleffy: Blocked accompaniment in your hands. Tomorrow — rolled. Same notes, different feel.

title: Broken accompaniment
subtitle: Act 20 · Chords & accompaniment
goal: Play the same triads as quarter-note patterns — chord notes one at a time.
piece_context: Yesterday the chord was a block. Today it rolls — one note at a time. Same chord, completely different feel.

pages.figure: Two LH staves side by side. Left: I chord as whole-note block. Right: I chord as four quarter notes C-E-G-E.
pages.cleffy: Same chord. Two ways. Yesterday — block. Whole thing at once. Today — broken. One note at a time.

pages.figure: LH staff. Four quarter notes: C E G E. Beat numbers 1 2 3 4 above.
pages.cleffy: Broken chord — the same three notes, one per beat. Pattern for I: C on beat 1. E on beat 2. G on beat 3. E on beat 4. Still the I chord. Just stretched across the bar.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: blocked I. B: broken I (C-E-G-E).

audio.A: I chord played as a block, held four beats
audio.B: I chord as four quarter notes — C, E, G, E

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Different feel. Block — heavy, anchored. Broken — moving, rolling.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. C3-E3-G3 lighting in order, then E3.
pages.cleffy: Play broken I. C, E, G, E. Four quarter notes. LH. Pinky, middle, thumb, middle.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. F3-A3-C4 in order, then A3.
pages.cleffy: Broken IV. F, A, C, A. Same pattern, new chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. G3-B3-D4 in order, then B3.
pages.cleffy: Broken V. G, B, D, B.

pages.figure: Four bars, LH only. I - IV - V - I, each rolled as root-3rd-5th-3rd.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. I, IV, V, I. Broken. Left hand only. New chord on every beat 1.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: whole-note C. LH: broken I pattern (C-E-G-E).
pages.cleffy: RH holds one note — C. Whole bar. LH rolls the chord underneath. Let the LH rock.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH whole-note C. LH broken I. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. RH holds C. LH rolls C-E-G-E. Feel the hands doing different things.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1: RH C-D-E-C, LH broken I. Bar 2: RH F-E-D-C, LH broken IV.
pages.cleffy: Real thing. RH walks a melody. LH rolls a different chord each bar. Slow.

pages.figure: Grand staff. I - IV - V - I. RH melody, LH broken chords.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. I, IV, V, I. LH broken under. Don't rush. Let the chord tones ring.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hear that lift? That's motion in the left hand. Block sits. Broken breathes.

pages.figure: Same four bars. No glow.
pages.cleffy: Again. No highlights.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Blocked or broken?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 99. A broken chord rolling out left-to-right on the bass staff, melody floating on top.
pages.cleffy: Broken accompaniment. Chord split across the beats. Tomorrow — your first piece gets harmonized. Happy Birthday. Full sound.

real_piano: Play the I-IV-V-I broken progression, LH only, every day. Four quarters per chord. Steady.
Try it as a root-3rd-5th-3rd pattern in any key you know — C, F, or G works best.
When steady, sing any tune you like over it. Feel how the LH carries the chord while the voice floats.

questions.figure: LH staff with four quarter notes: C E G E
questions.prompt: What kind of accompaniment is this?

questions.audio: A broken chord — four quarter notes, C E G E
questions.prompt: Blocked or broken?
Play the I chord broken in the left hand — C, E, G, E as four quarter notes.
questions.accept: C3 E3 G3 E3 in order as quarters

remediation.title: Broken chords, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rolling the chord feels different from grabbing it. We'll go slow — one beat at a time, one chord at a time.

completion.cleffy: Broken in your hands. Tomorrow — act boss. Happy Birthday with full harmony.

title: Happy Birthday harmonized
subtitle: Act 20 · The return
goal: Play Happy Birthday — the tune you learned 75 lessons ago — with full left-hand harmony underneath.
piece_context: Lesson 25 was your first real song — Happy Birthday, right hand only, in C. Today you play it again. But now the left hand holds I, IV, and V underneath. Same tune. Full sound. This is what the whole act was for.

piece.title: Happy Birthday to You (harmonized)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — familiar RH melody with LH blocked triads
piece.full_sequence: RH: G G A G C B | G G A G D C | G G G(high) E C B A | F F E C D C. LH: I | I | IV I | V7 I.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a piano with a birthday cake on top. Sheet music labeled LESSON 25 on one side, LESSON 100 on the other. Arrow between them.
pages.cleffy: Lesson 25. You played Happy Birthday for the first time. Right hand only. Lesson 100. Today. Same song. Left hand joins now. Full harmony. Seventy-five lessons ago. Listen to what's different.

pages.figure: Two play buttons side by side. A: Lesson 25 version (RH only). B: today's version (RH + LH chords).

audio.A: Happy Birthday, right hand melody alone
audio.B: Happy Birthday with LH blocked I, IV, V chords underneath

pages.cleffy: Same tune. Both times. First — just the melody. Thin. Second — chords underneath. Full. Like a real piano piece.

pages.figure: RH staff showing full Happy Birthday melody. All four lines.
pages.cleffy: The melody. You know this. G, G, A, G, C, B. Line 1. G, G, A, G, D, C. Line 2. G, G, high G, E, C, B, A. Line 3 — remember, the third G leaps up an octave. F, F, E, C, D, C. Line 4.

pages.figure: Four phrases with chord symbols above each. Phrase 1: I. Phrase 2: I. Phrase 3: IV then I. Phrase 4: V then I.
pages.cleffy: The chord plan — simplified for us. Phrase 1 — I. Phrase 2 — I. Phrase 3 — IV, then I. Phrase 4 — V, then I. Six chords total. I, I, IV, I, V, I. Lands home.

pages.figure: LH keyboard showing three chord grabs: I (C3-E3-G3), IV (F3-A3-C4), V (G3-B3-D4).
pages.cleffy: Quick reminder — your three chord shapes. I: C, E, G. IV: F, A, C. V: G, B, D. Pinky always on the root. Middle on the third. Thumb on the fifth.

pages.figure: LH staff. Six blocked chords in order: I, I, IV, I, V, I.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. The six chords, as whole-note blocks. Slow. Clean grabs.

pages.figure: Treble staff with full melody. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. The full melody. You know it. Don't rush.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Phrase 1 RH: G G A G C B. LH: I block held.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 1. Both hands. LH grabs I on beat 1 and holds. RH plays the melody above.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Phrase 2 RH: G G A G D C. LH: I block held.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 2. LH still on I. RH: G, G, A, G, D, C.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Phrase 3 RH: G G G(high) E C B A. The third G leaps up an octave. LH: IV then I.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 3. Longer. And the famous one — the third G jumps up a whole octave. High G. LH changes mid-phrase too. Start on IV for G, G, high G, E. Switch to I for C, B, A. Feel the octave leap in the RH and the LH move underneath.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Phrase 4 RH: F F E C D C. LH: V7 then I.
pages.cleffy: Phrase 4. Closing. LH plays V7, then lands on I. V7 is our V chord with an F added on top — G, B, D, F — so it fits under the melody's F and pulls harder toward home. V7 under F, F, E. I under C, D, C. Ends home. Full stop.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full Happy Birthday. LH I I IV-I V-I. Training wheels on.
pages.cleffy: The whole thing. All four phrases. Six chord changes. Guides on. Slow. Breathe. If the LH is late — slow down. Let the chord arrive under the melody, not after it.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full piece. No glowing keys. Just the music.
pages.cleffy: No highlights now. You read, you play. Both hands. Stumble? Keep going.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Lesson 25 — thin little melody on the right hand. Lesson 100 — same tune, full sound, both hands carrying harmony. You hear the difference. Big difference.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Same piece. LH now broken instead of blocked — root-3rd-5th rolled through each bar.
pages.cleffy: Bonus version. Same chords — broken this time. LH rolls each chord as quarters. RH melody, same as always. Rocking, rolling feel. Try it.

pages.figure: Banner — ACT 20 BOSS mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones before the act closes.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 20 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding Happy Birthday sheet music with chord symbols above it. A gold 100 in the corner.
pages.cleffy: Act 20 — done. Lesson 100 — done. Quick look at what you did in this act. I, IV, V. Primary chords. Where chords change inside a song. Blocked accompaniment. Broken accompaniment. And today — Happy Birthday, the piece you earned on lesson 25, now harmonized with full left-hand chords. You're halfway through Tier 3. Clair de Lune is twenty-five lessons away. Next act — inversions, chord changes that actually glide, and Twinkle harmonized. More full sound.

real_piano: Play harmonized Happy Birthday every day this week. Blocked chords first. Slow.
When blocked feels easy — try the broken version. Same chords, rolled.
Play it for someone. Tell them you couldn't do this ninety-nine lessons ago.
Go back and play the Lesson 25 version (RH only). Feel how thin it sounds. That's how much you've grown.
virtual_only: Replay pages 12-13 three times this week

questions.figure: Chord symbols for Happy Birthday: I I IV-I V-I
questions.prompt: Which chord ends the piece?

questions.figure: LH staff showing the IV chord
questions.prompt: What are the three notes of the IV chord in C major?

questions.figure: Phrase 3 of Happy Birthday with chord symbols IV then I
questions.prompt: Does the LH change chord during phrase 3?

questions.audio: Happy Birthday played with full LH harmony
questions.prompt: Is the LH playing one note, or full chords?

questions.audio: Last chord of the piece — C-E-G held
questions.prompt: Which chord resolves the piece?
Play the final bar of harmonized Happy Birthday — RH D-C, LH I chord held.
questions.accept: D4-C4 in RH with C3-E3-G3 held in LH

remediation.title: Happy Birthday harmonized, phrase by phrase
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Two hands doing genuinely different things — that's real work. We'll break it down phrase by phrase. LH alone first, then RH, then glue them together. Slow.

completion.cleffy: Lesson 100. Your first piece, played back to you with both hands and full harmony. You're halfway through Tier 3. Keep going.

title: Chord inversions (play)
subtitle: Act 21 · Three shapes, one chord
goal: Play all three shapes of the C chord — root, 1st inversion, 2nd inversion.
piece_context: You saw inversions back in Act 18. Today your hand learns all three shapes.

pages.figure: Three handprints side by side on the keys. Each one a different shape. All play C major.
pages.cleffy: Same chord. Three handprints. Root. First inversion. Second inversion. Today your hand learns all three.

pages.figure: Staff and keyboard showing C-E-G, E-G-C, G-C-E side by side. Labeled root, 1st, 2nd.
pages.cleffy: C major in root position — C, E, G. Lowest note is C. First inversion — E, G, C. Lowest note is E. Second inversion — G, C, E. Lowest note is G. Same three letters. Rearranged.

pages.figure: Right-hand fingering shown on each shape. Root: 1-3-5. 1st inv: 1-2-5 or 1-3-5. 2nd inv: 1-3-5.
pages.cleffy: Right-hand fingers. Keep it simple. Root: 1, 3, 5 — thumb, middle, pinky. First inversion: 1, 2, 5. Second inversion: 1, 3, 5.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 glowing. Fingers 1-3-5.
pages.cleffy: Root position. Press C, E, G together. One press.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4, G4, C5 glowing. Fingers 1-2-5.
pages.cleffy: First inversion. E, G, C. Thumb on E. Pinky on C. Press all three at once.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4, C5, E5 glowing. Fingers 1-3-5.
pages.cleffy: Second inversion. G, C, E. Thumb on G. Pinky on E.

pages.figure: Three play buttons — root, 1st, 2nd. Each one a block chord.

audio.A: C major root position (C-E-G)
audio.B: C major first inversion (E-G-C)

pages.cleffy: Three versions of the same chord. Same letters. The one on bottom colors the sound a little differently. Same C major, though. All three.

pages.figure: Two keyboards. Top: hand jumping far to reach next chord. Bottom: hand staying close, using inversion.
pages.cleffy: Why three shapes? So your hand doesn't have to jump far. The closest shape wins.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Play root, then 1st inv, then 2nd inv — going up the keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Climb up. Root, then first, then second. Each one a fresh press. Don't rush.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Second, first, root — going down.
pages.cleffy: Back down. Second, first, root.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Order: root, 2nd, 1st, root.
pages.cleffy: Mixed order. Root, second, first, root again.
pages.followup_cleffy: Three handprints. Your hand knows them now.

pages.figure: Flashcard — 'The lowest note names the inversion. C on bottom = root. E on bottom = 1st. G on bottom = 2nd.'
pages.cleffy: One rule to remember. The lowest note tells you the inversion. C low = root. E low = 1st. G low = 2nd.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 101. Three handprints drawn on a keyboard. Cleffy holding up three fingers.
pages.cleffy: Three shapes of C. Your hand knows them. Tomorrow — smooth chord changes. Pick the right shape, barely move your hand.

real_piano: Play all three inversions of C major every day. Root, 1st, 2nd. Slowly.
Try the same three shapes with F major — F, A, C. Same 1-3-5 fingering.

questions.figure: Chord E-G-C stacked on staff
questions.prompt: Which inversion?

questions.figure: Chord G-C-E stacked on staff
questions.prompt: Which inversion?

questions.figure: Chord C-E-G stacked on staff
questions.prompt: Which inversion?

questions.audio: C major first inversion (E-G-C)
questions.prompt: Is this the same chord as C major?
Play C major in first inversion — E, G, C together.
questions.accept: E4, G4, C5 pressed simultaneously

remediation.title: One shape at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three handprints is a lot on one day. Let's do one shape, then the next, then the next — no rush.

completion.cleffy: Three shapes. One chord. Tomorrow — gliding between chords using the one that fits.

title: Smooth chord changes
subtitle: Act 21 · Share a finger
goal: Move between chords by sharing a finger — barely moving your hand.
piece_context: You know the three shapes. Now pick the shape that keeps your hand still.

pages.figure: Two hands. Left one jumps far across the keyboard between chords. Right one barely moves — one finger stays put.
pages.cleffy: Amateurs jump. Pros glide. The trick is picking the inversion that shares a note with the next chord. Share a finger. Slide the rest.

pages.figure: Three chord progression — C, F, G. Shown as root-position blocks far apart on keyboard.
pages.cleffy: C major. F major. G major. The three chords that build most songs. In root position they sit far apart. Your hand would leap every time.

pages.figure: C chord (C-E-G) and F chord (C-F-A) side by side. C note glowing in both.
pages.cleffy: Look at C major and F major. Both contain a C. If F is in second inversion — C, F, A — the C sits on bottom. One finger stays. The others shift.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, E, G glowing. Fingers 1-3-5.
pages.cleffy: Start at home. C major, root.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, F, A glowing. Thumb stays on C. Middle finger slides from E to F. Pinky from G to A.
pages.cleffy: F major, second inversion. C, F, A. Thumb doesn't move. Middle slides up one. Pinky slides up one. Tiny movements.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C root then F 2nd inv. Thumb circled — it stays.
pages.cleffy: Both in order. C root. Then F. Thumb stays on C the whole time.

pages.figure: F (C-F-A) and G (B-D-G) side by side. No shared note — but neighboring notes.
pages.cleffy: F to G. No shared note. But G in first inversion — B, D, G — sits right next to F's shape. Each finger slides one key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B3, D4, G4 glowing. Fingers 1-2-5.
pages.cleffy: G major, first inversion. B, D, G. Thumb on B. Pointer on D. Pinky on G.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F 2nd inv then G 1st inv. Arrows showing each finger sliding one step.
pages.cleffy: F to G. Every finger moves one step down. C down to B. F down to D — that's a bigger one. A down to G. Still tiny compared to leaping.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full C → F → G → C progression. Hand stays in one zone the whole time.
pages.cleffy: All three. C root. F second inversion. G first inversion. Back to C root. Your hand barely leaves its spot.
pages.followup_cleffy: Feel that? One hand position. Three chords.

pages.figure: Two play buttons — A: all root position (jumpy). B: with inversions (smooth).

audio.A: C, F, G, C all in root position — notes leap
audio.B: C, F/2nd, G/1st, C — notes glide

pages.cleffy: Same chords. Two versions. The second one sounds connected. That's voice-leading.

pages.figure: Flashcard — 'Voice-leading: picking the closest shape of the next chord.'
pages.cleffy: The technique has a name. Voice-leading. Classical composers built whole careers on it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same C → F → G → C. At a slightly faster tempo.
pages.cleffy: Once more. Cleaner. Tempo 60.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 102. One hand drawn with arrows showing tiny slides between chord shapes.
pages.cleffy: Share a finger. Slide the rest. Tomorrow — left hand and right hand doing different things at the same time.

real_piano: C → F → G → C with voice-leading. Slow. Feel the thumb staying on C from C to F.
Try the same idea starting from F. Find the shared note with C.

questions.figure: C major root (C-E-G) next to F major second inversion (C-F-A)
questions.prompt: Which note is shared between these two chords?

questions.audio: C, F/2nd, G/1st, C progression — smooth version
questions.prompt: Do the chords sound connected or jumpy?
Play C major root, then F major second inversion — keeping your thumb on C.
questions.accept: C-E-G then C-F-A

remediation.title: One chord change at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Voice-leading is new. Let's take one pair of chords, then the next pair, and build the progression slowly.

completion.cleffy: Smooth changes. Shared fingers. Tomorrow — hands doing different things.

title: Two-hand coordination
subtitle: Act 21 · Both hands, one mind
