goal: Play a pattern where the left and right hands do different things at the same time.
piece_context: Up until now, hands have mostly moved together or one held still. Today they pull apart.

pages.figure: Two hands drawn as two brains, connected by a thin line. Cleffy in the middle, conducting.
pages.cleffy: Until now your hands did the same job, or one held still. Today they pull apart. Left plays when right is resting. Right plays when left is resting.

pages.figure: Grand staff showing 4/4. LH bass C on beats 1 and 3 (quarter notes). RH C-E-G on beats 2 and 4 (quarter notes).
pages.cleffy: The pattern. 4/4 time. Left hand plays bass C on beats 1 and 3. Right hand plays a C chord on beats 2 and 4. One, two, three, four. LH, RH, LH, RH.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome clicks under it.

audio.A: LH bass C on 1 and 3, RH C-E-G chord on 2 and 4, steady at 60 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen. Low note. High chord. Low. High. Pulse never stops. The two hands trade beats.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bass C glowing. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Bass C on beats 1 and 3. Rest on 2 and 4. Count out loud: one, two, three, four.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, E, G glowing. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. C chord on beats 2 and 4. Rest on 1 and 3. Count: one, TWO, three, FOUR.

pages.figure: Diagram. Two boxes. Box 1: 'Say the beat out loud.' Box 2: 'Press only on that hand's beat.'
pages.cleffy: Before both hands — one trick. Say "one, two, three, four" out loud. LH presses on one and three. RH presses on two and four. Your voice stays steady. Your hands take turns.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH bass C on 1, 3. RH C-E-G on 2, 4. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Very slow. 50 bpm. Say it out loud as you play.

pages.figure: Same grand staff. Metronome still 50.
pages.cleffy: Again. Same tempo. Cleaner this time.
pages.followup_cleffy: Your hands are doing different things. That's real.

pages.figure: Brain diagram. Each hand's beat lit up at different moments.
pages.cleffy: It feels strange because your brain wants both hands to do the same thing. Slow practice teaches your brain otherwise. A week of this pattern and it will feel natural.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars now. Bar 1: LH C on 1, 3, RH C chord on 2, 4. Bar 2: LH G on 1, 3, RH B-D-G (G chord 1st inv) on 2, 4.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Same pattern. But the chord changes in bar 2. C chord first bar. G chord second bar. Both hands move to a new note at the start of bar 2.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. C, F, G, C progression underneath the pattern.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. C. F. G. C. Pattern stays the same. Chord underneath changes.

pages.figure: Score snippet — a simple waltz-adjacent accompaniment. Label: this pattern lives everywhere.
pages.cleffy: This pattern shows up in thousands of songs. Bass note. Chord. Bass. Chord. The engine of folk, pop, classical.

pages.figure: Four bars again. Tempo 60 now.
pages.cleffy: Once more. 60 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 103. Two hands drawn trading beats, steady metronome between them.
pages.cleffy: Hands doing different things. Brain catching up. Tomorrow — this same pattern, but we push the tempo.

real_piano: The four-bar pattern (C-F-G-C) every day. Start at 50 bpm. Don't push faster until it feels easy.
Count out loud. One, two, three, four. Your voice is the anchor.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7-8 three times. Build the slow version first.

questions.figure: Grand staff with LH on 1, 3 and RH on 2, 4
questions.prompt: When does the left hand play?

questions.audio: Pattern played — bass, chord, bass, chord
questions.prompt: Do both hands play on the same beat?
Play one bar: LH bass C on 1 and 3, RH C-E-G chord on 2 and 4. Slow.
questions.accept: LH and RH alternating quarter notes, one bar

remediation.title: One hand, then the other, then together
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two-hand coordination is a real wall. Expected. We're going to slow everything down and rebuild the pattern from each hand alone.

completion.cleffy: Hands doing different things. That was today. Tomorrow — speed it up. Slow, medium, fast.

title: Independence drill
subtitle: Act 21 · Slow, moderate, fast
goal: Drill yesterday's two-hand pattern at three tempos.
piece_context: Yesterday you got the pattern. Today you get fluent.

pages.figure: Three dials on a dashboard — SLOW (50), MODERATE (70), FAST (90). Metronome ticking.
pages.cleffy: Same pattern. Three speeds. Slow to get it right. Moderate to get it steady. Fast to get it real.

pages.figure: Flashcard — 'Drill days are for locking in. No new ideas. Just reps.'
pages.cleffy: Today isn't a new skill. Today is reps. That's how coordination settles in.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four-bar C-F-G-C progression. LH bass on 1, 3. RH chord on 2, 4.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday's pattern. C. F. G. C. LH bass note on one and three. RH chord on two and four.

pages.figure: One bar of C. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Warm up. One bar. C chord only. 50 bpm.

pages.figure: Four bars. Tempo dial on SLOW. 50 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Slow round. 50 bpm. Full four bars. Don't rush. Precision first.

pages.figure: Cleffy, eyes closed, listening.
pages.cleffy: Quick check. Were the beats even? Were both hands landing on the click? If yes — move on. If no — do slow again.

pages.figure: Four bars. Tempo dial on MODERATE. 70 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Moderate. 70 bpm. Faster than yesterday ever felt.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing to a dial spinning faster. Caption: 'Fast makes you trust your hands.'
pages.cleffy: Fast tempo does a thing slow tempo can't. It stops letting your brain micromanage. Your hands start doing the work.

pages.figure: Four bars. Tempo dial on FAST. 90 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Fast. 90 bpm. Trust your hands.

pages.figure: Graph showing accuracy dipping slightly at the fast tempo, then coming back.
pages.cleffy: Stumbled at 90? Normal. Fast always gets sloppy before it gets clean. Go back to slow for a round, then try fast again.

pages.figure: Four bars. 50 bpm again.
pages.cleffy: Back to slow. Clean up whatever got sloppy.

pages.figure: Four bars. 90 bpm. Final fast run.
pages.cleffy: One more at 90. Last time.
pages.followup_cleffy: Cleaner. Fingers know the pattern now.

pages.figure: Flashcard — 'The slow → fast → slow cycle is how every pianist gets faster.'
pages.cleffy: This cycle works for anything on piano. Slow until clean. Speed up. Sloppy. Slow again. Speed up cleaner. Every hard piece gets learned this way.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 104. Three dials on a dashboard, all spinning at different speeds.
pages.cleffy: Slow, moderate, fast. Tomorrow — Act 21 boss. Twinkle Twinkle with full harmony.

real_piano: The four-bar pattern at three tempos. Slow, moderate, fast, back to slow. Every day.
If 90 feels impossible — try 80 first. Build up one week at a time.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5, 7, 9 — one round of each tempo

questions.figure: Three dials — slow, moderate, fast
questions.prompt: Which order should you drill a pattern?

questions.audio: Same pattern at 50 bpm, then 90 bpm
questions.prompt: Is the pattern the same in both clips?
Play one bar of the pattern at 70 bpm.
questions.accept: LH C3 and RH C-E-G chord alternating, moderate tempo

remediation.title: Just the slow tempo
remediation.opening_cleffy: If fast broke it — that's the drill doing its job. Let's go back to 50 bpm and make slow feel effortless first.

completion.cleffy: Three tempos. Same pattern. Your hands faster than they were yesterday. Tomorrow — Act 21 boss. Twinkle Twinkle with both hands.

title: Twinkle Twinkle harmonized
subtitle: Act 21 · A full two-hand piece
goal: Play Twinkle Twinkle with the full melody in the right hand and supporting chords in the left.
piece_context: Everything from Act 21 lands here. Inversions. Voice-leading. Hand independence. One short, whole piece.

piece.title: Twinkle Twinkle Little Star
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — RH melody + LH chord progression

pages.figure: Cleffy holding Twinkle Twinkle sheet music. A grand staff behind him. Act 21 boss banner.
pages.cleffy: Act 21 boss. Twinkle Twinkle. Full melody in your right hand. Full harmony underneath. Everything from this act lands here.

pages.figure: Big play button. Preview of the whole arrangement.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle, both hands, RH melody with LH block chords, clean

pages.cleffy: Listen. Melody you know. Chords holding it up. The harmony is what makes it sound like real piano music, not a one-finger tune.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Full Twinkle melody: C C G G A A G | F F E E D D C.
pages.cleffy: The melody. Same one you knew before you could read. C, C, G, G, A, A, G. Twinkle twinkle little star. F, F, E, E, D, D, C. How I wonder what you are.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Melody notes above. Below, chord letters written out bar by bar: C | C | F | C || F | C | G | C.
pages.cleffy: The chords underneath. C. C. F. C. Then F. C. G. C. The three chords you've been working with all act.

pages.figure: Flashcard — 'C root. F 2nd inv. G 1st inv.' — same choices from lesson 102.
pages.cleffy: You've met these inversions before. C root. F in second inversion. G in first inversion. Your left hand barely moves the whole song.

pages.figure: Treble staff. First phrase highlighted. C C G G A A G.
pages.cleffy: Right hand, first phrase. Slow. C, C, G, G, A, A, G.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Second phrase. F F E E D D C.
pages.cleffy: Right hand, second phrase. Comes back down. F, F, E, E, D, D, C. Lands on home.

pages.figure: Bass/grand staff. LH plays one chord per bar: C root, C root, F 2nd inv, C root, F 2nd inv, C root, G 1st inv, C root.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. One chord per bar. Hold each one for four beats. C, C, F, C. F, C, G, C.

pages.figure: Grand staff. First half of the piece. RH melody over LH blocked chords.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. First half only. LH holds the chord. RH plays the melody above. Slow. 55 bpm.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Second half. Same structure.
pages.cleffy: Second half. F bar, C bar, G bar, C bar.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full Twinkle. Guides on. Tempo 55.
pages.cleffy: Full piece. With guides. Slow.

pages.figure: Same grand staff. No guides. You read, you play.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read the staff. Play it. Stumble? Keep going to the next bar.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Full melody. Full harmony. Both hands doing real work.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Tempo 75.
pages.cleffy: Up to proper tempo. 75 bpm. Don't stop for stumbles.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 21 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 21 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding Twinkle sheet music. A pyramid of acts showing 21/50 complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 21. Closed. You played a whole piece with full harmony. Your right hand and left hand each had their own job. Everything from this act showed up: three shapes of a chord, voice-leading, hand independence, drill. One week ago you were doing one-hand melodies. Next act — interval names. 2nds, 3rds, all the way to octaves. The vocabulary to talk about what you've been playing.

real_piano: Twinkle with both hands every day this week. Start slow. Build up to 80 bpm by week's end.
If you have family around — play it for someone. It's a real piece now.
For a challenge: try broken chords in the LH instead of blocked. Roll C-E-G one note at a time.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-13 — guided, then no guides, then at tempo

questions.figure: First bar of Twinkle — RH C, LH C-E-G chord
questions.prompt: What chord is the LH playing in bar 1?

questions.figure: LH chord on bar 7 — B-D-G
questions.prompt: Which chord and inversion?

questions.figure: Full piece on grand staff
questions.prompt: Does the LH play one chord per bar or many?

questions.audio: Twinkle with full harmony
questions.prompt: Is the melody the one you know?

questions.audio: Final bar of Twinkle ending on C major
questions.prompt: Does the piece end on the home note?
Play the first bar of Twinkle — RH C above LH C-E-G chord.
questions.accept: RH C4 and LH C major chord together

remediation.title: Twinkle, hands separately first
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Expected to be a real test. We're going to rebuild it — RH alone, LH alone, then bar by bar together. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 21 sealed. A full two-hand piece with harmony. You can play Twinkle Twinkle like it's real music. Because it is. Tomorrow — intervals by name. See a 3rd. Hear a 5th.

title: Interval names (2nd–5th)
subtitle: Act 22 · Intervals
goal: Give number names to the moves you already know — step is a 2nd, skip is a 3rd, plus 4ths and 5ths.
piece_context: New act. You've been playing steps, skips, and leaps since Tier 1. Now they get proper names — the grown-up vocabulary for distance between notes.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a staff with two notes — a C and an E — with a ruler-like bracket labelled '3rd' between them. Title: INTERVALS.
pages.cleffy: New act. Intervals. Every move between two notes has a size — and a name. You've been playing them for a hundred lessons. Today you learn what they're called.

pages.figure: Staff with C and D side by side. Bracket labelled '2nd'. Arrow showing count: 1 (C), 2 (D).
pages.cleffy: Here's the rule. Count both notes, including where you start. C to D — start on C, that's 1. D is 2. A 2nd. Every interval is counted this way. Include the first note.

pages.figure: Staff showing C to D. Below: 'STEP = 2ND'. A callout: 'The smallest move.'
pages.cleffy: A 2nd is a step. Line to next space, or space to next line. You've known this move since lesson 16. Today it just got a number.

pages.figure: Staff showing C to E. Bracket labelled '3rd'. Count: 1 (C), 2 (D), 3 (E).
pages.cleffy: A 3rd is a skip. Line to next line, or space to next space. C, D, E — count all three. A 3rd. Same move you've been calling a skip.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: 2nd (C-D). B: 3rd (C-E).

audio.A: C and D played one after the other, then together
audio.B: C and E played one after the other, then together

pages.cleffy: Listen. First — a 2nd. Small. Close. Second — a 3rd. A little more open.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and D4 glowing. Label: '2nd'.
pages.cleffy: Play a 2nd. C to D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and E4 glowing. Label: '3rd'.
pages.cleffy: Play a 3rd. C to E. Skip over the D.

pages.figure: Staff showing C to F. Count: 1 (C), 2 (D), 3 (E), 4 (F). Bracket labelled '4th'.
pages.cleffy: A 4th. C to F. Count all four. Bigger than a skip. Small leap. On the staff — line to space two spots away, or space to line two spots away.

pages.figure: Staff showing C to G. Count: 1-2-3-4-5. Bracket labelled '5th'.
pages.cleffy: A 5th. C to G. Count five. Line to line with one line between, or space to space with one space between. Important one — you've been playing 5ths in every chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and F4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 4th. C to F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and G4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 5th. C to G. Outer notes of a C chord. Familiar distance.

pages.figure: Table: 2nd=step, 3rd=skip, 4th=small leap, 5th=leap. Staff examples beside each.
pages.cleffy: Four names so far. 2nd — step. 3rd — skip. 4th — small leap. 5th — leap. Old moves, new labels.

pages.figure: Staff showing C, then C-D (2nd), C-E (3rd), C-F (4th), C-G (5th). Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Climb the ladder. Each time return to C, then reach one note farther. C-D. C-E. C-F. C-G. Play all four pairs.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 106. A ruler with four marks: 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th.
pages.cleffy: Four intervals named. Tomorrow — the bigger ones. 6th, 7th, octave.

real_piano: Pick any white key. Play a 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 5th above it. Say the name as you play.
Play the C chord (C-E-G). Notice: C to E is a 3rd, E to G is a 3rd, C to G is a 5th. Chords are stacks of intervals.

questions.figure: Staff with C and E
questions.prompt: What interval is this?

questions.figure: Staff with C and G
questions.prompt: What interval is this?

questions.figure: Staff with C and D
questions.prompt: A step is also called a...

questions.audio: C and G played together — a 5th
questions.prompt: Is this a small interval or an open one?
Play a 3rd above C. (C to E.)
questions.accept: C4 and E4

remediation.title: Intervals by counting, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Numbers on moves you already know. Let's slow it down — count every note, including the first.

completion.cleffy: Four intervals in the pocket. 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th. Tomorrow — the reach-farther ones.

title: Interval names (6th–octave)
subtitle: Act 22 · Intervals
goal: Name the bigger intervals — 6th, 7th, and the octave (8th).
piece_context: Second half of the interval family. Same counting rule, just farther apart.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a ruler stretched wide between two C's an octave apart. Title: THE BIG ONES.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday — 2nd through 5th. Today — 6th, 7th, octave. Same counting rule. Bigger reaches.

pages.figure: Staff showing C4 to A4. Count marked: 1-2-3-4-5-6. Bracket labelled '6th'.
pages.cleffy: A 6th. C up to A. Count both notes and everything between. Six steps. Warm, full sound. Common in melodies.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and A4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 6th. C to A.

pages.figure: Staff showing C4 to B4. Count: 1-2-3-4-5-6-7. Bracket labelled '7th'.
pages.cleffy: A 7th. C up to B. Almost an octave — but stops one note short. Big reach. Slightly unsettled sound.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and B4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play a 7th. C to B. Stretch.

pages.figure: Staff showing C4 to C5. Count: 1-2-3-4-5-6-7-8. Bracket labelled 'OCTAVE (8th)'.
pages.cleffy: The octave. C to the next C. Eight notes — count them. Same letter name, higher. You've known octaves since lesson 8.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and C5 glowing. Thumb on C4, pinky on C5.
pages.cleffy: Play an octave. C to C. Thumb and pinky.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: 6th. B: 7th. C: octave.

audio.A: C4 and A4 played together — a 6th
audio.B: C4 and B4 played together — a 7th
audio.C: C4 and C5 played together — an octave

pages.cleffy: Three sounds. 6th — warm. 7th — restless. Octave — open and settled.

pages.figure: Three staff snippets side by side: 6th (line to space farther up), 7th (line to line), octave (line to line, one more step).
pages.cleffy: Look at the staff shapes. A 6th crosses more lines than a 5th. A 7th is almost the whole staff. An octave — same letter, one clef-width apart.

pages.figure: Keyboard sequence: C4-A4, then C4-B4, then C4-C5.
pages.cleffy: Play all three. C to A. C to B. C to C. 6th, 7th, octave.

pages.figure: Full interval table. 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, octave — with staff examples of each.
pages.cleffy: The whole family now. Eight names. 2nd through octave. Every distance between two notes.

pages.figure: Staff showing C paired with each higher note: D, E, F, G, A, B, C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Climb the whole ladder. C returns each time. C-D, C-E, C-F, C-G, C-A, C-B, C-C.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Every interval in the language of music — played once.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 107. A ruler with all eight marks, 2nd through octave.
pages.cleffy: Eight names. Full vocabulary. Tomorrow — your ears learn to hear them.

real_piano: From any C, play up to each note and say the interval name: 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, octave.
Find an octave in any song you know. They're everywhere — big-sounding leaps.

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and A4
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and C5
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and B4
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C4 and C5 played together
questions.prompt: Same letter, higher. That's an...
Play an octave above C4.
questions.accept: C4 and C5

remediation.title: Bigger intervals, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The counting trips people up at 6 and 7. We'll take each one slow, count every note, say the name.

completion.cleffy: Eight intervals. Full family. Tomorrow — your ear gets in on it. Hearing the size, not seeing it.

title: Hearing intervals
subtitle: Act 22 · Intervals
goal: Tell intervals apart by ear — the sound of each size.
piece_context: Eyes know the names. Now the ears catch up. Every interval has a personality — small or open, sweet or restless.

pages.figure: Cleffy with cupped hand to ear. Eight intervals shown as colored bars of different widths.
pages.cleffy: You can name them on paper. Can you name them by sound? Today the ear catches up.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: 2nd. B: octave.

audio.A: C and D played together — a 2nd, close and tight
audio.B: C and C an octave apart — wide and open

pages.cleffy: Start with the extremes. First — a 2nd. Close. Almost buzzy. Second — an octave. Wide open. Same note really.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 2ND.

audio.A: C and D played together

pages.cleffy: The 2nd. Neighbors. Tight and a little scratchy. This is the sound of a step.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 3RD.

audio.A: C and E played together — sweet, the start of a major chord

pages.cleffy: The 3rd. Sweet. Relaxed. First two notes of a C chord. Happy sound.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Two options: 2nd, 3rd.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. 2nd or 3rd? Small and buzzy — or sweet and open?

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 4TH.

audio.A: C and F played together — strong, hymn-like

pages.cleffy: The 4th. Strong. Hymn-like. Think of the first two notes of 'Here Comes the Bride.' That's a 4th.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 5TH.

audio.A: C and G played together — open, Star Wars opening

pages.cleffy: The 5th. Open. Powerful. First two notes of the Star Wars theme. Or Twinkle Twinkle. Rock solid.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Two options: 4th, 5th.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. 4th or 5th?

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 6TH.

audio.A: C and A played together — warm, movie-theme sweet

pages.cleffy: The 6th. Warm. A little swoony. Think of 'My Bonnie Lies Over the Ocean' — first two notes.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 7TH.

audio.A: C and B played together — tense, wanting to resolve

pages.cleffy: The 7th. Tense. Wants to resolve. Almost an octave — but stops short. Restless.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: OCTAVE.

audio.A: C4 and C5 played together — open, hollow, same note

pages.cleffy: The octave. Hollow. Same note really. Think 'Somewhere Over the Rainbow' — first two notes jump an octave.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Options: 6th, 7th, octave.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. 6th, 7th, or octave?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 5. Options: 2nd, 3rd, 5th, 6th, octave.
pages.cleffy: Mixed. Five rounds. Any of these five.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 108. Eight wave shapes, each a different width — the sound-shapes of each interval.
pages.cleffy: You hear them now. Small. Sweet. Strong. Open. Tomorrow — sight-quiz. See it, name it, fast.

real_piano: Pick two random white keys. Play them together. Count the interval. Say the name out loud.
Play a favorite melody. Notice the first two notes — is it a 2nd, a 3rd, a 4th? Most famous tunes start with a small interval.

questions.figure: Staff with C and G
questions.prompt: Before playing — what interval is this?

questions.audio: C and D played together
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C and G played together
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C4 and C5 played together
questions.prompt: What interval?
Play any 5th above C.
questions.accept: C4 and G4

remediation.title: Hearing intervals, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Ears take longer than eyes. Normal. We'll group them by feel — small, open, tense — and build up.

completion.cleffy: Ears trained. You hear distance now, not just notes. Tomorrow — fast sight-quiz. Eyes catch up to the ears.

title: Interval sight-quiz
subtitle: Act 22 · Intervals
goal: See an interval on the staff and name it fast — no counting, just shape-recognition.
piece_context: You know the names. You hear the sounds. Now — sight-read the shapes. Fast. The goal is recognition, not counting.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a stopwatch. A staff behind him showing six interval pairs.
pages.cleffy: Third day of intervals. You can count. You can hear. Today — see and name. No counting. Just recognize.

pages.figure: Two staff snippets. Left: line-to-line (odd). Right: line-to-space (even).
pages.cleffy: First shortcut. Line to line, or space to space — the number is odd. 3rd, 5th, 7th. Line to space, or space to line — the number is even. 2nd, 4th, 6th, octave.

pages.figure: Staff chart. Each interval shown: 2nd (adjacent), 3rd (one skipped), 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, octave. Labels under each.
pages.cleffy: Memorize the shapes. A 3rd — one line between two notes. A 5th — two lines between. A 7th — three lines between. Even ones — count the spaces between.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. A staff with two notes. Options: 3rd, 5th.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. 3rd or 5th? One line between — 3rd. Two lines between — 5th.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Options: 2nd, 4th.
pages.cleffy: Even ones now. 2nd or 4th?

pages.figure: Staff showing C4 and C5. Highlight: the notes are one full clef apart.
pages.cleffy: The octave has a signature shape. Two C's, two E's, two G's — same letter, one clef-width apart. Learn it at a glance.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Options: 6th, Octave.
pages.cleffy: 4 rounds. 6th or octave?

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 5. A staff with two notes each round. Options: 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th.
pages.cleffy: Mixed. Five rounds. Small-and-medium intervals.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Any interval 2nd through octave.
pages.cleffy: Everything. Six rounds. Any of the eight.

pages.figure: Staff with C4 and E4. Prompt: name it, then play it.
pages.cleffy: Name this one in your head. Then play both notes.

pages.figure: Staff with G3 and D4. Prompt: name it, then play it.
pages.cleffy: Name. Then play.

pages.figure: Staff with F4 and F5 (octave).
pages.cleffy: One more. Big one. Name. Play.
pages.followup_cleffy: Octave. You saw it before you counted it.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 8. Faster tempo. All intervals. Label: SPEED ROUND.
pages.cleffy: Speed round. Eight rounds. Trust the shape.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 109. A staff with a blur of interval pairs, each labelled.
pages.cleffy: Shapes learned. You see a 3rd without counting now. Tomorrow — boss. Everything together.

real_piano: Open any piece of sheet music. Pick five random pairs of adjacent notes. Name each interval.
Line-to-line means odd. Space-to-space means odd. Line-to-space means even. Chant it till it sticks.

questions.figure: Staff with two notes, line to next line
questions.prompt: Odd or even interval?

questions.figure: Staff with G and B (one line between)
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and G4
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C and E played together
questions.prompt: What interval?
Play a 4th above G. (G to C.)
questions.accept: G3 or G4 plus the C a 4th above

remediation.title: Shapes, slowly
remediation.opening_cleffy: Speed comes after shape. We'll look at each interval shape for a beat longer. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Shapes locked in. You recognize faster than you count. Tomorrow — act boss. See, hear, name, play. All eight intervals.

title: Interval drill: all 8
subtitle: Act 22 · Intervals boss
goal: Master all eight intervals across all four modes — see, hear, name, play.
piece_context: Act 22 closes. Four days of intervals — names, sounds, shapes — become one test. Every interval from 2nd to octave, every mode you know.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a fan of eight cards — 2nd through octave. Act 22 boss banner.
pages.cleffy: Act 22 boss. Eight intervals. Four modes. One lesson. Everything you built this act, proved today.

pages.figure: A big reference chart: all eight intervals, each with a staff example, a keyboard distance, and a one-word feel (tight, sweet, strong, open, warm, tense, hollow).
pages.cleffy: The whole family. Eight distances. 2nd tight. 3rd sweet. 4th strong. 5th open. 6th warm. 7th tense. Octave hollow.

pages.figure: One play button. Plays all eight intervals from C, in order.

audio.A: C4 played with each note from D up to C5 in turn — all eight intervals

pages.cleffy: Listen — every interval, once, climbing. 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, octave.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. A staff with two notes. SEE mode — name the interval.
pages.cleffy: SEE mode. Six rounds. Any interval.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. HEAR mode. Two notes play; you name.
pages.cleffy: HEAR mode. Six rounds. Listen, name.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Prompt: play a 2nd, then a 3rd, both above C.
pages.cleffy: PLAY mode. Start small. C up a 2nd (D), then C up a 3rd (E).

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 with F4, then C4 with G4.
pages.cleffy: C up a 4th (F), then C up a 5th (G).

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 with A4, then B4, then C5.
pages.cleffy: The big three. C up a 6th. 7th. Octave.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G as the root. Play a 3rd (B), 5th (D), octave (G).
pages.cleffy: New root — G. Up a 3rd to B. Up a 5th to D. Up an octave to G. Intervals work from any note.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. Faster staff drill — any interval, any root.
pages.cleffy: Mixed roots, mixed intervals. Six.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 6. HEAR drill at speed.
pages.cleffy: Mixed HEAR. Six more. Trust the feel.

pages.figure: Staff showing C followed by every interval up to the octave. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Build the whole ladder, from C. C-D, C-E, C-F, C-G, C-A, C-B, C-C. Seven pairs. All eight intervals named along the way.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Ladder now coming back down from C5.
pages.cleffy: Coming back down. C5 to every lower note. Intervals work going down too.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Up the ladder and back. Every interval, both directions.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 22 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. Boss check — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 22 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding the fan of eight interval cards. Pyramid showing 22/50 acts done.
pages.cleffy: Act 22. Closed. You see intervals. You hear intervals. You name them. You play them. Four days ago, a 3rd was just a skip. Now you know it's the warm one, the sweet one, the first two notes of a C chord. Next act — new keys. D major. Two sharps. B-flat major. Two flats. And the Minuet in G gets moved to D.

real_piano: Pick a favorite tune. Name the interval between every two notes, as you play. First few tries will be slow. That's the point.
Make a game: close your eyes, have someone play two notes. Name the interval.
Play the C chord as stacked 3rds: C to E (3rd), E to G (3rd). Then F chord: F to A, A to C. Every major chord = two stacked 3rds.

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and F4
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.figure: Staff with C4 and C5
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.figure: Staff with G3 and D4
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C and D played together
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C and A played together
questions.prompt: What interval?

questions.audio: C4 and C5 played together
questions.prompt: What interval?
Play a 5th above C. (C to G.)
questions.accept: C4 and G4

questions.prompt: Play an octave above G. (G to the next G.)
questions.accept: G3 and G4, or G4 and G5

remediation.title: Intervals by mode, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — big one. Expected. We'll rebuild by mode. See first, until every shape is automatic. Then hear. Then play. One interval at a time.

completion.cleffy: Act 22 sealed. Intervals used to be shapes on a page. Now they're sounds, names, distances in your fingers. Tomorrow — new keys. D major. Two sharps. Your old friend Minuet in G moves house.

title: D major
subtitle: Act 23 · Two sharps — F# and C#
goal: Read and play in D major — two sharps, F-sharp and C-sharp.
piece_context: Act 23 opens. You know G major (1 sharp) and F major (1 flat). Now we add a second accidental. D major is next up the sharp ladder.

pages.figure: Treble staff with D major key signature — F# on top line, C# in third space. Title: D MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: New key. Two sharps. F-sharp and C-sharp. Every time. Same shapes you already read — new colors.

pages.figure: Close-up of the D major key signature. Arrow pointing to F# on line 5 and C# in space 3.
pages.cleffy: The two sharps sit in order. F-sharp first — top line. Then C-sharp — third space. Any F, any octave — sharp. Any C, any octave — sharp.

pages.figure: Staff with D major scale spelled out: D E F# G A B C# D. Key signature at the left.
pages.cleffy: D major scale. D E F-sharp G A B C-sharp D. Two black keys in there. Both the sharps the key signature warned you about.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D E F# G A glowing (D position). Key signature F# C#.
pages.cleffy: D position — D E F-sharp G A. F is the only black key in the first five. Go slow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full D major scale highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Full D major scale. D to D, eight notes. Watch the F and the C — both sharp.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: D major scale ascending

pages.cleffy: D major. Bright. A little warmer than G. Listen — two black keys slide by.

pages.figure: Staff in D major. Melody: D F# A F# D. Mostly skips.
pages.cleffy: Short melody in D. D F-sharp A F-sharp D. The F has no symbol next to it — key signature already said it's sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D F# A F# D.
pages.cleffy: Play it.

pages.figure: Staff in D major. Melody: D E F# G A B C# D. Key signature at left.
pages.cleffy: Now a melody that uses both sharps. D E F-sharp G A B C-sharp D. Same shape as the scale — because it is.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full scale phrase.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Two black keys — F and C.

pages.figure: Staff in D major. Various notes — some F's, some C's, some naturals.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. In D major — sharp or natural?

pages.figure: Staff in D. Melody: D A F# A D C# D. Key sig at left.
pages.cleffy: Real phrase. 7 notes. Both sharps show up.

pages.figure: Table: C major (0), G major (1 sharp: F#), D major (2 sharps: F#, C#).
pages.cleffy: Three keys you know now. C — none. G — one. D — two. Each step up adds one sharp.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 111. D major key signature, two sharps highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D major is yours. Two sharps, handled. Tomorrow — the other side. B-flat major. Two flats.

real_piano: Play the full D major scale, both hands, slow. Every F and every C is sharp.
Play a phrase: D A F# A D C# D. Feel how the black keys fall under your fingers.

questions.figure: D major key signature
questions.prompt: Which two notes are sharp in D major?

questions.figure: Staff in D major with a note in the third space (C)
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.audio: D major scale
questions.prompt: How many black keys do you hear?
Play the first 5 notes of D major: D-E-F#-G-A.
questions.accept: D4 E4 F#4 G4 A4

remediation.title: D major, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two sharps is one more than last time. Slow walk — F-sharp first, then C-sharp, then both together.

completion.cleffy: D major, locked in. Tomorrow — two flats. B-flat major.

title: B-flat major
subtitle: Act 23 · Two flats — B♭ and E♭
goal: Read and play in B-flat major — two flats, B-flat and E-flat.
piece_context: The flat side of the ladder. F major was one flat. B-flat is two. Tons of wind-instrument music lives here — and plenty of piano.

pages.figure: Treble staff with B♭ major key signature — B♭ on middle line, E♭ in top space. Title: B♭ MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: New key. Two flats. B-flat and E-flat. Every time. You already know one of them — B-flat from F major. Now the other joins.

pages.figure: Close-up of the B♭ key signature. Arrow pointing to B♭ on line 3 and E♭ in space 4.
pages.cleffy: Two flats, in order. B-flat first — middle line. Then E-flat — fourth space, the top one. Any B, any octave — flat. Any E, any octave — flat.

pages.figure: Staff with B♭ scale: B♭ C D E♭ F G A B♭. Key signature at the left.
pages.cleffy: B-flat scale. B-flat C D E-flat F G A B-flat. Two black keys. Both the flats the key signature named.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B♭ C D E♭ F glowing. Key signature B♭ E♭.
pages.cleffy: B-flat position — B-flat C D E-flat F. Two black keys in the first five. Slow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full B♭ major scale highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Full B-flat major scale. Eight notes. Both B's are flat. E is flat too.

pages.figure: Play button.

audio.A: B-flat major scale ascending

pages.cleffy: B-flat major. Warm. Rounder than D. Horn players love this key.

pages.figure: Staff in B♭ major. Melody: B♭ D F D B♭. Skips on the B-flat chord.
pages.cleffy: Short melody. B-flat D F D B-flat. No symbol in front of the B — the key signature already handled it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B♭ D F D B♭.
pages.cleffy: Play it.

pages.figure: Staff in B♭ major. Melody: B♭ C D E♭ D C B♭. E♭ highlighted — no symbol needed.
pages.cleffy: Now a melody that hits E-flat too. B-flat C D E-flat D C B-flat. Both flats in one phrase.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase using B♭ and E♭.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Two black keys — B and E.

pages.figure: Staff in B♭ major. Various notes.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. In B-flat major — flat or natural?

pages.figure: Staff in B♭. Melody: F G A B♭ A G F E♭ F.
pages.cleffy: Real phrase. 9 notes. Both flats show up.

pages.figure: Table: C major (0), G major (1#), D major (2#), F major (1♭), B♭ major (2♭).
pages.cleffy: Five keys now. Sharps side: G, D. Flats side: F, B-flat. C sits in the middle — no sharps, no flats.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 112. B♭ major key signature, two flats highlighted.
pages.cleffy: B-flat major, in. Tomorrow — every major key has a twin. Minor keys.

real_piano: B-flat major scale up and back, both hands. Every B flat, every E flat.
Try this phrase: F G A B-flat A G F E-flat F. The black keys should start feeling easy.

questions.figure: B♭ major key signature
questions.prompt: Which two notes are flat in B-flat major?

questions.figure: Staff in B♭ major with a note in the top space (E)
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.audio: B-flat major scale
questions.prompt: Which key — D major or B-flat major?
Play B-flat C D E-flat F.
questions.accept: Bb3 C4 D4 Eb4 F4

remediation.title: B-flat major, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two flats. Slow pass — B-flat first, then E-flat, then both in a phrase.

completion.cleffy: B-flat major, done. Five keys known — three sharp-side, two flat-side, plus C. Tomorrow — every major key has a shadow. Minor keys.

title: Relative minors
subtitle: Act 23 · Every major key has a twin
goal: Hear that every major key has a matching minor key — same notes, different home.
piece_context: You've been in major keys the whole time. Now the shadow. A minor key uses the same notes as a major one — it just starts in a different spot and sounds sad instead of bright.

pages.figure: Cleffy standing between two doors. One bright and sunlit (major). One dim and blue (minor). Title: TWIN KEYS.
pages.cleffy: Every major key has a twin. Same seven notes. Different feel. Bright one — major. Dim one — minor.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C major scale. B: A minor scale.

audio.A: C major scale from C
audio.B: A minor scale from A (natural minor, white keys)

pages.cleffy: Same seven white keys, both times. First — starting on C. Bright. Major. Second — starting on A. Sad. Minor. Identical notes. Different home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C highlighted, then arrow down three half-steps to A. Label: 'Relative minor = 3 half-steps down.'
pages.cleffy: The trick — count down three half-steps from the major key. C down three is A. So C major's twin is A minor.

pages.figure: Table. C major / A minor. G major / E minor. F major / D minor. Each with its shared key signature.
pages.cleffy: Three pairs you'll see everywhere. C major — A minor. No sharps, no flats. G major — E minor. One sharp. F major — D minor. One flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C D E F G A B C.
pages.cleffy: C major scale. White keys, C to C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A B C D E F G A.
pages.cleffy: Same white keys. Start on A this time. Feel the difference — same notes, new mood.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: C major scale played both ways
audio.B: A minor scale played both ways

pages.cleffy: Listen again. Same notes, different landing. Major wants to rest on C. Minor wants to rest on A.

pages.figure: Staff. G major key signature (F#). Two scales side by side — G to G, and E to E.
pages.cleffy: G major has one sharp — F-sharp. Its twin is E minor. Same F-sharp. Starts on E.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E F# G A B C D E.
pages.cleffy: E minor scale. Starts on E. Every F is sharp — key is shared with G major.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: G major scale
audio.B: E minor scale

pages.cleffy: G first — bright. Then E minor — shadowy. Same sharp, different feel.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Bright or sad? Major or minor?

pages.figure: Staff. F major key signature (B♭). Two scales — F to F, and D to D.
pages.cleffy: F major has one flat. Its twin is D minor. Same flat, same seven notes. D minor just starts lower down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D E F G A B♭ C D.
pages.cleffy: D minor scale. Every B is flat — shared with F major.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 113. Three pairs of doors: C/A, G/E, F/D. Major bright, minor shaded.
pages.cleffy: Three pairs of twins. C and A. G and E. F and D. Same notes, different homes. Tomorrow — taking a tune and moving it to a new home. Transposition.

real_piano: Play C major scale, then A minor scale. Same white keys — just a different starting note.
Play a short tune you know, then try landing it on A instead of C. Notice the mood flip.

questions.figure: Table of major/minor pairs
questions.prompt: What is the relative minor of C major?

questions.figure: Key signature with one sharp
questions.prompt: This key signature belongs to G major and which minor?

questions.audio: A minor scale ascending
questions.prompt: Major or minor?
Play the A minor scale from A to A — all white keys.
questions.accept: A3 B3 C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 A4

remediation.title: Twin keys, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Major and minor is the biggest mood-flip in music. We'll slow it — one pair at a time, ear first.

completion.cleffy: Three pairs of twins known. Major and minor. Tomorrow — taking a tune and moving it to a new key. Transposition.

title: Transposition
subtitle: Act 23 · Same tune, new home
goal: Take a melody you know and move it into a different key.
piece_context: All the key-signature work from this act pays off here. If you know a tune in C, you can play it in G, F, D, or B-flat — same shape, new home.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a small house. Three keys on the wall behind him — C, G, F. Title: SAME TUNE, NEW HOME.
pages.cleffy: One tune. Many homes. Take a melody you know. Start it on a new note. Same shape follows. That's transposition.

pages.figure: Staff in C major. Mary Had a Little Lamb, first phrase: E D C D E E E.
pages.cleffy: You know this one. Mary Had a Little Lamb. In C major. E D C D E E E. Mar-y had a lit-tle lamb.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 D4 C4 D4 E4 E4 E4 highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Play it in C. Your old key.

pages.figure: Staff close-up. Arrows showing: step down, step down, step up, step up, same, same.
pages.cleffy: Look at the shape — not the letters. Down a step, down a step, up a step, up a step, same, same. That shape is the tune. The letters are just what it lands on.

pages.figure: Arrow from C position to G position. Staff shows G major key signature.
pages.cleffy: Move every note up a fifth. Keep the same shape and the same roles. In C, Mary starts on E — the third note of the scale (the mediant). In G, the third note of the scale is B. So Mary starts on B, not G. E becomes B. D becomes A. C becomes G. New home — G major.

pages.figure: Staff in G major (F# key sig). Melody: B A G A B B B.
pages.cleffy: Mary in G. B A G A B B B. Same tune. Same shape. Same roles — 3 2 1 2 3 3 3. New home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B4 A4 G4 A4 B4 B4 B4.
pages.cleffy: Play it in G. Start on B — the mediant, same role as E was in C.

pages.figure: Two play buttons.

audio.A: Mary in C
audio.B: Mary in G

pages.cleffy: Same tune, twice. Different home. Your ear recognizes it instantly — even though almost no notes are the same.

pages.figure: Staff in F major (B♭ key sig). Melody: A G F G A A A.
pages.cleffy: One more. F major this time. Third of F major is A — so Mary starts on A. Same role, new letter. E becomes A. D becomes G. C becomes F. A G F G A A A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A G F G A A A.
pages.cleffy: Play it in F. No black keys in this phrase — lucky.

pages.figure: Three-step recipe card. 1. Know the shape. 2. Pick a new starting note. 3. Let the key signature handle the accidentals.
pages.cleffy: Three steps. That's the whole trick. Know the shape. Pick a new home. Let the key signature do the rest.

pages.figure: Staff. Full first line of Mary: E D C D E E E D D D E G G.
pages.cleffy: Full first line in C. Both phrases.

pages.figure: Staff in G major. Full first line transposed.
pages.cleffy: Same full line. Now in G. Every note up a fifth. B A G A B B B A A A B D D.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. You just transposed a tune you've known since you were three.

pages.figure: Round counter. Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Mary plays — which key is she in?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 114. Mary Had a Little Lamb written three times — in C, G, and F.
pages.cleffy: Same shape. New homes. Tomorrow — act boss. We take a Minuet you already know, and move it.

real_piano: Pick a tune you know — Hot Cross Buns, Twinkle, anything simple. Play it in C. Then play it starting on G. Same shape, new letters.
Try Mary Had a Little Lamb in all three keys this week. C, G, F. One each day.

questions.figure: Mary Had a Little Lamb in C (E D C D E E E)
questions.prompt: What's the same when you move this tune to a new key?

questions.figure: Mary transposed to G major (B A G A B B B)
questions.prompt: Why does Mary in G start on B, not G?
options: A mistake — it should start on G
B is the third of G major, same role as E was in C
Because G major has an F-sharp

questions.audio: Mary Had a Little Lamb in G major
questions.prompt: Is this the same tune as the one in C?
Play Mary's first phrase in G: B A G A B B B.
questions.accept: B4 A4 G4 A4 B4 B4 B4

remediation.title: Transposition, step by step
remediation.opening_cleffy: Moving a tune is a real brain-switch. We'll do it one interval at a time — first the shape, then the home, then the key signature's job.

completion.cleffy: Transposition unlocked. Tomorrow — act boss. Minuet in G, moved to D. Your first real transposed piece.

title: Minuet in D
subtitle: Act 23 · The same Minuet in a new home
goal: Take the Minuet you already know in G and play it in D major.
piece_context: Act 23 boss. Two new keys, relative minors, transposition — all of it lands on one real piece. Petzold's Minuet. You played it in G. Now you play it in D.

piece.title: Minuet in D (transposed from Minuet in G)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — same shape as Minuet in G, new key

pages.figure: Cleffy holding two copies of the same sheet music — one labeled G MAJOR, one labeled D MAJOR. Act 23 boss banner.
pages.cleffy: Act 23 boss. The Minuet you know, in a new key. Everything from this act — two-sharp reading, transposition — all lands here.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: Minuet in G. B: Minuet in D.

audio.A: Opening of Minuet in G — D G A B C D G G
audio.B: Opening of Minuet in D — A D E F# G A D D

pages.cleffy: Same piece. Twice. First — old home. G major. Second — new home. D major. Same shape. Your ear says it's the same tune.

pages.figure: Staff in G major. Minuet opening: D5 G4 A4 B4 C5 D5 | G4 G4. Key sig F#.
pages.cleffy: The Minuet in G. First two bars. D G A B C D. Then G G. You learned this one in Act 16.

pages.figure: Staff in D major. Same shape transposed: A5 D5 E5 F#5 G5 A5 | D5 D5. Key sig F# C#.
pages.cleffy: Same piece. New home. A D E F-sharp G A. Then D D. The shape is identical. Every note moved up the same distance.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Lines connecting each note in G to its twin in D. All shifted up by a fifth.
pages.cleffy: Every note moved up five steps. D became A. G became D. B became F-sharp. The distance between each pair of notes — unchanged.

pages.figure: D major key signature with F# and C# highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D major — two sharps. F-sharp, C-sharp. Watch the F when it comes up. And watch for C — it's sharp too, even though this phrase doesn't hit it yet.

pages.figure: Treble staff in D. First bar: A D E F# G A.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. First bar only. A D E F-sharp G A.

pages.figure: Treble staff in D. Second bar: D D (two quarter notes).
pages.cleffy: Second bar — lands home. D D.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Bars 1-2 together.
pages.cleffy: Both bars. Right hand only. Slow.

pages.figure: Bass staff in D. LH: D3 half-note, then A3 quarter-note, then D3 for bar 2.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. Root note of each bar. Bar 1 — D. Bar 2 — D again. Simple anchor.

pages.figure: Grand staff in D. Both bars. Guides on. Tempo 55.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Slow. 55 bpm. Right hand sings. Left hand holds.

pages.figure: Grand staff in D. Same two bars. No guides.
pages.cleffy: No guides now. Read it. Play it. Remember — every F is sharp.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Same Minuet. New key. Two hands.

pages.figure: Grand staff in D. Bars 1-2. Tempo 75.
pages.cleffy: Up to tempo. 75 bpm. Don't stop for stumbles.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 23 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 23 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding Minuet sheets in G and D. A pyramid showing 23/50 acts complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 23. Closed. Two new keys — D and B-flat. Twin minors. Transposition. And a real piece moved to a new home. Three months ago, key signatures were a mystery. Now you're reading them and moving tunes between them. Next act — phrasing. What makes a musical sentence feel like a sentence.

real_piano: Play the two bars of Minuet in D every day. Aim for 80 bpm by end of week.
For fun — play the same two bars in G (the old key) right after. Your hands will notice how close they are.
Challenge: try the same transposition on another tune you know. Twinkle in G. Ode to Joy in D.

questions.figure: First two bars of Minuet in D major
questions.prompt: Which key signature is this piece in?

questions.figure: Note on the top line of the treble staff in D major
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.figure: Side-by-side: Minuet in G vs Minuet in D
questions.prompt: What changed between the two versions?
options: The key and the starting notes

questions.audio: Opening of Minuet in D
questions.prompt: Is this the same tune as Minuet in G?

questions.audio: Final note of the phrase — D
questions.prompt: Does the phrase end on the home note?
Play the first bar of Minuet in D: A D E F# G A.
questions.accept: A4 D5 E5 F#5 G5 A5

remediation.title: Minuet in D, hands separate first
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Real test. We'll rebuild it — RH alone in the new key, then LH, then bar by bar together. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 23 sealed. A real piece, moved to a real new key. Every piece you know from now on — you can move it too. That's a pianist's skill. Tomorrow — phrasing. How music breathes.

title: What is a phrase?
subtitle: Act 24 · Phrasing
goal: Hear and see a phrase as a musical sentence — it starts, peaks, and ends.
piece_context: Act 24 opens. You can read notes, shape dynamics, play in new keys. Now — how music groups itself. A phrase is where music takes a breath.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano. A speech bubble with three dots • peak • three dots. Banner: ACT 24 — PHRASING.
pages.cleffy: New act. Music isn't just a line of notes. It groups itself — into phrases. A phrase is a musical sentence.

pages.figure: A spoken sentence underlined: 'Mary had a little lamb.' Small arch drawn above the word 'little' as the peak.
pages.cleffy: A sentence has shape. It starts. It has a main word. It ends. "Mary had a LITTLE lamb." Little is the peak. Music works the same way.

pages.figure: Treble staff — Mary Had a Little Lamb first phrase: E D C D E E E. A slur arches above the whole group.
pages.cleffy: Here's a musical sentence. Mary Had a Little Lamb, first phrase. E, D, C, D, E, E, E. The curve above the notes says these belong together. One phrase.

pages.figure: Play button. Waveform of the phrase with a slight arch drawn over it.

audio.A: Mary Had a Little Lamb first phrase, played with a gentle arch — starts quiet, peaks in the middle, softens at the end

pages.cleffy: Listen. Hear the shape? It begins. It rises. It lands. That's a phrase.

pages.figure: Same phrase on the staff — now labeled: START (E D C) · PEAK (D E E) · END (E). Three colored brackets.
pages.cleffy: Three parts to a phrase. Start — where it sets out. Peak — the most important note, usually near the middle or just after. End — where it settles.

pages.figure: Play button. Twinkle Twinkle first phrase.

audio.A: Twinkle Twinkle first phrase — C C G G A A G

pages.cleffy: Another one. Twinkle Twinkle, first phrase. Same idea. Starts on C. Climbs to A. Lands on G. Beginning. Peak. End.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 D4 C4 D4 E4 E4 E4 glowing in order. Slur drawn above.
pages.cleffy: Play Mary Had a Little Lamb's first phrase. E, D, C, D, E, E, E. One sentence. No stopping inside it.

pages.figure: A melody written out with two phrases shown. A small gap — and a tiny breath mark — between them.
pages.cleffy: Phrases end. Then a new one begins. Between two phrases — a tiny breath. A small space. That's how music breathes.

pages.figure: Play button. Two full phrases of Mary Had a Little Lamb, with a clear breath between them.

audio.A: Two phrases of Mary Had a Little Lamb — the first ends, a breath, then the second begins

pages.cleffy: Two phrases, back to back. Hear the tiny space between them? That's the end of sentence one, start of sentence two.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 C4 G4 G4 A4 A4 G4 glowing. Slur above.
pages.cleffy: Now Twinkle's first phrase. C, C, G, G, A, A, G. Start. Climb. Land.

pages.figure: Staff. A long curved line over six notes. Label: SLUR / PHRASE MARK.
pages.cleffy: That curve above the notes — the slur — often marks a phrase. It says: these notes go together. Don't chop them apart.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Short melodies — some one phrase, some two.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. One phrase or two?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Mary Had a Little Lamb — both phrases. Slight pause between them.
pages.cleffy: Both phrases of Mary Had a Little Lamb. E D C D E E E. Tiny breath. D D D. E G G. Two sentences. Not one long line.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two phrases. One song.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 116. A long sentence with a curve above it, ending in a period.
pages.cleffy: A phrase is a musical sentence. Start. Peak. End. Tomorrow — phrases that ask, and phrases that answer.

real_piano: Play any tune you know. Notice where the phrases end — the natural stopping points.
Sing the melody. Where do you breathe? That's where the phrase ends.
virtual_only: Replay pages 4 and 6 — listen for the arch.

questions.figure: Staff with a curved slur over seven notes.
questions.prompt: What does the curved line above the notes usually mean?

questions.figure: Diagram: START → PEAK → END.
questions.prompt: A musical phrase has three parts. Which of these is NOT one of them?

questions.figure: Two short phrases separated by a small gap.
questions.prompt: What is the small gap between two phrases?

questions.audio: Mary Had a Little Lamb first phrase
questions.prompt: How many phrases did you hear?
Play the first phrase of Mary Had a Little Lamb — E D C D E E E.
questions.accept: E4 D4 C4 D4 E4 E4 E4 played as one phrase

remediation.title: Phrases, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Phrases are a feel thing. Sentences for the ear. We'll slow it down and listen to one at a time.

completion.cleffy: Act 24 is open. Music speaks in sentences now. You'll hear them everywhere. Tomorrow — call and response. Phrases that talk to each other.

title: Question-and-answer phrases
subtitle: Act 24 · Call and response
goal: Hear how one phrase asks a question and the next one answers.
piece_context: Phrases often come in pairs. The first one hangs. The second one resolves. Ode to Joy is built from this.

pages.figure: Two speech bubbles facing each other. One with a question mark. One with a period.
pages.cleffy: Phrases travel in pairs. First one asks. Second one answers. Call and response.

pages.figure: Two spoken sentences stacked: 'How are you?' (question) and 'I am fine.' (answer). Arrows between them.
pages.cleffy: Two sentences. First one goes up at the end — a question. Second one comes down — an answer. Music does this too. Almost exactly this.

pages.figure: Two play buttons labeled QUESTION and ANSWER.

audio.A: A short phrase ending on an unresolved note — sounds like a question
audio.B: A short phrase ending on the home note — sounds like an answer

pages.cleffy: Listen. First one ends on a note that hangs in the air. Second one ends on the home note. Lands. Done. Question. Answer.

pages.figure: Treble staff — first two phrases of Ode to Joy written out, labeled QUESTION and ANSWER. Ends of each phrase circled.
pages.cleffy: Ode to Joy. You know this one. Phrase one — E E F G G F E D. Ends on D. Hangs. Question. Phrase two — E E F G G F E D C. Ends on C. Home. Answer.

pages.figure: Play button. First two phrases of Ode to Joy.

audio.A: Ode to Joy opening — two phrases. First ends on D (hanging). Second ends on C (home).

pages.cleffy: Same notes almost. Only the last note changes. But it changes everything. The second phrase answers the first.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 E4 F4 G4 G4 F4 E4 D4 glowing. Label: QUESTION.
pages.cleffy: Play the question. Ode to Joy, phrase one. E, E, F, G, G, F, E, D. Ends on D — not home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4 E4 F4 G4 G4 F4 E4 D4 C4 glowing. Label: ANSWER.
pages.cleffy: Now the answer. Same until the end — then one more note. E, E, F, G, G, F, E, D, C. Lands on C. Home.

pages.figure: Two arrows. First arrow ends mid-air with a question mark. Second arrow lands on a target marked HOME.
pages.cleffy: Why does it work? The first phrase ends on a note that isn't the home note. Your ear wants more. The second phrase gives it. Lands on home. Ear is satisfied.

pages.figure: Two short clips labeled A and B.

audio.A: A four-bar phrase ending on the dominant (hangs)
audio.B: A four-bar phrase ending on the tonic (lands)

pages.cleffy: Which one hangs? Which one lands?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full question-and-answer pair of Ode to Joy.
pages.cleffy: Play both. Question. Tiny breath. Answer. E E F G G F E D — breathe — E E F G G F E D C.

pages.figure: Three song titles with Q and A arrows: Twinkle Twinkle · Mary Had a Little Lamb · Happy Birthday. Each shows a question phrase and an answer phrase.
pages.cleffy: It's everywhere. Twinkle does it. Mary does it. Happy Birthday does it. Once you hear it, you can't unhear it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Twinkle's first pair of phrases.
pages.cleffy: Twinkle Twinkle, two phrases. C C G G A A G — that's the question. Ends on G — hangs. F F E E D D C — answer. Ends on C. Home.

pages.figure: Round counter.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Is this a question phrase or an answer phrase?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 117. Two phrases — one with '?' above it, one with '.' above it.
pages.cleffy: Phrases talk. One asks. One answers. Tomorrow — the little mark that tells you where to breathe.

real_piano: Play Ode to Joy's first two phrases. Feel how phrase one hangs and phrase two lands.
Hum any tune you know. Notice: almost every phrase has a partner.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6 and 7 — question, then answer.

questions.figure: Two phrases. First ends on D (not home). Second ends on C (home).
questions.prompt: Which phrase is the question?

questions.figure: A phrase ending on the home note.
questions.prompt: A phrase that ends on the home note usually sounds like...

questions.figure: Ode to Joy opening two phrases.
questions.prompt: How are these two phrases related?

questions.audio: A phrase that ends on a hanging note
questions.prompt: Question or answer?
Play the question phrase of Ode to Joy — E E F G G F E D.
questions.accept: E4 E4 F4 G4 G4 F4 E4 D4

remediation.title: Question and answer, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Call and response. A pattern you'll hear forever. Let's isolate it — one phrase at a time, very slow.

completion.cleffy: Two phrases, two jobs. Ask and answer. Tomorrow — breath marks. The little comma above the staff.

title: Breath marks
subtitle: Act 24 · Where to breathe
goal: Read a breath mark — the tiny comma above the staff — and lift your hand slightly before the next note.
piece_context: Singers breathe. Wind players breathe. Pianists don't need to — but they should play as if they did.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a small comma floating above a staff. Title: THE BREATH MARK.
pages.cleffy: A tiny comma. Sits above the staff. It says: lift. Breathe. Then keep going.

pages.figure: Sketch — a singer taking a breath between phrases, a flute player pausing. Underneath: a pianist doing the same with a slight hand lift.
pages.cleffy: Singers have to breathe. Flute players too. The piano doesn't need air. But it should sound like it does. A breath mark tells you where.

pages.figure: Close-up of a staff with a tiny comma (apostrophe-style) floating just above the top line, between two notes. Label: BREATH MARK.
pages.cleffy: Here's the symbol. A little comma or apostrophe, floating above the staff. Between two notes. Always says the same thing — lift, tiny silence, then the next note.

pages.figure: Two hand diagrams. Left: finger on key, note playing. Right: hand slightly lifted, gap shown, then finger returning for next note.
pages.cleffy: On the piano — you release the note a hair early. A tiny gap. Not a whole rest. Just enough space for a breath. Then play the next note normally.

pages.figure: Two play buttons — WITHOUT BREATH and WITH BREATH.

audio.A: A melody played all connected, no breath between phrases
audio.B: Same melody, with a clear breath mark lift between the two phrases

pages.cleffy: Listen. First — no breath. Run-on sentence. Second — breath mark. Space to settle. Hear how the second one feels like music, not a list?

pages.figure: Staff with Mary Had a Little Lamb. Breath marks placed between phrases — after the first E-D-C-D-E-E-E group, and again later.
pages.cleffy: Breath marks live between phrases. Almost always. End of sentence — breath — new sentence.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Mary Had a Little Lamb, first phrase. Breath mark after E-D-C-D-E-E-E. Second phrase highlighted next.
pages.cleffy: Play the first phrase. Tiny lift at the breath mark. Then the next phrase. E D C D E E E — breathe — D D D.

pages.figure: Two side-by-side examples. One has a quarter rest. One has a breath mark. Labels and durations called out.
pages.cleffy: A rest and a breath mark — not the same. A rest has a measured length. One beat. Two beats. A breath is tiny. You don't count it. You just feel it.

pages.figure: Three play buttons.

audio.A: A melody with no break
audio.B: A melody with a clear breath mark lift
audio.C: A melody with a full quarter rest

pages.cleffy: Three clips. Which one has the breath mark?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Ode to Joy first two phrases with a breath mark between them.
pages.cleffy: Ode to Joy. Two phrases. Breath between them. E E F G G F E D — lift — E E F G G F E D C. Don't crash through it. Let the first phrase end before the second starts.

pages.figure: A scale: no break → comma → quarter rest → half rest. Comma is second smallest.
pages.cleffy: Sizes of space, smallest to largest. Connected. Breath mark. Quarter rest. Half rest. The breath is barely there. Just enough for the ear.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Mary Had a Little Lamb — full tune with one breath mark between the two phrases.
pages.cleffy: Mary Had a Little Lamb, full. First phrase. Breath. Second phrase. Done.
pages.followup_cleffy: Tiny lift. Big difference.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 118. A tiny comma above a staff. Below it: 'lift, breathe, continue.'
pages.cleffy: Breath marks. Tiny commas. Big difference. Tomorrow — phrase-shaped dynamics. Soft, loud, soft.

real_piano: Play any tune you know. Put a tiny lift between phrases. Not a pause — a lift.
Try the same tune without any breaths. Hear the difference.
virtual_only: Replay page 5 — without and with breath.

questions.figure: Staff with a small comma floating above, between two notes.
questions.prompt: What does this mark mean?

questions.figure: A breath mark vs a quarter rest.
questions.prompt: What's the difference between a breath mark and a quarter rest?
options: A breath is tiny, a rest has a measured length

questions.figure: Two phrases with a breath mark between them.
questions.prompt: Where do breath marks usually appear?
options: In the middle of a word-like group
At the beginning of a piece

questions.audio: A melody with a clear breath mark between phrases
questions.prompt: Did you hear a tiny lift between the two phrases?
Play Mary Had a Little Lamb, first phrase, with a tiny lift at the end.
questions.accept: E4 D4 C4 D4 E4 E4 E4 with a small release after the last E

remediation.title: Breathing at the piano
remediation.opening_cleffy: A breath mark is a feel. Small. Honest. We'll take one phrase at a time — listen, lift, listen again.

completion.cleffy: Your phrases can breathe now. Tomorrow — soft in, loud in the middle, soft out. Dynamics across a phrase.

title: Phrase-shaped dynamics
subtitle: Act 24 · Soft · louder · soft
goal: Shape a phrase with dynamics — start soft, peak in the middle, soften at the end.
piece_context: You know phrases. You know dynamics. Put them together — p, mf, p, across one sentence of music. This is how real pianists play.

pages.figure: Staff with a phrase. Above it — a big gentle arch drawn: p rising to mf, falling back to p.
pages.cleffy: You know phrases. You know dynamics. Today — you put them together.

pages.figure: A 7-note phrase with markings written in: p at the start, mf at the peak note, p again at the end. Arch drawn over.
pages.cleffy: The rule for most phrases. Start soft. Grow through the middle. Peak on the main note. Soften to the end. p — mf — p.

pages.figure: Side-by-side. Left: flat phrase, all mf. Right: same notes shaped p-mf-p. Cleffy pointing at the right one with a small smile.
pages.cleffy: A flat phrase sounds like typing. A shaped phrase sounds like someone talking. Same notes. Different story.

pages.figure: Two play buttons — FLAT and SHAPED.

audio.A: Ode to Joy first phrase, played mf the whole way, flat
audio.B: Same phrase — starts p, builds to mf at the peak (G), softens to p at D

pages.cleffy: Listen. Flat first. Then shaped. Same phrase. Feels completely different.

pages.figure: Ode to Joy first phrase — E E F G G F E D. The high G circled and labeled PEAK.
pages.cleffy: Where's the peak? Usually the highest note. Or the one just after the middle. In Ode to Joy, phrase one — the G. That's where the sentence wants the weight.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Ode to Joy phrase one. Arch drawn: p at E, grows, mf at G, softens, p at D.
pages.cleffy: Play Ode to Joy, phrase one. Shape it. Start soft on E. Grow into the rise. A little fuller on G. Softer coming down to D. E E F G G F E D.

pages.figure: Staff with explicit dynamic markings written under the notes: p · < (crescendo hairpin) · mf · > (diminuendo hairpin) · p.
pages.cleffy: Sometimes composers write it out. p — crescendo hairpin — mf — diminuendo hairpin — p. That shape has a name. You're looking at it.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Shaped or flat?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Mary Had a Little Lamb first phrase. Peak marked on the high E. Arch drawn over.
pages.cleffy: Mary Had a Little Lamb, first phrase. Shape it. E D C D E E E. Start soft. A little more on the top notes. Land soft.

pages.figure: Two phrases. Left: subtle arch (correct). Right: dramatic swoop from pp to ff (too much). X through the right one.
pages.cleffy: Don't overdo it. A small arch is enough. p to mf and back. Not pp to ff. Too big, and it sounds like a cartoon.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Twinkle first phrase C C G G A A G. Peak on A. Arch drawn.
pages.cleffy: Twinkle, first phrase. C C G G A A G. Peak on the A. Gentle arch.

pages.figure: Three clips, each with a peak in a different spot.

audio.A: A phrase peaking at its highest note, mid-phrase
audio.B: A phrase peaking near the start
audio.C: A phrase peaking near the end

pages.cleffy: Three phrases. Where's the peak?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Ode to Joy, both phrases. Each with its own arch — p, mf, p. Tiny breath between.
pages.cleffy: Both phrases of Ode to Joy. Each gets its own arch. Question phrase — shape it. Breath. Answer phrase — shape it, land softer on C.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two sentences. Each one breathing on its own.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 119. A gentle dynamic arch over a phrase — p · mf · p.
pages.cleffy: Phrase-shaped dynamics. Soft in. Full in the middle. Soft out. Tomorrow — the act boss. Beethoven. A real phrase.

real_piano: Play Ode to Joy with p-mf-p shape on every phrase. Keep it small and honest.
Pick any tune you know. Find the peak note of each phrase. Play it just a little fuller.
virtual_only: Replay page 4 and page 6 — feel the flat vs shaped difference in your fingers.

questions.figure: A phrase with a dynamic arch drawn above — p rising to mf, back to p.
questions.prompt: What is this dynamic shape called on most phrases?
options: An arch — p to mf and back

questions.figure: A phrase with several notes. The highest note is circled.
questions.prompt: Where does a phrase usually peak?
options: On or near the highest note

questions.figure: A hairpin < followed by mf followed by >.
questions.prompt: What does this series of markings tell you?

questions.audio: A phrase with a gentle arch — starts p, grows, softens
questions.prompt: Is this phrase flat or shaped?
Play Ode to Joy's first phrase with a gentle arch — soft to louder to soft.
questions.accept: E4 E4 F4 G4 G4 F4 E4 D4 with volume rising to the G and softening toward D

remediation.title: Shaping a phrase, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Shape is small. It's about feel, not force. We'll take one short phrase and build the arch, note by note.

completion.cleffy: A phrase can breathe now. And it can rise and settle. Tomorrow — act boss. Beethoven's Bagatelle. Real music, real phrasing.

title: Beethoven Bagatelle
subtitle: Act 24 · First real phrase shaping
goal: Play an 8-bar Beethoven Bagatelle phrase with breath, shape, and call-and-answer — everything from Act 24, together.
piece_context: Act 24 ends here. Everything you learned — phrases, question-and-answer, breath marks, dynamic arcs — lands on one real Beethoven phrase. Simplified. 8 bars. G major. Your second Beethoven piece since Für Elise.

piece.title: Bagatelle (simplified, after Beethoven Op. 119)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — 8 bars, stepwise with one leap, phrase-shaping focus

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano with a small portrait of Beethoven on the wall. Banner: ACT 24 BOSS — FIRST REAL PHRASING.
pages.cleffy: Act 24 boss. Beethoven. Bagatelle. A small piece — that's what bagatelle means. Short. Simple. Full of shape.

pages.figure: Three small music icons: Für Elise, Moonlight, Bagatelle Op. 119. A bagatelle is highlighted with label: A TRIFLE. A LITTLE GEM.
pages.cleffy: Bagatelle means "trifle." Little gem. Beethoven wrote a lot of them. Short pieces. Packed with feeling. We're taking one — simplified — eight bars. G major.

pages.figure: Big play button. The 8-bar phrase plays with full shape.

audio.A: Eight bars, G major, 3/4 — first phrase hangs on D (question), second phrase lands on G (answer). Gentle arch on each.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Whole thing. Two phrases. First one hangs. Second one lands. And both of them breathe.

pages.figure: Treble staff. 8 bars in 3/4, G major key signature (F sharp). Melody: G A B | B A G | G A B | D (held) :: B A G | G F sharp E | D E F sharp | G (held). Breath mark between bars 4 and 5.
pages.cleffy: Here it is. Eight bars. Two phrases of four. Breath mark between them. F is sharp — G major. You've done this.

pages.figure: Same staff — phrase 1 labeled QUESTION (ends on D), phrase 2 labeled ANSWER (ends on G). Peaks circled: B in phrase 1, B in phrase 2.
pages.cleffy: First phrase — question. Rises to B, hangs on D. Second phrase — answer. Comes back down. Lands on G. Home. Peaks in the middle of each phrase. That's where the weight goes.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First phrase glowing: G4 A4 B4 B4 A4 G4 G4 A4 B4 D5. Arch drawn above.
pages.cleffy: First phrase only. The question. G A B, B A G, G A B, D. Gentle arch. Peak is the B in the middle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Second phrase glowing: B4 A4 G4 G4 F#4 E4 D4 E4 F#4 G4. Arch drawn.
pages.cleffy: Second phrase. The answer. B A G, G F sharp E, D E F sharp, G. Same shape. Lands on G. Home.

pages.figure: Staff with a breath mark drawn clearly between bar 4 and bar 5. Arrow pointing at it.
pages.cleffy: The breath. Right between the two phrases. After the D. Before the B. Tiny lift. Let the question hang for a second before the answer comes.

pages.figure: Both phrases on staff. Dynamics written in: p at bar 1, crescendo hairpin to mf at bar 2 peak, diminuendo back to p at bar 4. Breath. p at bar 5, mf at peak, p on final G.
pages.cleffy: Plan for dynamics. Each phrase does p — mf — p. Small arch. Not dramatic. The answer ends softest — on the home G. Let it settle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full 8-bar phrase. Tempo 60. Breath mark between. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Both phrases. Slow. 60 bpm. Question — breath — answer. Shape each one.

pages.figure: Same piece. Tempo 60. Dynamics marked. Focus on the arch.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Now — shape them. Soft at the start. A little fuller on the peaks. Softest on the final G. Don't rush the breath. Let it land.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same piece. Tempo 80. Full phrasing.
pages.cleffy: Up a little. 80 bpm. Everything at once. Question. Breath. Answer. Arches. Land soft.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was phrasing. Real phrasing. On a real Beethoven.

pages.figure: Keyboard. No guides. No markings. Just the staff. Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: Last time. No guides. Read it. Shape it. Stumble? Keep going. Phrases don't stop for mistakes.
pages.followup_cleffy: Beethoven. Eight bars. Two phrases. Fully shaped.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 24 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 24 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding a tiny sheet labeled BAGATELLE. A pyramid: 24/50 acts closed.
pages.cleffy: Act 24. Closed. You played Beethoven with phrasing. Question. Answer. Breath. Arc. A week ago notes were just notes in a line. Now you're speaking in sentences. Next act — review. All of Tier 3, one last look. Then the tier boss — Clair de Lune.

real_piano: Play the 8-bar Bagatelle every day. Start at 60 bpm. Build up to 80 by week's end.
Try it without any shaping — all flat. Then shape it. Feel the difference your hands make.
If someone's in the room — play it for them. It's Beethoven. That counts.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-13 — shaped, then at tempo, then no guides.

questions.figure: Staff with two 4-bar phrases and a breath mark between them.
questions.prompt: What does the small comma between bars 4 and 5 tell you?
options: Take a tiny lift — breath mark

questions.figure: First phrase ends on D. Second phrase ends on G.
questions.prompt: Which phrase is the answer — the one that lands on home?

questions.figure: A dynamic arch written above a phrase — p to mf to p.
questions.prompt: What's the shape of each phrase?
options: Soft in, fuller in the middle, soft out

questions.audio: Both phrases of the Bagatelle, fully shaped, with breath
questions.prompt: Does the second phrase land on home?

questions.audio: One phrase played flat, then same phrase played with an arch
questions.prompt: Which version has a dynamic arc?
Play the first phrase of the Bagatelle — G A B, B A G, G A B, D — with a gentle arch.
questions.accept: G4 A4 B4 B4 A4 G4 G4 A4 B4 D5 with volume shaping around the peak

remediation.title: The Bagatelle, one phrase at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Supposed to be a real test. We'll take it apart — phrase by phrase. Notes first. Breath next. Shape last. Slow.

completion.cleffy: Act 24 sealed. You played Beethoven with phrasing. That's not a small thing. That's how pianists play. Next act — the Tier 3 review. Everything we built. Then the tier boss.

title: Review: articulation
subtitle: Act 25 · Review
goal: Refresh every way you've learned to touch a key — legato, staccato, slurs, accents, tenuto, marcato.
piece_context: Last act before the Tier 3 boss. Four review lessons in a row. Today — the touch family. All of it, one more time.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a shelf labelled TOUCH FAMILY — six icons lined up: ribbon, bouncing ball, curved slur, accent wedge, tenuto line, marcato hat.
pages.cleffy: Review week. Four lessons. Then the Tier 3 boss. Today — every way you've learned to touch a key.

pages.figure: A grid of six cards. Legato, staccato, slur, accent, tenuto, marcato. Each with its symbol and a one-word label.
pages.cleffy: Six members. Legato — smooth. Staccato — short. Slur — connect these. Accent — louder. Tenuto — held. Marcato — firmly marked. You know them all. We're just lining them up again.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: legato line. B: staccato line.

audio.A: C D E F G played smooth and connected
audio.B: C D E F G played short and bouncy

pages.cleffy: First — legato. Second — staccato. Smooth ribbon, then bouncing ball. Same notes.

pages.figure: Staff: five quarter notes under a big slur. Keyboard below — C to G glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play it legato. C D E F G. Stay down on each note until the next finger presses.

pages.figure: Staff: five quarter notes with dots above. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Staccato. Touch, release. Touch, release. Pop each one off.

pages.figure: Staff: a three-note slur over D E F. Arrow pointing to the curve: 'connect these'.
pages.cleffy: A slur tells you: connect these notes. The curve hugs the group that goes together. Legato, as a mark on the page.
