pages.figure: Staff: D E F under a slur, then a small gap, then G A under another slur. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Two slurred groups. D E F, breathe, G A. Smooth inside each group. Tiny lift between.

pages.figure: Staff: four quarter notes. The third has a wedge accent above it. Callout: 'one note louder'.
pages.cleffy: Accent — the wedge mark. One note louder than the rest. The others stay their normal volume. Just this one steps forward.

pages.figure: Staff: C D E F, E marked with an accent. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E, F. Push harder on the E. Four even notes — one stands out.

pages.figure: Two cards. Left: tenuto — short line above a note, label 'held full length'. Right: marcato — hat symbol above a note, label 'firmly marked'.
pages.cleffy: Tenuto — the little line. Hold the note its full length. Weight, not volume. Marcato — the small hat. Firmer than an accent. A spoken word with force.

pages.figure: Staff: four quarter notes, each with a tenuto line above. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Tenuto run. G A B C. Lean into each note. Hold its full length.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which touch is this?

pages.figure: Staff: three legato notes, then three staccato, then one accent. Eight notes total.
pages.cleffy: Everything at once. Three legato. Three staccato. Two notes, the second accented. Smooth, smooth, smooth — pop, pop, pop — normal, LOUD.
pages.followup_cleffy: One phrase. Three touches. That's craft.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 121. The shelf of six touch icons, each checked off.
pages.cleffy: Touch family — reviewed. Tomorrow — the hand tricks. Fingering.

real_piano: Play any scale you know. Once legato, once staccato. Once with every other note accented.
Open any piece. Find one slur, one staccato dot, one accent. Play that bar three times.

questions.figure: Staff with a curved line over four notes
questions.prompt: What does this mark tell you?

questions.figure: Staff with a dot above a quarter note
questions.prompt: How is this note played?

questions.figure: Staff with a wedge above one note
questions.prompt: What is this mark?

questions.audio: Four notes played smooth and connected
questions.prompt: Which touch?
Play C D E F staccato — short, bouncy.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 played detached

remediation.title: Touch family, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Six members is a lot at once. Let's walk through each one slow — one touch, one example, one try.

completion.cleffy: Every touch in your hands now. Tomorrow — fingering. All the hand tricks.

title: Review: fingering
subtitle: Act 25 · Review
goal: Refresh every hand trick from Act 17 — why fingering matters, thumb-under, cross-over, finger substitution.
piece_context: Second review lesson. The hand tricks. You've been using these since Act 17 — scales wouldn't work without them.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up a hand, fingers labelled 1-2-3-4-5. Below: three small icons — thumb-under, cross-over, substitution.
pages.cleffy: Day two of review. Fingering. Choreography for your hand. Three tricks. One more pass.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: smooth fingering, hand stays in shape. Right: random fingering, hand twisted up.
pages.cleffy: Quick reminder. Fingering is planning which finger plays which note. A good plan — your hand flows. A bad plan — you stop, stretch, panic.

pages.figure: Cartoon right hand — middle finger on E, thumb tucking under to land on F. Arrow showing the motion.
pages.cleffy: Trick one — thumb-under. Middle or ring finger plays a note. Thumb slides under the hand and lands on the next one. That's how scales keep going when you run out of fingers.

pages.figure: Keyboard: C major scale C D E F G. Fingering shown: 1 2 3 1 2. Arrow at the thumb-under between E and F.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. Play C D E F G. Fingers one, two, three — thumb under — one, two. Slow. Feel the thumb slide under on the way to F.

pages.figure: Cartoon right hand coming down a scale — thumb on F, middle finger crossing OVER the thumb to land on E. Arrow.
pages.cleffy: Trick two — crossing over. The opposite of thumb-under. Going down a scale, the middle or ring finger crosses over the thumb. Same idea, reversed.

pages.figure: Keyboard: G F E D C descending. Fingering: 2 1 3 2 1 (cross-over at F to E).
pages.cleffy: Now coming down. G F E D C. Fingers two, one — three crosses over — two, one. Let the middle finger travel.

pages.figure: Full scale up and down. Fingering under each note: 1 2 3 1 2 3 2 1 3 2 1.
pages.cleffy: Up and down, one smooth line. Thumb-under going up. Cross-over coming down.

pages.figure: Cartoon hand holding down a note with finger 3, then silently swapping to finger 1 on the same key. Two-frame comic.
pages.cleffy: Trick three — finger substitution. Same key, same sound. Swap fingers silently. Why? Free up a finger for what comes next.

pages.figure: Keyboard: C4 glowing. Fingering shown: press with 3, swap to 1, then play D, E with 2, 3.
pages.cleffy: Press C with finger 3. Hold. Slide finger 1 onto the same key. Let 3 lift. Now play D, E. Two, three. The sound didn't break. You just changed hands on the key.

pages.figure: Excerpt from Minuet in G with fingering numbers written above every note.
pages.cleffy: In real music, good editors write fingerings above the notes. Follow them. They've thought about it more than you have time to.

pages.figure: First two bars of Minuet in G: D5 G4 A4 B4 C5 D5 G4 G4. Fingering: 5 1 2 3 4 5 1 5 (matches Lesson 85).
pages.cleffy: Minuet in G, opening. D, G, A, B, C, D, G, G. Pinky on the high D pickup. Thumb drops to G. Walk up 1-2-3-4-5 to D. Thumb back to G. Pinky for the last G.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Animations of each move.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Which trick is this?

pages.figure: Staff: one octave C major scale up and down. Fingering numbers underneath.
pages.cleffy: Full octave. Up with thumb-under. Down with cross-over. One breath.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's a scale. Real choreography.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 122. A hand with all five fingers spread, each labelled. Three checkmarks next to the three tricks.
pages.cleffy: Three tricks, re-held. Tomorrow — chords and intervals. All the theory so far.

real_piano: One octave C major scale, right hand. Up and down. Slow. Watch the thumb on the way up and the middle finger on the way down.
Same scale, left hand. Mirror fingering — 5 4 3 2 1 3 2 1.

questions.figure: Hand diagram, thumb sliding under middle finger
questions.prompt: Which trick is this?

questions.figure: Scale going down with finger 3 crossing over finger 1
questions.prompt: Which trick is this?

questions.figure: One key held, two fingers taking turns on it
questions.prompt: Which trick is this?

questions.audio: A smooth ascending scale
questions.prompt: Did the hand use thumb-under somewhere in this scale?
Play C D E F G right hand with fingers 1 2 3 1 2.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 ascending

remediation.title: Fingering, one trick at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three tricks in one lesson is a lot. Let's walk them separately — slower, one hand only.

completion.cleffy: Fingering sharpened. Tomorrow — chords, intervals, all the theory you've picked up.

title: Review: chords & intervals
subtitle: Act 25 · Review
goal: Refresh every chord and interval concept from Acts 18, 20, 21, 22 — triads, major/minor, inversions, I-IV-V, interval names.
piece_context: Third review lesson. All the theory you've picked up in Tier 3 — stacked up, side by side.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a blackboard. Three columns: INTERVALS | TRIADS | I-IV-V. A check mark at the top of each.
pages.cleffy: Theory day. Everything you've learned about notes stacked together. Intervals. Chords. Inversions. Primary chords.

pages.figure: Staff with a row: C-D (2nd), C-E (3rd), C-F (4th), C-G (5th), C-A (6th), C-B (7th), C-C (8ve). Labels underneath.
pages.cleffy: Intervals. The distance between two notes, counted inclusively. 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th, 6th, 7th, octave. Step is a 2nd. Skip is a 3rd.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C stays constant. Each round lights a different note above it.
pages.cleffy: Climb the ladder. C up to each one. C-D. C-E. C-F. C-G. C-A. Listen for how each distance sounds.

pages.figure: Staff: C E G stacked. Labels: root, third, fifth. Arrow: 'three notes, every other letter'.
pages.cleffy: A triad. Three notes stacked. Root, 3rd above it, 5th above that. Skip a note, skip a note. C, E, G.

pages.figure: Two triads side by side. C major (C E G) in bright color. C minor (C Eb G) in cool color. Labels.
pages.cleffy: Two flavors. Major and minor. Major — bright. Minor — sad. The middle note decides. Lower it by a half-step and major becomes minor.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C major triad. B: C minor triad.

audio.A: C major chord played as a block
audio.B: C minor chord played as a block

pages.cleffy: First — major. Second — minor. Same frame. Different mood.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 E4 G4 glowing together.
pages.cleffy: Play a C major triad. C, E, G — all three at once.

pages.figure: Staff: three C chord shapes. Root (C E G), 1st inv (E G C), 2nd inv (G C E). Labels.
pages.cleffy: Same three notes. Different bottom note. Different stacking. Root position — C on bottom. First inversion — E on bottom. Second inversion — G on bottom. All the C chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard showing three positions in sequence.
pages.cleffy: Play all three. Root. First inversion. Second inversion.

pages.figure: Staff in C major: I (C-E-G), IV (F-A-C), V (G-B-D). Roman numerals under each.
pages.cleffy: The primary three. I, IV, V. In C major — C chord, F chord, G chord. These three build most pop and classical tunes you know.

pages.figure: Play button. A four-bar progression: C - F - G - C.

audio.A: I IV V I progression in C major, block chords

pages.cleffy: I to IV to V back to I. Home — away — tension — home. The shape of a thousand songs.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three chords highlighted in order.
pages.cleffy: Play each. C chord. F chord. G chord. Back to C.

pages.figure: Two staves. Left: C E G played together (blocked). Right: C, then E, then G one at a time (broken). Labels.
pages.cleffy: Two ways to play any chord. Blocked — all at once. Broken — one at a time, rolled out. Same notes. Different texture.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C E G lighting up in sequence, then a second round together.
pages.cleffy: Play C, E, G one at a time. Then all three together. Broken, then blocked.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 123. A neat tower of three blocks: INTERVALS, TRIADS, I-IV-V. All checked.
pages.cleffy: Intervals, chords, inversions, primary three — all held. Tomorrow — phrasing. The last review before the big one.

real_piano: C major triad. Play it blocked, then broken. Then first inversion. Then second inversion.
Play C, then F, then G, then C. One chord per measure. Any voicing you like.

questions.figure: Staff with C and G
questions.prompt: What interval is this?

questions.figure: Staff: C E G stacked
questions.prompt: What is this?

questions.figure: Staff: E G C stacked (E on bottom)
questions.prompt: What is this?

questions.audio: A C minor chord played as a block
questions.prompt: Major or minor?
Play a C major triad — C, E, G together.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 simultaneously

remediation.title: Chords and intervals, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: A lot of theory in one lesson. Let's split it back apart — intervals first, chords second, I-IV-V last.

completion.cleffy: Theory — re-seated. Tomorrow — phrasing. How all of this breathes.

title: Review: phrasing
subtitle: Act 25 · Review
goal: Refresh every expression tool — phrase shape, question-and-answer, breath marks, phrase-shaped dynamics.
piece_context: Last review. Tomorrow — Tier 3 boss. Today we walk over phrasing one more time, because expression is what makes music sound like music.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a speech bubble — a wavy arc rising and falling. Below, a staff with the same arc drawn over a phrase.
pages.cleffy: Last review. Phrasing. Musical sentences. Tomorrow is the boss — today we breathe.

pages.figure: Staff: an 8-note phrase. Curved arc drawn above — rising to the middle, falling to the end.
pages.cleffy: A phrase is a musical sentence. It starts. It peaks somewhere in the middle. It ends. The arc you see — that's the shape.

pages.figure: Play button. A single 8-note phrase with natural shaping.

audio.A: An eight-note phrase: softer start, louder middle, softer ending

pages.cleffy: Listen. Hear the rise and fall? That's a phrase breathing.

pages.figure: Staff: C D E F G F E D. Dynamic arc marked — p at start, growing to mp in middle, back to p at end.
pages.cleffy: Play this phrase. Start soft. Grow to the middle. Soften at the end. C D E F G F E D. Ride the curve.

pages.figure: Staff: two short phrases. The first ends on an unstable note (with a question mark). The second resolves (with a period).
pages.cleffy: Question and answer. Two phrases talking. First phrase ends up in the air — a question. Second phrase comes back and lands — an answer.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: question phrase (ends on G). B: answer phrase (ends on C).

audio.A: Short phrase ending unresolved on G
audio.B: Short phrase ending resolved on C

pages.cleffy: First phrase — hanging. Second phrase — home. You feel the difference, not just hear it.

pages.figure: Staff: bar 1 'C E G' ending on G. Bar 2 'G E C' ending on C. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play the question. C E G. Leave it in the air. Then the answer. G E C. Land.

pages.figure: Staff with a small comma mark between two phrases. Callout: 'breathe here'.
pages.cleffy: Breath mark — the little comma above the staff. Tiny pause. Lift the hand. A singer would take a breath here. Piano doesn't need air. The pause is the breath.

pages.figure: Staff: four-note phrase, breath mark, four-note phrase. Keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Play both halves. Lift after the fourth note. Tiny silence. Then continue. C D E F — lift — G A B C.

pages.figure: Staff: one long phrase with hairpin crescendo toward the middle and hairpin diminuendo at the end.
pages.cleffy: Phrase-shaped dynamics. Soft at the start. Grow toward the peak. Soften to the end. The music doesn't stay one volume. It breathes.

pages.figure: Short Beethoven-style phrase — simple melody shaped with a dynamic arc. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Phrase from the Bagatelle. Play it with shape. Quiet start. Push through the middle. Release at the end.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. Two phrases per round — one shaped, one flat.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Which one has shape?

pages.figure: Staff: longer 12-note line with breath mark in the middle, dynamic arc over each half.
pages.cleffy: All at once. Two phrases, one breath between. Shape each half. Breathe in the gap.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's music, not just notes.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones. Last review check.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 124. An arc over a staff. Small text: 'Tier 3 boss tomorrow.'
pages.cleffy: Phrasing — re-held. Four reviews, done. Tomorrow — Tier 3 boss. Clair de Lune. You're ready.

real_piano: Any piece you know. Play one phrase with a clear shape — soft-loud-soft. Then play it flat. Hear the difference.
Pick a favorite melody. Mark where you would breathe. Play it with those breaths.

questions.figure: A staff with a rising-falling arc drawn above a phrase
questions.prompt: What does the arc tell you?
options: Play all notes the same volume

questions.figure: A small comma above a staff between two groups of notes
questions.prompt: What is this mark?

questions.figure: Two phrases — first ends on G, second ends on C
questions.prompt: Which phrase sounds finished?

questions.audio: A phrase played with clear rise-fall dynamics
questions.prompt: Did this phrase have a clear shape?
Play C D E F G F E D — with a gentle rise and fall in volume.
questions.accept: Full phrase with visible shaping attempt

remediation.title: Phrasing, one idea at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four expression ideas in one lesson — too many to hold at once. Let's walk through one at a time. Shape. Breath. Q-and-A. Dynamic arc.

completion.cleffy: Every review — done. Tomorrow — fifty lessons of Tier 3 collapse into one piece. Clair de Lune. Opening only. Get some sleep.

title: Clair de Lune (opening)
subtitle: Tier 3 · The second summit
goal: Read and play the opening bars of Debussy's Clair de Lune — simplified to D major. End of Tier 3.
piece_context: Debussy's most famous piece. Moonlight, in French. The opening is slow, quiet, unfinished-sounding. The original key is D-flat major — six flats. We're playing it in D major for now. Same shape, easier reading.

piece.title: Clair de Lune (opening bars — simplified)
piece.difficulty: low-intermediate — slow, soft, thirds in the right hand over a low pedal tone

pages.figure: Cleffy at the top of a second pyramid. Behind him, two tiers — Tier 1 and Tier 2 — marked in gold. Tier 3 marked in gold too. A small moon above.
pages.cleffy: Second summit. Tier 1 took you from nothing to reading one staff. Tier 2 gave you the grand staff and both hands. Tier 3 gave you craft. Today — Clair de Lune. The piece everyone recognizes.

pages.figure: Cover-style image. 'Clair de Lune — Claude Debussy, 1890.' A moon over water.
pages.cleffy: Claude Debussy. French. Wrote this in his twenties. The title means 'moonlight.' It's about atmosphere more than notes. Slow. Soft. Unsure of itself.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: D-flat major with 5 flats, label 'original'. Right: D major with 2 sharps, label 'ours'. Arrow between them.
pages.cleffy: One note about the key. Debussy wrote it in D-flat — five flats. A lot. You'll play that version someday. Today we're in D major. Two sharps. F-sharp and C-sharp. Same shape, easier reading.

pages.figure: Play button. An elegant still frame.

audio.A: Clair de Lune opening bars played very softly, pedaled

pages.cleffy: Listen. This is the opening. Very soft. Very slow. Space between the notes. No hurry anywhere.

pages.figure: A two-staff excerpt. Top staff: slow descending thirds in the right hand. Bottom staff: a single long D note. Everything marked pp.
pages.cleffy: Here's how the opening is built. Right hand — two notes at once, thirds, drifting down slowly. Left hand — one low D. Held forever. The whole thing marked *pp*. That means very, very soft.

pages.figure: Close-up of RH staff: A-F# played together, then G-E, then F#-D. Each pair bracketed and labelled '3rd'.
pages.cleffy: The right hand plays thirds — two notes a 3rd apart, at the same time. First pair: A and F-sharp. Next: G and E. Next: F-sharp and D. Three stepwise pairs, moving down. Same shape you've been playing since Act 22.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A4 and F#4 glowing together.
pages.cleffy: Play the first pair. A and F-sharp together. Right hand. Use fingers 3 and 1. Soft. Barely touch the keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three pairs light up in order: A-F#, G-E, F#-D.
pages.cleffy: All three pairs, in order. Slow. Down a step each time. A-F-sharp. G-E. F-sharp-D.

pages.figure: Bass staff: a single D2 held as a dotted whole note. Label: 'hold through the phrase'.
pages.cleffy: Left hand is easy. One note. Low D. Press it once at the start of the phrase. Hold it. It's the floor the right hand drifts over.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D2 glowing in the bass.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. Low D. Press and hold. Just one note. Feel the depth.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: A-F# third. LH: D2 held underneath.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Left hand presses D first. Then right hand lays the A-F-sharp third on top. Like a stone dropped into a pool.

pages.figure: Grand staff: four bars. RH plays three slow descending thirds, then lifts and drifts up to a higher pair. LH holds D throughout. Dynamic mark: pp. Slow tempo mark.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. Everything you just played, laid out. Left hand — one low D, held through all four bars. Right hand — three descending thirds, a breath, then one more pair high up. The last pair floats up instead of down. A little sigh.

pages.figure: Right hand only. Six pairs, then one floating pair: A-F#, G-E, F#-D, (lift), F#-A.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. Three descending thirds. Lift. One pair up high. A-F-sharp, G-E, F-sharp-D, then F-sharp-A on top. Quiet. Slow.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full opening. Tempo 40. Dynamic: pp. Both hands.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Tempo 40. Very slow. Left hand presses D and holds it. Right hand places each pair gently on top. Feel it float, not press.

pages.figure: Grand staff. No glowing keys. Just the page.
pages.cleffy: Now no guides. You read, you play. Slow as you need. The piece is slow anyway.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was Debussy. The opening of the most famous impressionist piano piece ever written — in your hands.

pages.figure: Same four bars. A long dynamic hairpin — barely-there start, slight swell at the top pair, sigh at the end.
pages.cleffy: One more time. Now with breath. Start barely audible. A tiny swell when the top pair arrives. Sigh at the end. This is phrasing — Tier 3's gift.

pages.figure: Banner — Tier 3 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. Tier boss — take your time.

pages.figure: A pyramid showing Tier 1 (gold), Tier 2 (gold), Tier 3 (gold). Cleffy at the top. Seven small icons at his feet — one for each piece played.
pages.cleffy: Look back. Tier 1 — one staff, one hand, first song. Happy Birthday. Tier 2 — both clefs, both hands, real pieces. Für Elise. Tier 3 — craft. Articulation. Fingering. Chords. Phrasing. Seven pieces in your hands now. Minuet in G. Canon in D. Gymnopédie. Happy Birthday harmonized. Twinkle harmonized. Minuet in D. Beethoven Bagatelle. And now the opening of Clair de Lune. A hundred and twenty-five lessons. Two tiers behind you.

pages.figure: Pyramid preview with Tier 4 highlighted. Four piece thumbnails: Prelude in C, The Entertainer, Nocturne, Moonlight opening.
pages.cleffy: Tier 4 — intermediate. This is where you become a pianist, not a piano student. Bach's Prelude in C — a whole piece made of broken chords. The Entertainer — Scott Joplin, syncopation, ragtime. Chopin's Nocturne — Op. 9 No. 2, one of the most beautiful phrases in music. Moonlight Sonata, first movement — the opening eight bars. You don't have to start tomorrow. You've earned a pause.

pages.figure: Celebration card — TIER 3 COMPLETE 👑. Big golden trophy. Cleffy under a moon, holding sheet music that says Clair de Lune.
pages.cleffy: Tier 3. Complete. You play piano with craft now. Touch. Fingering. Harmony. Expression. Not just the right notes — the right notes the right way. That's a real pianist's job. Rest tonight. Moon's up. Tier 4 opens when you're ready.

real_piano: Opening of Clair de Lune, once a day. Very slow. Very soft. No rush to speed it up.
Play it for someone. Turn the lights down. Tell them it's Debussy.
Revisit one piece from each tier — Happy Birthday, Für Elise, Minuet in G. Notice how small they feel now.
virtual_only: Replay pages 14-16 until the opening feels automatic

questions.figure: Two notes stacked: F-sharp and A
questions.prompt: What is the distance between these notes?

questions.figure: Dynamic marking: pp
questions.prompt: What does pp mean?

questions.figure: Bass staff: a whole note D held across four bars
questions.prompt: How is this left-hand note played?
options: Pressed once, held the whole time

questions.figure: Key signature with two sharps
questions.prompt: Which key is this lesson in?

questions.audio: Clair de Lune opening
questions.prompt: Is this piece played loud or soft?

questions.audio: Two descending thirds in a row
questions.prompt: Is this moving up or down?
Play the first third — F-sharp and A together, right hand.
questions.accept: F#4 and A4 played simultaneously

remediation.title: Clair de Lune, one pair at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Big piece. Big moment. Let's take it apart. One third at a time, then one hand at a time, then slowly together. There's no rush on this one. The piece itself is unhurried.

completion.cleffy: Tier 3, sealed. You play with craft now. Real touch, real shape, real sound. One hundred and twenty-five lessons. Half the road. Big breath. Then Tier 4 whenever you're ready. The moon's not going anywhere.

title: A major (3 sharps)
subtitle: Act 26 · More keys
goal: Read and play a simple phrase in A major — F-sharp, C-sharp, G-sharp, every time.
piece_context: Tier 4 opens. You just finished Clair de Lune. Now — more keys. A major adds one more sharp to what you know.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a treble staff with three sharps stacked at the start. Banner: TIER 4. A MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: Welcome to Tier 4. You finished Clair de Lune. That was Tier 3. Now — more keys. More color. Today, A major.

pages.figure: Treble staff showing the key signature for A major — F# on top line, C# on third space, G# on space above the staff.
pages.cleffy: Three sharps. F, C, G. Every F is F-sharp. Every C is C-sharp. Every G is G-sharp. Say it out loud once — F, C, G.

pages.figure: Treble staff with the A major scale written out: A B C# D E F# G# A. Key signature at the front.
pages.cleffy: The scale. A B C D E F G A — but three of those notes are sharp. C, F, and G. Same three every time.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: A MAJOR SCALE.

audio.A: A major scale ascending, slow and clean

pages.cleffy: Listen. Bright. Warm. Open. A is a singer's key — lots of songs live here.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A B C# D E highlighted. Key signature showing 3 sharps.
pages.cleffy: A position — five fingers. A, B, C-sharp, D, E. C-sharp is the black key right after C. Keep that one in mind.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full A major scale highlighted: A B C# D E F# G# A.
pages.cleffy: Full scale. A B C-sharp D E F-sharp G-sharp A. Three black keys inside the climb. Slow.

pages.figure: Staff in A major with a plain F note (no symbol in front of it) circled. Arrow to keyboard: F# key lit.
pages.cleffy: Here's the rule. No symbol on the F — still play F-sharp. The key signature already said so. It doesn't repeat itself.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A B C# D E F# — played in order.
pages.cleffy: Play up to F-sharp. A, B, C-sharp, D, E, F-sharp.

pages.figure: Staff in A major. Melody: A C# E A — simple arpeggio-like shape. No accidentals written.
pages.cleffy: Short melody. A, C-sharp, E, A. Both C and E on the page — but the C is sharp because the key said so.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A C# E A.
pages.cleffy: Play it. A, C-sharp, E, A. Four notes.

pages.figure: Staff in A major. Melody: A B C# D E D C# B A.
pages.cleffy: Nine notes. Up the scale, then back. Watch the C on the way up and the C on the way down — both sharp.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'In A major, what do you play?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. In A major — natural or sharp?

pages.figure: Keyboard with full A major scale.
pages.cleffy: One more full scale. All eight notes. Remember — F, C, G are sharp.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 126. A major key signature with F#, C#, G# glowing. Banner: TIER 4 · KEY 1.
pages.cleffy: A major. Three sharps. F, C, G. Tomorrow — the flat side. E-flat major.

real_piano: Play the full A major scale, slow. Say each sharp out loud as you hit it — F-sharp, C-sharp, G-sharp.
Pick any melody you know in C. Try it starting on A instead. Let the three sharps happen.

questions.figure: A major key signature
questions.prompt: How many sharps in A major?

questions.figure: A major key signature
questions.prompt: Which three notes are sharp?

questions.figure: Staff in A major with a plain C note
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.audio: A major scale ascending
questions.prompt: Is this a major-sounding scale?
Play the first five notes of A major: A B C# D E.
questions.accept: A4 B4 C#5 D5 E5

remediation.title: A major, one sharp at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three sharps is a jump from two. We'll take them one at a time. F-sharp first. Then C-sharp. Then G-sharp.

completion.cleffy: A major — three sharps, known. Tomorrow — three flats. E-flat major.

title: E♭ major (3 flats)
subtitle: Act 26 · More keys
goal: Read and play a simple phrase in E-flat major — B-flat, E-flat, A-flat, every time.
piece_context: Yesterday, the sharp side. Today, the flat side. E-flat major — the key of Mozart, Beethoven, a lot of jazz.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a treble staff with three flats stacked at the start. Banner: E-FLAT MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: Other side of the map. Flats today. Three of them. B, E, A. E-flat major.

pages.figure: Treble staff showing the E-flat major key signature — Bb on middle line, Eb on top space, Ab on second space.
pages.cleffy: Three flats. B, E, A. Every B is B-flat. Every E is E-flat. Every A is A-flat. B, E, A — the same pattern backwards from A major's F, C, G.

pages.figure: Treble staff with the E-flat major scale written out: Eb F G Ab Bb C D Eb. Key signature at the front.
pages.cleffy: The scale. E F G A B C D E — but three of those are flat. E, A, and B. Same three every time.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: E-FLAT MAJOR SCALE.

audio.A: E-flat major scale ascending, slow and warm

pages.cleffy: Listen. Warm. Round. A little darker than A major. Composers pick E-flat for solemn, noble music.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb F G Ab Bb highlighted. Key signature showing 3 flats.
pages.cleffy: E-flat position. Eb, F, G, A-flat, B-flat. Three of the five are black keys. Takes a minute to find.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full E-flat scale highlighted: Eb F G Ab Bb C D Eb.
pages.cleffy: Full scale. Eb, F, G, Ab, Bb, C, D, Eb. Slow. Feel the black keys under your fingers.

pages.figure: Staff in E-flat major with a plain B note circled. Arrow to keyboard: Bb key lit.
pages.cleffy: Same rule as the sharp keys. No symbol on the B — still play B-flat. The key signature handles it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb F G Ab Bb — played in order.
pages.cleffy: Play up to B-flat. Eb, F, G, Ab, Bb.

pages.figure: Staff in E-flat major. Melody: Eb G Bb Eb — arpeggio shape.
pages.cleffy: Short melody. E-flat, G, B-flat, E-flat. The B is flat. The first and last E are flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb G Bb Eb.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Eb, G, Bb, Eb. Four notes.

pages.figure: Staff in E-flat major. Melody: Eb F G Ab Bb Ab G F Eb.
pages.cleffy: Nine notes. Up the scale, back down. Every A and every B — flat. Don't forget on the way down.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'In E-flat major, what do you play?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. In E-flat major — natural or flat?

pages.figure: Keyboard with full E-flat scale.
pages.cleffy: One more full scale. All eight notes. Remember — B, E, A are flat.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 127. E-flat major key signature with three flats glowing. Banner: TIER 4 · KEY 2.
pages.cleffy: E-flat major. Three flats. B, E, A. Tomorrow — read in both. Same shapes, new colors.

real_piano: Play the full E-flat scale, slow. Say each flat out loud as you hit it — B-flat, E-flat, A-flat.
Play an A major scale, then an E-flat major scale, back to back. Same shape, different colors.

questions.figure: E-flat major key signature
questions.prompt: How many flats in E-flat major?

questions.figure: E-flat major key signature
questions.prompt: Which three notes are flat?

questions.figure: Staff in E-flat major with a plain A note
questions.prompt: What do you play?

questions.audio: E-flat major scale ascending
questions.prompt: Does this scale sound warm and major?
Play the first five notes of E-flat major: Eb F G Ab Bb.
questions.accept: Eb4 F4 G4 Ab4 Bb4

remediation.title: E-flat major, one flat at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three black keys in a row of five — it's a hand-feel problem, not a reading one. We'll walk it slowly. B-flat first. Then E-flat. Then A-flat.

completion.cleffy: E-flat major — three flats, known. Tomorrow — reading short pieces in both three-sharp and three-flat worlds.

title: Three-sharp / three-flat reading
subtitle: Act 26 · More keys
goal: Read and play short phrases in A major and E-flat major without losing track of the key signature.
piece_context: Two new keys in two days. Now — put them to work. Short phrases. Eyes on the key signature. Fingers follow.

pages.figure: Cleffy between two staves — one with three sharps, one with three flats. Banner: SAME SHAPES, NEW COLORS.
pages.cleffy: You have A major. You have E-flat major. Today — read in both. Same shapes on the staff. Different hands on the keyboard.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. Left: 3 sharps (F#, C#, G#). Right: 3 flats (Bb, Eb, Ab).
pages.cleffy: First glance. Before you play a note — look at the key signature. Three sharps? A major. F, C, G are sharp. Three flats? E-flat major. B, E, A are flat.

pages.figure: Treble staff in A major. Notes: A C# E — a stacked-3rd shape.
pages.cleffy: A shape in A major. Notes on lines: A, C, E. The C is sharp — the key said so.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A C# E.
pages.cleffy: Play it. A, C-sharp, E.

pages.figure: Treble staff in E-flat major. Notes: Eb G Bb — same shape, different place.
pages.cleffy: Same shape — skip, skip. But in E-flat major. E, G, B — the E and the B are flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb G Bb.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Eb, G, Bb.

pages.figure: Staff in A major. Eight notes: A B C# E C# B A — walking up, then down.
pages.cleffy: Short phrase in A major. Seven notes. Two sharps show up — C on the way up, C on the way down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A B C# E C# B A.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Watch the two C's.

pages.figure: Staff in E-flat major. Seven notes: Eb F G Bb G F Eb.
pages.cleffy: Short phrase in E-flat major. Two flats show up — the Eb at the start, the Bb in the middle, the Eb at the end.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb F G Bb G F Eb.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Don't forget the flats.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Each round: a key signature flashes. 3 sharps or 3 flats — which key?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Name the key from the signature alone.

pages.figure: Two mini-staves stacked — top in A major, bottom in E-flat major. Each four notes.
pages.cleffy: Switch keys mid-exercise. Four notes in A, then four notes in E-flat. Reset your eyes when the key changes.

pages.figure: Staff in A major. Nine notes: A C# E F# E D C# B A.
pages.cleffy: Longer A major phrase. Nine notes. Two sharps live inside — F and C.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 128. A and E-flat side by side. Banner: TWO KEYS, READ.
pages.cleffy: Two new keys, reading clean. Tomorrow — the circle of fifths. The map that holds all twelve keys at once.

real_piano: Play a short phrase in A, then the same phrase in E-flat. Your ears will hear the family resemblance.
Write (or choose) any 5-note phrase. Play it in both keys.

questions.figure: Key signature with three sharps
questions.prompt: What key is this?

questions.figure: Key signature with three flats
questions.prompt: What key is this?

questions.figure: Staff in A major with plain notes on F and C lines
questions.prompt: What accidentals do you apply?

questions.audio: A major scale, then E-flat major scale
questions.prompt: Are these both major scales?
Play A, C-sharp, E — an A major chord rolled.
questions.accept: A4 C#5 E5

remediation.title: Keys side by side, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two keys in one lesson is a lot. We'll do each one by itself first — A major alone, then E-flat alone, then switch.

completion.cleffy: Two keys read. Both colors. Tomorrow — the map. All twelve keys on one circle.

title: Circle of fifths intro
subtitle: Act 26 · The map of all keys
goal: See how all 12 keys live on one circle — clockwise adds sharps, counter-clockwise adds flats.
piece_context: You've met C, G, D, A, F, Bb, Eb. Seven keys already. Today — the picture that ties them all together.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a large circular diagram — a clock face with letters around it. Banner: THE CIRCLE OF FIFTHS.
pages.cleffy: One picture. Twelve keys. One circle. This is the map musicians use to keep all the keys straight. It's called the circle of fifths.

pages.figure: Circle diagram. C at the 12-o'clock position. All other letters grayed out.
pages.cleffy: Top of the circle — C major. No sharps, no flats. The starting point.

pages.figure: Circle diagram. C at top, then clockwise: G (1 sharp), D (2 sharps), A (3 sharps), E, B, F#. Sharp counts labeled.
pages.cleffy: Clockwise — each step adds one sharp. G has one. D has two. A has three. Keep going and you reach E, B, F-sharp.

pages.figure: Circle diagram. C at top, then counter-clockwise: F (1 flat), Bb (2 flats), Eb (3 flats), Ab, Db, Gb. Flat counts labeled.
pages.cleffy: Counter-clockwise — each step adds one flat. F has one. B-flat has two. E-flat has three. Keep going and you reach A-flat, D-flat, G-flat.

pages.figure: Two keyboards. Top: C to G, five white keys counted (C-D-E-F-G). Bottom: C to F, five white keys the other way.
pages.cleffy: Why "fifths"? Each step around the circle is a 5th apart. C up to G — that's five notes. G up to D — five notes. And so on. A fifth clockwise adds a sharp. A fifth counter-clockwise adds a flat.

pages.figure: Play button. Five fifths in a row climbing: C-G, G-D, D-A, A-E, E-B.

audio.A: Ascending fifths: C then G, G then D, D then A, A then E, E then B

pages.cleffy: Listen. That's the climb up the circle. Every jump is a fifth — the same distance, every time.

pages.figure: Circle with C, G, D, A, F, Bb, Eb highlighted in gold. Others faded. Label: KEYS YOU ALREADY KNOW.
pages.cleffy: You already know seven of these. C. G. D. A. F. B-flat. E-flat. More than half the circle. You've been filling in the map this whole time.

pages.figure: Four treble staves showing key signatures for G (1#), D (2#), A (3#), E (4#). The new sharp each time is circled.
pages.cleffy: The sharps come in a fixed order. G adds F-sharp. D adds C-sharp. A adds G-sharp. E adds D-sharp. F, C, G, D — always in that order.

pages.figure: Four treble staves showing key signatures for F (1b), Bb (2b), Eb (3b), Ab (4b). The new flat each time is circled.
pages.cleffy: The flats come in a fixed order too. F adds B-flat. Bb adds E-flat. Eb adds A-flat. Ab adds D-flat. B, E, A, D — always in that order. Reverse of the sharps. Clean.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four root notes lit: C, G, D, A. Label: FOUR STEPS CLOCKWISE.
pages.cleffy: Play the first four keys clockwise. Just the root notes. C, G, D, A. Each one a fifth up from the last.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four root notes lit going down: C, F, Bb, Eb. Label: FOUR STEPS COUNTER-CLOCKWISE.
pages.cleffy: Now counter-clockwise. C, F, B-flat, E-flat. Each one a fifth down from the last.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Each round: a key signature shown. Student names the key.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Name the key from the signature.

pages.figure: Circle with two arrows — one curving clockwise, one counter-clockwise. Label: MOST SONGS MOVE BY FIFTHS.
pages.cleffy: Why musicians love this circle. Most chord changes in most songs move by fifths. C to G. G to D. A minor to D. The circle is the shape of harmony itself.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Short 3-note scale fragment in each of C, G, D, A — just the first three notes of each.
pages.cleffy: Play the first three notes of each scale, moving clockwise. C-D-E. G-A-B. D-E-F#. A-B-C#.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 129. Full circle of fifths with all 12 keys. Banner: THE MAP IS YOURS.
pages.cleffy: Twelve doors. One circle. Sharps clockwise. Flats counter-clockwise. Tomorrow — a full piece in C. Bach's Prelude. Nothing but arpeggios.

real_piano: Draw the circle from memory. Start with C at the top. Put G, D, A going clockwise. Put F, B-flat, E-flat going counter-clockwise.
Play a C major scale, then a G major scale, then a D major scale. Feel the map move.

questions.figure: Circle of fifths with C at top
questions.prompt: Going clockwise, what happens at each step?

questions.figure: Circle of fifths with C at top
questions.prompt: Which direction adds flats?

questions.figure: Three-sharp key signature
questions.prompt: How many steps clockwise from C?

questions.figure: Three-flat key signature
questions.prompt: Which key is this?

questions.audio: Ascending fifths: C to G to D to A
questions.prompt: Is each jump the same size?
Play four root notes clockwise from C: C, G, D, A.
questions.accept: C G D A in any reasonable octave

remediation.title: The circle, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Big picture. Let's walk it slowly. C at top. One door at a time, clockwise first.

completion.cleffy: The circle is yours. You already knew most of it — now you can see it whole. Tomorrow — Bach's Prelude in C. Act 26 boss.

title: Prelude in C (Bach)
subtitle: Act 26 · A piece of pure arpeggios
goal: Play the first four bars of Bach's Prelude in C — slow, even, broken chords changing underneath.
piece_context: Your first Tier 4 piece. No melody to worry about. No sharps or flats. Just even arpeggios and the harmony slowly changing.

piece.title: Prelude in C, BWV 846
piece.difficulty: intermediate — steady arpeggios, hand coordination, very slow

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a yellowed manuscript. Banner: ACT 26 BOSS · PRELUDE IN C · BACH. Subtitle: Well-Tempered Clavier, Book 1.
pages.cleffy: First Tier 4 piece. You just finished Tier 3. Clair de Lune was four lessons ago. Now — Bach. The Prelude in C.

pages.figure: A title page: 'The Well-Tempered Clavier, Book 1, 1722 — J.S. Bach.' Prelude No. 1 circled.
pages.cleffy: Bach wrote 48 preludes. This is the first one. No melody. No sharps, no flats. Just broken chords — arpeggios — rolling quietly. One of the gentlest pieces in all of classical music.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: PRELUDE IN C · BARS 1-4.

audio.A: Bach Prelude in C, first 4 bars, gentle, quarter note = 66

pages.cleffy: Listen to the first four bars. Same pattern, every bar. Only the notes of the chord change.

pages.figure: One bar written out: eight notes. LH: C3-E3. RH: G3-C4-E4-C4-E4-C4 with some positions — shows the 8-note shape.
pages.cleffy: Eight notes per bar. Same shape, every bar. Left hand plays two low notes. Right hand plays five. Then the last three of the right hand repeat. Eight notes. One bar. Bach did this for 35 bars.

pages.figure: Bar 1 on the grand staff. Notes labeled: C3 E3 (LH) G3 C4 E4 G3 C4 E4 (RH). Chord box: C major.
pages.cleffy: Bar 1. C major chord, broken. LH: low C, E above it. Stays down. RH: G, C, E — rolled up. Then G, C, E again. Same five notes twice.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH fingers on C3 and E3. Both glowing.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. Bar 1. C and E, low. Play them together. Hold.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH pattern: G3, C4, E4, G3, C4, E4. Five notes, then three more.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. Five notes. G, C, E, then G, C, E again. Don't hold. Let each note ring for a moment, then move.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 1. Quarter note = 60. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Both hands, bar 1. LH holds C and E. RH rolls up G, C, E — twice. Slow. Even. Don't rush the climb.

pages.figure: Bar 2 on the grand staff. LH: C3 D3. RH: A3 D4 F4 A3 D4 F4. Chord box: D minor / 7.
pages.cleffy: Bar 2 — harmony moves. LH holds C and D now. RH rolls A, D, F. Then again. Same shape as bar 1. New notes. A gentle, slightly darker color.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 2. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Bar 2. LH: C and D. RH: A, D, F — twice.

pages.figure: Bars 3 and 4 on the grand staff. Bar 3: B2 D3 + G3 D4 F4 (G7-ish). Bar 4: C3 E3 + G3 C4 E4 (back to C major).
pages.cleffy: Bar 3 — LH moves to B and D. RH: G, D, F. Bar 4 — back home. Same as bar 1. C major. Chord change. Chord change. Back to rest.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 3. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Bar 3. LH: B and D. RH: G, D, F — twice.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 4. Back to C major. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Bar 4. Same as bar 1 — LH C and E, RH G-C-E twice. Feel the landing. That's home.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All four bars. Guides on. Tempo 60. Subtitle: FIRST PLAY-THROUGH.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. Guides on. Very slow — 60. Don't stop for stumbles. Keep the pattern rolling.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. Quarter note = 66 — Bach's suggested tempo. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: Real tempo now. Quarter note equals 66. No guides. Read the pattern. Let the hands roll.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That's Bach. 1722. Four bars of it, in your hands.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 26 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding the Bach manuscript. Banner: 26/50 ACTS DONE · TIER 4 BEGUN.
pages.cleffy: Act 26 closed. Three new keys this week. A major. E-flat major. The whole circle of fifths. And you played Bach. Real Bach. Gently. Next act — sixteenth notes. Four notes per beat. The fast stuff starts.

real_piano: Play the first four bars every day this week. Start at 55 bpm. Build up to 66 by week's end.
Listen to a full recording of the Prelude. Glenn Gould, Andras Schiff, or Lang Lang — all different, all beautiful.
Try bar 1 alone, ten times in a row. The evenness comes from repetition.

questions.figure: Bar 1 of the Prelude
questions.prompt: What chord is outlined in bar 1?

questions.figure: The full 8-note pattern of one bar
questions.prompt: How many notes per bar?

questions.figure: Key signature of the Prelude
questions.prompt: How many sharps or flats?

questions.audio: Bar 1 of the Prelude, played slowly
questions.prompt: Is there a separate melody, or just broken chords?

questions.audio: Bars 1-4 played cleanly
questions.prompt: Does the last bar sound like home?
Play bar 1 — LH C and E, RH rolls G, C, E, G, C, E.
questions.accept: C3 and E3 held, then G3 C4 E4 G3 C4 E4

remediation.title: Prelude, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss, first one in Tier 4. Expect a real test. We'll rebuild it. Bar 1 alone. Hands separately. Then bar 2, same way. One brick at a time.

completion.cleffy: Act 26 sealed. Your first Bach. Three new keys. The full circle. And a piece that's almost 300 years old, gently alive in your hands. Tomorrow — sixteenth notes. Time to speed up.

title: Sixteenth notes
subtitle: Act 27 · Four notes per beat
goal: Recognize, count, and play sixteenth notes — four per beat.
piece_context: Tier 4 rhythm act begins. You already know eighths — two per beat. Sixteenths split that in half again. Four per beat. The building block of every fast passage.

pages.figure: Stack of note durations on a staff — whole, half, quarter, eighth, sixteenth. Each labeled with how many per beat. Title: SMALLER PIECES.
pages.cleffy: New act. Rhythm sharpens up. Quarter — one per beat. Eighth — two. Sixteenth — four. Each level, cut the piece in half.

pages.figure: Single sixteenth note drawn — filled oval, stem, TWO flags. Then four sixteenths beamed together with a double beam.
pages.cleffy: Shape: filled oval, stem, two flags. When sixteenths are in a row, the flags become a double beam. Two beams instead of one — that's how you spot them.

pages.figure: Bar of 4/4. Four beats, each broken into 4 sixteenth notes. Count printed below: 1-e-and-a 2-e-and-a 3-e-and-a 4-e-and-a.
pages.cleffy: Count: one-e-and-a, two-e-and-a, three-e-and-a, four-e-and-a. The number is the beat. The "and" is the halfway point — same as eighths. "E" and "a" are the new ones. They fill in the quarters of the beat.

pages.figure: Metronome at 60. Below: 16 sixteenth notes on Middle C across one bar.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 + 16 sixteenth notes on Middle C, one bar

pages.cleffy: Listen. Four taps per click. That's sixteenths.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: eight eighths in a bar. B: sixteen sixteenths in a bar.

audio.A: 8 eighth notes on C, tempo 60
audio.B: 16 sixteenth notes on C, tempo 60

pages.cleffy: Same bar length. Twice the notes in the second one. Eighths underneath, sixteenths on top.

pages.figure: Staff. Bar with one quarter, then four beamed sixteenths, then one half note. Time signature 4/4.
pages.cleffy: On the staff. Look for the double beam. One thick beam — eighths. Two thick beams — sixteenths.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. Metronome at 50. Four sixteenths per beat, one bar total.
pages.cleffy: Play C. Four taps per click. Slow metronome. Say the count out loud: one-e-and-a, two-e-and-a.

pages.figure: Bar: quarter, quarter, four sixteenths, four sixteenths. Counts under each beat.
pages.cleffy: Mix. Two quarters, then two beats of sixteenths. Feel the shift — slow, slow, fast fast fast fast.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Staff with either a quarter, eighth pair, or sixteenth group.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which note?

pages.figure: Staff. One bar in 4/4. Sixteenth notes: C D E F G A B C, ascending. Beamed in groups of four.
pages.cleffy: Scale up. Eight notes — a half-bar of sixteenths. Two beats. Count one-e-and-a, two-e-and-a.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 3. A bar plays on each round.
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. How many notes in the bar?

pages.figure: Bar: two eighths, then four sixteenths, then a half. Counts labeled.
pages.cleffy: Two eighths, four sixteenths, one half. All in one bar. Slow and clean.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 131. Four beamed sixteenths drawn on a staff. Caption: one-e-and-a.
pages.cleffy: Sixteenths. Four per beat. Double beam is the flag. Tomorrow — dotted eighth plus sixteenth. The galloping rhythm.

real_piano: Metronome at 50. Tap Middle C four times per click. Say the count out loud — one-e-and-a, two-e-and-a.
Play C-D-E-F as eighth notes. Then again as sixteenths. Feel how the speed doubles.

questions.figure: Four notes beamed with a double beam
questions.prompt: What kind of notes?

questions.figure: One beat, broken into four equal parts
questions.prompt: How many sixteenths fit in one beat?

questions.audio: 16 sixteenth notes on Middle C, one bar at tempo 60
questions.prompt: How many notes in the bar?
Play four sixteenth notes on Middle C in one beat at 55 bpm.
questions.accept: C4 × 4 at sixteenth duration, tempo ~55

remediation.title: Sixteenths, very slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four notes per beat takes practice. Drop the metronome to 40. We'll count every piece — one-e-and-a — out loud, before we play.

completion.cleffy: Sixteenths unlocked. Rhythm just doubled its vocabulary. Tomorrow — the galloping rhythm. Dotted eighth and sixteenth, together.

title: Dotted eighth + 16th
subtitle: Act 27 · The galloping rhythm
goal: Read and play the dotted-eighth-plus-sixteenth pattern — long-short, long-short.
piece_context: A rhythm you've heard a thousand times. Marches. Ragtime. 'We Will Rock You.' Same shape — a long note, a short note, glued together. That's the gallop.

pages.figure: Cleffy tapping foot — long-short, long-short. A horse-galloping graphic behind. Title: THE GALLOP.
pages.cleffy: New rhythm today. Long-short, long-short. The galloping rhythm. A march. A horse. A rock anthem. You know this one.

pages.figure: Staff. A dotted eighth note beamed to a single sixteenth. The beam has two bars on the sixteenth side, one on the dotted side. Label: dotted 8th + 16th = one beat.
pages.cleffy: On the page: a dotted eighth beamed to a sixteenth. The dot on the eighth adds half its value. Together they fill one beat. Fat note, tiny note. Long-short.

pages.figure: Beat split into four sixteenths. The first three are bracketed (dotted eighth = 3 sixteenths). The last one alone (sixteenth). Label: 3 + 1 = 4.
pages.cleffy: The math. Split the beat into four sixteenths. Dotted eighth takes the first three. The sixteenth takes the last one. Three quarters of the beat, then a flick.

pages.figure: Bar showing 'one — — a, two — — a, three — — a, four — — a.' The long note covers 1, e, and. The short note lands on 'a.'
pages.cleffy: Count it: one — AND a, two — AND a. Hold through 1, e, and. Then the sixteenth pops on "a." Long-short, long-short.

pages.figure: Metronome at 60. Below: a bar of four dotted-eighth + sixteenth pairs on Middle C.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 + four dotted-eighth + sixteenth pairs on C4

pages.cleffy: Listen. Long, short. Long, short. Feel the bounce. That's the gallop.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: straight eighths. B: dotted eighth + sixteenth pairs.

audio.A: 8 even eighth notes on C, tempo 60
audio.B: 4 dotted eighth + sixteenth pairs on C, tempo 60

pages.cleffy: Two patterns. Same tempo. Same number of beats. First — even eighths. Walking. Second — dotted-eighth and sixteenth. Galloping.

pages.figure: Staff. One beat. Dotted eighth + sixteenth on Middle C. Counts: 1 — AND a.
pages.cleffy: Just one pair. Middle C. Long note. Tiny quick note right before the next beat.

pages.figure: Bar of 4/4. Four dotted-eighth + sixteenth pairs, all on C. Slow tempo.
pages.cleffy: Four pairs in a row. One full bar. Long-short, long-short, long-short, long-short.

pages.figure: Three icons — marching boots, horse, sheet music for 'The Entertainer.' Caption: where the gallop lives.
pages.cleffy: Where you hear this rhythm: Military marches. Folk songs. Ragtime — all over Scott Joplin. Once you know the feel, you'll spot it everywhere.

pages.figure: Staff. Two-bar melody. Gallop pairs on C and E, then a half note on G to close.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Gallop on C, gallop on E, gallop on G, gallop on G, then land. Long-short through the whole bar.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. A bar plays each round.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Even eighths or galloping dotted-sixteenth?

pages.figure: Staff. C major scale up, each two-note step played as gallop. C-D E-F G-A B-C.
pages.cleffy: Gallop up the scale. Each pair — long-short. Slow. Precision over speed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 132. A horse silhouette galloping across a staff. The rhythm notation underneath.
pages.cleffy: Dotted eighth plus sixteenth. The gallop. Long-short. Long-short. Tomorrow — triplets. Three notes in the time of two.

real_piano: Metronome at 55. Play Middle C as dotted-eighth + sixteenth pairs. Say 'long-short' out loud.
Find any song you know that has this rhythm. Sing along. Feel where the 'short' note lands.

questions.figure: A dotted eighth note beamed to a single sixteenth
questions.prompt: How many beats does this pair take?

questions.figure: Bar of four gallop pairs
questions.prompt: How would you describe this rhythm?

questions.audio: Four dotted-eighth + sixteenth pairs on C4
questions.prompt: Even eighths or galloping?
Play two gallop pairs on Middle C — long-short, long-short.
questions.accept: C4 × 4 with dotted eighth + sixteenth durations

remediation.title: Gallop, much slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: This rhythm feels weird the first time. Even with a metronome. We'll drop to 40 bpm and clap it before we touch a key.

completion.cleffy: The gallop is yours. You'll spot it in pieces for the rest of your life. Tomorrow — triplets. A different kind of three.

title: Triplets
subtitle: Act 27 · Three notes in the time of two
goal: Recognize, count, and play triplets — three equal notes squeezed into one beat.
piece_context: Triplets bend the rules. Normally, a beat splits into 2 or 4. A triplet splits it into 3 — three even notes in the space where two would live. The number 3 above the notes is the signal.

pages.figure: Three beamed eighth notes with a small 3 above them and a bracket. Title: THE ODD ONE.
pages.cleffy: Most rhythm splits evenly. Two eighths per beat. Four sixteenths. Not today. Today we fit three into the space of two. Triplets. The little 3 is the signal.

pages.figure: Eighth-note triplet drawn large — three notes beamed together, with a bracket and a small numeral 3 above.
pages.cleffy: Shape: three eighth notes beamed together. Above the beam — a little 3. Sometimes a bracket. That 3 says: squeeze these three into one beat.

pages.figure: One beat. Below it, three triplet eighths with the count 'tri-puh-let' or '1-trip-let' written underneath.
pages.cleffy: Count: one-trip-let, two-trip-let, three-trip-let, four-trip-let. Three equal syllables per beat. Not faster. Just evened into three instead of two.

pages.figure: Two bars side by side. Left: four beats, each with two eighths (8 notes). Right: four beats, each a triplet (12 notes). Both labeled.
pages.cleffy: Two rhythms, same time. Eighths — eight notes, two per beat. Triplets — twelve notes, three per beat. Different feel. Same bar.

pages.figure: Metronome at 60. Below: one bar of four triplets on Middle C.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 + four eighth-note triplets on C4, one bar

pages.cleffy: Listen. Three even taps per click. One-trip-let, two-trip-let. Feel the roll.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: straight eighths. B: triplets.

audio.A: 8 eighth notes on C, tempo 60
audio.B: 12 triplet eighths on C, tempo 60

pages.cleffy: First — eighths. Two per beat. Even. Second — triplets. Three per beat. Rolls a little. Same tempo. Different subdivision.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. One beat. Three triplet eighths.
pages.cleffy: Just one triplet. Three taps on C. Say it: one-trip-let. Even spacing.

pages.figure: Bar of 4/4. Four triplet groups, all on C. Metronome at 55.
pages.cleffy: Four triplets in a row. One full bar. One-trip-let, two-trip-let, three-trip-let, four-trip-let.

pages.figure: Three examples: Moonlight Sonata opening, Swing feel label, waltz jazz bass. Caption: triplets are everywhere.
pages.cleffy: Triplets show up in: Moonlight Sonata — the whole opening is triplet arpeggios. Jazz and blues — the "swing" feel is triplets in disguise. Waltzes and folk tunes. Once you know the feel, you hear it everywhere.

pages.figure: Staff. A C major arpeggio as a triplet — C E G. Played four times in a bar.
pages.cleffy: Triplets with different notes. C, E, G — a C arpeggio. One triplet on each beat. Even. Rolling.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Each round plays one beat of either eighths or triplets.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Two per beat or three per beat?

pages.figure: Bar: quarter, quarter, triplet, triplet. Counts labeled under each.
pages.cleffy: Mix. Two quarters — walking. Then two triplets — rolling. Feel the change mid-bar.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 133. Three beamed eighths with a small 3 above, glowing. Caption: one-trip-let.
pages.cleffy: Triplets. Three in the space of two. The little 3 tells you. Tomorrow — rhythm puzzles. Clap first. Then play.

real_piano: Metronome at 55. Tap Middle C three times per click. Say 'one-trip-let, two-trip-let' out loud.
Play a C major arpeggio (C E G) as triplets. One triplet per beat for a full bar.

questions.figure: Three eighth notes beamed with a small 3 above
questions.prompt: What does the 3 mean?

questions.figure: Bar with four triplet groups
questions.prompt: How many total notes in this bar?

questions.audio: One bar of triplet eighths on C at tempo 60
questions.prompt: How many notes per beat?
Play a C major arpeggio — C, E, G — as a triplet in one beat.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 as triplet eighth notes

remediation.title: Triplets, with chanting
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three-against-two is a new thing for the brain. It'll click. We'll chant "one-trip-let" over and over before we touch a key.

completion.cleffy: Triplets unlocked. You just earned the Moonlight Sonata opening — that's all triplets. Tomorrow — puzzle time. Rhythm without pitch.

title: Rhythm puzzles
subtitle: Act 27 · Clap before you play
goal: Read a rhythm, clap it, then play it on one pitch. Rhythm as its own skill.
piece_context: The secret of professional sight-readers — they never read pitch and rhythm at the same time. First, the rhythm. Clap it. Count it. Only then add notes. Today you build that habit.

pages.figure: Cleffy tapping one hand on the other. A rhythm staff above with no pitches, just durations. Title: CLAP FIRST.
pages.cleffy: A trick pianists use forever. Clap the rhythm before you play it. No pitch. No keys. Just the beat. Once the rhythm is in your body, the notes come easy.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: student staring at notes, frozen. Right: student clapping, loose and calm.
pages.cleffy: Why clap first? Your brain can only do one new thing at a time. If the rhythm is solved, pitch is half the problem.

pages.figure: A one-line staff (just a single line) with quarters, eighths, a triplet, and a sixteenth run. No pitch letters.
pages.cleffy: A rhythm-only staff. One line. No pitch. Only durations and beats. This is how you isolate rhythm.

pages.figure: Rhythm: quarter, quarter, two eighths, quarter. One bar. Plays as claps.

audio.A: Clap sounds: clap, clap, clap-clap, clap. Tempo 60.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Four beats. Count along. One, two, three-and, four.

pages.figure: Rhythm-only staff. Quarter, quarter, two eighths, quarter. All played on Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Same rhythm. On one pitch. Middle C. Clap it first if you need. Then play.

pages.figure: Staff: dotted quarter, eighth, two eighths, quarter. Counts written: 1-and-a 2, 3-and 4.
pages.cleffy: Puzzle 2. Dotted quarter, eighth, two eighths, quarter. Look at the counts. Figure out where each note lands.

pages.figure: Same staff as previous. On Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Middle C all the way through. Focus on the durations, not the pitch.

pages.figure: Rhythm staff: quarter, four sixteenths, two eighths, quarter. Plays as claps.

audio.A: Clap sounds: clap, clap-clap-clap-clap, clap-clap, clap. Tempo 55.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Sixteenths in the second beat. Count one, two-e-and-a, three-and, four.

pages.figure: Same staff. All on Middle C. Metronome at 55.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Middle C. Stay steady through the sixteenths.

pages.figure: Staff: quarter, triplet, quarter, triplet. Counts: 1, 2-trip-let, 3, 4-trip-let.
pages.cleffy: Puzzle 4 — triplets. Quarter, triplet, quarter, triplet. Read the "3" above the groups.

pages.figure: Staff as shown. Middle C. Metronome at 55.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Quarter — triplet — quarter — triplet. One bar.

pages.figure: Staff: two gallop pairs, two eighths, quarter. On Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Puzzle 5. Galloping start, then even eighths, then a quarter. Long-short, long-short, one-and, two.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Three rhythm staves shown per round. One plays as claps.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which rhythm did you hear?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Rhythm only.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 134. Two hands mid-clap. Caption: clap first, play second.
pages.cleffy: Rhythm puzzles, solved. Clap first. Play second. Every time. Tomorrow — an act boss. Scott Joplin. The Entertainer. Syncopation.

real_piano: Pick any piece you know. Before you play it — clap through one line. Just rhythm.
Make up four beats of rhythm on paper. Clap it. Then play it on one note.

questions.figure: A rhythm: quarter, two eighths, quarter, quarter
questions.prompt: How many notes total?

questions.figure: A rhythm with a triplet group and three quarters
questions.prompt: How many notes total?

questions.audio: Claps: clap, clap-clap, clap, clap. Tempo 60.
questions.prompt: Did beat 2 split into two eighths?
Play on Middle C: quarter, quarter, two eighths, quarter. One bar at 60 bpm.
questions.accept: C4 × 5 with durations quarter, quarter, eighth, eighth, quarter

remediation.title: Clap, then play
remediation.opening_cleffy: Clapping a rhythm before playing it is a habit. Worth building slowly. We'll clap every puzzle twice, then play. No shortcuts.

completion.cleffy: Rhythm is its own skill now. Clap first. Always. Tomorrow — the act boss. Scott Joplin. Syncopation. You'll feel something new.

title: The Entertainer
subtitle: Act 27 · Syncopation in action
goal: Feel syncopation — accents that land off the beat — through the opening of Scott Joplin's The Entertainer, heavily simplified.
piece_context: This is the act boss for rhythm. And here's the honest part — nobody plays The Entertainer well on their first try. It's a hard piece. Today is not about playing it at tempo. Today is about feeling what syncopation is — notes that land between the beats instead of on them. The feel that makes ragtime sound like ragtime.

piece.title: The Entertainer
piece.difficulty: intermediate — opening melody only, heavily simplified, slow

pages.figure: Cleffy in a bowler hat. An old piano roll behind him. Banner: ACT 27 BOSS — THE ENTERTAINER.
pages.cleffy: Act 27 boss. Scott Joplin. 1902. The Entertainer. Before we start — the honest truth. You are not playing this at full speed today.

pages.figure: Two boxes side by side. Left: 'what this lesson is NOT' — playing The Entertainer at tempo, both hands, all of it. Right: 'what it IS' — feeling syncopation through the opening melody, very slow, one hand.
pages.cleffy: What today IS: the first phrase of the melody. Right hand only. Much slower than the recording. What today IS: the feel of syncopation. What today IS NOT: playing The Entertainer as written. That takes years.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top — notes on beats 1, 2, 3, 4 (straight). Bottom — notes on the 'ands' between beats (syncopated). Accent marks on the offbeats.
pages.cleffy: Syncopation. New word, big idea. Normal rhythm — accents land on the beat. One, TWO, three, FOUR. Syncopated rhythm — accents land off the beat. In the cracks. Surprising. Ragtime lives on syncopation.

pages.figure: A play button. Caption: The Entertainer, opening phrase, real recording.

audio.A: The Entertainer opening — original tempo, piano solo, the famous tune everyone knows

pages.cleffy: Listen. Just the opening. Hear how the melody doesn't sit on the beats? It dances around them. That's the syncopation.

pages.figure: A play button. Caption: same phrase, half speed, simplified.

audio.A: The Entertainer opening melody simplified — D5, B4, D5, E5, C5 — played at slow tempo

pages.cleffy: Now the same melody — simplified. Slower. Five notes: D, B, D, E, C. Still has the off-beat feel, but manageable.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Five notes: D5 (space above middle line), B4 (middle line), D5, E5 (top space), C5 (third space).
pages.cleffy: The five notes you'll play. D — the space above the middle line. B — the middle line. D again — same place. E — top space. C — third space.

pages.figure: Staff. Five notes with rhythm: eighth, eighth, dotted quarter, eighth, quarter (or similar syncopation).
pages.cleffy: The rhythm. This is the puzzle part. Two quick notes, then a long held note, then a quick one, then a landed one. The "long note" is what throws the accent off the beat.

pages.figure: Keyboard with D5, B4, D5, E5, C5 glowing in order. No rhythm yet — even quarters.
pages.cleffy: Notes first. Rhythm second. Five notes, even quarters. D, B, D, E, C.

pages.figure: Rhythm staff on Middle C. Same syncopated pattern. Counts labeled: 1-and, 2-and-a, 3, 4.
pages.cleffy: Now the rhythm. On one note. Feel the syncopation. Count out loud as you play.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Five notes D5, B4, D5, E5, C5 with syncopated rhythm. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both together. Very slow. Fifty on the metronome. Speed does not matter. Feel matters.

pages.figure: Same phrase. Guides on. Tempo 55.
pages.cleffy: With guides. A little faster — 55. Still not ragtime speed. That's fine.

pages.figure: Same phrase. No guides. Read and play.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read the page. Listen for the offbeat accent on the long note.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. The opening of The Entertainer. Slow, simplified, and syncopated.

pages.figure: Same phrase, played twice in a row. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Once more, twice through. Each pass, lean into the held note. That's where the swing lives.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 27 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 27 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding an old Joplin sheet. A pyramid of acts showing 27/50 complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 27. Closed. You did not play The Entertainer today. You played five notes in its shape, at half speed. What you DID do — feel syncopation. Hear accents land off the beat. Know why ragtime sounds like ragtime. Sixteenths, galloping rhythm, triplets, puzzles, syncopation. All new. All yours now. Next act — 6/8 time. A whole different kind of beat.

real_piano: The opening of The Entertainer every day this week. Five notes. Slow metronome. Focus on the held note that creates the off-beat feel.
Try to speed it up gently across the week — 55, then 60, then 65 bpm. Stop the moment it gets sloppy.
Search 'The Entertainer Scott Joplin' — listen to any version. Feel where the accents land. You now understand why that feel exists.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 11, 12 — slow, guided, then unguided

questions.figure: Two rhythms — one with accents on the beats, one with accents between the beats
questions.prompt: Which is syncopated?
options: The one with accents on beats
The one with accents off beats

questions.figure: Five notes: D5, B4, D5, E5, C5 on the staff
questions.prompt: These are the five notes of which opening melody?

questions.figure: A rhythm: eighth, eighth, dotted quarter, eighth, quarter
questions.prompt: Which note creates the syncopated feel?
options: The dotted quarter held through the beat

questions.audio: The simplified Entertainer opening at 55 bpm
questions.prompt: Do the accents land on the beats or between them?

questions.audio: A syncopated phrase vs a straight phrase
questions.prompt: Which one is syncopated?
Play the five notes — D5, B4, D5, E5, C5 — with the syncopated rhythm at 55 bpm.
questions.accept: D5 B4 D5 E5 C5 with eighth, eighth, dotted quarter, eighth, quarter durations

remediation.title: Entertainer, even slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Expected to be a real test — and syncopation is a whole new feel. We'll slow to 40 bpm, clap the rhythm only, then add one note at a time. No rush. The feel matters more than the speed.

completion.cleffy: Act 27 sealed. You have advanced rhythm — sixteenths, gallops, triplets, syncopation. You also have honesty. You didn't fake The Entertainer. You understood it. Tomorrow — 6/8 time. A whole new meter.

title: 6/8 time
subtitle: Act 28 · Time signatures & meters
goal: Feel 6/8 as a compound meter — two big beats, each split into three — and count a simple melody in that groove.
piece_context: Act 28 opens. You've lived in 4/4 and 3/4 for a hundred lessons. Today — a new kind of beat. 6/8 feels like rolling, rocking, lilting. Think sea shanties, lullabies, 'Hickory Dickory Dock.' The count goes in twos, but each two splits into three.

pages.figure: Cleffy rocking side to side like a boat. A big 6/8 time signature floats above him. Banner: ACT 28 — 6/8 TIME.
pages.cleffy: New meter today. 6/8 time. Compound. Rolling. If 4/4 marches, 6/8 sways.

pages.figure: Staff with a big 6/8 time signature. Callouts: top number '6 = six eighth notes per bar'. Bottom number '8 = the eighth note gets one count'.
pages.cleffy: Top number — six. Bottom number — eighth. The eighth note gets one count. So — six eighth notes in each bar.

pages.figure: One bar of 6/8. Six eighth notes, beamed in two groups of three. First group labeled 'ONE-and-a', second group 'TWO-and-a'.
pages.cleffy: Here's the trick. Six eighths — but you feel them in two groups of three. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a. Two big beats. Each split into three.

pages.figure: Split. Left: 4/4 bar with quarters — 'simple: each beat splits into 2'. Right: 6/8 bar — 'compound: each beat splits into 3'.
pages.cleffy: New word. Compound. Simple meter — each beat splits into two. Compound meter — each beat splits into three. 6/8 is compound. That's why it rolls.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 6/8 PULSE.

audio.A: Six eighth notes grouped as ONE-and-a TWO-and-a, at a lilting tempo

pages.cleffy: Listen. Feel the two big beats. Not one-two-three-four-five-six even. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a. A boat on small waves.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'Hickory Dickory Dock' — melody in 6/8.

audio.A: Hickory Dickory Dock melody played in 6/8, slow and clear

pages.cleffy: You know this one. Hickory Dickory Dock. Most nursery tunes in that lilting style — they're in 6/8. Hear the two big beats under the words.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 highlighted. Six eighth notes on C4, beamed in two groups of three.
pages.cleffy: Tap the pulse on Middle C. Six eighths per bar. Lean into the first and fourth. That's where the big beats live.

pages.figure: Treble staff in 6/8. Simple melody: E5 D5 C5 E5 D5 C5 (two groups of three, beamed).
pages.cleffy: Simple melody. One bar of 6/8. E, D, C. E, D, C. Two groups. Step down from E, three times. Then again.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E5 D5 C5 lit in order. Time signature 6/8 above.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Six eighths. Lean on the first and fourth.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. Hickory Dickory melody simplified: G4 E4 C5 G4 E4 C5 (or similar three-note figure).
pages.cleffy: Hickory Dickory shape. Two bars. Same three notes, twice. That's the nursery lilt.

pages.figure: Keyboard with G4, E4, C5 lit in rocking order.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Feel the rock. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. One bar: quarter, eighth, quarter, eighth. The common 6/8 pattern.
pages.cleffy: Common 6/8 figure. Quarter, eighth. Quarter, eighth. Long-short, long-short. Still two big beats.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. Melody: E5 (quarter) D5 (eighth) C5 (quarter) D5 (eighth). Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Long-short melody. With guides. Feel the lilt.
pages.followup_cleffy: That roll — that's 6/8.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 136. A staff with 6/8 time signature and six beamed eighths. Cleffy on a small boat.
pages.cleffy: 6/8. Compound. Two big beats, each split in three. Tomorrow — 3/4 next to 6/8. They look alike. They feel nothing alike.

real_piano: Count 'ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a' out loud while tapping the six eighth notes. Lean into ONE and TWO.
Play Hickory Dickory Dock's opening three-note figure in both hands, slow. Feel the two-beat pulse.

questions.figure: A 6/8 time signature on a staff
questions.prompt: How many eighth notes fit in one bar of 6/8?

questions.figure: Six eighth notes beamed in two groups of three
questions.prompt: How many big beats do you feel per bar?

questions.figure: Two time signatures: 4/4 and 6/8
questions.prompt: Which is a compound meter?

questions.audio: A phrase in 6/8 with a lilting rock
questions.prompt: Does this feel like a march or a rock?
Play six eighth notes on Middle C, leaning on beats 1 and 4.
questions.accept: C4 six times with a felt two-beat pulse

remediation.title: 6/8, slower, clapped
remediation.opening_cleffy: Compound meter is a new feel. We'll slow down, count out loud, and clap before we play. Every pass, just two words — ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a.

completion.cleffy: 6/8 — felt. Tomorrow — we set it next to 3/4 and see why they're different.

title: 3/4 vs 6/8
subtitle: Act 28 · Time signatures & meters
goal: Tell 3/4 from 6/8 by feel — three even pulses vs two rolling pulses — and play a short phrase in each.
piece_context: Yesterday we met 6/8. Today we set it next to 3/4 — the waltz meter you've known since Tier 1. Six eighth notes fit in both. But the feel is completely different. 3/4 has three equal pulses. 6/8 has two pulses, each split in three. Once you hear it, you'll never unhear it.

pages.figure: Cleffy with two hats — left hat says 3/4, right hat says 6/8. Both contain the same six eighth notes.
pages.cleffy: Two meters. Same six eighth notes per bar. Totally different feel. Today we hear the difference.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top: 3/4 bar with six eighths beamed in three pairs (labeled ONE, TWO, THREE). Bottom: 6/8 bar with six eighths beamed in two triplets (labeled ONE, TWO).
pages.cleffy: Same six notes. Different beams. 3/4 — three pairs. Three equal pulses. 6/8 — two triples. Two rolling pulses.

pages.figure: Two count-lines. 3/4: 'ONE-and, TWO-and, THREE-and' — three pulses. 6/8: 'ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a' — two pulses.
pages.cleffy: 3/4 — ONE-and, TWO-and, THREE-and. Three big beats. 6/8 — ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a. Two big beats. Count out loud. Feel which hand the emphasis falls on.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 3/4 PULSE — three equal beats.

audio.A: Six eighth notes in 3/4, grouped in pairs, three equal pulses — ONE-and TWO-and THREE-and

pages.cleffy: Listen. 3/4. Three even pulses. Like a waltz.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 6/8 PULSE — two rolling beats.

audio.A: Six eighth notes in 6/8, grouped in triplets, two rolling pulses — ONE-and-a TWO-and-a

pages.cleffy: Same six notes. 6/8. Two rolling pulses. Like a boat.

pages.figure: Two play buttons side by side. A = 3/4. B = 6/8.

audio.A: A phrase in 3/4 (three even pulses)
audio.B: The same six notes in 6/8 (two rolling pulses)

pages.cleffy: A, then B. Same notes. Listen for the difference — three beats or two.

pages.figure: Staff in 3/4. Six eighths on G4, beamed in three pairs. Accent on each pair's first note.
pages.cleffy: Play six eighths in 3/4. Accent the first of each pair. ONE-and, TWO-and, THREE-and.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. Six eighths on G4, beamed in two triplets. Accent on notes 1 and 4.
pages.cleffy: Same six notes. Now in 6/8. Accent the first and the fourth. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a.

pages.figure: Two columns. Left (3/4): 'Happy Birthday', 'Minuet in G', 'Amazing Grace'. Right (6/8): 'Hickory Dickory', 'For He's a Jolly Good Fellow', 'Row Row Row Your Boat'.
pages.cleffy: Pieces you know. 3/4 — Happy Birthday, Minuet in G, Amazing Grace. 6/8 — Hickory Dickory, Jolly Good Fellow, Row Row Row.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Each plays a short phrase. Banner — 3/4 or 6/8?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen — 3/4 or 6/8?

pages.figure: Staff in 3/4. Waltz-like melody: C5 (quarter), E5 (eighth), G5 (eighth), E5 (quarter). Two-bar phrase.
pages.cleffy: 3/4 phrase. Waltz feel. Three-count pulse.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. Rolling melody: C5 E5 G5 E5 C5 E5 — one bar.
pages.cleffy: 6/8 phrase. Rolling feel. Two-count pulse.

pages.figure: Two bars side by side — first 3/4, then 6/8, both using the notes C5 E5 G5. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Same three pitches, different meters. Feel how the body wants to move differently in each.
pages.followup_cleffy: Different meters. Different worlds.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 137. Left half shows 3/4 (waltz). Right half shows 6/8 (boat). Cleffy split-screen between them.
pages.cleffy: 3/4 — three even pulses. 6/8 — two rolling pulses. Tomorrow — cut time. The beat changes again.

real_piano: Play Happy Birthday (3/4) and Row Row Row Your Boat (6/8) back to back. Feel the difference in your body.
Six eighths on any note — first in 3/4 (accent beats 1, 3, 5), then in 6/8 (accent beats 1, 4). Same notes, different emphasis.

questions.figure: A bar of six eighth notes beamed in two groups of three
questions.prompt: Which meter?

questions.figure: A bar of six eighth notes beamed in three pairs
questions.prompt: Which meter?

questions.figure: A count-line: ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a
questions.prompt: Which meter does this counting fit?

questions.audio: A phrase with three equal pulses
questions.prompt: Which meter?

questions.audio: A phrase with two rolling pulses
questions.prompt: Which meter?
