Play six eighth notes on G4, accenting the first of each pair (3/4 feel).
questions.accept: G4 six times with three-pulse accent pattern

remediation.title: 3/4 and 6/8 side by side
remediation.opening_cleffy: The two meters look the same on paper. The feel is what matters. We'll tap, count, and clap each — slowly — until the difference is obvious.

completion.cleffy: 3/4 and 6/8 — two separate worlds, now. Tomorrow — cut time.

title: Cut time
subtitle: Act 28 · Time signatures & meters
goal: Read cut time (2/2) — the half note as beat — and feel why music labeled this way moves in two, not four.
piece_context: Two meters down this act. Now a third — cut time. The 'C' with a line through it. 2/2. The half note gets the beat. Same notes as 4/4 on the page, but counted in two big pulses instead of four small ones. Marches, fast classical pieces, fiddle tunes — they live here.

pages.figure: A big C-with-a-slash (the cut time symbol) floating next to a staff. Cleffy holding scissors up to a 4/4 time signature.
pages.cleffy: Cut time. The symbol — a C with a line through it. Sometimes written 2/2. Same notes as 4/4, counted in half. Watch.

pages.figure: Staff with 2/2 time signature. Callouts: '2 = two beats per bar'. '2 = the half note gets the beat'.
pages.cleffy: Top number — two. Two beats per bar. Bottom number — two. The half note gets the beat. Not the quarter. The half.

pages.figure: Side by side. Left: the C-with-a-slash. Right: 2/2. Label: 'same thing'.
pages.cleffy: Two ways to write it. A C with a slash — the old symbol. Or 2/2 — the modern way. Same meter either way.

pages.figure: Two bars. Top — 4/4 with four quarter notes, counted 1-2-3-4. Bottom — 2/2 with the same four quarter notes, counted 1-and-2-and.
pages.cleffy: Here's the shift. 4/4 — four quarters, four beats. ONE-two-three-four. 2/2 — four quarters, two beats. ONE-and, TWO-and. Same notes. Half the counts. Everything feels faster.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: CUT TIME — two big beats.

audio.A: Four quarter notes in cut time at a brisk tempo — felt as ONE-and TWO-and

pages.cleffy: Listen. Two big pulses. Not four little ones. Two.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A = phrase in 4/4. B = same phrase in cut time.

audio.A: A simple march phrase felt as 4/4 — four counts per bar
audio.B: Same phrase felt as cut time — two counts per bar, faster pulse

pages.cleffy: A — four beats per bar. Comfortable. B — same notes, two beats per bar. Brisker. More drive.

pages.figure: Three examples: a military march, a fast classical allegro, a fiddle tune. All labeled 'cut time'.
pages.cleffy: When do you see cut time? Marches. Fast classical. Fiddle tunes. Anytime the music moves too fast to count four beats a bar.

pages.figure: Staff with cut time signature. Melody: C5, C5, G4, G4 — four quarter notes, one bar.
pages.cleffy: Four quarters. One bar of cut time. Count in two — ONE-and, TWO-and. Play brisk.

pages.figure: One bar of cut time with two half notes. Labeled 'ONE' and 'TWO' under each.
pages.cleffy: Now the half note is king. Two halves — one per beat. The simplest bar of cut time.

pages.figure: Staff in cut time. Two half notes: C5, G4.
pages.cleffy: Two halves. One beat each. Brisk tempo.

pages.figure: Two bars of cut time. Bar 1: four quarters (C5 E5 G5 E5). Bar 2: two halves (C5 G4).
pages.cleffy: March pattern. Two bars. Quick steps, then long steps. Feel the two big beats ticking underneath.

pages.figure: Cut time bar: quarter, two eighths, quarter, quarter. Notes: C5, D5, E5, F5, E5.
pages.cleffy: Eighths inside cut time fly by. Each eighth is half a beat-and-a-half — barely a blink. Just play clean.

pages.figure: Two bars in cut time with a simple melodic phrase. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Full phrase. With guides. Brisk — but keep the two big pulses steady.
pages.followup_cleffy: Cut time. Half notes drive. Everything moves.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 138. A C-with-slash next to 2/2 next to a marching Cleffy.
pages.cleffy: Cut time. Half = beat. Two pulses per bar. Tomorrow — sixteenths at speed.

real_piano: Play four quarter notes on Middle C. Count them once as 4/4 (four beats), then as cut time (two beats). Feel the shift.
Find any march — online, from memory, anything. Listen and count in two, not four.

questions.figure: A C with a vertical line through it at the start of a staff
questions.prompt: What meter is this?

questions.figure: A 2/2 time signature
questions.prompt: Which note gets the beat?

questions.figure: A bar of cut time with four quarter notes
questions.prompt: How many big beats does this bar have?

questions.audio: A phrase in cut time at brisk tempo
questions.prompt: How many big pulses per bar?
Play two half notes in cut time at 120 bpm — one beat each.
questions.accept: C5 G4 as half notes at 120 bpm

remediation.title: Cut time, eased in
remediation.opening_cleffy: Cut time reframes what a beat is. That's a real shift. We'll start at half speed, count in two, then bring the tempo up.

completion.cleffy: Cut time — half note drives the pulse. Two big beats. Tomorrow — sixteenths at speed.

title: Rhythm at speed
subtitle: Act 28 · Time signatures & meters
goal: Read short sixteenth-note patterns at increasing tempo — clean, even, no rushing.
piece_context: In L131 you met sixteenths. Four notes per beat. Today — at speed. Short one-bar patterns, started slow, stepped up gently. The point isn't to play fast. The point is to play fast CLEANLY.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a metronome dial, slowly turning it clockwise. Banner: RHYTHM AT SPEED.
pages.cleffy: Sixteenths. Four per beat. Today — at tempo. Not to impress. To stay clean.

pages.figure: One beat divided four ways. Four sixteenth notes beamed together. Label: 1-e-and-a.
pages.cleffy: Reminder. Sixteenths split a beat into four. Count: one-e-and-a, two-e-and-a. Say it out loud. It anchors the hand.

pages.figure: Cleffy frowning at a wobbly sixteenth run. Arrow labels: 'the trap — rushing on beat 3'.
pages.cleffy: The trap with fast notes — rushing. Beat 1 is steady. Beat 2 is steady. Beat 3 — the hand gets ahead of itself. Metronome fixes this. Always.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A = clean. B = rushed.

audio.A: Four sixteenth-note groups played perfectly even at 60 bpm
audio.B: Four sixteenth-note groups rushed — the third group accelerates

pages.cleffy: A — even. B — rushed. Your ear already knows which one is wrong. Trust it.

pages.figure: One bar in 4/4. Four sixteenths on C5, then held quarter. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Pattern one. Four sixteenths, then a quarter rest. Slow — sixty.

pages.figure: Same pattern. Metronome 72.
pages.cleffy: Same pattern. Seventy-two. Even. Not louder. Not pushed.

pages.figure: Same pattern. Metronome 84.
pages.cleffy: Eighty-four. Each sixteenth still distinct. If it starts to blur, we back off.

pages.figure: Staff: four sixteenths stepping C5 D5 E5 F5, then quarter rest.
pages.cleffy: New pattern. Four sixteenths, stepwise up. C, D, E, F. One beat total.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5 D5 E5 F5 lit in order. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Slow. Each note clean. Each finger lift.

pages.figure: Same pattern. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Eighty. Stay even.

pages.figure: Same pattern. Metronome 100.
pages.cleffy: One hundred. Last notch today. If it goes clean — we're done climbing.

pages.figure: Staff: eighth, two sixteenths, eighth, two sixteenths. One bar. Mixed rhythm.
pages.cleffy: Mixed pattern. Eighth, two sixteenths — twice. The sixteenths sit inside the second half of each beat. Counts: one-and-a, two-and-a.

pages.figure: Staff with mixed pattern. Notes: C5, D5, E5, C5, D5, E5. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Steady. No rush.

pages.figure: One full bar of 4/4: sixteen sixteenth notes, stepwise shape C5 D5 E5 F5 repeated. Metronome 72.
pages.cleffy: Full bar. Sixteen sixteenths. One bar. Steady metronome. Don't speed up mid-bar.
pages.followup_cleffy: Clean speed beats fast speed.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 139. Metronome with a clean click graph. Cleffy giving a small steady nod.
pages.cleffy: Sixteenths at speed. You climbed from 60 to 100 without blurring. Tomorrow — 6/8 at speed. Act boss. Tarantella.

real_piano: One pattern, one metronome, five minutes a day. Start at 60. Every day add 4. No mistakes — if the mistakes start, drop back 4.
Any scale you know — play it in sixteenths, slow, then one notch faster each pass.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5-7 (the tempo climb) and 14 (full bar)

questions.figure: Four sixteenth notes beamed together
questions.prompt: How many sixteenths fit in one beat?

questions.figure: A rhythm: eighth, two sixteenths
questions.prompt: How should you count this?

questions.figure: Two sixteenth-note runs — one even, one with a rushed third beat
questions.prompt: Which is correct?

questions.audio: A clean sixteenth-note pattern
questions.prompt: Is this even or rushed?
Play four sixteenths on C5 evenly at 80 bpm.
questions.accept: C5 four times as sixteenths at 80 bpm, even

remediation.title: Slower first
remediation.opening_cleffy: Fast isn't the goal. Even is. We'll drop to 50 bpm, clap each sixteenth, then add the keyboard one pattern at a time.

completion.cleffy: Sixteenths — clean, even, up to a hundred. Tomorrow — act boss. Tarantella. 6/8 with drive.

title: Tarantella
subtitle: Act 28 · Time signatures & meters
goal: Play a simplified Tarantella shape in 6/8 — feel the Italian dance rhythm, even at a slow tempo.
piece_context: Act 28 boss. You've met 6/8, 3/4 vs 6/8, cut time, and sixteenths at speed. Now — a Tarantella. A fast Italian folk dance in 6/8, meant to be played wildly. You will not play it wildly today. You'll play a simplified six-note pattern at a slow tempo. The real Tarantella is much faster — you are playing its shape, not its speed.

piece.title: Tarantella
piece.difficulty: intermediate — opening shape only, slow tempo, right hand only

pages.figure: Cleffy in a red neckerchief, tapping a tambourine. Italian village square behind him. Banner: ACT 28 BOSS — TARANTELLA.
pages.cleffy: Act 28 boss. The Tarantella. An Italian folk dance. Fast, wild, 6/8. But first — the honest truth.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left: 'the real Tarantella' — fiddles, tambourines, dizzy fast tempo. Right: 'today' — a simplified six-note shape, slow tempo, one hand.
pages.cleffy: The real Tarantella runs at 160, 180, sometimes faster. Tonight — sixty to seventy-two. Much slower. You're playing the shape of the dance, not its speed. That shape still teaches you 6/8.

pages.figure: Illustration of a spider. Old Italian text. 'Tarantula → Tarantella'.
pages.cleffy: The name comes from the tarantula. Legend said dancing the Tarantella cured a tarantula bite. Whether or not it works — the music survived. Hundreds of years old.

pages.figure: Treble staff in 6/8. Melody: D5, E5, F5, E5, D5, C5 — six eighth notes, beamed in two groups of three.
pages.cleffy: Six notes. One bar of 6/8. D, E, F, E, D, C. Up, then down. That rocking shape — that's the Tarantella engine.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: real Tarantella at full tempo.

audio.A: Traditional Italian Tarantella at full tempo — fast 6/8 with tambourine

pages.cleffy: Listen to the real thing. Fast. Don't panic — you're not trying to match it. Just feel the two rolling pulses. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: your simplified version, 72 bpm.

audio.A: Simplified six-note Tarantella figure D E F E D C played at 72 bpm in 6/8

pages.cleffy: Now yours. Six notes. Seventy-two. Same shape. Much slower. Still has the lilt.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D5 E5 F5 E5 D5 C5 highlighted in order. Even quarters, no 6/8 rhythm yet.
pages.cleffy: Notes first. Even quarters. No rhythm yet. D, E, F, E, D, C.

pages.figure: Staff in 6/8. Same six notes as eighth notes, beamed in two triplets. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Now in 6/8. Six eighths. Sixty. Lean on the first and fourth. ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a.

pages.figure: Same staff. Accent marks above beats 1 and 4 of the bar. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Sixty-six. Accent the first and fourth this time. Make the dance pulse.

pages.figure: Grand staff in 6/8. Two bars of the six-note figure, repeated. Guides on. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. With guides. Same shape, twice through.

pages.figure: Same two bars. No guides. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read the page. Feel the two pulses. Lean, lean. Lean, lean.

pages.figure: Four bars. The six-note figure, then a variant: D E F E D A4 (ending on A). Repeat the opening, then the variant.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. The figure plus a little ending. Last bar ends low — A — to close the phrase.

pages.figure: Same four bars. Tempo 72 — a touch faster.
pages.cleffy: Seventy-two. One notch up. Still not Italian-village-speed. Still teaching the feel.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. The Tarantella shape — yours now.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 28 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 28 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy with a tambourine. A pyramid showing 28/50 acts complete. Italian village at sunset.
pages.cleffy: Act 28. Closed. You did not play the Tarantella at real speed today. That would take months of fast-finger work. What you DID do — feel 6/8 as a dance. Tell 3/4 from 6/8. Read cut time. Play sixteenths at 100 without blurring. And carry a Tarantella shape, clean, in both hands of your ear. Time signatures and meters — you can read them all now. Next act — dynamics and tempo words.

real_piano: Play the six-note Tarantella figure every day this week. Start at 60. By Friday, try 80. If it gets sloppy, drop back.
Pick any 6/8 piece you've heard — Row Row Row, For He's a Jolly Good Fellow, any jig. Play its opening. Count ONE-and-a, TWO-and-a out loud.
Search 'tarantella piano' — listen to a few. Tchaikovsky wrote one. Rossini too. Feel the dance even at fast speeds.

questions.figure: A six-note figure beamed in two groups of three
questions.prompt: Which meter is this?

questions.figure: A 6/8 bar with accents on notes 1 and 4
questions.prompt: How many big beats are felt per bar?

questions.figure: The six-note melody D E F E D C on staff
questions.prompt: What country did the Tarantella come from?

questions.audio: The simplified Tarantella figure at 66 bpm
questions.prompt: How many big pulses do you feel per bar?

questions.audio: A real Tarantella and a waltz played back to back
questions.prompt: Which is the Tarantella?
Play the six-note Tarantella figure — D5, E5, F5, E5, D5, C5 — in 6/8 at 66 bpm, leaning on beats 1 and 4.
questions.accept: D5 E5 F5 E5 D5 C5 as six eighth notes in 6/8 at 66 bpm

remediation.title: Tarantella, slower still
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss, and a new meter still fresh — expected to be a stretch. We'll drop to 54 bpm, play three notes at a time, then six, then the full phrase. The real Tarantella is fast. You are learning the shape, not the speed. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 28 sealed. Time signatures — all the important ones, now under your hands. 4/4, 3/4, 6/8, cut time. You can read them. You can feel them. Tomorrow — dynamics and tempo words. How to play loud, soft, fast, slow.

title: pp to ff
subtitle: Act 29 · Dynamics & tempo
goal: Read and play the full dynamic range — pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff — six voices from whisper to thunder.
piece_context: Act 29 opens. Rhythm is done. Now — expression. You know p and f from way back in Tier 2. Today the range opens up. Six markings, whisper to thunder.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano. Above him, six labels stacked in a column — pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff — growing from a whisper icon to a thunder icon. Banner: ACT 29. DYNAMICS.
pages.cleffy: Act 29. Rhythm is behind you. Now — expression. You know loud and soft. Today the range widens.

pages.figure: A vertical chart. Six rows stacked. From top to bottom: ff (fortissimo / very loud), f (forte / loud), mf (mezzo-forte / medium loud), mp (mezzo-piano / medium soft), p (piano / soft), pp (pianissimo / very soft). Volume bars grow on the right.
pages.cleffy: Six voices. At the top — ff. Fortissimo. Very loud. Then f — forte. Loud. mf — mezzo-forte. Medium loud. mp — mezzo-piano. Medium soft. p — piano. Soft. pp — pianissimo. Very soft.

pages.figure: Three Italian word cards. 'mezzo' = medium. 'piano' = soft. 'forte' = loud. A suffix '-issimo' at the bottom labeled 'very'.
pages.cleffy: Three Italian words. Mezzo — medium. Piano — soft. Forte — loud. Stick 'issimo' on the end — very. That's the whole system.

pages.figure: Play button. Six middle-C notes played in a row, each one louder than the last. Labels under each: pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff.

audio.A: Middle C played six times, each at increasing volume from pianissimo to fortissimo

pages.cleffy: Listen to the ramp. Same note. Six volumes. Whisper to thunder.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C highlighted. Two markings on the staff — p, then pp. Volume meter bars shown.
pages.cleffy: Two soft voices. Play C soft. Then play C even softer. pp is almost nothing. Barely a sound.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C highlighted. Two markings — f, then ff.
pages.cleffy: Two loud voices. Play C loud. Then play C louder still. ff is thunder — but never harsh. Full, not angry.

pages.figure: Chart zoomed on mp and mf. 'mp — medium soft, lean toward quiet'. 'mf — medium loud, lean toward strong'. An arrow between them labeled 'the busy middle'.
pages.cleffy: mp and mf — the middle two. Most music actually lives here. Not whisper, not thunder. Just speaking.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C D E highlighted. Staff: C marked mp, D marked mf, E marked mf.
pages.cleffy: Three notes. First one medium-soft. Next two medium-loud. Small step up in volume — not a jump.

pages.figure: Staff with six notes: C D E F G A. Each one marked pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff in order.
pages.cleffy: Six notes. Climb the dynamic ladder. Start at pp. Finish at ff. One step louder each note.

pages.figure: Staff with six notes: A G F E D C. Each one marked ff, f, mf, mp, p, pp in order.
pages.cleffy: Now the other way. Thunder back down to whisper. Six notes. Lose a little on every one.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Which dynamic?' Options shown per round.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen. Name the marking.

pages.figure: Staff: four notes C E G E. First marked pp, last marked ff, middle two unmarked.
pages.cleffy: Pianissimo to fortissimo. Direct. The middle notes are your bridge. Grow the sound as you climb.

pages.figure: Staff: eight notes. Dynamic markings under: pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff, f, mf. A shape that rises then settles.
pages.cleffy: A whole phrase. Eight notes. Whisper, build, arrive, ease back. This is how real pieces breathe.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Six voices, one line.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 141. Six dynamic markings stacked in a column, each glowing. Banner: RANGE UNLOCKED.
pages.cleffy: Six voices, known. pp to ff — the full range. Tomorrow — sudden changes. Subito.

real_piano: Play Middle C six times. Each one a different dynamic — pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff. Feel the difference in your hand.
Take any piece you know. Pick one phrase. Play it at pp. Then the same phrase at ff. Listen to what changes.

questions.figure: Dynamic marking ff
questions.prompt: What does ff mean?

questions.figure: Dynamic marking mp
questions.prompt: What does mp mean?

questions.figure: Six markings in a column: pp, p, mp, mf, f, ff
questions.prompt: Which is the loudest?

questions.audio: A note played pianissimo
questions.prompt: Which dynamic?
Play six notes ascending, each one louder — pp to ff.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 A4 with growing volume

remediation.title: pp to ff, one step at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Six voices is a lot at once. We'll take two at a time. pp and p first. Then mp and mf. Then f and ff. By the end, all six will feel different in your hand.

completion.cleffy: Full dynamic range — yours. Tomorrow — subito. Sudden changes, no warning.

title: Subito changes
subtitle: Act 29 · Dynamics & tempo
goal: Read and play subito markings — sudden piano and sudden forte — where the dynamic drops or leaps without warning.
piece_context: Yesterday — the six-voice range. Today — how composers surprise you. A loud phrase, then suddenly soft. No ramp, no warning. The weather changes mid-sentence.

pages.figure: Cleffy under a split sky — sunny on the left, storm cloud on the right, a sharp line between them. Banner: SUBITO.
pages.cleffy: Subito. Italian for 'sudden'. A weather change in the music — no warning.

pages.figure: Two markings on a staff. Left: 'sub. p'. Right: 'sub. f'. Arrows point to the note they apply to.
pages.cleffy: Two markings. sub. p — suddenly soft. sub. f — suddenly loud. Sometimes written just 'subito p' or 'subito f' in full.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Top: a crescendo hairpin growing into f — labeled 'GRADUAL'. Bottom: a row of f notes, then a bar line, then sub. p — labeled 'SUDDEN'. Big arrow highlighting the instant drop.
pages.cleffy: The key word is sudden. A crescendo grows slowly. Subito doesn't grow. It snaps. One note loud. Next note soft. Right at the bar line.

pages.figure: Play button. Four loud notes, then four soft notes, no gradual change. Label: FOUR LOUD. SUB. P. FOUR SOFT.

audio.A: Four forte notes on middle C, then four piano notes — abrupt volume drop, no ramp

pages.cleffy: Listen. Four loud. Then — drop. That's subito piano. The surprise is the point.

pages.figure: Play button. Four soft notes, then four loud notes, abrupt rise. Label: FOUR SOFT. SUB. F. FOUR LOUD.

audio.A: Four piano notes on middle C, then four forte notes — abrupt volume rise, no ramp

pages.cleffy: Now the other direction. Four soft. Then — bang. Subito forte. A shout out of a whisper.

pages.figure: Staff: eight notes. First four marked f. Bar line. Sub. p under note five. Notes five through eight marked p.
pages.cleffy: Four loud, four soft. No ramp. The drop happens at the bar line. Land softly the moment you cross.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a Beethoven-style score. Arrow pointing to a sub. p marking in the middle of a passage. Caption: 'It wakes the listener up.'
pages.cleffy: Why do composers do this? Expectation. A crescendo tells you what's coming. Subito doesn't. It wakes the listener up.

pages.figure: Staff: six notes. First three marked pp. Bar line. Sub. f under note four. Notes four through six marked ff.
pages.cleffy: Three whispers. Then thunder. Let the pp really be quiet — that makes the ff bigger.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Gradual change or subito?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen — is the change gradual, or sudden?

pages.figure: Staff showing a crescendo hairpin into a bar line. Past the bar line — sub. p. Arrow: 'CRESCENDO, THEN SNAP.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the trickiest version. A crescendo — getting louder. Then just when you expect the payoff — sub. p. Loud climb, soft landing. Heartbreaking.

pages.figure: Staff: seven notes. First five rise from p, crescendo hairpin under them. Bar line. Sub. p under note six. Last two notes marked pp.
pages.cleffy: Five-note climb. Getting louder. Bar line. Soft. Pull the sound way back, fast.

pages.figure: Staff: eight notes. Markings alternate by bar: f, bar, sub. p, bar, sub. f, bar, sub. p. Weather keeps changing.
pages.cleffy: The weather keeps flipping. Two loud. Two soft. Two loud. Two soft. Snap into each one — no easing in.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's control. Loud and soft, on command.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Audio plays subito p or subito f. Banner: 'Subito piano or subito forte?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which direction did the weather flip?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 142. A cloud on one side, a sunbeam on the other, lightning bolt between them. Label: SUBITO.
pages.cleffy: Sudden soft. Sudden loud. The weather in music. Tomorrow — speed. Tempo words.

real_piano: Take any piece you know. Pick a spot. Play the bar before loud, then the next bar suddenly soft. Notice how different the phrase feels.
Practice the control itself — play a scale at f, then at the top snap to pp on the way down. Make the drop clean.

questions.figure: Marking: sub. p
questions.prompt: What does this mean?

questions.figure: A crescendo hairpin next to a sub. p marking
questions.prompt: What happens at the sub. p?

questions.figure: Italian word: subito
questions.prompt: What does subito mean?

questions.audio: Loud notes followed by an abrupt drop to soft
questions.prompt: Gradual or subito?
Play four loud notes, then drop suddenly to four soft notes.
questions.accept: 8 notes with a clear sudden volume drop between notes 4 and 5

remediation.title: Subito, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Sudden is hard — your hand wants to ease into the change. We'll practice the snap on a single note first. Loud. Stop. Soft. Then build up to phrases.

completion.cleffy: Subito — known. You can surprise a listener. Tomorrow — speed words. Adagio. Allegro.

title: Tempo markings
subtitle: Act 29 · Dynamics & tempo
goal: Read the four most common tempo words and know roughly how fast each one is — adagio, moderato, allegro, presto.
piece_context: Volume has a full range. So does speed. Today — the Italian words composers write at the top of a piece to tell you how fast. Four of them, most common, in order from slow to blazing.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing to the top-left corner of a staff. The word 'Allegro' floats there in italic serif. Banner: TEMPO.
pages.cleffy: Tempo. The word written above the very first note of a piece. Italian again. Tells you the speed.

pages.figure: Four rows stacked. Top to bottom: Presto (≈ 160 bpm, label: very fast), Allegro (≈ 120 bpm, label: fast), Moderato (≈ 90 bpm, label: walking), Adagio (≈ 60 bpm, label: slow). A metronome icon on each row.
pages.cleffy: Four words to know. Adagio — slow, about 60 bpm. The heartbeat. Moderato — walking pace, about 90. Allegro — fast, about 120. Presto — very fast, about 160. Running.

pages.figure: Italian word cards with translations. Adagio = 'at ease'. Moderato = 'moderate'. Allegro = 'cheerful'. Presto = 'quickly'.
pages.cleffy: The Italian tells the story. Adagio — 'at ease'. Not just slow, but relaxed. Moderato — 'moderate'. No rush. Allegro — 'cheerful'. Bright, lively. Presto — 'quickly'. Urgent. Tempo is a mood, not just a number.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: ADAGIO — 60 BPM.

audio.A: A simple scale played at 60 bpm — slow, spacious, like a heartbeat

pages.cleffy: Adagio. Listen. Room between the notes. Space to breathe.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: MODERATO — 90 BPM.

audio.A: Same scale played at 90 bpm — walking pace

pages.cleffy: Moderato. A steady walk. Not slow, not fast. Just moving.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: ALLEGRO — 120 BPM.

audio.A: Same scale played at 120 bpm — quick, lively

pages.cleffy: Allegro. Lively now. This is where most fast music lives.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: PRESTO — 160 BPM.

audio.A: Same scale played at 160 bpm — very fast

pages.cleffy: Presto. Running. Hard to play. Harder to keep clean.

pages.figure: Staff: five notes C D E F G. Tempo marking: Adagio. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Five notes. Adagio. Slow. Let each one ring before the next.

pages.figure: Same five notes. Tempo marking: Moderato. Metronome 90.
pages.cleffy: Same five. Moderato now. Walking pace. Steady.

pages.figure: Same five notes. Tempo marking: Allegro. Metronome 120.
pages.cleffy: Allegro. Lively. Don't rush the notes — let the metronome pull you.

pages.figure: A ruler labeled bpm. Markers along it — 60 adagio, 76 andante, 90 moderato, 108 allegretto, 120 allegro, 160 presto. Caption: 'There are more — but these four are the main ones.'
pages.cleffy: There are more words between these. Andante — walking slowly. Allegretto — a little less than allegro. You'll see them. But the four you already know cover most music.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Which tempo marking fits?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen and pick.

pages.figure: Staff with a C-D-E-F-G-F-E-D-C phrase. Three versions stacked: top marked Adagio 60, middle Moderato 90, bottom Allegro 120.
pages.cleffy: Same phrase. Three speeds. Feel the mood change. Same notes, different piece.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 143. Four tempo words stacked in italic serif, each with a metronome icon and bpm label.
pages.cleffy: Four speeds known. Adagio to presto. Tomorrow — bending the tempo. Rubato.

real_piano: Set the metronome to 60. Play a scale. Then 90. Then 120. Feel the difference in your hand at each speed.
Pick any piece you know. Play it moderato. Then try it adagio. Notice how the adagio version asks for more from you — more shape, more care.

questions.figure: The word: Allegro
questions.prompt: Roughly how fast?

questions.figure: The word: Adagio
questions.prompt: Roughly how fast?

questions.figure: Four words: adagio, moderato, allegro, presto
questions.prompt: Which is the fastest?

questions.audio: A phrase played at 60 bpm
questions.prompt: Which tempo marking fits?
Play a five-note scale at Moderato (90 bpm).
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 steady at 90 bpm

remediation.title: Tempo words, paired with numbers
remediation.opening_cleffy: The Italian gets mixed up at first. Allegro and adagio sound alike. We'll pair each word with a number, drill it, and play it. By the end they'll feel different to your hand.

completion.cleffy: Four tempos, known. Tomorrow — bending them. Rubato. Stretch, then give it back.

title: Simple rubato
subtitle: Act 29 · Dynamics & tempo
goal: Stretch a note inside a phrase, then speed up the next notes to pay it back — so the phrase still lands on time.
piece_context: Tempo marking at the top of the page sets the speed. But real players don't follow it like a robot. They stretch some notes, hurry others — then land back on the beat. That's rubato. Borrowed time, paid back inside the phrase.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a pocket watch that has a soft rubber band wrapped around it, stretched and snapping back. Banner: RUBATO.
pages.cleffy: Rubato. Italian for 'stolen'. Stolen time — borrowed from one note, given back to the next.

pages.figure: A phrase of eight notes along a horizontal timeline. First note stretched wide. The next seven compressed to catch up. A banner at the end labeled 'BACK ON THE BEAT'.
pages.cleffy: Here's the rule. Stretch a note — let it sing a little longer. The next notes move a little faster to catch up. By the end of the phrase, you're back on the beat. Always.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top: 'slowing down' — arrow points to every note, all stretched. Bottom: 'rubato' — one note stretched, the rest hurry. Caption: 'Rubato is local. It lives inside a phrase.'
pages.cleffy: Rubato is not just slowing down. Slowing down means every note is slower. Rubato means ONE note sings, and the others pay for it.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome clicks. Label: STRICT TIME — every note locked to the click.

audio.A: An eight-note phrase played in perfectly strict time at 70 bpm — metronome clicking underneath

pages.cleffy: Listen. Strict time. Correct — but a little mechanical.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome clicks. Label: WITH RUBATO — note three stretched, notes four and five hurry back.

audio.A: The same eight-note phrase with rubato — note three held longer, notes four and five quicker, landing back on the beat by note six

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Rubato. Hear note three linger? Then the hurry back. That's a phrase breathing.

pages.figure: A phrase-arch. Mountain-shape curve over eight notes. Arrow at the peak of the arch labeled 'HERE'. Caption: 'Stretch the note that matters most — the peak, the turning point, a surprising note.'
pages.cleffy: Which note do you stretch? Usually the most important one. The peak of the phrase. The turning point. A note that surprises. Let it sing. Then catch up.

pages.figure: Staff: five notes C D E F G. Note E marked with a wavy bracket — 'stretch'. Metronome 70.
pages.cleffy: Five notes. Stretch the middle one — E. Hold E a beat longer than written. Then hurry F and G to land on time.

pages.figure: Staff: seven notes, arch shape — C E G A G E C. Note A (the peak) marked 'stretch'. Metronome 70.
pages.cleffy: Climb to the peak. Let A linger. Then roll back down, a little faster. You'll land on the last C exactly on the beat.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top staff: right-hand melody with a stretched note. Bottom staff: left-hand accompaniment, steady as a clock. Arrow labeled 'LH KEEPS TIME'.
pages.cleffy: Real rubato trick — the left hand keeps steady. Right hand floats. Left hand anchors. Only later, the hands drift together. But not yet.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: RIGHT HAND WITH RUBATO, LEFT HAND STEADY.

audio.A: A melody with stretched notes in the right hand, while a steady left hand plays a quarter-note bass line at 70 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen. Bass is a metronome. Melody dances around it. That's the Chopin sound. Coming soon.

pages.figure: Staff: eight-note phrase. C D E G E D C G. Arch shape. Stretch bracket over the G at the peak. Metronome 70.
pages.cleffy: Eight-note phrase. Rubato on the peak G. Make it sing. Hurry back. Land on the final G.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up a hand. Caption: 'A little goes a long way. Rubato every note = nothing means anything.'
pages.cleffy: Warning. A little rubato is expressive. Too much is seasick. One stretched note per phrase. Maybe two. Save it for what matters.

pages.figure: Staff: longer phrase, twelve notes. Shape rises then falls. Stretch brackets on two notes — one at the peak, one at the turn before the end.
pages.cleffy: Twelve notes. Two stretches. One at the peak. One just before the last note. Let those two sing. The others carry the line.
pages.followup_cleffy: Phrase breathing. That's what we're after.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Strict time or rubato?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen — strict or stretched?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 144. A rubber band stretched over a metronome. Caption: BORROW. PAY BACK.
pages.cleffy: Rubato. Borrow time. Pay it back. The sound of a living pianist, not a machine. Tomorrow — Chopin. Act boss. Rubato goes to work.

real_piano: Take any simple scale. Play it at 70 bpm. Pick one note — usually the top. Hold it a beat longer. Speed the rest to land on time.
Try the two-hand trick. Play a steady quarter-note bass in the left hand. Improvise a right-hand melody over it, stretching one note per phrase. Listen to them drift and re-align.

questions.figure: Italian word: rubato
questions.prompt: What does rubato mean?

questions.figure: Phrase with a stretched note marked
questions.prompt: After the stretched note, what should happen?
options: Hurry the next notes to land on the beat

questions.figure: A piece marked 'adagio, poco rubato' at the top
questions.prompt: How often should rubato happen?
options: Every phrase — a little, in the right place

questions.audio: A phrase with one stretched note and a hurried return to the beat
questions.prompt: Strict time or rubato?
Play a five-note scale at 70 bpm. Stretch the top note. Hurry the last two.
questions.accept: 5 notes with a clear stretch on note 3, hurried notes 4 and 5

remediation.title: Rubato, one stretch at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rubato is feel, not math. That makes it slippery. We'll start with one stretched note in a three-note phrase. Small, clear. Then build up.

completion.cleffy: Rubato — yours. Tomorrow — Chopin. The Nocturne. Where rubato was born.

title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 (phrase)
subtitle: Act 29 · Chopin's breath
goal: Play the first phrase of Chopin's Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 — very slowly, legato, with rubato on the singing notes, right hand over a simple waltz-bass left hand.
piece_context: Act 29 boss. The moment Tier 4 stops being technique and starts being music. Chopin wrote this Nocturne at 20. It's in E-flat major — three flats — a warm, candle-lit key. Today, one phrase. Right hand sings. Left hand rocks in a gentle oom-pah-pah. Very slow. Very legato. Small rubato on the notes that matter.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major
piece.difficulty: intermediate — one phrase only, simplified LH, very slow tempo

pages.figure: Cleffy at a candlelit piano. A Chopin portrait on the wall. Banner: ACT 29 BOSS — NOCTURNE OP. 9 NO. 2.
pages.cleffy: Act 29 boss. Chopin. 1832. Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2. He was 20 when he wrote this. Still one of the most played pieces in the world.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left: 'what today is NOT' — the whole nocturne, full tempo, ornaments, all of it. Right: 'what today IS' — the first phrase of the melody, very slow, simplified left hand, with legato and rubato.
pages.cleffy: What today IS: the first phrase. Right-hand melody. Simple waltz-like left hand. What today IS: legato. Singing tone. A little rubato. What today IS NOT: the full piece. That takes Tier 5. Today — one perfect phrase.

pages.figure: Treble and bass staff. Three flats at the start — B-flat, E-flat, A-flat. Banner: E-FLAT MAJOR.
pages.cleffy: E-flat major. Three flats. You already know this key from lesson 127. Warm. Round. The key of sleep and moonlight.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Chopin Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2, opening, real recording.

audio.A: Chopin Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major — opening phrase, slow tempo, a famous recording with legato melody and rocking left hand

pages.cleffy: Listen. The melody floats. The left hand rocks. Breath in every note. No hurry.

pages.figure: Treble staff in E-flat major. Nine notes marked: Bb4, G4, Bb4, Ab4, G4, Ab4, Bb4, C5, Bb4. Slur arched over all of them.
pages.cleffy: The melody. Nine notes. B-flat, G, B-flat — first three, feel the rise. A-flat, G, A-flat — gentle step down, step up. B-flat, C, B-flat — the peak, and home. One big slur. One breath.

pages.figure: Keyboard in E-flat major. The nine melody notes highlighted in order: Bb4, G4, Bb4, Ab4, G4, Ab4, Bb4, C5, Bb4.
pages.cleffy: Notes first. Even quarters. No rhythm yet. B-flat, G, B-flat, A-flat, G, A-flat, B-flat, C, B-flat.

pages.figure: Bass staff in E-flat major. A slow waltz pattern — bass note on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3. Labels: Eb3 (root), then Bb3-G4 (chord), then Bb3-G4 (chord). Oom-pah-pah.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. Simpler than the real one. Low E-flat on beat one — the oom. Two chords above — pah, pah. Oom, pah, pah. Rocking like a cradle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb3, then Bb3-G4 together, twice. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Slow. Low note. Chord. Chord. Keep the low note soft. The chords softer.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top: detached notes — each with space between them. Label: DETACHED. Bottom: slurred line — each note overlapping the next. Label: LEGATO.
pages.cleffy: Before we play the melody — legato. Every note connects to the next. No gaps. Stay on the key until the next one presses. Then lift. Chopin is legato almost always.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same nine melody notes. Slur arch shown. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. Legato. Hear each note melt into the next. Very slow. Sixty on the click.

pages.figure: The nine-note melody with stretch brackets over two notes — the C5 at the top of the phrase, and the final Bb4. Arrows labeled 'SING HERE' and 'LAND HERE'.
pages.cleffy: Rubato. Two places. The C — the peak of the phrase. Let it sing. The final B-flat — come home slowly. Land it. Everywhere else, keep moving.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Nine notes. Stretch brackets over C5 and final Bb4. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Right hand with rubato. Everything flows — but the C lingers. The last B-flat arrives like a sigh.

pages.figure: Grand staff in E-flat. Right hand: the nine-note melody. Left hand: simplified oom-pah-pah under each three-note group. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Very slow — 56. Left hand keeps steady. Right hand floats on top. If the hands drift apart a moment — good. That's rubato working.

pages.figure: Same grand staff. Guides off. Stretch brackets over the C and the final Bb. Dynamic marking: p at the start.
pages.cleffy: Guides off. Read the page. Piano — soft throughout. Let the C lift out. No rush. This phrase is a sentence. Say it slow.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. One phrase of Chopin. Breath, legato, rubato — all of it, in your hands.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 29 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 29 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding a candlestick. A pyramid of acts showing 29/50 complete. A small Chopin portrait tucked in the corner.
pages.cleffy: Act 29. Closed. You just played Chopin. One phrase, simplified, slow — but real Chopin, with legato and rubato and a left hand that rocked like a cradle. Dynamics from pp to ff. Sudden changes. Tempo words. Rubato. All yours now. You are becoming a pianist. That sentence is earned. Next act — scale recognition. Arpeggios. The Alberti bass. A different Chopin waltz ahead.

real_piano: The nine-note melody every day. Right hand only, legato, slow. Say each note out loud as you play it — B-flat, G, B-flat, A-flat, G, A-flat, B-flat, C, B-flat.
Add the left hand oom-pah-pah. Start at 50 bpm. Build up only when it stays smooth.
Find a recording of the full Nocturne. Listen once through with your eyes closed. Notice where the pianist stretches time. That's rubato, in the wild.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 13, 14 — right hand alone, then both hands guided, then unguided

questions.figure: Key signature with three flats
questions.prompt: What key is this Nocturne in?

questions.figure: Nine-note melody: Bb4 G4 Bb4 Ab4 G4 Ab4 Bb4 C5 Bb4
questions.prompt: Which note is the peak of the phrase?

questions.figure: A left-hand bass-chord-chord pattern
questions.prompt: What's this pattern called?

questions.audio: The opening phrase played legato, with rubato on the peak
questions.prompt: Is this legato or staccato?

questions.audio: A passage with one stretched note and hurried notes after
questions.prompt: What is the pianist doing?
Play the nine-note right-hand melody — Bb4, G4, Bb4, Ab4, G4, Ab4, Bb4, C5, Bb4 — legato, at 60 bpm.
questions.accept: All 9 notes correct, connected, at a slow steady tempo

remediation.title: Nocturne, gentler
remediation.opening_cleffy: This is the biggest act boss yet. Expression, legato, rubato, hands together — all at once. We'll go back to the right hand alone, very slow. Three notes at a time. Then the left hand alone. Then both. No rush. Chopin waited for you.

completion.cleffy: Act 29 sealed. You played Chopin. Dynamics, tempo, rubato — all in one phrase. This is the place Tier 4 turned into music. Remember today.

title: Scale recognition
subtitle: Act 30 · Pattern recognition
goal: See a stepwise run as one chunk — 'ascending scale from C' — not five separate notes.
piece_context: Advanced reading isn't faster note-naming. It's chunking. When your eyes see C-D-E-F-G, they should register one thing — a scale — not five things. Today we train that shift.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a staff with C D E F G highlighted as a single connected shape. Banner: ACT 30 — PATTERN RECOGNITION.
pages.cleffy: Act 30. New skill — chunking. Stop reading every note. Start reading shapes.

pages.figure: Two panels. Left: five notes with labels C-D-E-F-G called out individually. Right: the same five notes circled as one block labeled 'ascending scale from C'.
pages.cleffy: Left — the slow way. Five notes. Five names. Right — the fast way. One shape. One name. Scale. Starts on C. Goes up five.

pages.figure: Staff. Five stepwise notes climbing. Arrows between each pair labeled 'step, step, step, step'.
pages.cleffy: A scale run — steps in a row. Line, space, line, space. Or space, line, space, line. Never skips. Never leaps. Always adjacent.

pages.figure: Two staves side by side. A: C D E F G rising. B: G F E D C falling.
pages.cleffy: Two directions. Ascending — climbing. Descending — falling. Same notes. Opposite shape.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: ascending. B: descending.

audio.A: C major five-finger scale ascending — C D E F G
audio.B: C major five-finger scale descending — G F E D C

pages.cleffy: Listen to the shape. Up one sounds like a climb. Down one sounds like a fall. Your ear already knows.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 lighting in order.
pages.cleffy: Play an ascending scale run. C, D, E, F, G. Don't name each one. Feel the shape — one move upward.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 F4 E4 D4 C4 lighting in order.
pages.cleffy: Descending. G, F, E, D, C. One move. One shape. Downward.

pages.figure: Staff with a longer melody: C D E F G then a leap to C5 then D5. The first five notes boxed and labeled 'SCALE'.
pages.cleffy: Here's a phrase. First five notes — scale. One chunk. Then a leap. Then two more notes. Read the chunk first. Then the leap. Then the two.

pages.figure: Three staves. A: D E F# G A. B: G A B C D. C: F G A Bb C. Each labeled as an ascending five-note scale from its starting note.
pages.cleffy: Scales don't only start on C. Any note can start one. Still a scale. Still one chunk. The shape is what you recognize — not the notes.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 A4 B4 C5 D5 lighting.
pages.cleffy: Scale from G. Five notes up. G, A, B, C, D.

pages.figure: Staff. Eight notes ascending: C D E F G A B C. Labeled 'full scale'.
pages.cleffy: Eight notes. A full scale from C to C. Still one chunk. Still one shape. Just longer.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Scale or not?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. You hear a short run. Was it a scale — all steps in a row — or not?

pages.figure: Staff. Eight notes descending: C B A G F E D C. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Full descending scale. C down to C. One motion. One shape.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 146. Five stepwise notes circled as one shape, labeled 'one chunk'.
pages.cleffy: Scales. Now one thing, not many. Tomorrow — arpeggios. Same skill, different shape.

real_piano: Play a five-note scale up and down from any starting note. Out loud, say 'scale' as you play — not each letter.
Open any sheet music you have. Find one run of stepwise notes. Circle it in your head as one chunk.

questions.figure: Staff showing five stepwise ascending notes
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.figure: Staff showing C E G — three notes skipping
questions.prompt: Is this a scale run?
options: No — those are skips, not steps

questions.figure: Staff showing G A B C D ascending
questions.prompt: Describe this as one chunk.

questions.audio: A five-note ascending scale
questions.prompt: Scale or not?
Play an ascending scale from C — C, D, E, F, G.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 in order

remediation.title: Scale shapes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Chunking is a real shift — from naming to shape-seeing. We'll spend more time on the visual pattern. Just the shape. No names yet.

completion.cleffy: Scales — chunked. Tomorrow — arpeggios get the same treatment.

title: Arpeggio recognition
subtitle: Act 30 · Pattern recognition
goal: See a rolled triad — C-E-G-C — as one chunk, not four separate notes.
piece_context: Yesterday — scales as chunks. Today — arpeggios. When you see C-E-G-C, your eyes should read 'C major arpeggio, ascending' — one thing, not four.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a staff showing C4 E4 G4 C5 stacked visually like a ladder. Banner: ARPEGGIOS.
pages.cleffy: Arpeggios. Triad. Rolled. One note at a time. See four notes. Read one shape.

pages.figure: Two panels. Left: a C major triad as a block — C-E-G stacked. Right: the same notes rolled out in time — C E G C.
pages.cleffy: Take a triad. Stretch it across time. Block becomes line. That's an arpeggio.

pages.figure: Staff. Four notes: C4 E4 G4 C5. Each arrow between labeled 'skip, skip, skip'. Callout: 'ARPEGGIO = all skips, same chord'.
pages.cleffy: The shape — all skips. No steps. Line to line to line. Or space to space to space. That's how your eye spots one.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top: C D E F G labeled 'scale — steps'. Bottom: C E G C labeled 'arpeggio — skips'.
pages.cleffy: Scale — steps. Arpeggio — skips. Same page, opposite rule.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: C major block chord. B: C major arpeggio — C E G C ascending.

audio.A: C major triad played as a block
audio.B: C major arpeggio — C E G C played in sequence

pages.cleffy: Listen. Same chord. Two ways. First — block. Second — arpeggio. Rolled chord. Broken chord. Arpeggio. All the same word.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 E4 G4 C5 lighting in order.
pages.cleffy: Play a C major arpeggio. C, E, G, C. Don't name them. Feel the shape — one upward roll.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5 G4 E4 C4 lighting in order.
pages.cleffy: Now down. C, G, E, C. Same shape. Opposite direction.

pages.figure: Three staves. A: F A C F labeled 'F major arpeggio'. B: G B D G labeled 'G major arpeggio'. C: A C E A labeled 'A minor arpeggio'.
pages.cleffy: Arpeggios start anywhere. Root, third, fifth, octave. That's the recipe. The chord's name is the arpeggio's name.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F3 A3 C4 F4 lighting.
pages.cleffy: F major arpeggio. F, A, C, F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G3 B3 D4 G4 lighting.
pages.cleffy: G major arpeggio. G, B, D, G.

pages.figure: Staff. A phrase: C E G C — D C B A. First four notes boxed as 'ARPEGGIO'. Last four as 'scale down'.
pages.cleffy: A phrase in the wild. First four — arpeggio. Last four — scale. Two chunks. Not eight notes.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner — 'Scale or arpeggio?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen. Scale — steps — or arpeggio — skips?

pages.figure: Staff. C E G C G E C — full up-and-down arpeggio.
pages.cleffy: One arpeggio, up and down. Seven notes. One shape.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 147. An arpeggio curve rising across the staff, labeled 'one chunk'.
pages.cleffy: Arpeggios — chunked. Tomorrow — a specific LH pattern every classical player knows. Alberti bass.

real_piano: Play C, F, and G major arpeggios — up and down, slow. Say 'arpeggio' as you play, not each letter.
Spot an arpeggio in any music you have. The tell — all skips, same chord.

questions.figure: Staff showing C E G C ascending
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.figure: Staff showing F A C F ascending
questions.prompt: Describe this as one chunk.

questions.figure: Two staves — one with stepwise notes, one with all-skip notes
questions.prompt: Which is the arpeggio?

questions.audio: A C major arpeggio — C E G C
questions.prompt: Scale or arpeggio?
Play a C major arpeggio — C, E, G, C.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 C5 in order

remediation.title: Arpeggio shapes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The shape matters more than the names. We'll drill scale-vs-arpeggio eyes until the difference is instant.

completion.cleffy: Arpeggios — chunked. Tomorrow — Alberti bass. A hand-pattern you've heard a thousand times.

title: Alberti bass
subtitle: Act 30 · Accompaniment patterns
goal: Play the Alberti pattern — bass, top, middle, top — in the left hand, under a still right hand.
piece_context: Mozart wrote pages of it. Haydn, Clementi, every classical sonata has some. The Alberti bass is a specific rolling pattern — not root-third-fifth-third, but lowest-highest-middle-highest. Once your hand knows it, entire pieces unlock.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a small grand piano. LH glowing with a repeating rolling motion. Banner: ALBERTI BASS.
pages.cleffy: Mozart. Haydn. Early Beethoven. They all used the same left-hand pattern. Alberti bass. Named after the guy who wrote a lot of it.

pages.figure: Staff. LH playing the I chord as four quarters: C E G E. Next to it — C3, G3, E3, G3 (Alberti order).
pages.cleffy: Broken chord, you know. Root-third-fifth-third. C E G E. Alberti is different. Bass, top, middle, top. C, G, E, G.

pages.figure: LH staff. Arrows labeled: 1 — lowest (bass). 2 — highest (top). 3 — middle. 4 — highest (top). Repeated.
pages.cleffy: The hand move — down, up, middle, up. Pinky, thumb, middle, thumb. Four beats. Four fingers. One shape.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: Alberti bass, I chord, two bars.

audio.A: Alberti bass pattern on C major — C3 G3 E3 G3, repeating for two bars

pages.cleffy: Listen. That rolling, rocking feel. Every classical pianist knows this sound. It's the engine under thousands of tunes.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. C3 G3 E3 G3 highlighted in order. Finger numbers: 5, 1, 3, 1.
pages.cleffy: LH alone. Slow. C, G, E, G. Pinky, thumb, middle, thumb.

pages.figure: LH staff. Alberti on I, two full bars. Tempo 65.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Same pattern both times. Let the hand find the groove.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. F3 C4 A3 C4. Finger numbers: 5, 1, 3, 1.
pages.cleffy: Now on IV. Same pattern, new chord. F, C, A, C. Bass, top, middle, top.

pages.figure: Keyboard, LH. G3 D4 B3 D4.
pages.cleffy: V. G, D, B, D.

pages.figure: LH staff. Four bars — I, IV, V, I. Each an Alberti pattern. Tempo 65.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. I, IV, V, I. LH only. Alberti every bar. New chord on every beat 1.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: whole note C. LH: one bar of Alberti on I.
pages.cleffy: Add the right hand. RH holds one long note. LH keeps rolling underneath. Two hands doing completely different things.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH whole C5. LH Alberti on I. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: RH presses C and holds. LH rolls. C, G, E, G.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: C D E C across one bar. LH: Alberti I. Two bars total.
pages.cleffy: RH gets a tiny melody. C, D, E, C. LH keeps rolling. Don't let it stop.
pages.followup_cleffy: That sound. That's Mozart's left hand. Three hundred years of it.

pages.figure: Same two-bar phrase. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: Same bar. No highlights. Trust the pattern.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 148. Four arrows showing the Alberti shape: down, up, middle, up.
pages.cleffy: Alberti in your left hand. Bass, top, middle, top. Tomorrow — a different pattern. Three beats. Oom-pah-pah.

real_piano: LH Alberti on I, IV, V, I. Slow. Every day. Let the pattern become automatic.
Pick any melody you know in C. Play it RH while LH rolls Alberti underneath. Twinkle works.

questions.figure: LH staff with four quarter notes: C3 G3 E3 G3
questions.prompt: What pattern is this?

questions.figure: LH staff with four quarter notes: F3 C4 A3 C4
questions.prompt: Which chord is this Alberti pattern on?

questions.figure: Two LH staves — one broken (C E G E), one Alberti (C G E G)
questions.prompt: Which is Alberti?

questions.audio: Alberti bass rolling on C major — C3 G3 E3 G3
questions.prompt: Is this Alberti bass?
Play one bar of Alberti bass on I — C, G, E, G — as four quarter notes.
questions.accept: C3 G3 E3 G3 in order

remediation.title: Alberti, one finger at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four-finger pattern is a lot to ask all at once. We'll do pinky-thumb first. Then add middle. Then the full loop.

completion.cleffy: Alberti bass — a pattern your hand now owns. Tomorrow — oom-pah-pah. The waltz.

title: Waltz bass
subtitle: Act 30 · Accompaniment patterns
goal: Play the waltz LH — bass note on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3 — in 3/4 time.
piece_context: Every waltz you've ever heard uses the same left-hand shape. Bass note — thump. Chord — tap. Chord — tap. Oom-pah-pah. Three beats. Chopin, Strauss, Tchaikovsky, all of it.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a small ballroom behind him. Couples frozen mid-turn. Banner: WALTZ BASS.
pages.cleffy: Every waltz — same left hand. Thump. Tap. Tap. Oom. Pah. Pah.

pages.figure: LH staff in 3/4. Bar 1: low C (quarter), then E3-G3 chord (quarter), then E3-G3 chord (quarter). Labels above: OOM PAH PAH.
pages.cleffy: Three beats per bar. Beat 1 — bass note. Low. Strong. Beats 2 and 3 — chord. Higher. Soft.

pages.figure: Time signature 3/4 with the numbers highlighted. Three beats counted below: 1, 2, 3.
pages.cleffy: 3/4 time. Three beats per bar. You know this. Waltzes live here. Always.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: Waltz bass on I, two bars.

audio.A: Waltz bass on C major — bass C2, chord C3-E3-G3, chord C3-E3-G3, two bars

pages.cleffy: Listen. Low note. Higher chord. Higher chord. Low note. Higher chord. Higher chord. Your feet already want to move.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Low C2 glowing. Finger 5 labeled.
pages.cleffy: Start with the thump alone. Low C. Pinky. One hit, held for a beat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3-E3-G3 chord glowing. Thumb, middle, pointer.
pages.cleffy: Now the chord. C, E, G — together. One tap.

pages.figure: LH staff. One bar in 3/4. Bass C2, chord C3-E3-G3, chord C3-E3-G3.
pages.cleffy: Full bar. Bass. Chord. Chord. Oom. Pah. Pah.

pages.figure: LH staff. Four bars of waltz on I. Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. Same chord. Don't stop between bars. Let the beat carry.

pages.figure: LH staff. Bar 1: C2 + C-E-G chords. Bar 2: G2 + G-B-D chords. Labeled 'I / V'.
pages.cleffy: The chord changes — new bass, new chord. Bar 1 — I. Bass on C, chord C-E-G. Bar 2 — V. Bass on G, chord G-B-D.

pages.figure: LH staff. Two bars. I then V. Tempo 75.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. I, then V. Waltz pattern.

pages.figure: LH staff. Four bars in 3/4: I, IV, V, I. Each with waltz pattern.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. I, IV, V, I. Bass changes every bar. LH only. Slow.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: E5 D5 C5 across three beats (one per beat). LH: waltz on I.
pages.cleffy: RH melody on top. Three notes. E, D, C. LH keeps oom-pah-pah underneath. Don't let it drop.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's a waltz. Right hand sings. Left hand dances.

pages.figure: Same bar. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: Again. No highlights.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 149. 'OOM PAH PAH' written across three beats, with a couple waltzing below.
pages.cleffy: Waltz bass. Oom. Pah. Pah. Tomorrow — act boss. Chopin. A real waltz, with this pattern underneath.

real_piano: Play the waltz bass on I, IV, V, I — every day. Say oom-pah-pah out loud. Keep the beat steady.
Pick any melody in 3/4 you know — Happy Birthday works. Add a waltz bass underneath.

questions.figure: LH staff in 3/4 — bass note on beat 1, chord on beats 2 and 3
questions.prompt: What pattern is this?

questions.figure: Time signature showing 3/4
questions.prompt: How many beats per bar in a waltz?

questions.figure: LH staff with bass C2 then chord C-E-G twice
questions.prompt: Which chord is this waltz bar on?

questions.audio: Waltz bass on C — low C, chord, chord, repeating
questions.prompt: Is this a waltz bass?
Play one bar of waltz bass on I — low C, then C-E-G chord twice.
questions.accept: C2, then C3-E3-G3 chord, then C3-E3-G3 chord

remediation.title: Oom-pah-pah, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Big jump from low bass to chord — takes practice. We'll break it: just the bass, just the chord, then the handoff.

completion.cleffy: Waltz bass — yours. Tomorrow — Chopin. Waltz in A minor. The real thing, simplified.

title: Chopin Waltz in A minor
subtitle: Act 30 · Pattern recognition + accompaniment
goal: Play a simplified opening of Chopin's A minor Waltz — waltz bass in the LH under a descending melancholy melody in the RH.
piece_context: The act boss. Everything this act taught — scales, arpeggios, Alberti, waltz bass — now under one piece. Chopin's posthumous A minor Waltz (B.150) is famously accessible. Melancholy, slow, in 3/4. Simplified heavily — your job today is not to play Chopin as written. Your job is to feel a real waltz under your hands.

piece.title: Waltz in A minor (posthumous)
piece.difficulty: intermediate — opening only, heavily simplified, slow tempo

pages.figure: Cleffy in an old Parisian salon. Candlelight. A small upright piano. Banner: ACT 30 BOSS — CHOPIN.
pages.cleffy: Act 30 boss. Chopin. Waltz in A minor. Written late in his life. One of the most-played pieces a beginning pianist ever touches.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left: 'what today IS NOT' — the full waltz, at tempo, with ornaments. Right: 'what today IS' — the opening melody with a simplified waltz bass, slow.
pages.cleffy: Honest up front. You're not playing Chopin's waltz as written. That piece has ornaments, rubato, three sections. You're playing eight bars. Simplified. Slow. Waltz bass underneath. Still — this is Chopin. In your hands.

pages.figure: Staff showing A minor key signature (no sharps or flats — same as C major). Note the tonic is A.
pages.cleffy: A minor. Same signature as C major — no sharps, no flats. Different home note. A, not C. That's why it sounds sad and C major doesn't.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Chopin, Waltz in A minor, opening phrase — real recording.

audio.A: Chopin Waltz in A minor (B.150) — first 8 bars, real piano recording, moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: Listen. The real thing. Hear the waltz — oom-pah-pah — underneath? Hear the melody falling? That's why it hurts.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: simplified version — what you'll play.

audio.A: Simplified Chopin A minor Waltz — RH E5 D5 C5 B4 A4 descending, LH waltz bass on A minor, slow tempo

pages.cleffy: Now the version you'll play. Slower. Stripped down. RH melody — five notes falling. E, D, C, B, A. LH waltz bass — A minor chord, oom-pah-pah.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Five notes descending: E5, D5, C5, B4, A4. Each a half note in a 3/4 bar with a rest — so one note per bar lasting most of the bar.
pages.cleffy: RH first. Five notes. All steps down. E, D, C, B, A. Each note sits on beat 1 and holds.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E5 D5 C5 B4 A4 lighting in order. Treble staff above.
pages.cleffy: RH alone. Five notes. Slow. Each one — press, hold, next.

pages.figure: LH staff in 3/4. Bar 1: A2 (bass), then A3-C4-E4 (chord), then A3-C4-E4 (chord). Label: A MINOR WALTZ BASS.
pages.cleffy: LH. Waltz bass, A minor version. Bass A — low. Then A minor chord — A, C, E — twice. Same oom-pah-pah. Different chord than yesterday.

pages.figure: LH staff. Keyboard showing A2, then A3-C4-E4.
pages.cleffy: LH alone. One bar. A minor waltz. Bass. Chord. Chord.

pages.figure: LH staff. Three bars of A minor waltz bass. Tempo 75.
pages.cleffy: Three bars. Same chord every bar. Just the waltz. Keep it steady.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1. RH: E5 held. LH: A2, A3-C4-E4, A3-C4-E4.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. One bar. RH presses E on beat 1 and holds. LH plays the full waltz under it.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Five bars. RH: E D C B A — one note per bar. LH: A minor waltz bass each bar. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Five full bars. The simplified Chopin. RH — a new melody note each bar 1. LH — never stops rolling.
pages.followup_cleffy: Chopin. In your hands. Simplified. Slow. Real.

pages.figure: Same five bars. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: No highlights. Read the staff. Don't rush. Chopin is slow music.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 30 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 30 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy in a Parisian salon with a copy of the Chopin waltz. Pyramid showing 30/50 acts complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 30. Closed. Scales as chunks. Arpeggios as chunks. Alberti bass. Waltz bass. Then — Chopin. A real piece, simplified, actually under your hands. That's not nothing. That is real music. Next act — hand independence. Two brains, one body.

real_piano: The five-bar Chopin melody every day. RH first, LH second, then both. Slow metronome — 55 to 60.
Search 'Chopin Waltz in A minor posthumous' online. Listen to any recording. You now understand the shape — RH falls, LH waltzes.
Once comfortable, try to extend: add bars 6-8 of the original if you can find a simplified score. Optional.

questions.figure: LH staff in 3/4 — A2 bass, then A3-C4-E4 chord twice
questions.prompt: What pattern is this?

questions.figure: Treble staff showing E5 D5 C5 B4 A4 descending, one note per bar
questions.prompt: Describe the shape of this melody.

questions.figure: Key signature with no sharps and no flats, tonic noted as A
questions.prompt: What key is this?

questions.audio: The simplified A minor waltz — RH descending melody, LH oom-pah-pah
questions.prompt: What left-hand pattern do you hear?

questions.audio: A major waltz and an A minor waltz, back to back
questions.prompt: Which one is the minor (sad) one?
Play one bar of the simplified Chopin — RH E5 held, LH A2 then A3-C4-E4 chord twice.
questions.accept: RH E5 sustained over LH A2, A3-C4-E4 chord, A3-C4-E4 chord

remediation.title: Chopin, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Two hands, minor key, real piece — a lot at once. We'll build it one bar at a time. RH alone. LH alone. Then one bar together. Then two. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 30 closed. Chopin — your first. You now see scales and arpeggios as shapes. You have Alberti and waltz bass in your left hand. And you played Chopin. Simplified, slow, real. Next — hand independence. The hands get their own brains.

title: Hand independence
subtitle: Act 31 · Two brains, one body
goal: Play the right hand and left hand in rhythmically different patterns — RH quarters over LH halves.
piece_context: You've done two-hand coordination where the hands trade beats. Now the next layer — hands playing at different speeds, in different rhythms, at the same time. The default of almost all piano music.

pages.figure: Cleffy with two thought bubbles — one for each hand. Both bubbles have different notes inside.
pages.cleffy: Two hands. Two minds. Your left is counting in halves. Your right is counting in quarters. Same bar. Different jobs.

pages.figure: Grand staff, 4/4. LH: two half notes (C3, C3). RH: four quarter notes (G4, G4, G4, G4). Beats numbered underneath.
pages.cleffy: The picture. Left hand — two half notes. One on beat 1. One on beat 3. Right hand — four quarter notes. One on every beat. LH is slow. RH is twice as fast.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome at 60.

audio.A: LH plays half notes C3 on beats 1 and 3. RH plays quarter notes G4 on beats 1, 2, 3, 4. Both together, slow.

pages.cleffy: Listen. The low note holds. The high note keeps going. Each time RH presses, LH is either starting or still holding.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 lit. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Right hand first. Four quarter notes on G. Steady pulse.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3 lit. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Two half notes on C. Press, hold for two beats. Press again.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Dotted vertical lines between beats. RH quarters lined up with 1, 2, 3, 4. LH half notes anchored on 1 and 3. Arrows showing LH holds through 2 and 4.
pages.cleffy: Here's how they fit. Beat 1 — both hands press together. Beat 2 — RH presses. LH is still holding. Beat 3 — both press together again. Beat 4 — RH presses. LH is still holding. Together, apart, together, apart.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH G4 quarters, LH C3 halves. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. 50 bpm — very slow. Count out loud. One, two, three, four.

pages.figure: Same staff. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Again. Cleaner. LH note should ring through two RH presses.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two speeds, at once.

pages.figure: Two gears turning at different speeds, meshing. Cleffy watching.
pages.cleffy: Why this is hard. Your brain wants both hands to press together every time. Independence means one hand keeps its own clock while the other keeps its own. It comes from slow practice. Not from trying harder.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. Bar 1: LH C3, RH G4 quarters. Bar 2: LH G2, RH D4 quarters. Tempo 55.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Same rhythm. New notes in bar 2. LH moves to G. RH moves to D. Rhythm doesn't change.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars — C, F, G, C progression. RH quarters on the root. LH halves on the root.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. The hands keep their speeds. The notes change. C. F. G. C.

pages.figure: A score snippet — Alberti-ish left hand under a slower right-hand melody. Label: 'the default sound of classical piano.'
pages.cleffy: This idea — hands at different speeds — is the default of classical music. One hand moves. The other holds or moves less. You will meet this pattern in every piece from here on.

pages.figure: Four bars again. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Once more. 60 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 151. Two gears turning at different speeds. Banner: HAND INDEPENDENCE — DAY 1.
pages.cleffy: Two brains, one body. Day one. Tomorrow — LH holds a chord while RH plays a melody. The sound of most piano music you've ever heard.

real_piano: The four-bar pattern every day. 50 bpm, no faster, until the hands feel settled.
Count out loud. One, two, three, four. The voice keeps the clock.
If one hand drags — stop and play it alone for ten seconds, then try both again.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7-8 until the slow version feels steady.

questions.figure: Grand staff — LH half notes, RH quarter notes
questions.prompt: How many RH notes for every LH note?

questions.figure: Beats 1, 2, 3, 4 numbered under a bar
questions.prompt: On which beats do both hands press together?

questions.figure: Two gears turning at different speeds
questions.prompt: What is hand independence?
options: Each hand keeping its own clock

questions.audio: RH quarters over LH halves at 60 bpm
questions.prompt: Are the two hands moving at the same speed?
Play one bar — LH C3 half notes, RH G4 quarter notes, both together.
questions.accept: LH halves on beats 1 and 3, RH quarters on beats 1-2-3-4

remediation.title: Hands apart, then together, very slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: Independence is a wall. Expected. We'll play each hand alone at 40 bpm, then glue them together one beat at a time.

completion.cleffy: Hands at different speeds. That was today. Tomorrow — LH holds a chord, RH plays a melody above it.

title: LH chords + RH melody
subtitle: Act 31 · The default texture
goal: Play a melody in the right hand while the left hand holds a chord underneath — the sound of most classical piano.
piece_context: You saw this in Lesson 98 — blocked accompaniment. Now you're ready for it as real independence. LH holds. RH sings.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a piano. LH on a held chord (drawn as a solid block). RH on a moving melody line above.
pages.cleffy: LH holds a chord. RH sings a melody. One hand stays still. The other moves. This is the sound of most piano music you've ever heard.

pages.figure: Grand staff, 4/4. Bar 1: LH whole-note chord C-E-G. RH melody C5-D5-E5-D5 (quarter notes). Bar 2: same LH. Melody continues C5-B4-C5-rest.
pages.cleffy: Here's the picture. LH — a whole-note chord. C, E, G. Held for the full bar. RH — a melody. Four quarter notes across the top. One hand: held. Other hand: moving.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome 60.

audio.A: LH C-E-G chord held as a whole note. RH melody C5-D5-E5-D5 on beats 1-2-3-4, slow.

pages.cleffy: Listen. The chord rings under. The melody walks on top. Hear how the chord never stops sounding?

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 lit. (LH plays C3, E3, G3 — adjust to LH.) LH chord block.
pages.cleffy: LH alone. Hold the C chord. Four beats. Don't let go.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5, D5, E5 lit. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: RH alone. Four notes. C, D, E, D.

pages.figure: Grand staff. One bar. LH whole-note chord drawn as a tall block. RH quarters drawn as arrows moving across the top.
pages.cleffy: Here's the trick. LH presses once at beat 1 — and stays down. RH presses four times while LH is holding. Your left hand will want to move with the right. It must not.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH C-E-G whole note. RH C5-D5-E5-D5 quarters. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. 50 bpm. LH presses once. RH sings four notes while it holds.

pages.figure: Same staff. 50 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Again. Keep LH pressed. Let it ring under the melody.
pages.followup_cleffy: That sound. That's classical piano in three seconds.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. Bar 1: LH C-E-G chord, RH C5-D5-E5-D5. Bar 2: LH G-B-D chord, RH G4-A4-B4-A4.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. The chord changes in bar 2. LH moves to G chord. RH starts a new melody line on G. Both hands find a new place at the same moment — beat 1 of bar 2.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. C chord, then G chord. 55 bpm.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars — C, F, G, C. LH chord, RH melody in each.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. C. F. G. C. The melody traces each chord gently.

pages.figure: Three tiny score snippets — a hymn, a Mozart sonata, a Chopin nocturne. Each labeled with 'LH chord, RH melody'.
pages.cleffy: LH chord, RH melody. Hymns. Mozart sonatas. Chopin nocturnes. Different notes. Same idea.

pages.figure: Four bars again. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Once more. 60 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 152. LH hand resting on a chord. RH hand drawn mid-melody above.
pages.cleffy: Chord under. Melody over. Tomorrow — reverse. LH plays the tune. RH holds. Less common. Harder for most people. Worth learning.

real_piano: The four-bar progression (C, F, G, C) daily. LH chord, RH melody. 55 bpm.
When the chord changes — move both hands exactly together on beat 1.
Listen for LH ringing under the melody. If LH keeps releasing early, hold it longer on purpose.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7-8 and page 11 until the chord change feels clean.

questions.figure: Grand staff — LH whole-note chord, RH quarter-note melody
questions.prompt: How many times does the LH press in one bar?

questions.figure: A texture with LH holding a chord while RH plays a tune
questions.prompt: What is this texture called?

questions.figure: Two bars, chord changing from C to G
questions.prompt: When does the LH move to the new chord?

questions.audio: LH chord held under RH quarter melody
questions.prompt: Which hand is moving more?
Play one bar — LH holds C-E-G chord, RH plays C5-D5-E5-D5.
questions.accept: LH C-E-G sustained, RH four quarters in sequence

remediation.title: Hold the chord, walk the melody
remediation.opening_cleffy: The trick is LH staying down while RH moves. We'll practice LH holding a chord for a full bar with no melody at all, then layer RH in one note at a time.

completion.cleffy: Chord under, melody over. You have the default classical texture. Tomorrow — swap the roles.

title: LH melody + RH chords
subtitle: Act 31 · Swap the roles
goal: Play a melody with the left hand while the right hand holds a chord above — the reverse of yesterday.
piece_context: Yesterday — RH melody, LH chord. Standard. Today — swap. LH carries the tune while RH holds above. Rarer in piano music, but essential. Some of the most beautiful moments in Schumann, Chopin, Beethoven happen exactly here.

pages.figure: Cleffy flipping a coin. One side: 'RH melody / LH chord'. Other side: 'LH melody / RH chord'. Today's side is up.
pages.cleffy: Flip yesterday over. LH sings the melody. RH holds the chord above. Your left hand is not used to leading. Today it does.

pages.figure: Grand staff, 4/4. RH: whole-note chord C4-E4-G4. LH: quarter-note melody C3-D3-E3-D3.
pages.cleffy: The reverse picture. RH — whole-note chord, held for the full bar. LH — four quarter notes walking across underneath. The melody is down low now. The chord rings above it.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome 60.

audio.A: RH chord C4-E4-G4 held as a whole note. LH melody C3-D3-E3-D3 on each beat. Slow, warm.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Chord on top. Melody moving underneath. Different feel than yesterday. Deeper. More shadowed.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 lit. RH block chord.
pages.cleffy: RH alone. Hold the C chord. Four beats. Don't let go.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3, D3, E3 lit. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: LH alone. The melody. C, D, E, D. Take your time. This is your left hand leading — it's a new feeling.

pages.figure: Brain diagram. Right hemisphere brighter than left. Caption: 'RH usually leads. Today it doesn't.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the honest bit. Your right hand has led almost every lesson you've played. Your brain expects it. Today you ask the left hand to take the front — and the right to just hold still. It will feel clumsy at first. That's correct.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH C-E-G whole note. LH C3-D3-E3-D3 quarters. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. 50 bpm. RH presses the chord once. LH walks the melody underneath.

pages.figure: Same staff. 50 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Again. Keep RH down. LH is the one doing the work. Listen for its shape.
pages.followup_cleffy: Your left hand just sang.

pages.figure: Same grand staff, but RH chord drawn faded. LH melody line highlighted in gold.
pages.cleffy: A small mental switch. Treat the LH as the voice. Listen to its line. The RH chord is background — like wind behind a singer.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. Bar 1: RH C-E-G chord, LH C3-D3-E3-D3. Bar 2: RH G-B-D chord, LH G2-A2-B2-A2.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. RH chord changes in bar 2. LH melody continues.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars — C, F, G, C. RH chord, LH melody.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. LH carries the tune through all four.

pages.figure: A score snippet — Schumann-style texture. LH melody line highlighted. Label: 'the left hand as tenor voice.'
pages.cleffy: Real music does this all the time. Schumann loves it. Chopin uses it for cello-like sighs. When the melody drops to the left hand, the piece changes color.

pages.figure: Four bars. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: One more. 60 bpm.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 153. A coin landed LH-up. Cleffy holding it, smiling faintly.
pages.cleffy: Left hand led. Right hand held. Tomorrow — a drill. Same patterns, three speeds. Slow, moderate, fast.

real_piano: The four-bar progression with LH melody every day. Listen for the tune — it's in the bass now.
Switch texture mid-session. Play yesterday's pattern, then today's back to back. Feel how they differ.
If the LH melody sounds weak or uneven — slow to 40 bpm. The left hand needs more reps.

questions.figure: Grand staff — RH whole-note chord, LH quarter-note melody
questions.prompt: Which hand plays the melody?

questions.figure: Today's texture vs yesterday's, side by side
questions.prompt: What's flipped compared to yesterday?
options: The melody moved from RH to LH

questions.figure: LH melody line highlighted under a held RH chord
questions.prompt: What role is the RH playing?

questions.audio: LH melody walking under a held RH chord
questions.prompt: Where is the melody?
Play one bar — RH holds C4-E4-G4 chord, LH plays C3-D3-E3-D3 melody.
questions.accept: RH C-E-G sustained above, LH four quarters moving below

remediation.title: Let the left hand lead, very slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: LH leading is new. Expected that it's shaky. We'll play the LH melody alone, loud and proud, until it feels like a real tune. Then layer RH on.

completion.cleffy: The left hand led today. That's a real unlock. Tomorrow — a drill. Same patterns, pushed slow to fast.

title: Coordination drill
subtitle: Act 31 · Slow, moderate, fast
goal: Take the two-hand patterns from this act and push them from slow to fast without losing independence.
piece_context: Three days of two-hand independence. Today — push the tempo. The pattern is the same. Your hands have to keep their separate clocks at 60, 80, and 100 bpm.
