pages.figure: Cleffy at a metronome dial. Three marks on the dial: 60, 80, 100. A finger hovering at 60.
pages.cleffy: Three days of independence. Today — push the speed. 60. 80. 100. Same pattern, faster clocks.

pages.figure: Grand staff, 4/4. LH whole-note chord C-E-G. RH quarter-note melody C5-D5-E5-D5. The same pattern from Lesson 152.
pages.cleffy: The pattern. From Lesson 152. LH holds. RH walks. Nothing new on the page. The work is in the speed.

pages.figure: Three play buttons stacked. 60 bpm / 80 bpm / 100 bpm.

audio.A: Same pattern — LH C chord held, RH C5-D5-E5-D5 — played three times, first at 60, then 80, then 100 bpm.

pages.cleffy: Three tempos of the same bar. Slow. Medium. Fast. Notice — at 100 it still breathes. Speed isn't panic.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Pattern. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: First pass. 60 bpm. Warm up. Keep LH down.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars: C chord, G chord. 60 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. 60 bpm. Chord change in bar 2.

pages.figure: A checklist. Three items: 'LH stays down.' 'Count out loud.' 'Don't tense the shoulders.'
pages.cleffy: Before you push the tempo — three things. Left hand stays down through the bar. Count out loud. If your shoulders climb toward your ears, stop and shake them out.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Pattern. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Now 80. Same pattern.

pages.figure: Same two bars. 80 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Two bars at 80. Chord change included.
pages.followup_cleffy: 80 is moderate — most music lives near here.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a hand up. Two common errors drawn: 1) LH lifting early. 2) Hands locking to the same beat.
pages.cleffy: At 100, two things usually break first. LH releases early — chord stops ringing under the melody. Both hands lock together — independence collapses. Watch for those. Slow back down if either happens.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Pattern. Metronome 100.
pages.cleffy: 100 bpm. Fast, but not scary.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. 100 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Two bars at 100. Keep LH pressed. Keep counting.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH melody C3-D3-E3-D3, RH chord C4-E4-G4 held. Metronome 80. (Yesterday's pattern.)
pages.cleffy: Quick switch. Yesterday's pattern. 80 bpm. LH melody. RH chord.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Back to LH chord / RH melody. Two bars. Tempo 90 (between 80 and 100).
pages.cleffy: One more. 90 bpm. The pocket.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 154. Metronome arm swinging. Three marks glowing: 60, 80, 100. Banner: DRILL COMPLETE.
pages.cleffy: Slow, moderate, fast. Same pattern. Tomorrow — Für Elise. The full piece. You've known the opening for 80 lessons. Tomorrow the rest.

real_piano: Pick one tempo each day. Monday 60. Tuesday 80. Wednesday 100. Circle back the next week.
If 100 falls apart, don't muscle through. Drop to 90. Earn it back.
Do both textures — LH chord / RH melody, and its reverse. Alternate days.

questions.figure: Metronome dial showing 60, 80, 100
questions.prompt: Which tempo is 'moderate' — where most music lives?

questions.figure: Checklist — LH stays down, count out loud, relax shoulders
questions.prompt: Why count out loud during fast practice?

questions.figure: Two common errors at fast tempo
questions.prompt: What breaks first when you push the speed?

questions.audio: Same pattern at 60 and then 100 bpm
questions.prompt: Is the pattern the same at both speeds?
Play one bar of LH chord / RH melody pattern at 80 bpm.
questions.accept: Pattern played cleanly with independence at 80

remediation.title: Inch up, don't leap
remediation.opening_cleffy: Speed collapses independence faster than anything. Normal. We'll move 60 → 65 → 70 → 75 → 80, step by step. No jumps.

completion.cleffy: Slow to fast. Same pattern, three clocks. Tomorrow — Für Elise. Full.

title: Für Elise (full)
subtitle: Act 31 · Your first full Beethoven
goal: Play the full A-B-A-C-A structure of Für Elise — the opening theme you've known for 80 lessons, plus the B and C sections that surround it.
piece_context: Lesson 75 taught you the opening theme. That was Tier 2 boss. For 80 lessons you've carried those notes in your hands — E, D-sharp, E, D-sharp, E. Today: the rest of the piece. The major-key middle (the B section — brighter, hopeful) and the turbulent chromatic middle (the C section — stormy, then it resolves). A-B-A-C-A. Your first full Beethoven piece.

piece.title: Für Elise (full)
piece.key_center: A minor (with F-major B section, A-minor C section)
piece.difficulty: intermediate — full A-B-A-C-A structure, simplified, single-hand melody focus

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a yellowed Für Elise score. Behind him — a pyramid of 155 lessons. The page-1 theme from Lesson 75 highlighted in gold.
pages.cleffy: Eighty lessons ago, you learned five notes. E, D-sharp, E, D-sharp, E. You've carried them this whole time. Today — the rest of the piece.

pages.figure: Diagram. Five boxes in a row: A | B | A | C | A. Labels underneath: 'opening theme' / 'bright middle (F major)' / 'theme returns' / 'stormy middle' / 'theme returns — last time'.
pages.cleffy: The whole shape. Five sections. A — the theme you already know. B — a brighter middle. F major. Hopeful. A again — theme returns. C — a stormy middle. Chromatic, unsettled. A again — theme comes home one last time. A-B-A-C-A. This is called rondo form.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Für Elise, complete, recording.

audio.A: Für Elise, complete — A theme, B section, A return, C section, A final return. At reference tempo.

pages.cleffy: Listen all the way through. Notice where the theme comes back. Notice what's in between. Three returns of A. Two detours in between.

pages.figure: Staff. The opening theme from Lesson 75: E5 D#5 E5 D#5 E5 B4 D5 C5 A4.
pages.cleffy: The A theme. Your old friend. E, D-sharp, E, D-sharp, E, B, D, C, A. Played up an octave today — E5, D#5 — because the piece will roam higher.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E5, D#5, E5, D#5, E5, B4, D5, C5, A4.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Familiar territory.

pages.figure: Staff. B section melody simplified: C5 G4 F5 E5 D5 C5 B4. Key signature showing one flat (F major). Label: 'brighter — the sun comes out.'
pages.cleffy: B section. F major. One flat. Seven notes: C, G, F, E, D, C, B. It climbs up, then walks down. Brighter feel. The sun comes out for a moment.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: B section, simplified, slow.

audio.A: B section melody played simply: C5, G4, F5, E5, D5, C5, B4 — warm, moderate.

pages.cleffy: Listen to it. Warmer than the A theme. No shadow in it. This is Beethoven letting the piece breathe before it darkens again.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5, G4, F5, E5, D5, C5, B4 lit in order.
pages.cleffy: Play the B melody. Seven notes. Slow.

pages.figure: Staff showing the final two notes of B (C5, B4) leading straight into the opening of A (E5, D#5). An arrow connecting them.
pages.cleffy: Transition. B lands on B-natural. Then the A theme starts again on E. Small lift of the hand. Breath. Then the old theme returns.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The B ending + A opening. C5, B4, [breath], E5, D#5, E5.
pages.cleffy: Play them stitched. End of B, into start of A.

pages.figure: Staff. C section simplified — a short chromatic passage: A4 B4 C5 D5 E5 F5 E5 D5. Label: 'the storm — notes moving by half steps.'
pages.cleffy: C section. The stormy one. Chromatic — meaning it moves by half steps. Tense. Unsettled. Simplified here to eight notes: A, B, C, D, E, F, E, D.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A4, B4, C5, D5, E5, F5, E5, D5.
pages.cleffy: Play the C melody. Slow. Let it feel tense.

pages.figure: A roadmap across the page: A (9 notes) → B (7 notes) → A (9 notes) → C (8 notes) → A (9 notes). Arrows linking them. A total count at the bottom.
pages.cleffy: The whole path today. A, then B, back to A, then C, home to A. Forty-two notes, walked end to end.

pages.figure: Full A-B-A-C-A roadmap. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: All five sections. Guides on. 60 bpm. Go slowly. Breathe between sections.

pages.figure: Full structure. No guides. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read. You play. Take the time you need.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. The full Für Elise, yours now.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 31 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 31 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding the full Für Elise score. A gold banner: 'FIRST FULL BEETHOVEN.' The old Lesson 75 theme drawn fading into today's full structure.
pages.cleffy: Act 31. Closed. Eighty lessons ago, five notes. Today, a full Beethoven piece. A, B, A, C, A. You walked the whole rondo. You have your first complete Beethoven. That's a real thing to own. Next act — the sustain pedal. The foot joins the hands.

real_piano: Für Elise full structure once a day. Slow first. Whole week at 60 bpm, no faster.
Play just the B section three times, then just the C section three times. The old A theme is fine — the new sections need the reps.
Play it for one person this week. Tell them it's all of Für Elise, not just the part everybody knows.
virtual_only: Replay pages 14 and 15 — full structure with and without guides.

Play the B section melody: C5, G4, F5, E5, D5, C5, B4.

remediation.title: Für Elise, one section at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. A full piece in one lesson is a lot. We'll take each section on its own — A alone, B alone, C alone — and only stitch them once each feels solid.

completion.cleffy: Act 31 sealed. You have Für Elise — the whole thing. Not just the part everyone knows. The bright middle, the stormy middle, and all three returns of the theme. Your first complete Beethoven piece. A real one. Tomorrow — the sustain pedal. The sound of piano opens up.

title: Sustain pedal intro
subtitle: Act 32 · Sustain pedal
goal: Meet the sustain pedal — what it is, where it is, and how to press it with your foot.
piece_context: New act, new world. For the last 155 lessons you've only used your hands. Today we add your right foot. The pedal changes everything — it lets notes ring after your fingers leave the keys.

pages.figure: Cleffy standing beside a grand piano. Three brass pedals glinting at the bottom. Banner: ACT 32 · SUSTAIN PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: New act. New body part. For 155 lessons, hands only. Today — your right foot joins the band.

pages.figure: Close-up of three piano pedals on a grand piano. Labels: LEFT — soft pedal (una corda). MIDDLE — sostenuto. RIGHT — sustain (damper).
pages.cleffy: There are three pedals. Left one softens. Middle one holds specific notes — rarely used. The right one is the star. The sustain pedal. That's the one.

pages.figure: Cross-section of a piano. Dampers lifted off the strings. Caption: 'Pedal down = dampers lifted = notes ring.'
pages.cleffy: Inside the piano, little felt dampers sit on the strings. Dampers stop notes from ringing after you lift your finger. The sustain pedal lifts all the dampers at once. Every note you played keeps ringing.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: SAME CHORD — WITH AND WITHOUT PEDAL.

audio.A: A C major chord played, fingers released — sound stops abruptly. Then the same chord played with pedal — sound blooms and rings for 4 seconds.

pages.cleffy: Listen. First — no pedal. Chord stops the moment fingers lift. Second — pedal down. Chord blooms. Rings on its own.

pages.figure: Illustration of a right foot on the sustain pedal. Heel on the floor, toe resting on the pedal. Arrow showing the pedal pivoting down from the ball of the foot.
pages.cleffy: How to press it. Heel stays on the floor. Always. Toe rests on the pedal. Press with the ball of the foot. Heel down, toe down. That's the grip.

pages.figure: Two illustrations side by side. Left: foot flat, heel on floor — smooth arc of motion. Right: heel lifted, whole foot floating — shaky arrow.
pages.cleffy: Why heel down? Control. Your foot is a lever now. The heel is the hinge. Heel lifted — no hinge. Foot wobbles. Pedal thumps.

pages.figure: Split screen. Left: real piano with a foot on the pedal. Right: Sonata app on a laptop with a little pedal icon at the bottom of the keyboard.
pages.cleffy: Quick note. If you're on a real piano — press pedal when I say so. If you're on a virtual keyboard — don't worry. The app holds the sound for you when a pedal moment happens. Same lesson, both ways.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Pedal icon at bottom — PRESS ON CUE.
pages.cleffy: First try. Play C. Hold it. Now press the pedal — heel down, toe down. Now lift your finger. The note keeps ringing.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: C HELD BY PEDAL, 5 SECONDS.

audio.A: Single C4 note, finger lifts after half a second, pedal holds the sound ringing gently for 5 seconds, then pedal releases and sound cuts.

pages.cleffy: Listen to what you just did. One finger press. Five seconds of sound. That's the pedal's job.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major triad — C4 E4 G4 — glowing. Pedal icon lit.
pages.cleffy: Now a chord. Play C, E, G together. Pedal down before you lift your hand. Let it ring.

pages.figure: Pedal icon in three states: UP, DOWN, UP. Arrow from down to up labeled 'lift foot cleanly.'
pages.cleffy: The release matters too. Lift your toe — pedal comes up — dampers drop — sound stops. No stomp. No slam. Smooth up, smooth down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Pedal icon cycling: down, up, down, up.
pages.cleffy: Play C. Pedal down. Lift finger — note rings. Pedal up — note stops. Press again. Feel the lever.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major then G major. Pedal guidance: down, up, down, up.
pages.cleffy: Two chords. C major, then G major. Pedal down for C, pedal up before G, pedal down again for G. Don't hold pedal through both. That would mix them — we'll cover that tomorrow.
pages.followup_cleffy: Right foot, doing its first real job.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick questions.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 156. A silhouette of a foot on a pedal. Banner: ACT 32 BEGINS.
pages.cleffy: Sustain pedal. Right foot. Heel down, toe down. Tomorrow — change pedal with each new chord.

real_piano: Sit at the piano. Heel on floor. Foot on sustain pedal. Press and release ten times without playing anything. Feel the lever.
Play any chord you know. Press pedal after. Let it ring. Then release.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8, 10, and 13 — pay attention to the pedal icon cues

questions.figure: Three piano pedals
questions.prompt: Which pedal is the sustain pedal?

questions.figure: Foot on pedal
questions.prompt: Where should your heel be?

questions.figure: Inside of a piano
questions.prompt: What does the sustain pedal actually do?
options: Lifts the dampers so notes keep ringing

questions.audio: Chord played with pedal, then without
questions.prompt: Which version rings longer?
Play C major chord (C4 E4 G4). Hold the pedal so the chord rings after your hand lifts.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 played together, sound sustains after release

remediation.title: Pedal, no notes
remediation.opening_cleffy: Coordinating foot with hands is new. We'll practice the foot alone first. No notes. Just pedal down, pedal up, ten times in a row. Then one note. Then a chord.

completion.cleffy: Right foot in. The pedal is yours. Tomorrow — change pedal every time the harmony changes.

title: Pedal with each chord
subtitle: Act 32 · Sustain pedal
goal: Change the pedal every time a new chord arrives — release as the new harmony presses down.
piece_context: Yesterday — one pedal press, one ring. Today — many pedal changes in a row. One per chord. This is how pedal actually works in real music.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano, one foot on pedal, hands hovering over C and G chord positions. Banner: ONE CHORD, ONE PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday — pedal on, pedal off. Once. Today — change pedal every time the chord changes.

pages.figure: A diagram. Chord 1 with a pedal marking below. Chord 2 arrives — the pedal marking lifts just before and presses again right after. Caption: 'Change on the new chord.'
pages.cleffy: The rule. New chord presses — old pedal releases — new pedal presses. All in a fast motion. Like a little duck dip under the new note.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: C AND G MIXED — NO PEDAL CHANGE.

audio.A: C major chord held with pedal, then G major chord played while pedal still held — ugly dissonant mix of both chords ringing.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Someone held pedal through both chords. C and G are ringing at the same time. Muddy. Thick. Wrong.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: C AND G — WITH PEDAL CHANGE.

audio.A: C major chord with pedal, then G major chord with a clean pedal change — each chord rings cleanly without overlap.

pages.cleffy: Same two chords. Pedal changed between them. C rings clean. G rings clean. No mud. That's the job.

pages.figure: A timeline. Chord 1 at beat 1, pedal down. Chord 2 at beat 2 — pedal lifts at beat 2, then presses again right after. Tiny gap shown.
pages.cleffy: Timing. This part is tricky. Lift the pedal JUST as your finger hits the new chord. Press it back down almost immediately. Tiny gap. The ear barely notices.

pages.figure: Small card. Split — real piano foot + virtual keyboard toggle. Caption: 'Real piano = press the pedal. Virtual = the app handles the overlap cleanup for you.'
pages.cleffy: Quick reminder. Real piano — your foot does the work. Lift and press on every chord change. Virtual — the app knows where chords change and handles the ringing for you. Either way, listen for mud.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major then G major. Pedal guidance arrows: DOWN at chord 1, UP-DOWN at chord 2.
pages.cleffy: Two chords. C, then G. Pedal down with C. At G — lift and press.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, F, G chord positions. Pedal changes marked under each.
pages.cleffy: Three chords now. C, F, G. Three pedal changes. One per chord.

pages.figure: Staff with four chords labeled: C — Am — F — G. Pedal marks under each. Tempo marking: slow.
pages.cleffy: A common four-chord progression. C, A minor, F, G. Every chord gets its own pedal.

pages.figure: Keyboard with the four-chord progression. Pedal icons cycling on each chord.
pages.cleffy: Play the progression. Slow. Pedal change on every new chord. Listen for clean between chords.

pages.figure: Same four chords. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: A little faster. Sixty. The pedal has to move faster too.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. Label: WHICH ONE HAS A MISSED PEDAL CHANGE?

audio.A: Same 4-chord progression cleanly pedaled
audio.B: Same 4-chord progression but pedal is held through chord 3 — F rings into G causing mud

pages.cleffy: Two recordings. One is clean. One has a missed pedal change — a muddy moment. Listen for the mud.

pages.figure: Same four-chord progression. No guide dots. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Your ear checks the pedal now. If you hear mud — pedal stayed down too long.
pages.followup_cleffy: Four chords, four pedals, one clean chain.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick ones.
pages.cleffy: Three to close.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 157. Four chord boxes in a row, each with its own pedal dip under it.
pages.cleffy: One chord, one pedal. That's the default. Tomorrow — train your ears. Pedal on or off? You'll hear the difference.

real_piano: The four-chord progression — C Am F G — every day. Focus on the pedal change, not the chords.
Listen for mud. If two chords overlap, you missed the change. Slow down and try again.
Try the progression C G Am F as well. Different chords, same pedal rule.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 11, 13 — listen for the clean break between chords

questions.figure: A chord progression with pedal marks
questions.prompt: How often should the pedal change?

questions.figure: The pedal-change timing diagram
questions.prompt: When do you lift the pedal?
options: As the new chord presses down
After the new chord has been ringing

questions.figure: Muddy-sounding chord
questions.prompt: Two chords ringing together means:
options: The pedal stayed down too long

questions.audio: C chord into G chord with no pedal change — two chords ringing together
questions.prompt: Does this sound clean?
Play C — F — G chords with a pedal change on each.
questions.accept: C, F, G chords played with clean separation between each chord

remediation.title: Two chords at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four chord changes is a lot of foot work. We'll back up to two chords only — C and G — until the pedal change feels automatic. Then add a third, then a fourth.

completion.cleffy: New chord, new pedal. Rule learned. Tomorrow — ear training. Is the pedal on? Or off? You decide.

title: Pedal hearing
subtitle: Act 32 · Sustain pedal
goal: Train your ear — tell the difference between a passage played with pedal and one played without.
piece_context: The pedal is invisible — no one can see it from the audience. You'll know by sound alone. Today, your ears learn what pedal sounds like.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a finger to his ear. Two waveforms behind him — one short and dry, one long and ringing. Banner: PEDAL HEARING.
pages.cleffy: Day three of pedal. No playing today. Ears only. You'll hear pedal on. Pedal off. Learn the difference.

pages.figure: Two sound-wave pictures. Top: short, separated notes — labeled 'DRY.' Bottom: long, overlapping notes — labeled 'PEDALED.'
pages.cleffy: What to listen for. Pedal off — notes stop when fingers lift. Clean gaps. Pedal on — notes keep ringing. They bleed into each other. Warm, thick, connected.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: VERSION A — FIRST FIVE NOTES OF HAPPY BIRTHDAY.

audio.A: Happy Birthday opening — G G A G C B — played cleanly with no pedal, each note stopping crisply

pages.cleffy: Version A. Listen twice.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: VERSION B — SAME FIVE NOTES.

audio.A: Same Happy Birthday opening — G G A G C B — played with full pedal, all notes ringing together into one smear

pages.cleffy: Version B. Same five notes. Different pedal.

pages.figure: Two buttons — A and B. Question: 'Which one had the pedal on?'

audio.A: Replay of the two versions back to back

pages.cleffy: Which version had the pedal?

pages.figure: Diagram: a single note's sound envelope. Arrow pointing to the tail. Label: 'This tail tells you.'
pages.cleffy: The secret is the tail. Every note has a fade. A tail of sound after the attack. Pedal on — long tail. Pedal off — short tail. Train your ear on the tail.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: ROUND 1.

audio.A: A four-note scale fragment — C D E F — played crisply without pedal

pages.cleffy: Round 1. Pedal on, or off?

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: ROUND 2.

audio.A: A four-note scale fragment — C D E F — played with full pedal, notes blurring together

pages.cleffy: Round 2.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: ROUND 3 — chord.

audio.A: A C major chord played and released cleanly — no pedal

pages.cleffy: Round 3. A chord.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: ROUND 4 — chord.

audio.A: A C major chord played, fingers release immediately, but pedal holds it ringing for several seconds

pages.cleffy: Round 4.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: ROUND 5 — careful.

audio.A: Slow sustained chord with half-pedal — some ring, but damper partly down; a gentle in-between

pages.cleffy: Round 5. Trickier. Slower. Listen to the tail.

pages.figure: Two little album covers. Left: a Bach Prelude. Right: a Chopin Nocturne. Caption: 'Bach usually — dry. Chopin usually — pedaled.'
pages.cleffy: In real music — Bach often goes without pedal. Crisp, clean lines. Chopin almost always has pedal. Warmth, blur, color. Different eras, different conventions.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: A FAMOUS PIECE — WHICH ONE HAS PEDAL?

audio.A: First four bars of Bach Prelude in C, played dry, no pedal, clean arpeggios

pages.cleffy: A real piece. Pedal or no?

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: A DIFFERENT FAMOUS PIECE.

audio.A: Opening phrase of Chopin Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2, richly pedaled, notes blooming into each other

pages.cleffy: One more.

pages.figure: Banner — three listening questions.
pages.cleffy: Three final rounds. Ears ready.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 158. Cleffy cupping an ear. Banner: EAR FOR PEDAL · UNLOCKED.
pages.cleffy: You can hear pedal now. On or off, clean or wet. Tomorrow — what happens when pedal goes wrong. Mud. And how to avoid it.

real_piano: Listen to any recording — pop, classical, jazz. Try to guess when the pianist is pedaling and when they aren't.
Play the same chord two ways: once dry, once pedaled. Close your eyes. Listen to the tail.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7 through 14. Do each round twice. Your ear gets faster.

questions.figure: Two sound waveforms — one with short tails, one with long tails
questions.prompt: Which waveform shows pedal on?

questions.figure: Diagram of a note's envelope
questions.prompt: What part of the sound tells you about pedal?

questions.figure: Two composers
questions.prompt: Who is more likely to use heavy pedal?

questions.audio: A chord played dry — clean release
questions.prompt: Pedal on or off?

questions.audio: A chord played with pedal held — long ring
questions.prompt: Pedal on or off?

questions.audio: A scale fragment with pedal blurring the notes
questions.prompt: Pedal on or off?
No play requirement — this was a listening lesson. Just confirm.
questions.accept: Any input or tap-to-continue

remediation.title: Hearing the tail
remediation.opening_cleffy: Pedal on or off — it's all in the tail of the sound. We'll slow way down. Single notes only. Listen to what happens after your finger lifts. That's where the answer lives.

completion.cleffy: Ears trained. You can tell pedal by sound alone. Tomorrow — the mud. How pedal goes wrong, and how to fix it.

title: Pedal mistakes
subtitle: Act 32 · Sustain pedal
goal: Understand muddiness — what causes it — and learn the fix: syncopated pedaling, where the pedal presses AFTER the new note.
piece_context: Everyone who learns pedal makes mud. It's the pedal beginner's sin. Today — the exact cause, and the exact fix, used by every pianist since the 1830s.

pages.figure: Cleffy looking a little sheepish. Behind him: a murky brown splotch labeled 'MUD.' Banner: THE PEDAL'S BIGGEST SIN.
pages.cleffy: Today — the classic mistake. Mud. Every pianist makes it. Including the greats, when they were young.

pages.figure: Diagram: two chords overlapping. Sound waves blending. Red arrow: 'OLD HARMONY + NEW HARMONY = MUD.'
pages.cleffy: Mud is what happens when two harmonies ring at once. You play chord one. Pedal stays down. You play chord two. Chord one is still ringing. Now both chords are smashed together. Ugly.

pages.figure: Play button. Warning label: MUDDY.

audio.A: C major, F major, G major, C major progression played with the pedal held down through the entire thing — an ugly blur of notes

pages.cleffy: Listen. Pedal held through four chords. All four are ringing at the end. That's mud.

pages.figure: Two chords. A long pedal line underneath holding through both. Red X.
pages.cleffy: Cause number one. Pedal held too long. Didn't release on the new chord. Most common beginner mistake. Fixable with attention.

pages.figure: A scale. Every note under a pedal. Red X.
pages.cleffy: Cause number two. Pedaling a scale. Every scale note rings into the next. The whole scale becomes a blur. Scales usually want dry hands. No pedal.

pages.figure: Diagram: new chord hits on beat. Pedal lifts ON the beat, presses DOWN slightly after. Caption: 'SYNCOPATED PEDAL.'
pages.cleffy: The fix. It has a name — syncopated pedal. New note arrives. Pedal lifts at the same moment. Then — slightly after — pedal presses down again. Press AFTER the new note. Not with it. Not before.

pages.figure: Timeline. Moment 1: pedal up, new chord strikes — dampers catch the old strings clean. Moment 2: pedal back down — new chord rings on fresh pedal.
pages.cleffy: Why it works. When the pedal is up for that tiny instant, the old strings get damped. Then the new chord alone rings on the new pedal. Clean.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: SAME FOUR CHORDS — SYNCOPATED PEDAL.

audio.A: C F G C progression played with clean syncopated pedal — each chord rings fully but none overlap

pages.cleffy: Same four chords as the muddy version. Now with syncopated pedal. Hear the difference. Each chord blooms. None collide.

pages.figure: Side-view diagram of a foot. Three frames. 1: Pedal down, old chord ringing. 2: Finger strikes new chord, foot lifts. 3: Foot drops back down.
pages.cleffy: The foot motion. At the new chord — UP. Right then. Immediately after — DOWN. Feels like a quick hiccup under the chord. You'll get it.

pages.figure: Split screen. Real piano: foot doing the up-down-hiccup motion. Virtual app: pedal icon cycling automatically.
pages.cleffy: Real piano — your foot does the hiccup. Virtual — the app times the pedal changes for you. But the principle matters. You're training an ear, and a concept, that carry to any piano.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two chords: C then G. Pedal diagram showing the syncopated release-and-press on chord 2.
pages.cleffy: Try it. C major. Pedal down. Now G major — pedal up RIGHT as you strike, back down right after.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C F G C progression. Syncopated pedal marks on each new chord.
pages.cleffy: Four chords. Syncopated pedal on each. Slow. Don't rush the pedal.

pages.figure: Four listening rounds. Banner: MUD OR CLEAN?

audio.A: A chord sequence with pedal — sometimes muddy (pedal held too long), sometimes clean (syncopated)

pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Each one — mud or clean?

pages.figure: Same four-chord progression. No guides. Tempo 55.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You're pedaling by feel now. If you hear mud — release the pedal harder on the new chord.
pages.followup_cleffy: Clean pedal. On purpose.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick ones.
pages.cleffy: Three to close.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 159. Cleffy holding a clean white cloth in one hand, a brown splotch in the other. Banner: SYNCOPATED PEDAL · LEARNED.
pages.cleffy: Mud — identified. Syncopated pedal — your fix. Tomorrow — the act boss. The Prelude in C, again. But this time — with pedal.

real_piano: The four-chord progression — C F G C — with syncopated pedal. Every day. Listen for mud.
Try any scale with pedal held down. Listen to the mud. Now try it dry. That's why scales usually don't get pedal.
Listen to any recording. When the pianist shifts harmony, their foot is doing the hiccup. You know the move now.

questions.figure: Two overlapping chords in a sound diagram
questions.prompt: What causes mud?
options: Pedal held across a harmony change

questions.figure: Syncopated pedal timing diagram
questions.prompt: In syncopated pedal, when does the pedal press down?
options: At the same time as the new note

questions.figure: A scale passage
questions.prompt: Should you usually pedal a fast scale?

questions.audio: Four chords with pedal held through all of them — muddy
questions.prompt: Is this muddy or clean?

questions.audio: Four chords with syncopated pedal — clean
questions.prompt: Is this muddy or clean?
Play C F G chords with syncopated pedal — no mud between chords.
questions.accept: C, F, G chords with clean pedal changes between each

remediation.title: Pedal hiccup, slow
remediation.opening_cleffy: Syncopated pedal is new for your foot. Expect it to feel weird. We'll slow to 40. Two chords only. Foot does just the up-down hiccup, ten times on the same two chords, until the muscle learns it.

completion.cleffy: Mud — avoided. Syncopated pedal — earned. Tomorrow — a piece you know, reborn with pedal. Act boss.

title: Prelude in C with pedal
subtitle: Act 32 · Sustain pedal
goal: Play the first four bars of Bach's Prelude in C — same notes as L130, now with a pedal change on each bar's harmony.
piece_context: You played these four bars thirty lessons ago, dry. Today you return with a right foot in the mix. One pedal change per bar — because each bar is one harmony. The piece blooms.

piece.title: Prelude in C, BWV 846
piece.difficulty: intermediate — same notes as L130, pedal added on each harmonic change

pages.figure: Cleffy holding the same yellowed Bach manuscript from Lesson 130. A small pedal icon glowing at the bottom. Banner: ACT 32 BOSS · PRELUDE IN C · WITH PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: Act 32 boss. You know this piece. You played it in Lesson 130. Dry. Today you come back with a foot.

pages.figure: Two cards side by side. Left: L130 — Prelude, dry, no pedal. Right: L160 — Prelude, with pedal, one change per bar.
pages.cleffy: Same four bars. Same notes. Same fingers. What's new — one pedal change per bar. Each bar is one chord. So each bar gets one pedal.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: PRELUDE IN C — BARS 1-4 — WITH PEDAL.

audio.A: Bach Prelude in C bars 1-4, played with a clean pedal change on each bar's new harmony — rich, blooming, each bar rings as one chord

pages.cleffy: Listen. Same piece you know. Now every bar blooms. Pedal holds the broken chord together. Each bar becomes one ringing chord.

pages.figure: One bar of the Prelude written out. All 8 notes belong to one C major chord. Pedal bracket under the whole bar. Caption: 'One harmony = one pedal.'
pages.cleffy: Why the pedal works here. Every note in bar 1 is part of one C major chord. Hold pedal — all eight notes ring together as C major. No mud. Just one big chord, rolled out.

pages.figure: All four bars of the Prelude on a grand staff. Pedal brackets under each bar. Chord labels above: C — Dm7 — G7 — C.
pages.cleffy: The four bars and their chords. Bar 1 — C major. Bar 2 — D minor 7. Bar 3 — G7. Bar 4 — C major. Four chords. Four pedal changes. One per bar.

pages.figure: Zoomed view of bar 1 into bar 2. Pedal line drops under bar 1. At the exact start of bar 2 — pedal lifts and presses again (syncopated). New pedal line under bar 2.
pages.cleffy: Timing — syncopated pedal, from yesterday. At the downbeat of each new bar — pedal hiccups. Up as the new bass note strikes. Down right after.

pages.figure: Split. Real piano: foot pedaling on each downbeat. Virtual: app does the pedal automatically as you play each bar.
pages.cleffy: Real piano — your foot on every downbeat. Bar 1, bar 2, bar 3, bar 4. Virtual — the app pedals with you, bar by bar. You focus on the notes.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 1. Pedal bracket under. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Bar 1. Pedal down as you play the first note. Let all eight notes ring into each other. Pure C major.

pages.figure: Grand staff bar 2. Pedal bracket under. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Bar 2. At the new bass note — pedal hiccup. Up, then down. All eight notes of bar 2 ring together as D minor 7.

pages.figure: Grand staff bars 1-2. Two pedal brackets, syncopated change between.
pages.cleffy: Both bars now. Pedal changes right on the downbeat of bar 2. Listen — should hear C major ring, then clean break, then D minor ring.

pages.figure: Grand staff bars 3-4. Pedal brackets under each.
pages.cleffy: Bars 3 and 4. G7 then home. Two more pedal changes.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars, four pedal changes. Guides on. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. Pedal on every downbeat. Slow. Don't chase tempo. Chase clean pedal changes.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. L130 version — dry. L160 version — pedaled. Question: 'Hear the difference?'

audio.A: Bars 1-4 of the Prelude played dry — the L130 sound, crisp arpeggios
audio.B: Bars 1-4 of the Prelude played with pedal — each bar a bloom of chord

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Two different pieces, almost. The pedal version is how Bach is usually played today. Dry Bach is also lovely — but this is the standard modern sound.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All four bars. Quarter = 66. No guides. Pedal brackets still shown.
pages.cleffy: Bach's tempo — 66. No guides. Your foot knows the shape now. Four pedal changes. One per bar.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Same Bach. Foot added. The piece is bigger now.

pages.figure: Banner — act 32 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 32 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy tipping his hat. The L130 Bach manuscript next to him, now with little pedal marks drawn under each bar. Banner: 32/50 ACTS DONE.
pages.cleffy: Act 32. Closed. Your right foot is a musician now. Pedal basics. Pedal per chord. Pedal hearing. Pedal mistakes. And Bach, with pedal. You have earned the right to pedal every piece going forward — wisely. Next act — triplets in detail. The Moonlight Sonata is getting close.

real_piano: The first four bars of the Prelude, every day this week. Pedal change on every bar. Start at 55 bpm, build to 66.
Play bar 1 alone, ten times. Feel the foot hiccup on the downbeat of an imaginary bar 2. Then do the same with bar 2, 3, and 4.
Listen to Glenn Gould play the Prelude. He plays it almost dry. Listen to Lang Lang play it — fully pedaled. Two legitimate choices. Both beautiful.
virtual_only: Replay pages 12 and 14 — the guided and unguided full runs

questions.figure: Four bars of the Prelude with chord labels
questions.prompt: How many pedal changes in the first four bars?

questions.figure: One bar of the Prelude
questions.prompt: Why can you hold pedal through the whole bar?
options: Because every note belongs to the same chord

questions.figure: Downbeat of a new bar with a pedal hiccup
questions.prompt: When does the pedal release?
options: Exactly at the downbeat of the new bar

questions.audio: Prelude bars 1-4 with clean pedal changes
questions.prompt: Does this sound clean or muddy?

questions.audio: Prelude played dry vs played pedaled
questions.prompt: Which has more bloom and warmth?
Play the first two bars of the Prelude — with a clean pedal change between bar 1 and bar 2.
questions.accept: Bar 1 (C3 E3 + G3 C4 E4 pattern) and bar 2 (C3 D3 + A3 D4 F4 pattern) with clean separation

remediation.title: Prelude with pedal, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Combining hands you already know with a foot that's four days old. We'll go bar by bar again. Bar 1 alone, with pedal, ten times. Then bar 2 alone. Then stitch them together. The foot will catch up.

completion.cleffy: Act 32 sealed. The sustain pedal is yours. The Prelude you learned dry is now a piece that blooms. Next — triplets. The Moonlight Sonata is getting closer every act.

title: Triplets in detail
subtitle: Act 33 · Triplets deeper
goal: Play triplets perfectly even — three equal notes inside one beat, no rushing, no dragging.
piece_context: You met triplets back in L133 — three notes in the time of two. Act 33 goes deeper. The Moonlight Sonata opening is nothing but triplet arpeggios for bars and bars. Before we get there, the triplet has to be perfectly even. That's today.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a metronome. A bar of four triplet groups glowing behind him. Banner: ACT 33 — TRIPLETS DEEPER.
pages.cleffy: New act. Triplets again. You know the shape. Three notes, one beat. Now we make them perfect.

pages.figure: Two triplets side by side. Left — three notes perfectly even spaced. Right — first two crammed, last one late. Red X on the uneven one.
pages.cleffy: The mistake: rushing the first two, dragging the third. Or the opposite — first note, then the last two crammed together. A triplet is three equal pieces. Equal. Not nearly-equal.

pages.figure: A bar of 4/4 split two ways. Top stave — four quarter notes (the beats). Bottom stave — four triplet groups (twelve notes). Gridlines showing where each triplet note falls between the beats.
pages.cleffy: The real math. Three-against-two. Two halves of a beat would be eighths. Three thirds of a beat — a triplet. The middle note lands nowhere familiar.

pages.figure: Metronome at 60, ticking. Underneath — four triplet groups on Middle C playing exactly three taps per click.

audio.A: Metronome at 60 with four triplet groups on C4 — three taps per click, perfectly even

pages.cleffy: Listen. Click, tap-tap-tap. Click, tap-tap-tap. Three even taps between every click. That's the target.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: perfectly even triplet. B: uneven triplet (first two rushed).

audio.A: Four even triplets on C at 60 bpm
audio.B: Four uneven triplets — first two rushed, last one late

pages.cleffy: First — even. Each note the same size. Second — rushed. The first two crash together. Hear the difference. Learn to catch yourself doing B.

pages.figure: Bar of four triplets. Under each group, syllables: ONE-trip-let, TWO-trip-let, THREE-trip-let, FOUR-trip-let. The number on beat, 'trip' between, 'let' just before the next beat.
pages.cleffy: Say it out loud. One-trip-let, two-trip-let. Equal weight on each syllable. Not ONE-trip-let. Just one-trip-let. Say it before you play it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Middle C. Metronome 50. One triplet.
pages.cleffy: Slow. Fifty on the click. One triplet on C. Say one-trip-let as you play.

pages.figure: Bar of four triplet groups on C. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Full bar. Four triplets. Every note the same size. If one feels rushed — slow down next pass.

pages.figure: A rolling wheel. Three spokes marked 1, trip, let. Caption: triplets roll. Eighths march.
pages.cleffy: Feel it. Eighths march — left right left right. Triplets roll — one-trip-let, one-trip-let. Rolling, not marching. That's the whole lesson.

pages.figure: Bar. Four triplet groups: C-D-E, C-D-E, C-D-E, C-D-E. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Three notes, three fingers. C, D, E. Four groups in a row. Roll.

pages.figure: Same pattern. Metronome 65.
pages.cleffy: A little faster. Sixty-five. Evenness first. Speed never comes before evenness.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Four rounds — each plays a triplet. Pick: even or rushed.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Is this triplet even — or rushed?

pages.figure: Bar: two beats of eighths on C, then two beats of triplets on C. The subdivision change.
pages.cleffy: First half — eighths. Two per beat. Second half — triplets. Three per beat. Feel the roll kick in.
pages.followup_cleffy: That switch is the real test. Keep the beat steady. Change the subdivision inside it.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 161. Triplet glowing with three equal spokes. Banner: EVEN, SMOOTH, NO RUSHING.
pages.cleffy: Triplets. Even. Rolled. Not rushed. Tomorrow — triplet shapes. Rising, falling, rocking.

real_piano: Metronome at 55. Play four triplets on C. Say 'one-trip-let, two-trip-let' out loud with each group.
Record yourself on your phone. Listen back. Are they really even? The ear catches what the hand misses.

questions.figure: Bar with four triplet groups
questions.prompt: How many notes fit inside one beat of a triplet group?

questions.figure: A triplet labeled 'one-trip-let'
questions.prompt: Which syllable lands on the beat?

questions.figure: Two triplets — one even, one rushed
questions.prompt: Which is correct?

questions.audio: Two triplets played — one even, one uneven
questions.prompt: Which is even?
Play four even triplets on C4 at 55 bpm.
questions.accept: Four triplet groups on C4, even spacing, tempo 55

remediation.title: Triplets, chant then play
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rhythm is ear first, hand second. We'll chant "one-trip-let" for a full minute before touching a key.

completion.cleffy: Even triplets. The foundation of Moonlight. Tomorrow — shapes. Rising, falling, rocking.

title: Triplet shapes
subtitle: Act 33 · Triplets deeper
goal: Play triplets in three shapes — rising (C-E-G), falling (G-E-C), and rocking (C-E-C).
piece_context: A triplet is a container. What notes go inside it? Three common shapes. Up. Down. Back-and-forth. Learn the shapes and you can spot a triplet at a glance, not count one note at a time.

pages.figure: Cleffy with three arrows — one pointing up, one down, one curving. Banner: THREE SHAPES.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday — triplets even. Today — what the three notes actually do. Three shapes. Three patterns. Almost every triplet is one of them.

pages.figure: Staff. Triplet: C4 E4 G4. Arrow up. Label: RISING.
pages.cleffy: Rising. C, E, G. Skip, skip. Up the C chord. The most famous shape — it's the Moonlight opening.

pages.figure: Staff. Triplet: G4 E4 C4. Arrow down. Label: FALLING.
pages.cleffy: Falling. G, E, C. Same notes, reversed. Skip, skip, down. The mirror of rising.

pages.figure: Staff. Triplet: C4 E4 C4. Curved arrow — up then back down. Label: ROCKING.
pages.cleffy: Rocking. C, E, C. Up, then back. Goes nowhere, feels like a pulse. Common in folk tunes and slow accompaniments.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: rising. B: falling. C: rocking. Each plays one bar of triplets.

audio.A: Four rising triplets C-E-G on C4 E4 G4 at 60 bpm
audio.B: Four falling triplets G-E-C on G4 E4 C4 at 60 bpm
audio.C: Four rocking triplets C-E-C on C4 E4 C4 at 60 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen to each. Rising climbs. Falling drops. Rocking stays. Same three notes — different order, different feel.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 highlighted. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Rising first. C, E, G. Fingers 1, 3, 5 — thumb, middle, pinky.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4, E4, C4 highlighted. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Falling. G, E, C. Fingers 5, 3, 1 — pinky, middle, thumb.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and E4 only. Rocking motion. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Rocking. C, E, C. Two fingers only — 1 and 3. Thumb comes back.

pages.figure: Three triplets on staff. Student has to label each one — rising, falling, or rocking.
pages.cleffy: See the shape without counting each note. Notes going up the page — rising. Going down — falling. Up then back — rocking.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Four rounds — each plays one triplet shape.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which shape do you hear?

pages.figure: Bar: rising, rising, falling, falling.
pages.cleffy: Two rising, two falling. Climb twice. Drop twice. Same notes.

pages.figure: Bar: rising, rocking, falling, rocking.
pages.cleffy: All three shapes. Rising, rocking, falling, rocking. Different shape each beat.
pages.followup_cleffy: Three shapes, one bar. That's real triplet vocabulary now.

pages.figure: Rising triplets on C-E-G. Metronome 65.
pages.cleffy: One more rising. A little faster — 65. This is the shape you'll live inside for the next few lessons.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 162. Three arrows: up, down, curving. Banner: THREE SHAPES KNOWN.
pages.cleffy: Rising, falling, rocking. Tomorrow — the rising one gets a name. The three-note arpeggio.

real_piano: Play C-E-G rising triplets, four in a row, metronome 60. Fingers 1-3-5.
Try the same three shapes starting on G instead of C. G-B-D rising, D-B-G falling, G-B-G rocking.

questions.figure: A triplet: C4 E4 G4
questions.prompt: Which shape is this?

questions.figure: A triplet: G4 E4 C4
questions.prompt: Which shape is this?

questions.figure: A triplet: C4 E4 C4
questions.prompt: Which shape is this?

questions.audio: A rising triplet C-E-G
questions.prompt: Rising, falling, or rocking?
Play a rising triplet: C4, E4, G4.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 as a triplet group

remediation.title: Shapes, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three shapes is a lot for one sitting. We'll isolate each — four bars of rising, then four of falling, then rocking.

completion.cleffy: Three triplet shapes. You know them by sight and by sound. Tomorrow — the rising one becomes the arpeggio.

title: The 3-note arpeggio
subtitle: Act 33 · Triplets deeper
goal: Play the rising C-E-G as a single unit — one shape, one gesture — with fingering 1-3-5.
piece_context: A rising triplet on root-third-fifth has a name: a 3-note arpeggio. It's not three separate notes. It's one chord, unrolled. And this exact shape — C-E-G, rolled as triplets — is the engine of the Moonlight Sonata first movement. Learning to see and play it as a unit is the key to the whole piece.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a C major chord in his hand. Next to him — the same three notes rolled out one by one. Banner: ONE CHORD, UNROLLED.
pages.cleffy: Today the triplet gets a name. C, E, G — rising, as a triplet — is a 3-note arpeggio. Not three notes. One chord, unrolled.

pages.figure: Top: C major triad stacked — C, E, G all played at once. Bottom: same notes as triplet, one at a time. An equals sign between them.
pages.cleffy: Arpeggio means: play a chord one note at a time. Three notes of the C chord — C, E, G — rolled instead of stacked. Same harmony. Different delivery.

pages.figure: Moonlight Sonata opening — first two bars of the right hand. Triplet arpeggios labeled. Caption: every beat is a 3-note arpeggio.
pages.cleffy: Look at the Moonlight Sonata opening. Every single beat. A 3-note arpeggio. Learn this shape — you've learned the whole piece's engine.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 E4 G4 with finger numbers: 1 on C, 3 on E, 5 on G.
pages.cleffy: The fingering. One, three, five. Thumb on the bottom. Middle on the middle. Pinky on the top. Hand barely moves. Three fingers do the work.

pages.figure: Play button. One bar of four C-E-G triplet arpeggios.

audio.A: Four rising C-E-G triplet arpeggios on C4 E4 G4 at 60 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen. One shape, four times. Up, up, up. Up, up, up. That rolling feel — that's the engine.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4. Fingers 1, 3, 5 marked. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: One arpeggio. C, E, G. Fingers 1, 3, 5. Let the hand shape itself — no reaching.

pages.figure: Bar of four C-E-G triplet arpeggios. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Four in a row. Same shape, same fingers. Don't reset between them. Stay in the shape.

pages.figure: A hand. Over it, a curved arc from thumb to pinky. Caption: one gesture, not three key-presses.
pages.cleffy: Think of it as one gesture. Not three separate key-presses. The hand rolls from thumb to pinky. Three notes, one motion.

pages.figure: Same four arpeggios. Focus on smoothness. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Same four arpeggios. Focus: smooth. Each note equal. No bump, no gap.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 B4 D5 highlighted. Fingers 1, 3, 5. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Same shape, new home. G major arpeggio. G, B, D. Fingers 1, 3, 5 again. The shape doesn't change — the notes do.

pages.figure: Four C-E-G triplet arpeggios. Metronome 65.
pages.cleffy: Back to C. A little faster. Sixty-five. Shape, not speed. If it gets bumpy — slow down.

pages.figure: Drill. Four rounds — each plays a C or G arpeggio.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. C arpeggio or G arpeggio?

pages.figure: Two bars — eight C arpeggios in a row. Endurance. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Eight in a row. Two bars. This is what Moonlight demands. Sustained rolling.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two full bars. You just played the engine of Moonlight Sonata.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 163. C major chord rolled into a triplet. Banner: THE MOONLIGHT ENGINE.
pages.cleffy: The 3-note arpeggio. Fingering 1-3-5. Tomorrow — add the pedal. That's when it starts sounding like Moonlight.

real_piano: Metronome at 60. Play C-E-G triplet arpeggios for one full minute. Fingers 1-3-5.
Try the same shape on G (G-B-D), F (F-A-C), and D (D-F#-A). Same fingering every time.

questions.figure: C major triad stacked and C-E-G as triplet
questions.prompt: What's the relationship between these?
options: Same chord — one stacked, one rolled
The triplet is a different key

questions.figure: C4, E4, G4 on the keyboard
questions.prompt: What fingering for a right-hand 3-note arpeggio?

questions.figure: Moonlight Sonata opening — right hand
questions.prompt: What shape repeats through the whole opening?

questions.audio: Four C-E-G triplet arpeggios at 60 bpm
questions.prompt: What is this shape?
Play four C-E-G triplet arpeggios at 60 bpm, fingers 1-3-5.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 repeated four times as triplets

remediation.title: Arpeggio, one finger at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: One-three-five isn't automatic yet. Fair enough. We'll isolate each finger — thumb, middle, pinky — before rolling them together.

completion.cleffy: The 3-note arpeggio. The engine is yours. Tomorrow — add the right foot. The pedal changes everything.

title: Triplets with pedal
subtitle: Act 33 · Triplets deeper
goal: Combine the C-E-G triplet arpeggio with sustain pedal — the exact texture of Moonlight Sonata's opening. One bar.
piece_context: Yesterday — the 3-note arpeggio shape. Act 32 — the sustain pedal. Today they meet. Triplet arpeggio, pedal held down, the notes blur into one warm chord. That blur is the Moonlight sound. One bar today. The real piece comes in Act 34.

pages.figure: Cleffy sitting at a quiet, dim-lit upright. A moon out the window. Banner: THE MOONLIGHT SOUND.
pages.cleffy: Act 32 — pedal. Yesterday — triplet arpeggio. Today they meet. And what comes out is the opening of the Moonlight Sonata.

pages.figure: Left: C-E-G arpeggio dry, three separate notes. Right: same arpeggio with pedal down, all three notes ringing together at the end.
pages.cleffy: Without pedal — three separate notes. Each one fades. With pedal — all three sustain. By the end of the triplet, you hear a full C chord ringing. The arpeggio becomes the chord it came from.

pages.figure: Play button labeled DRY. C-E-G triplet arpeggios, no pedal.

audio.A: Four C-E-G triplet arpeggios at 55 bpm, no pedal, dry

pages.cleffy: Dry version. No pedal. Hear how each note is its own thing? Clean, but thin.

pages.figure: Play button labeled WITH PEDAL. Same arpeggios, pedal held through each bar.

audio.A: Four C-E-G triplet arpeggios at 55 bpm with sustain pedal held through the bar

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Pedal held. Everything rings. The three notes become one warm chord. That's Moonlight.

pages.figure: Time diagram. Beat 1 — pedal goes DOWN. Beats 1-4 — pedal STAYS down. Beat 1 of next bar — pedal comes UP then back DOWN on the new chord.
pages.cleffy: Rule for today. One pedal change per bar. Down at the start. Hold through all four beats. Lift and press again when the next chord arrives.

pages.figure: Play button. A real recording — Moonlight Sonata, bar 1. Just one bar.

audio.A: Moonlight Sonata 1st movement, bar 1 only — slow, with pedal, real piano sound

pages.cleffy: Listen. Just bar 1 of the real piece. Same shape you played yesterday. Same pedal rule. You're listening to something you can almost play.

pages.figure: C-E-G triplet arpeggio, four times. No pedal. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Warm up dry first. Four arpeggios, no pedal. Know the shape before we blur it.

pages.figure: Two-row diagram. Top: the hand playing C-E-G triplets across the bar. Bottom: a foot icon going DOWN on beat 1, STAY across all beats, then UP-DOWN at the bar line.
pages.cleffy: Foot and hand do different jobs. Hand — rolls the triplets. Four groups. Foot — down once, holds, lifts at the bar line.

pages.figure: One bar of four C-E-G triplet arpeggios. Pedal indicator below: down on beat 1, hold, release at end. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: One bar. Pedal down on beat 1. Hold it the whole bar. Feel the notes pile up.

pages.figure: A cross-section of the bar — all three notes (C, E, G) shown sustaining simultaneously by beat 4. Caption: by the end of the bar, the whole chord is ringing.
pages.cleffy: Listen as you play. By beat 4, all three notes are ringing together. The arpeggio has become the chord. That's the moonlight blur.

pages.figure: Same one bar. Metronome 45. Dynamic marking: pp (very soft).
pages.cleffy: Slower. Softer. Forty-five on the click. Moonlight is quiet. Play almost whispering.

pages.figure: Full one-bar phrase as song. Guides on. Tempo 50. Pedal indicator below.
pages.cleffy: One bar as a phrase. Guides on. Pedal down through. Think of the whole bar as one long breath.

pages.figure: Same bar. No guides. Play it from the page. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: No guides. Read the shape. Pedal down. Four rolling triplets. Pedal up at the end.
pages.followup_cleffy: That sound. That quiet, blurred, rolling sound. You are one act away from playing the real thing.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 164. A glowing C chord rippling outward. Banner: MOONLIGHT TEXTURE.
pages.cleffy: Triplet arpeggio plus pedal equals Moonlight texture. Tomorrow — the act boss. Triplet arpeggios in five keys, at tempo.

real_piano: Play C-E-G triplet arpeggios with pedal, one bar at a time. Pedal down on beat 1, lift on the bar line.
Play very softly — pp. Moonlight lives in the whisper range.
Listen to a recording of Moonlight Sonata 1st movement, bar 1 only. Match your sound to it.

questions.figure: Arpeggio with pedal diagram — pedal down beat 1, hold through bar
questions.prompt: How often does the pedal change in this bar?

questions.figure: C-E-G arpeggio with pedal held
questions.prompt: By the end of the bar, what's ringing?
options: All three notes — the full C chord

questions.figure: Moonlight Sonata opening
questions.prompt: Which two elements combine to make the Moonlight texture?

questions.audio: Same arpeggios twice — once dry, once with pedal
questions.prompt: Which has the Moonlight blur?
Play one bar of C-E-G triplet arpeggios with pedal down through the bar, at 50 bpm.
questions.accept: Four C-E-G triplet groups at 50 bpm

remediation.title: Pedal and triplets, separately first
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two new things at once is hard. We'll do the hand alone, then the foot alone, then put them together slowly.

completion.cleffy: Moonlight texture. Yours. Tomorrow — act boss. Five keys, at tempo.

title: Triplet arpeggio drill
subtitle: Act 33 · Triplets deeper
goal: See, hear, and play 3-note triplet arpeggios in five keys — C, G, F, D, A — at gradually rising tempo.
piece_context: Act 33 boss. Not a piece today — a drill. Same shape you know, moved to five home keys. See it, hear it, play it, at rising tempo. When you close this lesson, you have the technical vocabulary for Moonlight. Act 34 builds C-sharp minor on top of it. Then L170 is the first four bars of the real sonata.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a banner — ACT 33 BOSS. Five colored circles labeled C, G, F, D, A. Three-note arpeggios stacked above each.
pages.cleffy: Act 33 boss. Not a piece today. A drill. Same triplet arpeggio shape. Five different homes. See, hear, play.

pages.figure: Five staves stacked. Top to bottom: C (C-E-G), G (G-B-D), F (F-A-C), D (D-F#-A), A (A-C#-E). Each shown as a triplet arpeggio.
pages.cleffy: Five keys. Five arpeggios. All rising triplets. C — C, E, G. G — G, B, D. F — F, A, C. D — D, F-sharp, A. A — A, C-sharp, E. Two of them have a sharp. The others are all white.

pages.figure: Keyboard showing all five arpeggios. Each one labeled 1-3-5. Caption: same three fingers, every key.
pages.cleffy: The fingering never changes. One, three, five. Thumb, middle, pinky. Same hand shape. Different keys.

pages.figure: Five play buttons — one per key. Each plays one bar of triplet arpeggios.

audio.A: Four C-E-G arpeggios at 60 bpm
audio.B: Four G-B-D arpeggios at 60 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen through each. Same shape. Different color every time. Notice the two with sharps — D and A — have a slightly brighter edge.

pages.figure: One bar C-E-G triplet arpeggios. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Key one. C. Fingers 1, 3, 5.

pages.figure: One bar G-B-D triplet arpeggios. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Key two. G. G, B, D. All white keys.

pages.figure: One bar F-A-C triplet arpeggios. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Key three. F. F, A, C. Also all white.

pages.figure: One bar D-F#-A triplet arpeggios. F-sharp highlighted in a different color. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Key four. D. D, F-sharp, A. The F is a black key now. Middle finger lands on black.

pages.figure: One bar A-C#-E triplet arpeggios. C-sharp highlighted. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Key five. A. A, C-sharp, E. Middle finger on black again.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Five rounds — one per key. Student hears arpeggio, names the key.
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. Which arpeggio is this?

pages.figure: One bar C-E-G arpeggios. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Tempo up. Sixty on the click. C arpeggio. Smoothness matters more than speed.

pages.figure: One bar G-B-D arpeggios. Metronome 70.
pages.cleffy: Seventy. G arpeggio. If it gets messy — slow back down.

pages.figure: Three bars — C, G, F arpeggios back to back. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Three keys in a row. C, G, F. One bar each. Don't stop between.

pages.figure: Four bars of C-E-G arpeggios. Metronome 60. Label: like Moonlight.
pages.cleffy: Final run. Four bars. C arpeggio. Soft. Imagine the pedal. Imagine the moonlight.
pages.followup_cleffy: Four bars of rolling triplet arpeggios. At tempo. That's Act 33. Sealed.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 33 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 33 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy next to a piano with moonlight streaming in. Map of acts: 33/50 complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 33. Closed. Triplets, deeper. Shapes known. Fingering burned in. Pedal combined. Five keys under your hand. You are now holding the engine of the Moonlight Sonata in your two hands. Next act — C-sharp minor. The actual key. The actual piece. It starts at lesson 170.

real_piano: Cycle through all five keys every day — C, G, F, D, A. One bar of triplet arpeggios each. Metronome 60.
Try playing C arpeggios with pedal held through each bar. That's Moonlight waiting.
Push the tempo up one notch each day. 60, 65, 70. Stop the moment it gets rough.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5-9 (all five keys), then 13 and 14.

remediation.title: Drill, one key at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Five keys is a lot. We'll walk them one by one. Spend a full minute on each before moving on. By the end, all five will feel like the same shape.

completion.cleffy: Act 33 sealed. You have the triplet arpeggio — in five keys, at tempo, with pedal. Ten lessons from now — Moonlight Sonata, bars 1 through 4. Keep rolling.

title: Minor-key shape
subtitle: Act 34 · Minor keys + Moonlight intro
goal: Hear and see why minor sounds darker than major — the lowered third is the color change.
piece_context: Act 34 opens the road to Moonlight. You had the twin-keys idea back at L113 — every major has a minor relative. Now you go deeper. What actually makes a scale sound minor? One note. The third.

pages.figure: Cleffy standing between a sunlit door and a moonlit door. Banner: ACT 34 · MINOR KEYS.
pages.cleffy: New act. Minor keys. The road to Moonlight. You already know they sound sad. Today — why.

pages.figure: Two staves stacked. Top — A major scale: A B C# D E F# G# A. Bottom — A minor scale: A B C D E F G A. Third, sixth, and seventh notes circled on both with arrows down.
pages.cleffy: Look at the two A scales. Major has three sharps. Minor has none. The third, sixth, and seventh got lowered. The third is the one that changes the color.

pages.figure: Keyboard zoomed to A position. A to C# labeled 'major third' (bright arrow). A to C labeled 'minor third' (dim arrow).
pages.cleffy: A to C-sharp — major third. Bright. A to C — minor third. One half-step lower. Dark. Same distance from home, different mood.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A major scale, then A minor scale.

audio.A: A major scale ascending
audio.B: A natural minor scale ascending — all white keys from A

pages.cleffy: First — A major. Bright. Second — A minor. Sad. Same starting note. Same ending note. Different middle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A and C# glowing. Finger 1 on A, finger 3 on C#.
pages.cleffy: Play the major third. A, then C-sharp. That's the bright sound.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A and C glowing. Finger 1 on A, finger 3 on C.
pages.cleffy: Now the minor third. A, then plain C. One half-step lower. Feel the flip.

pages.figure: Banner: NATURAL MINOR = major with lowered 3, 6, 7. Diagram showing a major scale with arrows pulling three notes down.
pages.cleffy: The formula. Take any major scale. Lower the third. Lower the sixth. Lower the seventh. That's the natural minor. Same staircase, three shadows.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A B C D E F G A — all white keys.
pages.cleffy: A minor scale. All white keys, A to A. Listen as you climb — hear the sad thirds.

pages.figure: Staff. D minor scale: D E F G A Bb C D. Lowered third (F) and lowered sixth (Bb) highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Same rule, different home. D minor. Third is F — not F-sharp. Sixth is B-flat. Darker version of D major.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D E F G A Bb C D.
pages.cleffy: D minor scale. Watch the B-flat near the top.

pages.figure: Six rounds. Banner — 'Third: major or minor?'
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. One third each. Bright or dark? Major or minor?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two A chords side by side — A-C#-E (major), A-C-E (minor). Play left chord, then right.
pages.cleffy: Two chords. A major, then A minor. Move only the middle finger. Down one half-step. That's the whole change.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 166. Moonlit door half-open. Text: one note makes all the difference.
pages.cleffy: Minor keys. The third is the color. Tomorrow — C-sharp minor. Four sharps. The Moonlight key.

real_piano: Play A major scale, then A minor. Listen for the third — the C# vs C. That's the whole flip.
Play any major chord you know. Lower the middle note by one half-step. Now it's minor.

questions.prompt: Which interval is the minor third?

Play the A minor scale, A to A, all white keys.

remediation.title: Minor, one third at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Minor is a feel, not a formula — but the formula helps. We'll slow it. One third at a time. Ear first, then keys.

completion.cleffy: Minor keys — the third is the shadow. Tomorrow — C-sharp minor. Four sharps. The key of Moonlight.

title: C-sharp minor (the Moonlight key)
subtitle: Act 34 · Minor keys + Moonlight intro
goal: Meet C-sharp minor — four sharps, the home key of the Moonlight Sonata.
piece_context: Yesterday — what makes minor sound minor. Today — the specific minor key Beethoven chose for Moonlight. Four sharps. Cold. Deep. Its relative major is E major — but the piece lives in the shadow, not the light.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a staff with four sharps stacked at the front. Moonlight silhouette behind. Banner: C# MINOR.
pages.cleffy: C-sharp minor. Four sharps. F, C, G, D. The key Beethoven chose for Moonlight.

pages.figure: Treble staff close-up. Key signature for C# minor / E major — F# (top line), C# (third space), G# (space above), D# (fourth line).
pages.cleffy: Four sharps, in order. F-sharp. C-sharp. G-sharp. D-sharp. Say them out loud — F, C, G, D. Sharp each one.

pages.figure: Staff with key signature. C# minor scale written: C# D# E F# G# A B C#.
pages.cleffy: The scale. C-sharp, D-sharp, E, F-sharp, G-sharp, A, B, C-sharp. Four black keys going up. Three white keys — E, A, B — between them. Third is E. Lowered from E-sharp. That's the minor shadow.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: C# MINOR SCALE.

audio.A: C-sharp minor scale ascending, slow and clean

pages.cleffy: Listen. Cold. Dark. Deep. Not sad like a folk tune sad — a bigger sad. Noble.

pages.figure: Two doors side by side. E major (sunlit) and C# minor (moonlit). Both share the same four sharps.
pages.cleffy: Same four sharps as E major. E major is the bright twin. C-sharp minor is the shadow one. Beethoven wrote in the shadow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C# highlighted in two octaves — middle (C#4) and one below (C#3). Finger 1 on each.
pages.cleffy: Find C-sharp. The black key right after C. Two of them on our map — C-sharp below middle C, and C-sharp above.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C# D# E F# G# highlighted. Fingering: 1 2 3 4 5 shown above.
pages.cleffy: First five notes. C-sharp, D-sharp, E, F-sharp, G-sharp. Fingers one through five. Thumb on C-sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full C# minor scale highlighted, ascending.
pages.cleffy: Full scale going up. C-sharp, D-sharp, E, F-sharp, G-sharp, A, B, C-sharp. Three black keys, one white, one black, two whites, back to black. Slow.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor with plain F and plain D notes circled. Arrows to keyboard: F# and D# keys lit.
pages.cleffy: Reading rule again. Plain F on the page — still F-sharp. Plain D on the page — still D-sharp. The key signature already told you. It doesn't repeat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C# minor scale descending: C# B A G# F# E D# C#.
pages.cleffy: Coming down. Same notes, reversed. Listen for the landing on C-sharp at the bottom. That's home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C#-E-G# glowing. Label: C# minor chord, root-3-5.
pages.cleffy: The home chord. C-sharp, E, G-sharp. Root, minor third, fifth. This chord opens the Moonlight Sonata.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: C# MINOR CHORD — held and rolled.

audio.A: C-sharp minor triad held, then rolled bottom to top slowly

pages.cleffy: Listen. Held first. Then rolled. When Beethoven rolls it slowly — triplets — that becomes Moonlight.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First held chord, then rolled as triplet arpeggio: C#4 E4 G#4.
pages.cleffy: Play it as an arpeggio. Three notes up. Your fingers already know this shape — you drilled it in five keys last act.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 167. C# minor key signature glowing. A small moon rising. Text: THE MOONLIGHT KEY.
pages.cleffy: C-sharp minor. Four sharps. F, C, G, D. The home of the Moonlight Sonata. Tomorrow — reading short phrases in it.

real_piano: Play the C-sharp minor scale slowly, both directions. Say each sharp as you land — F-sharp, C-sharp, G-sharp, D-sharp.
Play the C-sharp minor chord — C#, E, G-sharp. Then roll it as an arpeggio. That's the Moonlight engine.

remediation.title: C-sharp minor, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four sharps is the most you've seen. We'll take it slow. The black keys first, then the whole scale.

completion.cleffy: C-sharp minor — met. Four sharps known. Tomorrow — reading short phrases in this key.

title: Reading in C-sharp minor
subtitle: Act 34 · Minor keys + Moonlight intro
goal: Read and play short phrases in C-sharp minor — the shapes you know, now with four sharps in play.
piece_context: You know the key. You played the scale. Now read it. The interval shapes are the same — steps look like steps, skips look like skips — but every F, C, G, and D is sharp. Same alphabet, new accent.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a staff in C# minor. Banner: READING IN C# MINOR.
pages.cleffy: Same key. Now we read it. The shapes haven't changed. The colors have.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top — three notes in C major: C D E (step, step). Bottom — same shape in C# minor: C# D# E (step, step). Intervals labeled identically on both.
pages.cleffy: Two little phrases. Same shape. Top — C, D, E. Bottom — C-sharp, D-sharp, E. Same steps. Different keys. That's all.

pages.figure: Staff with C# minor key signature at the front. Arrows from each sharp to the first F, C, G, D note in the phrase below.
pages.cleffy: Key signature up front. Four sharps. Every F — sharp. Every C — sharp. Every G — sharp. Every D — sharp. It doesn't repeat in the music. You just know.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Four notes: C#4, E4, G#4, E4. Stepwise and skipwise motion marked.
pages.cleffy: First phrase. Four notes. C-sharp, E, G-sharp, E. Up a third, up a third, down a third. Outlines the home chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C#4 E4 G#4 E4.
pages.cleffy: Play it. C-sharp, E, G-sharp, E. That shape is half of Moonlight already.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Six notes: C# D# E F# G# A. A rising line, mostly steps.
pages.cleffy: Second phrase. Six notes, rising. Plain F on the page — still F-sharp. Plain D on the page — still D-sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C#4 D#4 E4 F#4 G#4 A4 highlighted in order.
pages.cleffy: Rising six. Watch the black keys.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Eight notes: G#4 F#4 E4 D#4 C#4 B3 A3 G#3. Descending line.
pages.cleffy: Third phrase. Descending. Eight notes. All the way down from G-sharp to G-sharp an octave below.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G#4 F#4 E4 D#4 C#4 B3 A3 G#3 highlighted in order.
pages.cleffy: Play it down. Slow. Let each sharp land clean.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Six notes with skips: C#4 G#4 C#5 G#4 E4 C#4. A contour outlining the C# minor chord in two octaves.
pages.cleffy: Fourth phrase. Bigger leaps. C-sharp, G-sharp, C-sharp above, back down. All chord tones.

pages.figure: Keyboard spanning an octave. C#4 G#4 C#5 G#4 E4 C#4.
pages.cleffy: Reach up to the high C-sharp. Then back down through E to home.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: short C# minor melody.

audio.A: Slow melody in C-sharp minor, 8 notes, stepwise with one skip — sad, noble tone

pages.cleffy: Listen. A short C-sharp minor melody. That's the mood Beethoven lives in for Moonlight.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Longer combined phrase: C#4 E4 G#4 A4 G#4 F#4 E4 D#4 C#4. Mix of steps and skips.
pages.cleffy: One longer phrase. Nine notes. Up by thirds, then walk back down the scale to home.
pages.followup_cleffy: That phrase was all C-sharp minor. No accidentals written in the music. The key signature did all the work.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 168. C# minor phrase on a staff, glowing. Text: READING THE SHADOW KEY.
pages.cleffy: Reading C-sharp minor. Same shapes, new color. Tomorrow — the dotted rhythm that sits on top. The Moonlight melody itself.

real_piano: Play phrases 1 through 4 from today. Slow. Every sharp crisp.
Pick any short melody you know. Try reading it as if it were in C-sharp minor — every F, C, G, D sharp.

remediation.title: C-sharp minor, one phrase at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four sharps in every phrase is a lot to track. We'll take them one phrase at a time. Keys first, reading second.

completion.cleffy: Reading in C-sharp minor. Done. Tomorrow — dotted rhythms in this key. The Moonlight melody, without the pedal or the arpeggios.

title: Dotted rhythms in minor
subtitle: Act 34 · Minor keys + Moonlight intro
goal: Preview the Moonlight melody rhythm — long-short-short-long — in C-sharp minor, without pedal or arpeggios.
piece_context: You know the key. You can read in it. Now the rhythm that sits on top. The Moonlight melody is built on one dotted pattern, played again and again. Today — just that rhythm. No pedal. No rolling arpeggios underneath. Strip it down to the bones.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a simple melody on a staff. Banner: THE MOONLIGHT MELODY, STRIPPED.
pages.cleffy: One day away. Today — the melody rhythm. No pedal. No arpeggios. Just the bones.

pages.figure: Rhythm staff. One bar: dotted eighth, sixteenth, eighth, dotted quarter. Counts labeled: 1-e-a, 2, 3. Label: LONG-short-short-LONG.
pages.cleffy: Listen to the shape first. Long, short, short, long. Da — DAA — da — DAA.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: the rhythm, clapped.

audio.A: Clapping the dotted Moonlight melody rhythm at slow tempo — long, short-short, long, four bars in a row

pages.cleffy: Listen. Count with it. Same four-beat shape, over and over. That's the whole Moonlight melody engine.

pages.figure: Two rhythms side by side. Left — the galloping dotted eighth + sixteenth from L132. Right — the Moonlight long-short-short-long. Arrow between labeled 'same idea, longer tail.'
pages.cleffy: You met a cousin of this rhythm at Lesson 132 — the galloping one. Moonlight's version is slower. Noble, not racing. Same long-short math though.

pages.figure: Rhythm staff on C#4. Dotted eighth, sixteenth, eighth, dotted quarter. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Tap it out. One note — C-sharp. Count one-and, two, three. Let the long notes breathe.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. The same rhythm, now with notes: G#4, G#4, G#4, E4. Top voice of the Moonlight melody.
pages.cleffy: Now the actual notes. Three G-sharps, then an E. Four notes. One dotted figure. That's bar five of Moonlight, melody only.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G#4 three times, then E4. Rhythm labels above each note.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Three G-sharps, E. Dotted eighth, sixteenth, eighth, dotted quarter.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Second bar: G#4, G#4, G#4, F#4. Same rhythm, final note drops to F-sharp.
pages.cleffy: Bar two. Same rhythm. Last note drops a step. Three G-sharps, then F-sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G#4 three times, then F#4.
pages.cleffy: Play bar two. Same feel. New landing.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Two bars of melody: first lands on E, second on F#. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both bars back to back. Land on E. Land on F-sharp. Rest between.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: Moonlight melody, bars 5-8, no pedal, no arpeggios.

audio.A: The Moonlight melody top voice alone, bars 5-8, slow tempo, no accompaniment

pages.cleffy: Listen. Melody alone. No arpeggios rolling underneath. Just the top voice. The song inside the piece.

pages.figure: Staff in C# minor. Three bars: lands on E, F#, then G#. Metronome 52.
pages.cleffy: Three bars. Same rhythm each time. Lands on E, F-sharp, G-sharp. Climbing.

pages.figure: Same three-bar melody. Song interaction. No guides. Tempo 54.
pages.cleffy: Once more. Read the page. Let the long notes breathe. No rushing to the next.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the Moonlight melody skeleton. Tomorrow we put it over the arpeggios.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 169. Melody skeleton on staff, moon rising behind. Text: THE MOONLIGHT BONES.
pages.cleffy: Dotted rhythm. Long-short-short-long. In C-sharp minor. On its own. Tomorrow — the first four bars of the real sonata. All of it, slow.

real_piano: Clap the rhythm first — long, short, short, long — before you ever touch a key. Count out loud.
Play bar one: three G-sharps, E. Then bar two: three G-sharps, F-sharp. Over and over until the rhythm feels natural.
