remediation.title: Moonlight rhythm, even slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Dotted rhythms sound simple until you play them. We'll slow to 40. Clap before play. One bar at a time.

completion.cleffy: The Moonlight melody rhythm — yours. Tomorrow — the real thing. First four bars of the sonata. Slow.

title: Moonlight 1 — bars 1-4 (slow)
subtitle: Act 34 · Minor keys + Moonlight intro
goal: Play the opening four bars of Moonlight Sonata 1st movement — the texture, slow and sustained.
piece_context: Act 34 boss. The first brick of the graduation piece. Four bars. No melody yet — that comes at L171. Today is the TEXTURE — the endless triplet arpeggio engine in the right hand, over two deep pedal-tone octaves in the left. Adagio sostenuto means slow and sustained. Very slow. Every note held until the next. You have been building toward this moment for 34 acts.

piece.title: Piano Sonata No. 14 in C-sharp minor, Op. 27 No. 2 (Moonlight)
piece.time_signature: 4/4 (alla breve in original; treated as 4/4 here)
piece.difficulty: intermediate — opening texture only, very slow, both hands

pages.figure: Cleffy in a moonlit room. A single candle. A staff overhead with the first bar of Moonlight. Banner: ACT 34 BOSS · MOONLIGHT, BAR 1.
pages.cleffy: You've been building to this. Triplet arpeggios, C-sharp minor, dotted rhythm, pedal. All of it in one piece now. Moonlight Sonata. Bar one.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left — 'what today is NOT': full melody, full movement, at tempo. Right — 'what today IS': four bars of texture, very slow, no melody yet.
pages.cleffy: The honest part. Today IS: four bars. The texture. The engine. Very, very slow. Today is NOT: the melody. That lands tomorrow. Today is NOT: full speed. That lands in Tier 6.

pages.figure: Italian words on screen — ADAGIO SOSTENUTO. Translations below — ADAGIO = slow. SOSTENUTO = sustained, held.
pages.cleffy: Adagio sostenuto. Adagio — slow. Sostenuto — sustained. Held. Every note rings into the next. No gaps. No rushing.

pages.figure: Grand staff divided into three layers. Top — melody (greyed out, marked 'tomorrow'). Middle — RH triplet arpeggio C#-E-G#, rolling. Bottom — LH octave C#2 and C#3, held whole notes.
pages.cleffy: Three layers in this piece. Top — the melody. Not today. Middle — the triplet arpeggio. C-sharp, E, G-sharp. Rolling forever. Bottom — two deep C-sharps. Held under everything.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: Moonlight Sonata opening, real recording, bars 1-4.

audio.A: Moonlight Sonata opening, first four bars, real piano, with pedal, adagio sostenuto

pages.cleffy: Listen. The real thing. Four bars. Hear the rolling triplets in the middle. Hear the deep C-sharps underneath. No melody has entered yet.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Bar 1 of RH: four groups of triplet eighths — C#4 E4 G#4 repeated four times. Fingering 1-2-5 above.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. One bar, four triplets. Same three notes each time. C-sharp, E, G-sharp. Fingers — one, two, five. Thumb, index, pinky. New fingering. The triplet arpeggios you drilled before used 1-3-5 — those were triads. Moonlight uses 1-2-5 here because the C#-E-G# spread is a fifth — not a triad like before. New fingering for a wider hand position.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C#4 E4 G#4 highlighted. One bar of triplets. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. One bar of triplets. Same shape you drilled in five keys. This is just the C-sharp minor version.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Bars 1-2 of LH: C#2 and C#3 played together as an octave, held as whole notes. Just two octave chords, one per bar.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. Very simple. Two C-sharps together — one low, one higher. An octave. Hold it. Whole note. One chord, one bar.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C#2 and C#3 highlighted together. LH thumb on C#3, pinky on C#2.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Both C-sharps at once. Hold them four full beats.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Four bars of RH triplets — C#4 E4 G#4 rolling. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Right hand — four full bars. Same shape every bar. Steady. Even. No accent on any one note.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1 only. LH holds C#2-C#3 octave as whole note. RH plays four triplets of C#4-E4-G#4 above it. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. One bar. Left hand holds the octave. Right hand rolls the triplets. Very slow. Let it breathe.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1-2. LH plays C#2-C#3 octave at the start of each bar, holds through. RH triplets rolling above. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Left hand strikes once per bar, holds. Right hand keeps rolling. Don't stop between bars.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All four opening bars. Guides on. Metronome 48.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. Guides on. Forty-eight on the click. Left hand — strike, hold. Right hand — roll, endless.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All four opening bars. No guides. Metronome 48. Label: adagio sostenuto.
pages.cleffy: Once more. No guides. Read the page. Hear Beethoven listening.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. The first four bars of Moonlight. No melody yet. Just the world the melody will live in.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 34 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 34 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy looking at moonlight through a window, piano open. Map of acts: 34/50 complete. Banner: FIRST BRICK LAID.
pages.cleffy: Act 34. Closed. You just played the opening of Moonlight Sonata. Four bars, slow, both hands. First brick. The graduation piece starts here. Next act — bars five through eight. The melody enters. The piece really begins.

real_piano: Play the four bars every day. Slow. Never faster than 50 bpm this week — the speed will come much later.
Try it with the sustain pedal down the whole bar, then clear it when the left hand strikes the next bar. That's the sound.
Listen to the full Moonlight 1st movement recording at least once. Hear where you are — and where you're going.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 11, 12, 13, and 14. Each one builds the last.

questions.figure: Left-hand opening of Moonlight — one bar

questions.audio: Moonlight opening four bars, real recording

Play bar one of Moonlight: LH C#2-C#3 octave held, RH triplet arpeggio C#4-E4-G#4 four times, at 48 bpm.

remediation.title: Moonlight, bar by bar
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. First bar of the graduation piece. Big moment. We'll slow to 40. One hand at a time. One bar at a time. Four bars is enough for today. The rest of the piece is waiting — it's not running away.

completion.cleffy: Act 34 sealed. You played the first four bars of Moonlight Sonata. The first brick of your graduation piece is laid. Next act — bars five through eight. The melody enters.

title: Moonlight 1 — bars 5–8
subtitle: Act 35 · The first phrase
goal: Play bars 5–8 of Moonlight Sonata — the bars where the melody finally enters, sitting on top of the triplet engine.
piece_context: Bars 1–4 (Lesson 170) were just engine — triplet arpeggios humming in C-sharp minor. Today the melody arrives. Bar 5 is where Moonlight becomes Moonlight. A high G-sharp, held. Then that famous long-short-short dotted rhythm. The sound most people picture when they hear the word 'Moonlight' is these four bars.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st mvt — bars 5–8
piece.difficulty: intermediate — single-hand melody with dotted rhythm, simplified

pages.figure: Cleffy at a piano under a low moon. The triplet engine drawn fading from the left; a single high note appearing on the right.
pages.cleffy: Bars 1 through 4 were the engine. Bar 5 is where the melody enters. The sound everyone remembers.

pages.figure: A four-bar grid. Bars 1-4 labelled 'engine only — yesterday.' Bars 5-8 labelled 'melody enters — today.' A small moon above bar 5.
pages.cleffy: Eight bars total. Yesterday you played the first four — just triplets humming. Today — bars 5 through 8. A voice on top of the engine.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'bars 5-8, melody audible.'

audio.A: Moonlight Sonata bars 5-8 played slowly, pedaled. Melody clearly above the triplet figuration.

pages.cleffy: Listen. The high note on top is the melody. Same triplets underneath. But something above them now.

pages.figure: Staff. A single G-sharp 4 drawn very large and high, held over the first triplet group.
pages.cleffy: The melody note. G-sharp — just above the top line of the treble staff. Held. Long. It sits on top while the engine rolls underneath.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G#4 glowing, held.
pages.cleffy: Play it. G-sharp. Right hand. Hold it for a count of four.

pages.figure: Staff. Three G-sharps in a row with the rhythm: dotted-quarter, eighth, quarter. Labelled 'long — short — short.'
pages.cleffy: The famous rhythm. Long. Short. Short. Three G-sharps. The first one held. The second snaps quick. The third steps on the beat. Say it out loud — 'LONG short short.' That's Moonlight's melody.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'long-short-short, three times.'

audio.A: G#4 played three times in dotted rhythm — long, short, short — with pedal.

pages.cleffy: Listen again. Just the rhythm. Long-short-short. Like a sigh that keeps trying to finish.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G#4 glowing three times. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Play the rhythm. Three G-sharps. Long, short, short. Right hand, middle finger.

pages.figure: Staff showing bars 5-8 melody simplified: G#4 G#4 G#4 (bar 5) | G#4 G#4 A4 (bar 6) | A4 A4 G#4 (bar 7) | G#4 F#4 E4 (bar 8 — a little descent).
pages.cleffy: Four bars of melody. Bar 5 — three G-sharps. Bar 6 — two G-sharps, then a step up to A. Bar 7 — two A's, then back to G-sharp. Bar 8 — a small descent. G-sharp, F-sharp, E. Lands.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The simplified melody line lights up in order.
pages.cleffy: The whole melody, right hand only. Slow. Listen to the shape — mostly G-sharp, one step up, one step back, then down to rest.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Top: melody G#4 held. Bottom: LH octave C#2-C#3 bass + a rolling triplet figure (C#3-G#3-C#4 repeated). Arrows pointing up to the melody note.
pages.cleffy: Underneath the melody — the same engine as yesterday. Left hand holds a low C-sharp octave. The triplets roll between them. You don't need to play all of it today. Just picture it holding the melody up.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH melody. LH: a single low C#2 held, with one C#3 triplet group per bar. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Left hand — low C-sharp, hold. Right hand — the melody on top. Simplified. The engine is there, but quiet.

pages.figure: Bars 5-8, RH melody only, expressive. Tempo 45. Dynamic: pp.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Very slow. Very soft. Feel the long-short-short in every bar.

pages.figure: Full bars 5-8. Both hands. Guides off. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Bars 5 through 8. Both hands. No guides. Quiet. Like moonlight on water.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the first full phrase of Moonlight. Your hands just played the sound the whole world recognizes.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 171 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

real_piano: Bars 5-8, melody only, once a day. Hear the long-short-short rhythm in every bar.
Then add the low C-sharp bass. Don't worry about the triplet figure yet — just the melody over the bass note.
Sing the melody while your left hand holds the bass. Helps your ear.
virtual_only: Replay pages 13 and 14 — melody alone, then both hands.

questions.figure: The rhythm: dotted-quarter, eighth, quarter
questions.prompt: How is this rhythm described?

questions.figure: G-sharp note written above the top line of the treble staff
questions.prompt: What is this note?

questions.figure: Bar 8 melody: G#4, F#4, E4
questions.prompt: What's happening in this bar?

questions.audio: Bars 5-8 played with melody clearly on top
questions.prompt: Is the melody above the engine or below?
Play the long-short-short rhythm: three G-sharps, dotted-quarter, eighth, quarter.
questions.accept: G#4 three times with dotted-quarter + eighth + quarter rhythm

remediation.title: Bars 5-8, one hand at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Melody and engine at once is a lot for one lesson. Let's take the melody alone first. Get the long-short-short rhythm into your body. Then add the left hand. Slowly.

completion.cleffy: Bars 5 through 8. The melody arrives. Four lessons from here, you play the whole opening — all eight bars — as the Tier 4 boss. Tomorrow — what actually makes the melody sing.

title: Moonlight 1 — the melody line
subtitle: Act 35 · Hearing the top voice
goal: Bring out the top note of the triplet figure — the hidden melody line that runs through all eight bars.
piece_context: Here's a secret about Moonlight. Even in bars 1-4, before the melody officially enters, there is a melody — hidden in the top note of each triplet. G-sharp, then another G-sharp, then a small shift. When a real pianist plays Moonlight, they bring out those top notes ever so slightly. The piece stops being an engine. It starts being a song.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing up. A triplet group drawn — three notes stacked low to high. The top note circled in gold.
pages.cleffy: Here's a Moonlight secret. The melody isn't just in bars 5 through 8. It's been there the whole time. Sitting on top of every triplet.

pages.figure: Staff. Four triplet groups from bar 1 of Moonlight: G#3-C#4-E4 | G#3-C#4-E4 | G#3-C#4-E4 | G#3-C#4-E4. The top E4 of each group circled.
pages.cleffy: Here are four triplets from bar 1. Every group has three notes. Look at just the top one. E, E, E, E. That's a voice. That's melody. Hiding.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. One: 'flat — triplets even.' Two: 'top note brought out.'

audio.A: Bar 1 triplets played with all three notes equal volume.
audio.B: Same bar, same notes, but the top note is slightly louder.

pages.cleffy: Listen to both. First one — all three notes the same weight. Flat. Second one — top note a touch louder. Suddenly there's a melody. Same notes. Very different sound.

pages.figure: Diagram. Three fingers pressing a triplet. Arrow pointing at the pinky (finger 5) — 'slightly firmer.' Other two fingers marked 'softer.'
pages.cleffy: This is called voicing. Making one note in a chord or group sing above the others. In a right-hand triplet, the top note is usually the pinky. Press it a tiny bit firmer. Not louder. Just — firmer. The ear picks it up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Triplet C#4-E4-G#4. Metronome 50. Label: 'all equal.'
pages.cleffy: First — play it flat. Three notes, all the same weight. C-sharp, E, G-sharp.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same triplet. G-sharp (top) highlighted in gold. Label: 'top louder.'
pages.cleffy: Now voice the top. Same notes. Pinky presses firmer. Listen for the G-sharp on top — a little thread of melody.

pages.figure: Staff. The top-note line across bars 1-4 written alone: E4 E4 E4 E4 | E4 E4 E4 E4 | F#4 F#4 F#4 F#4 | E4 E4 E4 E4. Label: 'the hidden melody, bars 1-4.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the hidden melody across bars 1-4. Just the top notes. Mostly E. One bar of F-sharp. Back to E. Not dramatic. But it's there. Quiet motion. Like a second voice.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The top-note melody line alone.
pages.cleffy: Play just the top line. Right hand, pinky. E, E, E, E. E, E, E, E. F-sharp times four. E times four.

pages.figure: Staff. Bars 5-8 melody you learned yesterday: G#4-G#4-G#4-G#4-G#4-A4-A4-A4-G#4-G#4-F#4-E4. Arrow: 'same idea. Top voice — now louder.'
pages.cleffy: Bars 5 to 8 — same trick, bigger. The G-sharp you played yesterday is the top voice, made unmistakable. It was hiding in bars 1-4. Now it speaks.

pages.figure: Bar 5. RH triplet + the held G-sharp melody above it. Label: 'melody note held while triplets roll.'
pages.cleffy: Bar 5. The melody G-sharp is held on top while the triplet rolls underneath. Pinky stays down. Thumb and middle finger keep the engine going. That's Moonlight. One hand, two things at once.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'Moonlight bars 1-8, professional recording.'

audio.A: Moonlight bars 1-8 played with clear voicing — top notes audibly above the triplet engine.

pages.cleffy: Close your eyes. Listen for the top voice. From bar 1 onward. Don't listen to the triplets. Listen to whatever sits on top of them. That's the melody. It never left.

pages.figure: Bars 5-8 melody with simplified bass. Voicing label — 'bring out the top.'
pages.cleffy: Play bars 5-8 again. Right hand melody, left hand low C-sharp. This time — pinky firmer on the melody notes. Let them carry.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hear that? Same notes as yesterday. Different sound. That's what voicing does.

pages.figure: Diagram. A triplet engine labelled 'what everyone hears.' A thin gold line floating above it labelled 'what a pianist hears.'
pages.cleffy: One big idea to take with you. The audience hears the engine rolling. The pianist hears the melody floating on top. Your job — play both at once.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 172 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

real_piano: Play bar 1 twenty times — once with all three notes equal, once with the top note firmer. Feel the difference.
Then bars 5-8, melody over a held low C-sharp. Pinky firmer, thumb lighter.
Listen to a pro recording of Moonlight once this week. Now you know what to listen for.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5-6 (voicing drill) and page 12 (voiced melody).

questions.figure: Three-note triplet, top note circled in gold
questions.prompt: Which note should be slightly louder to bring out the melody?

questions.figure: A triplet C#4-E4-G#4
questions.prompt: Which finger usually plays the top note?

questions.figure: The word 'voicing' highlighted
questions.prompt: What does voicing mean?
options: Playing a note firmer so it's heard above others

questions.audio: Same bar played flat, then voiced
questions.prompt: Which version has a clearer melody line?
Play bar 1 triplet (C#4, E4, G#4) with the top note slightly firmer.
questions.accept: C#4-E4-G#4 triplet with audible emphasis on G#4

remediation.title: Voicing, one triplet at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Voicing is a new idea and a small movement. Easy to miss at first. We'll slow way down. One triplet. Flat. Then voiced. Back and forth until you can hear the difference yourself.

completion.cleffy: You just found the hidden melody. Every time you play Moonlight from here on, the melody is your focus. Not the triplets. Tomorrow — the foot. Pedal changes.

title: Moonlight 1 — pedal changes
subtitle: Act 35 · One change per bar
goal: Change the sustain pedal exactly once per bar — every time the harmony shifts underneath the melody.
piece_context: Beethoven marked Moonlight 'senza sordino' — no dampers. Pedal down, the whole time. But if you literally hold one pedal across eight bars of changing harmonies, you get mud. Modern pianists change the pedal once per bar — each time the chord underneath shifts. That's what keeps Moonlight blurred-but-not-muddy. The harmony map: C#m → C#m → B → E → A → F#m → G#7 → C#m. One pedal per bar, always.

pages.figure: Cleffy gesturing at his right foot. A pedal drawn below the piano, down-up-down-up-down. A moon above.
pages.cleffy: The pedal in Moonlight. Beethoven's note said: 'pedal down, no dampers.' Modern pianos are different. Hold one pedal for eight bars and the whole thing turns to mud. You change it. Once per bar.

pages.figure: Eight bars in a row. Each bar labelled with a chord: 1-C#m | 2-C#m | 3-B | 4-E | 5-A | 6-F#m | 7-G#7 | 8-C#m. Vertical lines between bars marked 'pedal change.'
pages.cleffy: Eight bars. Eight chords underneath the triplet engine. Look at the map. When the chord changes, the pedal changes. C-sharp minor. C-sharp minor. B. E. A. F-sharp minor. G-sharp seven. Home to C-sharp minor. Don't memorize the names today. Just see — the chord moves.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'no pedal changes — one long hold.'

audio.A: Moonlight bars 1-4 played with the pedal held down the entire time. Noticeably muddy.

pages.cleffy: Listen to this. Pedal held for four bars, no changes. Hear the mud? Every chord piled on top of the last. The sound smears.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'clean — one change per bar.'

audio.A: Same four bars, with pedal lifted and pressed again at each bar line. Clean, resonant, blurred but clear.

pages.cleffy: Now listen again. One change per bar. Still blurred. Still dreamy. But every chord comes through on its own. That's the trick.

pages.figure: Diagram. A bar with three beats marked. An arrow pointing to beat 1 — 'play the note.' A second arrow curving down to the 'and' of beat 1 — 'then press pedal.'
pages.cleffy: One small detail. The pedal doesn't press on the beat. You play the first note of the new bar. Then — a tiny moment later — the pedal comes down. That's called syncopated pedaling. You learned this back in Act 32.

pages.figure: Diagram of right foot pedaling. Sequence: 1) foot up at start of bar, 2) hands play, 3) foot presses down, 4) foot lifts at end of bar, 5) repeat.
pages.cleffy: The motion. Foot up at the start of each bar. Play the first note. Foot presses down — captures the new chord. Foot lifts at the end of the bar — clears the old chord. Down-up. Down-up. Once per bar.

pages.figure: Bar 1 of Moonlight. Triplet arpeggios C#-E-G# rolling. Pedal symbol at start, pedal-lift symbol at end. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Bar 1. C-sharp minor triplets. Play the first note, press the pedal, let the whole bar ring. Lift the pedal right at the end before the next bar starts.

pages.figure: Two bars side by side. Bar 3: B major chord underneath (B-D#-F#). Bar 4: E major chord underneath (E-G#-B). Arrows at bar lines: 'pedal changes here.'
pages.cleffy: Look at bars 3 and 4. Bar 3 — the engine shifts to a B chord. B, D-sharp, F-sharp. Bar 4 — shifts again to E. E, G-sharp, B. (Simplified — the real bar 4 is more harmonically restless.) Every bar, new chord, new pedal.

pages.figure: Two bars. Bar 1 C#m triplets. Bar 2 B major triplets. Pedal changes at the bar line. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. C-sharp minor, then B. Lift the pedal as you cross from bar 1 to bar 2. Foot up, play, foot down.

pages.figure: Full eight-bar roadmap again with pedal marks under each bar. Eight pedal-down symbols. Eight pedal-lift symbols. Caption: 'one change at every bar line.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the whole map. Eight bars. Eight pedal changes. Always at the bar line. Hands keep going. Foot does its own small dance.

pages.figure: Bars 1-4, simplified engine only. Pedal marks at each bar line. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Bars 1 through 4. Just the engine. Four pedal changes. Listen for clean chord edges — no smear between bars.

pages.figure: Bars 5-8. Melody on top, simplified engine, pedal changes at every bar line. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Bars 5 through 8. Melody returns. Same pedal rule. Change at every bar line. Three things at once now — melody, engine, pedal. Slow is fine.

pages.figure: Diagram. A sound wave. First part labelled 'blurred — pedal on.' A tiny dip at the bar line. Second part labelled 'still blurred — but new chord.' Caption: 'dreamy, not muddy.'
pages.cleffy: What you're listening for — The sound stays blurred. Still pedaled. Still dreamy. But at each bar line, the old chord drops out and the new one steps forward. That's the goal. Dreamy. Not muddy.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 173 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

real_piano: Play bars 1-4, pedal changing at every bar line. Listen for the clean edges.
Then bars 5-8, melody on top, pedal changing with the harmony.
If you can, do it in the dark. Easier to hear mud when you can't see.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11 and 12 — full bars with pedal changes.

questions.figure: Eight-bar pedal map, one change per bar
questions.prompt: How often does the pedal change in these 8 bars?

questions.figure: Pedal-lift mark at the bar line, then pedal-down mark just after
questions.prompt: When does the pedal go down on the new bar?
options: A tiny moment after the first note
At the end of the bar

questions.figure: Two versions of the same bars — one muddy, one clean
questions.prompt: What causes mud?
options: Not changing the pedal when the harmony changes

questions.audio: Same passage played two ways — held pedal vs changed pedal
questions.prompt: Which one is clean?
Play two bars of triplets — C-sharp minor then B — changing pedal at the bar line.
questions.accept: C#m triplets then B triplets with pedal change audible between

remediation.title: Pedal changes, two bars at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Pedal timing is subtle. Hands doing one thing, foot doing another. We'll walk it two bars at a time. Very slow. Listen more than play.

completion.cleffy: Pedal changes locked in. Tomorrow — all eight bars, slow, put together. The day after — the Tier 4 boss.

title: Moonlight 1 — play-through (slow)
subtitle: Act 35 · All eight bars
goal: Play all eight bars of Moonlight Sonata's opening — engine, melody, and pedal, together — very slowly.
piece_context: Four lessons of pieces: the engine (170), the melody (171), the voicing (172), the pedal (173). Today — all of it, end to end, slow. This is the rehearsal before tomorrow's boss. One clean, slow run of the full eight bars. No hurry. No showing off. Just every piece together, once.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st mvt — bars 1-8
piece.difficulty: intermediate — all elements combined, simplified, very slow

pages.figure: Cleffy at a piano. Behind him — four small icons in a row: engine, melody, top voice, pedal. All lit.
pages.cleffy: Four lessons. Four pieces of the puzzle. Today — all eight bars, all together, very slow. Rehearsal before the boss tomorrow.

pages.figure: A four-item checklist. 1) Triplet engine. 2) Melody bars 5-8. 3) Top-voice weight. 4) Pedal change every bar.
pages.cleffy: The four things to hold in mind. One — the engine rolls. Quiet. Even. Two — the melody sits on top in bars 5 through 8. Three — the top note of every triplet matters, even before the melody arrives. Four — the pedal changes at every bar line.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full 8 bars, simplified. Bars 1-4: LH C#2-C#3 octave, RH triplet engine C#4-E4-G#4 pattern. Bars 5-8: same plus melody on top — G#4 dotted rhythm.
pages.cleffy: Here it is, all eight bars laid out. Bars 1 to 4 — engine only. Bars 5 to 8 — melody enters. Engine keeps rolling underneath. Key signature — four sharps. C-sharp minor.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'all eight bars, slow — what you're about to play.'

audio.A: Moonlight bars 1-8 played simplified, slow (~50 bpm), pedaled, melody voiced.

pages.cleffy: Listen once through. Four bars of engine. Four bars of melody. Eight pedal changes. That's your whole piece today.

pages.figure: Bars 1-2 engine only. RH triplets C#-E-G#. LH low octave held. Tempo 45.
pages.cleffy: Warm up. Bars 1 and 2. Engine only. Left hand low octave. Right hand triplets. Slow.

pages.figure: Melody of bars 5-8, right hand only. Tempo 45.
pages.cleffy: Warm up the melody. Right hand alone. Long-short-short, four bars.

pages.figure: Two bars side by side — bar 4 (engine only, E chord) and bar 5 (melody G# enters, A chord underneath). Arrow between: 'melody arrives here.'
pages.cleffy: The key moment — where bar 4 becomes bar 5. Engine has been rolling for four bars. Now the melody lands. High G-sharp. Long. It sits there while the triplets keep going. That's the moment Moonlight becomes Moonlight.

pages.figure: End of bar 4 into bar 5 and 6. Engine continues; melody enters on the G-sharp. Tempo 45.
pages.cleffy: Play across the transition. End of bar 4, into 5, into 6. Keep the engine rolling. Place the melody note on top.

pages.figure: Eight-bar roadmap. Each bar labelled with its chord: C#m C#m B E A F#m G#7 C#m. Pedal-down symbol at start of each bar, pedal-lift symbol at the end.
pages.cleffy: The pedal path one more time. Eight bars, eight pedal changes. Always at the bar line. Don't overthink it. Foot up, hands play, foot down. Every bar.

pages.figure: Bars 1-4. Full engine, both hands, pedal. Tempo 45. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: First half. Bars 1 through 4. Engine only. Pedal change at every bar line.

pages.figure: Bars 5-8. Melody on top, simplified engine, pedal changes. Tempo 45. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Second half. Bars 5 through 8. Melody enters. Top voice clear. Pedal at every bar.

pages.figure: All 8 bars. Both hands, simplified engine, melody, pedal. Tempo 45. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: All eight bars. Guides on. Tempo 45. Slow is the whole point today.

pages.figure: All 8 bars. No guides. Tempo 45.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read. You play. If you stumble — slow down more. This is rehearsal.
pages.followup_cleffy: Eight bars of Moonlight, end to end. Tomorrow — same piece, as the Tier 4 boss.

pages.figure: Clock icon. A piano icon. A bed icon. Caption: 'play it twice tonight. Sleep on it.'
pages.cleffy: Tonight — play it twice. No more. No less. Then sleep on it. Tomorrow your hands will know it better than tonight. That's how this works.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 174 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

real_piano: The full eight bars, slow, once per day. Don't speed up.
If something falls apart, isolate that bar. Play it five times alone. Then go back to the full run.
Record yourself once. Listen. You'll hear what your hands can't feel yet.
virtual_only: Replay pages 12 and 13 — full run with and without guides.

questions.figure: An eight-bar map. Bars 1-4 engine, bars 5-8 melody + engine.
questions.prompt: Where does the melody enter?

questions.figure: The four-item checklist — engine, melody, voicing, pedal
questions.prompt: How many things are you keeping track of at once?

questions.figure: Tempo marking: 45 bpm
questions.prompt: Is this tempo fast or slow?

questions.audio: All eight bars played slowly, melody clearly voiced
questions.prompt: Do you hear a melody above the triplets from bar 5 onward?
Play bars 5-8 melody over the low C-sharp bass — simplified, slow.
questions.accept: Melody G#4 dotted rhythm + LH C# bass held

remediation.title: Full play-through, half-tempo
remediation.opening_cleffy: Eight bars, four things at once, is a lot. We'll take it at half-tempo. Two bars at a time. Every part checked before we combine them.

completion.cleffy: Rehearsal done. Tomorrow is the boss. Tier 4. Fifty lessons. Sleep. I'll see you there.

title: Moonlight Sonata 1st mvt (first 8)
subtitle: Tier 4 · The third summit
goal: Play the first eight bars of Beethoven's Moonlight Sonata — engine, melody, pedal — together. End of Tier 4. Halfway to graduation.
piece_context: Moonlight Sonata, first movement. Beethoven wrote it in 1801. The nickname came later — a poet said the opening reminded him of moonlight on Lake Lucerne. The name stuck. It is arguably the most famous piece of piano music ever written. Most people will never play it. Today you play its opening eight bars. Engine. Melody. Pedal. All of it. Fifty lessons of Tier 4 in your hands. Halfway to graduation.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st movement (bars 1-8)
piece.difficulty: intermediate — simplified for a student. Full triplet engine, octave bass, melody voiced, pedal changed every bar.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the top of a third pyramid. Behind him — Tiers 1, 2, 3 marked gold. Tier 4 marked gold now too. A low moon rising. Caption: 'third summit.'
pages.cleffy: Third summit. Tier 1 — Happy Birthday. Tier 2 — Für Elise theme. Tier 3 — Clair de Lune opening. Today — Moonlight. The real thing. First eight bars.

pages.figure: A sepia image. 'Sonata quasi una Fantasia, Op. 27 No. 2 — Ludwig van Beethoven, 1801.' Small caption: 'Adagio sostenuto. Si deve suonare tutto questo pezzo delicatissimamente e senza sordino.'
pages.cleffy: Beethoven wrote it in 1801. The full title is 'Sonata quasi una Fantasia' — sonata in the style of a fantasy. The nickname 'Moonlight' came later — after Beethoven was dead. A poet said the piece reminded him of moonlight on a lake. It stuck. Arguably the most famous piano piece ever written.

pages.figure: Two Italian phrases, large. 'Adagio sostenuto.' Below: 'delicatissimamente e senza sordino.'
pages.cleffy: Beethoven's instructions at the top of the page. Adagio sostenuto — slow, sustained. Delicatissimamente — most delicately. Senza sordino — without dampers. Pedal down. He was telling the player: quiet. Slow. Drowned in sound.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'opening eight bars, real recording.'

audio.A: Moonlight Sonata 1st movement, first 8 bars — at reference tempo, fully pedaled, voiced melody, from a real recording.

pages.cleffy: Listen. This is the piece you're about to play. Don't count. Don't analyze. Just listen.

pages.figure: Four panels. Panel 1: triplet engine. Panel 2: low octave bass. Panel 3: melody over engine. Panel 4: pedal changes. Each ticked off.
pages.cleffy: You have all four pieces. Triplet engine — Act 33. Low octave bass — Act 34. Melody voicing — two days ago. Pedal changes — yesterday. Today is the day they live in one pair of hands.

pages.figure: Staff with the C-sharp minor key signature — four sharps: F#, C#, G#, D#.
pages.cleffy: The key. C-sharp minor. Four sharps. F-sharp. C-sharp. G-sharp. D-sharp. A dark color. The reason Moonlight feels the way it feels. You've been reading it for five lessons now. It's familiar.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full eight bars of Moonlight simplified. LH: octaves on C#2-C#3 bar 1-2, B1-B2 bar 3, E2-E3 bar 4, A2-A3 bar 5, F#2-F#3 bar 6, G#2-G#3 bar 7, C#2-C#3 bar 8. RH: triplet arpeggios outlining each harmony, with the melody entering bar 5 (G#4 dotted rhythm line). Pedal marks at every bar line. pp. Adagio sostenuto.
pages.cleffy: The full map. Eight bars. Left-hand octaves on the bottom. Triplet engine in the middle. Melody on top from bar 5. Eight chords. Eight pedal changes. Marked pp. Very soft.

pages.figure: Bar 1. LH C#2-C#3 octave. RH triplet C#4-E4-G#4 four times. Pedal. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Warm up. Bar 1. Low octave in the left hand. Triplets in the right. Pedal down.

pages.figure: Melody bars 5-8, RH only. Tempo 50.
pages.cleffy: Warm up the melody alone. Right hand. Long-short-short. Four bars.

pages.figure: Bars 1-4. Full engine, both hands, pedal at each bar line. Tempo 50. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Bars 1 through 4. Engine only, both hands. Pedal at every bar line. Quiet. Even.

pages.figure: Bars 5-8. Engine + melody + pedal. Tempo 50. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Bars 5 through 8. Melody enters. Top voice clear. Pedal at every bar.

pages.figure: All 8 bars. Full — engine, melody, pedal. Tempo 48. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: All eight bars. Guides on. Tempo 48. This is the whole piece.

pages.figure: All 8 bars. No guides. Tempo 48.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read. You play. Slow. Quiet. Pedal changes at every bar.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Moonlight Sonata. First eight bars. In your two hands. Most adult self-teachers never get here. You did.

pages.figure: Same eight bars. Adagio sostenuto marking. Dynamic pp. One tiny hairpin over bar 5 — the melody's first entry. Tempo 48.
pages.cleffy: One more time. Now with breath. The first four bars — barely audible. The engine, humming. Bar five — the melody arrives. Let it speak. Not loud. Just — present. Adagio sostenuto. As Beethoven wrote it.
pages.followup_cleffy: This is a real, named masterpiece. You can now play the opening of one of the most important pieces in the repertoire. That's not a small thing.

pages.figure: Banner — Tier 4 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. Tier boss — take your time.

pages.figure: A pyramid showing Tier 1 (gold), Tier 2 (gold), Tier 3 (gold), Tier 4 (gold). Cleffy at the top. Seven small piece icons at his feet: Prelude in C, The Entertainer, Tarantella, Nocturne, Chopin Waltz, Für Elise full, Moonlight opening.
pages.cleffy: Look back. Fifty lessons ago, you were starting Tier 4. New keys. Sixteenth notes. Triplets. Pedal. Hand independence. Seven pieces passed through your hands in this tier. Prelude in C — Bach's arpeggios. The Entertainer — Joplin's syncopation. Tarantella — a fast Italian dance in 6/8. A phrase of the Chopin Nocturne — one of the most beautiful in music. The Chopin Waltz in A minor — waltz bass, melancholy melody. Für Elise — the full piece, not just the theme. And now — Moonlight. First eight bars. A hundred and seventy-five lessons. Three tiers and a half behind you.

pages.figure: Pyramid preview with Tier 5 highlighted. Piece thumbnails: Bach Invention No. 1, Nocturne (full), Chopin Prelude in E minor, Schumann Träumerei, Debussy Arabesque, Grieg Arietta, Clair de Lune (full).
pages.cleffy: Tier 5 — upper-intermediate. Fifty lessons ago, Tier 4 was about playing real pieces — real composers, real keys, real technique. Tier 5 is about expression — rubato, voicing, making music BREATHE. Bach Invention No. 1 — your first counterpoint. The full Nocturne — not one phrase. The whole Chopin. Chopin's Prelude in E minor. Schumann's Träumerei. Debussy's Arabesque. Grieg's Arietta. And — Clair de Lune, complete. You don't have to start tomorrow. You've earned a pause.

pages.figure: A long roadmap. 250 lessons in total. A marker at lesson 175. Caption: 'halfway to graduation.'
pages.cleffy: One more thing. You're past the halfway point. Graduation — lesson 250 — is the full Moonlight Sonata first movement. Every bar, from end to end. You've played the first eight bars already. The road from here goes somewhere real.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano, foot on pedal, low moon through a window. A candle. Caption: 'delicatissimamente.'
pages.cleffy: One small piece of Beethoven's advice. Delicatissimamente. Most delicately. He wanted this piece whispered, not spoken. Remember that the next time you sit down with Moonlight. It's not about volume. It's about care.

pages.figure: Celebration card — TIER 4 COMPLETE 👑. A big golden trophy. Cleffy under a low moon with the Moonlight score. Small text: 'halfway to graduation.'
pages.cleffy: Tier 4. Complete. You play real classical pieces now. Bach. Chopin. Beethoven. You have the opening of Moonlight in your hands. That's not practice. That's playing piano. Rest tonight. Tier 5 opens when you're ready. The moon's still up.

real_piano: Moonlight first eight bars, once a day. Very slow. Very quiet. Don't rush the tempo up yet.
Play it for someone. Turn the lights low. Don't announce what it is — let them guess.
Revisit one piece from each earlier tier — Happy Birthday, Für Elise theme, Clair de Lune opening. Notice how small they all feel now.
Listen to a full recording of Moonlight Sonata, first movement, once. You just played the opening eight bars of something 69 bars long.
virtual_only: Replay pages 13 and 14 — full run, no guides and with feel.

questions.figure: Key signature with four sharps
questions.prompt: What key is Moonlight in?

questions.figure: Tempo marking: Adagio sostenuto
questions.prompt: What does this mean?

questions.figure: Dynamic marking: pp
questions.prompt: How loud should this piece be?

questions.figure: The eight-bar pedal map
questions.prompt: How often does the pedal change?

questions.figure: Bars 5-8 — melody G-sharp with dotted rhythm
questions.prompt: How is the melody rhythm described?

questions.audio: Moonlight opening — first eight bars
questions.prompt: Do you recognize this piece?

questions.audio: Bars 1-4 (engine only) then bars 5-8 (melody enters)
questions.prompt: Where does the melody enter?

questions.audio: Two versions — one muddy (no pedal changes), one clean (pedal per bar)
questions.prompt: Which sounds right for Moonlight?
Play bar 1 of Moonlight — low C-sharp octave, triplet C#-E-G# four times, with pedal.
questions.accept: C#2/C#3 octave, four C#4-E4-G#4 triplet groups, pedaled

remediation.title: Moonlight, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Tier boss — big one. The biggest piece you've ever played. We'll break it bar by bar. One bar of engine. One bar with pedal. One bar with melody. Stitched only when each feels solid. There's no rush on this one. The piece itself is unhurried.

completion.cleffy: Tier 4, sealed. You play piano. Real piano. Real Beethoven. Real Chopin. Real Bach. A hundred and seventy-five lessons. Halfway home. Moonlight in your hands. Not the whole thing. But the opening — the part everyone knows. Rest tonight. Tier 5 whenever you're ready. The moon's not going anywhere.

title: Two voices, one brain
subtitle: Act 36 · Counterpoint intro
goal: Understand counterpoint — two independent melodies happening at the same time, one in each hand.
piece_context: Tier 5 begins. You just finished Moonlight. Now we step into music where both hands sing — each hand a singer, each with its own line. This is counterpoint. And at the end of this act, your first Bach Invention.

pages.figure: Cleffy in a quiet room. Banner behind him: TIER 5 · COUNTERPOINT · EXPRESSION · BIG PIECES. A small Moonlight sheet folded on a shelf.
pages.cleffy: Tier 5. Counterpoint. Expression. Big pieces. Moonlight is behind you. Sat in your hands for a week. Now — forward. Today a new idea. The biggest one yet.

pages.figure: Split diagram. Left side: RH with a melody line, LH with a block chord — labelled 'melody + accompaniment.' Right side: two independent melody lines, one in each clef — labelled 'counterpoint.'
pages.cleffy: Until now, most pieces had a melody hand and a helper hand. Right hand sang. Left hand held chords underneath. That's one way music works. Not the only way.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Top staff has a flowing line of eighth notes. Bottom staff has its own flowing line — different shape, different rhythm. Label: TWO VOICES.
pages.cleffy: Counterpoint. Two melodies at the same time, one in each hand. Both are tunes. Both matter. Neither is the background.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Bach — two voices, a few bars.

audio.A: Eight bars of Bach Invention in C major — both voices clear, moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: Listen. Bach. Try to follow the right hand. Then try to follow the left hand. Two tunes at once. That's the sound of counterpoint.

pages.figure: Two cartoon singers side by side. One labeled 'right hand,' the other 'left hand.' Speech bubbles with different melodic lines.
pages.cleffy: Think of it like two singers in a duet. Each one has their own line. Each one breathes on their own beat. Your two hands are the two singers.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: just the top voice.

audio.A: The top voice of the same Bach phrase, alone

pages.cleffy: Top voice alone. A complete tune on its own.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: just the bottom voice.

audio.A: The bottom voice of the same Bach phrase, alone

pages.cleffy: Bottom voice alone. Also a complete tune. Neither one is a chord. Neither one is filler.

pages.figure: Treble staff: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5. Keyboard below with those keys lit.
pages.cleffy: Play just the top voice. Seven notes. C D E F D E C. Slow.

pages.figure: Bass staff: C3 D3 E3 F3 D3 E3 C3. Keyboard below with those keys lit.
pages.cleffy: Now just the bottom voice. Same shape, two octaves down. Left hand. C D E F D E C.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Top voice eighth notes moving stepwise. Bottom voice moving in longer notes underneath. Arrows labelling: VOICE 1, VOICE 2.
pages.cleffy: On the page, counterpoint looks like two stories stacked. Top staff does one thing. Bottom staff does another. Your brain has to hold both at once.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: C5 E5. LH: C3 C3. Four notes total, in order.
pages.cleffy: Tiny taste of two voices. Play RH C, RH E, LH C, LH C. Each hand takes its turn. You're not done yet — just starting.

pages.figure: Cartoon of a head with two thought bubbles — 'right hand melody' and 'left hand melody' — both glowing at once.
pages.cleffy: One brain. Two voices. It feels impossible the first time. It's not. You will practice one voice alone. Then the other alone. Then together, slow.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 176. Two singers icon. Banner: TIER 5 · COUNTERPOINT BEGINS.
pages.cleffy: Counterpoint. Two voices, one brain. Tomorrow — making both hands equal. Neither one hiding.

real_piano: Play any short melody you know. Now play it one octave lower in the left hand, alone. Hear each hand as a tune, not an accompaniment.
Sit and listen to any Bach Invention (search 'Bach Invention No. 1'). Follow only the right hand once through. Then only the left hand. Then both.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, and 8 — listen to each voice alone, then play the top voice.

questions.figure: Grand staff with two independent melodic lines, one in each clef
questions.prompt: What is this texture called?

questions.figure: Diagram — RH melody line and LH block chords
questions.prompt: Is this counterpoint?
options: No — the left hand is a chord, not a melody

questions.figure: Two singers side by side, each with a different line
questions.prompt: In counterpoint, which hand is the main melody?

questions.audio: A Bach Invention phrase with two clear independent voices
questions.prompt: How many melody lines are happening?
Play the top-voice line: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5.
questions.accept: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 in order

remediation.title: Two voices, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Counterpoint is a new way of thinking. It takes a minute. We'll go slower. One voice. Then the other. Listening before playing.

completion.cleffy: Tier 5 is open. Counterpoint is in the room. Tomorrow — equal hands. Breaking the old habit that the left hand is the helper.

title: Equal-weight hands
subtitle: Act 36 · Counterpoint intro
goal: Play both hands at equal volume — no hand hiding, no hand shouting.
piece_context: Yesterday you met counterpoint. Today the physical part — teaching the hands to share the stage. The left hand has been the accompanist for 176 lessons. It has to unlearn that.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a balance scale. Left pan and right pan perfectly level. Banner: EQUAL WEIGHT.
pages.cleffy: New day. Old habit to break. Your left hand has spent 176 lessons being the helper. Today it stops helping and starts singing.

pages.figure: Two bar graphs side by side. Left graph: RH 80%, LH 20% — labelled 'old way: melody + accompaniment.' Right graph: RH 50%, LH 50% — labelled 'counterpoint: equal voices.'
pages.cleffy: Before — right hand loud, left hand quiet. That was right for melody plus chords. Now — both hands the same volume. Because both are melodies.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: same Bach phrase, but RH too loud.

audio.A: Bach two-voice phrase played with the right hand clearly louder than the left

pages.cleffy: Listen. Right hand wins. The left voice is mumbled. You can't follow the second tune. This is the old habit, and it ruins counterpoint.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: same phrase, balanced.

audio.A: The same Bach phrase with both voices at equal volume

pages.cleffy: Same phrase. Hands matched. Now both tunes are clear. Both are singing.

pages.figure: Cartoon hand sitting with even, relaxed weight across all five fingers — no finger collapsed, no finger over-pressing. Arrow: EVEN WEIGHT.
pages.cleffy: How to do it — the left hand presses as firmly as the right. Not softer. Not afraid. Same push, same sound.

pages.figure: Bass staff: C3 E3 G3 E3 C3. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Five notes. Play them firmly. C, E, G, E, C. Like each note is important.

pages.figure: Treble staff: C5 E5 G5 E5 C5. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Same shape, higher. Don't pound it. Match the volume you just gave the left.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Alternating notes — RH C5, LH C3, RH E5, LH E3, RH G5, LH G3. Arrows showing trade-off.
pages.cleffy: A trade-off exercise. Hands take turns. Every note the same volume — even when hands switch.

pages.figure: Keyboard with the alternating notes glowing one at a time.
pages.cleffy: Trade off. RH C, LH C, RH E, LH E, RH G, LH G. Listen — can you hear any difference between the hands? The goal: no.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Both hands play C — RH C5, LH C3 — as a single block, three times.
pages.cleffy: Both hands at once. Same note, two octaves apart. Three times. Press them perfectly together. Same volume. Listen for ONE sound, not two.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner: 'Which hand is louder?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen to a two-voice phrase. Is it balanced — or is one hand too loud?

pages.figure: Grand staff. Both hands play the same five-note shape in parallel, two octaves apart.
pages.cleffy: Both hands, same shape, same volume. Parallel. C, D, E, D, C — in both hands. Matched.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Two hands. One volume. Neither hiding.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 177. Scale tipped perfectly level. Banner: EQUAL WEIGHT.
pages.cleffy: Hands matched. Old habit on notice. Tomorrow — the eyes. Reading two staves at once.

real_piano: Play any five-note scale with both hands, two octaves apart. Listen. If the right is louder, soften it. If the left is weaker, press more. Keep matching until they sound like one sound.
Record yourself playing a two-hand phrase on your phone. Listen back. You'll hear which hand you favor — most people favor the right.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, and 10 — alone, alone, together.

questions.figure: Two bar graphs — one showing RH 80% LH 20%, the other RH 50% LH 50%
questions.prompt: Which balance is right for counterpoint?

questions.figure: Cartoon of a balanced piano hand
questions.prompt: In counterpoint, the left hand is…
options: The same volume as the right hand

questions.audio: A two-voice phrase with the right hand much louder than the left
questions.prompt: Is this balanced?
Play C5 and C3 together, three times, matched volume.
questions.accept: Both notes pressed simultaneously three times, no audible hand difference

remediation.title: Matching hands, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The left hand has spent a long time holding back. It needs permission to push. We'll do it one hand at a time, then together, very slowly.

completion.cleffy: Equal weight. Two singers, matched. Tomorrow — your eyes take on two staves at once.

title: Reading two lines at once
subtitle: Act 36 · Counterpoint intro
goal: Train the eyes to take in both staves at the same time — treble and bass, read together, not ping-ponged.
piece_context: In melody-plus-chords, you could glance at the left hand once per bar and get back to the melody. In counterpoint, both staves matter every single note. Your eyes have to change habits.

pages.figure: Cartoon eyes with arrows pointing to both staves of a grand staff at the same time. Banner: BOTH STAVES. AT ONCE.
pages.cleffy: New skill today. For your eyes. Until now — glance at the bass, get back to the treble fast. Now — both staves, all the time.

pages.figure: Grand staff. A path drawn from the treble note down to the bass, then back up — a ping-pong route. Arrow labelled: OLD HABIT.
pages.cleffy: The old way — read the treble, peek at the bass, jump back. Works for melody plus chords. The chord rarely changes. Breaks for counterpoint. The bass is a new note every beat.

pages.figure: Grand staff. A soft oval around both staves together, no ping-pong arrows. Arrow labelled: NEW HABIT — SEE THE WHOLE BAR.
pages.cleffy: New way — let both staves enter your eye at once. Your gaze sits in the middle. You see top and bottom in one look. Like watching two lanes of traffic. Not one, then the other.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Treble: C5 D5 E5 F5. Bass: C3 B2 A2 G2. Both lines moving — RH up, LH down — in parallel quarter notes.
pages.cleffy: Read this. Two lines. Top goes up — C D E F. Bottom goes down — C B A G. Try to see both movements at the same time, not one after the other.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: the four-bar two-voice phrase you just read.

audio.A: Four bars — RH ascending C D E F, LH descending C B A G, both in slow quarters

pages.cleffy: Now hear it. Two voices, moving in opposite directions. Simple.

pages.figure: Only the treble staff visible. C5 D5 E5 F5. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Top voice alone. C D E F.

pages.figure: Only the bass staff visible. C3 B2 A2 G2.
pages.cleffy: Bottom voice alone. C B A G. Downhill.

pages.figure: Close-up of a grand staff. A gaze-spot shown hovering between the two staves, with dotted lines fanning up to treble notes and down to bass notes.
pages.cleffy: Trick for the eyes — park your gaze between the two staves. Let the notes come to you from above and below. Not scanning up-down-up-down. Holding the middle.

pages.figure: Grand staff with both lines visible. Metronome marker: quarter = 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Very slow. Quarter equals fifty. First pair: C on top, C on bottom.

pages.figure: Diagram showing two arrows in a grand staff — top arrow going up, bottom arrow going down. Label: CONTRARY MOTION. Caption: hands moving in opposite directions — counterpoint's favorite trick.
pages.cleffy: A name for this. Contrary motion. Hands moving opposite directions. Counterpoint loves it — it makes the two voices stay distinct.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Longer contrary-motion phrase — RH C D E F G, LH C B A G F.
pages.cleffy: Longer phrase. Five notes each hand. RH up, LH down. Eyes on both.

pages.figure: Grand staff with groups of three notes bracketed together in both clefs. Arrows showing: SEE A GROUP, NOT A NOTE.
pages.cleffy: Old reading trick, now extra important. Don't read one note at a time. Read groups. Chunks. Your eyes take in a bar of each staff together, the way you take in a word not a letter.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Both hands moving in parallel — RH C D E F, LH C D E F, two octaves apart.
pages.cleffy: Now both hands move the same direction — parallel. Same shape, both going up. Still two voices. Still read both staves.
pages.followup_cleffy: Eyes stayed in the middle. Both staves got read. Good.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 178. Two staves with a gaze-dot hovering between them. Banner: EYES ON BOTH.
pages.cleffy: Both staves, one look. The eye trick of counterpoint. Tomorrow — the ear trick. Hear the left hand as a tune.

real_piano: Open any two-staff music you have — anything, even a hymn book. Rest your eyes between the staves. Try to read one bar of both clefs without ping-ponging. Slow.
Play a simple contrary-motion scale — both hands starting on middle C, right going up, left going down. Keep your eyes in the middle of the page.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9, 11, and 13 — two-line reading, slow.

questions.figure: Grand staff with two moving voices
questions.prompt: In counterpoint, where should your eyes rest?
options: Between the two staves, seeing both

questions.figure: Grand staff where RH moves up and LH moves down
questions.prompt: What's this called?

questions.figure: Grand staff where RH and LH both move up together
questions.prompt: What's this called?

questions.audio: A contrary-motion phrase — top voice ascending, bottom descending
questions.prompt: Are the voices moving the same direction or opposite directions?
Play contrary motion: RH C D E F going up, LH C B A G going down, together.
questions.accept: Four quarter-note pairs with RH ascending and LH descending

remediation.title: Reading two lines, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Reading two staves together is a real skill. It takes time. We'll slow down, use two notes at a time, and practice keeping the gaze between the staves.

completion.cleffy: Two lines, one look. Tomorrow — the ear learns to hear the left hand as a tune.

title: Counter-melody awareness
subtitle: Act 36 · Counterpoint intro
goal: Train the ear to hear the left-hand line as its own melody, following it consciously.
piece_context: Your eyes learned to read both staves. Your hands learned to share weight. Now the ear. Most people's ear locks onto the top of any texture. Time to widen it.

pages.figure: Cleffy cupping a hand to his ear. A grand staff behind him with the bottom staff glowing warmly. Banner: LISTEN DOWN.
pages.cleffy: Today — the ear. People hear the top of any music first. It's how ears are wired. Today we widen it. Listen down.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: a two-voice phrase.

audio.A: A Bach-style two-voice phrase, both voices equal volume, moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: Listen once. What did you follow? Most people follow the top voice. That's fine — it's louder in your head.

pages.figure: Same play button. Label: LISTEN AGAIN — FOLLOW THE BOTTOM.

audio.A: Same Bach-style two-voice phrase, unchanged

pages.cleffy: Same phrase. Don't touch the top this time. Follow only the bottom voice. Hum it in your head if it helps.

pages.figure: Two ribbon-lines stacked — top ribbon labelled MELODY, bottom ribbon labelled COUNTER-MELODY. Both colored equally.
pages.cleffy: A name for the bottom voice. Counter-melody. Not "bass." Not "accompaniment." A second tune that runs with the main one. Counter-melody — the other tune.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: just the counter-melody.

audio.A: Just the bottom voice of the Bach-style phrase, alone

pages.cleffy: Just the counter-melody alone. No top voice. See? A real tune. Singable.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: full texture — now try to pull the bottom voice forward with your ears.

audio.A: The full two-voice phrase again, both voices equal

pages.cleffy: Full thing again. Pull the bottom voice forward with your ears. Ignore the top. Let it blur. Harder. But trainable.

pages.figure: Bass staff. A counter-melody line: C3 D3 E3 C3 G3 E3 C3 — marked with phrase arrows.
pages.cleffy: The counter-melody has its own shape. Rise, turn, come home — just like any melody. Read it as a tune, not as bass notes.

pages.figure: Bass staff. C3 D3 E3 C3 G3 E3 C3. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Play the counter-melody alone. With feeling. Treat it like the main tune — because right now, it is.

pages.figure: Keyboard with LH notes glowing. Speech bubble from Cleffy: 'hum it as you play.'
pages.cleffy: Play it again. This time hum along if you can. Any pitch — just match the shape. Humming teaches the ear faster than anything.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner: 'Which voice is doing what?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. A two-voice phrase plays. Pick which voice moves more.

pages.figure: Grand staff with both voices drawn. Arrows following the shape of each — both marked with their own peak and valley. Caption: TWO STORIES, SAME TIME.
pages.cleffy: Each voice has its own story. Its own rises. Its own landings. When you play, you'll shape both. Not just the top.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH simple line: E5 E5 E5 E5. LH counter-melody: C3 D3 E3 C3. Arrow: LH LOUDER — just to feel the voice.
pages.cleffy: Now a flip. Play both hands — but make the LEFT hand the tune. Right hand, background. Left hand, sing. Strange feeling. Worth doing.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. The left hand as the tune. You can hear it now.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 179. Two ribbons of color, equally bright. Banner: TWO TUNES, HEARD.
pages.cleffy: The ear widened. Two tunes heard at once. Tomorrow — the act boss. Your first counterpoint piece. Bach.

real_piano: Pick any song with accompaniment you've played — Amazing Grace, Ode to Joy, anything. Play it with the LEFT hand louder than the right for a minute. Strange, but it teaches the ear.
Put on any recording with multiple instruments (Bach is best, but anything works). For 30 seconds, listen only to the lowest instrument. Don't let your ear drift up.
virtual_only: Replay pages 3, 5, and 12 — each one trains a different ear habit.

questions.figure: Two ribbons, top labelled MELODY and bottom labelled COUNTER-MELODY
questions.prompt: What is a counter-melody?
options: A second tune that runs with the main one

questions.figure: Grand staff with two voices
questions.prompt: In counterpoint, should the left-hand line sound like…?

questions.audio: A two-voice phrase where the bottom voice has a distinct melodic shape
questions.prompt: Does the bottom voice have a melodic shape — or is it just bass notes?
Play the counter-melody: C3 D3 E3 C3 G3 E3 C3, with shape — like it's the main tune.
questions.accept: The seven bass notes in order, with expressive phrasing

remediation.title: Hearing the counter-melody, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: The ear takes time to widen. That's normal. We'll isolate each voice, then mix them, slower.

completion.cleffy: Two tunes heard. Counter-melody no longer invisible. Tomorrow — Bach. Your first counterpoint piece.

title: Bach Invention No. 1 (bars 1–4)
subtitle: Act 36 · Your first counterpoint piece
goal: Play the opening four bars of Bach's Invention No. 1 — the subject in the right hand, imitated one bar later by the left hand.
piece_context: Act 36 boss. Your first real counterpoint piece — Bach, Invention No. 1 in C major, written around 1720 as a teaching piece for his son. We're doing only bars 1–4, very slow. This is not a race. It's your introduction to the idea of a subject — a little tune that one hand starts, and the other hand copies a moment later.

piece.title: Invention No. 1 in C major, BWV 772
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — simplified, bars 1–4 only, very slow

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a small hand-written manuscript. Banner: ACT 36 BOSS — BACH INVENTION No. 1. A quill pen resting beside him.
pages.cleffy: Act 36 boss. Johann Sebastian Bach. Around 1720. He wrote this for his son, to teach counterpoint. Today it teaches you.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left: 'what this lesson is NOT — the full Invention at tempo.' Right: 'what it IS — bars 1 to 4, very slow, the subject and its echo.'
pages.cleffy: The honest part first. Today is not the full Invention. That's a month of practice. Today — four bars. Slow. The opening subject and its first echo.

pages.figure: Treble staff, bar 1 only. Eight sixteenth-notes: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 G5. Labelled: THE SUBJECT.
pages.cleffy: The subject. A short musical phrase Bach is going to reuse the whole piece. Eight sixteenth-notes. C D E F D E C G. That's the whole idea of the Invention.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: the subject, one hand, slow.

audio.A: The eight-note subject: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 G5, played as even sixteenth-notes, slow

pages.cleffy: Listen. Eight sixteenth-notes. A rising, turning little tune that leaps up to G at the end. Remember that shape. You'll hear it again in a second — in the left hand.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 G5 glowing in order.
pages.cleffy: Play the subject. Right hand. Eight notes. C D E F D E C G. Slow and even.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1: RH plays the subject. Bar 2: LH plays the same subject, one octave lower, while RH continues above. Arrows: SUBJECT → IMITATION.
pages.cleffy: Now the trick. Bar 2 — the left hand copies. Same eight notes. One octave lower. The right hand keeps going above it. This is called imitation. One hand calls, the other answers.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: bars 1 and 2 — RH starts, LH answers.

audio.A: Bar 1: RH plays subject alone. Bar 2: LH plays same subject an octave lower while RH plays a continuing line above

pages.cleffy: Hear it. Right hand says it. Left hand answers a bar later. This is the sound of Bach. Of every fugue. Of counterpoint itself.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 D4 E4 F4 D4 E4 C4 G4 glowing — left hand, one octave lower than the RH subject.
pages.cleffy: Left hand's turn. Same eight notes. One octave down. C D E F D E C G. Middle C and below, ending up on G.

pages.figure: Treble staff, bar 2 only. A simple continuation line: G5 E5 G5 C5 — four notes — while the bass plays the subject below.
pages.cleffy: While the left hand copies the subject in bar 2, the right hand keeps moving. A simple continuation — G E G C. Just four notes. Bach wrote more. We're keeping it friendly.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G5 E5 G5 C5 glowing in order.
pages.cleffy: Right hand, bar 2 line. G E G C.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1 only. RH plays the subject; LH is silent.
pages.cleffy: Bar 1 together. Only the right hand plays the subject. Left hand rests. Very slow — quarter note equals 50.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 2 — RH plays continuation (G5 E5 G5 C5 as eighths) while LH plays subject (C4 D4 E4 F4 D4 E4 C4 G4 as sixteenths). Very slow.
pages.cleffy: Bar 2. Both hands now. Left hand plays the eight-note subject. Right hand plays its four continuation notes above. The hands are doing different things. That's counterpoint.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1 and 2 together. Metronome: 45.
pages.cleffy: Bars 1 and 2 in a row. Right hand starts alone. Left hand enters in bar 2. Right hand keeps going above. Slow. Forty-five.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 3-4 simplified — RH: E5 F5 G5 A5 F5 G5 E5 B5 (eight-note subject on E). LH: E4 held underneath. Very short, simplified.
pages.cleffy: Bars 3 and 4. Now the eight-note subject moves up — starting on E instead of C. Right hand: E F G A F G E B. Left hand holds an E underneath. Bach actually did more here. Today, the essentials.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1–4 complete, simplified. Metronome 45. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: All four bars. Guides on. Forty-five. This is Bach. Slow, simplified, and real.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Four bars of Bach. Two voices. Subject and imitation. Your first counterpoint piece. You did it.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 36 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy standing with a small Bach manuscript. Banner below: SUBJECT. IMITATION. COUNTERPOINT. A pyramid with 36/50 acts complete.
pages.cleffy: Act 36. Closed. Four bars of Bach. The subject said once, then echoed. What you learned this act — counterpoint, equal hands, two-stave reading, counter-melody awareness, subject-and-imitation. Next act — the rest of the Invention. Bars 5 through 12. The subject comes back upside down.

real_piano: Bars 1 and 2 only, every day this week. RH alone, then LH alone, then together — slow metronome. Thirty seconds each, three rounds.
Search 'Bach Invention No. 1 in C major' on YouTube. Listen once a day. Let your ear start to expect where the subject returns.
One warm-up exercise: play the eight-note subject in RH, pause a beat, then play it in LH one octave lower. Call and answer. Slow.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11, 13, and 15 — one-hand bar 1, both-hands bars 1–2, then the full four bars.

questions.figure: Treble staff with the eight-note shape C D E F D E C G
questions.prompt: What is a short musical phrase that Bach repeats throughout a piece called?

questions.figure: Grand staff. RH plays a phrase in bar 1. LH plays the same phrase, one octave lower, in bar 2.
questions.prompt: What is this technique called?

questions.figure: Bach Invention No. 1 opening — two voices, no clear melody-plus-accompaniment
questions.prompt: What kind of texture is this?

questions.audio: Bars 1 and 2 — RH plays subject in bar 1, LH plays same subject one octave lower in bar 2
questions.prompt: What happens in bar 2?
options: The left hand copies the right hand's subject

questions.audio: The eight-note subject alone
questions.prompt: How many notes are in the subject?
Play the subject: C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 G5 in the right hand, slow and even.
questions.accept: Eight notes in order, RH, even rhythm

remediation.title: Bach, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Counterpoint is a real shift in how you play. We'll take one bar at a time — subject alone, imitation alone, then the overlap. Forty bpm. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 36 sealed. Your first Bach. Your first counterpoint. You understand subject and imitation now — the engine of every fugue ever written. Next act — bars 5 through 12. The subject inverts. Turns upside down. Everything you learned here carries.

title: Invention: bars 5–12
subtitle: Act 37 · Bach Invention deeper
goal: Follow the subject of Bach's Invention No. 1 as it moves through new keys and appears inverted in bars 5 through 12.
piece_context: You met the subject in bars 1 through 4. Now Bach does what Bach does — he moves the subject through keys, flips it upside down, hands it between the voices. Today, just the shapes. Simplified. Slow.

piece.title: Invention No. 1 in C Major, BWV 772
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — melodic shapes only, bars 5–12, simplified

pages.figure: Cleffy at a harpsichord. Pages of Bach spread out. Banner: BARS 5–12.
pages.cleffy: Bars five through twelve. The subject is back. But different. Same melody — new key. Sometimes upside down.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top — the original subject going up (C-D-E-F-D-E-C). Bottom — the same shape flipped (C-B-A-G-B-A-C). An arrow between them labeled INVERSION.
pages.cleffy: Inversion. The subject, upside down. Every step up becomes a step down. Every leap flips direction. Same shape. Mirror image.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: the original subject from bars 1–2.

audio.A: The Invention No. 1 subject — C D E F D E C played cleanly, moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: First — remember the subject. Eight notes. Stepping up, dipping back, stepping up again.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: the subject inverted.

audio.A: The Invention subject inverted — C B A G B A C played cleanly, moderate tempo

pages.cleffy: Same subject. Inverted. Everything that went up now goes down. Bach does this in bar 7. Sneaky.

pages.figure: Eight boxes labeled bars 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12. Small captions — bar 5: subject in G. Bar 7: inverted. Bar 11: subject in D minor.
pages.cleffy: A map of the next eight bars. Bar five — subject in G. Bar seven — inverted. Bar eleven — minor. You don't have to play all that. You have to hear where it moves.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Bar 5 simplified: G4, A4, B4, C5, A4, B4, G4. Caption: subject, now in G.
pages.cleffy: Bar five. The subject — but starting on G instead of C. Same shape. New home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 A4 B4 C5 A4 B4 G4 glowing in order.
pages.cleffy: Play it. G, A, B, C, A, B, G. Slow. Feel how it's the same shape you know — just moved.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Bar 7 inverted shape: C5, B4, A4, G4, B4, A4, C5. Caption: subject, upside down.
pages.cleffy: Bar seven. The subject — inverted. Now it steps down where it used to step up. Your ear will recognize it and be surprised.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C5 B4 A4 G4 B4 A4 C5 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play the inversion. C, B, A, G, B, A, C. Same shape, flipped.

pages.figure: Treble staff. Bar 11 in D minor: D4, E4, F4, G4, E4, F4, D4. Caption: subject, in D minor.
pages.cleffy: Bar eleven. Subject in D minor. Same shape. Sadder key.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4 E4 F4 G4 E4 F4 D4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Play it. D, E, F, G, E, F, D. Hear the shift — the F turns the whole thing minor.

pages.figure: Staff with three phrases: bar 5 shape, bar 7 inverted, bar 11 minor. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: All three, one after the other. Hear the journey. G — flipped — minor.
pages.followup_cleffy: That was eight bars of Bach — in shape. The subject, travelling.

pages.figure: Banner. Three quick checks.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 181. A small map with three dots: G, inverted, D minor. Arrows connecting them.
pages.cleffy: Bars five through twelve. Mapped. The subject moves. The subject flips. The subject finds a minor key. Tomorrow — how to spot imitation when the hands copy each other.

real_piano: Play the bar 5 shape (G A B C A B G) every day. Then the inversion (C B A G B A C). Then the minor version (D E F G E F D).
Listen to a recording of Bach Invention No. 1. Try to hear bar 7 — the moment the subject flips upside down.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6-11 — feel the three versions of the subject

questions.figure: Two phrases. One stepping up. One stepping down — same shape flipped.
questions.prompt: What's the relationship between these two phrases?

questions.figure: The Invention subject starting on G instead of C
questions.prompt: What has happened to the subject?
options: It's been moved to a new key

questions.figure: Bar 11 of Bach Invention No. 1 — subject on D with an F natural
questions.prompt: Why does this passage sound minor?
options: It's in a minor key — D minor

questions.audio: The subject played normally, then inverted
questions.prompt: Which version is the inversion?
Play the inverted subject — C5, B4, A4, G4, B4, A4, C5.
questions.accept: C5 B4 A4 G4 B4 A4 C5 played in order

remediation.title: Bars 5–12, one shape at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Inversion is a big concept the first time. Let's take each version of the subject on its own. G first. Then flipped. Then minor. One at a time.

completion.cleffy: Bars five through twelve. The subject in three disguises. Tomorrow — spotting imitation. Watching the hands copy each other.

title: Reading imitative entries
subtitle: Act 37 · Bach Invention deeper
goal: Recognize imitation on the page — when a melody in one hand reappears in the other, a few beats later.
piece_context: Counterpoint is a conversation. One voice says something. The other voice answers — with the same thing. Today, you learn to spot the answer.

pages.figure: Two cartoon hands with speech bubbles. Left hand: 'C-D-E-F-D-E-C.' Right hand, two beats later: 'C-D-E-F-D-E-C.' Banner: IMITATION.
pages.cleffy: Imitation. One hand speaks. The other hand copies. Same notes. A beat or two later.

pages.figure: A grand staff. An arrow from a melody in the treble to the same melody appearing a bar later in the bass. Arrow labeled: THE COPY.
pages.cleffy: Here's what to look for. A melody in one hand. Then — delayed — the same shape in the other hand. Not an accident. Bach planned it.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: two hands. Right enters first. Left copies a bar later.

audio.A: A simple imitative entry — RH plays C-D-E-F, then one bar later LH plays C-D-E-F in a lower register

pages.cleffy: Listen. Two hands. Right first. Left follows — same notes, one bar later. That's imitation.

pages.figure: Opening two bars of Invention No. 1. Bar 1: RH plays the subject. Bar 2: LH plays the subject. Arrow connecting them.
pages.cleffy: Bach's Invention No. 1, bars 1 and 2. Right hand: the subject. Bar two — left hand plays the same subject. One hand answers the other. The whole piece works this way.

pages.figure: Treble staff, first voice. Keyboard with C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5 glowing.
pages.cleffy: First voice. Right hand. The subject. C, D, E, F, D, E, C.

pages.figure: Bass staff, second voice. Keyboard with C4 D4 E4 F4 D4 E4 C4 glowing.
pages.cleffy: Second voice. Left hand. Same subject — one octave down. C, D, E, F, D, E, C. Same shape.

pages.figure: Cartoon eye scanning a grand staff. Arrow bouncing between treble and bass, pointing at repeated shapes. Caption: TRAIN YOUR EYES.
pages.cleffy: The reading trick. When you see a melody, glance ahead — in the other staff — for the same shape. You won't always find it. When you do, it's imitation.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: both voices, one after the other — right then left.

audio.A: RH plays subject at C5, then LH plays same subject at C4 — back to back

pages.cleffy: Both voices. Back to back. Your ear hears the copy. Your eyes need to see it too.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Treble subject, then bass subject. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: You play both. Right hand first. Then left hand. Fourteen notes total. Slow.

pages.figure: Two phrases side by side. One labeled 'exact imitation.' The other labeled 'variant — a few notes changed.' A thought bubble: close enough.
pages.cleffy: One more thing. Imitation isn't always exact. Sometimes the second voice changes a note or two. Still imitation. Still a conversation.

pages.figure: Round counter 1 of 4. Imitation or no imitation?
pages.cleffy: Four quick rounds. Is the second voice copying the first?

pages.figure: Grand staff. Same two voices, tempo 72.
pages.cleffy: Both voices again. Slightly faster — 72. Feel the conversation.
pages.followup_cleffy: Call. Response. That's what two voices do in Bach.

pages.figure: Banner — pre-check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 182. Two speech bubbles in conversation. A scroll of Bach manuscript behind them.
pages.cleffy: Imitation. One voice speaks. The other voice copies. You can spot it now. On the page. In your ears. Tomorrow — how two voices breathe differently at the same time.

real_piano: Open any Bach score you can find. Scan for shapes that repeat across staves. Mark them in pencil if you can.
Play the subject with one hand. Then the same notes an octave lower with the other. Feel the call and response.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8-9 — both voices, back to back

questions.figure: A grand staff. RH plays C-D-E-F in bar 1. LH plays C-D-E-F in bar 2.
questions.prompt: What is this called?

questions.figure: Two voices. The second voice plays the same melody as the first, two beats later.
questions.prompt: The second voice is ___ the first.

questions.figure: A Bach Invention fragment — shape in RH, same shape appears in LH one bar later
questions.prompt: Which part of the piece are you looking at?

questions.audio: RH plays a melody. LH enters a bar later with the same melody.
questions.prompt: Is the left hand imitating the right?
Play the subject in both voices — RH first (C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5), then LH (C4 D4 E4 F4 D4 E4 C4).
questions.accept: both subjects played back-to-back, same shape

remediation.title: Imitation, spelled out
remediation.opening_cleffy: Imitation takes a minute to see on the page. Let's slow down. One voice at a time. Then side-by-side, note by note.

completion.cleffy: Imitation. Unlocked. Tomorrow — the two hands breathe at different times. Different phrases. Same moment.

title: Phrasing two voices
subtitle: Act 37 · Bach Invention deeper
goal: Shape each voice independently — one hand can crescendo while the other stays level, one can phrase off while the other phrases on.
piece_context: Until now, both hands shared a feeling. In counterpoint, they don't. Each voice is a singer. Two singers in the same room, singing different phrases, at the same time. Breathing separately.

pages.figure: Two cartoon singers. One with mouth open on a held note. The other just inhaling, about to start. Banner: TWO BREATHS.
pages.cleffy: Two voices. Each one breathes on its own clock. One can swell while the other settles.

pages.figure: A grand staff. Treble: a slur arching over four notes with crescendo. Bass: a slur ending earlier with decrescendo starting. The slurs don't line up.
pages.cleffy: Look at the slurs. The top voice arcs over four notes. The bottom voice ends its slur two beats earlier. They don't match. They're not supposed to.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: both voices shaped the same — both crescendo together.

audio.A: Two voices playing same phrase shape — both crescendo from soft to loud together, flat and uninteresting

pages.cleffy: First — both voices shaped the same way. Both swell. Both settle. Same arc. Sounds fine. A little flat.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: same notes. Right crescendos while left stays even.

audio.A: Same two voices — RH swells from soft to loud, LH stays at same volume throughout. Much more interesting texture.

pages.cleffy: Same notes. Different shaping. Right hand swells. Left stays level. Hear how much more alive that is?

pages.figure: A teacher's-card graphic. Rule 1: if one voice has the subject, it gets the attention. The other voice holds steady.
pages.cleffy: Rule one. When one voice has the subject, that voice leads. The other voice supports — quieter, steadier.

pages.figure: Rule 2: each voice has its own phrase marks. Breathe where the slur ends — not where the other hand breathes.
