pages.cleffy: Rule two. Breathe where YOUR slur ends. Not where the other hand's slur ends. Two singers. Two breaths.

pages.figure: Treble staff. RH subject with a crescendo through it. Keyboard shows C5 D5 E5 F5 D5 E5 C5.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Shape it — soft at the start, a little louder on the high F, ease back. The subject, sung.

pages.figure: Bass staff. LH holding two half-notes, even volume. C3 then G3.
pages.cleffy: Now left hand alone. Two held notes. Flat. Steady. You are the room the right hand sings in.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH subject with hairpin crescendo. LH two half-notes with flat dynamic mark. Both happen in the same four beats.
pages.cleffy: Both at once now. Right hand shaping. Left hand flat. Same four beats — two different intentions.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Same as page 9. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Very slow. Fifty on the metronome. Right hand breathes. Left hand doesn't.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH now has the subject with a crescendo. RH holds two long notes, flat.
pages.cleffy: Swap. Now left hand has the subject. Right hand is the room. Lead voice crescendos. Accompanying voice stays flat.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH subject with dynamic shaping. RH two half-notes, flat. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Left hand leads this time. The shaping moves downstairs.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two voices. One shaped. One steady. The shaped one always leads.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH with a long slur. LH with two shorter slurs. Both have phrase marks — they don't line up.
pages.cleffy: One more. Both voices shape — but differently. Right hand one long arc. Left hand two shorter arcs. Don't worry about being exact. Feel the independence.

pages.figure: Banner — pre-check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 183. Two singers standing back-to-back, each with their own breath cloud. A Bach manuscript between them.
pages.cleffy: Two voices. Two breaths. Same moment. The hand with the subject leads. The other hand gives it space. Tomorrow — we speed the whole thing up. Adagio to moderato.

real_piano: Pick any two-hand piece you know. Play it once with both hands shaping together. Then play it again — right hand shapes, left hand stays flat. Hear the difference.
Say out loud which hand has the melody as you play. When the melody moves hands, the shaping moves with it.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10 and 12 — feel both hands playing different dynamic shapes at once

questions.figure: Grand staff. Treble slur over 4 notes. Bass slur over 2 notes. They start at the same moment but end differently.
questions.prompt: What should the pianist do?
options: Breathe at the end of both slurs, at different times
Breathe at the same time for both hands

questions.figure: A passage with the subject in the right hand and accompaniment in the left
questions.prompt: Which hand should lead in dynamic shape?
options: Right hand — it has the subject

questions.figure: Two voices, both marked with hairpins, but the hairpins are in different places
questions.prompt: What does this tell you?
options: It's a mistake in the score

questions.audio: Two voices — one crescendos, the other stays flat
questions.prompt: Are the two voices shaped the same or differently?
Play the subject in the right hand with a crescendo to the F, while the left hand holds C3 then G3 flat.
questions.accept: RH plays subject with dynamic shape, LH holds steady

remediation.title: Two voices, hand by hand
remediation.opening_cleffy: This is a big idea — two voices, two intentions. Let's go hand by hand. Shape the right. Then add a flat left. Then swap. Slow and separate until it clicks.

completion.cleffy: Two voices. Two breaths. You can shape them independently now. Tomorrow — we take the Invention from slow to moderato.

title: Invention at tempo
subtitle: Act 37 · Bach Invention deeper
goal: Bring the Invention subject from adagio (60) up to moderato (96), one small step at a time.
piece_context: You have the shapes. You have the imitation. You have the two-voice independence. Now — speed. Not a leap. A climb. Six bpm at a time. If it falls apart, back down. Then up again.

pages.figure: A metronome with an arrow starting at 60 climbing through 66, 72, 80, 88, 96. Banner: ADAGIO TO MODERATO.
pages.cleffy: Today — tempo. Start slow. End moderato. Not a jump. A climb.

pages.figure: Five rungs of a ladder. Each rung labeled with a tempo — 60, 72, 80, 88, 96. Caption: only climb when the rung below is clean.
pages.cleffy: The rule. Only go faster when the current tempo is clean. Any mistake — back down a notch. Slow learning beats fast mess.

pages.figure: Three boxes: NOTES the same. RHYTHM the same. SHAPE the same. Only TEMPO changes.
pages.cleffy: The notes don't change. The rhythm doesn't change. The shape doesn't change. Only the speed. Everything else stays put.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome at 60.

audio.A: The Invention subject played at adagio, 60 bpm — very slow and clear

pages.cleffy: Sixty. Adagio. Very slow. Every note audible.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Subject in both voices. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Play at sixty. Both voices, back to back — right then left. Find your breath here before we climb.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome at 72.

audio.A: The Invention subject played at 72 bpm — noticeably more flowing

pages.cleffy: Seventy-two. Still slow. But moving now.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Metronome 72.
pages.cleffy: Your turn. Seventy-two. If you stumble, stay here. If you don't, we climb.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Metronome 80. Caption: andante.
pages.cleffy: Eighty. Andante. Walking pace. Breathe with it.

pages.figure: A thought-bubble card. Cleffy: is it clean? If yes — climb. If no — go back to 72 for one more try.
pages.cleffy: Checkpoint. Is it clean? Every note landing? Right voice leading? If yes — keep climbing. If no — drop back one rung.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Metronome 88.
pages.cleffy: Eighty-eight. Getting real now. Stay relaxed. Shoulders down.

pages.figure: Play button. Metronome at 96. Caption: MODERATO.

audio.A: The Invention subject played at moderato, 96 bpm — brisk, confident, this is the target tempo

pages.cleffy: Ninety-six. Moderato. That's our target. Hear how the shape of the subject clicks at this speed?

pages.figure: Grand staff. Metronome 96. Caption: target tempo.
pages.cleffy: Ninety-six. Moderato. Play it. Both voices. If it falls apart — no shame. Back down to eighty-eight.
pages.followup_cleffy: Moderato Bach. That's where this lives.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Metronome 96. No guides. Caption: one clean run.
pages.cleffy: One more. No guides. Ninety-six. This is the tempo we play the whole thing at tomorrow.

pages.figure: Banner — pre-check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 184. A climbed ladder with a flag at the top: 96 BPM. Bach's manuscript at the base of the ladder.
pages.cleffy: Adagio to moderato. In five steps. Tomorrow — the whole Invention. Twenty-one bars. Both hands. End to end. Don't read ahead. Just rest.

real_piano: Practice the subject with a metronome. Start at 60. Climb 6-8 bpm only when the current speed is perfect. This is how you learn all tempo-building music.
If 96 falls apart, drop to 72 and rebuild. Never force it through. Speed follows cleanness.

questions.figure: A ladder of tempos — 60, 72, 80, 88, 96
questions.prompt: What's the rule for climbing the ladder?
options: Only go up when the current tempo is clean
Start at the top and work down

questions.figure: A metronome at 96
questions.prompt: What tempo marking is this?

questions.figure: A pianist playing at 88, making mistakes
questions.prompt: What should they do?

questions.audio: The Invention subject at 60 vs 96
questions.prompt: Which tempo is moderato?
Play the subject in both voices at moderato — 96 bpm.
questions.accept: subject in both voices played cleanly at or near 96 bpm

remediation.title: Climbing, one rung at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Tempo building is hard. Most people push too fast. Let's take it one rung at a time. Three reps at each tempo before moving up. No skipping rungs.

completion.cleffy: Adagio to moderato. You climbed it. Tomorrow — the whole thing. Start to finish. Act 37 boss. Sleep on it.

title: Bach Invention No. 1 (full)
subtitle: Act 37 · Your first complete Bach
goal: Play the entire Invention No. 1 — simplified but complete, all 21 bars, both hands, moderato.
piece_context: Milestone day. You have played openings. You have played simplified pieces. Today — a whole Bach Invention, end to end. Both hands. Every bar. Simplified, yes. But complete. This is a real classical piece, played by real pianists, and now by you.

piece.title: Invention No. 1 in C Major, BWV 772
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — all 21 bars, simplified melodic shapes, moderato

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a harpsichord. A full score of the Invention open behind him. Banner: ACT 37 BOSS — INVENTION No. 1, COMPLETE.
pages.cleffy: Act thirty-seven boss. Bach Invention No. 1. All of it. Twenty-one bars. Two voices. Both hands.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Left: 'what today IS NOT — playing Bach's exact score, every inner note.' Right: 'what it IS — the full arc of his piece, simplified shapes, both hands, moderato.'
pages.cleffy: Honesty first. What this is — the full journey of the piece. Twenty-one bars of shapes, both hands. What this is not — every sixteenth note Bach wrote. That's your next year of work. The arc. The whole arc.

pages.figure: 21 boxes in a row, labeled bars 1-21. Colored regions: EXPOSITION (1-6) · DEVELOPMENT (7-14) · RETURN (15-21). Arrows for imitation across bars.
pages.cleffy: A map. Three parts. Exposition — bars one through six. Subject introduced, both hands answer. Development — seven through fourteen. Subject moves through keys. Inverted. Return — fifteen through twenty-one. Back to C. Strong finish.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: full simplified version, moderato, both voices.

audio.A: The simplified full Invention No. 1 — 21 bars at 88 bpm, both voices, melodic shapes only. Start in C, middle section in G and D minor, return to C.

pages.cleffy: Listen once. All the way through. Don't read along. Just feel the journey. It starts home. It wanders. It comes home.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1-6. RH subject, then LH subject, then continuing motion in both.
pages.cleffy: Section one. Exposition. Right hand announces the subject. Left hand copies in bar two. Both keep moving together through bar six.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1-6 simplified. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Play section one. Both hands. Start slow — eighty. The subject, then the answer, then a brief duet.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 7-14. Arrows showing subject moving to G, then inverted, then into D minor.
pages.cleffy: Section two. Development. Subject moves to G. Then flips upside down — inversion. Then finds a minor key. D minor. Sadder.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 7-14 simplified. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: The journey section. G. Inverted. D minor. Every two bars moves somewhere new.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 15-21. Subject returns in C. Final cadence with a fermata on the last C chord.
pages.cleffy: Section three. Return. Subject comes back home. C major again. Final cadence — the big resolving chord. Fermata. Done.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 15-21 simplified, ending with a C major chord with fermata. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: The homecoming. Subject returns. Final cadence. Hold that last chord.

pages.figure: Three colored bars stacked — exposition, development, return. A line drawn from start to finish. Caption: one piece.
pages.cleffy: All three sections. One piece. You have the pieces. Now the whole.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All 21 bars. Metronome 72. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Full piece. Slow. Guides on. Seventy-two. First time end to end. Breathe.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All 21 bars. Metronome 88. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Again. Eighty-eight. Guides still on. Closer to moderato. Feel it opening up.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All 21 bars. Metronome 96. No guides. A small CANDLE icon — performance mode.
pages.cleffy: Full piece. Moderato. No guides. This is performance. Twenty-one bars. Two voices. Bach. From you.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. A full Bach Invention. Both voices. Twenty-one bars. No guides. You played a real classical piece, end to end.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 37 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 37 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding a scroll that reads 'BWV 772 — played.' The Bach portrait nods in the background. A pyramid of acts showing 37/50.
pages.cleffy: Act thirty-seven. Closed. You played a complete Bach Invention. First one ever. Both voices. Twenty-one bars. Moderato. That is not a small thing. That is counterpoint — the oldest technique in Western music — coming out of your hands. Next act — all the scales. White keys. Black keys. Up and down. The foundation under everything you've played. Rest today. You earned it.

real_piano: Play the full simplified Invention every day this week. Start at 72, work up to 96. If any section cracks, slow it, rebuild, try again.
Listen to a professional recording of BWV 772 — any version. You now understand what's happening in every bar. You'll hear the subject move. You'll hear the inversion. That's literacy.
If you're feeling bold — find the real Bach score and read through it slowly. Don't try to play it. Just follow along while the recording plays.
virtual_only: Replay pages 12, 13, 14 — the full piece at three tempos
Replay page 14 once a day — the performance version, no guides

questions.figure: The 21-bar map: bars 1-6, 7-14, 15-21
questions.prompt: What are the three sections of the Invention?

questions.figure: Bar 7 — subject starting on G instead of C
questions.prompt: What has happened to the subject in the development?
options: It's been moved to a new key

questions.figure: Bar 21 — final C major chord with a fermata
questions.prompt: Why is there a fermata on the last chord?
options: To hold the final resolution — the piece ends here

questions.audio: The full simplified Invention at 88 bpm
questions.prompt: Roughly how many sections does the piece have?

questions.audio: The subject inverted vs the subject original
questions.prompt: Which version appears in the development section?
Play the full Invention No. 1, simplified, at 88 bpm or higher, both hands.
questions.accept: all 21 bars played end to end, both voices, at or near moderato

remediation.title: Invention, section by section
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. A full Bach piece. This is hard. Let's go section by section. Exposition first — three tries. Then development. Then return. Only when each section is clean do we string them together.

completion.cleffy: Act thirty-seven. Sealed. You played a complete Bach Invention. Real counterpoint. Two voices, end to end. This is a piece people play in recitals. You played it today. Tomorrow — scales. All the keys. A whole act of foundation work.

title: Scales: all white-key keys
subtitle: Act 38 · All-keys scales & arpeggios
goal: Play the major scale in seven keys that start on white notes — C, G, D, A, E, F, and B-flat — one octave, right hand.
piece_context: Act 38 opens the all-keys act. Twelve doors. Seven of them start on white keys, five on black. Today — the seven white-starting doors. You already have C, G, D, A, F, Bb from earlier acts. E is the new one. Tomorrow handles the black-key starts.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a hallway with twelve doors. Seven are open and glowing; five are still dark. Banner: ACT 38 · ALL TWELVE DOORS.
pages.cleffy: New act. Seven lessons from Moonlight prep. Twelve major keys. You've met most of them. Today — the seven that start on a white key.

pages.figure: A ring of seven labeled doors: C, G, D, A, E, F, Bb. Sharp and flat counts below each (0, 1#, 2#, 3#, 4#, 1b, 2b).
pages.cleffy: Seven white-starting keys. C — zero accidentals. G — one sharp. D — two. A — three. E — four. F — one flat. B-flat — two flats.

pages.figure: A keyboard with the whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half pattern marked between eight notes. Banner: EVERY MAJOR SCALE IS THIS SHAPE.
pages.cleffy: One formula, every key. Whole, whole, half. Whole, whole, whole, half. The shape never changes. Only the starting door changes.

pages.figure: Right hand on C major scale. Thumb-under marked between 3 and 4. Fingering 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5 labeled across eight notes.
pages.cleffy: Standard right-hand fingering — one, two, three, thumb-under, one, two, three, four, five. For six of these seven keys it works the same way. B-flat is the odd one — we'll note it when we get there.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major scale C4 to C5. Fingering labeled.
pages.cleffy: Key one. C major. All white. One, two, three, thumb-under, one, two, three, four, five.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G major scale G3 to G4. F-sharp highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Key two. G major. One sharp — F becomes F-sharp. Same fingering.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D major scale D4 to D5. F-sharp and C-sharp highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D major. Two sharps — F-sharp, C-sharp. The third finger and the thumb-under land on black keys. That's fine.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A major scale A3 to A4. F-sharp, C-sharp, G-sharp highlighted.
pages.cleffy: A major. Three sharps — F, C, G. Stay calm. The fingering is identical to C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E major scale E4 to E5. F-sharp, G-sharp, C-sharp, D-sharp highlighted.
pages.cleffy: E major. Four sharps — F, C, G, D. New key for you. Four black keys in the scale. Same fingering rule still holds.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F major scale F4 to F5. B-flat highlighted.
pages.cleffy: F major. One flat — B becomes B-flat. Same fingering.

pages.figure: Right hand over B-flat. Fingering labeled: 4-1-2-3-1-2-3-4. Caption: B-flat starts on finger 4, not 1.
pages.cleffy: One odd fingering. B-flat major. Start on finger four, not thumb. Makes the thumb-under land on C, a white key. Cleaner.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bb major scale Bb3 to Bb4. B-flat and E-flat highlighted.
pages.cleffy: B-flat major. Two flats — B-flat, E-flat. Start on finger four.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Five rounds — one scale played per round.
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. Which scale is this?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three scales back to back — C, then G, then D. Each one octave.
pages.cleffy: Three keys in a row. C, G, D. No stopping between. The home note of each becomes the jumping-off point.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 186. Seven doors now glowing. Five still dark. Caption: seven down, five to go.
pages.cleffy: Seven doors open. Tomorrow — the five that start on black keys. Different fingering story.

real_piano: One scale a day, rotating through all seven — C, G, D, A, E, F, B-flat. One octave up and down.
Once the notes feel steady, add a metronome at 60. Let the fingers find the fingering without looking.
B-flat is the outlier — start on finger four. Give it an extra pass.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5-12 — one scale at a time

questions.figure: Whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half pattern
questions.prompt: How many half-steps are in every major scale?

questions.figure: B-flat major scale on staff
questions.prompt: Which finger starts the B-flat major scale in the right hand?

questions.figure: E major key signature
questions.prompt: How many sharps does E major have?

questions.audio: A G major scale ascending
questions.prompt: Which key is this?

questions.audio: An E major scale ascending
questions.prompt: How many sharps did you hear?
Play the G major scale, one octave, right hand.
questions.accept: G3 A3 B3 C4 D4 E4 F#4 G4

remediation.title: White-key scales, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Seven keys is a stack. Let's slow down. One scale per page. Fingering first, speed never. By the end of this remediation, all seven will feel like the same shape.

completion.cleffy: Seven doors open. Five to go. Tomorrow — the black-key starts. E-flat, A-flat, D-flat, G-flat.

title: Scales: black-key keys
subtitle: Act 38 · All-keys scales & arpeggios
goal: Play the major scale starting from the four black-key homes — E-flat, A-flat, D-flat, G-flat — and learn why the fingering shifts.
piece_context: Yesterday — seven white-starting scales. Today — the four that start on black. The rule changes here. Thumbs avoid black keys when possible, so the fingering slides around. That's the whole idea.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the same hallway from L186. Seven doors glow. Four remaining doors are dark — labeled Eb, Ab, Db, Gb. Banner: THE BLACK-KEY STARTS.
pages.cleffy: Four doors left. All start on a black key. Same scale shape. Different fingering story.

pages.figure: Four labeled black keys with their flat counts: Eb (3b), Ab (4b), Db (5b), Gb (6b). Arrow showing each adds one flat to the last.
pages.cleffy: E-flat has three flats. A-flat has four. D-flat has five. G-flat has six — the most of any major key.

pages.figure: Right hand diagram. Rule at the top: THUMB STAYS OFF BLACK KEYS. Small shapes showing thumb-under landing on a nearby white key in each scale.
pages.cleffy: Here's the rule that changes the fingering. The thumb is short. It lives on white keys whenever possible. When the scale starts on black, the thumb grabs a white key later in the scale. That shifts everything.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Eb major scale. Fingering labeled: 3-1-2-3-1-2-3-4. Thumb lands on F and Bb... wait, thumb lands on F and Ab (white).
pages.cleffy: E-flat major fingering — three, one, two, three, one, two, three, four. Start on finger three. Thumb lands on F, then on C. Both white. That's the whole trick.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb major scale Eb4 to Eb5. Eb, Ab, Bb highlighted as black keys.
pages.cleffy: E-flat major. Start on finger three. Three flats — B, E, A.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Ab major scale. Fingering labeled: 3-1-2-3-1-2-3-4. Thumb on C and F.
pages.cleffy: A-flat major. Same fingering as E-flat — three, one, two, three, one, two, three, four. Thumb on C. Thumb on F. Both safely white.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Ab major scale Ab3 to Ab4.
pages.cleffy: A-flat major. Start on finger three. Four flats — B, E, A, D.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Db major scale. Fingering labeled: 2-3-1-2-3-1-2-3. Thumb on F and C.
pages.cleffy: D-flat major. New fingering — two, three, one, two, three, one, two, three. Start on finger two. Thumb still on F and C. The home note is a black key, so the thumb can't start there.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Db major scale Db4 to Db5. Five flats — Db, Eb, Gb, Ab, Bb highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D-flat major. Start on finger two. Five flats.

pages.figure: Keyboard with Gb major scale. Fingering labeled: 2-3-1-2-3-4-1-2. Thumb on B and F.
pages.cleffy: G-flat major. Six flats — the most you'll ever see. Fingering — two, three, one, two, three, four, one, two. Thumb finds the only two white keys in this scale — B (C-flat) and F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Gb major scale Gb3 to Gb4. All six flats highlighted.
pages.cleffy: G-flat major. Start on finger two. Almost all black. Feel the shape under your fingers.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Four rounds — one scale played per round.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which black-key scale is this?

pages.figure: Keyboard. E-flat then A-flat back to back. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: Two of the friendlier flat scales in a row. E-flat into A-flat. Same fingering on both.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G-flat scale again, slower. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: G-flat once more. Slow. Feel how the hand sits on the black keys. Chopin loved this key. You'll see why in a few lessons.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 187. All twelve doors now glowing. Caption: TWELVE DOORS OPEN.
pages.cleffy: All twelve doors open. Tomorrow — arpeggios. Same twelve homes, a different shape.

real_piano: One black-key scale a day, rotating E-flat, A-flat, D-flat, G-flat. Go slow enough that the fingering feels automatic.
Pair them up — E-flat then A-flat (same fingering), D-flat then G-flat (different fingering each).
If G-flat feels wild, that's normal. Six flats takes time to internalize.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5, 7, 9, 11 — one scale at a time

questions.figure: A keyboard with the thumb highlighted
questions.prompt: What's the fingering rule for black-key scales?
options: Thumb only on the first note

questions.figure: G-flat major key signature
questions.prompt: How many flats does G-flat major have?

questions.figure: E-flat major and A-flat major scales
questions.prompt: What do their right-hand fingerings have in common?

questions.audio: An E-flat major scale ascending
questions.prompt: Which key is this?
Play the E-flat major scale — one octave, right hand, starting on finger 3.
questions.accept: Eb4 F4 G4 Ab4 Bb4 C5 D5 Eb5

remediation.title: Black-key scales, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Black-key fingering is a whole new habit. Expected. We'll walk it one scale at a time, thumb-under only, no tempo pressure. The hand learns the shape by repetition, not by thinking.

completion.cleffy: Twelve doors. All open. Tomorrow — arpeggios in all twelve keys. The other foundational shape.

title: Arpeggios in all keys
subtitle: Act 38 · All-keys scales & arpeggios
goal: Play the four-note major arpeggio — root, third, fifth, octave — ascending and descending in every key.
piece_context: You did three-note arpeggios back at L163 for Moonlight. Now you widen them to four notes — add the octave on top. Twelve keys. Up and down. The second foundational shape of a pianist's hands.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding two shapes — a ladder (scale) and a wider rail (arpeggio). Banner: ARPEGGIOS · ALL TWELVE KEYS.
pages.cleffy: Second foundational shape. Scales walk. Arpeggios jump. Root, third, fifth, octave. Four notes per key.

pages.figure: Staff with C major arpeggio: C-E-G-C. Arrows showing root (1), third (3), fifth (5), octave (8).
pages.cleffy: The four-note arpeggio. One. Three. Five. Eight. Take every other note of the scale, then jump to the octave to close it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C E G C going up, then C G E C going back down. Arrows showing both directions.
pages.cleffy: Up and down. Up — one, three, five, eight. Down — eight, five, three, one. Same four notes, mirrored.

pages.figure: Right hand diagram. Fingering on four-note arpeggio: 1-2-3-5 ascending, 5-3-2-1 descending. Caption: skip finger four.
pages.cleffy: Standard right-hand fingering. Going up — one, two, three, five. Skip four. Going down — five, three, two, one. The octave gets the pinky. Clean.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C arpeggio up and down — C4 E4 G4 C5 C5 G4 E4 C4.
pages.cleffy: Key one. C major arpeggio. Up, then straight back down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G arpeggio. G-B-D-G and back.
pages.cleffy: G. G, B, D, G. All white keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D arpeggio then A arpeggio — both up and down. F# and C# highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D and A, back to back. D — D, F-sharp, A, D. A — A, C-sharp, E, A. Middle finger lands on black both times.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E arpeggio. E-G#-B-E and back.
pages.cleffy: E major arpeggio. E, G-sharp, B, E.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F arpeggio then Bb arpeggio. Bb highlighted.
pages.cleffy: F and B-flat. F — all white. B-flat — home note is black. Start B-flat on finger two.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E-flat then A-flat arpeggios. Both start on black.
pages.cleffy: E-flat and A-flat. Both homes are black. Start each on finger two. Pinky on the octave.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D-flat and G-flat arpeggios. All flats highlighted.
pages.cleffy: D-flat and G-flat. The two flat-heavy homes. D-flat — D-flat, F, A-flat, D-flat. G-flat — G-flat, B-flat, D-flat, G-flat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B arpeggio. B-D#-F#-B and back. Five sharps.
pages.cleffy: B major arpeggio. B, D-sharp, F-sharp, B. The twelfth key. Closes the circle.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Six rounds — one arpeggio per round across all keys.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Which arpeggio is this?

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, G, F arpeggios in a row, each up and down. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Three keys, back to back. C, G, F. Each up and down. Don't stop between.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 188. Twelve doors each with both shapes — a ladder and a rail — drawn inside. Banner: BOTH SHAPES, EVERY KEY.
pages.cleffy: Scales up. Arpeggios across. Tomorrow — spotting these shapes hiding inside real music.

real_piano: Four arpeggios a day, rotating through the twelve. Up and down, four notes. Metronome 60.
Pair scale + arpeggio in the same key — one scale, then one arpeggio. Feel the ladder, then the rail.
Pick your weakest key and play its arpeggio ten times slowly before bed.

questions.figure: C major arpeggio on the staff
questions.prompt: What four notes make up a four-note arpeggio?

questions.figure: Right hand on a C arpeggio
questions.prompt: Which finger plays the octave going up?

questions.figure: B major arpeggio
questions.prompt: What's the third of a B major arpeggio?

questions.audio: A G major arpeggio up and down
questions.prompt: Which arpeggio is this?
Play the D major arpeggio up and down: D, F-sharp, A, D, then back.
questions.accept: D4 F#4 A4 D5 D5 A4 F#4 D4

remediation.title: Arpeggios, four notes at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Twelve is a lot. Let's slow it. One arpeggio per page, hands-separate, no metronome. When the shape feels automatic in C, the others fall in line.

completion.cleffy: Both shapes in twelve keys — scales and arpeggios. Real pianist's hands now. Tomorrow — finding these shapes hiding inside pieces you already know.

title: Scale-in-a-phrase
subtitle: Act 38 · All-keys scales & arpeggios
goal: Recognize scale fragments hiding inside real music — and play them as one motion instead of six separate notes.
piece_context: Back at L146 you learned to see a scale as one thing. Now — seeing scale fragments in pieces you already know. Bach Invention. Clair de Lune. Once you spot them, the notes stop being individuals. The passage becomes one gesture.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a magnifying glass over a music staff. A six-note run is circled; each note has a number (1-2-3-4-5-6). Banner: SCALE HIDING IN PLAIN SIGHT.
pages.cleffy: Real music is full of scales. Not whole scales. Fragments. Four notes, five notes, six. Once you see them, the passage reads as one shape.

pages.figure: Two versions of the same passage. Top — six separate note names listed. Bottom — a bracket over all six with the label SCALE FRAGMENT.
pages.cleffy: Look at the same bar two ways. Top — six notes. Six decisions. Slow. Bottom — one scale fragment. One decision. Fast. Your eyes need the bottom view.

pages.figure: Staff — Bach Invention bar 2, right hand: the sixteenth-note run C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C. Bracket labeled: C major scale, one octave.
pages.cleffy: Bach Invention, bar two. Right hand. Eight sixteenth notes. It's not eight notes. It's the C major scale, up an octave.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Invention bar 2 — the scale run.

audio.A: A C major scale played in sixteenth notes ascending from C4 to C5 at 80 bpm

pages.cleffy: Listen for the scale under the tempo. You already know this shape. Bach just put it in a sixteenth-note suit.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major scale one octave, labeled as Invention material. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Play it as a scale. Not as eight separate notes. One shape, start to finish.

pages.figure: Staff — Clair de Lune, opening right hand: Db-Eb-F-Gb descending pattern written out. Bracket labeled: Db major scale fragment (top four notes coming down).
pages.cleffy: Clair de Lune, opening bars. Right hand. What looks like a melody is actually the top of a D-flat major scale — coming down. Debussy is using the scale as a falling shape.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Clair de Lune opening — the scale fragment.

audio.A: A descending Db major scale fragment — Ab5 Gb5 F5 Eb5 Db5 — played slowly, molto espressivo

pages.cleffy: Listen. It sounds like longing. What makes it sound like longing? It's a scale, falling. The shape does half the expressive work.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five descending notes — Ab5 Gb5 F5 Eb5 Db5. Metronome 50, soft.
pages.cleffy: Five notes, coming down. Soft. Let the scale shape carry the feel.

pages.figure: Bass staff — Bach Invention, a left-hand moment: G-A-B-C ascending. Bracket labeled: scale fragment, left hand.
pages.cleffy: Scale fragments live in the left hand too. This is the bass of the Invention — four notes, C major, going up. Same shape you've been drilling. Just written for the other hand.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G3-A3-B3-C4 ascending, left hand.
pages.cleffy: Four notes. Left hand. G to C. Hear it as a scale fragment, not four notes.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Five rounds. Each round plays a short passage — student says whether it contains a scale fragment, an arpeggio fragment, or neither.
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. Scale, arpeggio, or neither?

pages.figure: A passage on the staff. Three brackets across it: scale, leap, scale. Caption: READ IN CHUNKS, NOT NOTES.
pages.cleffy: Reading strategy. Eyes land on the first note. Ask — scale? Arpeggio? Leap? Then read the whole chunk as that shape. You'll always be one chunk ahead of your hands.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase with two scale fragments joined by a leap: C-D-E-F, then jump to G, then G-A-B-C.
pages.cleffy: Two scale fragments with a leap between. Read each fragment as one shape.

pages.figure: Full Bach Invention bar 2 notation. Metronome 55.
pages.cleffy: One more pass at the Invention run. This time — feel it as a single falling-into-place gesture. Not eight notes. One scale.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 189. A score with scale and arpeggio fragments circled throughout. Caption: once you see them, you see them everywhere.
pages.cleffy: Scales live inside pieces. So do arpeggios. Tomorrow — act boss. All twelve keys. Fast.

real_piano: Pull out any piece you know — Invention, Clair de Lune opening, Prelude in C. Find one scale fragment in it. Play that fragment as one shape.
Do the same for arpeggio fragments. Pieces are 60% scales and arpeggios in disguise.
Read a new passage with this question first: where are the scales? Eyes before fingers.
virtual_only: Replay pages 3-8 — both fragments in context

questions.figure: Eight sixteenth notes ascending: C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.figure: Five descending notes Ab-Gb-F-Eb-Db
questions.prompt: What shape is this?
options: A Db major scale fragment, falling

questions.figure: A passage with a bracketed scale fragment
questions.prompt: Why read it as one shape instead of note by note?
options: It's faster and you read further ahead

questions.audio: A short melody containing a four-note ascending scale fragment
questions.prompt: Does this phrase contain a scale fragment?
Play a C major scale fragment — C, D, E, F, G — as one shape, smooth.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 played as one gesture

remediation.title: Finding shapes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: This is a new way of seeing. Takes a few passes. We'll look at three short passages — one scale, one arpeggio, one mixed. Eyes first, fingers second.

completion.cleffy: Scales and arpeggios — not just exercises. They are the building blocks of everything. Tomorrow — act boss. All twelve keys, scales and arpeggios, fast.

title: Scale & arpeggio speed drill
subtitle: Act 38 · All-keys scales & arpeggios
goal: Play all twelve major scales and all twelve major arpeggios cleanly at a rising tempo — the act boss drill.
piece_context: Act 38 boss. Not a piece today — a drill, like L40 and L110 and L165. Four lessons of scales and arpeggios in twelve keys. Today they all have to be there, under your fingers, at speed. When you close this lesson, you have the technical vocabulary of every working pianist.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a starting gate. Twelve colored tracks stretch behind him — each labeled with a key. Banner: ACT 38 BOSS · ALL TWELVE DOORS.
pages.cleffy: Act 38 boss. Not a piece today. A drill. Twelve keys. Two shapes each. Fast.

pages.figure: Two columns. Left — WHAT THIS IS NOT — 'one long perfect performance'. Right — WHAT IT IS — 'twelve short sprints, each as clean as you can make it'.
pages.cleffy: Honest framing. This isn't one long, perfect performance of everything. It's twelve short sprints. Each as clean as you can make it, at a tempo you can hold.

pages.figure: Circle of fifths. Each key highlighted in sequence order for today's drill: C, G, D, A, E, B, F, Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb. Arrows connecting them in order.
pages.cleffy: Order of the day. Clockwise around the circle from C, then the flats counter-clockwise from F. Sharps first, flats second. Twelve doors, in a row.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major scale + arpeggio. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Door one — C. Scale, then arpeggio. Both up and down. Eighty on the click.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G major scale + arpeggio. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: G. One sharp. Same pattern.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D then A — scales only, back to back. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: D and A — scales, both. F-sharp, C-sharp in D. Add G-sharp in A.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E then B — arpeggios only. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: E and B — arpeggios. Four sharps, then five. Middle of the circle.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F and Bb — scales, both. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Flip sides. F and B-flat. Scales. F all white except B-flat. B-flat starts on finger four.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E-flat and A-flat — arpeggios. Metronome 75.
pages.cleffy: E-flat and A-flat. Black-key homes. Arpeggios. Start on finger two each time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D-flat and G-flat — arpeggios. Metronome 75.
pages.cleffy: D-flat and G-flat. The deep flat doors. Five flats, six flats. Take your time.

pages.figure: Drill banner. Six rounds — random scale or arpeggio from any key, played at tempo.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Name the key. Scale or arpeggio — doesn't matter. Just the home note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major scale + arpeggio at rising tempo — 90 bpm.
pages.cleffy: Tempo up. Ninety. Just C, both shapes. When it gets sloppy — back off.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G-D-A scales, back to back. Metronome 85.
pages.cleffy: Three keys, no stopping. G, D, A. Keep the pulse going between scales.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Final run — C scale, C arpeggio, F scale, F arpeggio. Metronome 80.
pages.cleffy: Final run. Two keys, both shapes. C then F. The act boss closes with this.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Twelve doors, all open. Act 38 — sealed.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 38 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 38 COMPLETE 🎯. All twelve doors glowing gold. Map of acts: 38/50 complete. Cleffy with a quiet fist-pump.
pages.cleffy: Act 38. Closed. Every major key. Scales and arpeggios. At tempo. You now have the technical vocabulary of a working pianist. Next act — ornaments. Trills, mordents, turns. The little decorations that make Baroque and Chopin sparkle.

real_piano: Every day, pick four of the twelve keys at random. Play scale and arpeggio in each. Rotate through all twelve across the week.
Metronome starts at 70. Raise by five only when it's genuinely clean.
Weakest key gets double attention. You know which one it is.

questions.figure: Circle of fifths with all twelve keys labeled
questions.prompt: How many major keys are there?

questions.figure: A major scale fingering 1-2-3-1-2-3-4-5 for the right hand
questions.prompt: Which keys use this exact fingering?

questions.figure: A four-note arpeggio on the staff
questions.prompt: What four scale degrees make up a major arpeggio?

questions.audio: A major scale in an unspecified key at 80 bpm
questions.prompt: Is this a scale or an arpeggio?

questions.audio: An A-flat major arpeggio at 80 bpm
questions.prompt: Scale or arpeggio?
Play the C major scale and arpeggio — one octave each, up and down — at 80 bpm.
questions.accept: C scale + C arpeggio both up and down at 80 bpm

remediation.title: Drill, slower, fewer keys at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Twelve keys is a mountain. Expected. We'll drop to 60 bpm and work four keys per page — sharps first, flats second. Three remediation sessions through this, and you'll find all twelve show up.

completion.cleffy: Act 38 sealed. Scales and arpeggios in every key, at tempo. Real pianist's hands. Next — ornaments. Trills. The decorations that make Baroque and Chopin sparkle.

title: Ornaments intro
subtitle: Act 39 · Ornaments
goal: Recognize the common ornaments on the page — trill, mordent, turn, grace note — and know what each one asks you to do.
piece_context: Act 38 gave you scales and arpeggios in every key. Act 39 adds the sparkle on top. Ornaments are the small decorations composers write above their notes — tiny extra flurries that make a simple melody feel alive. This week, you learn to spot them. The symbols. The names. What each one means before you try to play it.

pages.figure: Cleffy sprinkling tiny gold stars above a staff line. Banner: ACT 39 — ORNAMENTS.
pages.cleffy: New act. Ornaments. The little decorations that sit above a note. Tiny flurries of sound that make a plain melody sparkle.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top — a plain quarter note C. Bottom — the same C with 'tr' above it. Arrow: 'same note, now decorated.'
pages.cleffy: An ornament is a small extra motion on top of a main note. The main note is still there. The ornament adds a quick curl of sound. Baroque composers loved them. Chopin loved them. You meet them now.

pages.figure: Four boxes in a row. 1: 'tr' — trill. 2: '~' (short squiggle) — mordent. 3: '~' (longer turn symbol, like an S on its side) — turn. 4: tiny crossed eighth note — grace note.
pages.cleffy: Four common ornaments. Learn their faces today. Trill. Mordent. Turn. Grace note. Four little flurries, four different shapes on the page.

pages.figure: Staff. A whole note C with 'tr' written above it and a wavy line extending to the right. Label: TRILL.
pages.cleffy: First — the trill. Written as 'tr' above the note. Rapid alternation between the main note and the one just above it. Like shivering between two keys. Fast. Sparkling.

pages.figure: Staff. A quarter note C with a short squiggle (~) above it. Label: MORDENT. Small arrow showing main note - upper note - main note.
pages.cleffy: The mordent. A tiny squiggle above the note. Main note, upper neighbor, main note — quick. Three notes in the blink of an eye.

pages.figure: Staff. A quarter note C with a sideways-S turn symbol above it. Label: TURN. Arrow: upper - main - lower - main.
pages.cleffy: The turn. A sideways 'S' above the note. Four notes curling around the main one. Upper, main, lower, main. Like spinning around home and landing back.

pages.figure: Staff. A normal quarter note C, with a tiny crossed-out eighth note B just before it. Label: GRACE NOTE.
pages.cleffy: The grace note. A tiny note just before a main note. Crossed through. Snaps in quickly — steals only a sliver of time from the main note. You get the full lesson on it tomorrow.

pages.figure: Four play buttons labeled trill, mordent, turn, grace note. All on Middle C.

audio.A: Middle C with a trill — rapid C-D-C-D-C-D alternation over one beat
audio.B: Middle C with a mordent — quick C-D-C
audio.C: Middle C with a turn — D-C-B-C curling around the main note
audio.D: Middle C preceded by a quick B grace note — B-C

pages.cleffy: Listen to each. Same main note. Four different sparkles. Trill shivers. Mordent pops. Turn curls. Grace note flicks.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Text: 'no ornament yet — just the main note.'
pages.cleffy: Before the flurries, play the plain note. Middle C. Held. Every ornament you'll meet sits on top of a main note like this one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, D4, C4 glowing in order. Squiggle above. Text: a slow mordent — main, upper, main.
pages.cleffy: Try a mordent — slow. C, D, C. Three notes in a row. At tempo they blur into a flick. For now, just the shape.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4, C4, B3, C4 glowing in order. Turn symbol above. Text: slow turn around Middle C.
pages.cleffy: Now a turn — slow. Upper, main, lower, main. D, C, B, C. Four notes curling around home.

pages.figure: Four rows. Each row shows the symbol and a one-line definition. tr — trill (rapid alternation). ~ short — mordent (main-upper-main). S-shape — turn (upper-main-lower-main). Tiny crossed note — grace note (flick before main).
pages.cleffy: The four faces, side by side. Trill. Mordent. Turn. Grace note. You don't have to play them fast yet. Just know them on sight.

pages.figure: Round counter. Four rounds.
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Which ornament did you hear?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 191. Four tiny gold stars above a staff, each labeled with its ornament name.
pages.cleffy: Four ornaments. Four faces on the page. Tomorrow — the grace note, up close. The tiniest sparkle of the four.

real_piano: Pick any piece you know. Find a long note near a cadence. Add a mordent — main, upper, main — and hear how it decorates the line.
On Middle C, practice the four ornament shapes slowly: C (main), C-D-C (mordent), D-C-B-C (turn), B-C (grace). Learn the finger patterns.
virtual_only: Replay pages 3, 8, and 13. Say the name out loud each time you see a symbol.

questions.figure: A note with 'tr' written above it
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.figure: A quarter note with a small squiggle (~) above it
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.figure: A tiny crossed eighth note just before a regular quarter note
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.audio: A sustained note with rapid alternation between two pitches
questions.prompt: Which ornament did you just hear?

questions.audio: Three quick notes — main, upper neighbor, main
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?
Play a slow mordent on Middle C — C, D, C.
questions.accept: C4 D4 C4 played in order

remediation.title: The four ornaments, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Four new symbols in one sitting is a lot. We'll take them one at a time. Name, picture, sound, shape. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Four sparkles unlocked. Trill. Mordent. Turn. Grace note. Tomorrow — the grace note, close up.

title: The grace note
subtitle: Act 39 · Ornaments
goal: Read and play a grace note — a tiny crossed-out note with no time of its own that flicks onto the main note.
piece_context: Yesterday you met four ornaments. Today, the smallest of them. A grace note looks like a mini eighth note with a slash through its stem, glued just before a regular note. It has no time in the bar. It steals a hair of time from its neighbor, flicks, and is gone.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a tiny note on his fingertip, about to flick it onto a regular note. Banner: THE GRACE NOTE.
pages.cleffy: Today — the smallest ornament. A grace note. Tiny. Quick. Gone. So fast the bar doesn't count it.

pages.figure: Staff. A normal quarter note C4, with a tiny crossed-out eighth note B3 immediately before it. The small note is half-size, has a slash through the stem, and a beam touching the main note.
pages.cleffy: Here it is on the page. A mini eighth note with a slash through its stem. Sits just left of a regular note. Half-size. Leaning into its neighbor.

pages.figure: Two bars side by side. Left bar — four quarter notes on beats 1, 2, 3, 4. Right bar — same four notes, but the third one has a grace note glued in front. Counter below: '1 - 2 - 3 - 4' on both bars. Caption: 'the grace note doesn't add a beat.'
pages.cleffy: Key idea — a grace note has no time of its own. The bar is still four beats. The grace note steals a sliver from the main note's start. You don't count it. You just flick it.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: main note only. B: same note with a grace note flicked in front.

audio.A: A single Middle C held for one beat
audio.B: A quick B grace note flicking into a held Middle C — B-C, the B very short

pages.cleffy: First — plain C. Second — the same C, with a tiny B flicked in front. Hear how the B lives for less than a blink.

pages.figure: Zoomed timeline of one beat. Normal version: 100% filled with main note. Ornamented version: tiny sliver at the start marked 'grace' then the rest marked 'main'. Caption: same total time.
pages.cleffy: A closer look. One beat, before and after. Before — all main note. After — a sliver of grace, then the rest is main. Total time unchanged. The main note just starts a hair late.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Text: plain main note, no ornament.
pages.cleffy: Plain first. Play Middle C. Hold for one beat. Get the feel.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B3 dim-glowing (small), then C4 bright-glowing (large). Arrow: B flicks, C lands.
pages.cleffy: Now the grace note version. Slow for now. Touch B, then immediately C. Don't hold the B. Flick it.

pages.figure: Same two keys. Arrow now curved and tight — showing the flick is quick.
pages.cleffy: Same two notes. This time — fast. B is almost a click. C is the note you want to hear sing.

pages.figure: Two staves. Top — grace note F leans into a main G (from above). Bottom — grace note A leans into a main G (from below). Caption: grace notes can come from either neighbor.
pages.cleffy: Grace notes can come from above or below. The main note is still the target. The grace note is just the flick that lands there.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F4 dim-glowing (small), then E4 bright (large). Text: grace note from above.
pages.cleffy: Grace from above. Flick F, land on E. Fast.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A short melody: D4, E4, (grace F4) G4, E4. Text: grace note tucked inside a phrase.
pages.cleffy: Grace note inside a phrase now. Play D, E. On the next note, flick F into G. Then E. The phrase keeps its beat. The grace lives inside it.
pages.followup_cleffy: Good. The grace flashed by — but the phrase never broke step.

pages.figure: Staff. Four quarter notes — C, D, E, F — with a grace B3 before the C. Metronome at 60.
pages.cleffy: Now in time. Metronome at 60. Four quarter notes. The grace lives just before beat 1. Don't stretch the bar. Flick and land.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner: 'grace note — yes or no?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Yes or no — did you hear a grace note?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 192. A tiny crossed note in mid-flight, arcing into a full note. Caption: the flick that disappears.
pages.cleffy: The grace note. A flick before the main note. No time of its own. Stolen seconds. Gone. Tomorrow — the trill. More alternations, but the same idea on steroids.

real_piano: Find a familiar tune. Pick one long note. Add a grace note from the note above — flick it, then land. Keep the beat.
On Middle C, practice flicking in B, then D, then any neighbor. Fast. Don't hold the grace.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7, 8, and 12. Feel the flick, not the count.

questions.figure: A tiny crossed eighth note just before a full quarter note
questions.prompt: How much time does the grace note take from the bar?
options: None — it steals a sliver from the main note

questions.figure: A grace note shown above and a grace note shown below a main note
questions.prompt: Can a grace note come from above OR below the main note?

questions.figure: Two bars. First with plain quarter notes. Second with a grace note before the first quarter.
questions.prompt: Do both bars have the same total number of beats?

questions.audio: A Middle C with a B grace note flicked in front
questions.prompt: Grace note or plain note?

questions.audio: A plain held Middle C
questions.prompt: Grace note or plain note?
Play a grace note B3 flicking into Middle C. B is short; C is the note you hold.
questions.accept: B3 then C4 played in quick succession, B shorter than C

remediation.title: Grace notes, one flick at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: The grace note is easy to understand and hard to feel. We'll slow it down — first the two keys, then the flick.

completion.cleffy: One ornament fully yours. Tomorrow — the trill. More notes, faster, same idea.

title: Trill mechanics
subtitle: Act 39 · Ornaments
goal: Build a clean trill — rapid alternation between a main note and its upper neighbor, controlled and even.
piece_context: Yesterday — the grace note, one flick and done. Today — trills. The ornament that doesn't flick once. It alternates. Four, eight, twelve times in the space of a beat. Today isn't about speed. It's about evenness. Fast and messy means nothing. Slow and even is the goal, and speed follows.

pages.figure: Cleffy with two fingers bouncing rapidly on adjacent keys. Blur lines showing the motion. Banner: TRILL MECHANICS.
pages.cleffy: Trill day. Two notes, alternating fast. Main and upper neighbor, over and over. Sounds like shimmer.

pages.figure: Staff. A long note C4 with 'tr' above and a wavy squiggle extending to the right. Below the staff, the 'hidden' sequence: C-D-C-D-C-D-C-D over the span of that note.
pages.cleffy: A trill is a shorthand. The page shows one note with 'tr'. What you play is that note alternating with the one above it. One symbol, many notes underneath.

pages.figure: Staff. C4 with tr above — arrow pointing up to D4. Caption: trill on C = alternate with D (the nearest note up in the current key).
pages.cleffy: Which note is the neighbor? The one just above — in the current key. Trill on C in C major? Alternate with D. Trill on C in F major? Still D. The key decides the exact neighbor.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: four alternations slow. B: eight alternations medium. C: as-many-as-possible fast.

audio.A: C4 and D4 alternating slowly — C-D-C-D, four alternations over two seconds
audio.B: C4 and D4 alternating medium speed — eight alternations over one second
audio.C: C4 and D4 alternating rapidly — as many as possible in one second

pages.cleffy: Same trill. Three speeds. Slow. Medium. Fast. Listen to the evenness in each. Messy-fast sounds worse than clean-slow.

pages.figure: Cartoon right hand. Finger 1 on C, finger 2 on D. Arrows: 1 down, 2 down, 1 down, 2 down. Caption: alternate the two fingers, one at a time.
pages.cleffy: The fingers. For a trill on C with D above, use 1 and 2, or 2 and 3. One finger per key. Alternate, don't overlap. Small motion. Close to the keys.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and D4. Counter: 4 alternations total.
pages.cleffy: Four alternations. Slowly. C, D, C, D. Clear. Even. Speed is not the job yet.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same two keys. Counter: 8 alternations.
pages.cleffy: Now eight. Same tempo on the clock, but doubled up. Keep them even. If one wobbles, slow down.

pages.figure: Two columns. Left: 'messy fast' — uneven blocks of different sizes. Right: 'clean slow' — identical blocks in a row. Caption: even beats fast, every time.
pages.cleffy: The honest rule of trills. Clean and slow beats fast and messy. Every time. Speed follows evenness. Not the other way around.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same two keys. Counter: 12 alternations. Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: Twelve this time. A little faster. Light fingers. Stay close to the keys. Don't lift high.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and D4 alternating, ending on C4. Text: 'end on the main note.'
pages.cleffy: A trill lands back on the main note when it ends. Play six alternations, then one more C to finish. Hear the resolution.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 and A4. Text: trill on G — still the upper neighbor rule.
pages.cleffy: Try it on a new note. Trill on G. Neighbor is A. Eight alternations, land on G.

pages.figure: A ladder with rungs. Rung 1: '4 alternations.' Rung 2: '8 alternations.' Rung 3: '12.' Rung 4: 'as many as you can, even.' Caption: climb in order.
pages.cleffy: The trill practice ladder. Four even. Then eight. Then twelve. Then as many as you can keep even. If a rung gets wobbly — drop one rung. Fix it. Climb again.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and D4. No count shown. Timer — one second. Text: 'as many as you can, clean.'
pages.cleffy: One second. Trill on C with D. As many as you can — cleanly. If it gets muddy, stop. Quality first.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. A trill, built from the ground up. Don't chase speed yet. Evenness first. Always.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 193. A wavy line with little stars along it. Caption: two fingers, a thousand shimmers.
pages.cleffy: Trills. Alternate, don't press. Even beats fast. Tomorrow — the tricky part. The same 'tr' symbol can mean two different things, depending on the era.

real_piano: Trill on Middle C with D. Every day. Four alternations, then eight, then twelve. Stop the moment it gets uneven.
Try a trill on any long note in a piece you know. Land back on the main note when it ends.
Play with metronome. 60 bpm. Four sixteenths per beat. Feel the even click.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, 9, and 13. Climb the ladder.

questions.figure: A whole note with 'tr' and a wavy line above
questions.prompt: What does this ornament ask you to do?
options: Alternate rapidly between the main note and the one just above
Play a grace note before it

questions.figure: A trill on C in C major
questions.prompt: Which note is the upper neighbor?

questions.figure: Two trills — one uneven, one even
questions.prompt: Which is the goal?

questions.audio: A clean, even trill on C and D
questions.prompt: Was the trill even or uneven?

questions.audio: Eight alternations between two notes, ending on the lower note
questions.prompt: Did the trill land on the main note?
Play eight even alternations between C4 and D4, ending on C.
questions.accept: C4 D4 alternating eight times, landing on C4, evenly timed

remediation.title: Trills, four notes at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Trills are a feel. Fingers learn before brains do. We'll start at four alternations, very slow. Evenness first, speed much later.

completion.cleffy: A real trill — not just a word. Tomorrow — the same symbol, two different eras, two different rules.

title: Baroque vs Romantic ornaments
subtitle: Act 39 · Ornaments
goal: Understand why the same 'tr' symbol means something different in Bach than in Chopin — and how to choose which rule to follow.
piece_context: Here's a thing nobody tells beginners. The ornament symbols haven't changed in 300 years — but the rules for playing them have. A Bach trill starts differently from a Chopin trill. Both are right, for their own time. Today you learn the two dialects, and how to tell which one the music is asking for.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a crossroads. Left sign points to 'BACH · 1720'. Right sign points to 'CHOPIN · 1840'. Same 'tr' symbol floats above both. Banner: SAME SYMBOL, TWO RULES.
pages.cleffy: Here's something that trips up every pianist at some point. The 'tr' symbol has meant two different things across history. Same face on the page. Different instructions underneath.

pages.figure: Timeline. Left half — Baroque, 1600–1750, Bach's face. Right half — Romantic, 1820–1910, Chopin's face. Line between them labeled 'the rules changed here.'
pages.cleffy: Two eras. Baroque — Bach and friends. Romantic — Chopin and friends. Roughly a hundred years between them. The ornament rules shifted in that gap. And nobody updated the symbols.

pages.figure: Staff. A note with 'tr' and a small label: BAROQUE. Arrow showing: start on the upper note, ON the beat. Below: 'D C D C D C' with the first D circled and beat 1 marked under it.
pages.cleffy: Baroque rule. Bach's rule. Start on the UPPER note. ON the beat. If the printed note is C, the trill starts with D, right where beat 1 falls.

pages.figure: Staff. A note with 'tr' and a small label: ROMANTIC. Arrow showing: start on the main note, on the beat. Below: 'C D C D C D' with the first C circled and beat 1 marked.
pages.cleffy: Romantic rule. Chopin's rule. Start on the MAIN note. On the beat. If the printed note is C, the trill starts with C itself.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: Baroque trill on C. B: Romantic trill on C. Both at the same tempo.

audio.A: A Baroque trill on C — starts on D, alternates D-C-D-C-D-C, lands on C
audio.B: A Romantic trill on C — starts on C, alternates C-D-C-D-C-D, lands on C

pages.cleffy: Same written note. Same tempo. First — Baroque. Hear the upper note come first. Second — Romantic. Hear the main note come first.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4 glowing first (labeled '1st'), then C4, alternating, ending on C4. Banner: BAROQUE STYLE.
pages.cleffy: Baroque version. Start on D. Land on C. D, C, D, C, D, C, C.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing first (labeled '1st'), then D4, alternating, ending on C4. Banner: ROMANTIC STYLE.
pages.cleffy: Romantic version. Start on C. Land on C. C, D, C, D, C, D, C.

pages.figure: Two columns. Left: 'Baroque clues' — composer born before 1750, dance titles like Minuet or Gigue, harpsichord in mind. Right: 'Romantic clues' — composer born after 1800, Nocturne or Prelude, piano-as-we-know-it.
pages.cleffy: How do you know which rule to use? Look at the composer. Look at the year. Look at the title. Bach, Handel, Scarlatti — Baroque. Chopin, Schumann, Liszt — Romantic.

pages.figure: Center figure — Mozart and Haydn. Label: CLASSICAL (1750–1820). Text: 'in between — often Baroque-style, sometimes not. Modern editions usually tell you.'
pages.cleffy: Between the two eras is Classical. Mozart. Haydn. Usually follows the old Baroque rule — but not always. For anything in this gray zone, a good edition tells you what to do.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Text: play the same trill both ways — Baroque first (starts D), then Romantic (starts C). Feel which you prefer.
pages.cleffy: Play both. Right after each other. Baroque first — start on D. Then Romantic — start on C. Hear your own ears pick up the difference.

pages.figure: Table. Rows: Trill, Mordent, Turn. Columns: Baroque rule, Romantic rule. Each cell shows a small snippet.
pages.cleffy: The rule shift isn't only trills. Mordents and turns also change a little between the eras. You don't need the full table memorized today. Just know the idea — era matters.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4, C4, D4 on a Middle-C mordent. Banner: BAROQUE MORDENT — starts upper, on the beat.
pages.cleffy: A Baroque mordent on C. Starts on D. Four fast notes: D, C, D, C. On the beat. Crisp.

pages.figure: Postcard. Big text: 'When in doubt — follow the edition. When no edition — ask the year.' Caption: trust your ears after that.
pages.cleffy: The practical rule of thumb. If your sheet music says how to play it — follow that. If it doesn't — look at the year. Before 1800? Try Baroque. After? Try Romantic. Then trust your ears.

pages.figure: Four rounds. Banner: 'Which era did this trill come from?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Listen for which note starts the trill. Upper first — Baroque. Main first — Romantic.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 194. Two doors labeled 1720 and 1840, each with the same 'tr' symbol on it. Caption: same key, two rooms.
pages.cleffy: Two dialects of ornament. One symbol. Tomorrow — the act boss. All four ornaments, back to back. See, hear, play.

real_piano: If you play any Bach you know — find a trill. Try it both ways. Hear the Baroque version sound right for the piece.
Same thing in a Chopin piece — Nocturne, Waltz, anything. Romantic starts on the main note. Feel how it fits.
Pick any long note. Trill it Baroque style (upper first), then Romantic style (main first). Learn your own preference.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, and 10. The two styles, back to back.

questions.figure: A trill on C with the Baroque rule applied
questions.prompt: In the Baroque rule, which note does the trill start on?

questions.figure: A trill on C with the Romantic rule applied
questions.prompt: In the Romantic rule, which note does the trill start on?

questions.figure: Timeline — Baroque (1600–1750) and Romantic (1820–1910) with composer names
questions.prompt: Which composer would use the Baroque trill rule?

questions.audio: A trill on C that starts with D (upper note first)
questions.prompt: Baroque or Romantic?

questions.audio: A trill on C that starts with C (main note first)
questions.prompt: Baroque or Romantic?
Play a Baroque-style trill on C — start on D, alternate, land on C.
questions.accept: D4 C4 D4 C4 D4 C4 C4 — upper note first, landing on C

remediation.title: Two trills, side by side
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two rules in one lesson is a stretch. We'll take one era, then the other. Listen first, play second, label last.

completion.cleffy: Two dialects of one symbol. Baroque up top, Romantic on home. Tomorrow — act boss. All four ornaments, all you've learned.

title: Ornament drill
subtitle: Act 39 · Ornaments
goal: See, hear, and play each of the four common ornaments cleanly — trill, mordent, turn, grace note — all at moderate tempo.
piece_context: Act 39 boss. Four lessons of ornaments, sitting on top of each other. Today you prove you can see one, hear one, and play one — fast and clean. Not one piece, not one style. Every common decoration, back to back. The sparkle rack.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a gold rack holding four decorated notes — one trill, one mordent, one turn, one grace note. Banner: ACT 39 BOSS — THE ORNAMENT DRILL.
pages.cleffy: Act 39 boss. Four ornaments. See, hear, play. All of them. No piece today. Just sparkle.

pages.figure: Four panels in a row. 1: 'tr' — trill. 2: short squiggle — mordent. 3: sideways S — turn. 4: tiny crossed note — grace note. Each with a one-line definition.
pages.cleffy: Your rack of four. Trill — rapid alternation. Mordent — main, upper, main. Turn — upper, main, lower, main. Grace — tiny flick. Names on sight, first.

pages.figure: Four play buttons in a row, each labeled with an ornament name.

audio.A: A trill on Middle C with D
audio.B: A mordent on Middle C — C, D, C
audio.C: A turn around Middle C — D, C, B, C
audio.D: A B grace note flicking into Middle C

pages.cleffy: Now on the ear. Four sparkles. One after the other. Get the flavor of each before you play them.

pages.figure: Banner — 'THE TEST.' Below: each ornament as a symbol, then what to play, then at what tempo. Four rows.
pages.cleffy: Here's the plan. One page per ornament. See it. Hear it. Play it. Move on. Then all four in a row. Then a drill.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 and D4. Banner: 1 OF 4 — TRILL. Eight even alternations, land on C.
pages.cleffy: Trill. Eight alternations on C and D. Land on C. Even, then clean. Not fast.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, D4, C4 in a tight cluster. Banner: 2 OF 4 — MORDENT.
pages.cleffy: Mordent on C. Three notes — C, D, C. Quick. In one beat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4, C4, B3, C4. Banner: 3 OF 4 — TURN.
pages.cleffy: Turn around C. Four notes — D, C, B, C. Upper, main, lower, main.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B3 small/dim, then C4 big/bright. Banner: 4 OF 4 — GRACE NOTE.
pages.cleffy: Grace note into C. Flick B, land on C. B is almost a click. C sings.

pages.figure: Round counter. Eight rounds. Banner: 'which ornament did you hear?'
pages.cleffy: Eight rounds. Name the ornament.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Sequence laid out: trill (C-D eight times, C), gap, mordent (C-D-C), gap, turn (D-C-B-C), gap, grace (B-C). Tempo 80.
pages.cleffy: All four, back to back. Trill, then mordent, then turn, then grace. Short rest between each — just enough to reset.
pages.followup_cleffy: That was the whole rack. Four different shapes, four different feels, one phrase.

pages.figure: Banner: 'Baroque or Romantic?' Two columns with one clue each — composer and year.
pages.cleffy: Quick reminder from yesterday. Baroque — start on the upper note. Romantic — start on the main note. Same symbols. Two dialects.

pages.figure: Keyboard. D4 first, then alternation. Banner: BAROQUE style.
pages.cleffy: One Baroque trill to seal it in. Start on D. Land on C. Upper-first.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 first, then alternation. Banner: ROMANTIC style.
pages.cleffy: Now Romantic. Start on C. Land on C. Main-first.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 39 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 39 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding a tiny gold rack with four sparkling notes. A pyramid of acts showing 39/50 lit.
pages.cleffy: Act 39. Closed. Four ornaments. Four symbols. Two eras. All in your hands. Your melodies can sparkle now. A long note can become a trill. A simple arrival can get a grace flick. Next act — the Nocturne. Chopin's breath. Rubato, phrasing, voicing. The stuff that makes music breathe.

real_piano: Pick any piece you know. Find three long notes. Add an ornament to each — one trill, one mordent, one grace note. Keep the beat.
Run the full rack every day: eight alternations of a trill, one mordent, one turn, one grace. Five minutes, once a day.
On any Bach you're working on, try a Baroque trill. On any Chopin, try a Romantic one. Feel how the rule fits the music.
virtual_only: Replay pages 5, 6, 7, 8, and 10 — the rack, and then all four together.

questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.figure: A sideways-S symbol above a quarter note
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.figure: A tiny crossed eighth note just before a regular quarter note
questions.prompt: Which ornament is this?

questions.audio: Three quick notes — main, upper, main
questions.prompt: Which ornament?

questions.audio: Four quick notes — upper, main, lower, main
questions.prompt: Which ornament?

questions.audio: A Baroque-style trill — starts on the upper note
questions.prompt: Baroque or Romantic?
Play a mordent on Middle C — C, D, C, quickly.
questions.accept: C4 D4 C4 played in fast succession

questions.prompt: Play a turn around Middle C — D, C, B, C.
questions.accept: D4 C4 B3 C4 played in order

questions.prompt: Play eight even alternations of a trill on C and D, landing on C.
questions.accept: C4 D4 alternating eight times, landing on C4

remediation.title: Ornaments, one rack at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — expected to stretch you. Four ornaments is a lot to keep straight. We'll take one shelf at a time. Name, sound, shape, play. No rush. You'll land it.

completion.cleffy: Act 39 sealed. The rack is full. Trill. Mordent. Turn. Grace. Two eras. Four symbols. All yours. Next — Chopin. The act where the melody starts to breathe.

title: Nocturne: melody as speech
subtitle: Act 40 · Singing at the piano
goal: Read a short Nocturne phrase the way you'd read a sentence out loud — with pickup-notes, inflection, and a pause at the comma.
piece_context: Act 40 begins. Four lessons to prepare you for the full Nocturne at 200. Today, the idea that holds it all together — Chopin writes sentences. Pickup-notes like a breath before speaking. A rise in the middle like stress on a word. A small pause at the comma. A settle at the period. The right hand sings the way a voice speaks.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major (Nocturne-style phrase)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — four-bar phrase, melody only, focus on shaping

pages.figure: Cleffy reading a handwritten sentence out loud from a sheet of paper. Banner: ACT 40 — THE NOCTURNE.
pages.cleffy: Act 40. Four lessons, and you play the whole Nocturne. Today — the idea that ties it all together. Chopin writes sentences. Read them out loud.

pages.figure: A spoken sentence written out: 'Before I speak, I breathe, then I rise, then I settle.' Each phrase bracketed and labelled pickup / stress / comma / period.
pages.cleffy: Think of a line you'd say out loud. A tiny breath before the first word — that's a pickup-note. A small rise in the middle — that's the stress. A pause at the comma. A settle at the period. Chopin does all four.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 — a Nocturne-style phrase, right hand only, slowly.

audio.A: A four-bar Nocturne phrase in E-flat major — a pickup, a gentle rise to a peak note, a small pause, then a settle on the tonic. Played legato, very slow.

pages.cleffy: Listen to a phrase in the Chopin manner. A pickup. A rise. A pause. A settle. Let your ear mark each one.

pages.figure: Treble staff in E-flat. A tiny F4 eighth note at the end of bar 1, leading into Bb4 in bar 2. Arrow and label: PICKUP.
pages.cleffy: Pickup-note. F — the last breath before the sentence. Small. Light. Not on a strong beat. It sets up the B-flat that follows.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F4 dim, Bb4 bright. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: Play the pickup and the first real note. F — soft, almost a whisper. B-flat — lands. Finger 2, then finger 4.

pages.figure: Treble staff in E-flat. A seven-note phrase: F4 pickup, Bb4, D5, Eb5 (peak, stress), D5, C5, Bb4 (landing). Slur arching over all. Small breath mark after the Eb5.
pages.cleffy: The full sentence. Seven notes. F, B-flat, D, E-flat — up to the top, that's the stress. Breath mark. Small pause. D, C, B-flat — the settle. One slur. One idea.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Seven melody notes highlighted in order.
pages.cleffy: Notes only. No shape yet. Even quarters. F, B-flat, D, E-flat, D, C, B-flat.

pages.figure: The same seven-note melody. A dynamic hairpin rising up to Eb5, then falling back. Arrows: 'stress here', 'breathe here', 'land here'.
pages.cleffy: Now the inflection. Rise into the E-flat. That's your loudest note. Breathe after. A beat of space. Fall home to B-flat. That's your period.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same seven notes with a hairpin overlay. Longer hold on Eb5 and on final Bb4. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: With shape. Say the sentence. F breathes in. B-flat steps in. D climbs. E-flat sings. Small pause. Then D, C, B-flat — settle.

pages.figure: Banner: 'SAY IT OUT LOUD.' A cartoon speech bubble next to the staff.
pages.cleffy: One trick. Before you play it, say the notes out loud. 'F — B-flat — D — E-flat — D — C — B-flat.' Say it the way you want to play it. Then let your hands copy your voice.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The same seven notes, with the shape markings underneath. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Say it, then play it. Same shape both times. Your hand follows your voice.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. A phrase spoken, not just played.

pages.figure: Same melody. Guides off. Small p dynamic marking, hairpin to the peak, breath mark after. Metronome 54.
pages.cleffy: Now alone. No guides. Soft. Sing the E-flat. Sigh on the B-flat.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 196 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Cleffy closing a book titled 'Chopin — the Nocturnes.' A moon outside the window.
pages.cleffy: Chopin writes sentences. Pickup, stress, breath, settle. Tomorrow — the stretched and returned time that makes those sentences feel alive. Rubato, for real.

real_piano: Say the seven notes out loud — F, B-flat, D, E-flat, D, C, B-flat — then play them. Match the voice and the hand.
Play the phrase once a day, slow, with the rise to E-flat and the settle on B-flat.
Find any melody you know. Pick one phrase. Ask: where's the pickup, the stress, the comma, the period? Chopin didn't invent this — every tune has it.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9, 11, 12 — the three shaped passes of the phrase.

questions.figure: Seven-note phrase with the E-flat at the top
questions.prompt: Which note is the 'stress' of this sentence?

questions.figure: An eighth-note F at the end of a bar, leading into a long B-flat on beat 1 of the next bar
questions.prompt: What do we call that little F?

questions.figure: A breath mark between two notes
questions.prompt: What does a breath mark ask you to do?

questions.audio: The phrase played with a clear rise to the E-flat and a settle on the final B-flat
questions.prompt: Which note is loudest?

questions.audio: The phrase played with a pause after the peak
questions.prompt: What is the pianist doing at the pause?
Play the seven-note phrase — F, B-flat, D, E-flat, D, C, B-flat — with a rise to the E-flat and a settle on the B-flat.
questions.accept: All seven notes correct, legato, with an audible peak on Eb5 and a slower final Bb4

remediation.title: The phrase, even slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: New idea — melody as a spoken sentence. Takes a minute to click. Let's split it. Pickup. Rise. Settle. One piece at a time, very slow.

completion.cleffy: One phrase, spoken. Tomorrow — rubato. The time inside the sentence.

title: Rubato, seriously
subtitle: Act 40 · Stretch and give back
goal: Linger on peak notes and arrive slightly early on resolution notes, so every phrase stretches and returns its time — the Chopin way.
piece_context: Lesson 144 gave you simple rubato — one stretched note, a little pause. Today we go deeper. Chopin-style rubato has two jobs: linger on the peak, arrive early on the resolution. Stretch here, give it back there. The beat keeps walking underneath. Your melody just bends around it.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major (phrase study)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — one phrase, deep rubato focus

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano, pulling a rubber band gently between two hands. One end labeled 'stretch', the other 'give back'. Banner: RUBATO.
pages.cleffy: Rubato. You met it at 144. Today it goes deep. Chopin's word. It means stolen time. Two jobs. Linger on the peak. Arrive early on the resolution.

pages.figure: A horizontal timeline split in two. Left half: a note stretched wide. Arrow labeled 'linger — take from the beat'. Right half: a note crunched narrow. Arrow labeled 'give back — arrive sooner'. A metronome at the bottom ticks even the whole time.
pages.cleffy: Two halves. First — a peak note lingers. You take a little extra time on it. Second — a resolution note arrives a touch early. You give that time back. The pulse under it all never changes.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: NO RUBATO — even quarters, metronome feel.

audio.A: The seven-note Nocturne phrase from lesson 196, played metronomically even — no stretching, no settling.

pages.cleffy: First, listen without rubato. Every note gets the same time. Nothing special. Notice how flat it feels.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: WITH RUBATO — peak lingers, resolution early.

audio.A: The same phrase, this time with the E-flat lingering slightly past its beat, and the final B-flat arriving a hair early. Same average tempo.

pages.cleffy: Now with rubato. The E-flat takes a breath of extra time. The final B-flat lands a little early. Same tempo on average. But it sings now.

pages.figure: The seven-note phrase from yesterday. A bracket over the peak E-flat labeled 'LINGER +'. A bracket over the final B-flat labeled 'EARLY −'.
pages.cleffy: Where to stretch. Where to give back. Peak gets the plus. Final B-flat gets the minus. Plus and minus. It balances.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Phrase shown. The peak Eb5 is held longer than its written value. Metronome ticking 56.
pages.cleffy: Just the linger first. Play the phrase. Hold the E-flat a beat longer than it says. Metronome keeps ticking — ignore it on that one note.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same phrase. The D, C, Bb4 at the end crunched a little earlier. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Now the giving back. After the peak, the three notes home arrive a touch quicker. You're paying back the time the E-flat borrowed.
