pages.figure: A ledger drawn like a bank book. Top row: '+1 second on the E-flat'. Bottom row: '−1 second on the D-C-Bb'. A total line at the bottom: '= 0. Account balanced.'
pages.cleffy: It's an account. Take one second from the peak. Give one second back on the way home. Your total pulse stays honest. The phrase sounds human.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full phrase. Both adjustments together — peak stretched, end crunched. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Both together. Linger on the peak. Arrive early at home. The account balances.

pages.figure: A new six-note phrase in E-flat: Eb5, F5, G5, Ab5 (peak), G5, F5. Bracket over Ab5: LINGER. Bracket over last F5: EARLY.
pages.cleffy: A second phrase. Same rule. E-flat, F, G, A-flat — climb. G, F — settle. Linger on A-flat. Arrive early on F.

pages.figure: Keyboard. New six-note phrase. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Same two jobs.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Two phrases. Two balanced accounts.

pages.figure: A small warning sign: 'NOT ALL THE TIME.' Next to it a stopwatch, a heart, a metronome.
pages.cleffy: One caution. Rubato is for the important notes. The peaks. The arrivals. Not every note. If every note stretches, nothing does. A little salt. Not a pile.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The first phrase. No guides. p dynamic. Rubato brackets in place. Metronome 54.
pages.cleffy: Final pass. Phrase one. Soft. Linger. Give back. Sing.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 197 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a stopwatch in one hand and a tiny feather in the other. Banner: 'Stolen and returned.'
pages.cleffy: Stretch. Give back. Peak. Resolution. Tomorrow — the left hand. Not oom-pah-pah this time. Something wider. Like a guitar.

real_piano: Metronome at 56. Play the seven-note phrase with the metronome ticking. Linger on the E-flat by one click. Arrive a click early on the final B-flat. Listen to the pulse keep going underneath.
Play any piece you know. Pick one peak note per phrase. Stretch it just a little. Just a little.
Listen to a recording of the full Nocturne. Count the stretches in the first minute. You won't get them all. That's the point.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, 9 — linger, give back, and both.

questions.figure: A peak note marked with a plus sign, and a resolution note marked with a minus sign
questions.prompt: Which note gets the extra time?

questions.figure: A ledger showing +1 second and −1 second balancing to zero
questions.prompt: What does this picture teach about rubato?
options: Time you borrow, you give back

questions.figure: A warning sign saying 'not every note'
questions.prompt: Why not stretch every note?
options: If every note stretches, nothing does

questions.audio: The phrase played metronomically even versus the same phrase with rubato
questions.prompt: Which version uses rubato?

questions.audio: A phrase with the peak note clearly lingering longer than written
questions.prompt: Where did the pianist borrow time?
Play the phrase — F, B-flat, D, E-flat, D, C, B-flat — with the E-flat held longer and the final B-flat arriving a touch early.
questions.accept: All seven notes, with an audibly longer Eb5 and a slightly earlier final Bb4

remediation.title: Rubato, with a metronome on
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rubato's weird. Your hands want to speed up and slow down evenly. Let's put the metronome on and practice borrowing and returning time while the pulse keeps going. Slow first.

completion.cleffy: Rubato, deep. Stretch. Give back. Balance. Tomorrow — the left hand goes wide.

title: Left hand as guitar
subtitle: Act 40 · Wide-reach accompaniment
goal: Play Chopin's wide left-hand pattern — a low bass note, then a stretched chord an octave-plus higher — as one calm, guitar-like gesture.
piece_context: Act 40 continues. You know Alberti bass (148) and waltz bass (149). Chopin uses a third kind — wider than either. A single low bass note, then a two-or-three-note chord a tenth or more above it. The hand jumps, then rolls gently. Like a guitarist hitting the bass string with the thumb, then strumming up. This lesson teaches the pattern in isolation so tomorrow's pedal lesson — and Sunday's full piece — can sit on top of it.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major (LH pattern)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — left-hand-only drill, wide reach

pages.figure: Cleffy with a classical guitar slung over one shoulder and his left hand at the piano. Banner: LH AS GUITAR.
pages.cleffy: Think of a guitar for a minute. Thumb hits the low string. Fingers strum the chord above. Boom — strum. Chopin's left hand, exactly.

pages.figure: Three boxes. Left: Alberti bass — four close notes, labeled 'Act 30'. Middle: waltz bass — boom-chord-chord, labeled 'Act 30'. Right: Chopin bass — low note far below, chord far above, labeled 'TODAY'.
pages.cleffy: You've met two left-hand styles. Alberti — four close notes rolled. Classical. Waltz — boom-chord-chord. Chopin used that at 145. Today, a third. Wider than both.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Low Eb2 at the bottom as a dotted quarter. Then, an octave-and-a-third above, a stacked chord Bb3 and G4 as a dotted quarter. Arrow labeled 'a tenth — 10 notes apart'. Second arrow: 'the gap is the feature'.
pages.cleffy: Here's the shape. Low E-flat 2 — way down in the bass. Then a chord — B-flat 3 with G 4 on top — way up. The gap between them is ten notes. A tenth. The space is part of the sound.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: the Nocturne LH pattern alone, two bars, slow.

audio.A: A low Eb2 followed by the Bb3-G4 chord, then Bb3-G4 again, twice — slow, with a pedaled, ringing bass.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Low note. Chord. Chord. Notice the ring of the bass under the chords. That's the guitar sound.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Only Eb2 glowing in the lowest octave. Finger 5.
pages.cleffy: Start with the bass. E-flat 2. Finger 5 — your pinky. Deep. Slow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bb3 and G4 glowing together. Fingers 2 and 1.
pages.cleffy: Now the chord. B-flat 3, G 4 — together. Thumb on G. Finger 2 on B-flat. Light. A strum.

pages.figure: A left hand drawn with a ruler. Pinky on Eb2, thumb on Bb3. Measurement: 'a tenth, about 16 inches.'
pages.cleffy: That's a reach. Pinky to thumb. You don't grab them both at once. You bounce between them. Pinky first — then hand lifts — then thumb and two lay the chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb2 glows, then Bb3-G4 glow. Arrow shows the hand traveling up.
pages.cleffy: Bass, then chord. Separate moves. Low note — lift — land the chord on top. Don't rush the trip.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Eb2 on beat 1, then Bb3-G4 chord twice above — on beats 2 and 3. One bar labeled: 'bass, chord, chord.'
pages.cleffy: The full bar. Bass once. Chord twice. Bass, chord, chord. Three beats.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eb2 glows, then Bb3-G4, then Bb3-G4 again. Metronome 60.
pages.cleffy: One bar. Slow. Pinky deep. Then light up. Chord. Chord.

pages.figure: Bass staff. Two bars of the pattern. First bar Eb2 + Bb3-G4 chord. Second bar Bb2 + Ab3-F4 chord. Labels under each bar: 'I chord' and 'V chord'.
pages.cleffy: Two bars now. First bar sits on E-flat. Your home chord. Second bar shifts — bass on B-flat, chord changes to A-flat and F. Home, then away.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two bars of the pattern, alternating I and V. Metronome 58.
pages.cleffy: Two bars. Home, then away.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Two chords of guitar-bass. Chopin's left hand, in your hand.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same two bars. Dynamic: p. No guides. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Soft. Even. The left hand is a cradle. It rocks. It doesn't shout.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 198 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Cleffy's left hand resting on the low E-flat key. A small guitar icon tucked beside it.
pages.cleffy: Wide-reach bass. Guitar style. Pinky down. Strum up. Rock. Tomorrow — the pedal joins in. You listen for mud, you change when it blooms.

real_piano: Left hand alone. Two bars, bass-chord-chord, home and away. Very slow. Metronome at 56. Every day.
Feel the hand lift between the bass and the chord. Don't grab them at once. Let the pinky pay the deep note first, then travel up.
If the reach is hard, skip the G4 at the top — just play Bb3 alone. You can add the G back later.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 12, 13 — the pattern, the two bars, the soft pass.

questions.figure: A bass-clef bar with a low Eb2 on beat 1 and a Bb3-G4 chord on beats 2 and 3
questions.prompt: What is the pattern called?

questions.figure: A low note and a chord with a large gap between them, arrow labeled '10 notes'
questions.prompt: What interval separates the bass from the chord?

questions.figure: A hand reaching from pinky on Eb2 up to thumb on Bb3
questions.prompt: How do you play the pattern?
options: Pinky first, then lift, then lay the chord

questions.audio: A Chopin-style wide bass pattern with a ringing low note under a chord
questions.prompt: What does the left hand do first?

questions.audio: Alberti bass compared to the wide Chopin pattern
questions.prompt: Which one has a wider reach?
Play one bar of the pattern — Eb2, then Bb3-G4, then Bb3-G4 — slowly, left hand only.
questions.accept: Eb2 followed by two Bb3-G4 chords, steady, left hand only

remediation.title: The reach, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Big stretch. New shape for the hand. We'll drop the top note, shrink the bar, and rebuild. Pinky alone. Then thumb alone. Then one bar, slow.

completion.cleffy: Left hand, guitar style. Pinky deep. Strum up. Rock. Tomorrow — the pedal. Listen for mud.

title: Pedaling by ear
subtitle: Act 40 · Listen for the mud
goal: Change the sustain pedal the moment the harmony changes — cued by your ear catching 'mud,' not by a printed symbol.
piece_context: Acts 32 gave you the basics of pedal. You know heel-down, toe-down, and the pedal-with-each-chord rule (157). You know what mud sounds like (159). Today you stop reading pedal marks and start hearing when to change. The cue comes from the sound itself — the moment it blooms fuzzy, you lift, and the next chord clears it. Essential for the Nocturne tomorrow, where the pedal changes can be printed, or not.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major (pedaling study)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — left-hand pattern with pedal cued by ear

pages.figure: Cleffy with one hand cupped by his ear, the other foot on a pedal. Banner: PEDAL BY EAR.
pages.cleffy: Lesson 157 told you to change pedal when the harmony changes. Today's the ear part. You won't read the pedal marks. You'll hear them.

pages.figure: A sound-wave picture. Clean at the left. Around the middle it gets 'fuzzy' — many overlapping frequencies. Arrow labeled 'mud — this is the signal.' Then a pedal change. The sound clears again.
pages.cleffy: The signal is mud. Two different chords held at the same time through one pedal — they smear. Mud is the ear's alarm bell. When you hear it, you lift.

pages.figure: Two play buttons side by side. Left: CLEAN. Right: MUDDY.

audio.A: Two chords played, the pedal changed cleanly between them
audio.B: The same two chords, the pedal held through both — a muddy blur

pages.cleffy: Two examples. First — the pedal changes. Each chord rings clean. Second — the pedal holds. Two chords stack. Mud. Hear the difference before you play anything.

pages.figure: A timeline. Chord lands on beat 1. Just after, the pedal lifts and presses again. A tiny gap labeled 'overlap — barely.' The harmonic symbol changes above each chord.
pages.cleffy: The rule. The old chord ends. The new chord lands. Right after the new chord, your foot lifts and presses. Tiny overlap. The new chord catches. The old one dies.

pages.figure: Play button. Four chords, each with a pedal change right after. Caption: 'listen for the lift.'

audio.A: Four chords — I, V, vi, IV in E-flat. Pedal lifted and re-pressed right after each new chord. Clean throughout.

pages.cleffy: Four chords. Four changes. Right after each new chord, the pedal flicks. No mud.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Just a Bb3 chord repeating twice. Below it, a pedal icon. Arrow: 'press, lift right after the chord, press again.'
pages.cleffy: Foot drill. Only the right hand. Play the chord. Press pedal. Play it again. Lift and re-press right after. Listen for clean.

pages.figure: A stack of chords, each new one piled on. Each new one makes the 'mud' bar wider. Arrow: 'the longer you wait, the louder the mud.'
pages.cleffy: One more thing. The longer you wait to change pedal, the louder the mud gets. Change as soon as the new chord lands. Not a second later.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four-chord progression in E-flat: Eb-G-Bb, Bb-D-F, C-Eb-G, Ab-C-Eb. Pedal changes right after each chord.
pages.cleffy: Four chords, RH. Change pedal right after each. If you hear any blur — that's your signal to lift sooner next time.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH pattern from yesterday — Eb2, then Bb3-G4 chord. Pedal symbol under. Arrow: 'one pedal per bar — change when the bass changes.'
pages.cleffy: Back to yesterday's pattern. One pedal per bar. Press down when the bass lands. Lift and re-press when the new bass lands.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Yesterday's two-bar pattern. LH only. Pedal changes at the start of bar 2. Metronome 58.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. Pedal on. Bar 1 — pedal down under E-flat bass. Bar 2 — as the B-flat bass lands, flick the pedal. Listen.
pages.followup_cleffy: Did the bar change feel clean? Or did it blur? If it blurred — flick sooner. Right on the new bass.

pages.figure: A banner: 'YOUR EAR IS THE CONDUCTOR.' Below: three small rules. 'Mud = change.' 'Clean = wait.' 'When in doubt, less pedal.'
pages.cleffy: One rule from now on. Your ear is the boss. Not the printed page. Not the metronome. Mud — change. Clean — wait. When in doubt, less pedal, not more.

pages.figure: Round counter. Five rounds. Banner: 'was that muddy or clean?'
pages.cleffy: Five rounds. Clean or muddy? Trust your ear.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two bars of the Nocturne LH pattern. Pedal flicks on every bar change. No guides. Dynamic p. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: Final pass. LH and pedal together. Soft. Clean. Each bar its own chord.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 199 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Cleffy with headphones on, one foot hovering over the sustain pedal.
pages.cleffy: Ear in charge. Mud — flick. Clean — hold. Tomorrow — the whole Nocturne. Everything you've built this act, in one piece.

real_piano: Play any four-chord progression you know. Pedal each one. Stop the second you hear blur. Flick and start again.
Record yourself playing two bars of the Nocturne bass with pedal. Listen back. Can you hear the bar change? If it smears, you waited too long.
Some pianists pedal less than the score says. Some pedal more. Your ear is the tiebreaker.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8, 10, 12, 13 — change on time, LH with pedal, mud-spot drill, final pass.

questions.figure: A sound-wave picture with a fuzzy blur labeled 'mud'
questions.prompt: What does mud mean at the piano?
options: Two chords are sounding through one pedal

questions.figure: A timeline showing a chord landing, then the pedal flicking right after
questions.prompt: When do you change pedal?
options: Right after the new chord lands

questions.figure: A banner reading 'your ear is the conductor'
questions.prompt: What decides when to change pedal?

questions.audio: Two chords played with a clean pedal change between them
questions.prompt: Is this clean or muddy?

questions.audio: Two chords played through one held pedal — a smear
questions.prompt: Is this clean or muddy?
Play two bars of the Nocturne LH pattern with the pedal — flicking when the bass changes.
questions.accept: The two-bar pattern played left hand only with a clean pedal change on the second bar's bass

remediation.title: Pedal, one flick at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Foot timing takes a minute to lock in. We'll drop the left hand and just work the foot — chord, lift, press, chord. Tiny drill. Then add the hand back.

completion.cleffy: Pedal, by ear. Mud — flick. Clean — hold. Tomorrow — the whole Nocturne.

title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 (full)
subtitle: Tier 5 · Mid-boss — your first complete Chopin
goal: Read and play Chopin's Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 — simplified, complete — A section, B section, and the return. Legato, rubato, wide bass, pedal by ear.
piece_context: Two hundred lessons in. Halfway through Tier 5. This is a mid-boss — not a tier-closer (that's 225 with Clair de Lune) but a milestone big enough to count. Your first whole Chopin Nocturne. Simplified, yes. Shorter than the real thing, yes. But whole — A section, B section, A section back. The four tools from this act all come together: melody-as-speech, rubato, guitar-bass, pedal by ear. The original is in 12/8 felt as slow 4. We'll keep that feel, simplify the rhythm so it's readable, and hold the key at E-flat major where you've been living all act.

piece.title: Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2 in E-flat major — simplified, complete
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — simplified, complete A-B-A form, roughly 16 bars

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano under a window. Full moon. A portrait of Chopin above the instrument. Banner: LESSON 200 — MID-BOSS — NOCTURNE COMPLETE.
pages.cleffy: Lesson 200. Halfway through Tier 5. Halfway through this road. Today — your first complete Chopin. The whole Nocturne Op. 9 No. 2. Simplified. But whole.

pages.figure: Three boxes labeled A, B, A. A bracket under all three: 'THE WHOLE PIECE.' Small text: 'simplified — 16 bars, E-flat major.'
pages.cleffy: A, B, A. Three sections. A — the famous first phrase. You met this at 145. B — the middle. New melody, a little more motion. A — the first phrase comes back home. Simplified. About 16 bars. About 90 seconds of music.

pages.figure: Four tool icons in a row: a speech bubble (melody-as-speech), a stopwatch with stretch marks (rubato), a guitar (wide LH), a foot on a pedal (pedal by ear). Banner: 'YOU HAVE EVERYTHING.'
pages.cleffy: Four tools. Act 40 built them. Read the melody like speech. Stretch the peaks, arrive early on landings. Left hand wide and rocking. Pedal when your ear catches mud. You have everything. Today we use it.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'The full piece, simplified, slow — one listen.'

audio.A: The complete simplified Nocturne — A section melody over wide bass, a B section that climbs and returns, and the A section coming home — played slowly in E-flat major, about 90 seconds, with real rubato and pedal

pages.cleffy: Listen once. The whole piece. Mark the sections in your head as they pass. A, then B, then A again. Notice when the peaks stretch. Notice the bass rocking underneath.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. RH: the opening Nocturne melody — Bb4, G4, Bb4, Ab4, G4, Ab4, Bb4, C5, Bb4 (first phrase), then F4, Bb4, D5, Eb5, D5, C5, Bb4 (second phrase — from 196). LH: Eb2-Bb3G4-Bb3G4 in each bar (from 198). Labels: PHRASE 1, PHRASE 2.
pages.cleffy: A section. Four bars. Two phrases. Phrase 1 — the one from lesson 145. Nine notes ending on B-flat. Phrase 2 — the one from 196. Seven notes, up to E-flat, home to B-flat. Two sentences, one paragraph.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Sixteen right-hand notes — both A-section phrases, in order. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: RH only. Section A. Phrase 1, breath, phrase 2. Legato throughout.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Section A, both hands. LH: bass-chord-chord pattern under each bar. RH: the melody. Guides on. Metronome 54.
pages.cleffy: Both hands, section A. Slow. Left hand rocks. Right hand sings. Peaks linger. Landings arrive. (Simplified — the real Nocturne has more chord beats per bar.)

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four new bars in E-flat. RH: a more active line climbing from C5 up to F5 and back — C5, D5, Eb5, F5, Eb5, D5, C5, Bb4. LH: continues the wide bass, now shifting through Ab-Eb-Bb chords. Label: B SECTION.
pages.cleffy: B section. New melody. More motion now. The line walks up — C, D, E-flat, F — then settles back down. Left hand keeps rocking, but the chords move. A-flat here. E-flat there. Back to home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Eight RH notes of the B section climb-and-return. Metronome 56.
pages.cleffy: RH only. Section B. Walk up to F. Walk home to B-flat.

pages.figure: The B section staff with a big bracket over the F5: 'PEAK OF THE WHOLE PIECE.' A hairpin swelling into it and away from it.
pages.cleffy: The F is the highest note of the piece. The whole Nocturne climbs for this note. When it arrives, you linger. Rubato. All of it. After — you settle, not rush.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Section B both hands. LH: Ab2-Eb3 bass, Ab3-C4-Eb4 chords, then Eb2 bass, Bb3-Eb4-G4 chords, etc. Guides on. Metronome 54.
pages.cleffy: Both hands, section B. Still slow. Up to F. Linger. Then home. Hear the bass shift under you. New chords, same rock.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four more bars — identical to section A's first four. Label: A — RETURN.
pages.cleffy: A, one more time. Same as the opening. Same phrase 1, same phrase 2. But after the B section, it feels different. Home, after a trip. Same notes, new meaning.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Section A both hands. Guides on. Metronome 54. Dynamic: p again, even softer than before.
pages.cleffy: Section A again. Softer than the first time. The piece is winding down. Coming home.

pages.figure: Full score — A, B, A. Sixteen bars total. Dynamics marked: p, cresc. to peak F5, then dim. to pp at the return. Metronome 52.
pages.cleffy: Whole piece. Guides on. Slow — 52. A — settle in. B — climb to F. A — come home softer. Breathe between sections.

pages.figure: Same score. No guides. Reader on their own. Metronome 52.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read. All your tools. Use them.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was a complete Chopin Nocturne, played by you. A piece concert pianists spend years on. In your hands today.

pages.figure: Same score. Dynamic and rubato marks drawn in. Linger bracket over F5 in section B, over the Eb5 in section A phrase 2. Pedal flicks at every bar change. Dynamic p at start, cresc to the F5, dim back to pp. Metronome 50.
pages.cleffy: One more pass. Everything you have. Sing the peaks. Rock the bass. Flick the pedal. Soft throughout except the climb to F. This is the one.

pages.figure: Banner — Mid-boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Five quick ones. Mid-boss — take your time.

pages.figure: A small gallery of every piece in your hands so far. Tier 1: Happy Birthday. Tier 2: Für Elise theme. Tier 3: Clair de Lune opening. Tier 4: Moonlight opening, Prelude in C, Nocturne phrase. Tier 5 so far: Bach Invention. And — at the top, glowing — the Nocturne complete.
pages.cleffy: Look back at Act 40. Melody as speech — you read phrases like sentences. Rubato — you borrow time and pay it back. Left hand as guitar — pinky deep, strum up, rock. Pedal by ear — mud tells you when to lift. Four tools. One piece tonight. Two hundred lessons. A complete Nocturne. That's real.

pages.figure: Pyramid preview. Tier 5 midway flag planted. Ahead: L201-225. Four small thumbnails — 'Half-pedal,' 'Voicing,' 'Arabesque,' 'Clair de Lune (full).'
pages.cleffy: You are halfway through Tier 5. Not done. Next — pedal gets finer. Half-pedal, flutter, syncopated. Subtle color. Voicing — thumb melodies, inner melodies, left-hand tunes. Debussy's Arabesque. Schumann's Träumerei. Grieg's Arietta. Then, at 225 — the real tier boss. Clair de Lune, all of it. The one you started at 125. But not today. Today was its own summit.

pages.figure: Celebration card — LESSON 200 · NOCTURNE COMPLETE 👑. Cleffy at a candlelit piano, moon out the window, a page of Chopin sheet music on the stand. A small 200 star on the corner.
pages.cleffy: Lesson 200. You play a complete Chopin Nocturne. Simplified, yes. But the whole piece. Start to finish. Two hundred lessons ago, you learned what a staff was. Tonight, you speak Chopin. Halfway there. Rest up. Tier 5 keeps going when you're ready.

real_piano: The full Nocturne, once a day. Very slow. All four tools on — speech-shape, rubato, wide bass, pedal by ear.
Play it for someone you care about. Turn the lights down. Tell them it's Chopin, 1832, and he was twenty when he wrote it.
Find a concert recording — Rubinstein, Pollini, Horowitz. Listen once with eyes closed. Mark the lingers and the landings. You do this already, in a smaller way.
Revisit lesson 145's phrase. Notice how small it feels now.
virtual_only: Replay pages 14, 15, 16 — the three full-piece passes. One with guides, one without, one with all the expression.

questions.figure: Three boxes labeled A, B, A
questions.prompt: What form is the Nocturne in?

questions.figure: The F5 at the peak of the B section, with a hairpin swelling into it
questions.prompt: What is special about the F?
options: It's the highest note of the piece

questions.figure: A key signature with three flats
questions.prompt: What key is this Nocturne in?

questions.figure: A left-hand bass note followed by a chord a 10th above
questions.prompt: What's the nickname for this pattern?

questions.figure: A pedal flick right after a new bass note lands
questions.prompt: When do you change pedal?
options: A beat before the new bass
Right after the new bass lands
Never — one pedal for the whole piece

questions.audio: The A section played first, then the B section, then the A section again
questions.prompt: Did the opening melody return?

questions.audio: The climb to F5 and the settle back down in the B section
questions.prompt: Where is the peak of the piece?
options: In the middle — the B section

questions.audio: Two takes — one muddy-pedal, one clean-pedal
questions.prompt: Which one has clean pedaling?
Play the A section — both hands, 16 notes in the RH over the wide-bass LH — slowly.
questions.accept: The A section played with both hands, tempo around 54, with the RH melody legato and the LH bass-chord-chord pattern audible underneath

remediation.title: The Nocturne, section by section
remediation.opening_cleffy: Big piece. Mid-boss. Expected to stretch you. Let's unstack. A section alone, both hands, very slow. Then B alone. Then the return. Then — and only then — all three strung together. No rush. This Nocturne has been waiting 200 years. It'll wait for you too.

completion.cleffy: Lesson 200. Sealed. A complete Chopin Nocturne. Your hands. Your ear. Your time. Halfway through Tier 5. Halfway through the whole road. The moon's up. Rest tonight. Tomorrow — half-pedal, and the colors inside the sustain.

title: Pedal: half-pedal
subtitle: Act 41 · Advanced pedal technique
goal: Press the sustain pedal partway — letting some sound through without a full blur.
piece_context: Act 41 begins. For 45 lessons the pedal has been a switch — on or off. Now it becomes a dial. Half-pedal is the first step into pedal as color, not just sustain.

pages.figure: Cleffy at a grand piano, right foot on the pedal, eyes half-closed. Banner: ACT 41 — ADVANCED PEDAL. Below: 'PEDAL AS COLOR.'
pages.cleffy: New act. Same foot. Everything changes. The pedal is not a switch. It's a dial. Four lessons to learn the gradient.

pages.figure: A vertical slider labeled PEDAL. Three stops: UP (no sustain), HALF (partial), DOWN (full). A dot sits at HALF.
pages.cleffy: Up. Half. Down. Three positions — and everything in between. Today — half. Pedal pressed partway. Dampers hover just above the strings.

pages.figure: Cross-section of the piano. Dampers in three states — fully seated on strings (up), barely touching (half), lifted clear (down). Half state highlighted.
pages.cleffy: At half-pedal the dampers aren't seated. Not lifted either. They brush the strings. Some sound bleeds through, some gets absorbed. You get ring — but not the full bloom of a down pedal.

pages.figure: Three play buttons in a row: UP, HALF, DOWN. Each plays the same chord.

audio.A: C major chord, no pedal — dies quickly
audio.B: Same chord, half-pedal — rings briefly, softer tail
audio.C: Same chord, full pedal — blooms fully for several seconds

pages.cleffy: Same chord. Three pedal depths. Up — dies fast. Down — blooms long. Half — in between. A short, soft ring.

pages.figure: Three musical scenarios shown as small icons. 1: fast passage labeled 'too much full pedal = mud.' 2: quiet melody labeled 'full pedal = too heavy.' 3: transition labeled 'fading out.'
pages.cleffy: When to reach for half. Fast passages where full pedal turns into mush. Quiet places where a full bloom feels too thick. Any transition where you want the sound to thin, not stop.

pages.figure: Close-up of a right foot on the pedal. Three depth marks on the side: 0%, 50%, 100%. The toe is sitting at the 50% mark.
pages.cleffy: Feel your foot find the middle. Heel on the floor. Toe presses — but only partway. You'll feel a resistance point before the pedal bottoms out. That's your landmark.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Pedal meter on the side with a dot at HALF.
pages.cleffy: Play C. Let it ring a second. Half-pedal. Feel the toe stop before the bottom. Listen — the note fades faster than a full pedal, slower than none.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'CHORD WITH HALF-PEDAL — 3 SECONDS TAIL.'

audio.A: C major chord on half-pedal. Rings about three seconds, tail softer and thinner than full pedal.

pages.cleffy: That's the color. Shorter tail. Less bloom. A whisper of sustain, not a hall.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major triad. Pedal meter at HALF.
pages.cleffy: Play C, E, G together. Half-pedal down as you strike. Hold the depth. Let the chord fade on its own.

pages.figure: Side-by-side staves. Top: a fast scale run with FULL PEDAL — wavy lines showing blur. Bottom: same run with HALF PEDAL — cleaner lines, still some ring.
pages.cleffy: Same passage. Two depths. Full — the notes smear into each other. Half — the notes stay legible, but they still glow.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five-finger scale C to G. Pedal meter at HALF.
pages.cleffy: Play C, D, E, F, G. Keep half-pedal the whole time. Listen — the notes ring a little, but you can still hear each one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Scale twice — first with FULL pedal indicator, second with HALF pedal indicator.
pages.cleffy: Same scale, twice. First — full pedal. Listen to the blur. Then — half pedal. Hear the difference.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two different rooms. Same five notes.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A quiet four-note line: E4 G4 A4 G4. Half-pedal active.
pages.cleffy: A quiet phrase, half-pedal throughout. Light touch. Shallow foot. Let the sound breathe just a little.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick questions.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 201. A dial at the 50% mark with gentle light spilling out. Banner: HALF-PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: Half-pedal. Partway down. Some ring, not the full bloom. Tomorrow — fast releases. Flutter pedal.

real_piano: Find the resistance point on your pedal. Press ten times to the middle only. Feel where it stops short of the bottom.
Play any piece you know with pedal. Try a quiet section at half-depth. Listen for the thinner tail.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7, 9, and 12 — feel the three depths.

questions.figure: A pedal meter with the dot between UP and DOWN
questions.prompt: What is this position called?

questions.figure: A cross-section of piano dampers hovering just above the strings
questions.prompt: What depth is the pedal at?

questions.figure: A fast scale with wavy blur lines
questions.prompt: Which pedal depth would clean this up without killing the ring?

questions.audio: A chord with half-pedal — short tail
questions.prompt: Which depth is this?
Play C, D, E, F, G with half-pedal held throughout. Notes should ring slightly but stay legible.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 played with partial sustain — some ring, no full blur

remediation.title: Half-pedal, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Half-pedal is a feel, not a measurement. We'll find the resistance point first. Foot only, no notes. Ten times. Then one note. Then a chord.

completion.cleffy: First dial position found. The pedal has depth now. Tomorrow — flutter. Fast partial releases.

title: Pedal: flutter
subtitle: Act 41 · Advanced pedal technique
goal: Use fast, partial pedal releases to thin the sound without fully changing it.
piece_context: Yesterday — depth. Today — speed. Flutter pedal is a rapid series of tiny lifts. Not a full change — just a quick clearing. The trick for long phrases where harmony evolves but you don't want a hard pedal break.

pages.figure: Cleffy watching a foot on a pedal twitch up-down-up-down in fast bursts. Small motion blur. Banner: FLUTTER PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: New trick. Fast little releases. Not a full change. Not a full hold. A flutter — tiny lifts while the foot stays near the pedal.

pages.figure: A timeline. Full pedal shown as a solid bar. Flutter shown as the same bar with tiny notches cut out at regular intervals. Label: 'nicks of silence.'
pages.cleffy: Flutter is pedal-mostly-down, with quick little ups. Each lift is a nick of silence — just enough to drop some of the old sound. The new notes keep ringing. The old ones get thinned.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: long phrase, full pedal, no flutter. B: same phrase, flutter applied.

audio.A: A descending melodic line held under full pedal — notes start to pile into a hum
audio.B: The same line with flutter pedal — each new note is clear, old notes fade faster

pages.cleffy: Same phrase. Two pedals. First — full pedal. Mud builds. Second — flutter. The mud gets swept out every beat.

pages.figure: Three scenarios as icons. 1: a long legato phrase with many different harmonies. 2: a descending scale with pedal. 3: a big dynamic swell where too much ring would swamp it.
pages.cleffy: When flutter earns its keep. Long phrases that change harmony slowly — too many to pedal-change, too smeared to hold. Scales with pedal. Big swells. Anywhere you need to clean — not reset.

pages.figure: A foot on a pedal, viewed from the side. Arrow showing the toe lifting maybe an inch, then back down. Tempo marking: 'one flick per beat.'
pages.cleffy: The motion. Stay near the bottom. Flick the toe up an inch. Drop it back down. Small. Fast. The heel never leaves the floor.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'ONE FLUTTER — HELD CHORD, SINGLE LIFT.'

audio.A: A chord held under pedal. Mid-way through, a quick partial lift — volume dips slightly — pedal drops back. Chord continues.

pages.cleffy: One flutter. Listen for the dip. The sound doesn't stop — it thins, then resumes.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 glowing. Pedal meter animating: DOWN — flick UP — back DOWN.
pages.cleffy: Play C. Hold the pedal down. One quick flutter while it rings — flick the toe up, drop it back. Feel the sound thin and return.

pages.figure: A staff showing four quarter notes. Below each note: a small upward arrow labeled 'flick.' Caption: 'one flutter per beat — or more.'
pages.cleffy: Flutter has a rhythm. Most common — one flick per beat. Fast passages — two per beat. Slow passages — one per bar.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 D4 E4 F4. Pedal meter showing a flicker on each beat.
pages.cleffy: Four notes. Pedal mostly down. Flick on each beat as the next note arrives. The sound should never fully stop.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: scale with full pedal. B: same scale with flutter.

audio.A: An ascending five-finger scale under unbroken full pedal — notes blur together
audio.B: Same scale with a small flutter on each note — notes stay clearer while still ringing

pages.cleffy: Same scale. A is full pedal. B is flutter. A smears. B glows. That's the whole difference.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five-finger scale C to G. Pedal meter flickering on each note.
pages.cleffy: Scale up, pedal fluttering. One flick per note. Listen — each note is clear, but the whole phrase still rings.

pages.figure: Keyboard. G4 down to C4. Pedal flutter marked on each beat.
pages.cleffy: Now down. G, F, E, D, C. Flutter each note. Descending lines build mud fastest. Flutter fixes it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A gentle melody: E4 G4 A4 G4 E4 D4 C4. Flutter marked throughout.
pages.cleffy: A small phrase. Flutter throughout. Listen for both — the ring and the clarity.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's flutter. A clean phrase, still bathed in ring.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick questions.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 202. A foot on a pedal with small flicker lines above the toe. Banner: FLUTTER.
pages.cleffy: Flutter. Fast tiny lifts. Sweep the mud without breaking the ring. Tomorrow — syncopated pedal. The advanced way to change pedal cleanly.

real_piano: Sit at the piano. No notes. Flutter the pedal — flick, flick, flick — to a metronome at 60. Small motions. Heel on floor.
Play a scale two octaves with flutter. One flick per note. Listen for the clarity.
On any piece with pedal — try fluttering once per bar. Notice which spots need it and which don't.
virtual_only: Replay pages 7, 9, 11, and 13 — feel the flicker rhythm.

questions.figure: A pedal timeline with tiny notches cut out at regular intervals
questions.prompt: What technique does this show?

questions.figure: A fast descending scale starting to blur
questions.prompt: Which technique would clear the mud without stopping the ring?

questions.figure: A diagram of a foot on the pedal with a small upward arrow from the toe
questions.prompt: How big is the flutter motion?
options: A full lift to the top
A small toe flick, heel stays down

questions.audio: A scale played with flutter pedal — clear but still ringing
questions.prompt: Which technique is this?
Play C, D, E, F, G. Flutter the pedal on each note — one flick per beat.
questions.accept: Five-finger ascending scale played with flutter pedal — notes clear, pedal mostly down

remediation.title: Flutter, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Flutter is a motion more than a rule. We'll practice the foot alone first. Slow metronome. Ten flicks in a row. Then add one note. Then a scale.

completion.cleffy: Flutter in the foot. The ring stays, the mud goes. Tomorrow — syncopated pedal. Changing pedal after the beat, not with it.

title: Pedal: syncopated
subtitle: Act 41 · Advanced pedal technique
goal: Change the pedal just AFTER a new note lands — the standard technique for clean, connected harmony.
piece_context: This is the one you'll use for the rest of your life. Syncopated pedal — also called legato pedal — is how real pianists change pedal without a gap. Press the new note first; lift and re-press the pedal an instant after.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a timeline on a whiteboard. An arrow labeled NEW NOTE. A second arrow labeled PEDAL, lagging just behind the first. Banner: SYNCOPATED PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: The most important pedal technique. Change the pedal after the new note — not with it. Also called legato pedal. Same thing.

pages.figure: Timeline. Top row: 'note presses' marked on beats 1, 2, 3, 4. Bottom row: 'pedal changes' marked at the same beats 1, 2, 3, 4. Between each beat: red label 'GAP.'
pages.cleffy: The classic beginner pedal — change pedal WITH the new note. Problem. For one instant, nothing rings. A tiny gap. A gap between every chord. The legato dies.

pages.figure: Same timeline. Top row: 'note presses' on beats 1, 2, 3, 4. Bottom row: 'pedal changes' shifted slightly right — just after each note. Caption: 'note catches the pedal.' Green glow between each note.
pages.cleffy: The fix. Press the new note. Then — lift and re-press the pedal. The new note catches the fresh pedal mid-sustain. No gap. No mud.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: 'pedal-on-beat' — chord changes with audible gap. B: 'syncopated' — smooth connection.

audio.A: Four chords — C, F, G, C — with pedal changing exactly on each beat. A faint gap sits between each chord.
audio.B: Same four chords — pedal changes slightly after each new chord. Seamless, no gap.

pages.cleffy: Same chords. Two foot patterns. A — pedal changes with the beat. Listen for the gaps. B — pedal changes after the beat. Smooth.

pages.figure: Three-part sequence. 1: Finger presses new chord. Pedal DOWN (holding old ring). 2: Pedal UP (clearing). 3: Pedal DOWN again (catching the new chord). Arrow labeled 'split second.'
pages.cleffy: Three-part motion. Fingers land. Pedal goes up. Pedal goes down. All in a split second. Foot is late. On purpose.

pages.figure: A measure in 4/4. Each beat has a chord label. Under each chord, a tiny 'and' marking the foot change — C (1) chord, pedal change (+1), F (2) chord, pedal change (+2), etc.
pages.cleffy: Count it. Beat 1 — new chord. "And" of 1 — pedal flips. Beat 2 — new chord. "And" of 2 — pedal flips. Every time.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'VERY SLOW — TWO CHORDS, SYNCOPATED PEDAL.'

audio.A: C major chord with pedal. F major chord lands — a half-beat later pedal lifts and re-presses. Long smooth join.

pages.cleffy: Very slow. Two chords. Hear the second chord arrive. Hear the pedal flip just after — tsssk. Smooth into the next ring.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C major then F major. Pedal meter with arrows: DOWN on C (hold), UP after F lands, DOWN again.
pages.cleffy: Two chords. C major, then F major. On F — land fingers first. Lift pedal a half-beat later. Re-press. Don't rush the foot. Late on purpose.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, F, G. Pedal markings: syncopated change between each.
pages.cleffy: Three chords. C, F, G. Syncopated pedal between each. Fingers first. Foot late. Smooth connection.

pages.figure: Two overlapping wave shapes. Old chord's sound fading. New chord's sound rising. Overlap highlighted in gold. Caption: 'the fingers hold the bridge.'
pages.cleffy: Why it works. When you lift the pedal, the old chord stops ringing in the strings. But your fingers are already on the new chord. The new sound bridges the gap. Pedal re-presses. New chord takes the ring.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C, Am, F, G progression. Syncopated pedal throughout.
pages.cleffy: Four chords. C, A minor, F, G. Syncopated pedal between all four. No gaps.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'CHOPIN-STYLE PHRASE, SYNCOPATED PEDAL.'

audio.A: A slow romantic phrase — left hand wide chords, right hand melody — every chord change pedaled syncopated. Smooth as water.

pages.cleffy: Listen. A real phrase, real style. Every chord changes. No gaps anywhere. That's syncopated pedal doing its job.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A small melody in RH — E G A G E D C. LH chord changes on each beat. Syncopated pedal on every chord change.
pages.cleffy: Small melody. Left-hand chord every two beats. Syncopated pedal at each chord change. The melody should stay connected. No foot-gaps allowed.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the technique that holds everything together. Every good pianist you've ever heard is doing that, every bar.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick questions.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 203. A timeline with notes on the beat and pedal flips just after each. Banner: SYNCOPATED PEDAL.
pages.cleffy: Syncopated pedal. Fingers first, foot after. The invisible skill behind every smooth pianist. Tomorrow — when NOT to pedal.

real_piano: Sit at the piano. Play C and F major, back to back. Syncopated pedal. Ten times slow. Count: chord — and — chord — and.
Pick any piece you know with pedal. Replace every pedal change with syncopated. Listen for gaps disappearing.
Say it out loud while you play: 'finger first, foot late.' Let the words set the timing.
virtual_only: Replay pages 8, 9, and 11 — feel the lateness in the foot.

questions.figure: A timeline with note changes on the beat and pedal changes slightly after each
questions.prompt: What technique is this?

questions.figure: A timeline with note changes and pedal changes both happening exactly on the beat
questions.prompt: What's wrong with this pattern?
options: There will be tiny gaps between chords
The pedal is in the wrong foot

questions.figure: A three-step diagram: 1 finger lands, 2 pedal up, 3 pedal down
questions.prompt: What is this sequence?

questions.audio: Four chords with tiny gaps between each (pedal-on-beat)
questions.prompt: What's the problem here?
options: Pedal changes are on the beat — causing gaps

questions.prompt: Play C major, then F major. Use syncopated pedal — finger first, pedal lifts and re-presses just after.
questions.accept: C major chord, F major chord, played with pedal changing late between them — smooth connection

remediation.title: Syncopated pedal, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Syncopated pedal is a timing. It takes reps to feel. We'll do two chords only. Very slow. Say 'finger — foot' out loud every time. Ten in a row.

completion.cleffy: Syncopated pedal. The hidden hinge of good playing. Tomorrow — the opposite. Where to use NO pedal at all.

title: Pedal: dry passages
subtitle: Act 41 · Advanced pedal technique
goal: Recognize the passages that sound better with no pedal at all — Bach, fast classical runs, scherzos, any music built on clean line.
piece_context: Three lessons of pedal depth, speed, and timing. Today — the opposite. Sometimes the right answer is no pedal. A dry passage has no sustain. Every note is exactly as long as the finger holds it. Bach wrote before the sustain pedal existed — his music still breathes without it.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up a single index finger — 'no foot today.' Behind him, a pedal with a big X over it. Banner: DRY PASSAGES.
pages.cleffy: The quiet truth of pedaling. Sometimes the right answer is no pedal. Three lessons of tricks — today, nothing.

pages.figure: A staff with a short phrase. No pedal marks at all. Caption: 'dry = fingers only = each note ends when you lift.'
pages.cleffy: Dry passages. No sustain. Each note ends the instant you lift your finger. Just fingers, just strings. The oldest sound on the piano.

pages.figure: Four panels. 1: Bach Invention (pre-pedal music). 2: Fast classical scale run. 3: A scherzo — quick playful movement. 4: A melody where the notes are meant to separate.
pages.cleffy: Four places to go dry. Bach and other Baroque music — written before sustain pedals existed. Fast scales and runs — pedal turns them to soup. Scherzos — quick, crisp, playful. Anywhere the notes want to stay separate.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: 'WITH PEDAL' — a Bach Invention phrase with full sustain pedal. B: 'NO PEDAL' — same phrase, no sustain.

audio.A: A Bach Invention phrase played with continuous sustain pedal — voices blur, harmony becomes thick and unclear
audio.B: Same Bach phrase with no pedal — each voice distinct, conversation between hands audible

pages.cleffy: Bach, two ways. With pedal — two voices become one smear. No pedal — both voices breathe. You hear the conversation. This is why Bach is dry.

pages.figure: A staff with two melodic lines — one in treble, one in bass — labeled 'two voices.' Arrow pointing to each: 'each needs to be heard.'
pages.cleffy: Why Bach needs dry. Two independent voices. Each has its own story. Pedal glues them together. The stories get lost.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH: C E G E. LH: G E C E. Played at the same time. Pedal icon crossed out.
pages.cleffy: Short Bach-style phrase. Two hands talking. No pedal. Fingers only. Hear both voices.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: fast scale with pedal. B: same scale, no pedal.

audio.A: A fast two-octave scale played with full pedal — blurs into a single wash of sound
audio.B: Same scale, no pedal — each note clear, crisp articulation

pages.cleffy: Fast scale. Same notes, different pedal. With pedal — mush. Without — every note sparkles.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 to G4 fast. Pedal crossed out. Tempo 120.
pages.cleffy: Five-finger scale. Fast. No pedal. Clean fingers. Every note cut sharp.

pages.figure: A flowchart. Top: 'Should I pedal?' Branches: Is it Baroque? → NO. Is it fast and linear? → NO. Is the melody supposed to stay separate? → NO. Is it slow, chordal, romantic? → YES.
pages.cleffy: Quick test before you pedal. Is it Baroque? Don't pedal. Is it fast and scalar? Don't pedal. Are the notes meant to stay separate? Don't pedal. If it's slow, chordal, and romantic — pedal.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A quick, bouncy eight-note run. Pedal crossed out.
pages.cleffy: Scherzo fragment. Quick, light, bouncy. Dry fingers. No pedal. Pop, pop, pop.

pages.figure: A piece of music. First half labeled 'dry — fast run.' Second half labeled 'pedal — chordal coda.' Arrow showing the foot coming down at the boundary.
pages.cleffy: Many pieces mix both. Dry for the fast section. Pedal for the slow closing chords. Knowing when to stop pedaling is just as important as knowing when to start.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First four notes: fast scale, pedal crossed out. Then: a held chord with pedal.
pages.cleffy: Two sections. First — fast scale, dry. Then — a held chord with pedal. Watch the switch. Fingers crisp, then foot arrives.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A longer Baroque-style phrase with two voices. Pedal crossed out throughout.
pages.cleffy: Longer Baroque phrase, both hands. Dry throughout. Fingers have to do all the legato work. No foot help.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the old sound. The piano before sustain. Every note earned by the finger.

pages.figure: Banner — three quick questions.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 204. A pedal with a thin X over it and a wooden harpsichord silhouette behind. Banner: DRY PASSAGES.
pages.cleffy: Dry. No sustain. Sometimes the best pedaling is no pedaling. Tomorrow — a Chopin Prelude that uses pedal for pure color. Act boss.

real_piano: Play any Bach piece you know — or just a scale passage — with no pedal. Let the fingers do every bit of the legato. Notice how much work they do.
In any piece you're learning, find one passage that's currently pedaled. Try it dry. Which way sounds better?
Play a fast five-finger scale with pedal down, then again with no pedal. Get the difference in your ears.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 8, and 13 — feel fingers doing the work alone.

questions.figure: A staff with a Bach Invention — two independent melodic lines
questions.prompt: How much pedal does this music want?

questions.figure: A flowchart. 'Is it Baroque?' with YES arrow
questions.prompt: If the answer is yes, what should you do?

questions.figure: A fast descending scale marked allegro
questions.prompt: What happens if you pedal this with full sustain?

questions.audio: Bach played with pedal (blurry) vs Bach played dry (clear voices)
questions.prompt: Which version lets you hear both voices clearly?
Play a five-finger scale fast — C up to G and back — with no pedal. Keep it crisp.
questions.accept: Fast five-finger scale, ascending and descending, no sustain pedal

remediation.title: Dry playing, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Dry playing means the fingers do all the work — harder than it sounds. We'll do a short phrase, slowly, no pedal. Then faster. Then a real Bach-style bit.

completion.cleffy: Dry in the toolkit. Four pedal tools in your foot now. Tomorrow — the act boss. Chopin uses pedal as pure mood.

title: Chopin Prelude in E minor
subtitle: Act 41 · Advanced pedal technique
goal: Play a simplified opening of Chopin's Prelude Op. 28 No. 4 in E minor — using the full pedal toolkit to hold mood and color.
piece_context: Act 41 boss. Four lessons of pedal technique behind you — half-pedal, flutter, syncopated, dry. Now the most famous pedal piece in the world. Chopin's Prelude in E minor. Eight bars, a low E bass held still while the right-hand chords sink chromatically above. The pedal holds the whole thing together — a long, grieving cloud of sound.

piece.title: Prelude in E minor, Op. 28 No. 4
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — very slow, chromatically sinking RH chords, held LH bass, pedal as color

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a dark stage with a single spotlight. A music sheet open to Prelude Op. 28 No. 4. Banner: ACT 41 BOSS — CHOPIN PRELUDE IN E MINOR.
pages.cleffy: Act 41 boss. The most famous pedal piece ever written. Chopin. E minor. Eight bars.

pages.figure: Staff preview — the opening four bars. RH: re-struck eighth-note chords that lower one voice chromatically, bar by bar. LH: a single low E, held. Marked 'Largo.' Tempo: 44.
pages.cleffy: Chopin wrote this in 1839. Left hand — one low E bass note, held still. Right hand — re-struck chords that sink, half-step by half-step, one voice at a time. Pedal holds every bar. No change until the chord does.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'CHOPIN PRELUDE IN E MINOR — 8 BARS, LARGO.'

audio.A: The first eight bars of Prelude Op. 28 No. 4 — LH holds a low E bass, RH re-strikes soft chords that slowly sink chromatically, pedal held whole-bar. A deep, mournful cloud of sound that shifts color as each inner voice descends.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Eight bars. Very slow. The left hand is an anchor. The right hand is the weather — sinking. Pedal holds each bar like a long breath.

pages.figure: The eight bars sketched as eight rectangles. A curved pedal-down line spans each bar, with a syncopated change at each bar-line. Label: 'one pedal per bar, syncopated at the bar-line.'
pages.cleffy: Your pedal job. One pedal per bar. Full depth. Syncopated change at each bar-line — fingers first, foot late. Every bar is one color. The bar-line is where the color shifts.

pages.figure: Staff — LH only. A low E2 held as a whole note under each bar. Steady anchor.
pages.cleffy: Simplified left hand first. One note. Low E. Held under every bar. No motion. Just an anchor. Let the right hand do the drama.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH E2 held as a whole note. No pedal yet.
pages.cleffy: LH alone. One low E, held. Tempo — barely moving. 44. No pedal yet. Just the hand.

pages.figure: Staff — RH only. Eight chord voicings across eight bars. Each chord re-struck in eighths. One inner voice drops a semitone at each bar: B3-E4-G4, B3-E4-F#4, A3-E4-F#4, A3-D#4-F#4, A3-D4-F#4, G#3-C#4-F4, G3-C4-E4, F#3-B3-D#4. Half-steps marked.
pages.cleffy: Right hand — simplified chord voicings, one per bar. Each bar, one note in the chord slides down a half-step. This is the grief — three voices, sinking a semitone at a time.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH chromatic chord descent. Each chord re-struck in eighths for one bar, then the next voicing. No pedal.
pages.cleffy: RH alone. Re-struck chords. One voicing per bar, each slightly lower. Feel each shift. A single voice drops. Everything else stays.

pages.figure: Play button. Label: 'RH WITH ONE-PEDAL-PER-BAR.'

audio.A: Right hand alone — re-struck chords, one voicing per bar sinking chromatically, with syncopated pedal change at each bar-line. Each chord blooms into a held cloud, then dissolves as the next voicing arrives.

pages.cleffy: Listen — RH with pedal. Each chord blooms. Each bar-line is a pedal flip. The pedal paints the color of each voicing.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH chromatic chord descent. Pedal meter — DOWN for each bar, syncopated change at bar-lines.
pages.cleffy: RH again. This time — pedal every bar. Fingers land on the new voicing. Pedal flips just after. Let it ring.

pages.figure: A grand staff showing the first two bars. LH: low E2 as a whole note. RH: re-struck B3-E4-G4 chord in eighths. Pedal bar marked underneath.
pages.cleffy: Both hands together now. LH — one long low E. RH — shimmer of chord, re-struck. One pedal for the whole bar.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bar 1 only. LH E2 held, RH B3-E4-G4 chord repeating above.
pages.cleffy: One bar. Both hands. Pedal down. LH holds E. RH repeats the chord. Slow. Steady.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Bars 1-4. LH: E2 held each bar. RH: chord voicings sinking chromatically — B3-E4-G4, B3-E4-G4, B3-E4-F#4, A3-E4-F#4. Pedal syncopated at each bar-line.
pages.cleffy: First four bars. Both hands. Pedal flip at every bar-line. Fingers first, foot after. Very slow. Sink into the grief.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full 8-bar phrase. LH: E2 held every bar (anchor). RH: chord voicings sinking chromatically across eight bars — each bar, one inner voice drops a semitone. Pedal one-per-bar, syncopated.
pages.cleffy: All eight bars. The whole opening. One pedal per bar. Syncopated at the bar-line. This is the piece. Let it sink.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's the opening of one of the most played pieces in all of piano music. Slow, simple, devastating.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 41 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 41 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy holding a candle, Chopin silhouette behind. A pyramid of acts showing 41/50 lit. Tier 5 is filling up.
pages.cleffy: Act 41. Closed. Four pedal tools — half, flutter, syncopated, dry. One Chopin Prelude. The right foot is a full instrument now. Not a switch. A brush. Next act — voicing. Making one finger sing louder than the rest, while the others hold the harmony.

real_piano: Play the RH chromatic chord descent alone, every day, with one pedal per bar. Get the syncopated change clean at each bar-line. Five minutes.
Play the full eight bars at tempo 44. Don't go faster. The slowness is the piece.
Listen to a professional recording of the Prelude — any pianist, any version. Hear what their pedal does. You'll notice it now.
If you have any romantic piece in your hands — a Nocturne, a slow waltz — try applying syncopated pedal to every bar change. Compare to how you did it before.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10, 13, and 14 — feel the one-per-bar pedal.

questions.figure: The opening bars of Chopin Prelude Op. 28 No. 4 with pedal marks
questions.prompt: How often does the pedal change?

questions.figure: The RH chord voicings sinking across eight bars, one inner voice dropping a semitone each bar
questions.prompt: Which hand is doing the chromatic descent?

questions.figure: A pedal marking where the foot changes slightly after each new bar begins
questions.prompt: What technique is this?

questions.figure: A tempo marking of 44 BPM
questions.prompt: How would you describe this speed?

questions.audio: The first four bars of the Prelude with pedal held per bar
questions.prompt: How many pedal changes did you hear?

questions.audio: Two versions of the Prelude — one with pedal-on-beat (gaps), one with syncopated pedal (smooth)
questions.prompt: Which version matches the Chopin tradition?

questions.audio: The RH chromatic chord descent without pedal, then with pedal-per-bar
questions.prompt: Which version feels more like the Prelude?
Play the first two bars — LH holds low E, RH re-strikes the B-E-G chord, syncopated pedal at the bar-line.
questions.accept: LH E2 held under RH B3-E4-G4 chord repeating across two bars, with pedal change at bar 2

questions.prompt: Play just the RH chromatic chord descent — one voicing per bar, each bar one inner voice dropping a semitone — with syncopated pedal every bar.
questions.accept: Eight chromatically descending RH chord voicings, pedaled once per bar

questions.prompt: Play the opening four bars, both hands, with pedal.
questions.accept: Bars 1-4 of the Prelude — LH low E held, RH chord voicings sinking chromatically, syncopated pedal at each bar-line

remediation.title: Prelude in E minor, one bar at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss. Chopin. Three things happening at once — the low E bass held steady, the right-hand chords sinking chromatically above, the pedal holding each bar whole. We'll build it one hand at a time, one bar at a time. Very slow. No rush. You'll land it.

completion.cleffy: Act 41 — sealed. Chopin Prelude in your hands. Four pedal tools, one grieving piece. The right foot is yours. Next act — voicing. Your thumb learns to sing.

title: Voicing: top-melody
subtitle: Act 42 · Voicing
goal: Play a right-hand chord so the top note sings above the others — the most common voicing in all of piano music.
piece_context: New act. Voicing — the art of making one note in a chord louder than the others. You met it in Moonlight, bringing out the top of every triplet. Now it becomes its own skill. Five lessons. Top, middle, bottom, drill, Schumann.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a banner — ACT 42 · VOICING. Below, a three-note chord drawn, with the top note glowing gold.
pages.cleffy: New act. Voicing. A chord is three or four notes pressed together. But one of them can sing louder than the rest. That's voicing.

pages.figure: Staff. A C major chord C4-E4-G4 written as a stack. G4 (top) circled and labelled 'the melody.'
pages.cleffy: When a pianist plays a chord, the melody is usually the top note. The ear loves the top. It's the brightest voice in the stack. Your job — help it sing.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: chord with all notes equal. B: same chord, top note firmer.

audio.A: C major chord C4-E4-G4, all three notes equal volume.
audio.B: Same chord, top G4 noticeably louder — a melody appears.

pages.cleffy: Listen to both. First — flat. A block. Just a chord. Second — top note firmer. Suddenly there's a tune hiding in it.

pages.figure: Cartoon right hand pressing C-E-G. Arrow tilting the hand weight toward the pinky side. Label: 'lean pinky. Lighten thumb and middle.'
pages.cleffy: The physical trick — tilt your hand weight toward the pinky side. Finger 5 sinks a little deeper. Fingers 1 and 3 brush the keys. Same chord. Different weight.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 highlighted. Label: 'all equal.'
pages.cleffy: Play it flat first. C, E, G together. Three equal notes. A block of sound.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 — G4 glowing gold. Label: 'top sings.'
pages.cleffy: Same chord. Pinky firmer this time. Lean your weight toward the G. Let it ring above the other two.
pages.followup_cleffy: Hear that G floating on top? Same three keys. The G is singing now.

pages.figure: Four chords in a row on the staff: C-E-G, C-F-A, B-D-G, C-E-G. Top notes circled: G, A, G, G. Arrow labeled 'the melody.'
pages.cleffy: Watch what happens across four chords. The top notes make a line. G, A, G, G. A tiny tune. The bottom notes are harmony. The top notes are melody.

pages.figure: Play button — four chords with tops voiced.

audio.A: Four chords — C-E-G, C-F-A, B-D-G, C-E-G — each with top note firmer. G-A-G-G melody is audible.

pages.cleffy: Listen for the top line — G, A, G, G. Not the bottom. Not the middle. The top.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four chords. Top notes highlighted: G4, A4, G4, G4.
pages.cleffy: Play the four chords. Pinky firmer each time. Let the top notes connect into a melody — G, A, G, G.

pages.figure: Diagram — one chord, three arrows. Each arrow points to a different note, labelled: 'you choose which one sings.'
pages.cleffy: Voicing is a choice. Usually the top. Sometimes not. The pianist decides which voice the audience hears. Today — top. Tomorrow — middle. Then the bottom.

pages.figure: Staff. Four voiced chords at 50 bpm — hymn-like. Labels: 'lean pinky.'
pages.cleffy: One more, slower. Feel the lean. Pinky sinks. Thumb and middle are quiet helpers.

pages.figure: Round counter. Four rounds — top voiced or flat?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Did the top sing, or was the chord flat?

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 206 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 206. A three-note chord with the top note glowing.
pages.cleffy: Top voicing. The most common kind. Tomorrow — the voice inside the chord. A hidden melody in the middle.

real_piano: Play any chord you know. First flat. Then lean toward the pinky. Feel your hand tilt.
Play a hymn — Amazing Grace, Happy Birthday — block chords. Bring out the top. The melody pops.

questions.figure: A three-note chord with the top note circled in gold
questions.prompt: What is being voiced?

questions.figure: A right hand pressing three notes, leaning toward the pinky side
questions.prompt: Which finger presses firmer for top voicing?

questions.figure: The word 'voicing' with three chord notes, one highlighted
questions.prompt: What does voicing mean?
options: Making one note in a chord louder than the others

questions.audio: A chord played flat, then the same chord with top note firmer
questions.prompt: Which version has a clear melody note?
Play C-E-G with the top G singing above the other two.
questions.accept: C4-E4-G4 chord with audible emphasis on G4

remediation.title: Top voicing, one note at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Voicing is a small movement and a new ear skill. Easy to miss. We'll go flat, then voiced, back and forth. One chord. Until the top sings on its own.

completion.cleffy: First kind of voicing unlocked. The top. Tomorrow — the voice hiding in the middle.

title: Voicing: inner melody
subtitle: Act 42 · Voicing
goal: Play a three-note chord with the middle note singing above the others — a hidden melody inside the texture.
piece_context: Yesterday — the top. Today — the middle. The hardest voicing, and the most magical. This is what makes Schumann's Träumerei sound the way it does. A melody buried inside a warm blanket of chords.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a chord with the middle note glowing gold. Banner: THE HIDDEN MELODY.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday — top voicing. Today — harder. The middle note sings. A melody hiding inside the chord.

pages.figure: Staff. C4-E4-G4 chord stack. E4 (middle) circled in gold. Label: 'inner voice.'
pages.cleffy: Same C chord. Same three notes. But this time — the E in the middle is the melody. Top and bottom sit quiet. The middle speaks.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: flat. B: top voiced. C: middle voiced.

audio.A: C-E-G chord, all three notes equal.
audio.B: Same chord, top G firmer.
audio.C: Same chord, middle E firmer — a different note sings.

pages.cleffy: Three versions of the same chord. Flat, top, middle. Listen to the third one. The E is floating in the middle. Warm.

pages.figure: Cartoon right hand pressing C-E-G. Middle finger (3) glowing. Arrows showing weight on finger 3, less on 1 and 5.
pages.cleffy: The trick — finger 3 presses firmer. Fingers 1 and 5 brush. Harder than the top. Your middle finger has to push past the pinky's strength. This is the skill piano players chase for years.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4. E4 glowing gold.
pages.cleffy: Try it. C, E, G — lean into the E. Middle finger deeper. Thumb light. Pinky light.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two labelled attempts: first G gold, second E gold.
pages.cleffy: Now both in a row. First — same chord, top G voiced. Then — middle E voiced. Feel the hand tilt change.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two different chords. Same three keys. That's the power of voicing.

pages.figure: Diagram. A chord sitting still, with an arrow from the middle note stretching out into a melodic line.
pages.cleffy: Why voice the middle? Because the best composers hide melodies there. Schumann. Brahms. Bach. A tune in the middle, wrapped in chords on either side. Warm, inward, private.

pages.figure: Staff. Three chords: C-E-G, C-F-G, C-E-G. Middle notes circled: E, F, E. Label: 'inner melody — E F E.'
pages.cleffy: Here's an inner melody across three chords. Middle notes — E, F, E. A tiny shape. Outside notes stay put. The middle moves.

pages.figure: Play button. 'Three chords — middle notes voiced.'

audio.A: Three chords C-E-G, C-F-G, C-E-G played with middle notes firmer — E F E is audible as a melody.

pages.cleffy: Listen for E, F, E. Not the top G — that's sitting still. The middle is the one moving. The middle is the tune.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Three chords. Middle notes highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Three chords, middle voiced. Finger 3 sinks each time. Outside fingers brush.

pages.figure: Staff. Five chords with the middle voice moving — E, F, E, D, E. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Longer now. Five chords. The middle is the melody. E, F, E, D, E. Can you hear the shape?
pages.followup_cleffy: The middle sang. That's an inner melody.

pages.figure: Round counter. Four rounds — top, middle, or flat?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Top voiced, middle voiced, or flat?

pages.figure: Two small cards side by side. Card 1: top note gold. Card 2: middle note gold. Banner below: 'two voicings. One more tomorrow.'
pages.cleffy: Two voicings now in your hands. Top — the common one. Middle — the magical one. Tomorrow — the left hand takes over.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 207 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 207. A chord with the middle note glowing, a small arrow labelled 'hidden.'
pages.cleffy: Inner voicing. The tough one. Tomorrow — the left hand sings the tune.

real_piano: Play C-E-G. Voice the top. Then voice the middle. Alternate, ten times. Feel your hand re-tilt.
Any piece you know with block chords — try voicing the middle note. Even if it sounds wrong, the skill is in your fingers.

questions.figure: A three-note chord with the middle note circled in gold
questions.prompt: What is being voiced?

questions.figure: Right hand pressing C-E-G with finger 3 highlighted
questions.prompt: Which finger presses firmer for middle voicing?

questions.figure: Three chords with middle notes E-F-E circled
questions.prompt: What is the inner melody here?

questions.audio: A chord with the middle note firmer
questions.prompt: Which voice is singing?
Play C-E-G with the middle E singing above the others.
questions.accept: C4-E4-G4 chord with audible emphasis on E4

remediation.title: Middle voicing, slowed down
remediation.opening_cleffy: Middle voicing is the hardest of the three. Finger 3 has to push past finger 5's strength. We'll slow down. One chord. Tilt in. Tilt out. Until the middle note speaks on its own.

completion.cleffy: The middle can sing now. Tomorrow — the tune moves to the left hand.

title: Voicing: LH melody
subtitle: Act 42 · Voicing
goal: Play a left-hand bass melody while the right hand keeps a quieter chord accompaniment — roles reversed.
piece_context: Top voicing — one hand. Middle voicing — one hand. Today — both hands. The left hand carries the melody. The right hand holds soft chords above. Role reversal. This is how Liszt and Rachmaninoff wrote some of their richest passages.

pages.figure: Cleffy with two hands drawn — left hand glowing gold, right hand softer. Banner: LEFT HAND TAKES THE TUNE.
pages.cleffy: Two voicings so far — top and middle. Both in the right hand. Today the tune moves down. Left hand sings. Right hand accompanies.

pages.figure: Two panels. Panel 1: 'USUAL — RH melody, LH chords.' Panel 2: 'TODAY — LH melody, RH chords.'
pages.cleffy: The usual — right hand melody, left hand chords. Today — the opposite. Low voice sings. High voice supports.

pages.figure: Play button. 'Left hand melody, right hand chords.'

audio.A: A simple four-note bass melody C3-D3-E3-F3 with soft right-hand chords C4-E4-G4 held above.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Bass is loud. Chords above are soft. The low voice is the one telling the story.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH: C3-D3-E3-F3 quarter notes, circled in gold. RH: a held C-E-G chord above. Label: 'LH melody, RH support.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the score. Left hand plays the line — C, D, E, F. The melody. Right hand holds a chord on top. Soft. Quiet. Support.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C3, D3, E3, F3 highlighted. LH only.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone first. C, D, E, F. Firm. This is the melody. Let it sing.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4 highlighted. Label: 'brush — not press.'
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Press C-E-G once. Softly. Barely there. Think 'brush the keys,' not 'press.'

pages.figure: Scale diagram. LH side weighted down — 'loud melody.' RH side floats up — 'quiet chords.'
pages.cleffy: The balance — left heavier, right lighter. Your arms will want to do the opposite. Right hand usually leads. Fight that. Low voice wins today.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH melody C3-D3-E3-F3, RH holds C-E-G chord. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Both hands now. Left hand walks — C, D, E, F. Right hand holds the chord. Gentle.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: RH too loud (bad). B: LH louder (good).

audio.A: Same passage, but right-hand chord is too loud — it covers the bass melody.
audio.B: Same passage, bass clearly louder — the melody is audible.

pages.cleffy: Two versions. First — right hand too loud. The tune drowns. Second — left hand louder. The tune lives.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH: longer bass melody C3-D3-E3-G3-F3-E3. RH: C-E-G chord held for each. Label: 'longer line.'
pages.cleffy: Longer bass melody now. Six notes. C, D, E, G, F, E. A little arc. Right hand keeps the same chord overhead.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Six-note LH melody, RH chord above.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Left hand sings the arc. Right hand holds. Lean into the bass. Brush the top.
pages.followup_cleffy: The bass was the melody. The chord floated above like mist.

pages.figure: Round counter. Four rounds. 'Which hand has the melody?'
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Left hand melody, or right hand melody?

pages.figure: Three cards. 1: top note gold (RH). 2: middle note gold (RH). 3: bass line gold (LH). Banner: 'three voicings — three tomorrow in one drill.'
pages.cleffy: Three voicings now. Top. Middle. Bottom. Tomorrow — all three in one chord. Your choice each round.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 208 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 208. Grand staff with LH line glowing gold, RH chord soft above.
pages.cleffy: Left hand can lead now. Tomorrow — same chord, three voicings in a row. Your hand has to re-tilt every time.

real_piano: Play any LH melody you know. Hold a C-E-G chord in the right hand while you do it. Keep the right hand quiet.
Take a piece you know — swap the roles. RH holds chords, LH plays the top line from memory. Weird, useful practice.

questions.figure: Grand staff with LH quarter notes circled in gold, RH holding a chord
questions.prompt: Which hand has the melody?

questions.figure: Balance diagram — LH weighted heavy, RH light
questions.prompt: When the left hand sings, how should the right hand play?

questions.figure: Three cards: top voicing, middle voicing, bottom voicing
questions.prompt: How many voicings have you met so far?

questions.audio: A passage where the left hand melody is clearly louder than the right hand chord
questions.prompt: Which hand has the melody?
Play C3-D3-E3-F3 in the left hand while holding C-E-G in the right. Bass louder.
questions.accept: LH C3 D3 E3 F3 firm, RH C4 E4 G4 chord softer

remediation.title: Left hand melody, one note at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Role reversal is strange at first. Most pieces put the tune up top. We'll go slow. LH alone. RH alone. Together. Quiet on top, loud on bottom. You'll get it.

completion.cleffy: Three voicings in your hands now. Tomorrow — the drill. Same chord, different voice each round.

title: Voicing drill
subtitle: Act 42 · Voicing
goal: Play the same C major chord three times, each time emphasizing a different voice — top, middle, bottom — on command.
piece_context: Drill day. Three voicings, one chord. Your hand has to re-tilt on demand. This is the skill Träumerei needs tomorrow — and every piece with a hidden melody for the rest of your life.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of three glowing C chords in a row — top note gold, middle note gold, bottom note gold. Banner: ONE CHORD, THREE VOICES.
pages.cleffy: Drill day. One chord. Three voices. Your choice each round. Top, middle, bottom — re-tilt the hand on command.

pages.figure: Three panels in a row. 1: C-E-G, G circled gold. 2: C-E-G, E circled gold. 3: C-E-G, C circled gold. Labels: 'TOP · MIDDLE · BOTTOM.'
pages.cleffy: Three versions of the same chord. Same three keys — C, E, G. Every time. Only the weight changes. Pinky, middle, thumb.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: top. B: middle. C: bottom.

audio.A: C-E-G chord with top G firmer.
audio.B: Same chord with middle E firmer.
audio.C: Same chord with bottom C firmer.

pages.cleffy: Listen to all three. Same notes. Three different chords, really. The ear picks the loudest. That's the melody.

pages.figure: Three cartoon hands in a row. 1: tilted toward pinky. 2: centered, finger 3 deep. 3: tilted toward thumb.
pages.cleffy: Hand positions for each. Top — lean pinky side. Middle — sink finger 3. Bottom — lean thumb side. Three small tilts. Practice switching between them.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G. G4 glowing gold. Banner: 'ROUND 1 · TOP.'
pages.cleffy: Round one — top. Pinky sings. Lean right. Brush the thumb.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G. E4 glowing gold. Banner: 'ROUND 2 · MIDDLE.'
pages.cleffy: Round two — middle. Finger 3 sinks. Thumb and pinky brush.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C-E-G. C4 glowing gold. Banner: 'ROUND 3 · BOTTOM.'
pages.cleffy: Round three — bottom. Thumb sinks. Lean left. Brush the pinky.
pages.followup_cleffy: Three different chords out of the same three keys. Different hand, each time.

pages.figure: Staff. Four chords in a row, same C-E-G each time. Top note glow → middle → bottom → top. Labels under each.
pages.cleffy: Now chain them. Top, middle, bottom, top. Four chords, four tilts. Like spinning a dial.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four chord strikes. Labels: top, middle, bottom, top.
pages.cleffy: Four chords. Re-tilt each time. Top. Middle. Bottom. Top.
pages.followup_cleffy: Your hand just did four different things with the same keys. That's voicing. On demand.

pages.figure: Round counter. Six rounds. 'Which voice sang?'
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Top, middle, or bottom?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Sequence: middle, bottom, top, middle. Labels change row by row.
pages.cleffy: Now out of order. Middle. Bottom. Top. Middle. Keep the tempo steady.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four chords, at 70 bpm. Mixed voicings.
pages.cleffy: Faster. Same mix. Tempo 70. Don't lose the voicing just because the tempo goes up.
pages.followup_cleffy: That was the whole dial. Three voices, any order, at tempo.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 209 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 209. A single chord with three glowing notes stacked, small arrows labeled 'top · middle · bottom.'
pages.cleffy: One chord, three voices, on demand. Tomorrow — Schumann. A piece where the melody lives inside. Act boss.

real_piano: Play C-E-G ten times. Top, middle, bottom — in any order you call out. Say the voice out loud before you press.
Try it on a G chord (G-B-D). A D chord (D-F#-A). Three voicings in every chord you know.

questions.figure: Three identical C-E-G chords, one with top gold, one with middle gold, one with bottom gold
questions.prompt: What's different about these three chords?

questions.figure: A hand tilted toward the thumb
questions.prompt: Which voicing is this hand preparing?

questions.figure: A hand with finger 3 sinking deeper than fingers 1 and 5
questions.prompt: Which voicing is this?

questions.audio: A chord with the bottom note firmer
questions.prompt: Which voice sang?

questions.audio: A chord with the middle note firmer
questions.prompt: Which voice sang?
Play C-E-G three times. First top voiced, then middle, then bottom.
questions.accept: Three C4-E4-G4 chords with distinct emphasis on G4, E4, C4 respectively

remediation.title: One chord, three voices — slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three voicings back-to-back is new. The hand re-tilt takes a minute to feel. We'll slow it down. One voice at a time. Say it out loud before you press.

completion.cleffy: Three voices on demand. The dial spins. Tomorrow — Schumann's Träumerei. Act boss. The masterclass in inner melody.

title: Schumann Träumerei
subtitle: Act 42 · Voicing
goal: Play the opening four bars of Schumann's Träumerei (simplified), bringing out the hidden melody inside the chords.
