piece_context: Act 42 boss. Träumerei — 'Dreaming' — is the seventh piece in Schumann's Kinderszenen, and one of the most beloved miniatures ever written. Its secret: the melody is not always on top. It lives inside the chord. Sometimes the top note. Sometimes tucked in the middle. That's what makes it so hard, and so tender. Today you play the first four bars.

piece.title: Träumerei (from Kinderszenen, Op. 15 No. 7)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — opening bars simplified, voicing is the real challenge

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of an old German songbook, cover reading KINDERSZENEN. A dream-cloud rising off the page. Banner: ACT 42 BOSS — TRÄUMEREI.
pages.cleffy: Act 42 boss. Träumerei. It means 'Dreaming.' Schumann wrote it in 1838. It's a page long. It's been played by every pianist who ever lived.

pages.figure: A photo-style sketch of Schumann, plus the famous opening upbeat of Träumerei on the staff — C4 rising to F4 and the chord.
pages.cleffy: Why this piece? Because the melody is hiding. Sometimes on top. Sometimes inside. Your job — find it and bring it out.

pages.figure: Play button. 'Träumerei — opening four bars, professional recording.'

audio.A: Opening four bars of Träumerei, slow tempo around 66 bpm, melody clearly voiced above the warm chordal texture in F major.

pages.cleffy: Listen once. Don't follow the notes. Just — float. Hear how the melody drifts. How the chords warm underneath.

pages.figure: Staff. The melody line alone: C4 (upbeat) — F4 — A4 — C5 — F4 — D5. Label: 'the melody. Just this. Everything else supports.'
pages.cleffy: Here's the melody — the thing you have to voice. Upbeat C. Then F on the downbeat. Up to A. Up to C. Drop to F. Reach to D. Six notes. That's the tune. Everything else is soft chord.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Melody notes highlighted. Right hand. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. The melody. C, F, A, C, F, D. Slow. Sing it.

pages.figure: Grand staff. LH F3-A3-C4 quarter chords. Label: 'the bed — warm, soft, under the melody.'
pages.cleffy: Underneath, left hand lays a bed. F, A, C — an F major chord, low. Soft. Warm. The melody sits on top of it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. F3, A3, C4 in the LH. Soft label.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. The bed. F-A-C chord. Quiet. Think blanket, not block.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: flat — melody buried. B: voiced — melody clear.

audio.A: The opening phrase played with every note equal volume — the melody is hard to hear.
audio.B: Same phrase with melody notes voiced firmer — the tune emerges.

pages.cleffy: Two versions. First — flat. The chord swallows the tune. You hear mush. Second — melody voiced. The tune rises. The dream starts.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1. RH melody C(upbeat)-F-A. LH F-A-C chord held. Keyboard below.
pages.cleffy: Bar one. Both hands. Right hand sings — C upbeat into F, then A. Left hand holds the chord beneath. Lean into the melody. Brush the bed.

pages.figure: Staff. Bar 2 of Träumerei. An inner voice (A3) circled inside the chord, with an arrow. Label: 'here — the melody goes INSIDE.'
pages.cleffy: Now the secret. In bar two, the melody dips into the middle of the chord. Same tune. Same piece. Different voicing. Middle voice wakes up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A chord with the inner note highlighted. Label: 'inner voice — firmer.'
pages.cleffy: Practice the inner voicing. C-E-A chord, middle E firmer. Finger 3 sinks. Thumb and pinky brush. This is Träumerei's trick.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bars 1-4 simplified. Melody highlighted throughout. Tempo 66.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. Simplified. Right hand has the tune. Left hand holds the warmth. Slow. Think of the title — dreaming.
pages.followup_cleffy: First four bars of Träumerei. A piece every pianist plays at some point in their life. You just did.

pages.figure: Play button. 'Horowitz playing Träumerei, 1987.'

audio.A: A professional recording of the full opening phrase of Träumerei, around 60 bpm, with expressive voicing and rubato.

pages.cleffy: Listen again. Now that you've played it. Hear what a master does with the voicings. Notes the same as yours. The melody never gets lost. No matter where it hides.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 42 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 42 COMPLETE 🎯. A small music book labelled KINDERSZENEN, a tiny dream-cloud rising. Pyramid of acts showing 42/50 lit.
pages.cleffy: Act 42. Closed. Voicing — top, middle, bottom. Schumann's Träumerei, opening bars, in your hands. The melody isn't always on top. You know that now. That. That was a Schumann piece. Big moment.

pages.figure: A door cracking open. Behind it — a blur of whole-tone scales and a watercolor sketch of Debussy. Banner: 'Next — Impressionism.'
pages.cleffy: Next act — Debussy's language. Whole-tone. Pentatonic. Ambiguity. New colors after centuries of tonality. Arabesque waits. See you in 211.

real_piano: Play the four-bar opening of Träumerei once a day. Slow. Around 60 bpm. The melody must sing every time.
Isolate bar 2 — the bar with the inner voicing. Play it twenty times. Until finger 3 sinks automatically.
Listen to two different recordings of Träumerei this week — Horowitz, Argerich, anyone. Hear how they voice differently.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9, 11, and 12 — bar 1, the inner voicing, and the full four-bar run.

questions.figure: Schumann's Kinderszenen cover with Träumerei circled
questions.prompt: Who wrote Träumerei?

questions.figure: The F major key signature — one flat, B-flat
questions.prompt: What key is Träumerei in?

questions.figure: A staff showing the melody diving from the top voice into an inner voice
questions.prompt: What makes Träumerei especially tricky to voice?
options: The melody moves into inner voices — not always on top

questions.audio: The opening phrase with melody clearly voiced
questions.prompt: Is the melody being voiced?

questions.audio: Same phrase with melody buried in the texture
questions.prompt: Can you hear the melody clearly?

questions.audio: Träumerei opening — tempo around 66 bpm
questions.prompt: What is the tempo feel?
Play the opening melody alone, right hand: C, F, A, C, F, D.
questions.accept: C4 F4 A4 C5 F4 D5 played in order with appropriate rhythm

questions.prompt: Play bar 1 with both hands — RH C upbeat into F-A, LH F-A-C chord beneath.
questions.accept: LH F3-A3-C4 chord held while RH plays C4 upbeat into F4, A4 with melody voiced

questions.prompt: Play an inner-voiced chord — C-E-A with the middle E firmer.
questions.accept: C4-E4-A4 chord with audible emphasis on E4

remediation.title: Träumerei, hand by hand
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — expected to stretch. Träumerei asks you to voice while reading a Romantic texture. We'll break it down. Melody alone. Bed alone. One bar at a time. You'll land it — this piece is worth every minute.

completion.cleffy: Act 42 sealed. Schumann in your hands. The melody isn't always on top. The piano has three voices — high, middle, low — and any of them can carry the tune. Next act — Debussy. Impressionism. Whole new language of sound.

title: Impressionist language
subtitle: Act 43 · Impressionism + Arabesque
goal: Hear and play the new sounds of Impressionism — whole-tone and pentatonic scales, no strong tonic, deliberate blur.
piece_context: New act, new world. You've played Classical and Romantic — tonic to dominant, sadness to brightness, clear destinations. Debussy threw that out. He wanted paint, not geometry. Today you meet his vocabulary — the whole-tone scale, the pentatonic, and music that refuses to land.

pages.figure: Cleffy in front of a blurred pastel painting — a lily pad, some water, no sharp edges. Banner: A NEW LANGUAGE.
pages.cleffy: New world. You've played music that goes somewhere. Home, away, home again. Debussy wrote music that floats. No home. Just color.

pages.figure: Two panels. Left: a crisp Classical chord progression with arrows I → V → I. Right: a Debussy chord floating in a blue wash, no arrows. Labels: CLASSICAL · IMPRESSIONIST.
pages.cleffy: Classical and Romantic music moves toward a goal. Tonic, dominant, tonic. Home, tension, home. Debussy didn't want a goal. He wanted a feeling. The blur is the point.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Six notes highlighted: C, D, E, F#, G#, A#. Arrow: 'every step is the same size.'
pages.cleffy: First new sound — the whole-tone scale. Six notes. Every step is a whole step. Same size every time. C, D, E, F-sharp, G-sharp, A-sharp. No half steps. No leading tone. Nothing pulls anywhere.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: whole-tone scale, C up to C.

audio.A: Whole-tone scale ascending and descending slowly, with soft pedal

pages.cleffy: Listen. Hear how it feels like it could stop anywhere? No single note wins. That's the point.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Six notes lighting up in order: C4, D4, E4, F#4, G#4, A#4, then C5.
pages.cleffy: Play it. C, D, E, F-sharp, G-sharp, A-sharp, C. Even. No accent. Let each note blur into the next.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five notes highlighted: C, D, E, G, A. Caption: 'no 4th, no 7th — no tension notes.'
pages.cleffy: Second new sound — pentatonic. Five notes. C, D, E, G, A. The two notes that create tension — the 4th and the 7th — are missing. Nothing can clash. It all just glows.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: a short pentatonic melody over a held low C.

audio.A: A floating pentatonic melody in the right hand over a held low C pedal tone

pages.cleffy: Listen. Every note sits comfortably on top of the low C. No wrong notes. Just shades.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five notes lighting up: C4, D4, E4, G4, A4, C5.
pages.cleffy: Play the pentatonic. C, D, E, G, A, C. Soft. Unhurried. Feel how peaceful it is.

pages.figure: A chord stack: C, E, G, B, D — labelled 'no one note is king.' Arrows floating off it in four directions.
pages.cleffy: Third idea — ambiguity. Debussy stacks chords that could mean two things at once. Is this a C chord with extra color? Or a G chord with a C on the bottom? He doesn't tell you. You just listen.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Five notes light up together: C3, E4, G4, B4, D5.
pages.cleffy: Try one. Press all five, softly, together. C, E, G, B, D. Let them ring. That's the Debussy sound.

pages.figure: Round counter. Six rounds.
pages.cleffy: Six rounds. Whole-tone or pentatonic?

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: a pentatonic line C-E-G-A-E-C. LH: a low C held as a whole note. Dynamic: pp.
pages.cleffy: One little phrase. Left hand holds low C. Right hand walks the pentatonic over it. Very soft. No weight.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 211. A watercolor wash of blue and gold with no sharp edges. Two labels floating in it: 'whole-tone' and 'pentatonic.'
pages.cleffy: New language unlocked. Whole-tone — every step the same. Pentatonic — no tension notes. Ambiguity — no one note in charge. Tomorrow — reading the dense pages Debussy likes to write. Don't panic. Find the shape.

real_piano: Play the whole-tone scale up and down from C, once a day. Slow. No accents.
Improvise on the black keys — that's a pentatonic scale. Low held note in the left hand, any notes in the right. There are no wrong ones.
Listen once to Clair de Lune or Arabesque No. 1. Notice the moments where the music doesn't seem to land.

questions.figure: A scale: C, D, E, F#, G#, A#, C
questions.prompt: Which scale is this?

questions.figure: A scale: C, D, E, G, A, C
questions.prompt: Which scale is this?

questions.figure: Two composers listed: Mozart and Debussy
questions.prompt: Which one writes music with a strong sense of 'home' chord and clear resolutions?

questions.audio: A whole-tone scale played slowly
questions.prompt: Whole-tone or pentatonic?

questions.audio: A floating pentatonic melody
questions.prompt: Whole-tone or pentatonic?
Play the pentatonic scale — C, D, E, G, A, C — softly.
questions.accept: C4 D4 E4 G4 A4 C5 played in order

remediation.title: Impressionist language, one color at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: New sound. Takes a minute to trust it. We'll slow it down — whole-tone first, just hear it. Then pentatonic, just feel it. No rush.

completion.cleffy: First step into Debussy's world. Whole-tone. Pentatonic. Blur on purpose. Tomorrow — his pages look scary. They're not.

title: Reading dense textures
subtitle: Act 43 · Impressionism + Arabesque
goal: Turn a dense, scary-looking page into a handful of simple shapes — scales, arpeggios, chords — instead of reading every note.
piece_context: Debussy pages look like a blizzard. Fifty notes in a bar, six ledger lines, accidentals everywhere. The trick is this — pianists don't read those notes. They read the shape. Today you learn to do the same.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding up an open Debussy score — pages crammed with black ink. His expression: calm, not panicked. Banner: DON'T PANIC.
pages.cleffy: Here's a Debussy page. A lot of ink. A lot of notes. Your first instinct — read every one. That's the wrong instinct.

pages.figure: Left panel: a twelve-note run on the staff, each note labeled individually. Right panel: the same run with one big arrow over it labelled 'C major scale, up an octave.'
pages.cleffy: Real pianists don't read twelve notes. They read one shape. That shape is a C major scale going up. One thought instead of twelve.

pages.figure: Three panels. 1: 'SCALE — stepwise, one direction.' 2: 'ARPEGGIO — skipping in a chord pattern.' 3: 'CHORD BLOCK — stacked notes, played together.'
pages.cleffy: Three shapes show up again and again. Scale — stepwise, one direction. Arpeggio — a chord broken out, one note at a time. Chord block — notes stacked, played together. Most pages are some mix of these three.

pages.figure: A busy-looking RH line on the treble staff — eight sixteenth notes going up by step from C to C. Highlight box drawn around it: 'SCALE.'
pages.cleffy: See it? Eight notes. Each one a step above the last. That's a scale — one thing, not eight.

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4 through C5 lighting up in order.
pages.cleffy: Play it as one shape. C major scale, one octave. Don't think in notes. Think 'scale up.'

pages.figure: Staff: C4-E4-G4-C5, rolled as sixteenth notes. Highlight box: 'ARPEGGIO.'
pages.cleffy: Same trick, different shape. Four notes, skipping in threes. That's a C major chord rolled out. One thought — 'C arpeggio up.'

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4, C5 lighting up in order.
pages.cleffy: Play the arpeggio. C, E, G, C. One shape.

pages.figure: Staff: a stack of four notes — C, E, G, B — with a quarter note stem. Highlight box: 'CHORD.'
pages.cleffy: Third shape — a chord block. Four notes. Played together. One thought — 'C chord with a B on top.'

pages.figure: Keyboard. C4, E4, G4, B4 lighting up together.
pages.cleffy: Press all four at once. C, E, G, B. Soft. Feel the color.

pages.figure: A messy-looking Debussy-style excerpt. Overlaid with three coloured boxes: blue box 'scale', green box 'arpeggio', gold box 'chord.' Caption: 'same page, three chunks.'
pages.cleffy: Look. The whole scary bar is three chunks. Scale up. Arpeggio up. Chord at the end. Your eye reads three things — not thirty.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The full sequence plays out in three visual groups.
pages.cleffy: Play the whole bar. First — scale C up to C. Then — C arpeggio up. Then — the chord. Three chunks, one breath.
pages.followup_cleffy: Sixteen notes. Three thoughts. That's how pianists read dense pages.

pages.figure: A short Debussy-style excerpt, two bars. Boxes drawn: 'pentatonic run', 'held chord', 'arpeggio down.'
pages.cleffy: Same trick works on Debussy. Big page, small number of shapes. A pentatonic run. A held chord. An arpeggio coming down. Three thoughts. That's the whole bar.

pages.figure: Grand staff, Debussy-style. RH: a slow descending pentatonic run. LH: a held low C then an arpeggio down C-G-E-C. Marked pp.
pages.cleffy: Play it. Right hand — pentatonic coming down. Left hand — held C, then a slow arpeggio down. Two hands, a few shapes.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 212. A Debussy page with three big friendly boxes drawn on it — scale, arpeggio, chord.
pages.cleffy: Density is a disguise. Underneath — a few shapes. Scale. Arpeggio. Chord. Tomorrow — the signature Debussy texture. Flowing right hand, rolling left.

real_piano: Open any piece you're working on. Circle every scale chunk, every arpeggio, every chord. See how little is left.
Play the C scale, then a C arpeggio, then a C chord — back to back. Every day. Eye sees one shape, hand plays it.

questions.figure: Eight notes on the staff moving up by step: C, D, E, F, G, A, B, C
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.figure: Four notes on the staff: C, E, G, C — each one skipping
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.figure: A stack of three notes played together: C, E, G
questions.prompt: What shape is this?

questions.audio: A C major scale played up and down
questions.prompt: Scale, arpeggio, or chord?
Play a C major arpeggio — C, E, G, C.
questions.accept: C4 E4 G4 C5 played in order

remediation.title: Finding shapes, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Dense pages feel like a wall at first. Normal. We'll break it down — one shape at a time, in isolation. You'll see the trick.

completion.cleffy: Dense pages decoded. Scale. Arpeggio. Chord. Three shapes. Most music is some mix. Tomorrow — arabesque. Flowing lines over rolling waves.

title: Arabesque patterns
subtitle: Act 43 · Impressionism + Arabesque
goal: Play the Debussy-style arabesque texture — a long, flowing right-hand line over a rolling left-hand arpeggio figure.
piece_context: An 'arabesque' — from Arabic decorative art — is a long curling line, like vines on a wall. In piano, it's a flowing right-hand melody that winds for bars at a time, while the left hand rolls underneath like water. It's the signature Debussy texture. Today you build it from the two parts.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at a curling vine-like shape painted on a wall. Title: ARABESQUE. Underneath: 'a long flowing line.'
pages.cleffy: Arabesque. The word comes from Arabic decorative art — long curling vines. In piano, it's a right-hand line that winds forever.

pages.figure: Grand staff split into two colored bands. Top band (blue): a long curving line of eighth notes. Bottom band (gold): rolling arpeggio patterns in groups of three. Label: TWO LAYERS.
pages.cleffy: The texture has two layers. Top — long, flowing right hand. One line, many notes. Bottom — left hand rolls through chords. Same shape, again and again. One sings. One flows.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: Debussy-style arabesque texture, slow.

audio.A: A slow arabesque — right hand flowing line over left hand rolling triplets, soft, pedaled

pages.cleffy: Listen. Right hand — a melody you could follow with your finger. Left hand — a cushion of rolling notes underneath. No weight. No hurry.

pages.figure: Bass staff: a repeating pattern — E2, B2, E3, G#3 in triplet eighths — bracketed as one group. Arrow showing the pattern repeats.
pages.cleffy: Start with the left hand. A four-note shape — low E, B, E, G-sharp. Rolled, not blocked. One after the other, smoothly. Same shape, over and over.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E2, B2, E3, G#3 lighting up in order.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. Play the roll. E, B, E, G-sharp. Soft. Even. Let it ring.

pages.figure: Keyboard. The same pattern lighting up three times in a row.
pages.cleffy: Three times in a row. Same pattern. No stops between. This is your cushion.

pages.figure: Treble staff: a slow descending line — B4, G#4, F#4, E4 — as quarter notes. Label: 'the vine.'
pages.cleffy: Now the right hand. A simple line. B, G-sharp, F-sharp, E. Coming down. Long notes over the rolling cushion below.

pages.figure: Keyboard. B4, G#4, F#4, E4 lighting up in order, as slow quarter notes.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. Slow. B, G-sharp, F-sharp, E. Let each note sing before the next one arrives.

pages.figure: Grand staff: one bar. RH quarter note B over LH four eighths E-B-E-G#. Arrows showing: 'RH note 1 lines up with LH note 1.'
pages.cleffy: How they fit. The right-hand note arrives at the start of each group. Left hand rolls four notes underneath while it rings. One long note sitting on top of four short ones.

pages.figure: Grand staff. One bar. RH: B4 quarter. LH: E2-B2-E3-G#3 eighths underneath.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Very slow. Left hand starts — E, B, E, G-sharp. Right hand places B on top at the start and holds.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars. RH: B4 quarter, G#4 quarter, F#4 quarter, E4 quarter. LH: the four-note roll, twice.
pages.cleffy: Two bars now. Right hand walks down — B, G-sharp, F-sharp, E. Left hand rolls underneath the whole time.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's an arabesque. One line above. One cushion below. Two layers, one sound.

pages.figure: Diagram. RH quarter note held long. LH rolling pattern. A soft-pedal icon. Caption: 'pedal holds the layers together.'
pages.cleffy: Why it floats. Pedal. Soft touch. No accent on any note. The left-hand notes blur into each other. The melody above doesn't crash — it drifts. That's the whole Debussy trick.

pages.figure: Same two bars. A dynamic hairpin under the RH — tiny swell on the second note, sigh at the end.
pages.cleffy: One more time. Now with breath. Let B sing slightly brighter. Sigh down to E. Left hand stays even — it's the water, not the melody.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 213. A vine curling across the top of a page, ripples flowing underneath.
pages.cleffy: Arabesque built. One flowing line. One rolling cushion. Tomorrow — same texture, faster. And two different rhythms at once.

real_piano: Left-hand roll only — E, B, E, G-sharp, repeat. Two minutes a day. Even, soft.
Both hands, two bars, very slow. Eyes on the right hand, ears on the left.
Once it feels steady, try it with the sustain pedal. Change the pedal each time the right hand changes notes.

questions.figure: Grand staff with a long slow melody on top and a rolling arpeggio on the bottom
questions.prompt: What is this texture called?

questions.figure: A repeating four-note shape in the bass: E, B, E, G#
questions.prompt: How is this played?
options: All at once, as a chord
Rolled, one note at a time

questions.figure: The word 'arabesque' with its origin listed as 'Arabic decorative art'
questions.prompt: What does the word 'arabesque' describe?

questions.audio: A flowing RH melody over a rolling LH arpeggio
questions.prompt: Which hand has the melody?
Play the left-hand roll — E2, B2, E3, G#3 — smooth and even.
questions.accept: E2 B2 E3 G#3 played in order

remediation.title: Arabesque, one layer at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two layers is a lot the first time. We'll go one at a time. Left hand alone until it's automatic. Right hand alone. Then stack. No rush.

completion.cleffy: Arabesque unlocked. One layer above, one below. Two hands, one sound. Tomorrow — faster. And the two hands in different rhythms.

title: Hand independence, fast
subtitle: Act 43 · Impressionism + Arabesque
goal: Play two different rhythms at once — right hand in triplets, left hand in quarters — smoothly and at tempo.
piece_context: Arabesque No. 1 has a famous trick — the right hand plays three notes to every left-hand beat. It's called 'two against three' or 'polyrhythm.' Sounds scary. It's not. You stop thinking about matching them up and let each hand run its own clock. Today you build that skill.

pages.figure: Cleffy with two clocks — one on each hand. Left clock ticks in 4. Right clock ticks in 3. Banner: TWO CLOCKS.
pages.cleffy: Today's trick — two hands, two rhythms, at the same time. Left hand goes one way. Right hand goes another. Two clocks, one brain.

pages.figure: A grid. Top row: three even notes spanning one beat, labelled 'RH triplet — three in one beat.' Bottom row: one note on the beat, labelled 'LH quarter — one on the beat.'
pages.cleffy: The classic Debussy pattern. Right hand — three notes per beat. A triplet. Left hand — one note per beat. A quarter. Three against one. They only meet at the start.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: a slow two-against-three pattern, RH and LH separated by ear.

audio.A: Right-hand triplets flowing over left-hand quarter notes, slow and even

pages.cleffy: Listen. Left hand is steady — thump, thump, thump. Right hand rolls three over each thump. They line up only on the downbeat.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E4, G#4, B4 lighting in sequence. Labeled TRIPLET.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone first. Three notes, one beat. E, G-sharp, B. Even. No accent on any one.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same three notes, lighting for four beats in a row.
pages.cleffy: Four beats of triplets. Twelve notes total. Don't rush. Three-per-beat, steady.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E2, B2, E2, B2 lighting on the beat.
pages.cleffy: Left hand alone. Four steady quarters. E, B, E, B. One per beat.

pages.figure: Grid showing four beats. Left column: one LH note per beat. Right column: three RH notes per beat. A vertical line shows them matching only at beat 1, 2, 3, 4 — not between.
pages.cleffy: They meet on every beat. Between the beats, the hands don't line up. Don't try to match them. Let each hand run its own clock.

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing at his head. Two thought bubbles — one says 'left on the beat,' the other says 'three in the gap.'
pages.cleffy: The trick. Set the left hand first. Steady. Your anchor. Then drop three right-hand notes into each gap. Don't count six. Just feel three.

pages.figure: Grand staff. One bar, 4 beats. LH: E2, B2, E2, B2. RH: triplets E-G#-B on each beat.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Very slow — tempo 40. Left hand sets the pulse. Right hand rolls over it. First time is always clunky. That's fine.

pages.figure: Same bar. Tempo bumped to 50.
pages.cleffy: Same pattern. A little faster. Tempo 50. Keep the left hand steady. Don't let the right hand drag.

pages.figure: Same bar. Tempo 60. Label: AT TEMPO.
pages.cleffy: Tempo 60. The actual Debussy feel. Let it flow now. Don't fight the two clocks — trust them.
pages.followup_cleffy: Two against three. Your hands just did two different things at the same time, at tempo. Big skill.

pages.figure: A preview page of Arabesque No. 1 — RH triplets, LH quarters, circled together.
pages.cleffy: This exact pattern lives all over Arabesque No. 1. Tomorrow — you'll see it on the real page. Today you built the engine.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars of the same pattern — eight RH triplets over eight LH quarters.
pages.cleffy: Two full bars now. Keep it going. Left hand metronome. Right hand flow.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 214. Two clocks side by side, hands working in different directions, both keeping time.
pages.cleffy: Two clocks running. One brain. Tomorrow — the piece this was all for. Arabesque No. 1. Debussy's first love-letter to the piano.

real_piano: Left hand alone, four steady quarters. Once that's automatic, add right-hand triplets.
Two bars of the pattern, every day. Start at 40 bpm. Only speed up when it's clean.
Don't count to six. Feel three-on-each. Each hand trusts itself.
virtual_only: Replay pages 9, 10, and 11 — the speed ladder

questions.figure: A grid with 3 notes in the top row and 1 note in the bottom row, per beat
questions.prompt: What is this rhythm called?

questions.figure: RH plays triplets, LH plays quarters. Arrows point to beat 1 and beat 2.
questions.prompt: Where do the hands line up exactly?

questions.figure: Cleffy thought bubble: 'set the ___ first, then drop three notes into each gap'
questions.prompt: Which hand sets the pulse?

questions.audio: Right-hand triplets over left-hand quarter notes
questions.prompt: Which hand has three notes per beat?
Play one bar — RH triplets E-G#-B on each beat, LH quarters E-B-E-B.
questions.accept: RH triplets over LH quarters for one bar at around 60 bpm

remediation.title: Two clocks, slower
remediation.opening_cleffy: Two against three is the one every piano student stumbles on. We'll go really slow — left hand alone, right hand alone, then together at 30 bpm. Trust me. It clicks.

completion.cleffy: Two clocks, ticking. Tomorrow — Debussy's first love-letter. Arabesque No. 1. Everything this week built toward it.

title: Arabesque No. 1 (A section)
subtitle: Act 43 · Impressionism + Arabesque
goal: Read and play the opening A section of Debussy's Arabesque No. 1 in E major — simplified — with the floating, unhurried feel the piece asks for.
piece_context: Debussy wrote the two Arabesques when he was twenty-six. Arabesque No. 1 in E major is his first real Impressionist voice on the piano — the flowing line, the triplet rolls, the refusal to rush. The opening is a descent: right hand rolling down from a high E, left hand placing chords underneath. Today you play that opening. Slowly. The whole point is to float.

piece.title: Arabesque No. 1 (A section — opening, simplified)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — triplet descents over rolled chords, soft throughout

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a faded score — 'Deux Arabesques · Claude Debussy · 1888.' Act 43 boss banner. Four small thumbnails at the bottom: whole-tone, dense page, arabesque texture, two clocks.
pages.cleffy: Act 43 boss. Arabesque No. 1. Debussy, age twenty-six. Everything this week was for this.

pages.figure: A watercolor of a garden. Caption: 'Arabesque No. 1 in E major — 1888.' Small text: 'two sharps, simplified.'
pages.cleffy: Debussy wrote this before Clair de Lune. It's his first time sounding like himself. Flowing. Soft. No hurry to get anywhere.

pages.figure: Grand staff. RH: a slow triplet descent starting high — E5, B4, G#4 — repeating down. LH: a rolled chord E2-B2-E3-G#3. Tempo 60. Dynamic pp.
pages.cleffy: We're teaching Arabesque's accompaniment texture today — the rolling triplet figure that runs underneath the melody. Right hand — triplets falling from the top. E, B, G-sharp. Then lower. Left hand — a rolled chord. The same four notes you practiced yesterday. Tempo 60. Soft all the way.

pages.figure: Play button. A still frame of a dawn sky.

audio.A: Opening bars of Arabesque No. 1, slow, soft, pedaled

pages.cleffy: Listen. Right hand rolls down, then down again, then drifts. Left hand holds the ground but never grabs it. Nothing is in a hurry.

pages.figure: Key signature with F# and C#. Caption: 'E major key signature — but we add G# and D# as we go.'
pages.cleffy: Key note. E major — two sharps, F-sharp and C-sharp. But Debussy colors the melody with more — G-sharp shows up, D-sharp too. Don't panic at the sharps. They're just the notes of the tune.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E5, B4, G#4 lighting in order.
pages.cleffy: Right hand. First triplet — E, B, G-sharp. Starting high. Rolling down.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Two triplets in a row — E5-B4-G#4, then B4-G#4-E4.
pages.cleffy: Two triplets. Coming down. E, B, G-sharp. Then B, G-sharp, E. Like water falling down two ledges.

pages.figure: Keyboard. E2, B2, E3, G#3 lighting up in order, not together.
pages.cleffy: Left hand. The chord, rolled. E, B, E, G-sharp. Same cushion you played yesterday. Soft. Even.

pages.figure: Grand staff. One bar. RH: triplet E-B-G# then B-G#-E. LH: the rolled chord E-B-E-G# underneath.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Tempo 50. Very slow. Left hand rolls the chord. Right hand drops two triplets on top. Don't match them up. Let each hand run.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars of simplified A section. Bar 1: E5-B4-G#4 / B4-G#4-E4 descent over the rolled chord. Bar 2: G#4-E4-B3 / E4-B3-G#3 continues down. Bar 3: the line floats back up E4-G#4-B4 / G#4-B4-E5. Bar 4: a held chord on E, pp. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. The whole A section, simplified. Bars 1-2 — the descent. All the way down. Bar 3 — the line floats back up to where it started. Bar 4 — rest on an E chord. Let it ring.

pages.figure: Right hand only. All four bars of the RH triplet line.
pages.cleffy: Right hand only. The whole line. Down. Down. Back up. Rest. Slow as you need.

pages.figure: Left hand only. Four bars. A rolled chord at the start of each bar, then held.
pages.cleffy: Left hand only. Four rolled chords, one per bar. E, B, E, G-sharp. Same cushion, again and again. Let each one ring.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full four bars. Tempo 50. Dynamic pp. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Tempo 50. Guides on. The whole A section. Float. Don't press.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Full four bars. Tempo 60. Guides off. Dynamic pp.
pages.cleffy: Tempo 60 now. Guides off. This is the real feel. Float. Don't ground. Let it blur a little.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was Debussy's first love-letter to the piano. Four bars of the real thing, in your hands.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 43 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Four quick ones. Act boss — take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 43 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy under a watercolor sky. Behind him: a music stand with the Arabesque score open. A pyramid showing 43/50 acts lit.
pages.cleffy: Act 43. Sealed. You speak Impressionism now. Whole-tone and pentatonic. Dense pages as three shapes. Arabesque texture. Two rhythms at once. And the opening of Debussy's Arabesque No. 1 — floating, not grounded. Next act — Romantic phrase shape. Long lines. Long breaths. Grieg.

real_piano: The A section of Arabesque No. 1, once a day. Very slow. Very soft. Don't rush it to speed — the piece doesn't want to be fast.
Play it for someone. Turn the lights down. Tell them it's Debussy — his first Impressionist piece.
When the two-against-three feels wobbly, drop back to hands separately for one day. That's not failure — that's how pianists practice.
virtual_only: Replay pages 13 and 14 until the full four bars feels comfortable

questions.figure: The title 'Arabesque No. 1' with composer 'Debussy' and year '1888'
questions.prompt: What key is this simplified version in?

questions.figure: RH plays triplets, LH plays a rolled chord, marked pp
questions.prompt: How should this be played?

questions.figure: A triplet on beat 1: E, B, G#
questions.prompt: How many notes does the right hand play per beat here?

questions.audio: The opening of Arabesque No. 1
questions.prompt: Is this music grounded and marching, or floating and unhurried?

questions.audio: A two-against-three pattern — RH triplets over LH quarters
questions.prompt: Which hand has three notes per beat?
Play the first right-hand triplet of the piece — E, B, G-sharp, starting high.
questions.accept: E5 B4 G#4 played as a triplet

questions.prompt: Play the left-hand rolled chord — E2, B2, E3, G-sharp 3, smooth and even.
questions.accept: E2 B2 E3 G#3 played in order

remediation.title: Arabesque, one layer at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — expected to stretch you. We'll rebuild it slowly. Left hand alone for a page. Right hand alone for a page. Then one bar at a time, together. You'll land it. The piece itself doesn't want you to rush.

completion.cleffy: Act 43 sealed. Impressionism — no longer a foreign language. The opening of Arabesque No. 1 — yours. Next — Romantic phrase shape. Long lines. The art of a musical breath.

title: Romantic phrase shape
subtitle: Act 44 · Long lines
goal: Hear and play phrases that stretch across 8 to 16 bars — not the short Classical sentences, the long Romantic breath.
piece_context: New act. Four lessons of Romantic expression, then Grieg's Arietta as the boss. Today — the first shift. Classical phrases are short sentences. Romantic phrases are paragraphs. Learn to breathe across a longer span.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano. Above him, two arcs drawn — a short one labeled CLASSICAL (4 bars), a long one labeled ROMANTIC (12 bars). Banner: LONG LINES.
pages.cleffy: New act. Four lessons, then Grieg. Today — the long line. Classical phrases are short. Romantic phrases stretch.

pages.figure: Side-by-side staves. Left: Mozart-style — four tidy 4-bar phrases, each with its own arch. Right: Chopin-style — one enormous 16-bar arch over the whole system.
pages.cleffy: Look at both. Mozart — four short sentences. Breath after each. Chopin — one long thought. The breath waits.

pages.figure: Two play buttons. A: Classical. B: Romantic.

audio.A: A Mozart-style melody with clear 4-bar phrase breaks
audio.B: A Chopin-style melody that sustains one arc across 12 bars without obvious breaths

pages.cleffy: Listen. First the Classical shape. Short breaths. Then the Romantic. One long one. Feel the difference in your chest.

pages.figure: A 12-bar phrase drawn as a single huge arc. Start p, climb through bars 5-7, peak at bar 8, descend through bars 9-12. No internal breath marks.
pages.cleffy: The Romantic breath. You start. You keep going. You don't let go until the whole sentence is done. One breath. Twelve bars. Patience.

pages.figure: A long Chopin-like phrase plays. A visual arc traces along as it moves — rising, peaking, falling.

audio.A: A 12-bar Romantic melody with a gentle climb to a mid-phrase peak, then a long descent

pages.cleffy: One sentence, twelve bars. Notice where it rises. Notice where it lands. You should be able to sing it in one breath — even if that breath is long.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A small 4-bar Classical phrase. C4 D4 E4 F4 G4 F4 E4 D4.
pages.cleffy: Start with the short one. Four bars. Simple arch. Breath at the end.

pages.figure: A longer 8-bar phrase drawn on the staff. Arch marked: p at start, build through bars 3-5, peak at bar 6, soften to p by bar 8. Pencil annotations shown.
pages.cleffy: Now longer. Eight bars. Before you play — plan. Where does it rise? Where does it peak? Where does it land?

pages.figure: Keyboard. 8-bar ascending-then-descending line. Peak at the E5. Long arch drawn above the whole thing.
pages.cleffy: Eight bars. One breath. Soft start. Grow slowly. Peak on the E. Release all the way to the last C. Don't stop growing in the middle. Don't rush the descent.
pages.followup_cleffy: Longer than you're used to. That's the point.

pages.figure: Same 8-bar phrase. Little breath-mark ticks shown between every bar — each one has a red X through it. A single breath mark at the very end has a check.
pages.cleffy: Resist the little breaths. Bar-lines aren't breaths in Romantic music. The only real breath is at the end of the sentence.

pages.figure: Round counter. 4 rounds. Banner: CLASSICAL OR ROMANTIC?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Short breaths or long?

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same 8-bar phrase. Dynamic arc labeled: p — cresc — mf — dim — p. Long, slow shaping.
pages.cleffy: Same eight bars. Now shape the dynamics. p at the start. Slow crescendo. mf around the peak. Slow diminuendo home. The growth is gentle. Not a staircase — a hill.

pages.figure: Cleffy with a hand over his chest. Speech bubble: 'If I can't sing it, I can't play it.'
pages.cleffy: The old trick. Before you play a long line, sing it. Out loud or in your head. If your voice can hold the arc, your hands can too.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A new 8-bar line, now with LH drone on C2. Long arch.
pages.cleffy: One more. Same shape, different notes. LH holds a low C. RH sings the long line.
pages.followup_cleffy: Long breath. Held all the way.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 216. One enormous arch over a long Romantic phrase. Below: 'one breath, many bars.'
pages.cleffy: The long line. Romantic breath. Tomorrow — plan a big arc over 16 bars. Climb and release.

real_piano: Pick any slow piece you know. Try playing eight bars as one phrase — no little breaths between bars.
Sing the melody first, out loud. Then play it. Notice if your hands breathe less when your voice does.
virtual_only: Replay page 8 and page 11 — the long phrase, with and without dynamics.

questions.figure: Two phrases. Left: four 4-bar arches. Right: one 16-bar arch.
questions.prompt: Which one is the Romantic shape?

questions.figure: A long phrase with small breath marks drawn between every bar.
questions.prompt: In a Romantic phrase, what's wrong with this?
options: Too many breaths — the line should hold

questions.figure: A 12-bar phrase with a single long arc drawn above.
questions.prompt: What does the arc tell you?
options: One breath, across all twelve bars

questions.audio: A long Chopin-style phrase sustaining across many bars without obvious breaks
questions.prompt: Classical short breaths or Romantic long line?
Play the 8-bar line — C E G C D E D C B G E C — as one long breath, with a gentle arch.
questions.accept: All 12 notes played with growth toward the E5 and release toward the final C4, no obvious gap

remediation.title: The long breath, gentler
remediation.opening_cleffy: The long line is a stretch. It takes patience your fingers don't have yet. We'll start with four bars. Then six. Then eight. Slowly.

completion.cleffy: The first Romantic breath. Tomorrow — plan the whole 16-bar arc. Climb, peak, release.

title: Dynamic arc over 16 bars
subtitle: Act 44 · Plan the climb
goal: Plan and play a dynamic arc across 16 bars — pencil cresc. and dim. where they belong, then execute.
piece_context: Yesterday — the long line exists. Today — you plan its shape. Sixteen bars is long enough that you have to decide, with a pencil, where the climb starts and where the release begins. Most students don't plan. That's why their playing sounds flat.

pages.figure: A 16-bar system on a staff. A huge, gentle arc drawn above it — labeled CLIMB, PEAK, RELEASE. Below: a pencil.
pages.cleffy: Yesterday — the long line. Today — you plan it. Pencil in hand.

pages.figure: A horizontal bar split into three regions. Bars 1-6: CLIMB. Bars 7-9: PEAK. Bars 10-16: RELEASE. Gentle arc on top.
pages.cleffy: Three parts to a long arc. The climb. The peak. The release. Each one has its own job.

pages.figure: Three arcs side by side. Peak at bar 4 (too early). Peak at bar 8 (middle — balanced). Peak at bar 14 (too late).
pages.cleffy: Where should the peak live? Usually around two-thirds through. Bar 10 or 11 of 16. Not the middle. A little past.

pages.figure: A 16-bar phrase plays. A moving arc traces above it — rising slowly for 10 bars, landing, then settling for 6.

audio.A: A 16-bar melody with a slow 10-bar crescendo to a gentle peak, then a longer 6-bar descent to pp

pages.cleffy: Sixteen bars. One arc. Notice — the climb takes longer than the release. The peak sits past halfway. That's the Romantic rule.

pages.figure: A 16-bar phrase with pencil marks written in: 'p' at bar 1, 'cresc.' starting at bar 3, 'mf' at bar 8, 'f' at bar 10 (peak), 'dim.' starting at bar 11, 'mp' at bar 14, 'p' at bar 16.
pages.cleffy: Before you play — write it in. Literally. p at the start. cresc. above the rising bars. mf where it lands. f at the peak. dim. then p at the end. Your pencil does the thinking once. Your hands do it every day after.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First 8 bars of a scale-based Romantic phrase. Climbing. p → mf.
pages.cleffy: First half. Eight bars of climb. Start p. Grow slowly. End at mf. Don't rush the growth. Let each bar be a little fuller than the last.

pages.figure: Zoomed-in view of the peak bar. Notes getting bigger. 'f' marked. A tiny star on the highest note.
pages.cleffy: The peak is one moment. Not a whole section — one bar, maybe one note. That's the place everything is heading toward.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Four bars around the peak. f at the high A5, then dim. starting immediately.
pages.cleffy: The peak area. Arrive at f. Hold that moment. Then start letting go right away. The release begins as soon as the peak is in the air.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Last 8 bars. Descending. f → p.
pages.cleffy: Second half. Eight bars of release. Start around f. Soften gradually. End at p. The descent takes patience — don't collapse to quiet in one bar.

pages.figure: The big 16-bar arc. Inside it — tiny small arches on every 2-bar unit. Caption: 'small waves inside the big one.'
pages.cleffy: One more thing. Inside the big arc, smaller waves live. Each 2-bar group has its own tiny rise and fall — but they all add up to the big one.

pages.figure: Round counter. 4 rounds. Banner: WHERE'S THE PEAK?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Where does the peak sit?

pages.figure: Keyboard. All 16 bars. Full arc drawn above. Tempo 72.
pages.cleffy: Now the whole thing. Sixteen bars. One arc. Climb. Peak. Release. Plan it. Play it. Listen.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's how a long phrase sounds when it's planned.

pages.figure: A piece of real sheet music on a music stand. Pencil marks visible: cresc., dim., a circled peak note. Caption: 'Every pianist does this.'
pages.cleffy: Real pianists mark their music. Peak note — circle it. Cresc — draw the line. Dim — draw it. Don't try to remember. Write it.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 217. The 16-bar arc, with cresc. and dim. penciled in. Below: 'plan the climb, plan the release.'
pages.cleffy: A plan for a long arc. Tomorrow — rubato. Push and pull. Time bends too.

real_piano: Take a piece you're learning. Find a 16-bar section. With a pencil, mark where the peak is and where cresc. / dim. go.
Play that section three times, following your own plan.
virtual_only: Replay page 12 — the full 16-bar arc — until the shape is in your ears.

questions.figure: A 16-bar phrase with an arc peaking at bar 11.
questions.prompt: Where does the Romantic arc usually peak?
options: Around two-thirds through, past the middle

questions.figure: Three parts of a long arc labeled.
questions.prompt: What are the three parts of a long dynamic arc?

questions.figure: Sheet music with 'cresc.' penciled in above bars 3-10.
questions.prompt: Why do pianists pencil cresc. onto the page?
options: To make the page look nicer
So the plan is visible every time they play

questions.audio: A 16-bar phrase with a slow climb, peak past the middle, and a long release
questions.prompt: Where does the peak sit in this phrase?
Play an 8-bar climbing line from p to mf, without rushing the growth.
questions.accept: Any ascending 8-bar line with audible, gradual crescendo

remediation.title: The 16-bar arc, in pieces
remediation.opening_cleffy: Sixteen bars is a lot to plan. Most students skip the pencil step. We'll break it into four 4-bar chunks. Plan each. Then stitch.

completion.cleffy: A plan on the page. A shape in the hands. Tomorrow — time bends. Push-pull rubato.

title: Tempo flexibility
subtitle: Act 44 · Push and pull
goal: Play push-pull rubato — speed up slightly toward a peak, stretch on the landing. Subtler than simple rubato.
piece_context: You've seen rubato twice already — in Act 29 and Act 40. Today — refine it. Beginners think rubato means 'slow down at the end.' It's more than that. Real rubato pushes toward the peak and pulls at the landing. A gentle push, then a gentle stretch. Breathing in time.

pages.figure: Two arrows over a phrase. First arrow — small push forward (→→). Second — small pull back (←). Banner: PUSH AND PULL.
pages.cleffy: Rubato again. Third time. This time — push AND pull. Not just slow at the end.

pages.figure: A phrase. A red arrow bending down at the last note only. Caption: 'Just a ritardando. Flat everywhere else.'
pages.cleffy: Most beginners think rubato is one thing. Slow down at the end. That's a ritardando. Real rubato is bigger.

pages.figure: Same phrase. Two arrows — a small forward push in bars 3-4 (approaching peak), a small backward stretch in bar 7 (the landing).
pages.cleffy: Real rubato. A tiny push as you climb toward the peak. Like leaning in. A tiny stretch as you land. Like breathing out. Both. Small.

pages.figure: Three play buttons. A: metronomic. B: ritardando only. C: true push-pull rubato.

audio.A: A phrase played perfectly in time, no flex
audio.B: Same phrase, only slowing at the very end
audio.C: Same phrase with a slight push toward the peak and a slight stretch on the landing note

pages.cleffy: Three versions. Flat. Slow-at-the-end. Push-pull. The third breathes.

pages.figure: A scale. Left pan up (push — faster). Right pan down (pull — slower). Caption: 'Time stolen here is paid back there.'
pages.cleffy: The Italian word — rubato — means stolen. Time you steal, you pay back. Push a little faster here. Pull a little slower there. It evens out.

pages.figure: Keyboard. 8-bar phrase. Peak on the E5. Tempo 80 — metronome ticking dead steady.
pages.cleffy: First — metronomic. Dead steady. Feel what no flex sounds like.

pages.figure: The 8-bar phrase. Arrow marked 'push' over bars 3-5 (climbing toward peak). Arrow marked 'pull' over the landing at bar 6-7.
pages.cleffy: Where to push — the bars right before the peak. The approach. Where to pull — the peak itself, and the first bar after. Small amounts. Like breathing a little faster up a hill.

pages.figure: Same keyboard. 8-bar phrase. Push arrow on bars 3-5, pull on bar 6-7. Tempo still 80 but with human flex.
pages.cleffy: Same notes. Now with flex. Lean forward into the climb. Stretch across the peak. Don't exaggerate — it should still feel like the same tempo overall.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's rubato. Push then pull.

pages.figure: Two boxes. Box 1: 'Too much push — racing' — an arrow zooming off the page. Box 2: 'Too much pull — dragging' — a snail. Caption: 'Subtle beats big.'
pages.cleffy: Two mistakes. Too much push — it sounds panicky. Too much pull — it sounds like the music died. Small is the whole trick.

pages.figure: Round counter. 4 rounds. Banner: SUBTLE OR OVERDONE?
pages.cleffy: Four rounds. Tasteful push-pull, or too much?

pages.figure: Snippet of a Chopin Nocturne. One long phrase. Arrows: tiny push up to the peak (high Bb4 circled), tiny pull on the landing. Caption: 'What Chopin actually asked for.'
pages.cleffy: Chopin wrote about this. 'The left hand keeps time. The right hand breathes.' Left hand steady. Right hand pushes and pulls over it.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A Chopin-flavored RH phrase with LH chord on each beat. Tempo 70. Push-pull arrows.
pages.cleffy: Chopin-style. LH holds the time. RH flexes. Bb4 is the peak. Push toward it. Stretch on it. Settle home.

pages.figure: Keyboard. A final 8-bar phrase. Arrows: push across bars 3-4, pull on the landing at bar 6. Gentle.
pages.cleffy: One more. Your call on how much. Lean forward. Then breathe out.
pages.followup_cleffy: The music can breathe in time now.

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 218. A phrase with tiny push and pull arrows. Below: 'steal time, give it back.'
pages.cleffy: Push-pull rubato. Subtler than slowing down. Tomorrow — memorization. How pianists actually do it.

real_piano: Pick a slow phrase from any piece. Find its peak. Try leaning forward into it, then stretching on the landing note.
Record yourself with and without flex. The second one will breathe — if you kept it small.
virtual_only: Replay page 4 — the three versions — and page 8, the push-pull play.

questions.figure: A phrase with a small push arrow before the peak and a small pull arrow on the landing.
questions.prompt: What is this shape called?

questions.figure: A phrase where only the last note is stretched.
questions.prompt: Is this full rubato?

questions.figure: A scale with one pan up and one pan down, labeled 'stolen' and 'given back.'
questions.prompt: What does rubato mean literally?

questions.audio: A phrase with a small forward push to the peak and a small stretch on the landing
questions.prompt: Is this metronomic, ritardando-only, or push-pull rubato?
Play an 8-bar phrase with a gentle push to the peak and a stretch on the landing.
questions.accept: Any 8-bar phrase with audible tempo flex — slight acceleration up, slight stretch at the peak/landing

remediation.title: Rubato, smaller
remediation.opening_cleffy: Rubato is subtle. Easy to overdo, easy to miss. We'll make it tiny. Just a touch. Then a touch more.

completion.cleffy: Time can bend now. Gently. Tomorrow — how to memorize. The three kinds of memory.

title: Memorization intro
subtitle: Act 44 · Three kinds of memory
goal: Meet the three kinds of memory pianists use — muscle, ear, and structural — and try each one on a short phrase.
piece_context: A first look at memorization. Tier 6 will spend a whole act on this. Today is a short intro — the three kinds of memory, named, tried, understood. You don't need to memorize anything today. Just meet the tools.

pages.figure: Three labeled boxes stacked: MUSCLE MEMORY (a hand), EAR MEMORY (an ear), STRUCTURAL MEMORY (a map). Banner: THREE KINDS.
pages.cleffy: Memorization. A quick look today. Tier 6 goes deeper. Three kinds of memory. Meet them.

pages.figure: A pianist mid-performance. Three speech bubbles above: 'my fingers know,' 'my ears know,' 'my head knows.' All at once.
pages.cleffy: Memorizing isn't one thing. Real pianists use three kinds of memory at once. When one slips, another catches you.

pages.figure: A hand on a keyboard, with the fingers glowing as if playing without looking. Caption: MUSCLE MEMORY — your hands know the shapes.
pages.cleffy: First — muscle memory. Your hands know the shapes. The key-to-key distances. The finger patterns. Built by repetition.

pages.figure: Keyboard. 5-note phrase. Cue: play it 3 times in a row. Feel the shape.
pages.cleffy: Try it. Play this five-note shape three times in a row. Look at your hand. Don't look at the screen. By the third time, your fingers should remember.

pages.figure: An ear icon, with musical notes flowing into it. Below, those same notes flowing back out of a head. Caption: EAR MEMORY — you hear what comes next before it plays.
pages.cleffy: Second — ear memory. You can hear the next phrase before you play it. Like knowing how a song goes.

pages.figure: A play button. The first 5 notes of Happy Birthday.

audio.A: Happy Birthday — opening phrase — first five notes

pages.cleffy: Listen. You know this one. Even if you've never played it today, you can hear what comes next. That's ear memory. You have it already for songs you know.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Happy Birthday first phrase outlined. No visual guides the second time.
pages.cleffy: Try it. Play Happy Birthday's opening. G G A G C B. Your ears lead your fingers.

pages.figure: A simple map of a piece: A SECTION (bars 1-8) — B SECTION (9-16) — A SECTION AGAIN (17-24). Arrows showing flow. Caption: STRUCTURAL MEMORY — the shape of the whole.
pages.cleffy: Third — structural memory. Knowing the map. Verse, chorus, verse. A, B, A. Not the notes — the form.

pages.figure: A pianist holding a map of a piece. Caption: 'If you get lost, you know where you are.'
pages.cleffy: Why this matters. If your fingers slip mid-performance, structure rescues you. You know where you are. You know what comes next section, even if you missed a note.

pages.figure: Three overlapping circles — muscle, ear, structural — with a gold star in the middle where they overlap. Caption: 'Strongest when all three lock in.'
pages.cleffy: The goal — all three at once. Fingers know. Ears know. Head knows. When one falters, the others hold you up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. An 8-bar phrase. Shape labeled A-B-A (2 bars - 4 bars - 2 bars).
pages.cleffy: Try all three on a short phrase. Play it through. Your hands learn. Your ears hear. Notice the A-B-A shape.
pages.followup_cleffy: Three kinds of knowing, all at once.

pages.figure: Cleffy seated. Speech bubble: 'Memorizing one piece well takes weeks, not minutes.' Calendar showing 3 weeks marked off.
pages.cleffy: The honest part. Memorizing one piece well takes weeks. Not minutes. Today is just the introduction. Tier 6 — you'll memorize a real piece.

pages.figure: Round counter. 3 rounds. Banner: WHICH KIND OF MEMORY?
pages.cleffy: Three rounds. Which kind of memory is being described?

pages.figure: Banner.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 219. Three small icons — hand, ear, map — linked by lines. Below: 'muscle, ear, structure.'
pages.cleffy: Three kinds of memory. Named. Tomorrow — Grieg. Arietta. Tiny piece, huge expression.

real_piano: Take one phrase of a piece you know. Play it with your eyes closed — that's muscle memory in action.
Sing the phrase first, then play it without reading. That's ear memory.
Name the sections of a piece you're learning — A, B, A, or verse/chorus. That's structural memory.
virtual_only: Replay page 11 — try playing the A-B-A phrase a few times and notice what's getting easier.

questions.figure: An icon of a hand on a keyboard playing without looking.
questions.prompt: Which kind of memory is this?

questions.figure: A pianist hearing the next phrase in their head before playing it.
questions.prompt: Which kind of memory is this?

questions.figure: A map of a piece: A section, B section, A section.
questions.prompt: Which kind of memory is this?

questions.audio: A familiar tune — Happy Birthday — playing its first phrase
questions.prompt: Can you hear what the next note would be? That's which kind of memory?
Play Happy Birthday's opening — G G A G C B — by ear, without reading it.
questions.accept: G4 G4 A4 G4 C5 B4 played in order

remediation.title: Three memories, one at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Three kinds is a lot to meet in one lesson. We'll take one at a time. Muscle. Ear. Structure. Slower.

completion.cleffy: Three ways to hold a piece in your head. Tomorrow — act boss. Grieg's Arietta. Tiny and huge.

title: Grieg Arietta
subtitle: Act 44 · Tiny piece, huge expression
goal: Play the opening of Grieg's Arietta Op. 12 No. 1 — simple melody, gentle LH pattern, big expressive breath across a small space.
piece_context: Act 44 boss. Grieg's Arietta — first piece in his Lyric Pieces, Op. 12. Only 19 bars in the original, simplified here to 8-12. Slow (Poco Andante e sostenuto, around 60 bpm). E♭ major. The whole act has been about expression on a long line — now you prove you can do it in a tiny room. Not a big flashy piece. A small room full of feeling.

piece.title: Arietta, Op. 12 No. 1
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — slow tempo, simple melody over gentle left-hand waltz-like pattern, huge expressive demands

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a small gold-framed portrait of Grieg. The sheet of the Arietta visible — short piece, only 19 bars. Banner: ACT 44 BOSS — GRIEG'S ARIETTA.
pages.cleffy: Act 44 boss. Grieg. Arietta. Nineteen bars. Tiny piece. Huge feeling.

pages.figure: A Norwegian landscape — fjord, pine trees. Grieg at a small upright piano. 'First piece of Lyric Pieces, 1867.'
pages.cleffy: Edvard Grieg. Norwegian. The Arietta is the first piece in his Lyric Pieces — a set of small, honest miniatures. Written at home, not for a concert hall.

pages.figure: Top of the Arietta score. Marking: 'Poco Andante e sostenuto. ♩ = 60.' Key signature: three flats (E♭ major).
pages.cleffy: Look at the top. Poco Andante e sostenuto. A little walking, sustained. 60 beats per minute. Slow. Three flats — E-flat major.

pages.figure: A play button. Duration: ~2 minutes. The full Arietta playing.

audio.A: The complete Arietta, played simply and expressively, about 2 minutes long

pages.cleffy: Listen all the way through. Short. Gentle. Deeper than it looks.

pages.figure: First two bars of the Arietta. RH: gentle singing melody starting on a high Bb4. LH: a broken-chord pattern climbing — Eb3, Bb3, Eb4, G4, Bb4.
pages.cleffy: The texture. Right hand sings the melody. One line. Left hand — a broken chord climbing under the melody. Eb low, then Bb, Eb, G, Bb up.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH broken-chord pattern: Eb3, Bb3, Eb4, G4, Bb4 — climbing across each bar. Tempo 60.
pages.cleffy: Left hand first. A broken chord climbing. Eb low, then up — Bb, Eb, G, Bb. Five notes rolling up across the bar. Smooth.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH melody, first 4 bars: Bb4 (long), Ab4, G4, F4, Eb4, long F4, Bb4.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. The opening melody. Starts high on Bb. Walks down. Lands on F. Simple. Sing it.

pages.figure: 8 bars of the Arietta. Arch drawn above: p at bar 1, gentle cresc. through bars 3-4, mp peak at bar 5, dim. back to p at bar 8.
pages.cleffy: The shape. Start soft. Grow a little through the middle. Peak — not loud, just fuller — at the midpoint. Soften home. The whole arc lives in p and mp. Nothing louder.

pages.figure: Keyboard. First 4 bars, both hands. Tempo 60. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. First four bars. LH keeps the gentle pulse. RH floats on top. Very slow. Very quiet.

pages.figure: Bars 3-5 of the melody. Tiny arrow: gentle push into the peak. Tiny arrow: gentle pull on the peak note. Almost invisible.
pages.cleffy: The rubato you learned — use it here. A tiny push into the peak bar. A tiny stretch on the peak note itself. Invisible to anyone not listening closely. That's the point.

pages.figure: Keyboard. All 8 simplified bars. Full arc drawn. LH pattern continuous.
pages.cleffy: The whole opening. Eight bars. LH — gentle, steady, quiet. Don't overplay the pattern. RH — sings. Breathes. Has the small push and pull at the peak.
pages.followup_cleffy: Tiny piece. Whole room of feeling.

pages.figure: Two side-by-side drawings. Left: a concert hall, empty. Right: a small wood-paneled room with one person at a piano. A lit candle. Caption: 'Grieg's room. Not a hall.'
pages.cleffy: The secret of small pieces. Don't play them for a hall. Play them for one person sitting next to you. That's the voice Grieg wrote in.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Same 8 bars. No guides this time. Tempo 60. Long arc shown.
pages.cleffy: Last time. Eight bars. No guides. Quiet. Sustained. One long breath across the whole piece. Not loud. Just felt.
pages.followup_cleffy: That's Grieg. That's the Arietta.

pages.figure: Banner — Act 44 boss mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones. Take your time.

pages.figure: Celebration card — ACT 44 COMPLETE 🎯. Cleffy and a small framed portrait of Grieg. A pyramid of acts showing 44/50 lit. Next preview: CLAIR DE LUNE coming.
pages.cleffy: Act 44. Closed. Long lines. Dynamic arcs. Push-pull rubato. Three kinds of memory. Grieg. A whole act about expression — on a long phrase, in a tiny piece. Both at once. Next act — Clair de Lune. Full piece, section by section. Tier 5 boss coming up soon.

real_piano: Play the 8-bar Arietta opening every day for a week. Once slow, once slower.
Find the peak note. Circle it in pencil. Every play-through, lean toward it and stretch on it.
After practicing, try playing only the RH melody — without LH. See if you can still shape the line.
Record yourself once. Listen back. Too loud? Too flat? Adjust tomorrow.
virtual_only: Replay pages 6, 7, 9, and 13 — LH alone, RH alone, first four together, then the whole thing.

questions.figure: The Arietta score top: 'Poco Andante e sostenuto. ♩ = 60.'
questions.prompt: What does this tempo marking tell you?
options: A little walking, sustained — around 60 bpm

questions.figure: The LH pattern of the Arietta — a broken chord climbing Eb3, Bb3, Eb4, G4, Bb4.
questions.prompt: What kind of texture is this?

questions.figure: The Arietta's 8-bar arc — p at start, mp at peak, p at end.
questions.prompt: How loud does the Arietta get?

questions.audio: The first 4 bars of the Arietta melody, played with a small push to the peak and stretch on the peak note
questions.prompt: What's happening at the peak note?
options: A gentle rubato — push and slight stretch

questions.audio: The Arietta LH broken-chord pattern alone — Eb3 Bb3 Eb4 G4 Bb4 climbing — four times
questions.prompt: Describe the LH pattern.
options: A broken chord climbing under the melody

questions.audio: A performance of the Arietta opening played gently, around mp
questions.prompt: How would you describe the overall dynamic?
Play the LH broken-chord pattern — Eb3, Bb3, Eb4, G4, Bb4 — four times in a row at 60 bpm.
questions.accept: Eb3 Bb3 Eb4 G4 Bb4 repeated four times, smooth climbing arpeggio

questions.prompt: Play the RH opening — Bb4 Ab4 G4 F4 Eb4 F4 G4 Bb4 — slowly, singing.
questions.accept: Those eight notes played in order, slowly, with sustained tone

questions.prompt: Play the first four bars hands together at 60 bpm, gently.
questions.accept: First four bars of the Arietta with LH pattern and RH melody together, quiet and slow

remediation.title: Arietta, one hand at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Act boss — and hands together is the hardest part. We'll do LH alone for three pages. RH alone for three. Then stitch. No rush. Expression comes after the notes are comfortable.

completion.cleffy: Act 44 sealed. Long lines. Dynamic arcs. Push-pull. Memory. Grieg. You played a piece that's about breath, not fireworks. That's a pianist's skill — the one most people never learn. Next act — Debussy. Clair de Lune, full piece, section by section.

title: Clair de Lune: A section
subtitle: Act 45 · Clair de Lune full
goal: Play the A section of Clair de Lune — the opening you first met in L125, now extended and polished.
piece_context: Act 45. Five lessons to build Clair de Lune, complete. Today — the A section. The famous opening. You already played it as the Tier 3 boss, 96 lessons ago. Extend it. Polish it. In D major, same simplification as before.

piece.title: Clair de Lune (A section — simplified)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — descending thirds, held pedal tones, soft touch, returning phrase

pages.figure: Cleffy at the foot of a small mountain. Banner: ACT 45 · CLAIR DE LUNE FULL. Caption: 'five lessons to the summit.'
pages.cleffy: New act. The last big piece before the tier boss — because the tier boss IS this piece. Five lessons. A section. B section. Climax. Play-through. Boss. You know how this one opens already. You played it at the end of Tier 3.

pages.figure: A piece map. Five labeled blocks in a row: A (opening), B (arpeggiated middle), CLIMAX (forte peak), A' (return), coda. A arrow under block A: 'today.'
pages.cleffy: The shape of Clair de Lune. A — the opening. Soft, drifting thirds over a low pedal note. B — an arpeggiated middle. Flowing, rolling. Climax — one forte peak, near the end. A' — the opening returns, quieter than before. Today — A.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'A section — opening and return.'

audio.A: Clair de Lune A section played softly, pedaled — opening phrase, a second phrase, then a gentle return of the opening.

pages.cleffy: Listen. The opening you know. A second phrase that follows. Then the opening comes back, even softer. That's the whole A section.

pages.figure: Grand staff. The four bars from L125 — RH descending thirds A-F#, G-E, F#-D, then F#-A floating up. LH holds D2. Marked pp.
pages.cleffy: Here it is. The four bars from Tier 3. Right-hand thirds drifting down. Left hand — one low D, held. D major. Two sharps. F-sharp and C-sharp.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. Both hands. Tempo 44. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Play the opening. The one you already know. Left hand presses D. Right hand lays each third on top. Soft. Very soft.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four new bars. RH: new descending pair pattern — E-G, D-F#, C#-E, D-F#. LH: moves A1 then D2 under the line. Dynamic: still pp, with a tiny hairpin near the middle.
pages.cleffy: After the opening, a second phrase. Right hand keeps moving in thirds. E-G. D-F-sharp. C-sharp-E. Back up to D-F-sharp. Left hand moves too — low A underneath, then back to D. Still whispering.

pages.figure: RH only. Four pairs: E-G, D-F#, C#-E, D-F#. Tempo 44.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Four new pairs. E-G. D-F-sharp. C-sharp-E. D-F-sharp. Soft fingers.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. LH moves A1 to D2. RH thirds. Tempo 42. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Both hands. Left hand moves now. Low A under the first two pairs. Back to low D for the last two. Don't press hard. Let it float.

pages.figure: Grand staff. The same four opening bars again — RH thirds, LH D2 held. A label above: 'the return — even quieter.' Dynamic: ppp.
pages.cleffy: Now the opening comes back. Exact same notes. But quieter than the first time. *ppp* — pianississimo. Three p's. As if it's drifting away.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Opening four bars. Tempo 42. Dynamic ppp.
pages.cleffy: Play the return. Softer than the first time. Barely touch the keys. Let the pedal do the singing.

pages.figure: Grand staff. All twelve bars of the A section. Tempo 42. Guides on. Dynamic arc: pp — slight swell — pp — ppp.
pages.cleffy: The whole A section. Twelve bars. Opening. Second phrase. Return. Slow. Soft. Unhurried.

pages.figure: Grand staff. No glowing keys. Tempo 42.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read, you play. Still soft. Still unhurried.
pages.followup_cleffy: The A section. Longer than Tier 3's version. You have the bones of Clair de Lune now.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 221 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 221. A moon over still water. Caption: 'A SECTION.'
pages.cleffy: A section — yours. Tomorrow — the B section. The arpeggiated middle. Different shape entirely.

real_piano: Clair de Lune A section, slow, once a day. Softer every time through.
Revisit the L125 opening. Notice how small it feels now.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11-12 — full A section, with and without guides.

questions.figure: A key signature with two sharps
questions.prompt: What key is our simplified Clair de Lune in?

questions.figure: Dynamic marking: ppp
questions.prompt: What does ppp mean?

questions.figure: A piece map with blocks labelled A, B, CLIMAX, A'
questions.prompt: Where is the A section in this piece?

questions.audio: Opening four bars, then a return four bars that's even softer
questions.prompt: Which version is softer — the first or the return?
Play the opening third — F-sharp and A together, very soft.
questions.accept: F#4 and A4 together, quietly

remediation.title: A section, one phrase at a time
remediation.opening_cleffy: Same twelve bars. Slower rebuild. Opening alone. Then second phrase alone. Then return. Then stitched.

completion.cleffy: A section in hand. Twelve bars, quiet, drifting. Tomorrow — B. The rolling middle.

title: Clair de Lune: B section
subtitle: Act 45 · Clair de Lune full
goal: Play the B section of Clair de Lune — a flowing arpeggiated middle where the left hand rolls under a right-hand melody.
piece_context: Day two of Clair de Lune. The A section drifts. The B section flows. The left hand rolls broken chords — the same Arabesque-style motion you played in Act 43. The right hand sings on top.

piece.title: Clair de Lune (B section — simplified)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — LH broken arpeggios, RH melody, quiet but more motion than A

pages.figure: Cleffy at the foot of the Debussy mountain. Banner: LESSON 222. A piece map with block B highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Day two. The B section. A section drifts. B section flows. Still quiet — but now the left hand is moving.

pages.figure: Split panel. Left: A section — RH thirds, LH one held note. Right: B section — RH single melody notes, LH rolling arpeggio pattern. Arrow between: 'new shape.'
pages.cleffy: Here's what changes. Left hand — no more one held note. It rolls. Right hand — no more stacked thirds. It sings, one note at a time. Totally different texture. Same quiet world.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'B section — the flowing middle.'

audio.A: Clair de Lune B section, softly pedaled — RH melody floats, LH broken chord underneath rolls gently.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Hear the two things at once. Top — a simple melody. Four or five notes. Bottom — a rolling pattern, never still.

pages.figure: Bass staff. A rolling six-note pattern: D3-F#3-A3-F#3-A3-D4. Labeled 'one bar — LH broken chord.'
pages.cleffy: The left-hand roll. Six notes, one bar. D. F-sharp. A. F-sharp. A. D — up an octave. Up, back, up, back, up. Like water.

pages.figure: Keyboard. LH pattern highlighted in order: D3-F#3-A3-F#3-A3-D4.
pages.cleffy: Play the left hand alone. Slow. Feel the shape. Up four notes, fall back, rise again.

pages.figure: Treble staff. A four-bar melody. Bar 1: F#4-A4. Bar 2: E4-D4. Bar 3: F#4-G4-F#4. Bar 4: A4-G4. Labeled 'RH sings over the roll — 2 to 3 notes per bar.'
pages.cleffy: The right hand. Two or three notes per bar — small shapes. Bar one rises F-sharp to A. Bar two falls E to D. Bar three peaks at G then settles. Bar four eases A down to G. Let each shape float while the left rolls underneath.

pages.figure: Keyboard. RH melody — small 2-3 note shapes per bar.
pages.cleffy: Right hand alone. Two or three notes per bar. Sing the shape. No rush.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Bar 1. RH F#4 then A4. LH D3-F#3-A3-F#3-A3-D4. Tempo 48.
pages.cleffy: One bar. Both hands. Right hand sings F-sharp into A. Left hand rolls underneath. Don't let the left hand get louder than the right.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. RH holds F#4, E4, F#4, A4 each bar. LH rolls D-F#-A-F#-A-D pattern each bar (shifting slightly under E melody). pp marked.
pages.cleffy: Four bars of the B section. Every bar — LH rolls. RH holds one melody note. Simple, once you see the shape.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Four bars. Tempo 46. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Four bars. Both hands. Left rolls under every melody note. Keep the left soft — it supports, it doesn't lead.

pages.figure: Diagram. Two hands. RH has a gold glow around its note. LH is in dim grey. Label: 'RH sings. LH supports.'
pages.cleffy: Voicing. Act 42's gift. Right hand note — loud enough to hear over the roll. Left hand roll — soft enough to stay underneath. Water flows. The singer sings above it.

pages.figure: Same four bars. RH notes glowing gold. Tempo 46. No guides.
pages.cleffy: Same four bars. Right hand firmer. Left hand softer. Let the top line sing.
pages.followup_cleffy: A melody, floating on water. That's the B section.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 222 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 222. A river under moonlight, a single bird above. Caption: 'B SECTION.'
pages.cleffy: B section — yours. Tomorrow — climax. The one loud moment in the whole piece.

real_piano: B section, slow, left hand alone. Then hands together.
Practice keeping LH softer than RH. It's a specific skill.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10 and 12 — hands together, with and without voicing focus.

questions.figure: Grand staff. RH long note, LH rolling six-note pattern.
questions.prompt: What is the left hand doing?

questions.figure: Diagram — RH gold, LH grey.
questions.prompt: Which hand should be louder in the B section?

questions.figure: Piece map with block B highlighted
questions.prompt: The B section is...

questions.audio: B section excerpt — RH long melody, LH rolling arpeggio
questions.prompt: Which hand is moving faster?
Play the LH roll alone — D3, F#3, A3, F#3, A3, D4.
questions.accept: Six-note LH pattern, even, soft

remediation.title: B section, hand by hand
remediation.opening_cleffy: Hands together is hard when one hand moves and the other holds. LH alone. RH alone. One bar together. Then four.

completion.cleffy: B section in your hands. Rolling water. Melody above. Tomorrow — the one loud moment. The climax.

title: Clair de Lune: climax
subtitle: Act 45 · Clair de Lune full
goal: Play the climax of Clair de Lune — the one forte peak in an otherwise whispered piece.
piece_context: Day three. Clair de Lune whispers for most of its length. Then, around bar 27, it blooms. One forte. One peak. Everything Debussy has been saving. Then it recedes back to quiet. Today — the bloom.

piece.title: Clair de Lune (climax — simplified)
piece.difficulty: upper-intermediate — the one loud moment, dynamic arc pp → f → pp

pages.figure: Cleffy pointing up at a single gold peak on the mountain. Banner: LESSON 223. Piece map — CLIMAX block highlighted.
pages.cleffy: Day three. The climax. Clair de Lune is quiet. Almost the whole way. One moment — it blooms. One forte. That's today.

pages.figure: A long curve over the full piece map. pp at the left. A peak labeled 'f' in the middle-right. pp at the right. Caption: 'one arc — thirty bars long.'
pages.cleffy: The shape of the whole piece. Starts *pp*. Stays *pp* through A and most of B. Grows. Grows more. Peaks at the climax — *f*. Then melts back to *pp*. One big curve.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'climax — approach, peak, release.'

audio.A: Clair de Lune climax passage: quiet rolling arpeggios growing louder, peaking with a bright forte chord, then dying back to pp within a few bars.

pages.cleffy: Listen. Quiet. Growing. Growing more. Then — bloom. A chord stands up. Then it fades.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two approach bars. LH continues the B-section roll. RH climbs a stepwise line: F#4, A4, B4, D5. A long crescendo hairpin underneath growing from pp to f.
pages.cleffy: The approach. Two bars. Left hand keeps rolling, like in B. Right hand climbs — F-sharp, G, A, then A, B, B, D. A crescendo under it all. Gets louder as the line climbs.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two bars approach. Tempo 48. Crescendo marking. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Play the approach. Crescendo as the right hand climbs. Start *pp*. End almost *f*.

pages.figure: Grand staff. The climax bar. RH: a full four-note chord D5-A4-F#4-D4. LH: D2-A2 octave-ish. Marked f. Pedal marked.
pages.cleffy: The peak. One big chord. Right hand — four notes. D, A, F-sharp, D — open and bright. Left hand — low D, low A. A deep base. Marked *f*. Forte. Loud. For one moment.

pages.figure: Keyboard. Full peak chord highlighted. LH: D2-A2. RH: D4-F#4-A4-D5.
pages.cleffy: Press the peak chord. Both hands together. Not harsh. Full. Warm. Let it ring.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two release bars. RH: D5, A4, F#4, D4 — stepping down. LH: back to D2 and D3 soft. Diminuendo hairpin from f back to pp.
pages.cleffy: The release. Two bars. Right hand — descends. D, A, F-sharp, D. Left hand — back to low D, quiet. Diminuendo. Forte to *pp* in two bars. The bloom folds back up.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Two release bars. Tempo 48. Diminuendo marking. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Play the release. Every note a little softer than the last. End as quietly as you started.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Five bars — two approach, one peak, two release. Full arc. Tempo 46.
pages.cleffy: The whole climax. Five bars. Approach. Peak. Release. One big breath.

pages.figure: Grand staff. Same five bars. No guides. Tempo 46.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You shape it. *pp* to *f* to *pp*. The biggest moment of the whole piece.
pages.followup_cleffy: The bloom, and the fold. Everything Debussy saved up. One chord.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding a single golden flower. Caption: 'one moment only.'
pages.cleffy: One thing about the climax. It only works because the rest of the piece is quiet. If you hammered every bar, this chord would mean nothing. Restraint is what makes the peak a peak.

pages.figure: Banner — Lesson 223 mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: Postcard — Lesson 223. A single gold peak on a dark hillside. Caption: 'CLIMAX.'
pages.cleffy: Climax — yours. Tomorrow — everything together. A-B-climax-A'. Slow.

real_piano: Climax passage, slow. Practice the crescendo — soft to loud is a skill.
Play the peak chord ten times. Find the warm sound, not the harsh one.
virtual_only: Replay pages 10-11 — the full climax arc.

questions.figure: Dynamic marking: f
questions.prompt: What does f mean?

questions.figure: Long arc over a piece map: pp at left, f in the middle, pp at right
questions.prompt: How many forte peaks are in Clair de Lune?

questions.figure: A hairpin opening wide
