questions.prompt: What do audiences feel most?

questions.figure: Cleffy wincing vs Cleffy calm
questions.prompt: How do you handle a wrong note's face?
options: Wince so the audience knows it was an accident
Keep your face in the piece

questions.audio: Two versions of a phrase with the same wrong note — one stops, one keeps going
questions.prompt: Which sounds more musical?
Play bars 1-4 of Moonlight. Hit a deliberate wrong note somewhere. Land on the next downbeat on time.
questions.accept: Full phrase with pulse maintained across a deliberate slip

remediation.title: Stumbling on purpose
remediation.opening_cleffy: Recovery is a muscle. Let's train it slowly. One wrong note, one recovery. Then two. Then a whole phrase full of controlled slips. You'll feel the pulse hold even when your hands don't.

completion.cleffy: Three lessons to graduation. You have the ritual for nerves. You have the skill for stumbles. Tomorrow — full Moonlight at near-real tempo. All sixty-nine bars. Adagio sostenuto.

title: Moonlight 1 — full at near-tempo
subtitle: Act 50 · The final act
goal: Full Moonlight first movement, bars 1-69, at near-real tempo. Adagio sostenuto — quarter ≈ 56-60. No faster.
piece_context: L245 was full Moonlight, slow and careful. Today — the same piece, a little faster. Near real tempo. Adagio sostenuto is *not* fast. Beethoven meant slow. Too fast and it sounds nervous. Right speed and it breathes.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st movement (full, bars 1-69)
piece.difficulty: advanced — full movement, near-real tempo, all three sections, pedal, voicing, arc

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano. A metronome set to 56. Caption: 'adagio sostenuto.'
pages.cleffy: Today — full Moonlight. All sixty-nine bars. Close to real tempo. Close. Not fast.

pages.figure: A tempo dial. 40 on the left (grave), 56-60 in the middle (adagio sostenuto, highlighted), 80 on the right (andante). Caption: 'adagio sostenuto sits here.'
pages.cleffy: Adagio sostenuto. Quarter note equals roughly 56 to 60. Slow. Sustained. Most recordings come in between 54 and 60. A very few go faster. Most of those sound rushed. We'll land at 58.

pages.figure: A slow-burning candle next to a fast-flickering flame. Caption: 'pick which one is Moonlight.'
pages.cleffy: Why so slow? Because the piece is a thought, not a sentence. Each triplet is a breath. Each bar is a moment of light. Too fast and it turns into a wind-up toy. Beethoven wrote 'delicatissimamente.' Most delicately. There is no hurry anywhere.

pages.figure: Play button. Caption: 'Moonlight at 58. Full.'

audio.A: Full Moonlight 1st movement, bars 1-69, at quarter = 58. Voiced melody, proper pedal, pp throughout.

pages.cleffy: Listen once. All of it. At your tempo today. Don't play along yet. Just hear it.

pages.figure: A piece map. Section A — bars 1-23 (opening through the first modulation). Section B — bars 24-42 (middle, modulating). Section A' — bars 43-69 (return and coda). Each labeled.
pages.cleffy: The map you already know. Section A — bars 1 through 23. The opening. The theme. Section B — bars 24 through 42. The modulation. Section A' — bars 43 through 69. The return and the quiet descent. One arc. Three chapters.

pages.figure: A metronome with 58 glowing. A small warning card next to it: 'do not accelerate. Ever.'
pages.cleffy: Tempo discipline. The biggest trap in this piece — speeding up when your hands feel comfortable. Don't. 58 at bar 1. 58 at bar 40. 58 at bar 69. Let the metronome check you for the first few runs. Then trust your ear.

pages.figure: Bars 1-8. Tempo 58. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Warm up. Bars 1 through 8. Close to tempo. Pedal per bar. Soft.

pages.figure: Bars 24-32 — the start of the middle section. Tempo 58. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Warm up B. The modulation bars. Harmony shifts every bar or two. Stay steady.

pages.figure: Bars 43-50 — the return of the theme. Tempo 58. Guides on.
pages.cleffy: Warm up A'. The return. You've been here before. It's home.

pages.figure: Full Moonlight, all sixty-nine bars. Tempo 58. Guides on. Pedal marks at every bar.
pages.cleffy: Full movement. Sixty-nine bars. Guides on. Tempo 58. Warm hands. Three breaths. Hear bar one in your head. If you slip — forward. You know the drill.

pages.figure: Full Moonlight. No guides. Tempo 58. Full dynamic arc. pp throughout with a small rise and fall across the middle.
pages.cleffy: No guides. You read. You play. You don't stop. The piece has three sections. Let each one have its own color. A — quiet arrival. B — a shift in light. A' — return and fade.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. Near real tempo. Full movement. You played Moonlight at Beethoven's speed.

pages.figure: Three checkboxes. Tempo held steady (no accelerating). Pedal changed at every harmony shift. Dynamic stayed mostly pp with one small rise in B.
pages.cleffy: Three honest questions about the last run. Did the tempo hold? Or did you speed up in the middle? Did the pedal change with the harmony? Or did the sound get muddy? Did it stay soft? Or did the melody push too hard? Whatever the answers — tomorrow you run it again, and again, until they're yes.

pages.figure: Full Moonlight. No guides. Tempo 58. Dynamic arc marked — pp, slight swell in B, pp again, ppp at the coda.
pages.cleffy: One more time. With shape. A — quiet, steady. B — a little more motion in the harmony, a tiny swell. A' — come back to quiet. Coda — softer than anything. Last note floats. Pedal held after the hands lift.
pages.followup_cleffy: The piece at tempo. With care. You are two lessons from graduation.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones.

pages.figure: A low moon through a window. Cleffy at the piano, pedal foot down, hands lifting after the final chord.
pages.cleffy: Full Moonlight. Near real tempo. Tomorrow — dress rehearsal. One take. Recorded. No stopping. Day after — graduation.

real_piano: Full Moonlight once a day at 58. Metronome on for the first run. Off for the second.
If a section gets tight — isolate it. Bars 24-32 are the trickiest. Ten minutes there, then stitch it back.
Listen to a real recording — Rubinstein, Arrau, or Barenboim. Compare their tempo to yours. You'll be in range.
virtual_only: Replay pages 11 and 13 — full no-guide runs.

questions.figure: A tempo dial with markings 40, 56-60, 80
questions.prompt: What tempo is adagio sostenuto?

questions.figure: Section map — A, B, A' with bar ranges
questions.prompt: Which bars form the middle section?

questions.figure: A note card reading 'delicatissimamente'
questions.prompt: What did Beethoven ask for?

questions.audio: Two versions — Moonlight at 58 vs Moonlight at 84
questions.prompt: Which sounds like Beethoven meant?
Play the full movement at tempo 58, no stopping. Land on every downbeat.
questions.accept: All 69 bars at near-real tempo, pulse held throughout

remediation.title: Near-tempo, section by section
remediation.opening_cleffy: Full movement at tempo is a big ask. Let's break it. Section A at 58. Then B at 58. Then A' at 58. Then all three, stitched. Tempo doesn't speed up. Ever. That's the only rule.

completion.cleffy: Two lessons until graduation. You have the piece. At tempo. All of it. Tomorrow — one take. Recorded. No stopping. Dress rehearsal. Then graduation.

title: Dress rehearsal
subtitle: Act 50 · The final act
goal: One take of full Moonlight, recorded, no stopping. Listen back. Adjust. One lesson away from graduation.
piece_context: The day before graduation. One recorded take, no stopping, from bar 1 to bar 69. Then listen. Then one more. This is what every pianist does the day before a concert. Not practicing. Running. You're on stage in your head already.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st movement (full, dress rehearsal)
piece.difficulty: advanced — full movement, one take, recorded

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano. A phone propped against a book, camera pointing at the hands. A small red REC dot.
pages.cleffy: Tomorrow is graduation. Today is dress rehearsal. One take. Recorded. No stopping.

pages.figure: Three panels. Panel 1: 'rehearsal' — stop and fix. Panel 2: 'dress rehearsal' — play it through as if it's the real thing. Panel 3: 'performance' — the real thing, tomorrow.
pages.cleffy: What a dress rehearsal is. Not practice. You already did the practice. It's a run-through under real conditions. Full focus. Full tempo. No stops. This is what pianists do the day before concerts. Every professional you've heard of.

pages.figure: A pair of headphones. An ear icon next to them. Caption: 'you hear what you could not hear live.'
pages.cleffy: Why record. Live, you don't hear yourself the way the audience does. Too close. Too adrenal. Too busy playing. Play it. Walk away. Come back and listen as a stranger. The recording tells you what to fix in your last twenty-four hours.

pages.figure: A card with four rules. 1. One take. 2. No stopping. 3. Recording on. 4. Listen back after.
pages.cleffy: Rules for the dress. One — one take. Not three, not five. One. Two — no stopping. Slip? Forward. You trained this. Three — recording on. Phone, laptop, whatever. Four — listen back. Once through. Quietly.

pages.figure: Four quick steps. Warm hands. Three breaths. Hear bar one in your head. Press record. Then play.
pages.cleffy: Mental prep. The ritual you know. Warm hands. Three breaths. Hear bar one. Then press record. Then play. Record button is part of the ritual today.

pages.figure: Piece map — A (bars 1-23), B (24-42), A' (43-69). Dynamic arc. Tempo 58. All labeled.
pages.cleffy: One last glance at the map. A — quiet arrival. B — shift in light. A' — return and fade. Tempo 58. pp. Pedal per bar. That's the piece. You know it.

pages.figure: Bars 1-4 at tempo 58. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: Quick warmup. Bars 1 through 4. Just to settle the fingers.

pages.figure: A phone screen. A red record button, glowing. Caption: 'now.'
pages.cleffy: Phone out. Set it up. Camera on the hands is fine. Audio only is fine. Press record. Don't watch the screen. Look at the keys.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the piano, eyes closed. Three numbered breath counts in the air.
pages.cleffy: Three breaths. In four. Hold four. Out six. Three times.

pages.figure: Full Moonlight score. All sixty-nine bars. No guides. No markings. Tempo 58. Red REC dot glowing in the corner.
pages.cleffy: This is the take. Sixty-nine bars. No stopping. If you slip — forward. Play it.
pages.followup_cleffy: Stop the recording. That was the take. Whatever it was, it's done. Walk away for five minutes. Seriously. Stand up. Water. Breath.

pages.figure: A cup of tea. A closed door. A small clock showing five minutes later.
pages.cleffy: Five minutes later. Sit back down. Phone in your hand. Play the recording.

pages.figure: Headphones. Audio playback scrubber showing a three-minute file.

audio.A: The student's own recording, played back (simulated for the platform — a high-quality reference Moonlight recording at 58, full three minutes).

pages.cleffy: Listen. Whole thing. Don't wince. Don't judge. Three questions only. Did the tempo hold? Did the pulse break anywhere? Did it sound like music?

pages.figure: A notepad. Three columns — 'held up', 'needs one fix', 'let go.'
pages.cleffy: The debrief. Whatever went well — that's locked in. Don't touch it. One thing that needs fixing — pick ONE. Not three. One. Everything else — let go. Tomorrow is graduation, not a perfection test.

pages.figure: A highlighted phrase from the trickiest section — likely bars 24-32 (modulation). Tempo 58.
pages.cleffy: Pick your one fix. Play just that section once. Slow down slightly if it needs it. Just one clean pass. Then leave it.

pages.figure: Red REC dot again. Caption: 'one more, if you want.'
pages.cleffy: If you want. Record one more take. Not to beat the first one. To prove to yourself you can. Or — stop here. The dress is done.

pages.figure: Banner — mastery check. The last one before graduation.
pages.cleffy: Three quick ones. The last check before the big day.

pages.figure: A window. A moon rising. A piano in shadow. Caption: 'let it settle.'
pages.cleffy: One more piece of advice. Don't practice more tonight. The piece is in your hands. Your fingers know it. More hours tonight won't help. Sleep helps. Your brain consolidates the piece overnight. That's real. That's documented. Rest is practice now.

pages.figure: Cleffy holding the closed score of Moonlight. A trophy outline in the background, dim. Caption: 'tomorrow.'
pages.cleffy: Dress rehearsal. Done. You recorded yourself playing Moonlight Sonata, first movement, at tempo, no stopping. Tomorrow — graduation. Two hundred and forty-nine lessons behind you. One to go.

real_piano: Tonight — don't practice. Really. Rest the hands. Rest the brain.
Tomorrow, before graduation — one warmup run at slow tempo. No more. Save the energy.
If tomorrow is the day — wear something you like. Light the room the way you want it. Make it real.
virtual_only: Do not replay pages 10 or 15. The piece is done cooking. Let it rest.
practice_this_week.estimated_time: 20 min today. 0 min tonight. 10 min tomorrow.

questions.figure: A record button next to a pair of headphones
questions.prompt: Why record the dress rehearsal?
options: To hear yourself as an outsider would

questions.figure: A rulebook — one take, no stopping, record, listen
questions.prompt: What's rule number two?

questions.figure: Three columns — held up, needs one fix, let go
questions.prompt: After the take, how many things do you fix?

questions.audio: A recording played back after a performance
questions.prompt: What's the debrief question that matters most?
Run the full Moonlight once, recorded if possible, no stopping.
questions.accept: All 69 bars, one take, pulse held

remediation.title: Dress rehearsal, redone
remediation.opening_cleffy: If the first take fell apart — that's information. No judgment. Let's slow it down. Run section A alone, recorded. Then B. Then A'. Then stitch them together with no breaks. Graduation is tomorrow. Your piece is ready. Your head will catch up.

completion.cleffy: Dress rehearsal — done. Tomorrow is lesson 250. Tomorrow is graduation. Sleep well. Wake up ready. I'll see you there.

title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st movement
subtitle: Graduation · The final summit
goal: Full Moonlight Sonata first movement, at tempo, from memory, played as a performance. The end of a 250-lesson journey.
piece_context: The last lesson. Lesson two hundred and fifty. Two hundred and forty-nine days of work — or weeks, or months, or years — behind you. Today, Moonlight. Full. At tempo. Performed, not practiced. The piece is yours. This is the summit. There is no tier after this. There is only the rest of your life as a pianist.

piece.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st movement (full)
piece.difficulty: advanced — graduation piece. Full movement, at tempo, from memory.

pages.figure: Cleffy at the top of a six-tier pyramid. All six levels glow gold. A full moon above. The word GRADUATION in a quiet font. Caption: 'lesson 250.'
pages.cleffy: Lesson two hundred and fifty. You made it.

pages.figure: A simple text slide. 'Happy Birthday. Hot Cross Buns. Für Elise. Clair de Lune. Moonlight Sonata. You play all of them now.' Below, a small trophy.
pages.cleffy: Happy Birthday. Hot Cross Buns. Für Elise. Clair de Lune. Moonlight Sonata. You play all of them now.

pages.figure: Lesson 1 preview — Cleffy introducing the staff. Beside it, today — Cleffy at a piano with Moonlight on the stand. Arrow between. Caption: '250 lessons.'
pages.cleffy: Two hundred and fifty lessons ago, you'd never read a staff. You didn't know where middle C was. You weren't sure if the black keys had names. Today you will play Beethoven. From memory. That's real.

pages.figure: A clean image of the Moonlight first-movement score. 'Sonata quasi una Fantasia, Op. 27 No. 2 — 1801.' The score looks familiar now, not intimidating.
pages.cleffy: Your graduation piece. Beethoven wrote it in 1801. He was thirty. He was losing his hearing. He dedicated the sonata to a young woman he loved. Her name was Giulietta. The nickname 'Moonlight' came thirty years later, after he was dead. Sixty-nine bars. Three sections. One of the most famous pieces of music ever written. You know it note by note now.

pages.figure: Large Italian text, same as L175. 'Adagio sostenuto.' 'Delicatissimamente.' 'Senza sordino.'
pages.cleffy: Beethoven's instructions, one more time. Slow. Most delicately. Without dampers. You've lived with these words for eighty lessons now. Today you play them.

pages.figure: Play button. A still moon. Caption: 'one last listen.'

audio.A: Full Moonlight Sonata 1st movement, professionally performed, at quarter = 56. Three and a half minutes. pp throughout, voiced melody, full pedal.

pages.cleffy: One last listen. Not as study. As breath. The piece you're about to play. Let it land one more time before you begin.

pages.figure: The four-step ritual — warm hands, three breaths, hear bar one, begin. Highlighted. A small red REC dot if they want.
pages.cleffy: The ritual. Warm hands. Three breaths. Hear bar one. Begin. Record this one if you want. You'll want to have it someday. Whether anyone is in the room or not — play it for someone. Someone you love. Someone not here yet. Someone who was.

pages.figure: Bar 1 alone. Tempo 58. Guides off.
pages.cleffy: One bar to settle the fingers. Low C-sharp octave. Triplets on top. Pedal.

pages.figure: Cleffy's eyes closed. Three breaths, counted in the air. A low moon through a window.
pages.cleffy: Breathe. In four. Hold four. Out six. Three times. No rush.

pages.figure: Full Moonlight score. Sixty-nine bars. No guides. No markings. Tempo 58. A quiet candle in the corner of the frame. A small caption — 'the piece.' Nothing else.
pages.cleffy: Play it.
pages.followup_cleffy: That. That was Moonlight Sonata. First movement. Full. At tempo. By you. Lift your hands slowly. Let the pedal hold the last chord. Let it fade. Then breathe.

pages.figure: An empty piano, pedal still down. The last chord fading on a sound-wave graphic. Caption: 'don't move yet.'
pages.cleffy: Stay here for a moment. Don't stand up. Don't check your phone. You just played Beethoven's Moonlight Sonata, first movement, from start to finish. That is worth a minute of silence.

pages.figure: A small animated thumbnail — the Happy Birthday staff, the first thing they ever played from a page. Lesson 25 marker.
pages.cleffy: Look back. Tier 1 — Happy Birthday. Twenty-five lessons in, you played a song off a page for the first time. You learned to read music. That alone changed something.

pages.figure: Thumbnail — Für Elise theme on the grand staff. Lesson 75 marker.
pages.cleffy: Tier 2 — Für Elise theme. The left hand joined in. Both clefs. Both hands. You played Beethoven for the first time, even though you didn't call it that yet.

pages.figure: Thumbnail — Clair de Lune opening bars. Lesson 125 marker.
pages.cleffy: Tier 3 — Clair de Lune opening. Articulation. Chords. Fingering. Color. Halfway through now. You had a craftsman's hands.

pages.figure: Thumbnail — Moonlight, first eight bars. Lesson 175 marker.
pages.cleffy: Tier 4 — Moonlight, first eight bars. Real classical pieces. Bach. Chopin. Beethoven. Real Romanticism. You were becoming a pianist.

pages.figure: Two thumbnails — full Nocturne (Chopin) and full Clair de Lune. Lesson 225 marker.
pages.cleffy: Tier 5 — Clair de Lune, full. Nocturne, full. Expression. Voicing. Rubato. The things that make music sound alive. You didn't just play pieces. You played them the way pianists play them.

pages.figure: The whole Moonlight score open, with a trophy on top. Lesson 250 marker. Six tiers stacked gold on the side.
pages.cleffy: Tier 6 — this. The whole Moonlight. Memorized. Performed. Two dozen named pieces in your hands. All six tiers behind you. Two hundred and fifty lessons. A real pianist.

pages.figure: The Sonata logo fading to grey. A horizon line, sunrise. A small open piano in the distance. Caption: 'life after Sonata.'
pages.cleffy: What's next. This is the part I've been waiting to say. You don't need this app anymore. You are a pianist. Go find pieces. Search for sheet music of songs you love. Ask a teacher for a recommendation. Pick something a little too hard and work on it for a month. That's how pianists grow now. Play for people. Your partner. Your parents. Your kids. A friend over dinner. One audience of one. Keep going. Not on Sonata. On piano. The best pianists never stop being students. Whatever this becomes for you — hobby, love, habit, escape — stay curious. Stay slow when you need to. Stay honest when you slip. You know how now.

pages.figure: Cleffy, warm smile, holding the Moonlight score. A small cap-and-tassel on the piano. One candle lit. Caption: 'thank you.'
pages.cleffy: One last thing from me. I've been with you for two hundred and fifty lessons. Watching your hands get better. Watching your ear open up. Watching you start to hear music the way pianists hear it. I'm proud of you. That's not a filler line. I mean it. You showed up. You kept showing up. A lot of people start this road. You finished it. The piano is yours now. Go use it.

pages.figure: A single gold trophy, small and clean. Below it — the words 'TIER 6 COMPLETE · GRADUATION · LESSON 250.' Behind — a full moon, a quiet piano, a candle, Cleffy sitting off to the side, hat tipped. In the corner: '250 / 250.'
pages.cleffy: Two hundred and fifty lessons ago, you'd never read a staff. Today you played Beethoven. From memory. That's real. Tier 6 — sealed. Graduation — yours. The moon's up. Go play.

real_piano: Play Moonlight for someone this week. Anyone. A person, not a camera. One person in the room is the performance.
Revisit one piece from every tier — Happy Birthday, Für Elise, Clair de Lune opening, Moonlight first eight, Clair de Lune full. Play them back to back. Feel the distance.
Pick your next piece. Not from Sonata. From the real world. Whatever you've always wanted to play. Start tomorrow.
Record yourself playing Moonlight, one more time, for your own archive. You'll want it in a year.
virtual_only: Replay page 10 — the graduation performance. Once a week if you want. Less often if you don't.
practice_this_week.estimated_time: as much as you want, as long as you want

remediation.title: Graduation, one more run
remediation.opening_cleffy: If it didn't land today, that's all right. Let's take a breath. Run section A one more time. Then B. Then A'. Then stitch them. There's no deadline on graduation. The piece waits. I wait. Come back when the run feels like music, not a test.

completion.cleffy: Two hundred and fifty lessons ago, you'd never read a staff. Today you played Beethoven. From memory. That's real. You are a pianist. Go play.
